guitar:pitchConstraint:seed.character: Seed playing on guitar feels like you have found a little three-note motif somewhere on the fretboard and you are turning it over and over — like Nile Rodgers worrying a funk riff or John Frusciante orbiting a pentatonic cell. Your fingers settle into a small cluster of frets and the variations bloom from that tiny home base.
guitar:pitchConstraint:seed.whyPractice: This builds motivic development — the ability to take a small idea and make it evolve. On guitar, it trains you to see multiple fingering options for the same three notes and to vary articulation rather than always reaching for new notes.
steps: Pick any three scale notes that sit comfortably under your fingers. This is your seed.
Play the seed simply — just the three notes in order, picked cleanly. Repeat twice to establish the motif.
Now vary ONE element: change the order of the three notes.
Next variation: keep the same three notes but change the rhythm.
Next: swap out one of the three notes for a neighboring scale degree.
Try articulation variations: hammer-on the second note, or slide into the first note.
After four or five variations, let the seed grow — add a fourth note, then return to the three-note core.
progression: Beginner: Choose three notes on two adjacent strings. Vary only order and rhythm.
Intermediate: Allow one note substitution per variation. Explore the same seed in a different octave.
Advanced: Develop the seed across multiple positions using slides. Layer in bends, harmonics, and dynamic shifts.
examples: Seed: root, third, fifth. Variation 1: fifth, third, root. Variation 2: root, fifth, third.
Seed: fifth, sixth, root. Variation: replace sixth with seventh — motif rises higher but keeps its shape.
Seed: root, second, third. Play with all hammer-ons, then all pull-offs, then picked staccato — same notes, three characters.
Seed: third, fifth, root. Slide up to a higher position and replay the same intervals on different strings.
Seed: root, fourth, fifth. Add a bend: pick the fourth and bend toward the fifth, then release and land on root.
guitar:pitchConstraint:seed.etude: Choose a seed: root on the fourth string, third on the third string, fifth on the third string. Play it straight, two beats per note, rest two beats. Repeat reversed: fifth, third, root. Now play it fast — all three notes in one beat, rest three beats. Next: replace the fifth with the sixth. Now slide the original seed shape up one position and play it there. Return to original and play the seed one final time, slowly.
guitar:pitchConstraint:seed.watchOut: The failure mode is abandoning the seed too quickly. If you swap out two of the three notes, you have a new idea, not a variation. Keep at least two of the original three notes for every variation.

guitar:pitchConstraint:forbidden.character: Forbidden notes turn your fretboard into a minefield — certain scale degrees are off limits, and your fingers have to navigate around the gaps. It is the angular, unexpected quality you hear in post-punk guitar lines. Your hand learns new pathways because the familiar ones are blocked.
guitar:pitchConstraint:forbidden.whyPractice: This forces fretboard awareness at a deep level — you cannot rely on muscle memory patterns because some of the usual landing spots are gone. It builds real-time interval visualization and helps you discover fresh melodic colors hiding in scale degrees you normally skip.
steps: Identify which scale degrees are forbidden. Visualize where those notes sit in your current position — picture those frets as dead zones.
Play slowly at first. Before each note, check: is this fret one of the forbidden degrees?
Notice the new intervals this creates. Removing the third forces you into fourths and fifths.
Use the forbidden frets as physical landmarks. Your fingers can touch them for muting, but never let them sound.
Practice phrases that approach a forbidden note and redirect at the last moment.
Try the same forbidden set in a different position on the neck.
progression: Beginner: One forbidden note. Stay in one position and simply skip that degree.
Intermediate: Two forbidden notes. Navigate across multiple strings, noticing the unusual interval jumps.
Advanced: Three forbidden notes. Move across positions while tracking which frets are off-limits in each new area.
examples: Forbidden: third. Play root, second, skip third, fourth, fifth. The missing third gives a suspended, open quality.
Forbidden: root. Play second, third, fifth, sixth, seventh, never resolving to home. Feels restless.
Forbidden: fifth and seventh. Play root, second, third, fourth, sixth. The usual strong tones are gone.
Forbidden: second. Leap from root to third, then step to fourth, fifth. The opening leap becomes a signature.
Forbidden: fourth and sixth. Root, second, third, fifth, seventh. Only the strong scale tones remain — bold and skeletal.
guitar:pitchConstraint:forbidden.etude: Set forbidden notes: third and seventh. Over the drone, play root on the fifth string, step to the second, skip the forbidden third and land on the fourth. Continue to fifth, play sixth then skip to root. Descend: high root, sixth, fifth, fourth, second, root. Play four phrases, each starting on a different available degree.
guitar:pitchConstraint:forbidden.watchOut: The classic mistake is overcorrecting by avoiding the area around the forbidden note entirely. If the third is forbidden, you can still play the second and fourth — you just cannot land on the third. Stay close to the danger zones; the tension of near-misses makes better music.

guitar:pitchConstraint:targetLanding.character: Target landing feels like every phrase is a road trip with one non-negotiable destination — you can take any route, wander through any fretboard neighborhood, but you always end up on that one target note. It is the gravitational pull you hear in blues guitar. Your fretting hand can roam, but it always knows where home is.
guitar:pitchConstraint:targetLanding.whyPractice: This builds phrase resolution — the ability to create tension and release it to a specific point. It trains your fretboard navigation to be goal-oriented: you start seeing routes TO a target note from any position.
steps: Identify your target note and find it in at least two places in your current position.
Start a phrase anywhere in the scale. Wander freely for three or four notes.
As you approach the phrase ending, steer toward your target. Slide, step, or leap to it.
Land on the target with conviction — vibrato, sustain, or a slight bend and release.
Rest for a beat or two, then launch a new phrase from a different starting point.
Experiment with approaching from above (descending) and from below (ascending).
Try landing on the target in a different octave each phrase.
progression: Beginner: Target is the root. Wander within one position and end every phrase on it.
Intermediate: Target is a non-root degree (fifth or third). Wander across two positions.
Advanced: Target is a tension note (second or seventh). Use bends, slides, or harmonics to resolve to it.
examples: Wander: fifth, sixth, fourth, third, second — slide into root (target).
Wander: root, third, fifth, seventh, sixth — land on fifth (target) with vibrato.
Wander: high root, seventh, fifth — leap down to low third (target).
Wander: second, fourth, sixth, fifth — bend the fourth up toward the third (target).
Wander across two positions: start near the nut, slide up, play three notes, slide down to land on the target root.
guitar:pitchConstraint:targetLanding.etude: Target note: the fifth. Over the drone, begin on the root in a low position. Climb: root, second, third — pause — then fourth, land on fifth with vibrato. Hold two beats. New phrase: start on the high root, descend seventh, sixth, skip to fourth, slide up into fifth (target). Third phrase: start on the third, leap to the seventh, step down sixth, fifth — arrive from above. Final phrase: play the fourth, bend it slowly toward the fifth, release, pick the fifth cleanly.
guitar:pitchConstraint:targetLanding.watchOut: The danger is making the target landing predictable by always approaching from the same direction. Vary the approach direction, the number of notes before landing, and the technique you use to arrive.

guitar:pitchConstraint:questionAnswer.character: Question and answer on guitar feels like a conversation between two sides of your fretboard — the question phrase reaches upward with unresolved tension, the answer brings it back down to earth. Think of B.B. King call-and-response with his own guitar: one phrase asks, the next replies.
guitar:pitchConstraint:questionAnswer.whyPractice: This builds phrase structure and melodic storytelling. It trains you to think in pairs — to leave space for a response and create contrasting phrase shapes. It develops your sense of tension and resolution across the fretboard.
steps: Play a question phrase: start low, move upward, end on an unresolved note (second, fourth, or sixth).
Pause for one or two beats. Let the question hang over the drone.
Play an answer phrase: start near where the question ended and descend, landing on a resolved note (root, fifth, or third).
Make the two phrases roughly the same length.
Vary the starting point of each question.
Try adding a bend at the top of the question — it emphasizes the upward reaching quality.
Make the answer phrase final note ring out with confidence.
progression: Beginner: Questions go up in one position, answers come down in the same position. Three to five notes each.
Intermediate: Questions can shift up the neck, answers shift back down. Vary length and rhythm.
Advanced: Questions use one timbral area (wound strings, lower frets), answers use another (unwound strings, higher frets).
examples: Question: root, second, third, fourth (hang). Answer: fourth, third, second, root (walk home).
Question: third, fifth, sixth, seventh (climbing, slight bend on seventh). Answer: seventh, fifth, third, root (descending firm landing).
Question: root, third, fifth, high root (leap up, sustain). Answer: high root, seventh, fifth, third, root (long cascade down).
Question: second, fourth, sixth (staccato, rising). Answer: fifth, third, root (legato, falling — mirror image).
Question: root on low string, slide up to fifth in higher position (suspended). Answer: fifth, fourth, third, second, root descending back.
guitar:pitchConstraint:questionAnswer.etude: Over the drone at a laid-back tempo. Question 1: pick root on the fifth string, hammer-on to second, pick third on the fourth string, slide up to fifth. Hold two beats. Answer 1: from fifth, step to fourth, third, second, land on root with vibrato. Rest two beats. Question 2: start on third, climb to fifth, sixth, bend into seventh. Let the bend ring. Answer 2: release bend to sixth, pull-off to fifth, step to third, resolve on root an octave lower.
guitar:pitchConstraint:questionAnswer.watchOut: The most common failure is making questions and answers that sound the same — both running up and down the scale. Questions MUST end higher than they start, answers MUST end lower. If both phrases have the same shape, you are playing two statements, not a conversation.

guitar:rhythmConstraint:river.character: River rhythm on guitar feels like a steady flowing stream of quarter notes — your pick hand falls into a hypnotic, even pulse, one note per beat, no gaps, no rushes. It is the meditative quality of a Tommy Guerrero instrumental or a Tinariwen desert groove where the guitar just flows.
guitar:rhythmConstraint:river.whyPractice: Even quarter-note flow trains your internal clock and exposes timing inconsistencies in your picking. It builds alternate picking discipline and teaches you to separate rhythm (locked) from melody (free).
steps: Set your metronome. Pick one note per beat — downstroke on every beat. No extra notes, no rests.
Focus on making every note the same duration and volume.
Move through the scale slowly, one note per beat.
Switch to alternate picking: down on beats 1 and 3, up on 2 and 4.
Add melodic interest — change direction, skip degrees — but NEVER change the rhythm.
Try the river flow on a single string, sliding between notes.
Practice over four bars without stopping or hesitating.
progression: Beginner: All downstrokes, one position, stepwise motion. Lock each pick to the metronome click.
Intermediate: Alternate picking, moving across positions with slides. Melodic choices get adventurous, rhythm stays locked.
Advanced: Mix picked notes with legato while maintaining the quarter-note pulse.
examples: Root, second, third, fifth — each on its own beat. Four notes, four beats, perfectly even.
Fifth, fourth, third, root on one string with slides, one per beat.
Root, fifth, third, root (octave higher) — steady quarters with string crossings.
Third, fourth, sixth, seventh — alternate picked, crossing strings, one note per beat.
Long descending line: high root, seventh, sixth, fifth, fourth, third, second, root — eight beats of even quarters.
guitar:rhythmConstraint:river.etude: Metronome at moderate tempo. Begin on root, fifth string. Alternate picking. Ascend: root, second, third, fourth (four beats). Continue: fifth, sixth, seventh, high root (four beats). Descend a different way: high root, sixth, fifth, third (four beats). Continue: second, root, seventh below, root (four beats). Sixteen beats of continuous, even quarter notes.
guitar:rhythmConstraint:river.watchOut: The sneaky failure mode is micro-rushes on string crossings. When your pick crosses strings, there is a natural tendency to speed up. Slow down, isolate crossings, make sure the new-string note lands exactly on the beat.

guitar:rhythmConstraint:burst.character: Burst rhythm on guitar is pure explosive energy — a fast flurry of four to six notes crammed into a beat or two, then absolute silence. It is the machine-gun lick that punk and surf guitarists fire off before dropping back to nothing.
guitar:rhythmConstraint:burst.whyPractice: Bursts train fast picking under pressure AND the discipline to STOP. Most guitarists can play fast but cannot stop cleanly. Bursts also build rhythmic contrast.
steps: Set the metronome. Play four to six notes as fast as you can within one or two beats, then rest.
Start with four-note bursts: sixteenth notes filling one beat, then three beats of silence.
Use alternate picking — down-up-down-up, fast and tight.
The silence must be REAL silence. Palm mute or lift your fretting hand to kill vibration.
Vary where in the bar the burst lands.
Make each burst a mini-phrase with direction.
Gradually increase the speed within the burst.
progression: Beginner: Four-note bursts on one string, all picked, with three beats of silence.
Intermediate: Five or six-note bursts crossing two strings, mixing picked and hammered notes.
Advanced: Rapid bursts using legato runs across three strings, landing on a target note that rings into the silence.
examples: Burst: root-second-third-fifth (four fast notes), then three beats of silence.
Burst: seventh-sixth-fifth-third-root (five-note descending cascade), silence.
Burst: root-third-root-fifth (four notes, two string crossings), silence.
Burst on top string: fifth-sixth-seventh-root-seventh (five fast notes with turnaround), cut to silence.
Syncopated burst: rest on beat 1, burst starts on the and-of-1.
guitar:rhythmConstraint:burst.etude: Bar 1: beat 1, four-note ascending burst (root-second-third-fifth), palm mute — silence for beats 2, 3, 4. Bar 2: rest beats 1-2, burst on beat 3 (high root-seventh-sixth-fifth, descending), silence beat 4. Bar 3: burst on the and-of-2 (third-fifth-sixth-fifth), silence. Bar 4: full silence. The empty bar makes the next burst hit harder.
guitar:rhythmConstraint:burst.watchOut: The dying burst — notes trail off gradually instead of cutting to silence sharply. After your last note, immediately flatten your fretting fingers to mute everything. Sound to silence like flipping a switch.

guitar:rhythmConstraint:space.character: Space is about what you do not play. At least half of every bar is silence, which means your notes become rare and precious. It is the Miles Davis approach applied to guitar: the long pauses, the way a note sits alone and means something.
guitar:rhythmConstraint:space.whyPractice: Space is the hardest constraint for guitarists because the instrument is always right there under your fingers. Learning to rest builds phrase maturity and the confidence to let the drone carry the music while you wait.
steps: Count four beats per bar. You may only play notes during two beats (or fewer).
Start by playing on beats 1 and 2, resting on 3 and 4.
Now reverse: rest on 1 and 2, play on 3 and 4.
Play a single note on beat 1, then rest for the entire remaining bar.
Vary which beats you fill across consecutive bars.
Use the silence actively: listen to the drone, feel the metronome.
When you do play, make each note count — vibrato, a bend, a slide.
progression: Beginner: Play on beats 1 and 2 only, rest on 3 and 4.
Intermediate: Vary which two beats you use each bar. Add expression to played notes.
Advanced: Play just one note per bar, placed on a different beat each time.
examples: Root on beat 1 with vibrato, rest, rest, rest.
Rest, rest, fifth on beat 3 (slide into it), third on beat 4.
Third on beat 1, rest, rest, root on beat 4. Two notes framing silence.
Rest, second on beat 2 (gentle bend), rest, rest.
Two bars: bar 1 entirely silent. Bar 2: root on beat 1, fifth on beat 2.
guitar:rhythmConstraint:space.etude: Bar 1: root on beat 1 with firm downstroke and vibrato. Rest beats 2-4. Bar 2: full silence. Bar 3: rest, rest, fifth on beat 3 with a slide from below. Bar 4: third on beat 1, let it decay, rest. Bar 5: rest, second on beat 2 with slight bend, rest, rest. Bar 6: full silence.
guitar:rhythmConstraint:space.watchOut: Nervous filling — you rest for a beat, get uncomfortable, and sneak in an extra note. Trust the space. Count out loud if needed.

guitar:rhythmConstraint:offbeat.character: Offbeat playing on guitar has that reggae skank energy — your pick hand lifts off the downbeat and lands between the clicks, on the ands. It feels syncopated and buoyant. It is the rhythmic engine of ska, reggae, and funk guitar.
guitar:rhythmConstraint:offbeat.whyPractice: Offbeats train rhythmic independence — the ability to feel the beat internally while playing against it. Essential for reggae upstrokes, funk scratches, and any syncopated rhythm part.
steps: Set the metronome. Tap your foot on every click but play ONLY between the clicks — on the and of each beat.
Start with upstrokes only — the natural motion for reggae and ska guitar.
Count out loud: 1-AND-2-AND-3-AND-4-AND. Only play on the ANDs.
Play one note per offbeat at first. Same note, four offbeats per bar.
Once solid, start changing notes — move through the scale but keep every note between beats.
Try accenting different offbeats: the and-of-2 and and-of-4 louder.
Practice stopping on an offbeat — three offbeats, leave the fourth empty.
progression: Beginner: One note per offbeat, all upstrokes, one position.
Intermediate: Vary notes and use both up and downstrokes on offbeats. Leave some offbeats empty.
Advanced: Mix offbeat single notes with offbeat chord stabs. Add ghost notes on the downbeats.
examples: All four offbeats: (rest)-root-(rest)-third-(rest)-fifth-(rest)-root.
Selective offbeats: only and-of-2 and and-of-4. Classic reggae.
Offbeat scale run: (rest)-root-(rest)-second-(rest)-third-(rest)-fourth.
Ghost note groove: muted scratch on downbeats, melodic notes on offbeats.
Offbeat with space: only two offbeats filled, the rest empty.
guitar:rhythmConstraint:offbeat.etude: Bar 1: mute strings and scratch on beats 1-4. Add: root on and-of-2 with upstroke, fifth on and-of-4 with upstroke. Bar 2: same scratches, third on and-of-1, root on and-of-3. Bar 3: drop scratches. Play only on offbeats: root, third, fifth, third. Bar 4: pick just one offbeat — and-of-4 — play root and let it ring.
guitar:rhythmConstraint:offbeat.watchOut: Drifting onto the downbeat — you start on the offbeat but gradually creep forward until you play right on the click. Keep your foot tapping on the clicks and deliberately delay your pick stroke.

guitar:rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed.character: Rhythmic seed on guitar is like a groove riff burned into your picking hand — a short rhythmic pattern that repeats over and over while your fretting hand changes the notes underneath. It is the foundation of riff-based guitar playing: think Nile Rodgers funk or surf-rock tremolo patterns.
guitar:rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed.whyPractice: This separates your rhythm hand from your pitch hand — a critical guitar skill. Most players let picking rhythm follow note choices, which makes playing shapeless. By locking in a rhythmic cell and varying only notes, you build groove consistency.
steps: Choose a short rhythmic cell — three or four beats.
Play this cell on a single note first. Repeat four times. Lock the rhythm into your picking hand.
Now keep the exact same picking pattern but change the fretting hand notes.
Try the cell starting on different beats of the bar.
Vary only the pitches: ascend, descend, leap — but the rhythmic cell never changes.
After eight to twelve repetitions, introduce one small rhythmic variation, then return.
Try the cell with different articulations: all downstrokes, alternate picking, palm-muted.
progression: Beginner: A simple two-beat cell repeated with different scale notes in one position.
Intermediate: A three-beat or syncopated cell across two positions.
Advanced: A complex cell with ties or rests, applied across the full fretboard.
examples: Cell: eighth-eighth-quarter-rest. Apply: root-second-third (rest), fifth-sixth-seventh (rest).
Cell: dotted quarter-eighth-half. A lilting long-short-long groove.
Cell: sixteenth-sixteenth-eighth-quarter rest. Quick ba-da-BUM (rest).
Cell: quarter-eighth rest-eighth-quarter. A groove with a gap in the middle.
Cell: triplet-quarter-rest. Three fast notes then one sustained.
guitar:rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed.etude: Cell: eighth-eighth-quarter-half rest. Play four times: root-second-third, rest. Third-fifth-sixth, rest. Sixth-fifth-third, rest (descending). Fifth-third-root, rest. Same rhythm every time. Then play eight more rounds freely but keep the da-da-DAH-rest cell intact.
guitar:rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed.watchOut: Letting the rhythmic cell morph without noticing. Record yourself: does the cell sound identical on repetition 1 and repetition 8? The rhythm is a CONSTANT.

guitar:rhythmConstraint:triplets.character: Triplets on guitar have a rolling, galloping energy — three notes per beat creating a da-da-da pulse that is somewhere between straight and swung. It is the rhythmic engine behind shuffle blues, Irish jig-style picking, and rolling Dire Straits fingerpicked passages.
guitar:rhythmConstraint:triplets.whyPractice: Triplets train a subdivision most guitarists avoid. Mastering them unlocks shuffle feel, 6/8 grooves, and the ability to switch between straight and swing at will. It also develops grouping notes in threes across strings — fundamental for sweep arpeggios.
steps: Set metronome to a slow tempo. Count trip-let-trip-let (three syllables per beat).
Start on a single note. Pick in triplets: down-up-down (beat 1), up-down-up (beat 2).
Add notes: one scale degree per triplet group.
Next: change the note on every stroke — three different notes per beat.
Practice triplets on adjacent strings: one note per string, rolling across three strings per beat.
Accent the first note of each triplet group.
Once comfortable, mix triplet beats with straight quarter-note beats.
progression: Beginner: Same note triplets, all on one string. Focus on even spacing.
Intermediate: Changing notes within each triplet, crossing two strings per beat.
Advanced: Rolling triplets across three strings, mixed with legato triplets for speed.
examples: Single-note triplets: root-root-root, second-second-second, third-third-third. One pitch per beat, three attacks.
Scalar triplets: root-second-third (beat 1), fourth-fifth-sixth (beat 2), seventh-root-seventh (beat 3).
Rolling triplets across strings: root (sixth string), fifth (fifth string), root (fourth string) — three per beat.
Triplets with repeated tones: root-third-root, root-fifth-root, root-sixth-root.
Legato triplets on one string: pick-hammer-pull — three notes, one beat.
guitar:rhythmConstraint:triplets.etude: Bar 1: root-root-root, second-second-second, third-third-third, fifth-fifth-fifth — triplets, one note per beat repeated three times. Bar 2: root-second-third, fifth-third-second, root-second-third, fifth-sixth-seventh — scalar triplets. Bar 3: rolling triplets across strings, shifting starting note each beat. Bar 4: just the first note of each triplet, rest for the other two — quarter-note feel echoing the triplet groove.
guitar:rhythmConstraint:triplets.watchOut: Letting triplets morph into straight sixteenth notes or a dotted-eighth swing. True triplets are three EVEN notes per beat. Record yourself: groups of three or groups of four?

guitar:dynamics:swell.character: A swell on guitar feels like turning a volume knob from zero to ten and back — a slow bloom of sound rising from the faintest whisper of the pick barely grazing the string to a full, ringing attack that fills the room. The right hand controls everything: the distance of the pick from the bridge, the angle of attack, the pressure. The cut to silence at the end is the whole trick — damp the strings with your palm and let the room ring.
guitar:dynamics:swell.whyPractice: Most guitarists have one default picking intensity and never leave it. The swell builds TRUE dynamic range in your picking hand — the ability to go from near-inaudible to commanding and back in a controlled arc. Without this skill, your solos and improvisations flatline at one volume level, and you lose one of the guitar's most expressive tools.
steps: Start with your pick barely touching the string — so light that only a ghostly harmonic comes through. This is your floor.
Grow the picking intensity smoothly over 4 bars. Each note should be slightly louder than the last — think dimmer knob, not light switch.
As you get louder, move your picking position closer to the bridge — this adds brightness and bite to the crescendo naturally.
Aim the crest at the highest or most exposed note in the phrase — the fifth or the peak of the arc.
At the crest, stop cleanly — palm-mute the strings on a beat. Silence is the back half of the gesture.
Listen to the room after you stop. That decay is part of the musical statement.
Repeat and make the starting volume EVEN quieter — use less pick, more angle, brush the string with the edge.
progression: Start by swelling a single repeated root note over 4 bars — pick pressure only, same fret, same string. Master the volume contour before adding pitch movement.
Swell across a scale ascent — root, second, third, fourth, fifth — each note picked slightly harder, cresting on the fifth with a full ring-out.
Reverse swell: start with a hard downstroke on the root, decay to barely audible by the fifth, then snap back to a forte accent note and palm-mute to silence.
examples: Root on the low string, repeated 8 times with gradually increasing pick attack, palm-mute on the last beat.
Root → third → fifth ascending on one string, each note picked harder, fifth rings out at full volume, then cut.
Scale run from root up to fifth and back — swell up on the ascent, crest at the top, sustain the descent at forte, palm-mute on the final root.
Root → fifth leap on adjacent strings — start the root at ghost-note quiet, land the fifth with full pick attack, hold 2 beats, damp.
Two-bar arc: bar 1 barely audible on root and second, bar 2 crescendo through third and fourth, crest on fifth, cut.
guitar:dynamics:swell.etude: Bars 1-2: root, second, third, fourth as quarter notes picked with the lightest possible touch — the pick should barely engage the string, each note fractionally harder than the last. Bars 3-4: fifth (held 2 beats at full pick attack — this is the crest, let it ring), fourth, third, second as quarter notes continuing at forte. Beat 1 of bar 5: root, palm-mute immediately to silence. Bar 5 beats 2-4: rest and listen to the room. Repeat, pushing the opening quieter each pass.
guitar:dynamics:swell.watchOut: The most common failure is starting too loud. If your opening note is at a comfortable picking volume instead of near-silence, you have nowhere to grow and the swell becomes a mild bump. The fix is to start with the pick at such a shallow angle that you genuinely wonder if the note came out — trust that the crescendo will build from there. The second failure is a gradual decay instead of a clean cut at the end: use your palm to kill all string vibration decisively.

guitar:dynamics:terraces.character: Terraces on guitar feel like stepping between three completely different rooms — a whisper room, a conversation room, and a concert hall. Each level is locked in and flat; the edges between them are hard switches in your picking hand. There is no ramp. The effect is architectural.
guitar:dynamics:terraces.whyPractice: This constraint breaks the habit of unconsciously picking harder as you climb the fretboard or softer as a phrase dies. It builds dynamic MEMORY in the picking hand — the ability to return to an exact attack intensity after leaving it.
steps: Before you start, physically define three picking intensities — light brush, medium stroke, full attack — and test each on the root.
Play the first 2 bars at light brush intensity. Lock it. Do not drift louder even as the melody rises.
On the downbeat of bar 3, switch HARD to medium stroke. The jump must be audible and deliberate, not a fade.
Hold medium stroke intensity for its full duration.
On the next terrace change, switch to full attack (or back down to light brush).
Listen specifically for the 'click' at each edge — if you hear any smoothing, you have crescendoed unconsciously.
Between rounds, change the order: ascending one round, then scrambled the next.
progression: Two terraces only — light picking for 2 bars, full attack for 2 bars. Master the hard switch.
Three terraces ascending — light, medium, full — over 6 bars.
Non-ascending terraces — medium, light, full, light in a jagged pattern.
examples: 2 bars light brush on root and second, 2 bars full attack on third and fourth, 2 bars light brush on fifth.
Light root, medium third, forte fifth — one note per bar, three hard dynamic levels.
Alternating light and full attack every bar on a rocking root-third pattern.
Four terraces across 8 bars: pp root, mf third, ff fifth sustained, p fourth-third-second-root.
Forte root up one octave, light fifth, medium third, light root — scrambled dynamics.
guitar:dynamics:terraces.etude: Bar 1-2: root, second as half notes at light brush picking. Bar 3-4: third, fourth as half notes at medium stroke (hard switch). Bar 5: fifth as whole note at full attack (hard switch). Bar 6: fifth to fourth at full attack. Bar 7-8: third, second, root at light brush (subito drop). Zero drift between terraces.
guitar:dynamics:terraces.watchOut: The near-universal failure is letting the light-picking section creep louder as the phrase progresses. The fix is to CONSCIOUSLY back off at the end of each terrace — aim to end the light section even softer than you started it.

guitar:dynamics:whisper.character: Whisper guitar is the sound of a conversation you have to lean in to hear — each note barely there, hanging in the air like smoke. The paradox is that it demands MORE control from both hands, not less: the pick must be precise, the fretting hand clean, every note centered, because at this volume any buzz or rattle is exposed.
guitar:dynamics:whisper.whyPractice: Whisper-quiet playing is the deepest picking-hand control exercise. When volume drops, any sloppiness in pick angle, fret buzz, or muted strings becomes instantly audible — so you are forced to refine your pick grip, attack angle, and fretting-hand pressure simultaneously.
steps: Sit in a quiet room — whisper dynamics get lost in ambient noise.
Hold the pick with a very light grip — the pick should almost float.
Start the first note with the pick barely engaging the string.
Keep your fretting hand precise — at this volume, fret buzz is magnified.
Put your attention on tone clarity — aim for a clean, bell-like tone on each note.
Listen for any scratchiness from the pick. If you hear it, angle the pick and lighten the attack.
If a note dies out, you have pressed too lightly with the fretting hand.
progression: Sustained root at the quietest pick attack you can manage, 4 beats, clean tone.
Root → third → fifth → third → root at whisper volume, listening for pitch clarity on string crossings.
Full round at whisper dynamics climbing to the highest position and returning.
examples: Root sustained at the softest pick attack, clean tone, 4 beats. Repeat 3 times.
Root → third → fifth → third → root, all whispered, each note clean.
Scale ascent root to fifth at whisper, careful on string crossings.
Two-bar phrase whispered arc with the fifth as the quiet peak.
Whisper a repeated root over the drone — test pick attack consistency.
guitar:dynamics:whisper.etude: Bars 1-2: root sustained for 8 beats at the absolute quietest pick attack with clean tone. Bars 3-4: root, second, third, fourth as quarter notes, still whispered. Bars 5-6: fifth sustained for 8 beats. Bars 7-8: fifth, fourth, third, second, root — descending and still whispered.
guitar:dynamics:whisper.watchOut: Guitarists confuse quiet playing with careless playing and let the fretting hand get sloppy — producing fret buzz and finger squeaks hidden at normal volume. The fix is to treat whisper volume as a magnifying glass on your technique.

guitar:dynamics:accentMap.character: Accent mapping on guitar feels like a spotlight moving across a dim stage — your picking hand moves quietly through the line, then one note per bar gets a sharp, bright downstroke that cuts through. The accented note jumps out like a word in bold.
guitar:dynamics:accentMap.whyPractice: This is the fundamental funk, blues, and improvisation skill of weighting notes unequally with your picking hand. Most beginners pick every note with identical force, which turns a melody into a list. Accent mapping builds the muscle to DECIDE which single note carries the meaning.
steps: Before you start, decide WHICH note in each bar gets the accent.
Play the quiet surrounding notes with a light, angled pick stroke.
When the accent note arrives, dig in with a firm downstroke from the wrist.
The accent should be 2-3 dynamic levels louder than the surroundings.
Immediately AFTER the accent, return to the light pick attack.
The rhythm of the accents becomes its own inner pulse.
Experiment with moving the accent off beat 1.
progression: Accent only on beat 1 of every bar — the strong beat.
Accent on the highest scale degree in each bar, wherever it falls rhythmically.
Accent on the 'and' of beat 2 — off-beat accents create funk or reggae feel.
examples: Quiet root-second-third-fourth with the THIRD accented by a sharp downstroke.
Quiet fifth sustained for 3 beats, accent on the sixth on beat 4.
Four-bar phrase where the accent WALKS across the meter.
Quiet descending scale with an accent on the second scale degree.
Quiet root-third-fifth-third arpeggio with the accent on the fifth.
guitar:dynamics:accentMap.etude: Bar 1: root, second, third, fourth — accent HARD on the third (beat 3). Bar 2: fifth, fourth, third, second — accent HARD on the fifth (beat 1). Bar 3: third, fourth, fifth, fourth — accent HARD on the fourth (beat 2). Bar 4: third, second, root, root — accent on the final root (beat 3).
guitar:dynamics:accentMap.watchOut: The quiet notes drift louder so the accent loses contrast. The fix is to CONSCIOUSLY under-pick the unaccented notes. Also watch for tensing the whole arm — the dig-in should come from a wrist snap.

guitar:dynamics:forte.character: Constant forte on guitar feels like filling the room with every note — each pick stroke committed, each string ringing at full resonance. The body stays loose, the wrist drives from a relaxed position, and the tone is big but not harsh.
guitar:dynamics:forte.whyPractice: Most guitarists either cannot find real forte or produce it by strangling the pick and tensing the arm. This constraint builds SUSTAINABLE loud playing — wrist-driven attack, resonance awareness, and the confidence to take up sonic space.
steps: Check your pick grip — firm but not death-grip. Forte comes from wrist speed, not squeeze pressure.
Start the first note at full volume immediately — no slow attack.
Keep the wrist RELAXED and fluid.
Listen to the guitar's resonance.
Keep the volume LOCKED across the whole phrase.
On higher frets and thinner strings, compensate consciously.
Breathe. Guitarists hold their breath at forte and tense up.
progression: Sustained root for 4 beats at forte — full pick attack, relaxed grip.
Full scale ascent root to fifth at constant forte, attention to string crossings.
Extended phrase with a leap at constant forte — the string crossing at the leap is the test.
examples: Sustained root for 4 beats at forte — no wavering.
Root, third, fifth arpeggio at constant forte — no volume bump or dip.
Scale ascent root to fifth with held fifth, then descent — every note equally present.
Leap from root to fifth, both at forte, identical volume.
Full-round arc, every note loud, every note ringing.
guitar:dynamics:forte.etude: Bar 1: root held 4 beats at full attack. Bar 2: root, third, fifth, third at identical forte. Bar 3: fifth held 4 beats. Bar 4: fifth, fourth, third, second at forte. Bar 5: root one octave up held 2 beats, then descend. Bar 6: second, root, rest — palm-mute cleanly.
guitar:dynamics:forte.watchOut: Guitarists confuse forte with tension and start death-gripping the pick. The tell is harsh tone and tired forearm. Forte comes from wrist speed against a relaxed hand, not brute force.

guitar:articulation:legato.character: Legato on guitar is the sound of notes melting into each other — hammer-ons, pull-offs, and slides replacing the pick so that the melody flows like water across the fretboard. Each note dissolves into the next with no gap, no re-attack, no percussive click from the pick.
guitar:articulation:legato.whyPractice: Legato forces your fretting hand to generate volume and sustain on its own — hammer-ons must be firm, pull-offs must snap, slides must maintain energy. Without legato command, you are tethered to the pick for every note.
steps: Pick the first note of each phrase, then use ONLY hammer-ons, pull-offs, and slides for the rest.
Hammer firmly right behind the fret wire.
Pull off with a slight sideways snap of the finger.
For slides, maintain even fretting pressure throughout.
Listen for any gap between notes — zero dead space.
On ascending runs, chain hammer-ons. On descending, chain pull-offs.
End each phrase with a slow slide or sustained note.
progression: Two-note legato — hammer from root to second and pull back. Master the connection.
Full scale ascent using only one initial pick and all hammer-ons.
Large-interval legato — slide from root to fifth, hammer to sixth, pull off, slide back.
examples: Root → second → third → fourth → fifth ascending, pick only the root, hammer everything else.
Root → fifth via slide, then descend using pull-offs only.
Undulating line: root, third, second, fourth, third, fifth — pick root, hammer and pull the rest.
Sustained root, slide into third, slide into fifth — true legato with no re-articulation.
Descending scale from fifth to root using pull-offs on every note.
guitar:articulation:legato.etude: Bar 1: pick root, hammer second (2 beats), hammer third (2 beats). Bar 2: hammer fourth (2 beats), slide to fifth (2 beats). Bar 3: fifth sustained 4 beats. Bar 4: pull off fifth to fourth, third, second, root as quarter notes. One initial pick stroke for the entire etude.
guitar:articulation:legato.watchOut: Weak hammer-ons that produce ghost notes instead of full-volume tones. The fix is to hammer with conviction, landing right behind the fret.

guitar:articulation:staccato.character: Staccato on guitar feels like bouncing a ball — each note has a sharp attack and an immediate cutoff, with clear silence in between. The picking hand strikes and the fretting hand lifts almost simultaneously. Bright, percussive, rhythmically precise.
guitar:articulation:staccato.whyPractice: Staccato trains right-hand and left-hand coordination — the ability to start and stop notes cleanly on command. Essential for funk rhythms, chicken-picking, and tight muted grooves.
steps: Pick each note with a crisp downstroke.
Immediately lift your fretting finger to kill the sustain.
Alternatively, use a light palm mute from the picking hand.
The silence between notes is part of the music.
Keep the pitch CENTERED — staccato does not mean sloppy.
Use fretting-hand lift for single lines, palm-mute for chords.
Keep the overall line musical — crisp words in a sentence.
progression: Staccato quarter notes on a single pitch, crisp and even.
Staccato scale ascent, one note per beat, each cut short.
Staccato eighth notes — faster, tighter coordination.
examples: Root repeated 8 times as bounced quarter notes.
Root, third, fifth, third arpeggio — each picked and immediately damped.
Scale from root to fifth, one bounced note per beat.
Quick root-second-root-second staccato pattern.
Descending staccato from fifth to root in eighth notes.
guitar:articulation:staccato.etude: Bar 1: root, root, root, root as staccato quarters. Bar 2: root, third, fifth, third bounced. Bar 3: fifth, fourth, third, second bounced. Bar 4: root, root, rest, root — two bounced, silence, one final bounce.
guitar:articulation:staccato.watchOut: Using too much palm mute turns staccato into a muffled thud with no pitch. Let the note ring for a split second with full tone BEFORE cutting it off.

guitar:articulation:slurredPairs.character: Slurred pairs on guitar have a lilting, rocking quality — each pair is a hammer-on or pull-off: two notes connected as one smooth gesture, with a brief silence before the next pair. It feels like a rhythmic pulse that turns a scale into a dance.
guitar:articulation:slurredPairs.whyPractice: This drills the musical unit of TWO through the hammer-on/pull-off pair — the foundation of all guitar ornamentation. It breaks the habit of picking every single note with equal weight.
steps: Group notes mentally into pairs before you start.
Pick the first note of each pair.
Hammer-on (ascending) or pull-off (descending) to the second note.
After the second note, lift briefly to create a tiny silence before the next pair.
Feel each two-note unit as one gesture.
Keep the lilted, rocking feel consistent.
If pairs blur into continuous legato, you have lost the gaps.
progression: Repeating root-second pairs on one string.
Ascending scale in pairs — pick root hammer second, pick third hammer fourth.
Wider-interval pairs — pick root slide to third, pick third slide to fifth.
examples: Root-second pairs, rocking on one string.
Root-third, third-fifth, fifth-third, third-root — arpeggio in pairs.
Ascending scale as pairs: root-second, third-fourth, fifth-sixth.
Descending: upper root-seventh, sixth-fifth, fourth-third, second-root.
Mixed pairs with different techniques each time.
guitar:articulation:slurredPairs.etude: Bar 1: pick root → hammer second, gap, pick third → hammer fourth. Bar 2: pick fifth → pull-off fourth, gap, pick third → pull-off second. Bar 3: pick root → slide to third, gap, pick third → hammer fifth. Bar 4: pick fifth → pull-off third, gap, pick second → pull-off root.
guitar:articulation:slurredPairs.watchOut: Losing the slur (both notes get picked) or losing the gap (continuous legato). You need BOTH — the pair connected AND the space between pairs preserved.

guitar:articulation:tenuto.character: Tenuto on guitar feels like pressing each note into the fretboard and holding it there — letting every note ring for its absolute full value. There is a stately, deliberate quality, like each note deserves its complete moment.
guitar:articulation:tenuto.whyPractice: Tenuto breaks the common habit of lifting the fretting finger too early. It trains HOLDING a note until the mathematically correct moment — the foundation of rhythmic integrity and sustain control.
steps: Mentally divide each beat into subdivisions.
Pick with a small initial emphasis — a gentle lean.
Hold the fretting finger DOWN for the note's COMPLETE value.
At the exact moment the note should end, pick and fret the next note.
Each note gets that same small pick-attack lean.
Listen for premature string death on weak beats.
On string crossings, keep the previous string ringing until the exact moment you pick the new string.
progression: Whole notes at tenuto — root held for 4 full beats, then second for 4.
Half notes — scale ascent with each note held its full two beats.
Quarter notes — faster, requires accurate subdivision.
examples: Root held for 4 full beats with gentle pick lean.
Scale from root to fifth in half notes, each held full duration.
Quarter note arpeggio: root, third, fifth, third — each held exactly one beat.
Root (2 beats), second (2 beats), third (4 beats) — every note to its boundary.
Dotted rhythm: root 1.5 beats, third 0.5 beats, fifth 1.5 beats, third 0.5 beats.
guitar:articulation:tenuto.etude: Bar 1: root as half note (full 2 beats), third as half note (full 2 beats). Bar 2: fifth as whole note (all 4 beats). Bar 3: fifth half note, fourth half note. Bar 4: third quarter, second quarter, root half note held to bar line.
guitar:articulation:tenuto.watchOut: Lifting the fretting finger a sixteenth note early, especially before string crossings. Count subdivisions and keep the finger pressed until the exact moment the next note arrives.

guitar:phraseLength:1bar.character: One-bar phrases on guitar feel like short declarative statements — play one idea, damp the strings, let the silence land. Nothing extra, no noodling, one fretboard idea at a time.
guitar:phraseLength:1bar.whyPractice: Short phrases teach you to COMMIT to a single fretboard idea and let it breathe. If you cannot make a one-bar phrase musical on its own, you are relying on length to carry meaning.
steps: Plan the fragment before you pick.
Play one bar of musical idea — a complete small gesture.
STOP. Damp the strings.
During the silence, listen to the room.
Take a mental breath and play another one-bar idea.
Feel each fragment as a complete sentence.
Resist connecting fragments into longer runs.
progression: Identical fragment repeated — master the rest before varying content.
Two alternating fragments — call and response.
Every fragment is different — improvise a new complete idea each bar.
examples: Root, second, third, second — damp. Rest. Repeat.
Root held 2 beats, hammer to third held 2 beats — damp.
Quick figure: root, third, fifth, third in eighths then palm-mute.
Single sustained fifth for 4 beats — damp. One note IS the thought.
Syncopated fragment with rests inside the bar.
guitar:phraseLength:1bar.etude: Bar 1: root, second, third, second as quarters. Bar 2: full rest. Bar 3: third, fourth, fifth, fourth. Bar 4: full rest. Bar 5: fifth, sixth, fifth, third. Bar 6: full rest. Bar 7: root, third, fifth, root. Bar 8: full rest.
guitar:phraseLength:1bar.watchOut: Running fragments together — either starting too early or letting the last note ring through the rest undamped.

guitar:phraseLength:2bar.character: Two-bar phrases feel like complete sentences — a setup and a payoff. Enough room to develop a gesture: a question in bar 1, an answer in bar 2. The natural breath length for most guitar melodies.
guitar:phraseLength:2bar.whyPractice: Two bars is the most common phrase length in Western song. It teaches you to THINK in sentences — pacing on the fretboard, knowing when to climb and when to resolve.
steps: Plan a two-bar arc — where does bar 1 go, how does bar 2 complete it?
Play bar 1 as the SETUP.
Play bar 2 as the ANSWER.
Let the two bars feel like a SINGLE GESTURE.
Land the final note with intention.
Rest briefly between phrases.
Shape the arc: rise toward bar 1 end, peak in bar 2, resolve.
progression: Symmetric — bar 1 ascends root to fifth, bar 2 descends back.
Call-and-answer — bar 1 rising question, bar 2 falling answer.
Asymmetric weight — bar 1 a held note, bar 2 active melodic line.
examples: Bar 1: root, second, third, fourth. Bar 2: fifth, fourth, third, root.
Bar 1: root held 4 beats. Bar 2: root, third, fifth, third.
Bar 1: fifth, fourth, third, second. Bar 2: third, second, root, root.
Bar 1: root, fifth, third, fifth. Bar 2: fourth, third, second, root.
Bar 1: floating third, fifth, fourth, sixth. Bar 2: fifth, third, second, root.
guitar:phraseLength:2bar.etude: Bar 1: root, second, third, fourth ascending. Bar 2: fifth as half note (peak), fourth, third as quarters (descending). Rest 2 beats. Bar 3: third, fourth, fifth, sixth. Bar 4: upper root as half note, fifth, third as quarters, root on last beat.
guitar:phraseLength:2bar.watchOut: Treating the two bars as separate fragments instead of one connected gesture. Plan your fretboard path to flow smoothly across the bar line.

guitar:phraseLength:4bar.character: Four-bar phrases feel like paragraphs — room for a full narrative arc across the fretboard. Bars 1-2 set the scene, bar 3 develops or peaks, bar 4 resolves. The length at which improvisation starts to feel composed.
guitar:phraseLength:4bar.whyPractice: Four bars trains LONG-FORM melodic shaping — the ability to plan a rise, peak, and fall across a bigger fretboard canvas. It builds stamina to maintain a musical idea over 16 beats.
steps: Plan the arc: where is the peak? Where does it resolve?
Bars 1-2 as SETUP — rising toward tension.
Bar 3 as PEAK — melodic high point.
Bar 4 as RESOLUTION — descend toward home.
Pace so bar 4 still has direction.
Feel all 16 beats as ONE thought.
Mix techniques — picked, hammered, slid — to keep it varied.
progression: Symmetric arch — bars 1-2 ascend, bar 3 peaks, bar 4 descends.
Delayed peak — bars 1-2 circle low, bar 3 leaps, bar 4 resolves.
Early peak with long decay — bar 1 leaps, bars 2-3 descend, bar 4 lands.
examples: Bars 1-2: scale ascent. Bar 3: fifth and sixth as half notes. Bar 4: fifth, third, second, root.
Bar 1: root held. Bar 2: developing. Bar 3: peak near upper root. Bar 4: resolution via pull-offs.
Bars 1-2: oscillation root-third. Bar 3: slide to fifth, hold. Bar 4: descent.
Bar 1: opening declaration. Bars 2-3: developing. Bar 4: return to root.
Bars 1-2: legato rise. Bar 3: upper root with bend. Bar 4: stepwise descent.
guitar:phraseLength:4bar.etude: Bar 1: root, second, third, fourth ascending. Bar 2: fifth, fourth, fifth, sixth continuing. Bar 3: upper root as half note with vibrato, seventh as half note (peak). Bar 4: sixth, fifth, third, root resolving.
guitar:phraseLength:4bar.watchOut: Peaking too early (in bar 2) or never peaking at all. CONSCIOUSLY plan which bar is the peak before you start.

guitar:phraseLength:8bar.character: Eight-bar phrases feel like telling a full short story across the fretboard — exposition, rising action, climax, falling action, conclusion. The fretting hand covers real distance, and the ear holds the opening idea while following the arc to resolution.
guitar:phraseLength:8bar.whyPractice: Eight bars is the ultimate test of long-form phrasing, fretboard navigation, and musical memory on guitar. It teaches you to develop ideas rather than just state them.
steps: Plan the shape: opening, development, climax, resolution. Peak usually bar 5 or 6.
Bars 1-2 as OPENING STATEMENT.
Bars 3-4 as DEVELOPMENT — vary the opening, explore adjacent positions.
Bars 5-6 as CLIMAX — highest position, most intense.
Bars 7-8 as RESOLUTION — descend, simplify, land on root.
Pace your energy — save something for the peak.
Use variety of techniques across all 8 bars.
Feel the entire 8-bar span as ONE gesture.
progression: Classic AABA shape — motif, repeat with variation, contrasting climax, return and resolve.
Continuous arc — single rising line to peak, held, rapid descent.
Blues-form 8-bar with root, fifth, and turnaround areas.
examples: Bars 1-2: opening motif. 3-4: repeat ending on fifth. 5-6: climax with vibrato. 7-8: resolution descent.
Bars 1-4: slowly climbing. 5-6: peak held. 7-8: rapid descent.
Bars 1-2: simple opening. 3-4: development. 5-6: climax. 7-8: descent home.
Bars 1-2: question. 3-4: parallel question higher. 5-6: answer from top. 7-8: final resolution.
Bars 1-4: avoiding root. 5-6: climax release to upper root. 7-8: long stepwise homecoming.
guitar:phraseLength:8bar.etude: Bar 1: root, second, third, fourth (opening). Bar 2: fifth, fourth, third, second (answering). Bar 3: root, third, fifth, third (developing). Bar 4: fifth, sixth, fifth, fourth (reaching higher). Bar 5: sixth, upper root, seventh, upper root (CLIMAX). Bar 6: upper root held 2 beats, seventh held 2 beats. Bar 7: sixth, fifth, fourth, third (descent). Bar 8: second, root, root, rest (landing).
guitar:phraseLength:8bar.watchOut: Running out of ideas by bar 5. The fix is to SAVE something — play the opening at moderate intensity so the climax has somewhere to grow.

pitchConstraint:leaps_rhythmConstraint:r.interaction: Leaps want drama, the river wants calm — when you lock big skips to an even quarter-note flow, you get a steady parade of surprises. The pulse becomes a neutral grid that makes every jump feel deliberate instead of wild, because the listener can count on exactly when the next leap will land.
steps: Set a calm tempo and feel the quarter pulse in your body before playing.
On beat 1 pick a low note, then on beat 2 leap to something at least a third away.
Keep one note per beat with no rushing and no dragging, letting only the pitch do the surprising.
Every few beats, aim a leap through the root or fifth so the ear has an anchor.
Resist the urge to fill in a step between leaps when your hand wants to settle.
examples: root → fifth → third → seventh → fourth → root, one note per beat
third → root up an octave → second → sixth → fourth → root, even quarters throughout
pitchConstraint:leaps_rhythmConstraint:r.watchOut: The river will seduce you into stepwise motion to keep things smooth — the instant you sneak a neighbor-note in, the leap rule is broken. Watch for tiny scalar fill-ins on beats you were unsure about.

pitchConstraint:leaps_rhythmConstraint:b.interaction: Leaps and bursts both thrive on surprise, so together they create tiny explosions of jagged intervals followed by held silence. The fast burst lets you fire off four or five big skips in a second, and then the silence gives the ear time to taste what just happened before the next eruption.
steps: Imagine a starting note low in the palette and a few target peaks to jump between.
On the downbeat, fire 4-6 quick notes with every pair at least a skip apart.
Let the burst settle onto the root or fifth as its resting point.
Hold the silence for 2-3 full beats and actually listen to the room.
When the next burst arrives, start it from a different register so each eruption has its own shape.
examples: root → fifth → third → seventh → root (burst) ... rest ... sixth → second → fourth → root (burst)
third → root up an octave → fifth → second → sixth (burst) ... rest ... fourth → seventh → third → root (burst)
pitchConstraint:leaps_rhythmConstraint:b.watchOut: Under speed pressure your fingers will default to scale runs — every burst must stay jagged, never smooth. If two consecutive notes inside a burst are neighbors, the burst is a failed leap.
pitchConstraint:leaps_rhythmConstraint:b.deeperInsight: The silence after a leap-burst is where the listener's brain replays the intervals — so the rests are not empty, they are the reverb of the skips.

pitchConstraint:leaps_rhythmConstraint:s.interaction: This is the sparse drama pairing. A single leap, then a long rest, then another single leap — the silence amplifies every interval because there's nothing else to distract from it. You're not playing phrases, you're placing little sculptures of two or three jumped notes and letting them ring.
steps: Choose a slow inner pulse and commit that at least half of every bar will be rest.
Drop one note, leap to a second note a skip or further away, then stop.
Count the silence in real beats — do not rush in with a third note.
When you do play again, start from a different part of the register so the new sculpture contrasts.
Every third or fourth entry, let the leap land on the root or fifth to give the ear an anchor.
examples: root ... fifth ... (long rest) ... third ... seventh ... (long rest) ... root
low second ... sixth ... (rest) ... fourth ... root up an octave ... (rest) ... third
pitchConstraint:leaps_rhythmConstraint:s.watchOut: The temptation under space is to fill the silence with more notes to feel productive — that collapses both constraints at once. If you catch yourself playing more than half the bar, you're not leaping sparsely, you're just leaping.

pitchConstraint:leaps_rhythmConstraint:o.interaction: Two destabilizing forces stacked: the beat is nowhere to be found and neither is the nearest note. Every attack lands in a rhythmic crack and every pitch lands a skip or more from the last, so the whole line feels like it's floating sideways. You have to hold the pulse entirely in your head because nothing you play reinforces it.
steps: Tap the quarter pulse silently in your foot and do not let your hand play on those beats.
Place the first attack on the and of one, leaping from wherever you imagined the root.
Every subsequent attack also lands between beats, each a skip or more from the previous.
Anchor every third or fourth leap on the root or fifth so the ear has something to grab.
If you lose the pulse, stop and reset with the foot before re-entering.
examples: (rest) root → (rest) fifth → (rest) third → (rest) seventh, all on the ands
(rest) second → (rest) sixth → (rest) fourth → (rest) root up an octave
pitchConstraint:leaps_rhythmConstraint:o.watchOut: When the leaps get tricky your hand will want to land ON a beat to stabilize — the first on-the-beat note kills the offbeat rule. Keep the foot tapping to catch this.

pitchConstraint:leaps_rhythmConstraint:r.interaction: A fixed little rhythmic stamp repeats while the pitches inside it keep jumping all over the place. This pairing is wonderful because the rhythm becomes familiar almost instantly, which makes the ear focus entirely on the wild melodic leaping happening inside the cell.
steps: Invent a 1-bar rhythmic cell — say, two sixteenths and an eighth, or a dotted quarter plus two eighths.
Play the cell with all leaps — every note inside the cell is a skip from the last.
Repeat the exact rhythm and re-seed the pitches with completely new leaps.
Make sure each cell starts or ends near the root or fifth as an anchor.
After four or five repetitions, let the pitches start to rhyme across cells.
examples: (cell 1) root → fifth → third ... (cell 2 same rhythm) second → sixth → fourth ... (cell 3) seventh → third → root up an octave
(cell 1) fourth → root → sixth ... (cell 2) third → seventh → second ... (cell 3) fifth → root → fourth
pitchConstraint:leaps_rhythmConstraint:r.watchOut: You will be tempted to let the rhythm drift when the leaps get scary — the cell must stay identical in duration and accent from bar to bar. If the rhythm changes, only the pitch constraint is active.

pitchConstraint:leaps_rhythmConstraint:t.interaction: Big intervals in a rolling three-feel create a galloping, almost folkloric dance where every triplet is a little hop from one skip to another. The triplet subdivision gives your hand momentum that wants to run in scales, and the leap rule constantly pulls it back out into the open air.
steps: Lock into a slow-to-moderate tempo and feel the triplet pulse as three even notes per beat.
On beat 1, play three notes that are all leaps from each other — never neighbors.
On beat 2, do the same with three new leapt notes.
Every other beat let the first triplet note land on the root or fifth as a grounding point.
Keep the triplet timing precise even when the leaps force awkward fingerings.
examples: (beat 1) root → third → fifth → (beat 2) second → sixth → fourth → (beat 3) seventh → third → root
(beat 1) fourth → root → fifth → (beat 2) third → seventh → second → (beat 3) sixth → fourth → root up an octave
pitchConstraint:leaps_rhythmConstraint:t.watchOut: Triplets invite scalar runs more than any other rhythm — if two adjacent notes inside a triplet are neighbors, you've silently reverted to stepwise. Listen specifically for smooth thirds between notes.
pitchConstraint:leaps_rhythmConstraint:t.deeperInsight: Because the ear hears triplets as a rolling wave, the leaps create the illusion of two separate lines braided together — almost a compound melody.

pitchConstraint:stepwise_rhythmConstrain.interaction: This is the purest walking melody you can play — every note is a neighbor and every neighbor arrives exactly on a quarter beat. The constraints reinforce each other into something almost hypnotic. Your whole job becomes shaping the direction and gesture, because the rhythmic and pitch motion are both pinned.
steps: Start on the root or fifth and commit to only neighbor motion.
Play one note per beat, letting the direction change whenever it feels right.
Make your direction changes feel intentional — don't just wiggle between two notes.
Aim for a clear contour over every four beats, even if it's just up-up-up-down.
Let the line breathe by occasionally repeating a single note before continuing.
examples: root → second → third → fourth → third → fourth → fifth → fourth, one note per beat
fifth → fourth → third → fourth → fifth → sixth → fifth → fourth, walking up and down
pitchConstraint:stepwise_rhythmConstrain.watchOut: Because both rules are smooth, the melody can turn into aimless noodling with no shape. Always know whether you're currently climbing or descending — never both at random.
pitchConstraint:stepwise_rhythmConstrain.deeperInsight: This is how Gregorian chant and most folk song melodies move — the mind accepts even the simplest stepwise-in-time line as 'music' almost automatically.

pitchConstraint:stepwise_rhythmConstrain.interaction: Fast scale runs with breathing room between them. The burst lets you fire off a rapid-fire sequence of neighbors, and because everything is stepwise the ear hears each burst as a compact scale fragment rather than chaos. The silence that follows lets the phrase settle before the next run.
steps: Pick a direction for the first burst — all up, all down, or a small arc.
Fire 4-6 neighbors in a row as fast as you can play them cleanly.
End the burst on or near the root or fifth so the rest feels grounded.
Hold 2-3 beats of silence and listen.
Start the next burst from a different scale degree so it doesn't feel like an echo.
examples: root → second → third → fourth → fifth (burst) ... rest ... sixth → fifth → fourth → third → second (burst)
third → fourth → fifth → sixth → fifth (burst) ... rest ... second → third → fourth → third → root (burst)
pitchConstraint:stepwise_rhythmConstrain.watchOut: Fast fingers may want to skip a neighbor to reach a target — any skip inside the burst kills the stepwise rule. Play slightly slower than your maximum until all neighbors are landing.

pitchConstraint:stepwise_rhythmConstrain.interaction: A tiny handful of neighbor notes, then long silence. The stepwise motion makes each small gesture feel like a sigh or a breath, and the rests let those little breaths hang in the air. You're basically composing a series of two- or three-note sighs separated by stillness.
steps: Play two or three neighbor notes, no more, as a single micro-gesture.
Let the last note ring into silence for at least half a bar.
Make every gesture move in a clear direction — two notes up or two down, not wiggling.
Every few gestures, resolve downward to the root or fifth as an anchor.
Keep the tempo slow enough that the rests feel patient, not empty.
examples: third → second → root ... (long rest) ... fourth → fifth ... (rest) ... sixth → fifth → fourth
fifth → sixth ... (rest) ... fourth → third → second ... (rest) ... root → second
pitchConstraint:stepwise_rhythmConstrain.watchOut: Patience is the enemy — you will want to fill the silence with more neighbors. The rest is the instrument as much as the notes.

pitchConstraint:stepwise_rhythmConstrain.interaction: A walking melody in which every step lands between the beats. The stepwise motion gives the line a gentle, almost lullaby-like character, while the offbeat placement keeps pulling the ground out from under it. The result is a wobbly, swinging walk where the listener's ear keeps re-finding downbeat that's never actually played.
steps: Tap the quarter pulse in your foot and commit to never playing on those beats.
Place the first note on the and of one, starting on or near the root.
Move to the neighbor above or below on the next and, and keep walking offbeat.
Let the contour change direction every few notes so it doesn't feel like a scale exercise.
Every few gestures, let the offbeat neighbor resolve toward the fifth or root.
examples: (rest) root → (rest) second → (rest) third → (rest) second, all on the ands
(rest) fifth → (rest) fourth → (rest) third → (rest) fourth → (rest) fifth, stepwise between beats
pitchConstraint:stepwise_rhythmConstrain.watchOut: Stepwise motion plus offbeat is very easy to accidentally slip onto the downbeat — the moment you lose the foot-tap, the syncopation collapses into on-the-beat walking.

pitchConstraint:stepwise_rhythmConstrain.interaction: A short rhythmic cell repeats while the stepwise melody inside it climbs and descends. Because both the rhythm and the pitch motion are smooth, the phrase becomes intensely coherent — almost an etude. This is one of the most idiomatic combinations in Western music, underlying countless folk and classical lines.
steps: Invent a 1-bar rhythmic cell with 3-5 notes, something like two eighths and a quarter.
Fill the cell with neighbor motion — every note a step from the previous.
Repeat the rhythm exactly, starting the new cell one or two degrees higher or lower.
Let every third or fourth cell pass through the root or fifth.
Keep the cell's accent pattern identical from repetition to repetition.
examples: (cell) root → second → third ... (cell) second → third → fourth ... (cell) third → fourth → fifth
(cell) fifth → fourth → third ... (cell) fourth → third → second ... (cell) third → second → root
pitchConstraint:stepwise_rhythmConstrain.watchOut: When the stepwise notes feel boring, you'll be tempted to squeeze in a skip to spice things up — that breaks stepwise. Find interest in direction changes instead.

pitchConstraint:stepwise_rhythmConstrain.interaction: Rolling three-note groups made entirely of neighbors create a smooth, almost liquid scale feel. This is the shape of a lot of classical ornament work — turns, mordents, and waving figures — because the triplet subdivision plus stepwise motion is a perfectly lubricated combination.
steps: Lock in a medium tempo where triplets feel natural, not rushed.
Play three neighbor notes per beat, all walking in a single direction.
Change direction on the downbeat of the next bar rather than mid-triplet.
Aim for the root or fifth to land on a strong beat every few bars.
Keep the three triplet notes perfectly even in volume and timing.
examples: (beat 1) root → second → third (beat 2) fourth → fifth → sixth (beat 3) fifth → fourth → third
(beat 1) fifth → fourth → third (beat 2) second → root → second (beat 3) third → fourth → fifth
pitchConstraint:stepwise_rhythmConstrain.watchOut: Uneven triplet timing is the classic failure here — the middle note of each triplet tends to rush or drag. Listen to the spacing more than the notes.

pitchConstraint:arch_rhythmConstraint:ri.interaction: A hill shape walked in even quarter notes. The pulse is pure and steady and the melody slowly climbs a scale-ish path up to a peak, then descends back down. Because the rhythm never varies, the arch lives entirely in the pitch contour — this is one of the most elemental melodic shapes in all of music.
steps: Pick a low starting note and a mental peak at or above the fifth.
Walk upward in quarters, one note per beat, mostly climbing.
Arrive at the peak on a strong beat and hold your intention there.
Walk back down in quarters to the starting area.
End the phrase on or near the root as the arch closes.
examples: root → second → third → fifth → sixth (peak) → fifth → third → second → root, one note per beat
low third → fifth → sixth → root up an octave (peak) → sixth → fifth → fourth → root
pitchConstraint:arch_rhythmConstraint:ri.watchOut: The river wants to march past the peak without marking it — the peak must feel like a clear turning point in the contour, not just the highest accidental note.

pitchConstraint:arch_rhythmConstraint:bu.interaction: Each burst is itself a tiny hill shape — a rapid-fire climb to a peak and a quick descent, then silence. The combination turns every phrase into a compact gesture, like a wave crashing and pulling back. The burst's speed lets you complete a whole arch in one breath, and the rest that follows lets the ear absorb the shape before the next wave arrives.
steps: Before each burst, picture the peak note you're aiming for — usually the fifth or sixth.
Fire 4-6 quick notes that climb to that peak and come back down.
Land the last note of the burst near the root for a soft resolution.
Hold 2-3 beats of silence and let the arch linger.
Start the next burst with a new peak, slightly higher or lower, to vary the waves.
examples: root → third → fifth → sixth → fourth → root (burst) ... rest ... second → fourth → sixth → fifth → third (burst)
third → fifth → root up an octave → fifth → second (burst) ... rest ... root → fourth → sixth → fourth → root
pitchConstraint:arch_rhythmConstraint:bu.watchOut: Under speed the burst tends to become a one-way run with no descent — without the come-down, there is no arch. Make sure every burst actually turns around.

pitchConstraint:arch_rhythmConstraint:sp.interaction: A slow, deliberate hill shape with silence between every few notes. The arch unfolds across the whole round as a single huge gesture: a note here, a note there, each one a little higher, then a turning point, then back down into silence. The space makes every step of the hill feel heavy with intention.
steps: Plan the arch as an arc across the entire round, not a single bar.
Play two or three notes climbing toward the peak, then rest a full half-bar.
Play two or three more climbing notes, then rest again.
Mark the peak note clearly — maybe give it the longest duration of all.
Descend through the same sparse texture until you land on the root.
examples: root → third ... (rest) ... fifth → sixth ... (rest) ... peak root up an octave ... (rest) ... sixth → fifth ... (rest) ... third → root
second → fourth ... (rest) ... sixth → peak seventh ... (rest) ... fifth → third ... (rest) ... root
pitchConstraint:arch_rhythmConstraint:sp.watchOut: With so many rests, the arch shape can evaporate — you must remember which side of the peak you're on. Keep a mental map of where you are in the hill.

pitchConstraint:arch_rhythmConstraint:of.interaction: A hill contour where every step lands between beats. The offbeat placement makes the climb feel like it's floating just ahead of the pulse, so the arch shape seems to hover rather than walk. When the peak arrives on an upbeat, it feels momentarily suspended before the descent pulls it back to ground.
steps: Tap quarters in your foot throughout — never play on those beats.
Start the climb on the and of one from a low note.
Step or skip upward on each subsequent offbeat attack, shaping a hill.
Let the peak land on an and too — do not give in and play it on a downbeat.
Descend through offbeats back toward the root area.
examples: (rest) root → (rest) third → (rest) fifth → (rest) peak sixth → (rest) fourth → (rest) root
(rest) second → (rest) fourth → (rest) peak sixth → (rest) fifth → (rest) third → (rest) root
pitchConstraint:arch_rhythmConstraint:of.watchOut: The peak is the moment you're most likely to betray the offbeat rule — the instinct is to put the high note on the downbeat where it feels strongest. Hold the line and keep it on the and.

pitchConstraint:arch_rhythmConstraint:rh.interaction: A fixed rhythmic cell repeats while the pitches inside climb to a peak and descend. The rhythm acts like a mold stamping out evenly shaped cells, and the arch reveals itself as each successive cell lives slightly higher, then the trajectory turns and the cells start to drop. The arch becomes a meta-shape traced by the repeating stamp.
steps: Pick a 1-bar rhythmic cell, maybe a quarter and two eighths.
The first cell lives near the root — fill it with low-register notes.
The next cell lives a little higher, the next higher still, each climbing toward the peak.
Let one cell sit at the peak — the hill's summit.
Each following cell steps back down toward the root.
examples: (cell) root → second → third ... (cell) third → fourth → fifth ... (cell peak) fifth → sixth → fifth ... (cell) fourth → third → root
(cell) low fourth → fifth ... (cell) fifth → sixth ... (cell peak) sixth → root up an octave ... (cell) fifth → fourth
pitchConstraint:arch_rhythmConstraint:rh.watchOut: If each cell just picks random notes, the arch disappears — the cells must climb and descend as a sequence. Think of the hill at the level of cells, not individual notes.

pitchConstraint:arch_rhythmConstraint:tr.interaction: A rolling three-feel hill — the triplet subdivision gives the arch a waltzing, breath-like momentum. Each beat's triplet walks a little higher than the last until you reach the peak, and the descent rolls back down on the same three-note pulse. The three-ness makes the hill feel rounded, like a sigh.
steps: Lock into a medium triplet feel where three notes per beat are comfortable.
Begin in the low register and let the first several beats climb toward the peak.
Give one beat to the peak — either a sustained high note or a triplet figure around it.
Roll back down through triplets in a mirror of the ascent.
End the arch near the root at the start of a bar.
examples: (beat 1) root → second → third (beat 2) fourth → fifth → sixth (beat 3 peak) sixth → seventh → sixth (beat 4) fifth → third → root
(beat 1) third → fourth → fifth (beat 2 peak) sixth → root up an octave → sixth (beat 3) fifth → fourth → third (beat 4) second → root
pitchConstraint:arch_rhythmConstraint:tr.watchOut: Triplets roll so smoothly that you can sail right past the peak without marking it — give the peak a clear moment of arrival, don't let it become just another triplet note.
pitchConstraint:arch_rhythmConstraint:tr.deeperInsight: Arched triplet lines are the DNA of a lot of Celtic reels and jigs — the combination reliably produces singing, dance-ready melodies.

pitchConstraint:seed_rhythmConstraint:ri.interaction: A 3-note pitch seed repeated in even quarter notes, with one note changing each time. The river's steady pulse makes the seed's tiny variations crystal clear — the ear hears each iteration and instantly notices which note moved. It's like watching someone slowly rotate a sculpture under even lighting.
steps: Pick a 3-note seed centered around the root, third, or fifth.
Play the seed in quarter notes, one note per beat.
On the next repetition, change exactly one note — maybe the last.
Keep the rhythm pinned to quarters no matter what pitch you swap in.
After several rotations, return to the original seed as a resolution.
examples: (seed) root → third → fifth → (var) root → third → sixth → (var) root → fourth → sixth → (var) root → fourth → fifth
(seed) third → fifth → second → (var) third → fifth → fourth → (var) third → sixth → fourth → (var) second → sixth → fourth
pitchConstraint:seed_rhythmConstraint:ri.watchOut: Under the steady river it's easy to swap two notes at once without noticing. Commit to exactly one change per repetition and listen closely to confirm.

pitchConstraint:seed_rhythmConstraint:bu.interaction: A 3-note pitch seed fired as a rapid burst, then silence, then the seed again with one note changed. Because the burst is fast the seed hits the ear as a compact unit, and the rest gives you time to decide which note to change next. Each burst is a new snapshot of the evolving seed.
steps: Settle on a 3-note seed near the root, fifth, or third.
Fire it as a 3-note burst at the start of a bar.
Hold 2-3 beats of silence.
During the silence, decide which single note to change.
Fire the modified seed as the next burst, and continue evolving it.
examples: (burst) root → third → fifth ... rest ... (burst) root → third → sixth ... rest ... (burst) root → fourth → sixth
(burst) fifth → third → root ... rest ... (burst) fifth → second → root ... rest ... (burst) fourth → second → root
pitchConstraint:seed_rhythmConstraint:bu.watchOut: The silence is thinking time, not decoration. If you don't plan the next change during the rest, the burst will either copy the last seed exactly or change too many notes.

pitchConstraint:seed_rhythmConstraint:sp.interaction: A 3-note pitch seed stretched across a slow, sparse texture — each note of the seed might have a full beat of rest after it. The space gives every note of the seed a chance to breathe, and the slow rotations of the variations feel almost meditative. It's the musical equivalent of watching someone turn a pebble in the sun.
steps: Pick a simple 3-note seed using the root and two nearby degrees.
Play the first seed very slowly with rests between each note.
Let the whole seed finish, then rest a full bar.
Play the next iteration of the seed, changing exactly one note.
Continue rotating the seed with the same patient spacing.
examples: (seed) root ... rest ... third ... rest ... fifth ... (long rest) ... (var) root ... rest ... third ... rest ... sixth
(seed) fifth ... rest ... root ... rest ... third ... (long rest) ... (var) fifth ... rest ... second ... rest ... third
pitchConstraint:seed_rhythmConstraint:sp.watchOut: The huge amount of silence can make you forget which note is in the current seed — keep the current seed's three pitches clearly in your head.

pitchConstraint:seed_rhythmConstraint:of.interaction: A 3-note pitch seed where every attack lands between the beats. The seed gives the ear something familiar to latch onto, while the offbeat placement makes the whole pattern feel like it's swinging sideways. When the seed finally mutates, the new note still lands on an and — you're sliding the content around inside the same floating rhythmic frame.
steps: Tap the quarter pulse and keep all attacks off of it.
Place each note of the seed on an and — three offbeat attacks per seed iteration.
Leave the next downbeat empty between iterations.
Change exactly one note each iteration, keeping the placement identical.
Anchor at least one note of the seed to the root or fifth.
examples: (rest) root → (rest) third → (rest) fifth ... (rest) root → (rest) third → (rest) sixth ... (rest) root → (rest) fourth → (rest) sixth
(rest) fifth → (rest) second → (rest) fourth ... (rest) fifth → (rest) third → (rest) fourth ... (rest) sixth → (rest) third → (rest) fourth
pitchConstraint:seed_rhythmConstraint:of.watchOut: Offbeat seeds tempt you to play the strong seed note on the downbeat to emphasize it — that breaks the rhythm rule. The emphasis has to come from touch, not from slipping onto a beat.
