The line climbs to one peak degree then descends back, and from first note to last it should feel like one continuous breath with no reattacks. The arch and the legato reinforce each other — the shape gives the ear a journey and the legato turns it into a single gesture.
steps: Pick a peak degree before you start and hold it in your ear
Climb toward the peak with connected motion
Let the peak sustain for a beat longer than the other notes
Descend back to the starting area with the same connection
Keep the whole phrase under one uninterrupted volume curve
examples: Root up to sixth down to third, all connected
Third up to root-above down to fifth as one arc
Second up to fifth down to root as a small smooth hill
pitchConstraint:arch_articulation:legato.watchOut: The descent usually breaks into individual notes while the ascent sounds connected. Protect the descent — the legato has to survive all the way back down.
pitchConstraint:arch_articulation:legato.deeperInsight: Arch plus legato is the shape of a spoken sentence. The peak is the stressed word.

pitchConstraint:arch_articulation:stacca.interaction: A hill made of bouncing dots. The pitch contour still climbs and descends, but every single note is short and detached, so the arch is drawn by the position of the dots in space rather than by a connected line. It has the cheeky feeling of skipping up a hill and back down.
steps: Decide on a peak degree before starting the phrase
Bounce short notes upward toward the peak
Make the peak note the same short length as all the others
Bounce short notes back down to the starting area
Keep every clip the same length so the shape reads clearly
examples: Root, third, fifth, sixth, fifth, third, root — each note short
Second, fourth, sixth, fourth, second — bouncy hill
Third, fifth, seventh, fifth, third — short arch
pitchConstraint:arch_articulation:stacca.watchOut: Do not reward the peak by letting it ring longer than the rest. The peak earns its emphasis through position in the shape, not through length.
pitchConstraint:arch_articulation:stacca.deeperInsight: Staccato arches are common in classical winds and in reggae upstroke lines. The shape is audible because the silences line it up.

pitchConstraint:arch_articulation:slurre.interaction: Draw the hill in paired gestures. Every two notes slur together and then a small gap lets the next pair begin, which turns the arch into a sequence of little swoops climbing to the peak and swooping back down. It has a wave-like rocking feel inside the larger arc.
steps: Pick a peak degree and plan pairs that climb toward it
Slur the first two notes together with no gap between them
Breathe briefly before starting the next pair
Let the pairs rise to the peak then descend in matching pairs
Keep the overall volume shape rising to the peak and softening back
examples: Root-third slurred, fifth-sixth slurred, fifth-third slurred, root slurred-second
Second-fourth slurred, fifth-sixth slurred, fifth-fourth slurred, second-root slurred
Third-fifth slurred, seventh-root-above slurred, seventh-fifth slurred, third-root slurred
pitchConstraint:arch_articulation:slurre.watchOut: The gap between pairs can break the shape if it is too long. Keep the breath tiny so the arch still reads as one phrase with gentle punctuation.
pitchConstraint:arch_articulation:slurre.deeperInsight: This is the phrasing of classical slow movements. The pairs are sighs climbing a hill.

pitchConstraint:arch_articulation:tenuto.interaction: A slow deliberate mountain. Every note in the arch gets its full value and the shape unfolds unhurriedly from base to peak and back. Because nothing is clipped, the ear gets to dwell on every degree along the climb and descent, which makes the peak feel genuinely earned.
steps: Choose a peak degree and a slow tempo that feels patient
Hold every ascending note all the way to the next pulse
Arrive at the peak and give it its full value without stretching
Descend with the same full-value commitment
Do not let the descent rush even though it feels familiar
examples: Root full, third full, fifth full, sixth full, fifth full, third full, root full
Second full, fourth full, sixth full, fourth full, second full
Third full, fifth full, seventh full, fifth full, third full
pitchConstraint:arch_articulation:tenuto.watchOut: The descent from the peak almost always speeds up. Stay honest with the pulse — a tenuto arch has to be even in both directions.
pitchConstraint:arch_articulation:tenuto.deeperInsight: This combo trains pitch endurance. Held notes reveal intonation the way a spotlight reveals dust.

pitchConstraint:seed_articulation:legato.interaction: Pick three degrees as your seed and play them as one connected gesture, then repeat the gesture changing exactly one note at a time. Because everything is legato, each variation feels like a slow morph — you hear the single change stand out against the sustain of the other two.
steps: Choose three degrees such as root, third, and fifth as the seed
Play the three notes connected with no gaps
Repeat the gesture and change exactly one of the three notes
Let the new note flow into and out of the other two smoothly
Continue for six to eight repetitions so the morph is clear
examples: Root-third-fifth, then root-third-sixth, then root-fourth-sixth
Second-fourth-sixth, then second-fifth-sixth, then third-fifth-sixth
Root-fifth-seventh, then root-fifth-sixth, then root-fourth-sixth
pitchConstraint:seed_articulation:legato.watchOut: The legato can hide the single-note change if your volume is uneven. Give the changing note enough presence that the morph is audible.
pitchConstraint:seed_articulation:legato.deeperInsight: This is how minimalist composers build phrases. Seed plus legato equals gradual transformation.

pitchConstraint:seed_articulation:stacca.interaction: Three seed degrees, each one short and detached, and every repetition swaps out one note. The staccato makes each cell sound like a tiny machine stamping out three distinct events, and the single-note change becomes a crisp substitution the ear can track easily against the repeated neighbors.
steps: Pick three degrees to form your seed
Play them as three clipped short notes
Repeat the cell keeping two notes identical and changing one
Keep the clip length identical across every note and every cell
Rotate which slot you change across eight cells
examples: Root-third-fifth, root-third-sixth, root-fourth-sixth all clipped
Second-fourth-sixth, second-fifth-sixth, third-fifth-sixth all clipped
Root-fifth-seventh, root-fifth-sixth, root-fourth-sixth all clipped
pitchConstraint:seed_articulation:stacca.watchOut: Repetition plus staccato can turn mechanical. Keep a little musical breath between cells so it sounds like decisions and not a drum machine.
pitchConstraint:seed_articulation:stacca.deeperInsight: Staccato seed work is the foundation of cell-based improvisation — Philip Glass and jazz bop lines both live here.

pitchConstraint:seed_articulation:slurre.interaction: Three seed degrees but you slur the first two together and leave the third one separate, then repeat the cell and swap one note. The pair shape gives the first two notes a tight togetherness while the third note feels like a comment or answer, and the single-note change moves across all three slots over time.
steps: Choose three degrees as your seed
Slur the first two notes with no gap between them
Leave a small breath before the third note of the cell
Change one of the three notes on the next repetition
Continue until each slot has been altered at least twice
examples: Root-third slurred, then fifth — repeat with root-third slurred, then sixth
Second-fourth slurred, then sixth — repeat with second-fifth slurred, then sixth
Root-fifth slurred, then seventh — repeat with root-fifth slurred, then sixth
pitchConstraint:seed_articulation:slurre.watchOut: The third note often gets orphaned and plays too loud. Keep it tucked into the character of the cell so it feels like it belongs.
pitchConstraint:seed_articulation:slurre.deeperInsight: Pair plus single is a classic three-note motif shape. Beethoven opened his Fifth with exactly this structure.

pitchConstraint:seed_articulation:tenuto.interaction: Three seed degrees each held for full value, then the cell repeats with one note changed. Because every note gets its full length the ear has plenty of time to compare the old version to the new version, making the single substitution feel like a gentle but unmistakable shift in color.
steps: Pick three degrees to anchor the cell
Play each note at its full value without clipping
Let the cell settle before beginning the next repetition
Repeat the cell and alter only one of the three notes
Move slowly — this combo rewards patience
examples: Root-third-fifth all full, then root-third-sixth all full
Second-fourth-sixth all full, then second-fifth-sixth all full
Root-fifth-seventh all full, then root-fifth-sixth all full
pitchConstraint:seed_articulation:tenuto.watchOut: Holding three notes to full value can drag the tempo into mud. Stay honest with the pulse even when the individual notes feel slow.
pitchConstraint:seed_articulation:tenuto.deeperInsight: Tenuto seed cells are how you train harmonic hearing. Every variation is a mini chord change you can feel.

pitchConstraint:forbidden_articulation:l.interaction: Remove one to three degrees from the scale and then play the remaining palette as a smoothly connected line. The gap in the scale becomes audible as a hole the ear expects to be filled, and keeping the line legato means you have to leap smoothly across that hole without bumping the reattack.
steps: Declare one, two, or three forbidden degrees before you start
Improvise through the remaining notes with connected motion
When a forbidden note would fall naturally, leap smoothly over it
Match volume across the leaps so the line stays one gesture
Let the phrase wander so the missing degrees get tested in context
examples: No second and no sixth — wander from root to third to fifth to seventh
No fourth — wander from root to third to fifth to sixth and back
No fifth and no seventh — wander between root, second, third, fourth, sixth
pitchConstraint:forbidden_articulation:l.watchOut: The ear will try to sneak the forbidden note in when you are not paying attention. Keep naming the taboo notes in your head as you improvise.
pitchConstraint:forbidden_articulation:l.deeperInsight: This is how pentatonic scales feel — five notes connected smoothly with two missing neighbors. You are training that same ear.

pitchConstraint:forbidden_articulation:s.interaction: Shrunk palette, short bouncy notes. Because each note is clipped the absence of the forbidden degrees is very obvious — the ear hears the available notes as a crisp set and catches any accidental trespass immediately. It feels like jumping on stepping stones with some stones removed.
steps: Pick one to three forbidden degrees and speak them out loud
Play only from the remaining degrees with short clipped notes
Keep all clip lengths identical regardless of register
Rest briefly when you feel tempted to play a forbidden note
Change the forbidden set after one minute and repeat
examples: No sixth and no seventh — pop between root, second, third, fourth, fifth
No second — pop between root, third, fourth, fifth, sixth, seventh
No third and no fifth — pop between root, second, fourth, sixth, seventh
pitchConstraint:forbidden_articulation:s.watchOut: Staccato makes wrong notes extra loud because there is no sustain to hide them in. A single forbidden bark ruins the whole phrase — stay vigilant.
pitchConstraint:forbidden_articulation:s.deeperInsight: The forbidden staccato sound is the signature of prepared piano and minimalist percussion — limited palette, crystal clear events.

pitchConstraint:forbidden_articulation:s.interaction: Shrunk palette played in slurred two-note gestures. Each pair must be built from the allowed degrees only, and the little gap between pairs gives the ear a chance to reset and confirm the forbidden notes are absent. It feels like a scavenger hunt where you can only use certain tiles.
steps: Declare one to three forbidden degrees and commit to the taboo
Build each pair from two allowed degrees slurred together
Open a small gap before the next pair
Rotate which allowed degrees you use across each new pair
Keep the forbidden notes completely out of every pair
examples: No second — root-third slurred, fifth-sixth slurred, seventh-root slurred
No fourth — root-second slurred, third-fifth slurred, sixth-seventh slurred
No fifth and no seventh — root-second slurred, third-fourth slurred, sixth-root slurred
pitchConstraint:forbidden_articulation:s.watchOut: The landing note of the pair is the most likely place for a forbidden note to sneak in. Pre-plan the pair in your head before you play it.
pitchConstraint:forbidden_articulation:s.deeperInsight: This combo teaches you to hear two-note intervals as belonging or not belonging to a set. Pure ear training in disguise.

pitchConstraint:forbidden_articulation:t.interaction: Shrunk palette held at full value. Every allowed degree gets its complete time slice, which means the forbidden notes are absent for long spans where the ear strongly wants to hear them filled in. It feels meditative and almost scriptural — these are the permitted colors and you will sit with them fully.
steps: Declare one to three forbidden degrees
Play only allowed degrees and hold each to full value
Resist any urge to rush through the held notes
Let silence fall naturally if you cannot decide the next allowed note
Rotate the forbidden set every two minutes
examples: No third — root full, second full, fifth full, sixth full, fourth full
No sixth and no seventh — root full, second full, third full, fourth full, fifth full
No second — root full, third full, fifth full, seventh full, sixth full
pitchConstraint:forbidden_articulation:t.watchOut: Holding a note to full value while your ear craves a forbidden note is hard. If you break and play the taboo, stop and restart the exercise to re-anchor the palette.
pitchConstraint:forbidden_articulation:t.deeperInsight: This is how modal improvisation is taught in some traditions — stay with the legal notes, hold them, and let the ear adjust.

pitchConstraint:targetLanding_articulati.interaction: Every phrase must end on one chosen target degree, and the entire journey to that target is played legato. The connected motion turns each phrase into one breath aimed at a specific destination, making the arrival feel inevitable rather than accidental.
steps: Pick a target degree before each phrase — often the root or fifth
Improvise a short connected line through the scale
Shape the last two or three notes to aim directly at the target
Release into the target with no gap between it and the previous note
Let the target sustain a little longer than the other notes
examples: Wander legato through sixth, fifth, fourth, landing on third
Wander legato through second, fourth, third, landing on root
Wander legato through seventh, sixth, fifth, landing on root
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_articulati.watchOut: Players often reattach the target note instead of sliding into it. Protect the final connection — the landing has to belong to the phrase, not arrive as a new event.
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_articulati.deeperInsight: This is the phrasing of cadence. Every classical cadence is a target landing in legato.

pitchConstraint:targetLanding_articulati.interaction: Every phrase ends on a chosen target degree but every note along the way is short and clipped, including the target itself. The arrival still feels decisive because the target is the rhythmic resting point of the phrase, but the whole journey has a bouncy staccato character that keeps it playful.
steps: Pick a target degree and say it out loud
Play a short clipped line wandering through the scale
Keep clip lengths even across all notes
Let the last clip land exactly on the target and then rest
Silence between phrases resets the ear for the next target
examples: Sixth, fifth, fourth clipped, landing clipped on third
Second, fourth, third clipped, landing clipped on root
Seventh, sixth, fifth clipped, landing clipped on root
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_articulati.watchOut: The target note often gets subconsciously held longer to emphasize it. Resist that — the arrival has to come from the rhythmic placement, not from extra length.
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_articulati.deeperInsight: Staccato target landings are the hallmark of bebop phrasing. The target arrives, and the line stops cold.

pitchConstraint:targetLanding_articulati.interaction: Every phrase aims at a chosen target degree, and the whole phrase is broken into slurred pairs with tiny gaps between them. The final pair has to resolve onto the target, which usually means the second note of the last pair is the target note itself — and that little slur into the destination is deeply satisfying.
steps: Pick a target degree for the phrase
Build pairs of slurred notes leading toward the target
Open a small gap between pairs but not within a pair
Arrange the final pair so its second note is the target
Let the target sustain briefly before starting a new phrase
examples: Sixth-fifth slurred, fourth-third slurred, second-root slurred with root as target
Seventh-sixth slurred, fifth-fourth slurred, third-root slurred with root as target
Second-fourth slurred, third-fifth slurred, fourth-third slurred with third as target
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_articulati.watchOut: The gap before the final pair is often shortened because you are eager to arrive. Keep the gap honest so the final pair has a clear launch point.
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_articulati.deeperInsight: This is the structure of Baroque cadences — pairs leading to a resolution, each pair a sigh toward home.

pitchConstraint:targetLanding_articulati.interaction: Every phrase ends on a target degree and every single note including the target is held to full value. The effect is solemn and unhurried — the journey to the target feels deliberate and the arrival feels like the phrase leaning its full weight onto the destination.
steps: Pick a target degree and commit to a slow tempo
Play each approach note at full value without clipping
Do not speed up as you near the target
Let the target note land at full value and then rest
Breathe before starting the next phrase toward the same target
examples: Sixth full, fifth full, fourth full, target third full
Second full, fourth full, third full, target root full
Seventh full, sixth full, fifth full, target root full
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_articulati.watchOut: The approach notes tend to shrink as you anticipate the target. Give each approach note its full value so the target does not feel like the only real event.
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_articulati.deeperInsight: Tenuto target landings teach gravity — the ear learns what it means for a phrase to really come home.

pitchConstraint:questionAnswer_articulat.interaction: Two phrases play as a pair — a rising question then a descending answer — and both phrases are played completely legato. The connected motion makes each half feel like a single breath, and the contrast between the rising breath and the falling breath sounds like a conversation where the answer gently resolves the question.
steps: Plan a rising phrase that climbs to a high note as the question
Play it with fully connected motion
Leave a breath of silence after the question
Play a descending answer with the same legato character
Let the answer land somewhere settled such as root or third
examples: Root up to sixth as question, sixth down to root as legato answer
Third up to root-above as question, fifth down to root as legato answer
Second up to seventh as question, seventh down to third as legato answer
pitchConstraint:questionAnswer_articulat.watchOut: The breath between question and answer is often too short. Give the question room to hang in the air before the answer begins speaking.
pitchConstraint:questionAnswer_articulat.deeperInsight: This is the structure of nearly every folk song melody. Two legato arcs, one up, one down.

pitchConstraint:questionAnswer_articulat.interaction: A rising question and a descending answer, but every note in both phrases is short and clipped. The staccato gives the dialogue a jaunty playful character — like two friends trading short quips — while the rising and falling shapes still do the work of asking and answering.
steps: Build a rising phrase to act as the question
Play it with clipped short notes of even length
Rest briefly after the question
Build a descending phrase as the answer with matching clips
Let the final answer note land short and settled
examples: Root, third, fifth, sixth clipped as question, sixth, fifth, third, root clipped as answer
Second, fourth, sixth, seventh clipped as question, seventh, fifth, third, second clipped as answer
Third, fifth, seventh clipped as question, seventh, fifth, third clipped as answer
pitchConstraint:questionAnswer_articulat.watchOut: Staccato dialogues tend to lose the difference between rising and falling shape because the clips dominate the feeling. Exaggerate the pitch contour so the shape reads.
pitchConstraint:questionAnswer_articulat.deeperInsight: This combo is the sound of folk fiddle calls and responses. Short, clear, unmistakable.

pitchConstraint:questionAnswer_articulat.interaction: A rising question and a descending answer, both built out of slurred two-note pairs with tiny gaps between pairs. The pair structure gives both halves of the dialogue a rocking speech-like rhythm, and the overall shape — rise then fall — still delivers the call and response shape.
steps: Plan a rising question made of two or three slurred pairs
Play each pair connected with a tiny gap before the next
Rest slightly longer after the final pair of the question
Build a descending answer out of slurred pairs as well
Make the last pair of the answer resolve onto a settled degree
examples: Root-third slurred, fifth-sixth slurred as question, sixth-fifth slurred, third-root slurred as answer
Second-fourth slurred, fifth-seventh slurred as question, seventh-fifth slurred, fourth-second slurred as answer
Third-fifth slurred, sixth-root-above slurred as question, root-above-sixth slurred, fifth-third slurred as answer
pitchConstraint:questionAnswer_articulat.watchOut: The gap between question and answer has to be bigger than the gaps between pairs, or the dialogue sounds like one long phrase. Protect that middle breath.
pitchConstraint:questionAnswer_articulat.deeperInsight: Slurred pairs in question-answer shape is the grammar of classical antecedent-consequent phrases.

pitchConstraint:questionAnswer_articulat.interaction: A rising question and a descending answer where every note of both phrases is held to full value. The slow unhurried character makes the dialogue feel weighty and deliberate, almost like a prayer call and response — every degree gets its full hearing and the arrival on the final answer note feels earned.
steps: Plan a rising question of three to five notes
Hold every note of the question to its full value
Rest for at least a full beat after the question
Play a descending answer holding every note to full value
Let the final answer note sustain a little longer as a resting point
examples: Root full, third full, fifth full, sixth full as question, sixth full, fifth full, third full, root full as answer
Second full, fourth full, sixth full as question, sixth full, fourth full, second full as answer
Third full, fifth full, seventh full as question, seventh full, fifth full, third full as answer
pitchConstraint:questionAnswer_articulat.watchOut: Tenuto descents rush, tenuto ascents drag. Use a metronome to keep both halves of the dialogue at the same patient tempo.
pitchConstraint:questionAnswer_articulat.deeperInsight: This is the pacing of Gregorian chant call and response. Full values, clear shapes, genuine dialogue.

pitchConstraint:leaps_phraseLength:1bar.interaction: One bar of pure jumping — no neighbors allowed, and the clock is tiny. You get maybe four notes before silence takes over, so every single leap has to earn its place. Think of it as a compressed firework: spark, jump, land, stop. The shortness actually helps because you can pre-hear each interval before your finger moves.
steps: Pick four notes from the scale that are never adjacent (e.g., root, fifth, third-up-high, seventh).
Play them on quarter notes, one bar, then rest a full bar.
Sing each interval in your head a half-beat before you play it.
If two notes land as neighbors, stop and re-pick.
Repeat the same four-note shape twice, then alter one leap.
examples: Root — fifth-above — third-above-root — seventh-below, then stop.
Second-degree — high root — fourth — sixth-below, then stop.
Root — seventh-above — fourth — high second, then stop.
pitchConstraint:leaps_phraseLength:1bar.watchOut: The bar is so short you'll rush past the silence and start the next fragment early, which blurs the 'say one thing and stop' feel. Let the rest bar breathe — the silence is half the phrase.
pitchConstraint:leaps_phraseLength:1bar.deeperInsight: Leaps read as intentional when they're framed by silence. One bar of leaps plus one bar of nothing gives the ear time to register the interval shape as a gesture, not a blur.

pitchConstraint:leaps_phraseLength:2bar.interaction: Two bars gives you enough room to build a real leap-based sentence with a beginning, a turn, and a landing. Aim for about six to eight notes total, each one skipping its neighbors. Because it's a complete thought, think in terms of 'question raised, question answered with another leap.' The fifth degree is a great pivot — leaping to it and away from it always sounds purposeful.
steps: Decide on a starting note and an ending note, both scale degrees you can name.
Fill the middle with four to six notes that never touch neighbors.
Play bar one as an ascending leap shape, bar two as a descending leap shape.
Anchor the fifth degree or root somewhere in the middle so the ear has a home.
Rate it: did any two adjacent notes sneak in as neighbors?
examples: Root → fifth → third-above — || — seventh-above → fourth → second → root.
Third → high root → fifth-above — || — second → sixth-below → fourth → root.
Fifth → root-below → third → seventh-above — || — fourth → sixth → second → fifth.
pitchConstraint:leaps_phraseLength:2bar.watchOut: Two bars is where people start cheating — a stepwise move slips in because it 'connects things smoothly.' If you hear a connector, you've broken the constraint. Leap through the transition instead.

pitchConstraint:leaps_phraseLength:4bar.interaction: Now you have a paragraph of leaps — roughly twelve to sixteen notes with room for real development. A four-bar leap phrase needs internal shape: maybe bars one and two climb through wide intervals, bar three is the peak with the biggest jump, bar four descends with leaps that land softer. This is where leaps stop feeling like a drill and start feeling like a melody that happens to skip.
steps: Sketch the arc first: where's the peak note, where's the final landing?
Bars 1-2: climbing leaps, each at least a third wide.
Bar 3: the biggest leap of the whole phrase — your dramatic moment.
Bar 4: descending leaps that resolve to a scale-degree anchor like root or third.
Play it twice and check that no two consecutive notes are neighbors.
Sing the shape without your instrument — can you hear it as one thought?
examples: Root up to fifth up to seventh — || — third-above down to sixth — || — leap to high root (peak) — || — fourth down to second down to root.
Third → fifth → root-above — || — fourth → seventh → second — || — high fourth (peak) → sixth-below — || — third → root → fifth-below → root.
Fifth → root → third — || — seventh → second → fourth — || — high fifth peak → root-above → third — || — sixth → fourth → root.
pitchConstraint:leaps_phraseLength:4bar.watchOut: In four bars, leap fatigue sets in — your brain wants to rest on a neighbor. Plan the peak bar in advance so you have something to aim for, otherwise the phrase wanders and the constraint degrades.

pitchConstraint:leaps_phraseLength:8bar.interaction: Eight bars of leaps is an epic — a twenty-four to thirty-two note zigzag that has to tell a story without any neighboring relief. Think of it as mountain-ridge walking: every step is a jump, but the whole ridge has a shape. You need at least two peaks, a valley in the middle, and a final descent that lands firmly on the root or fifth. The constraint becomes a character, not a rule.
steps: Map the eight bars into four two-bar sub-phrases before you play.
Sub-phrase 1: introduce the leap vocabulary with medium jumps.
Sub-phrase 2: reach a first peak with a big interval.
Sub-phrase 3: valley — descending leaps to a low scale-degree anchor.
Sub-phrase 4: climb to the biggest peak, then release to root.
Play the whole thing once, then again with more silence between sub-phrases.
examples: Bars 1-2: root-fifth-third-seventh — || — second-fourth-sixth-root. Bars 3-4: high third-fifth-root-above (peak 1) — || — fourth-second-sixth-below. Bars 5-6: root-fourth-seventh-below (valley) — || — third-fifth-second-fourth. Bars 7-8: high fifth (peak 2)-third-root-above — || — sixth-fourth-second-root.

Bars 1-2: third-root-fifth-above — || — seventh-second-fourth-sixth. Bars 3-4: high root-third-above — || — fifth-above-second-below (peak 1, valley). Bars 5-6: root-fourth-high-second — || — sixth-below-third-fifth. Bars 7-8: high fourth-root-above (peak 2) — || — third-fifth-below-root.
pitchConstraint:leaps_phraseLength:8bar.watchOut: The biggest failure at eight bars is monotony — leaps become a texture instead of a shape. Without pre-planned peaks and a valley, every bar sounds the same and the 'story' turns into aimless skipping.

pitchConstraint:stepwise_phraseLength:1b.interaction: One bar of walking — usually just four neighboring notes in a row. The tiny container forces you to commit to a single direction or a single direction-change. This is less melody and more gesture: a four-note climb, a four-note fall, or a three-up-one-down turn. It's meditative and surprisingly hard because there's no room to hide a wrong move.
steps: Pick a starting scale degree you can name (root, third, fifth work well).
Choose a direction: up, down, or one turn.
Play exactly four neighboring notes on quarter notes.
Rest at least one beat before considering the next fragment.
Check: did any note skip a neighbor?
examples: Root → second → third → fourth, stop.
Fifth → fourth → third → second, stop.
Third → fourth → fifth → fourth (one turn), stop.
pitchConstraint:stepwise_phraseLength:1b.watchOut: The phrase is so short that rhythmic stumbles become the whole event. Keep the quarter-note pulse strict — a rushed third note ruins the entire walking feel.

pitchConstraint:stepwise_phraseLength:2b.interaction: Two bars of pure walking gives you a full neighborly sentence — roughly six to eight notes, all touching their scale-step neighbors. You have room for one direction change now, which is where stepwise phrases start sounding like melodies instead of scales. Think bar one ascends, bar two descends, or build a small hill that peaks on beat three of bar one.
steps: Choose a scale degree to start on and a scale degree to land on.
Decide where the single turnaround will happen.
Walk up through neighbors to the turn point.
Walk back down through neighbors to your landing.
Play twice — check for any skipped neighbor.
examples: Root → second → third → fourth — || — fifth → fourth → third → second.
Fifth → sixth → seventh → root-above — || — seventh → sixth → fifth → fourth.
Third → fourth → fifth → fourth — || — third → second → root → second.
pitchConstraint:stepwise_phraseLength:2b.watchOut: Two bars of walking can turn into an obvious up-the-scale, down-the-scale exercise. Break the symmetry — land bar two on a different scale degree than you started bar one, so it feels like a sentence, not a drill.

pitchConstraint:stepwise_phraseLength:4b.interaction: Four bars is where stepwise writing becomes genuinely melodic — about twelve to sixteen notes, all neighboring, with room for two or three direction changes. You can build a real contour: climb, gentle turn, climb higher to a peak, fall through neighbors to a resolution. The ear experiences it as a walking melody that goes somewhere and comes home. Think of it as a paragraph where every word touches the one before it.
steps: Sketch the contour: starting degree, peak degree, landing degree.
Bars 1-2: climb through neighbors with one small dip along the way.
Bar 3: reach the peak — make sure the peak itself is approached by a neighbor.
Bar 4: step down through neighbors and resolve to a scale-degree anchor.
Play once, then sing it back without the instrument.
Revise any spot where the line stopped walking.
examples: Bar 1: root → second → third → fourth. Bar 2: third → fourth → fifth → sixth. Bar 3: seventh → root-above (peak) → seventh → sixth. Bar 4: fifth → fourth → third → root.
Bar 1: fifth → fourth → third → fourth. Bar 2: fifth → sixth → seventh → root-above. Bar 3: second-above → third-above (peak) → second-above → root-above. Bar 4: seventh → sixth → fifth → fifth.
Bar 1: third → second → root → second. Bar 2: third → fourth → fifth → sixth. Bar 3: seventh → sixth → seventh → root-above (peak). Bar 4: seventh → sixth → fifth → fourth → third.
pitchConstraint:stepwise_phraseLength:4b.watchOut: Four bars of walking will feel like a warm-up scale unless you give it a real peak and a non-obvious landing. Don't start on the root and end on the root — try starting on the third and landing on the fifth to break the 'scale practice' trap.

pitchConstraint:stepwise_phraseLength:8b.interaction: An eight-bar stepwise phrase is a long guided walk — twenty-four to thirty-two neighboring notes forming an extended melody. The constraint becomes a strength: because you can only step, the listener relaxes into your contour, and your choices of where to turn around become the whole drama. Plan two peaks, a plateau, and a long descent. This is where stepwise stops being a rule and starts being a vocal-style melody.
steps: Map eight bars into four two-bar sub-phrases.
Sub-phrase 1: gentle rise to a small peak, then a small dip.
Sub-phrase 2: climb higher, approach the first main peak by a neighbor.
Sub-phrase 3: plateau and small wave — stay near the peak without leaping.
Sub-phrase 4: long walking descent to resolve on the root or third.
Sing the whole thing unaccompanied — if it feels like a melody, you've done it right.
examples: Bars 1-2: root-second-third-fourth || third-fourth-fifth-sixth. Bars 3-4: fifth-sixth-seventh-root-above || second-above-root-above-seventh-root-above (peak 1). Bars 5-6: root-above-second-above-third-above-second-above || root-above-seventh-root-above-second-above. Bars 7-8: root-above-seventh-sixth-fifth || fourth-third-second-root.

Bars 1-2: third-fourth-fifth-fourth || third-fourth-fifth-sixth. Bars 3-4: seventh-root-above-second-above-root-above || seventh-root-above-second-above-third-above (peak). Bars 5-6: fourth-above-third-above-fourth-above-fifth-above || fourth-above-third-above-second-above-root-above. Bars 7-8: seventh-sixth-fifth-fourth || third-fourth-third-root.
pitchConstraint:stepwise_phraseLength:8b.watchOut: Eight bars of walking can drift forever without direction. If you don't pre-plan the peaks and the plateau, the line meanders and the listener disengages. Stepwise doesn't mean shapeless.

pitchConstraint:arch_phraseLength:1bar.interaction: A one-bar arch is a micro-hill — four notes that rise to a tiny peak on beat two or three, then come back down to a scale degree you can name. It's the smallest complete arch shape there is. Because the container is tight, every note matters: you can't waste one on a detour. Treat it as the musical equivalent of an eyebrow raise.
steps: Decide the peak note — usually the third or fourth above your starting note.
Place the peak on beat two or three of the bar.
Rise to the peak in two notes, descend from it in two notes.
Land on a scale-degree anchor (root, third, or fifth).
Rest a beat and check: was there a clear high point?
examples: Root → third → fourth (peak) → second.
Fifth → sixth → seventh (peak) → fifth.
Third → fifth → sixth (peak) → fourth → root (if five notes).
pitchConstraint:arch_phraseLength:1bar.watchOut: A one-bar arch without a rest after it blurs into the next arch and loses its shape. Let the bar breathe at the end so the peak registers as a peak.
pitchConstraint:arch_phraseLength:1bar.deeperInsight: Tiny arches stack beautifully — three or four of them in a row become a rolling-hills melody without ever violating the 'peak and return' rule.

pitchConstraint:arch_phraseLength:2bar.interaction: A two-bar arch is a natural sentence shape: rise through bar one, peak around the barline, fall through bar two. This is the classic phrase arc singers use without thinking. You have about six to eight notes to climb, crest, and resolve. The peak itself should feel like the emotional center of the phrase — not just the highest note, but the one the whole shape is leaning toward.
steps: Pick a starting scale degree and a landing scale degree (often the same).
Place the peak on beat four of bar one or beat one of bar two.
Bar 1: rise toward the peak with a mix of steps and small leaps.
Bar 2: descend from the peak and resolve to the landing note.
Sing it once — does the peak feel like the climax?
examples: Root → third → fifth → seventh (peak) — || — sixth → fourth → second → root.
Fifth → sixth → root-above → second-above (peak) — || — root-above → seventh → sixth → fifth.
Third → fifth → sixth → root-above (peak) — || — seventh → fifth → third → root.
pitchConstraint:arch_phraseLength:2bar.watchOut: Two bars is where arches go flat — people rise, peak, and then rush the descent. The fall needs as much attention as the climb, otherwise the arch becomes a lopsided bump.

pitchConstraint:arch_phraseLength:4bar.interaction: A four-bar arch is a real developed hill — about twelve to sixteen notes shaping a slow climb, a defined peak, and a patient descent. This is the shape of most folk-song phrases. You can take two bars to build, hit the peak in bar three, and use bar four for a graceful resolution. Give the climb some internal shape — small dips on the way up make the peak hit harder when it arrives.
steps: Decide your peak scale degree in advance — often the seventh, root-above, or third-above.
Bars 1-2: gradual climb with at least one small dip along the way.
Bar 3: reach the peak early in the bar and linger on it.
Bar 4: descend to a resolution on root, third, or fifth.
Mark which note is the peak — make sure it's rhythmically or durationally emphasized.
Play it, then hum it back without the instrument.
examples: Bar 1: root → third → second → fourth. Bar 2: third → fifth → fourth → sixth. Bar 3: seventh → root-above (peak) → seventh → sixth. Bar 4: fifth → third → second → root.
Bar 1: third → second → fourth → third. Bar 2: fifth → fourth → sixth → fifth. Bar 3: root-above → second-above (peak) → root-above → seventh. Bar 4: sixth → fifth → third → third.
Bar 1: fifth → fourth → sixth → fifth. Bar 2: seventh → sixth → root-above → seventh. Bar 3: second-above → third-above (peak) → second-above → root-above. Bar 4: seventh → sixth → fifth → fifth.
pitchConstraint:arch_phraseLength:4bar.watchOut: Four-bar arches often peak too early — by bar two — and then wander for two bars with nowhere to go. Hold back. Let bars one and two build, save the peak for bar three, give bar four to the descent.

pitchConstraint:arch_phraseLength:8bar.interaction: Eight bars is a mountain — a long ascent, a dramatic summit, and an extended descent. Unlike shorter arches, this one has room for a false summit: a small peak in bar three or four that sets up the real peak in bar five or six. The descent then unfolds over two or three bars, allowing the listener to feel the release. Done right, an eight-bar arch is the most emotionally satisfying shape in melodic writing.
steps: Map the eight bars: rise (bars 1-2), false peak (bar 3-4), main peak (bar 5-6), descent (bars 7-8).
Start on a low scale degree like root or fifth-below.
Climb gradually, mixing steps and small leaps, to the false peak.
Drop slightly after the false peak to build tension.
Push through to the real peak — the highest note of the whole phrase.
Descend with patience, landing on root or third in bar eight.
examples: Bars 1-2: root → second → third → fifth || fourth → sixth → fifth → seventh. Bars 3-4: sixth → root-above (false peak) || seventh → sixth → fifth → sixth. Bars 5-6: root-above → second-above → third-above (main peak) → second-above || root-above → seventh → root-above → seventh. Bars 7-8: sixth → fifth → fourth → third || second → root → second → root.

Bars 1-2: fifth-below → root → third → fifth || fourth → sixth → fifth → seventh. Bars 3-4: root-above → second-above (false peak) → root-above → seventh || sixth → seventh → root-above → second-above. Bars 5-6: third-above → fourth-above (main peak) → third-above → second-above || root-above → seventh → root-above → seventh. Bars 7-8: sixth → fourth → fifth → third || second → root → third → root.
pitchConstraint:arch_phraseLength:8bar.watchOut: Without a false peak, an eight-bar arch becomes boring — the listener predicts the top note too early. Always insert a small dip before the real summit to refresh the climb.

pitchConstraint:seed_phraseLength:1bar.interaction: A one-bar seed is the tightest version of this game: three notes repeated twice in a bar, with one swap per repeat. Because the bar is short, the listener actually hears the pattern — they register the original shape and then the substitution. It's almost like a magic trick. Pick a seed that's easy to sing so the change stands out clearly.
steps: Choose three scale degrees for your seed — name them out loud (e.g., root, third, fifth).
Play the three notes on beats 1, 2, 3 of the bar.
On beat 4, play the same first note of the seed or start the swapped version.
Across repetitions, change exactly one seed note at a time.
Rest and evaluate: did the swap feel deliberate?
examples: Seed = root, third, fifth. Bar: root → third → fifth → (rest). Next bar: root → third → sixth → (rest) — fifth swapped to sixth.
Seed = second, fourth, sixth. Bar: second → fourth → sixth → (rest). Next bar: second → fourth → seventh → (rest) — sixth swapped to seventh.
Seed = root, fourth, seventh. Bar: root → fourth → seventh → (rest). Next bar: second → fourth → seventh → (rest) — root swapped to second.
pitchConstraint:seed_phraseLength:1bar.watchOut: One bar is so short you may swap two notes at once just to fit new rhythms. Resist — the whole point is one change per repeat, so the ear can track it.

pitchConstraint:seed_phraseLength:2bar.interaction: Two bars lets you state the seed in bar one and vary it in bar two with exactly one note changed. This is the classic call-and-response feel built into a single phrase. The listener hears 'original idea, slightly new idea' and their brain loves the near-repetition. You have room for rhythm variation, but the pitch DNA stays ninety percent the same.
steps: Choose a three or four-note seed from the scale.
Bar 1: play the seed clearly, with a rest at the end.
Bar 2: play the seed with exactly one note replaced by a different scale degree.
Keep rhythm similar between the two bars so the pitch swap is what stands out.
Repeat the exercise changing a different note each round.
examples: Seed = root, third, fifth, sixth. Bar 1: root → third → fifth → sixth. Bar 2: root → third → fifth → seventh (sixth swapped).
Seed = second, fourth, fifth. Bar 1: second → fourth → fifth → (rest). Bar 2: second → third → fifth → (rest) (fourth swapped).
Seed = third, fifth, seventh. Bar 1: third → fifth → seventh → (rest). Bar 2: third → sixth → seventh → (rest) (fifth swapped).
pitchConstraint:seed_phraseLength:2bar.watchOut: The risk at two bars is that the variation drifts away from the seed entirely. If more than one note changes, it's no longer a seed variation — it's a new idea. Stay strict: one swap.

pitchConstraint:seed_phraseLength:4bar.interaction: Four bars is the sweet spot for seed work — state the seed twice, then vary it twice, each time with a single swap. You get a proper theme-and-variations in miniature. Because the listener has heard the original twice before any change, the first swap feels like a real musical event. The second swap reinforces the evolution. This is how motifs are actually built in folk and pop songwriting.
steps: Choose a four-note seed with a memorable shape.
Bars 1-2: state the seed clearly twice in a row, identical both times.
Bar 3: repeat the seed with one note swapped.
Bar 4: repeat the bar 3 version with a different note swapped.
Land the final note on a scale anchor like root or third.
Sing the whole thing back — the DNA should still be recognizable.
examples: Seed = root, third, fifth, third. Bar 1: root-third-fifth-third. Bar 2: root-third-fifth-third. Bar 3: root-third-sixth-third (fifth→sixth). Bar 4: root-fourth-sixth-third (third→fourth in slot 2).

Seed = fifth, sixth, root-above, fifth. Bar 1: fifth-sixth-root-above-fifth. Bar 2: fifth-sixth-root-above-fifth. Bar 3: fifth-seventh-root-above-fifth (sixth→seventh). Bar 4: fifth-seventh-second-above-fifth (root-above→second-above).
Seed = root, fourth, third, second. Bar 1: root-fourth-third-second. Bar 2: root-fourth-third-second. Bar 3: root-fifth-third-second (fourth→fifth). Bar 4: root-fifth-third-root (second→root).
pitchConstraint:seed_phraseLength:4bar.watchOut: Four bars tempts you to vary rhythm and pitch simultaneously, which breaks the exercise. Keep rhythm stable so every swap is heard as a pitch choice, not a musical accident.

pitchConstraint:seed_phraseLength:8bar.interaction: An eight-bar seed phrase is a full evolutionary sequence — state the seed, then variation after variation, each with one new note swapped. By the end of bar eight, you've walked the seed through four or five generations and it's noticeably different from where you started. The trick is that every transition is small, so the journey feels organic. This is how folk songs develop over verses in a single melodic arc.
steps: Pick a strong three or four-note seed and play it in bars 1-2 unchanged.
Bar 3: first variation — one note swapped.
Bar 4: second variation — carry forward bar 3 and swap a different note.
Bar 5: third variation — carry forward bar 4 and swap again.
Bars 6-7: fourth and fifth variations — keep the chain going.
Bar 8: resolve by landing on a scale-degree anchor, even if it's a swap.
examples: Seed = root, third, fifth. Bars 1-2: root-third-fifth twice. Bar 3: root-third-sixth (5→6). Bar 4: root-fourth-sixth (3→4). Bar 5: second-fourth-sixth (r→2). Bar 6: second-fourth-seventh (6→7). Bar 7: second-fifth-seventh (4→5). Bar 8: root-fifth-seventh landing on root.

Seed = third, fifth, seventh. Bars 1-2: third-fifth-seventh twice. Bar 3: third-fifth-root-above (7→R). Bar 4: third-sixth-root-above (5→6). Bar 5: fourth-sixth-root-above (3→4). Bar 6: fourth-sixth-second-above (R→2). Bar 7: fourth-seventh-second-above (6→7). Bar 8: third-seventh-second-above resolving to third.
pitchConstraint:seed_phraseLength:8bar.watchOut: Across eight bars, you'll lose track of what the seed was — the chain drifts so far that the final bar has no DNA left. Write the seed down before you start so you can still hear it in bar eight.

pitchConstraint:forbidden_phraseLength:1.interaction: One bar with notes off-limits is a great constraint warm-up: you only play four notes, so every single one has to avoid the forbidden set. If you forbid the root and fifth, suddenly the third and sixth become your new home. The short container makes the absence obvious — the bar sounds 'wrong' in a specific, learnable way, which teaches you what those forbidden notes were actually doing for you.
steps: Name your forbidden notes by scale degree (e.g., root and fifth).
Pick four legal notes for the bar.
Play them as quarter notes.
If you accidentally hit a forbidden note, stop and restart.
Rest a beat and note what the phrase felt like without the forbidden note.
examples: Forbidden: root. Legal phrase: third → second → fourth → third.
Forbidden: root and fifth. Legal phrase: second → fourth → sixth → third.
Forbidden: third. Legal phrase: root → second → fourth → fifth.
pitchConstraint:forbidden_phraseLength:1.watchOut: One bar tempts you to default to the forbidden note out of habit — especially if it's the root. Preview the bar mentally before you play so your hand doesn't fall into the old pattern.

pitchConstraint:forbidden_phraseLength:2.interaction: Two bars with forbidden notes gives you enough room to build a complete sentence that has to navigate around the missing pitches. You'll notice the phrase naturally tilts toward whichever scale degrees are 'available.' If you forbid the root and third, your phrase tends to center on the fifth or sixth, which creates an unusual modal color. That's the whole lesson — constraints reveal hidden palettes.
steps: Forbid one to three scale degrees by name.
Bar 1: play a four-note idea using only legal notes.
Bar 2: answer it with a four-note idea, still only legal notes.
Land the final note on a legal scale-degree anchor.
Reflect: did the phrase sound like it was in a different mode?
examples: Forbidden: root. Bar 1: third → fourth → fifth → third. Bar 2: second → fourth → sixth → fifth.
Forbidden: third and seventh. Bar 1: root → second → fourth → fifth. Bar 2: sixth → fifth → fourth → second.
Forbidden: fifth. Bar 1: root → third → fourth → sixth. Bar 2: seventh → sixth → fourth → third.
pitchConstraint:forbidden_phraseLength:2.watchOut: Two bars is where the hand cheats — a single beat of a forbidden note sneaks in. Play slowly and audibly name each note to stay honest.

pitchConstraint:forbidden_phraseLength:4.interaction: Four bars of forbidden-note melody starts to feel like a proper alternative scale. With about twelve to sixteen notes all avoiding the same one to three pitches, you're effectively composing in a subset of the scale. This reveals which notes are truly 'functional anchors' and which are just habit. Forbid the root and third, and you'll find yourself composing a floating, rootless phrase that resolves in unexpected ways.
steps: Name the forbidden notes before playing.
Bar 1: establish the legal vocabulary with a four-note idea.
Bar 2: develop it with a turn or dip.
Bar 3: climb to a peak using only legal notes.
Bar 4: resolve to the strongest legal scale degree you can find.
Listen back for any forbidden note slipping in.
examples: Forbidden: root. Bar 1: third-second-fourth-third. Bar 2: fifth-fourth-sixth-fifth. Bar 3: seventh-sixth-seventh-fourth-above (peak). Bar 4: sixth-fifth-fourth-third.
Forbidden: third and fifth. Bar 1: root-second-fourth-second. Bar 2: root-fourth-sixth-fourth. Bar 3: seventh-sixth-seventh-root-above (peak). Bar 4: seventh-sixth-fourth-root.
Forbidden: fifth and seventh. Bar 1: root-third-second-fourth. Bar 2: third-second-fourth-sixth. Bar 3: root-above-sixth-third-above-root-above (peak). Bar 4: sixth-fourth-third-root.
pitchConstraint:forbidden_phraseLength:4.watchOut: Over four bars, you'll feel the gravitational pull of the forbidden note — especially at the final resolution. Pre-choose your landing note to defend against the reflex.

pitchConstraint:forbidden_phraseLength:8.interaction: Eight bars of forbidden-note melody is basically composing in a reduced scale. With two or three notes gone from your vocabulary, you have to develop the remaining ones into a full arc — peaks, valleys, resolutions — without the usual crutches. The longer you play, the more your ear adapts, and by bar six you're hearing the 'available' notes as the new home. This is a great way to discover modes you didn't know you had.
steps: Name the forbidden scale degrees.
Map the eight bars into four two-bar sub-phrases.
Sub-phrase 1: introduce the legal vocabulary, center on the strongest legal note.
Sub-phrase 2: climb to a first peak using legal notes only.
Sub-phrase 3: dip into a valley, still within the legal set.
Sub-phrase 4: reach the main peak and resolve to the strongest legal scale degree.
examples: Forbidden: root. Bars 1-2: third-second-fourth-third || fifth-fourth-sixth-fifth. Bars 3-4: seventh-sixth-seventh-root-above (peak 1) || sixth-fifth-fourth-sixth. Bars 5-6: fourth-second-fourth-second (valley) || fifth-sixth-seventh-sixth. Bars 7-8: seventh-root-above-second-above-root-above (peak 2) || seventh-sixth-fifth-third.

Forbidden: root and fifth. Bars 1-2: third-second-fourth-sixth || seventh-sixth-fourth-third. Bars 3-4: sixth-seventh-root-above-second-above (peak 1) || root-above-seventh-sixth-fourth. Bars 5-6: third-second-fourth-second (valley) || third-fourth-sixth-seventh. Bars 7-8: second-above-third-above-second-above-root-above (peak 2) || sixth-fourth-third-third.
pitchConstraint:forbidden_phraseLength:8.watchOut: At eight bars, the forbidden notes start to feel arbitrary and you'll unconsciously add them back in. Re-read your forbidden list between sub-phrases. Discipline is the only way to stay in the alternate palette.

pitchConstraint:targetLanding_phraseLeng.interaction: One bar with a fixed landing note is the cleanest version of this exercise: you have three approach notes and a predetermined destination. Every choice is really a question — 'does this note set up the target?' The short bar forces commitment: you can't meander, you have to approach and arrive. The target becomes a magnetic point the phrase is pulled toward.
steps: Pick a target scale degree (third and fifth are satisfying landings).
Choose three approach notes that set up the target.
Play approach-approach-approach-TARGET on quarter notes.
Rest after the target so it rings.
Repeat with different approach paths to the same target.
examples: Target = third. Fifth → fourth → second → third.
Target = fifth. Root → third → fourth → fifth.
Target = root. Sixth → fourth → second → root.
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_phraseLeng.watchOut: At one bar, the target can feel formulaic — you just race to it. Slow down, and make sure the approach notes build expectation rather than just filling time.

pitchConstraint:targetLanding_phraseLeng.interaction: Two bars of target-landing gives you a real approach — six or seven notes that snake toward the landing and then commit. The target should feel inevitable when it arrives, not accidental. Practice approaching the same target from different directions: from below via stepwise motion, from above with a leap, from a distance with a wide scoop. Same destination, totally different emotional meaning.
steps: Choose a target scale degree and commit to landing on it at bar two, beat four.
Bar 1: set up the target with a four-note contour.
Bar 2: approach and land on the target with three lead-in notes.
Try the same target three different times with three different approaches.
Evaluate which approach made the landing feel strongest.
examples: Target = third. Bar 1: root → second → fourth → fifth. Bar 2: fourth → second → fourth → third.
Target = fifth. Bar 1: root → third → second → fourth. Bar 2: sixth → seventh → sixth → fifth.
Target = root. Bar 1: fifth → seventh → sixth → fourth. Bar 2: second → third → second → root.
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_phraseLeng.watchOut: The trap is that you aim for the target and then cheat by landing on it early (say, beat three of bar two instead of beat four). The last beat is the target beat — no earlier, no later.

pitchConstraint:targetLanding_phraseLeng.interaction: Four bars of target-landing is where the exercise becomes composition. You have three bars to wander, develop, tease the target, maybe almost land on it by accident — and then bar four commits to the real arrival. The target gains weight because you've delayed it. Try bar three approaching the target by one scale step and then turning away, so bar four's arrival feels like finally giving in.
steps: Name your target scale degree (root, third, or fifth are the most satisfying).
Bars 1-2: explore freely, establishing a scale vocabulary.
Bar 3: approach near the target but deliberately avoid it.
Bar 4: commit to the target, landing on the final beat.
Repeat the whole phrase with the same target but a different bar-3 tease.
examples: Target = root. Bar 1: third-fifth-fourth-sixth. Bar 2: fifth-fourth-second-fourth. Bar 3: second-root-wait-second-third-second (hovers near root). Bar 4: fourth-third-second-root.
Target = fifth. Bar 1: root-third-fourth-third. Bar 2: sixth-fourth-third-second. Bar 3: fourth-fifth-wait-sixth-fourth-sixth. Bar 4: seventh-sixth-fourth-fifth.
Target = third. Bar 1: fifth-fourth-second-fourth. Bar 2: sixth-fifth-seventh-fifth. Bar 3: fourth-third-wait-second-fourth-fifth. Bar 4: root-second-fourth-third.
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_phraseLeng.watchOut: Four bars gives you enough rope to forget the target exists. Write the target on a sticky note next to your instrument so every bar is asking 'am I still heading there?'

pitchConstraint:targetLanding_phraseLeng.interaction: Eight bars with a fixed landing is an exercise in patience. You have sixteen to twenty beats of exploration before the target, which means the target has to be both delayed and foreshadowed. Tease it once in bar three, once in bar five, and finally commit in bar eight. The target becomes a North Star the whole phrase orbits. Done well, the final landing feels like an arrival that was earned across the entire arc.
steps: Choose the target scale degree and write it down.
Bars 1-2: open with scale vocabulary, establish a tonal center.
Bar 3: brush past the target (one note) without landing.
Bars 4-5: climb to a peak, brush the target a second time.
Bars 6-7: descend and gather energy for the landing.
Bar 8: commit fully to the target on the final beat.
examples: Target = root. Bars 1-2: third-fifth-fourth-sixth || seventh-sixth-fifth-fourth. Bar 3: third-second-root (brush)-third. Bars 4-5: fourth-sixth-seventh-root-above (peak) || seventh-sixth-root (brush 2)-second. Bars 6-7: fourth-third-fifth-fourth || third-second-fourth-second. Bar 8: fourth-third-second-root.

Target = fifth. Bars 1-2: root-third-fourth-third || sixth-fourth-second-fourth. Bar 3: sixth-fifth (brush)-sixth-seventh. Bars 4-5: root-above-seventh-second-above-root-above (peak) || seventh-sixth-fifth (brush 2)-sixth. Bars 6-7: seventh-sixth-fourth-third || second-fourth-sixth-fourth. Bar 8: seventh-sixth-fourth-fifth.
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_phraseLeng.watchOut: Across eight bars, the target loses urgency — you stop caring by bar five. The brush-bys in bars three and five are there specifically to keep the target in your ear. Don't skip them.

pitchConstraint:questionAnswer_phraseLen.interaction: A one-bar question and a one-bar answer is the tightest Q&A you can do — two micro-fragments back to back. The question rises (ending on an unresolved scale degree like the second or seventh), the answer falls (ending on a stable scale degree like the root or third). Because each side is only four notes, every note is structural. This is great for learning the Q/A feel without the overhead of long phrases.
steps: Question bar: pick a low starting note and rise through four notes, ending on an unresolved degree.
Rest for a half-beat to mark the question.
Answer bar: start near the top and descend through four notes, ending on the root or third.
Sing the two bars back — does the answer feel like a reply to the question?
Repeat with the same question and different answers.
examples: Q: root → third → fifth → seventh. A: sixth → fourth → second → root.
Q: third → fifth → sixth → second-above. A: root-above → sixth → fourth → third.
Q: second → fourth → sixth → seventh. A: fifth → third → second → root.
pitchConstraint:questionAnswer_phraseLen.watchOut: Beginners make the question too long and the answer too short. Keep them symmetric — one bar each — and the feel becomes obvious. An unbalanced Q/A sounds like a mistake.

pitchConstraint:questionAnswer_phraseLen.interaction: Two-bar question, two-bar answer is the classic pop-song phrase structure. The question has enough room to establish a rising contour, and the answer has enough room to resolve it with a descent. The question typically lands on the second, seventh, or fifth; the answer lands firmly on the root or third. This is how verse couplets are built — once you have the Q/A rhythm in your body, songwriting stops being mysterious.
steps: Question bars 1-2: build a rising contour ending on an unresolved scale degree.
Answer bars 3-4: build a descending or settling contour ending on the root or third.
Keep the rhythms similar between Q and A so the pitch contrast stands out.
Play the four bars, then sing just the answer — does it feel like it's 'replying'?
Try three different answers to the same question.
examples: Q: root-third-second-fourth || third-fifth-fourth-seventh. A: sixth-fourth-fifth-third || second-fourth-second-root.
Q: third-second-fourth-third || fifth-sixth-seventh-second-above. A: root-above-seventh-sixth-fifth || fourth-third-second-third.
Q: fifth-fourth-sixth-fifth || seventh-sixth-root-above-second-above. A: root-above-seventh-sixth-fifth || fourth-third-fourth-root.
pitchConstraint:questionAnswer_phraseLen.watchOut: The mistake here is making the answer too similar to the question — same contour, same landing. The answer has to actually resolve what the question opened, which means a different ending scale degree and ideally a different shape.

pitchConstraint:questionAnswer_phraseLen.interaction: Four-bar question followed by four-bar answer gives you real compositional room. Each side becomes a developed paragraph. The question has space for a small internal arc — maybe it rises, dips, then rises again to an unresolved landing on the second or seventh. The answer then unfolds its own arc — perhaps a small climb, a descent, and a landing on the root. This is verse-form writing in miniature: two balanced paragraphs having a conversation.
steps: Question bars 1-4: build a four-bar contour with an internal dip and a rising landing.
The question must end on an unresolved scale degree (second, seventh, or fourth).
Answer bars 5-8: build a four-bar contour with an internal peak and a falling resolution.
The answer must land on the root, third, or fifth on the final beat.
Play the whole eight-bar cycle twice and notice how the Q/A balance feels.
Swap one note in the answer and see if the balance shifts.
examples: Q bars 1-4: root-third-second-fourth || third-fifth-fourth-sixth || fifth-fourth-third-fifth || sixth-fifth-seventh-second-above. A bars 5-8: root-above-seventh-sixth-fourth || fifth-fourth-third-second || fourth-third-fifth-fourth || third-second-fourth-root.

Q bars 1-4: third-fourth-fifth-third || sixth-fifth-seventh-sixth || root-above-seventh-sixth-root-above || second-above-root-above-second-above-fourth. A bars 5-8: root-above-seventh-sixth-fifth || fourth-sixth-fifth-fourth || third-second-fourth-third || second-root-second-root.
pitchConstraint:questionAnswer_phraseLen.watchOut: Four-bar Q/A tempts you to make each side a mini-phrase with its own internal resolution. Don't — the question should NOT resolve, that's the whole point. Save resolution for the answer.

pitchConstraint:questionAnswer_phraseLen.interaction: An eight-bar question answered by an eight-bar answer is a full sixteen-bar form — the length of a whole song section. The question has room to develop a complete arc with a peak, a near-resolution, and a final rising tension. The answer mirrors it with its own arc, climb, peak, and definitive landing. This is antecedent-consequent phrasing at its most expansive, and once you can do it, you can write entire verses from scratch.
steps: Question (bars 1-8): build a long arc with a rise, a false peak around bar 3, a dip, and a rising landing that stops on an unresolved scale degree.
The question must end in the air — on the second, seventh, or fourth.
Answer (bars 9-16): mirror the question's arc shape but with a descent bias and a definitive landing on the root.
The answer's internal peak should echo the question's peak position (e.g., if question peaks in bar 3, answer peaks in bar 11).
Play the full sixteen bars and listen for the relationship between Q and A.
Revise the answer if it doesn't feel like a reply to the question.
examples: Q bars 1-8: root-third-second-fourth || third-fifth-fourth-sixth || fifth-seventh-sixth-root-above (peak) || seventh-sixth-fifth-fourth || third-fifth-fourth-sixth || fifth-fourth-third-fifth || sixth-seventh-sixth-root-above || second-above-root-above-second-above-second-above (unresolved). A bars 9-16: root-above-seventh-sixth-fifth || fourth-sixth-fifth-seventh || sixth-root-above-second-above-third-above (peak) || second-above-root-above-seventh-sixth || fifth-fourth-third-fifth || fourth-third-second-fourth || third-second-fourth-third || second-root-third-root.

Q bars 1-8: fifth-fourth-sixth-fifth || seventh-sixth-root-above-seventh || second-above-third-above-second-above-root-above (peak) || seventh-sixth-fifth-sixth || seventh-root-above-seventh-sixth || fifth-fourth-sixth-fifth || sixth-seventh-root-above-seventh || second-above-root-above-second-above-fourth (unresolved). A bars 9-16: root-above-seventh-sixth-fifth || fourth-sixth-fifth-fourth || third-fifth-fourth-sixth (peak mirror) || fifth-fourth-third-fifth || fourth-third-second-fourth || third-second-fourth-third || second-root-second-fourth || third-second-fourth-root.
pitchConstraint:questionAnswer_phraseLen.watchOut: Over sixteen bars total, the relationship between question and answer dissolves unless you consciously mirror the arc positions. Mark where your question peaks and make the answer peak in the parallel spot — that's what makes eight-bar Q/A feel like one connected thought instead of two random phrases.

pitchConstraint:leaps_register:chest.interaction: Leaps in chest voice is a grounded gymnastics act. You're asking the sternum and ribs to vibrate on one note, then rocket to another without losing the floor underneath. The temptation is to push harder on the high note — but chest register collapses when you reach with the throat instead of the breath. This pair teaches you to leap from the belly, not the jaw.
steps: Before singing, place a hand flat on your sternum and feel the buzz on a low tonic drone — that's your chest reference.
Sing the low note of a leap, let it vibrate into the ribs for a full beat, then leap upward while keeping the hand on the sternum feeling for buzz.
If the hand buzz disappears on the high note, you flipped into head or throat — reset and leap again with more belly engagement, not more volume.
Between leaps, exhale fully to reset your support — no chest-voice leap survives on stale air.
examples: Tonic low, then leap up a fifth, fall back to tonic
Third low, leap to flat-seventh high, land on fifth
Root, up-a-sixth, down-a-third, root
pitchConstraint:leaps_register:chest.watchOut: The classic failure is squeezing the throat to manufacture the high note. If you feel any pinch at the larynx or hear the tone thin out, you've lost chest connection — drop the note down and rebuild from the ribs.

pitchConstraint:leaps_register:head.interaction: Leaps into head voice is a precision test in light placement. Head voice wants to hover — leaps demand sudden repositioning without cranking volume or dropping back into chest. The pitch accuracy challenge is real: the mask doesn't give you the same muscular feedback as chest, so you have to trust resonance location instead of force.
steps: Warm up head voice first on a gentle descending slide — feel the buzz move from cheeks to bridge of nose.
Sing the low note of a leap at a medium volume, then leap upward imagining the sound rising into the space above your eyes, not the throat.
Keep the jaw loose and the tongue forward — tension in either will crack the leap or pull you back to chest.
If a leap lands sharp or flat, don't muscle the correction — re-aim the resonance higher or lower in the mask and re-leap.
examples: Fifth, leap up a fourth to the upper tonic, fall to third
Third, leap up a sixth, step down to second
Tonic upper, leap down a fifth, leap back up
pitchConstraint:leaps_register:head.watchOut: Pushing volume to 'make' the leap land. Head voice amplifies with resonance placement, not breath pressure — if you feel your belly clench harder on the high note, you're forcing and the pitch will drift.

pitchConstraint:leaps_register:mixed.interaction: Mixed voice over leaps is the holy grail of bridging — you're jumping across the passaggio zone without any audible flip. This pair specifically trains the blend muscles: every leap crosses a register boundary, and mix voice has to meet you at both ends. Done right, it sounds like one unified voice; done wrong, it sounds like two voices stitched together.
steps: Find your passaggio zone on a siren first — notice where the voice wants to flip, and hang out there.
Sing the low note of a leap with a slightly brightened chest tone, then leap across the passaggio keeping the vowel shape identical.
Feel for vibration in both the sternum AND the mask simultaneously on the high note — that's the mix.
If you hear a crack or gear shift, slow down and practice the leap as a slow portamento first to map the muscle coordination.
examples: Low third, leap up a sixth through passaggio, sustain
Tonic, leap up a fifth across break, fall a third
Fifth low, leap up a fourth, leap up another third
pitchConstraint:leaps_register:mixed.watchOut: Picking a side. If the low note is pure chest and the high note is pure head, you've failed the mix — both notes should feel like the same hybrid tone, just at different pitches.

pitchConstraint:leaps_register:falsetto.interaction: Leaps in falsetto is a clean-air test. Falsetto has no chest connection — it's pure airflow through loose vocal folds — so leaps become a question of whether you can aim the airstream without the fold coordination snapping. The specific challenge here: most singers unconsciously tighten when they leap, which kills falsetto instantly.
steps: Start with a breathy sigh in falsetto to feel the loose, airy setup — cheeks soft, throat open.
Sing the low falsetto note with relaxed folds, then leap upward by sending more air, not more muscle.
On the upper note, check that your throat feels as open as it did on the low note — any squeeze and you're out of falsetto.
Between leaps, take a full low belly breath — falsetto eats air fast and leaps burn even more.
examples: Falsetto mid, leap up a fourth, fall a second
Upper tonic falsetto, leap down a fifth, leap back
Third falsetto, leap up a sixth, sustain and fade
pitchConstraint:leaps_register:falsetto.watchOut: Going pitchy on the leap because you tried to muscle it. Falsetto pitch is controlled by fold tension plus airflow — if you tighten to reach, the pitch sharpens and the tone hardens out of falsetto into a yelly head voice.

pitchConstraint:leaps_register:breathy.interaction: Leaps in a breathy tone is the intimacy-acrobatics combo. You're keeping the air-to-tone ratio high — more whisper than sing — while jumping between notes. The risk: leaps tempt you to commit harder, which means more fold closure, which kills the breathy quality. This pair teaches you to leap at close-mic volume.
steps: Set up the breathy tone by speaking a word on a single pitch with extra air — notice the warm, unfocused feel in the throat.
Sing the low note at that same breath-heavy setting, then leap upward keeping the air ratio constant.
Imagine the microphone two inches from your lips — you're not projecting, you're confiding, even on the leap.
Let the breath lead, not the pitch — send air first, let the note follow.
examples: Whispered tonic, leap up a fourth with more air
Third breathy, leap up a fifth, fall a step
Fifth, leap up a third, sigh down a fifth
pitchConstraint:leaps_register:breathy.watchOut: Cleaning up the tone on the leap because it feels safer. If the high note suddenly sounds clear and focused, you abandoned breathy — pull it back and over-breathe the next attempt.

pitchConstraint:stepwise_register:chest.interaction: Stepwise lines in chest voice are a foundation-builder. Unlike leaps, steps give the ribs and sternum time to stay engaged — the challenge is keeping that chest buzz consistent as the pitch moves, without the voice getting heavier or thinning out on each successive note. This is about walking, not jumping — steady sternum vibration all the way.
steps: Place a hand on your sternum and hum a stepwise descent — feel the buzz stay constant note to note.
Walk up a five-note scale in chest voice, checking that each note has the same floor of vibration.
If the top note feels thinner, back off on the climb — you should arrive at each step without straining, not reaching.
Keep the jaw free and the shoulders low — chest voice gets locked up by neck tension fast.
examples: Tonic up to fifth stepwise, back down
Third down to tonic, back up to fourth
Fifth down to second, rest, climb back
pitchConstraint:stepwise_register:chest.watchOut: Adding weight as you ascend. Chest voice wants to get heavier note by note — that's a trap. Each step should feel the same weight, not more, or you'll push yourself into a yell by the top of the scale.

pitchConstraint:stepwise_register:head.interaction: Stepwise in head voice is about agility in the mask. The notes are close together, so you can't use placement shifts to navigate them — you have to stay parked in the same resonance spot while the pitch moves around inside it. This pair builds flexibility without losing the floaty quality.
steps: Hum a stepwise phrase with lips together, feeling the buzz fixed in the cheeks and nose bridge.
Open to a vowel and sing the same phrase, keeping the buzz location unchanged.
Let the tongue do the pitch work — keep the throat still, the soft palate lifted, and move only what has to move.
If a note slides into chest, it's because you let the placement drop — reset to the mask and try again.
examples: Upper tonic down to fifth stepwise
Third up to sixth, back down
Fifth up to upper tonic, fall to third
pitchConstraint:stepwise_register:head.watchOut: Letting the voice drop into chest on the lower steps of the phrase. Head voice can extend surprisingly low if you keep the placement — don't bail into chest just because the pitch descends.

pitchConstraint:stepwise_register:mixed.interaction: Stepwise through mixed voice is a seamless blend test across your whole working range. Each step is a tiny opportunity to let the blend shift — and your job is to not let it. This combo teaches you to hold mixed voice steady as the pitch walks across the passaggio, so no single step sounds different from its neighbors.
steps: Pick a scale that crosses your passaggio zone and sing it stepwise with a siren first — map the terrain.
Sing the scale on a neutral vowel, monitoring for any step that sounds brighter, darker, or thinner than its neighbors.
Feel for simultaneous buzz in the sternum and the mask on every note — the ratio should stay constant.
If one step breaks the blend, isolate it with its neighbors and practice the three-note group until it's invisible.
examples: Fifth up to upper third stepwise, back down
Third up to sixth, crossing the break
Tonic up to upper tonic stepwise over a scale
pitchConstraint:stepwise_register:mixed.watchOut: The one rogue note. Mixed voice often has one pitch in the passaggio where the blend wobbles — find yours and drill it specifically, or your stepwise lines will always have that one weak link.

pitchConstraint:stepwise_register:falset.interaction: Stepwise falsetto is a delicate coordination exercise. Falsetto can feel wobbly on sustained notes — stepwise motion gives you something to move through instead of dwelling, which often actually stabilizes it. The challenge is keeping the fold setup loose and airy as each new step arrives.
steps: Sigh down an octave in falsetto to feel the loose, hooty quality.
Sing a stepwise descent in that same setup, letting each note get its own small puff of air.
Keep the cheeks soft and the throat open — no gripping, even on the lower steps where it'll be tempting.
Breathe between every two or three notes if needed — falsetto runs out of air fast.
examples: Upper tonic down a five-note scale
Third up to sixth stepwise in falsetto
Fifth down to tonic, rest, repeat higher
pitchConstraint:stepwise_register:falset.watchOut: Falsetto wobble on the sustained end of the phrase. If the last note of your stepwise line warbles, you ran out of breath support — breathe lower and start over.

pitchConstraint:stepwise_register:breath.interaction: Stepwise breathy lines are the intimate-speech combo. The steps keep the melody close to speech contour, and the breathy tone keeps the delivery hushed — together they sound like someone murmuring a secret in your ear. This pair trains micro-control of breath distribution across a phrase.
steps: Speak the melody first on your normal speaking pitch, with extra breath — get the warm murmur quality.
Now sing the stepwise line keeping that same air ratio — breath first, tone second.
Feel for warm, unfocused throat — if it clicks into focused tone, you've left breathy.
Pace your air: don't dump all the breath on the first note, spread it across the phrase.
examples: Third down to tonic stepwise, breathy murmur
Fifth up to seventh, back down, all airy
Tonic up to third, back to tonic, whispered
pitchConstraint:stepwise_register:breath.watchOut: Running out of air mid-phrase because breathy burns through breath fast. If the last note loses air and clicks into clear tone, your phrases are too long for the breath budget — shorten them.

pitchConstraint:arch_register:chest.interaction: Arch-shaped phrases in chest voice are about climbing with support and descending with release. The arch gives you a natural dramatic shape — the rise builds tension, the peak is the moment, and the descent lets you breathe out emotionally. In chest voice, the peak tests whether your support can handle the top of the arch without losing the floor.
steps: Plan the phrase: know where the peak is before you start, because that's where you'll need the most support.
Begin the climb with hand on sternum, feeling the buzz get a little stronger as you ascend.
At the peak, send breath upward from the belly — don't squeeze the throat to hit it.
On the descent, release tension but not support — let the voice settle back into the low buzz.
examples: Tonic up to fifth, peak at fifth, back to tonic
Third up to seventh, down to tonic
Fifth up to upper third, down to second
pitchConstraint:arch_register:chest.watchOut: Peaking too early. If you hit your loudest or strongest note before the top of the arch, you've got nowhere to go — the peak collapses. Save something for the top.

pitchConstraint:arch_register:head.interaction: Arch phrases in head voice are all about the peak moment as a shimmer of placement. The rise is a gradual climb in resonance location — you're moving the buzz upward through the mask — and the descent brings it back down without letting it drop into chest. The arch should feel like a wave cresting in your cheekbones.
steps: Start the arch on a low head-voice note, buzz centered in the cheeks.
