As you climb, let the resonance drift upward toward the bridge of the nose and the space above.
At the peak, keep the sound light — the peak is about clarity, not volume.
Descend by letting the buzz drift back down into the cheeks, never dropping into chest.
examples: Third up to upper tonic, back to third
Fifth up to upper fifth, down to third
Upper tonic up to upper third, down to fifth
pitchConstraint:arch_register:head.watchOut: Going shouty at the peak because head voice feels 'too small.' If you push the peak into volume, it snaps out of head and into yelled mix — stay light, trust the resonance.

pitchConstraint:arch_register:mixed.interaction: Arch phrases in mixed voice are the classic pop ballad shape — they climb through the passaggio, peak somewhere in the blend zone, and return home. This combo is deeply practical: it's what most emotional song climaxes actually sound like. The challenge is keeping the blend consistent on the ascent, at the peak, and on the release down.
steps: Map the arch across your passaggio first with a siren — feel where the blend has to hold the hardest.
On the rise, keep the vowel shape open and let the buzz split evenly between sternum and mask.
At the peak, lean slightly into the mask without abandoning the chest connection — this is the mixed-voice moment.
On the descent, release the mask brightness gradually, but don't dump all the way into pure chest until the final note.
examples: Tonic low up to upper third, back to tonic
Third up to upper fifth, back to third
Fifth up to upper tonic, down to second
pitchConstraint:arch_register:mixed.watchOut: Losing the mix at the peak under pressure. If the highest note flips into either pure head or belted chest, the arch breaks in the middle — slow the phrase down and practice the peak specifically until it holds the blend.

pitchConstraint:arch_register:falsetto.interaction: Arch phrases in falsetto are ghostly, dreamlike — the rise is airy, the peak is a whisper-cry, the descent sighs back down. This pair builds delicate dynamic control: you can't peak with volume in falsetto, so the peak becomes a shift in air and emotion rather than decibels. It's the 'haunted lullaby' combo.
steps: Set up falsetto with a loose sigh — feel the open, unforced quality in the throat.
Begin the climb with constant air, letting the pitch rise on the same breath stream.
At the peak, soften further instead of pushing — the peak is the thinnest, most delicate moment of the arch.
Descend into a long, breathy sigh — let the phrase fade instead of ending.
examples: Fifth up to upper third, sigh back down
Third up to sixth, fade to third
Upper tonic up to upper fifth, descend to tonic
pitchConstraint:arch_register:falsetto.watchOut: Trying to crescendo the arch like in chest or mix. Falsetto doesn't peak with loudness — it peaks with intimacy. If you push volume at the top, the falsetto snaps and you land in a hard head voice.

pitchConstraint:arch_register:breathy.interaction: Arch phrases in breathy voice are the cinematic-whisper combo — the whole phrase stays close-mic intimate while still having a dramatic shape. The challenge is making the peak feel like a peak without abandoning the breathy quality, which usually means letting more air through rather than more tone.
steps: Speak the phrase first at your softest breathy volume to feel the shape.
Sing the ascent keeping the air ratio high — more breath than tone, consistently.
At the peak, add breath, not pressure — the peak gets more air, not more voice.
Descend by reducing the pitch but keeping the same whispered quality to the final note.
examples: Tonic up to fifth, back to tonic, all breathy
Third up to sixth, down to second, whispered
Fifth up to upper tonic, back to third, hushed
pitchConstraint:arch_register:breathy.watchOut: Accidentally clicking into clear tone at the peak because it feels like 'the important note.' If the peak suddenly sounds focused while the rest of the phrase is airy, you broke the breathy spell — stay whispered throughout.

pitchConstraint:seed_register:chest.interaction: Seed motifs in chest voice are a study in grounded repetition. You pick three notes, sing them, then change one — the other two stay the same, so the shifted note stands out against a familiar frame. In chest voice, the body gets to memorize the buzz of each seed position, so when a note changes you feel it physically as well as hear it.
steps: Sing the seed three notes with hand on sternum, memorizing how each pitch buzzes in the ribs.
Change one note and notice how the body feedback shifts — the new pitch has a different resonance feel.
Keep the unchanged notes identical in weight and placement — only the changed note should sound different.
Use breath to reset between each seed variation — don't let fatigue make the repetition sound heavier over time.
examples: Tonic third fifth, then tonic fourth fifth
Second fourth sixth, then second fourth seventh
Third fifth tonic, then third fifth seventh
pitchConstraint:seed_register:chest.watchOut: Letting the unchanged notes drift. The whole point of the seed is the contrast between the fixed frame and the moving note — if you unconsciously change the 'fixed' notes, the exercise loses its point. Anchor them in the body.

pitchConstraint:seed_register:head.interaction: Seed motifs in head voice are a placement-precision drill. Because head voice is all about resonance location, each seed note gets its own tiny spot in the mask — and when you change one note, you have to find a new spot without disturbing the others. This builds fine motor control for the resonance muscles.
steps: Sing the three seed notes in head voice, feeling where each one lands in the mask.
Change one note and consciously aim for its new resonance spot without moving the others.
Keep the throat and tongue still — only the soft palate and vowel shape should adjust.
Check that the two unchanged notes still feel identical in both pitch and placement.
examples: Fifth upper-tonic third, then fifth upper-tonic fourth
Third fifth seventh, then third fifth sixth
Upper tonic upper third fifth, then upper tonic upper second fifth
pitchConstraint:seed_register:head.watchOut: The placement of the changed note bleeding into the next repetition of the unchanged notes. Head voice is sensitive — if you over-adjust for the changed note, the next 'fixed' note will come out in the new placement by mistake.

pitchConstraint:seed_register:mixed.interaction: Seed motifs in mixed voice train you to hold the blend steady while pitch changes around it. Because each seed shares two notes with the previous one, you have a built-in reference — if the unchanged notes lose their mix quality, you know your technique slipped. This is a stability drill disguised as a musical game.
steps: Sing the three seed notes with attention to simultaneous sternum and mask buzz.
Change one note keeping the mix ratio identical on all three.
If the changed note wants to flip to pure chest or pure head, slow down and re-find the blend on it.
Between variations, exhale and reset — tension builds quickly in mix work.
examples: Third fifth upper-tonic, then third sixth upper-tonic
Second fourth sixth, then second fourth seventh
Fifth seventh upper-third, then fifth seventh upper-fourth
pitchConstraint:seed_register:mixed.watchOut: The changed note being the one that breaks the mix. The whole point is that every note — including the new one — holds the blend. If the changed note flips, you haven't built mixed voice in that pitch yet.

pitchConstraint:seed_register:falsetto.interaction: Seed motifs in falsetto are a quiet-control study. Falsetto doesn't project, so repetition with small changes becomes almost meditative — you're listening intently to tiny differences in a hushed delivery. This pair teaches detailed pitch discrimination in a register where the muscle cues are soft.
steps: Sigh into falsetto and find a loose, airy setup.
Sing the three seed notes with consistent air and open throat.
Change one note keeping the fold setup loose — the change should be a pitch adjustment, not a muscle adjustment.
Breathe between every seed variation — falsetto burns through air faster than it seems.
examples: Upper tonic third fifth, then upper tonic fourth fifth
Third fifth sixth, then third fifth seventh
Fifth seventh upper-tonic, then fifth sixth upper-tonic
pitchConstraint:seed_register:falsetto.watchOut: Tightening to 'control' the pitch change. Falsetto pitch is shaped by airflow and tension balance — if you squeeze to move one note, you'll knock the other two out of tune as collateral damage.

pitchConstraint:seed_register:breathy.interaction: Seed motifs in breathy voice are an exercise in subtle variation. The breathy quality is so soft that small changes feel huge — each new note in the seed is like a tiny new confession. This pair trains you to stay intimate while still finding musical variety through pitch change alone.
steps: Speak the seed notes first in whispered tone to feel the breath-heavy setup.
Sing the seed with high air ratio, every note warm and unfocused.
Change one note keeping the whispered quality identical — the pitch shifts, the tone doesn't.
Refill breath between every variation — breathy drains air fast and the phrases will die otherwise.
examples: Tonic third fifth whispered, then tonic third fourth
Third fifth seventh, then third fifth sixth
Second fourth sixth, then second fourth fifth
pitchConstraint:seed_register:breathy.watchOut: The changed note accidentally getting clear because you 'committed' to the new pitch. Breathy doesn't reward commitment in the throat — if the new note sounds focused while the others stay airy, you unconsciously pressed.

pitchConstraint:forbidden_register:chest.interaction: Forbidden notes in chest voice force you to navigate around a gap in your usual pitch vocabulary. Because chest voice is your most-used register, forbidden constraints often hit your favorite notes — suddenly you can't lean on the tonic, or the fifth, or wherever your voice naturally wants to land. This builds flexibility by removing safety nets.
steps: Identify the forbidden notes and sing them once so the body knows what to avoid.
Sing your phrase with hand on sternum, chest-voice buzz intact, while consciously steering around the forbidden pitches.
If you almost sing a forbidden note, don't panic — glide past it to the nearest allowed note and keep going.
Between phrases, release the jaw — forbidden constraints create tension in the planning muscles fast.
examples: Scale without the tonic, around the fifth
Phrase avoiding the third, resolving on the seventh
Line skipping the fourth, leaning on fifth
pitchConstraint:forbidden_register:chest.watchOut: Over-thinking and losing the chest buzz while you plan. If the voice thins out because your brain is busy avoiding notes, drop the tempo way down and keep the body connected — thinking kills tone.

pitchConstraint:forbidden_register:head.interaction: Forbidden notes in head voice test your aim. Head voice is precise but delicate — if you have to skip over a pitch, you need a clean placement jump without the forbidden note sneaking in as a passing tone. This pair trains deliberate resonance targeting.
steps: Warm up head voice and mentally mark the forbidden pitches as 'empty space.'
Sing your phrase aiming each note directly into its mask spot, never letting the voice slide through a forbidden zone.
Keep the jaw loose — tension from planning will drag you into the very notes you're avoiding.
If a forbidden note escapes, slow down and re-aim the next attempt — don't rush to fix it in real time.
examples: Head-voice scale skipping the second and sixth
Phrase around the third, resolving high
Upper-register line avoiding the fifth
pitchConstraint:forbidden_register:head.watchOut: Sliding instead of jumping. Head voice can connect notes smoothly, but a smooth slide will glide right through a forbidden pitch — sometimes you have to actually interrupt the sound to skip cleanly.

pitchConstraint:forbidden_register:mixed.interaction: Forbidden notes in mixed voice add a layer of difficulty on top of the hardest technique. You're already working to hold the blend, and now you have to navigate pitch restrictions too. This pair reveals which notes are stable in your mix and which ones you lean on as a crutch — the forbidden ones will force that question.
steps: Know your mix-voice working range before you start — don't forbid notes outside it or you'll have no exercise left.
Sing the phrase with attention to the blend, steering around forbidden notes without breaking the sternum-plus-mask buzz.
If a forbidden note slips in because your blend slipped there first, practice the allowed neighbors with extra blend focus.
Reset breath often — mix voice with constraints is high cognitive load and you'll hold tension.
examples: Mixed phrase avoiding the third and seventh
Crossing passaggio without the fifth
Line around tonic and fourth, resolving on sixth
pitchConstraint:forbidden_register:mixed.watchOut: Losing the mix on the notes near the forbidden ones. The notes adjacent to gaps often break first because you're thinking too hard — keep the body connected and let the brain steer.

pitchConstraint:forbidden_register:false.interaction: Forbidden notes in falsetto are an airy puzzle. Falsetto is already a delicate register — adding restrictions means you have to navigate gaps without tightening to 'aim' at the allowed notes. This pair trains falsetto precision through disciplined airflow rather than muscle.
steps: Sigh into falsetto and find the loose setup.
Sing the phrase steering around forbidden notes while keeping the throat open and the air steady.
Use breath direction to hit the allowed notes — not fold tension.
Breathe generously between phrases — constrained falsetto uses extra air from the planning overhead.
examples: Falsetto phrase without the second and fifth
Upper-register line avoiding the third
Airy melody skipping the sixth, resolving on upper tonic
pitchConstraint:forbidden_register:false.watchOut: Squeezing the throat to 'make' the allowed notes land. Falsetto snaps into hard head voice the moment you tighten — if you feel the throat gripping, exhale and restart with more air.

pitchConstraint:forbidden_register:breat.interaction: Forbidden notes in breathy voice are like whispered word puzzles — you're avoiding certain pitches while keeping the whole thing hushed and intimate. The challenge is that the planning tension from the constraint can accidentally clean up the tone, pulling you out of breathy. Stay loose, stay airy.
steps: Speak the phrase first in whispered tone, practicing the skips without any pitch commitment.
Sing with high breath ratio, letting the phrase flow around the forbidden notes.
Keep the throat warm and unfocused — if it clicks into clear tone, the constraint made you tense.
Breathe often — breathy phrases are short by nature, and constraints make them shorter.
examples: Breathy phrase without the tonic, resolving on third
Whispered line avoiding the fifth
Airy melody skipping the second and seventh
pitchConstraint:forbidden_register:breat.watchOut: The breath ratio dropping when you concentrate. Mental effort makes most singers unconsciously engage more — check in every phrase that you're still over-breathing, not under.

pitchConstraint:targetLanding_register:c.interaction: Target landing in chest voice is about arriving somewhere with weight and conviction. Every phrase ends on the same target note — so you have to make each approach feel fresh while the landing stays rock-solid. The chest-voice target is a place your body knows, and each phrase is a different path home.
steps: Sing the target note first with hand on sternum — memorize its specific buzz.
Sing a phrase that ends on the target, feeling the buzz settle back into the same spot on the landing.
Let the landing be released, not forced — it's a homecoming, not a body slam.
Between phrases, exhale fully and reset breath — the body forgets the target buzz if you don't reset.
examples: Different approaches all landing on tonic
Phrases of various shapes ending on fifth
Lines landing on third with chest weight
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_register:c.watchOut: Hammering the target to 'make it count.' The target note should feel inevitable, not effortful — if you're crashing into it, you're over-supporting and the buzz will go harsh.

pitchConstraint:targetLanding_register:h.interaction: Target landing in head voice is about arriving at a specific placement spot over and over. The target's resonance location becomes a mental and physical homing beacon — no matter how the phrase meanders, you always find your way back to the same spot in the mask. This pair trains placement muscle memory.
steps: Sing the target in head voice, feeling exactly where it buzzes in the mask.
Sing the phrase with varied approaches, and on each landing, check that the buzz returns to the same location.
Keep the landing light — head-voice targets don't need to be loud to feel solid.
If a landing misses the placement, don't force the correction — sing the target alone once, then try the phrase again.
examples: Head-voice phrases landing on upper third
Different shapes ending on fifth in head
Lines resolving to upper tonic
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_register:h.watchOut: The placement drifting because you were busy with the approach. Phrases can have a lot of shape before the landing — don't let the complexity pull you off your target spot.

pitchConstraint:targetLanding_register:m.interaction: Target landing in mixed voice is the most practical of all target drills — because real songs land on the same note at the end of each chorus line, and real songs need you to land in mix, not pure chest or pure head. This pair directly simulates chorus hooks: different approaches, same landing, always in blend.
steps: Sing the target in mixed voice, feeling both sternum and mask buzz simultaneously.
Sing the phrase varying the approach, landing each time with the same blend ratio.
If the landing tips toward chest, lean back into the mask next time; if it tips toward head, re-engage the ribs.
Between phrases, sigh to reset — mix voice tension accumulates and the target will start to warp.
examples: Mixed phrases landing on upper tonic
Different approaches resolving to fifth
Varied shapes ending on upper third
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_register:m.watchOut: The target slowly shifting register over time. After a few repetitions, many singers' targets drift toward whichever register is easier — catch the drift early and recalibrate.

pitchConstraint:targetLanding_register:f.interaction: Target landing in falsetto is about arriving softly, over and over, in the same airy spot. Falsetto targets feel less 'solid' than chest or mix — there's no body weight to the landing — but that lightness is exactly what you're training. The target becomes a consistent whisper-point.
steps: Sing the target in falsetto, feeling the loose, open throat.
Sing the phrase with varied approaches, landing each time at the same soft setup.
Use air, not muscle, to arrive — the landing is a gentle float, not an impact.
Breathe before every approach — falsetto can't coast into a landing on empty air.
examples: Falsetto phrases landing on upper fifth
Airy lines resolving to upper tonic
Different approaches ending on upper third
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_register:f.watchOut: The target hardening into a non-falsetto tone on the landing. When you 'arrive,' there's a temptation to commit — but commitment in falsetto means closing the folds, which kills it. Stay breathy at the landing.

pitchConstraint:targetLanding_register:b.interaction: Target landing in breathy voice is the confessional-hook combo. Every phrase ends on the same whispered note — it's like a refrain you keep returning to in a hushed conversation. The target becomes emotional as well as physical, the place you keep sinking into.
steps: Speak the target in whispered tone first to set the breathy reference.
Sing the phrase with high air ratio, returning to the same whispered landing each time.
Let the landing be a sigh, not a statement — breath trailing off, not cut short.
Refill often — every landing costs air, and breathy uses more than it seems.
examples: Breathy phrases landing on third, whispered
Airy lines resolving to tonic
Hushed melodies ending on fifth
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_register:b.watchOut: The landing clicking into clear tone because it's the 'arrival.' If the last note suddenly sounds focused while the rest of the phrase stayed airy, the emotional landing punctured the breathy quality — keep it whispered even at the end.

pitchConstraint:questionAnswer_register:.interaction: Question-and-answer phrases in chest voice are a call-and-response with the body. The rising question tensions the ribs and sternum upward; the descending answer releases them back down. This pair is deeply physical — the dialogue happens between the upper and lower buzz locations of your chest register.
steps: Sing the rising question with hand on sternum, feeling the buzz climb toward the ribs' upper range.
Pause briefly — let the tension of the question hang in the air.
Sing the descending answer, feeling the buzz settle back down into the lower sternum.
Breathe fully between pairs — the Q/A shape is two phrases, not one, and each needs its own support.
examples: Rising tonic to fifth, descending fifth to second
Up third to sixth, down fifth to tonic
Climbing second to seventh, falling sixth to third
pitchConstraint:questionAnswer_register:.watchOut: Rushing from question to answer. The pause in between is what makes the pair feel like a dialogue — if you steamroll through, it sounds like one phrase instead of two. Breathe.

pitchConstraint:questionAnswer_register:.interaction: Question-and-answer in head voice is a dialogue of placements. The rising question moves the resonance upward in the mask; the descending answer brings it back down. Because head voice is placement-driven rather than weight-driven, the Q/A contrast comes from where the buzz lives, not how hard you push.
steps: Sing the rising question in head voice, letting the buzz drift upward in the mask.
At the top of the question, pause — let the light, suspended feel hang.
Sing the descending answer, letting the buzz drift back down through the cheeks.
Keep both phrases light — head voice Q/A isn't about drama, it's about placement shift.
examples: Rising fifth to upper tonic, descending upper tonic to third
Up third to sixth, down seventh to fourth
Climbing fourth to upper second, falling upper tonic to fifth
pitchConstraint:questionAnswer_register:.watchOut: Getting louder on the rising question because the climb feels like it needs energy. Head voice questions are soft and curious — if you project them, they stop sounding like questions and start sounding like declarations.

pitchConstraint:questionAnswer_register:.interaction: Question-and-answer in mixed voice is the pop-song phrase structure in its purest form. The rise pushes the blend upward through the passaggio; the fall brings it back down. Every songwriter's verse-to-pre-chorus move is shaped like this, and the Q/A mix drill is the direct technical rehearsal for real repertoire.
steps: Sing the rising question keeping the blend steady through the passaggio on the climb.
Pause at the peak — check that both sternum and mask buzz are still present.
Sing the descending answer, holding the blend on the way down rather than letting it flop into pure chest.
Breathe between pairs to reset the blend — tension creeps in fast.
examples: Rising tonic to upper third, descending upper third to fifth
Up third to upper tonic, down upper second to second
Climbing fifth to upper fifth, falling upper fourth to tonic
pitchConstraint:questionAnswer_register:.watchOut: The answer falling into pure chest just because it's going down. Mixed voice works in both directions — descents should still feel blended, not dumped into the ribs.

pitchConstraint:questionAnswer_register:.interaction: Question-and-answer in falsetto is an ethereal conversation with yourself. Both the question and the answer float — the contrast is shape and direction, not weight or force. This pair is almost dreamlike; it's the sound of someone thinking out loud in a barely-there voice.
steps: Sigh into falsetto to find the loose setup.
Sing the rising question with constant air, the pitch lifting on the breath stream.
Pause — let the question hang like a thought.
Sing the descending answer as an exhale, fading into the final note.
examples: Rising third to upper second, falling upper tonic to fifth
Up fifth to upper fourth, down upper third to sixth
Climbing fourth to upper tonic, falling upper tonic to third
pitchConstraint:questionAnswer_register:.watchOut: Tightening to lift the question. Falsetto questions have to rise with air, not muscle — if you feel the throat grip to climb, the whole pair will end up hard-edged instead of ghostly.

pitchConstraint:questionAnswer_register:.interaction: Question-and-answer in breathy voice is two whispered phrases in dialogue — the kind of private exchange that happens at 2am. The question asks something unfinished; the answer releases it. Both are close-mic intimate, and the contrast is entirely in the pitch shape, not the dynamic.
steps: Speak the Q and A first in whispered tone to feel the rhythm of the exchange.
Sing the rising question with high breath ratio, warm throat, unfocused tone.
Pause — breathe and let the question linger.
Sing the descending answer as a long sigh, the final note dissolving into air.
examples: Rising tonic to fifth whispered, falling fifth to second
Up third to sixth breathy, down fifth to tonic
Climbing fourth to seventh airy, falling sixth to third
pitchConstraint:questionAnswer_register:.watchOut: The answer going clearer than the question because the descent feels natural. Both halves of the pair need the same breath ratio — if one sounds airy and the other sounds focused, the intimacy breaks.

pitchConstraint:leaps_pickingHand:downst.interaction: Leaps demand your fretting hand jumps across strings and frets with no connective tissue, while all-downstrokes keeps the picking hand locked in one heavy, driving direction. The result is a rhythm-guitar workout that forces clean string targeting — every downstroke has to land exactly on the string your fretting hand just leapt to, or the attack buzzes. Think surf rock octave riffs, not noodly scalar lines.
steps: Anchor on the root at the 5th fret of your lowest scale string with your fretting-hand index finger.
Plan leaps of a 4th, 5th, or root-up-an-octave — skip at least one scale tone between every note.
Picking hand: lock the wrist and only use downstrokes, resting on the palm for control between hits.
Fretting hand: lift cleanly off each note so the open string doesn't ring when you jump.
Start at 60 BPM quarter notes, then try eighths once the jumps are accurate.
examples: Root (5th fret, 5th string) leaping to the 5th (5th fret, 3rd string) and back
Root to the root up an octave across three strings on the 7th fret
3rd of the scale to the b7 on a different string, all downstrokes
pitchConstraint:leaps_pickingHand:downst.watchOut: The killer here is the picking hand getting out of sync — downstrokes are slower than alternate picking, so the fretting hand will often arrive early and the note buzzes. Slow down until both hands land exactly together.
pitchConstraint:leaps_pickingHand:downst.deeperInsight: Downstroke-only leaping is how surf and punk guitarists get that driving, percussive riff feel. The uniform attack makes every leap sound intentional and weighty.

pitchConstraint:leaps_pickingHand:finger.interaction: Leaps plus fingerpicking is the big one — this is where your picking hand finally earns its keep. Every leap between scale tones means a different finger has to land on a different string, so you're training finger independence and string targeting simultaneously. The fretting hand is making dramatic position shifts while three or four fingers on the picking hand have to reassign themselves on the fly.
steps: Assign thumb to the lowest scale string (root area), index to the next, middle higher, ring highest.
Fret the root at the 5th fret with your index finger as your home base.
Plan every phrase with leaps of a 4th or bigger — no neighboring scale tones.
Picking hand: let the finger assignment follow the leap, even if it means ring finger plays two notes in a row.
Practice the phrase slowly as a pluck-and-hold chord first, then release into the melodic leap pattern.
examples: Root (5th string, 5th fret) leaping to the 5th (2nd string, 5th fret) — thumb then middle
3rd of the scale to root up an octave using thumb then ring
4th to b7 on adjacent strings skipping the 5th and 6th scale degrees
pitchConstraint:leaps_pickingHand:finger.watchOut: The common failure is letting fingers stray from their assigned strings when panicked — the picking hand gets messy and notes get missed. Commit to the string assignment and let the fretting hand do the acrobatics.
pitchConstraint:leaps_pickingHand:finger.deeperInsight: Classical and fingerstyle players use leaping arpeggio-like patterns constantly. This constraint pair is basically training you to play Travis picking or classical étude figures.

pitchConstraint:leaps_pickingHand:muted.interaction: Palm-muted leaps turn the guitar into a pitched percussion instrument. The leaps keep the pitches interesting, but the muting strips away sustain so every note is a short, chunky thump with just enough pitch to hear the interval. It's a great constraint for internalizing intervals because your ear has to work harder to catch the pitch in the attack transient.
steps: Rest the side of your picking-hand palm on the strings just in front of the bridge for muting.
Fretting hand anchors on the root at the 7th fret with your index finger.
Plan leaps of a 5th, 6th, or root-up-an-octave — only big intervals.
Picking hand: pick with medium attack, adjusting palm pressure so the pitch is audible but short.
Let each muted note ring for just a chunk of the beat, then release fretting pressure to mute it fully.
examples: Root to 5th leaping across two strings at the 7th fret
3rd to b7 as a muted power-leap on the 5th and 6th strings
Root up an octave with both notes palm-muted and choppy
pitchConstraint:leaps_pickingHand:muted.watchOut: Over-muting kills the pitch entirely and you lose track of the interval. Find the sweet spot where the palm weight lets about 30% of the pitch through.

pitchConstraint:leaps_pickingHand:hybrid.interaction: Hybrid picking + leaps is a country/jazz weapon. The pick handles the low leaping notes while middle and ring fingers snap the high ones, so large interval jumps between bass-string leaps and treble-string answers become effortless. Fretting hand makes the position shifts; picking hand distributes the labor between pick and fingers so you don't have to strum across unwanted strings.
steps: Hold the pick between thumb and index. Rest middle and ring on the 2nd and 1st strings.
Anchor on the root at the 5th fret of the 5th string with your fretting index.
Leap down to low roots with the pick, up to high 5ths or 3rds with middle or ring finger.
Fretting hand: prepare the next leap landing before the current note finishes ringing.
Avoid neighboring scale tones entirely — every move is a skip or bigger.
examples: Pick the root low (5th string, 5th fret), snap the 5th high (1st string, 5th fret)
Pick the 3rd on the 4th string, snap the root up an octave with middle finger
Pick b7 low, snap 3rd high as a leaping call-and-response
pitchConstraint:leaps_pickingHand:hybrid.watchOut: The fingers often over-pluck and get louder than the pick notes. Keep finger attack light so the low-pick leaps land with equal weight.

pitchConstraint:leaps_pickingHand:slide.interaction: Slide plus leaps is deceptive — with a slide you can't actually leap cleanly because the slide glisses through every pitch in between, so the leap becomes a fast sweep from one scale tone to a distant one. The constraint becomes about clarity of the landing note, not the journey. Fretting hand wears the slide and controls the glide distance; picking hand hits the start and re-picks the landing.
steps: Wear the slide on your ring or pinky finger over the string directly above the fret wire.
Start on the root at the 5th fret, 3rd string — pick it cleanly.
Plan leaps of a 5th or bigger to a specific scale tone higher up the neck.
Picking hand: pick the starting note, then re-pick as the slide arrives at the target.
Fretting hand: use the other fingers behind the slide to mute so the glissando doesn't ring out through every pitch in between.
examples: Root at the 5th fret sliding up to the 5th at the 12th fret, 3rd string
3rd of the scale leaping up an octave on the 2nd string
b7 down to the root a 7th below on the same string
pitchConstraint:leaps_pickingHand:slide.watchOut: Without behind-the-slide muting, the leap sounds like a horror-movie whoop through every pitch. Damp with your index and middle behind the slide so only the landing rings clean.

pitchConstraint:leaps_pickingHand:bends.interaction: Bending leaps is almost a contradiction — bends only reach a step or two before running out of room, so true leaps have to be faked with dramatic multi-step whole-tone or minor-third bends. Fretting hand does all the heavy lifting stretching the string; picking hand just provides the attack. Practically, this limits you to big one-string gestures within a tiny fret range.
steps: Pick a scale tone on the 2nd or 3rd string at the 7th fret — these bend easiest.
Fret with ring finger and reinforce with middle and index behind for pulling power.
Bend the string up a whole step to reach the scale tone two away — that's your leap.
For bigger leaps, try bending a minor third (1.5 steps) or release + re-pick at a new fret.
Picking hand: pick once at the start of each bend and let the pitch movement carry the phrase.
examples: Start on the 4th at the 7th fret, 3rd string, bend a whole step to the 5th (that's a leap in function)
Bend the b7 up a minor third to the root up an octave
Root bent up to the 9th as a big sweeping gesture
pitchConstraint:leaps_pickingHand:bends.watchOut: Bending more than a minor third at a time will go sharp and out of tune fast. Anchor multiple fingers behind the bending finger for support, and match the target pitch to a reference.
pitchConstraint:leaps_pickingHand:bends.deeperInsight: This is why blues players like Albert King sound so melodic with only a few frets — they leap through bends rather than fretboard movement.

pitchConstraint:stepwise_pickingHand:dow.interaction: Stepwise + all-downstrokes is a scale practice cliché for a reason — it builds rhythmic authority and wrist endurance. Every neighbor-note movement gets the same heavy attack, which makes scales feel like riffs rather than exercises. Fretting hand walks up and down the scale; picking hand stays locked to a downstroke pattern even though alternate picking would be easier.
steps: Pick a scale shape starting on the root at the 5th fret, 5th string.
Walk stepwise — only to the next scale tone up or down, never skipping.
Picking hand: strict downstrokes only, returning the pick above the string between each hit.
Fretting hand: keep fingers close to the frets so each new neighbor tone rings clean.
Start at 60 BPM quarter notes, then push to eighths when your wrist feels the burn.
examples: Scale walk from the root up to the 5th in two-octave position
Descending stepwise from the root up an octave to the 3rd below
Walk from the 2nd up to the 6th and back down
pitchConstraint:stepwise_pickingHand:dow.watchOut: Your wrist will tire fast and attack will weaken halfway through. Downstroke practice is actually about conditioning the forearm — stop when you lose even attack, not when the clock runs out.

pitchConstraint:stepwise_pickingHand:fin.interaction: Stepwise fingerpicking is beautiful but tricky because consecutive scale tones often sit on the same string — which means the same finger has to pluck twice in a row or you have to reassign. Fretting hand walks smoothly; picking hand has to decide whether to alternate fingers or stick with one.
steps: Assign thumb, index, middle, ring to the lowest four strings you'll use.
Anchor fretting index on the root at the 5th fret.
Walk the scale one neighbor tone at a time, crossing strings naturally.
Picking hand: alternate index and middle for any notes on the same string.
Let each note ring into the next for a legato walking-line feel.
examples: Root to 2nd to 3rd walking across two strings at the 5th fret
Descending scale from the 5th down to the root on the 3rd string
Stepwise walk from the 4th up to the root up an octave
pitchConstraint:stepwise_pickingHand:fin.watchOut: Using the same finger on consecutive same-string notes creates a gap where the string is re-prepared. Commit to index-middle alternation so the scale flows evenly.

pitchConstraint:stepwise_pickingHand:mut.interaction: Muted stepwise scales sound like a low-end chug walking through pitches — very metal, very reggae skank low-string. The neighbor-note motion gives you melodic contour while the palm mute keeps everything tight and percussive. Fretting hand has to be extra clean because the short muted notes expose every buzz.
steps: Rest picking-hand palm on the strings at the bridge for a firm mute.
Start on the root at the 3rd fret, 6th or 5th string — low strings mute best.
Walk the scale stepwise upward, one neighbor tone at a time.
Fretting hand: press only hard enough to get a clean pitch — the mute does the damping.
Picking hand: alternate picking for speed once stepwise motion is secure.
examples: Low root walking up to the 5th on the 6th string
Stepwise descent from the 5th to the root on the 5th string palm-muted
Chugging 1-2-3-4-5 walk on the low strings
pitchConstraint:stepwise_pickingHand:mut.watchOut: Fretting hand noise is amplified by the muted context — any sloppy finger lift sounds like a buzz. Keep fingertips perpendicular to the fretboard.

pitchConstraint:stepwise_pickingHand:hyb.interaction: Hybrid picking stepwise walks are a country/chicken-pickin' staple. The pick handles low-string bass walks while fingers pick upper-string neighbor tones, letting you play walking basslines under simultaneous melody. Fretting hand stays compact in one position; picking hand coordinates pick and fingers.
steps: Hold the pick, rest middle and ring on the top two strings.
Anchor fretting index on the root at the 5th fret.
Pick stepwise bass walks with the pick on the lower strings.
Snap stepwise upper-voice motion with middle and ring on top strings.
Keep everything in one neighbor-tone-at-a-time motion, no skips.
examples: Pick walks root-2nd-3rd on the 5th string, middle snaps the 5th to 6th above
Bass walks stepwise 5-6-b7-root up an octave, fingers pluck pedal tone
Stepwise walking bassline under a held fingerpicked chord tone
pitchConstraint:stepwise_pickingHand:hyb.watchOut: Fingers and pick get rhythmically off-beat from each other. Practice the bass walk alone first, then add the finger answer.

pitchConstraint:stepwise_pickingHand:sli.interaction: Stepwise and slide is the most natural possible pairing — a slide was built for gliding between neighbor tones. Each phrase becomes a continuous glissando from scale tone to scale tone with no breaks, like a lap steel or Hawaiian figure. Fretting hand wears the slide and inches it up the neck; picking hand re-picks to articulate each new tone without breaking the legato feel.
steps: Wear the slide on ring or pinky above a single string.
Pick the root at the 5th fret, 3rd string to start.
Glide the slide up two frets to the next scale tone, re-picking as you arrive.
Continue stepwise — one scale tone at a time, always sliding, never lifting.
Use behind-the-slide mute (index and middle) to keep the glides clean.
examples: Root stepping up to the 5th over five scale tones on the 3rd string
Stepwise slide descent from the 5th back to the root
Walking from the 2nd up to the 6th as a continuous slide phrase
pitchConstraint:stepwise_pickingHand:sli.watchOut: If the slide lifts even slightly between notes, the glissando becomes two separate bloops. Keep slide pressure consistent along the whole phrase.
pitchConstraint:stepwise_pickingHand:sli.deeperInsight: This is the core gesture of every slide guitar melody — pick-and-glide across neighbor tones. Ry Cooder, Derek Trucks, and David Lindley all live here.

pitchConstraint:stepwise_pickingHand:ben.interaction: Stepwise with bends means every neighbor-tone movement is achieved by bending, not fretting a new note. You pick once and bend up a half or whole step to the next scale tone, then release to return. Fretting hand does all the work; picking hand is mostly quiet. This is extremely expressive but limits phrases to short gestures.
steps: Pick a scale tone on the 2nd or 3rd string around the 7th fret.
Fret with ring, reinforced by middle and index behind for support.
Pick once, then bend up a half or whole step to reach the neighbor scale tone.
Release slowly back down to reach the lower neighbor tone — no re-picking.
Chain multiple bend-release gestures for stepwise lines without the pick moving.
examples: Pick the 4th at the 7th fret, bend a whole step to the 5th, release back
2nd bent up to the 3rd with a slow release for a vocal feel
Root bent to the 2nd and back for a stepwise wiggle
pitchConstraint:stepwise_pickingHand:ben.watchOut: Bending pitches drift sharp or flat mid-phrase. Train your ear on a reference drone so each bent neighbor tone hits the right scale tone exactly.

pitchConstraint:arch_pickingHand:downstr.interaction: An arch-shaped phrase — climb to a peak, descend back — played with all downstrokes is pure riff architecture. The uniform attack makes the arch feel monolithic and deliberate, like a heavy-metal lead or a driving surf melody. Fretting hand shapes the rise and fall; picking hand stays locked in heavy downward strokes throughout.
steps: Plan a phrase that climbs from the root up to the 5th or root up an octave, then returns.
Picking hand: downstrokes only — return the pick above the string after each hit.
Fretting hand: use the scale shape starting at the 5th fret, walking up to the peak and back.
Emphasize the peak note with extra weight and a slight hold before descending.
Start slow — downstrokes at speed is wrist fatigue territory.
examples: Root walking up to the 5th and back, six notes total
Root up to the root an octave higher over eight notes, then descending
3rd climbing to the 6th and back through scale tones
pitchConstraint:arch_pickingHand:downstr.watchOut: The descent is where downstroke players get sloppy — they speed up to compensate. Keep the descending half as deliberate as the climb.
pitchConstraint:arch_pickingHand:downstr.deeperInsight: Arch phrases with downstrokes are the DNA of metal riffs. The physical weight of each attack makes the shape feel like a wave crashing.

pitchConstraint:arch_pickingHand:fingerp.interaction: Arch shape + fingerpicking is the classical guitarist's favorite territory. The climb uses ascending finger rolls (thumb-index-middle-ring), the peak gets the ring finger hit, and the descent reverses the finger order. Fretting hand shifts position to build the arch; picking hand is a tiny choreographed ballet where each finger knows exactly when it plays.
steps: Assign thumb to the lowest string, index-middle-ring to the next three up.
Anchor fretting index on the root at the 5th fret.
Climb: thumb plays root, index the 3rd, middle the 5th, ring the root up an octave as the peak.
Descend: reverse the finger order — ring, middle, index, thumb — walking back down scale tones.
Let each note ring through the arch so the shape sounds like a blooming melody.
examples: Root, 3rd, 5th, root up an octave, 5th, 3rd, root — classic arpeggio arch
Scale arch from the root up to the 6th and back over seven notes
2nd climbing to the 5th as a peak, descending through 3rd and root
pitchConstraint:arch_pickingHand:fingerp.watchOut: The descent often gets louder than the climb because the ring and middle fingers have more leverage. Soften them consciously so the arch sounds symmetrical.
pitchConstraint:arch_pickingHand:fingerp.deeperInsight: This is literally how a classical arpeggio étude works — arches in one hand shape, rolling fingers to build and release tension.

pitchConstraint:arch_pickingHand:muted.interaction: A muted arch is a percussive swell — pitches climbing to a peak and falling, all chunked down to short thumps. The arch shape gives your ear a melodic contour while the mute keeps the rhythmic feel tight. Great for writing a palm-muted verse hook that has melodic intent.
steps: Palm-mute by resting picking-hand palm at the bridge.
Fret the root at the 5th fret, low string.
Climb through the scale toward the 5th or root up an octave as the peak.
Give the peak note a tiny bit more attack and slightly less palm pressure for emphasis.
Descend stepwise or in skips back to the root with full mute restored.
examples: Low root climbing to the 5th and back as a muted riff
Arch from the 2nd up to the 6th peak, descending palm-muted
Root up to root an octave higher as a chunky arch
pitchConstraint:arch_pickingHand:muted.watchOut: The peak note should pop out of the mute slightly — if it sounds the same as every other note, the arch is invisible to the listener.

pitchConstraint:arch_pickingHand:hybrid.interaction: Arch + hybrid picking distributes the shape between pick and fingers — pick walks up the low strings for the climb, fingers pluck the high strings for the peak and descent. Fretting hand shifts across strings to build the arch; picking hand hands off the phrase from pick to fingers mid-arch.
steps: Hold pick between thumb and index, rest middle and ring on top strings.
Start picking low scale tones on the 5th or 4th string climbing toward the peak.
At the arch peak (root up an octave or the 5th), switch to middle finger to pluck.
Descend using ring and middle on the upper strings back toward the pick.
Return to the pick for the bottom of the arch on the low strings.
examples: Pick root-3rd-5th climbing, middle finger snaps root up an octave, descend with ring-middle-pick
Pick walks from the 2nd up to the 4th, fingers take the 5th and 6th peak and return
Arch centered on the 5th with pick on the climb and fingers on the descent
pitchConstraint:arch_pickingHand:hybrid.watchOut: The handoff from pick to fingers often has a rhythmic hitch. Practice the transition point at half speed until it's seamless.

pitchConstraint:arch_pickingHand:slide.interaction: Arch shape with a slide is a continuous swoop up to a peak pitch and back — the slide glides through the climb, hits the peak scale tone, and glides back. Fretting hand wears the slide and shapes the whole phrase as one long gesture; picking hand re-picks only at key moments like the peak.
steps: Wear the slide above a single string, anchor with index and middle behind for muting.
Pick the root at the 5th fret, 3rd string.
Slide upward through scale tones toward a peak at the 5th or root up an octave.
Re-pick at the peak to emphasize it, then slide back down to the root.
Keep slide pressure consistent so the arch sounds like one continuous melodic gesture.
examples: Root sliding up to the 5th as a peak and sliding back to root
Arch from the 2nd up to the 6th on the 2nd string as a single slide phrase
Root swooping up to root an octave higher and descending
pitchConstraint:arch_pickingHand:slide.watchOut: Sliding is restricted to one string for clean arches — don't try to cross strings mid-slide. If you need a wider range, relocate to a higher string first.

pitchConstraint:arch_pickingHand:bends.interaction: Arch + bends is about compression — you can't really arch across multiple frets with bends alone, so the arch gets squeezed into one or two bent strings. Pick the starting note, bend up to the peak scale tone, release down through the descent. Fretting hand does all the shaping via bend depth; picking hand picks minimally.
steps: Anchor on a scale tone on the 2nd or 3rd string at the 7th fret.
Pick once with the picking hand.
Bend with ring finger (reinforced) up to the peak scale tone — usually a whole step above.
Hold the peak briefly, then release the bend slowly to descend back to the start.
For bigger arches, re-pick at the peak and release, or pre-bend and release to dive.
examples: Start on the 4th, bend a whole step to the 5th peak, release to the 4th — a three-note arch
Pre-bend the root to the 2nd, release slowly as a descending arch
Bend b7 up to the root peak then release to 6th
pitchConstraint:arch_pickingHand:bends.watchOut: The release is where pitch accuracy dies — players let the string unwind too fast and overshoot the bottom note. Control the release slowly to match the descending scale tone.

pitchConstraint:seed_pickingHand:downstr.interaction: Seed mode — three notes that repeat with one changing each time — played with all downstrokes creates a driving, hypnotic riff. The uniform attack makes the variations pop because your ear latches onto the one changing pitch. Fretting hand manages the seed set and its mutations; picking hand hammers downward throughout.
steps: Pick three seed notes from your scale — e.g., root, 3rd, 5th at the 5th fret area.
Play the seed cell with all downstrokes: root, 3rd, 5th.
Repeat the cell, changing ONE note to a neighbor scale tone: root, 3rd, 6th.
Next repetition, change a different note: root, 4th, 6th.
Keep the picking hand locked to downstrokes throughout for a riff feel.
examples: Seed: root, 3rd, 5th. Mutations swap in 4th, 6th, b7 one at a time
Seed: 2nd, 4th, 6th. Mutations walk to root, 3rd, 5th
Seed across two strings at the 5th fret, mutating the highest tone each pass
pitchConstraint:seed_pickingHand:downstr.watchOut: Downstroke fatigue kills the seed's hypnotic feel. Keep the tempo slow enough to sustain even attack across many repetitions.

pitchConstraint:seed_pickingHand:fingerp.interaction: Seed + fingerpicking is a meditative practice that trains finger memory of a three-note chord shape while the fretting hand mutates the pitches. Each seed becomes a tiny arpeggio, and mutations shift the voicing. Classical composers love this — it's basically how études work.
steps: Assign thumb, index, middle to the three strings of your seed notes.
Fretting hand anchors on the root at the 5th fret.
Pluck the seed: thumb-index-middle on root, 3rd, 5th.
Repeat with ONE note changed — e.g., root, 3rd, 6th — fingers stay on same strings.
Cycle mutations, letting each note ring into the next so the seed sounds like flowing arpeggios.
examples: Seed root-3rd-5th, mutating to root-4th-5th, then root-4th-6th
Seed 2nd-4th-6th on top three strings, mutations walk through adjacent scale tones
Seed low-mid-high tones across three strings at the 7th fret
pitchConstraint:seed_pickingHand:fingerp.watchOut: Because the fingers stay planted, fretting-hand mutations must land exactly on time or the arpeggio feels uneven. Clean fret changes are the whole game.

pitchConstraint:seed_pickingHand:muted.interaction: Muted seeds sound like a pitched drum loop — three tones repeating with subtle variations, all chunked and percussive. The mute strips the sustain so the seed pattern becomes rhythm-forward. Fretting hand changes one seed tone each repetition while picking hand maintains a steady palm-muted attack.
steps: Palm-mute by resting picking hand at the bridge.
Choose three seed tones — e.g., root, 4th, 5th on the 5th and 6th strings.
Pick the seed with steady alternate or downstroke attack, muted throughout.
Mutate one seed tone per repetition — walk by step to a neighbor scale tone.
Keep the rhythm perfectly steady — the variation should be melodic, not rhythmic.
examples: Seed root-4th-5th, mutating to root-3rd-5th, then root-3rd-6th
Low-string seed at the 3rd fret with one tone moving each pass
Three muted stabs per bar, one seed tone shifting each bar
pitchConstraint:seed_pickingHand:muted.watchOut: The mute amplifies any fretting-hand inconsistency — a missed mutation sounds like a dud. Pre-plan each mutation before you start the loop.

pitchConstraint:seed_pickingHand:hybrid.interaction: Hybrid picking a seed lets you split the three seed tones between pick (low) and fingers (high) so mutations of the high tones don't affect the low, and vice versa. This creates a call-and-answer within the seed itself. Fretting hand handles the mutations; picking hand keeps its role division locked.
steps: Pick handles the lowest seed tone on the 4th or 5th string.
Middle and ring handle the two higher seed tones on the 2nd and 3rd strings.
Fret the seed — e.g., root low, 3rd mid, 5th high at the 5th fret.
Pluck the full seed, then mutate ONE tone per pass, usually in the finger voices.
Keep the pick attack consistent so the fingers' mutations stand out.
examples: Pick root, fingers pluck 3rd and 5th, mutating the 5th down to the 4th then 3rd
Pick the 5th on the 4th string, fingers hold root and 3rd, mutating the 3rd to 4th
Pick pedal on the root, fingers cycle 3rd-5th-6th one mutation at a time
pitchConstraint:seed_pickingHand:hybrid.watchOut: It's tempting to only mutate the finger notes because they're easier. Challenge yourself by mutating the pick-note seed tone too.

pitchConstraint:seed_pickingHand:slide.interaction: Seeding with a slide is unusual because the slide can only play one note at a time cleanly. So the seed becomes three successive notes on one string, with the slide gliding between them, and mutations shift one of the three along the neck. Fretting hand glides the slide; picking hand re-picks each seed tone.
steps: Wear slide on ring or pinky, stay on one string.
Pick three seed tones on the 3rd string: e.g., root at the 5th fret, 3rd at the 9th, 5th at the 12th.
Play the seed by sliding and re-picking at each target.
Mutate one seed position per pass — move the highest target up two frets.
Use behind-the-slide mute so the glissando doesn't sound like three continuous sweeps.
examples: Seed root-3rd-5th on the 3rd string, mutating the 5th up to the 6th
Seed 2nd-4th-6th as three sliding targets on the 2nd string
Three targets on the 3rd string with the middle target walking each pass
pitchConstraint:seed_pickingHand:slide.watchOut: Slides make precise fret targets fuzzy — each seed tone needs to land exactly over the fret wire. Practice the seed as static targets first, then add the slide between.

pitchConstraint:seed_pickingHand:bends.interaction: Seed + bends is a tight gesture because you can't jump around — all three seed tones live on one string in one fret area, and mutations happen via bend depth. So a seed might be root, root bent a half step, root bent a whole step, with mutations changing which bend amount shifts. Fretting hand bends; picking hand picks the seed rhythm.
steps: Anchor on a scale tone on the 2nd or 3rd string at the 7th fret.
Three seed tones: the fretted tone, a half-step bend, a whole-step bend (for example).
Pick each seed tone in sequence, bending the string to reach the bent versions.
Mutate one of the three bend amounts — e.g., turn the half-step into a minor-third bend.
Keep the fretting anchor on the same fret so the seed location stays fixed.
examples: Seed: 4th, 4th bent to b5, 4th bent to 5th. Mutate bent tones between repetitions
Seed: b7, b7 bent to root, pre-bent and released to 6th
Root, root bent to 2nd, root bent to b3 as a bluesy seed cell
pitchConstraint:seed_pickingHand:bends.watchOut: Pitch accuracy on each bend is the whole point. Match each bent seed tone to a scale tone using a reference drone.

pitchConstraint:forbidden_pickingHand:do.interaction: Downstroke-only phrasing while avoiding 1-3 forbidden scale tones forces your fretting hand to hunt for creative paths through the scale. The uniform picking attack makes every non-forbidden note sound equally weighted, so the absence of the forbidden tones is very audible. Fretting hand plans the route; picking hand drills downstrokes.
steps: Pick a scale shape around the 5th fret and mentally mark 1-3 forbidden scale tones.
Plan phrases that walk around the forbidden tones — leap, skip, or pedal to avoid them.
Picking hand: strict downstrokes throughout.
Fretting hand: if you play a forbidden tone by accident, stop and restart the phrase.
Start slow so the ear can catch any illegal notes.
examples: Forbid the 3rd and 6th, walk the remaining scale tones with downstrokes
Forbid just the 4th, play leap-heavy downstroke phrases avoiding it
Forbid b7, riff in modal territory with everything except the 7th
pitchConstraint:forbidden_pickingHand:do.watchOut: Muscle memory of the full scale will pull your fretting hand to the forbidden tones reflexively. Go slow enough to make conscious choices.

pitchConstraint:forbidden_pickingHand:fi.interaction: Forbidden tones + fingerpicking lets you design custom voicings and patterns that deliberately avoid certain colors — like pentatonic playing forced on a diatonic scale. Fretting hand chooses only allowed tones; picking hand picks voicing shapes that happen to land on legal scale tones.
steps: Assign thumb, index, middle, ring to four strings.
Choose 1-3 forbidden scale tones and mark their fret positions as no-go zones.
Build fingerpicked voicings that use only allowed tones — essentially custom chord shapes.
Arpeggiate the allowed voicings with your finger assignments.
Let notes ring so the missing forbidden tones feel like intentional silence.
examples: Forbid 3rd and 6th — voicings built on root, 2nd, 4th, 5th, b7
Forbid 4th — travis-picking pattern on root-5th-3rd only
Forbid 2nd and 6th — arpeggio using root, 3rd, 5th, b7
pitchConstraint:forbidden_pickingHand:fi.watchOut: Your default chord shapes include forbidden tones. You'll need to build new voicings rather than just mute strings — plan the shapes before you start the arpeggio.

pitchConstraint:forbidden_pickingHand:mu.interaction: Muted forbidden-tone playing is great for riff writing — you're chugging with tonal restriction, which forces interesting rhythmic patterns on the remaining scale tones. Fretting hand walks around the forbidden zones; picking hand palm mutes steadily.
steps: Palm-mute at the bridge with picking hand.
Choose 1-3 forbidden scale tones, preferably ones your default riff relies on.
Walk stepwise or leap around the forbidden tones with a steady muted chug.
Keep the rhythmic pattern the same across phrases so the melodic choices stand out.
Restart if a forbidden tone sneaks in.
examples: Forbid 3rd and b7, riff on root-2nd-4th-5th-6th palm-muted
Forbid root, write a riff that orbits but never lands on root
Forbid 4th and 5th, focus on the remaining colors as muted chugs
pitchConstraint:forbidden_pickingHand:mu.watchOut: Forbidding the root makes riffs feel unstable — lean into it, but know that the phrase won't resolve until you stop.

pitchConstraint:forbidden_pickingHand:hy.interaction: Hybrid picking + forbidden tones lets the pick and fingers each navigate around the tabooed scale tones independently. You might forbid a tone in one voice and still use it in another for a weird dissonance, but the strict reading says forbid it everywhere. Fretting hand plans both voices around the forbidden tones.
steps: Hold pick, rest middle and ring on top strings.
Choose 1-3 forbidden scale tones.
Build a bass line on low strings with pick, avoiding forbidden tones.
Build a melody line on top strings with fingers, avoiding forbidden tones.
Verify both voices pass the forbidden test before you loop the phrase.
examples: Forbid 3rd and 6th — bass walks 1-2-5, fingers pluck 5-b7 above
Forbid 4th — bass pedal on root, fingers cycle 2nd-3rd-5th-b7
Forbid b7 — bass walks 1-4-5, fingers pluck 3rd-6th-root up an octave
pitchConstraint:forbidden_pickingHand:hy.watchOut: It's easy to let the bass line accidentally use a forbidden tone while focused on the melody. Write both voices out in scale degrees first.

pitchConstraint:forbidden_pickingHand:sl.interaction: Slide + forbidden tones is about precise fret targeting — you have to glide past certain positions without landing on them, which means re-picking at exact scale-tone frets and never resting on the forbidden ones. Fretting hand glides with extreme precision; picking hand re-picks only at legal stopping points.
steps: Wear slide on ring or pinky, one string at a time.
Choose 1-3 forbidden scale tones and note their fret positions on the chosen string.
Pick a starting scale tone and slide toward a target — but the target cannot be a forbidden fret.
Glide past forbidden frets without lingering, re-picking only at legal stops.
Use behind-the-slide mute so fleeting forbidden tones during the glide don't ring out.
examples: Forbid b5, slide from root up past the b5 position to the 5th, re-picking at the 5th
Forbid 3rd, slide from 2nd up to 4th without stopping at the 3rd
Forbid 6th, slide root to 5th to b7 with re-picks at each legal stop
pitchConstraint:forbidden_pickingHand:sl.watchOut: The slide naturally produces every pitch along the way — you can't actually skip the forbidden tone, you can only not rest on it. Keep the glide quick so it sounds like a bend-through rather than a landing.

pitchConstraint:forbidden_pickingHand:be.interaction: Forbidding scale tones in bend-only mode is interesting because bends naturally pass through in-between pitches. The constraint becomes: never land a bend on the forbidden scale tone. Pre-plan your bend targets to avoid the taboo pitches. Fretting hand does all the work.
steps: Anchor on a scale tone on the 2nd or 3rd string at the 7th fret.
Identify 1-3 forbidden scale tones and know which bend amounts from your starting note would land on them.
Pick the starting tone and bend only to legal scale tones.
If a bend passes through a forbidden tone momentarily, that's fine — just don't rest there.
Chain multiple bend-release gestures that always land on allowed tones.
examples: Start on 4th, forbid 5th, so bend only a minor third to b6
Forbid 3rd, bend 2nd up to 4th directly, skipping 3rd as a landing
Forbid b7, bend 6th up a whole step to root up an octave, skipping b7 as a rest point
pitchConstraint:forbidden_pickingHand:be.watchOut: Resting on a bend-in-progress is the common slip-up. If you pause a bend at a forbidden pitch for even a beat, it counts as playing the tone.

pitchConstraint:targetLanding_pickingHan.interaction: Target landing + downstrokes is like scale-tone target practice with a hammer. Every phrase has to end on the same scale tone, and every note leading up gets the same heavy attack. The uniform downstroke creates anticipation for the landing — your ear locks onto the target. Fretting hand plans each approach; picking hand drives home the landing.
steps: Pick your target scale tone — e.g., the 5th at the 7th fret, 4th string.
Plan a 4-8 note phrase that ends on the target.
Play the phrase with strict downstrokes, giving the final target a tiny bit more weight.
Reset and play a different approach phrase — same target.
Over many phrases, the target becomes the gravitational center.
examples: Target: the 5th. Phrases approaching from above, below, and by leap
Target: root up an octave. Phrases that build tension with downstroke chugs
Target: the 3rd. Phrases descending into it from different angles
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_pickingHan.watchOut: The target landing should feel inevitable — if you speed up on the last note, it sounds accidental. Keep the timing grid steady.

pitchConstraint:targetLanding_pickingHan.interaction: Fingerpicked target landings are about resolving a rolling arpeggio-like phrase onto a specific pitch each time. The fingerpicking creates flowing approach phrases; the landing is always the same scale tone — usually plucked with a distinctive finger for emphasis. Fretting hand shapes the approach; picking hand always reserves the same finger for the target.
steps: Assign thumb, index, middle, ring as usual.
Choose a target scale tone — e.g., root up an octave on the 1st string, 5th fret.
Reserve ring finger for the target landing.
Build approach phrases using thumb, index, middle on other scale tones.
End each phrase by ringing the target with the ring finger.
examples: Target: root up an octave, ring finger plucks it after approach arpeggios
Target: the 3rd, middle finger always lands it
Target: the 5th, pluck it with ring as the resolution of each rolling phrase
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_pickingHan.watchOut: The reserved finger gets stiff if it's only used for the landing. Warm it up with other notes too, and let it hover above the target string.

pitchConstraint:targetLanding_pickingHan.interaction: Muted target landings give you a pitched percussion riff that always ends on the same note. The mute strips sustain from the approach notes, making the landing pop when it hits (release the mute slightly for emphasis). Fretting hand plans the approach; picking hand mutes throughout and opens up slightly for the target.
steps: Palm-mute at the bridge with picking hand.
Choose a target — e.g., the root on the 5th string, 5th fret.
Play approach notes stepwise or with leaps, all palm-muted.
On the target, slightly lift the palm to let the pitch ring out.
Re-mute and start the next approach phrase.
examples: Approach chugs from the 3rd and 5th, target on root
Descending muted phrases all landing on the 5th
Leap-heavy muted approaches to root up an octave
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_pickingHan.watchOut: Letting the palm lift too long on the target makes the landing sound disconnected from the approach. It's a split-second release, not a pause.

pitchConstraint:targetLanding_pickingHan.interaction: Hybrid picking target landings let you reserve the fingers or the pick specifically for the target tone, creating a consistent sonic signature. For example: bass walks with the pick on any scale tone, but the target landing is always plucked with ring finger up high. Fretting hand navigates; picking hand ends every phrase with the reserved hit.
steps: Hold pick, rest middle and ring on top strings.
Choose a target high scale tone — e.g., the 5th on the 1st string, 8th fret.
Play approach bass walks or scale runs with pick on low strings.
Reserve ring finger for the target tone on the high string.
Every phrase ends with that ring-finger landing on the target.
examples: Pick walks root-2nd-3rd-4th, ring finger snaps target 5th up top
Pick descends from b7 to 4th, ring snaps target 3rd on top string
Pick pedal on root, ring snaps target root up an octave each time
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_pickingHan.watchOut: The contrast between pick and finger attack makes the landing distinctive — which is the point, but don't let it sound jarring. Match the dynamic level.

pitchConstraint:targetLanding_pickingHan.interaction: Slide + target landing is exquisite — every phrase is a glide toward one specific fret on one specific string. The slide dynamics make the landing feel earned, like a plane touching down. Fretting hand wears the slide and always converges on the target fret; picking hand re-picks at the start of each approach.
steps: Wear slide on ring or pinky above one string.
Choose a target fret position — e.g., the 5th at the 12th fret, 3rd string.
Pick a starting scale tone anywhere on the string.
Slide toward the target, re-picking at a midpoint or letting it ring to the landing.
Always converge on the same target fret — the ear will start to anticipate it.
examples: Target: 12th fret (5th of a scale at the 5th fret), slide from various starting positions
Target: root up an octave, slide from lower scale tones on the same string
Target: 3rd high up the neck, approach via glides from below
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_pickingHan.watchOut: Pitch accuracy on the landing is everything — a slide that lands a quarter-tone flat destroys the target's authority. Use a reference drone to verify.

pitchConstraint:targetLanding_pickingHan.interaction: Bend-based target landings are blues and rock staples — you pick a starting scale tone and bend up to the target pitch every time. The target is defined by the bent pitch, not the fret. Fretting hand does all the work; picking hand attacks the start and lets the bend carry the phrase.
steps: Anchor on a starting scale tone on the 2nd or 3rd string around the 7th fret.
Choose a target scale tone a half-step or whole-step above the starting note.
Pick the starting tone, then bend to the target pitch.
Every phrase pattern ends with this same bend-and-land gesture.
Reference the target against a drone so your ear locks the pitch.
examples: Start on the 4th, bend a whole step to the target 5th
Start on b7, bend a whole step to the target root up an octave
Start on the 2nd, bend a half step to the target b3 for a blues feel
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_pickingHan.watchOut: Bends undershoot the target when the fretting hand tires. Rest and re-reference the target pitch often.

pitchConstraint:questionAnswer_pickingHa.interaction: Question-answer with downstrokes is classic riff construction — a rising downstroke question followed by a descending downstroke answer. The uniform attack makes each phrase feel like a statement and a reply. Fretting hand designs the rising and falling shapes; picking hand hammers downward throughout.
steps: Plan a short rising phrase — scale tones climbing from root toward the 5th.
Play the question with strict downstrokes, ending on an unresolved tone.
Plan a matching descending phrase — from the 5th or higher back down to root.
Play the answer with strict downstrokes, landing on root for resolution.
Loop question-answer pairs at a steady tempo.
examples: Q: root-2nd-3rd-4th. A: 5th-4th-3rd-root
Q: root-3rd-5th. A: root up an octave-b7-5th-root
Q: 2nd-4th-6th. A: 5th-3rd-root
pitchConstraint:questionAnswer_pickingHa.watchOut: The answer often loses attack because downstrokes get tired after the question. Keep both halves at the same dynamic so the conversation feels balanced.

pitchConstraint:questionAnswer_pickingHa.interaction: Fingerpicked Q&A is like a classical dialogue between two voices — the rising question arpeggiates upward through scale tones, the descending answer rolls back down. Finger assignments reverse between Q and A. Fretting hand shapes both phrases; picking hand rolls forward for Q and backward for A.
steps: Assign thumb, index, middle, ring to four strings low to high.
Play the rising question with thumb-index-middle-ring across four scale tones.
Anchor fretting hand around the 5th fret for the question shape.
Play the descending answer with ring-middle-index-thumb through descending scale tones.
Let the answer resolve on a low root for gravitational closure.
examples: Q: root-3rd-5th-root up an octave. A: 5th-3rd-2nd-root
Q: 2nd-4th-6th-root up an octave. A: 5th-3rd-root
Q climbing a two-octave arpeggio, A descending a scale back
pitchConstraint:questionAnswer_pickingHa.watchOut: The reversing finger order makes the answer feel slower than the question. Practice the answer direction alone until it's as fluid as the climb.

pitchConstraint:questionAnswer_pickingHa.interaction: Muted Q&A is a tight, rhythmic conversation — rising muted chugs posing a question, descending muted chugs answering. The mute keeps both phrases percussive and compact, and the rhythmic contour tells the ear which is which. Fretting hand climbs and descends; picking hand palm-mutes steadily.
steps: Palm-mute at the bridge throughout.
Play a rising muted phrase — e.g., four scale tones climbing — as the question.
Play a descending muted phrase — four scale tones back down — as the answer.
Keep the dynamics tight and the rhythm grid strict.
End the answer on the root or a strong scale tone for resolution.
examples: Q: muted climb from root to 4th. A: muted descent from 5th to root
Q: root-3rd-5th chugs. A: root up an octave-5th-3rd-root chugs
Q: low-to-high muted scale. A: high-to-low muted scale
pitchConstraint:questionAnswer_pickingHa.watchOut: The mute makes pitch harder to hear, so the Q and A can sound identical rhythmically. Make sure the contour (up vs down) is obvious.

pitchConstraint:questionAnswer_pickingHa.interaction: Hybrid-picked Q&A lets you literally assign the question to one hand role and the answer to another — pick the question on low strings, snap the answer with fingers on high strings, or vice versa. This makes the conversation audibly two-voiced. Fretting hand supplies both phrases; picking hand trades tools mid-phrase.
steps: Hold pick, rest middle and ring on top strings.
Pick a rising question on low strings — e.g., root-3rd-5th climbing.
Snap the descending answer on high strings with middle and ring — e.g., root up an octave-5th-3rd.
Keep the rhythmic feel consistent so the Q and A sound like dialogue.
Alternate who asks — sometimes the fingers can question and the pick can answer.
examples: Pick Q: root-2nd-3rd-4th. Fingers A: 5th-3rd-root on top strings
Pick Q: low scale walk up to the 5th. Fingers A: high scale walk from root up an octave down to 3rd
Pick Q: root-5th-root up an octave. Fingers A: 6th-3rd-root
pitchConstraint:questionAnswer_pickingHa.watchOut: The transition from pick to fingers often has a timing gap. Rehearse the Q-to-A handoff as one fluid motion.

pitchConstraint:questionAnswer_pickingHa.interaction: Slide Q&A is a swooping dialogue — a rising glide asking the question, a descending glide answering. The slide's continuous pitch motion makes each phrase feel vocal and expressive. Fretting hand wears the slide and reverses direction between Q and A; picking hand re-picks minimally.
steps: Wear slide on ring or pinky, stay on one string for cleanest Q&A.
Pick the root at the 5th fret, 3rd string.
Slide upward to a scale tone — the 5th at the 12th fret — as the question.
Re-pick and slide back down to the root as the answer.
Use behind-the-slide muting to keep each direction clean.
examples: Q: root sliding up to the 5th. A: 5th sliding back down to root
Q: the 2nd sliding up to the 6th. A: 6th sliding back to the 3rd
Q: swooping up to root up an octave. A: gliding back down to the 3rd
pitchConstraint:questionAnswer_pickingHa.watchOut: If the Q and A use the same pitch range, they sound like one round-trip slide instead of two phrases. Add a brief pause or dynamic contrast between them.

pitchConstraint:questionAnswer_pickingHa.interaction: Bend-based Q&A is the BB King special — pick a note, bend up (question), release the bend (answer). The question is rising tension, the answer is releasing it, all on one string with no fretting-hand movement. Fretting hand does everything via bend depth; picking hand provides the initial attack.
steps: Anchor on a scale tone on the 2nd or 3rd string at the 7th fret.
Pick once and bend up a whole step — that's your question (rising).
Release the bend slowly back down to the starting pitch — that's your answer (descending).
Add vibrato at the bottom of the release to settle the answer.
Chain multiple Q-A bend gestures for a vocal, bluesy conversation.
examples: Q: 4th bent to 5th. A: released back to 4th
Q: b7 bent to root up an octave. A: released back to b7
Q: 2nd bent to b3. A: released back to 2nd
pitchConstraint:questionAnswer_pickingHa.watchOut: The release is where Q&A bend phrasing dies — players rush and overshoot the starting pitch. Match the release speed to the bend-up speed for balance.

rhythmConstraint:river_dynamics:swell.interaction: Even quarter notes give the swell something stable to ride on. The pulse never changes, but the volume breathes underneath it, turning a metronomic line into something alive. The listener's ear locks onto the steady beat while the intensity tells the emotional story.
steps: Set a count of 8 quarter notes and plan your arc: beats 1-2 barely audible, beats 3-5 growing, beat 6 peak, beats 7-8 drop to silence
Pick any note to start on and lock it against a tonic drone
Play the first quarter note so quietly you almost can't hear it yourself
Add volume in tiny increments each beat, not jumps
Let the last quarter note fade to nothing before the next cycle begins
Sing the arc on a vowel first, then match it on your instrument
examples: 8 quarter notes, all on the root of the scale, volume rising from whisper to forte across beats 1-6, then dropping to silence on beats 7-8
Quarter notes walking down from the fifth to the root, swell peaking on the middle note
Four quarter notes on root, four on second scale degree, swell arc spanning both groups
rhythmConstraint:river_dynamics:swell.watchOut: Most students crescendo too fast and reach peak volume by beat 3, leaving nowhere to go. Plan the peak moment before you start and protect it.
rhythmConstraint:river_dynamics:swell.deeperInsight: The steady quarter note is a canvas. Dynamics are the paint. If the pulse wavers while you swell, the whole effect breaks.

rhythmConstraint:river_dynamics:terraces.interaction: Steady quarter notes with sudden volume steps create a sculptural, architectural feel. The rhythm gives you a grid; the dynamics carve it into levels. It's almost like each bar becomes a different room, and the listener walks through them one pulse at a time.
steps: Choose three volume levels: quiet, medium, loud — and memorize how each one feels in your body
Play four quarter notes at the quiet level, locked against a drone on the tonic
Jump immediately to the loud level for the next four quarters — no fade between
Drop to medium for the next four, another clean jump
Anchor every phrase on a scale degree and keep the pitch choices simple so the terraces are clearly audible
Record one pass and listen for whether the jumps sound like jumps or accidental slides
examples: Four quiet quarters on the root, then four loud quarters on the third, then four medium quarters on the fifth
Eight quarter notes walking up the scale, alternating two-quiet / two-loud / two-quiet / two-loud
Quarter notes on a repeated second scale degree, terracing between whisper and forte every two beats
rhythmConstraint:river_dynamics:terraces.watchOut: The trap is unconsciously crescendoing between terraces. The moment of the jump should sound almost startling — if it sounds smooth, you're fading.

rhythmConstraint:river_dynamics:whisper.interaction: Quarter notes at the quietest possible volume force precision onto the pulse. When there's no power to hide behind, every slight timing error becomes obvious. This combo teaches control — the steady flow has to hold up when stripped of dynamic variety.
steps: Set a drone on the tonic and play a quarter note on the root at your absolute quietest
Play eight more quarters at that same volume, all on different scale degrees
Listen for any note that accidentally jumps louder — that's a control leak
Breathe in tempo with the quarter notes to steady your body
Anchor every bar on the fifth scale degree so the ear has a reference point
Close your eyes for a pass and feel the pulse through the quietness
examples: Eight whispered quarter notes walking from the root up to the fifth and back
Quarter notes on a single held second scale degree, whispered for a full 16-beat cycle
Quiet quarters alternating between the third and the fifth, staying even across all beats
rhythmConstraint:river_dynamics:whisper.watchOut: Whisper volume tempts players to rush because quiet feels urgent. The pulse needs to stay exactly as grounded as it would at full volume.
