Notes on 'and of 2' and 'and of 4' with pitches 5 and root — a light skipping pattern.
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_phraseLength:1b.watchOut: The moment you slip a note onto beat 1, the whole trick collapses. Your body will want to resolve to the downbeat — train yourself to leave it alone and let the 'and' feel like home.

rhythmConstraint:offbeat_phraseLength:2b.interaction: Two bars of offbeat is where reggae and ska live — your notes are all on the back half of each beat, and the downbeats are felt, not played. With two bars to work with, you can set up a tight little groove that locks to the metronome through pure offset placement. The ear starts hearing the silent downbeats as part of the pattern.
steps: Play only on 'ands' — the upbeats of each beat in both bars.
Use bar 1 to set up a short melodic motif using 3-4 upbeat attacks.
Use bar 2 to answer or extend that motif.
Let the downbeats breathe — the metronome plays them for you.
Land the final note on the 'and of 4' of bar 2 for extra lift.
examples: Bar 1 'ands' of 1, 2, 3, 4: root, 2, 3, 2. Bar 2 'ands': 3, 5, 3, root.
Bar 1 upbeats: 5, 3, 5, 3. Bar 2 upbeats: 2, root, 2, root — a reggae call and response.
Bar 1 'ands' of 2 and 4 only: 3 and 5. Bar 2 same placement: 2 and root — sparse ska-style chops.
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_phraseLength:2b.watchOut: Most students sneak a downbeat in without noticing. Record yourself and listen — if you hear any attack on beats 1 or 3, go back and rebuild the phrase.

rhythmConstraint:offbeat_phraseLength:4b.interaction: Four bars of offbeat is a full groove paragraph — the metronome plays all sixteen downbeats, and you weave your melody entirely between them. This is the constraint of classic reggae guitar skanks or Motown horn stabs. The downbeats become a phantom pulse you ride against, and the contour of your offbeat line can stretch across all four bars with real development.
steps: Commit to every note landing on an 'and' or a subdivision between beats.
Bars 1-2: establish a simple offbeat motif near the root.
Bars 3-4: develop it higher, aiming for the fifth or sixth on offbeats.
Land your final note on 'and of 4' of bar 4, pulling toward the next downbeat but never arriving.
Keep the attacks crisp so the offbeats read clearly.
examples: Bars 1-2: upbeats climb root-2-3-2, 3-5-3-2. Bars 3-4: 5-6-5-3, 5-3-2-root, all on 'ands'.
Every 'and' of every beat gets a note: bar 1 root-2-3-2, bar 2 3-2-root-2, bar 3 5-3-5-3, bar 4 2-root-2-root.
Sparser: 'and of 2' and 'and of 4' only across four bars — 3, 5 | 5, 6 | 6, 5 | 3, root.
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_phraseLength:4b.watchOut: By bar three the offbeat illusion starts to crack — you'll feel your hand drifting toward beat 1. Tap your foot on the downbeats so you can physically feel where NOT to play.

rhythmConstraint:offbeat_phraseLength:8b.interaction: Eight bars of offbeat is serious groove endurance. Thirty-two downbeats pass while you never touch a single one. This is a test of internal pulse as much as melodic shape — you're building an 8-bar story entirely between the beats. Done well, it sounds like the melody is floating on top of a wave that carries it.
steps: Stay strictly on 'ands' and subdivisions for all eight bars.
Bars 1-2: low, motif-stating phrases near the root.
Bars 3-4: develop with a climbing contour toward the fifth.
Bars 5-6: peak around the sixth or high root, still only on offbeats.
Bars 7-8: descend back to the root, landing the final note on the last 'and' of bar 8.
Count '1 and 2 and 3 and 4 and' under your breath to stay locked.
examples: Bars 1-2 offbeats climb root-2-3-5. Bars 3-4 offbeats 5-6-7-6. Bars 5-6 peak 6-high-root-6-5. Bars 7-8 descend to root — all on 'ands'.
Reggae-style skank: 'and of 2' and 'and of 4' for all 8 bars, pitches climbing and descending root-3-5-6-5-3-2-root.
Dense offbeats: eight upbeats per bar (on every half-beat 'and') with a pentatonic contour arching and returning across the 8 bars.
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_phraseLength:8b.watchOut: Eight bars is long enough that fatigue makes you slip back onto downbeats in bar six or seven. If that happens, shorten the exercise to 4 bars first and build stamina before returning to 8.

rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_phraseLength.interaction: A rhythm seed in a 1-bar fragment is just the cell itself, said once. You pick a tiny rhythmic shape — a three-note grouping, a dotted figure — and play exactly one iteration across the bar. Because there's only room for one or two iterations, the seed IS the fragment.
steps: Choose a short rhythmic cell (three eighths, two sixteenths and a quarter, etc.).
Play the cell once or twice within the bar, fitting the pulse.
Vary the pitches between iterations while keeping the rhythm identical.
End the bar on a strong scale tone.
examples: Cell: three eighths plus a rest. Iteration 1 on beat 1: root-2-3. Iteration 2 on beat 3: 3-2-root.
Cell: dotted-eighth-sixteenth-quarter. Play once starting beat 1 on pitches 5-6-5, then rest.
Cell: four sixteenths. Two iterations: 3-2-root-2 on beat 1, 5-3-2-root on beat 3.
rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_phraseLength.watchOut: Don't change the rhythm between iterations — that kills the seed. The pitches move, the rhythm repeats. If you only fit one iteration in the bar, that's fine.

rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_phraseLength.interaction: Two bars is the sweet spot for a rhythm seed to breathe. You get to state the cell two to four times, which is enough for the listener to catch the pattern and enjoy how the pitches change against a fixed rhythm. Bar 1 sets the seed, bar 2 reinforces and answers it.
steps: Define a short rhythmic cell that fits cleanly within one beat or two.
Play the cell twice in bar 1 with a rising pitch contour.
Play the cell twice more in bar 2 with a descending answer.
Keep the rhythm absolutely identical each iteration.
Let the pitch changes tell the story.
examples: Cell: eighth-eighth-quarter. Bar 1: root-2-3, 3-2-root. Bar 2: 5-3-2, 2-root-root.
Cell: quarter-two-eighths. Bar 1: root-3-5, 5-3-2. Bar 2: 3-5-6, 5-3-root.
Cell: four sixteenths. Four iterations: root-2-3-2, 3-2-3-5, 5-6-5-3, 3-2-root-2.
rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_phraseLength.watchOut: The seed has to stay rhythmically locked or it stops being a seed. If your rhythm drifts between iterations, slow the tempo until you can hit it cleanly.

rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_phraseLength.interaction: Four bars is where rhythm seeds become a proper study — the cell gets four to eight iterations, and you can hear the pitch variation develop into a real contour. This is the territory of minimalist composition: one rhythmic DNA strand, melodic variation as the only storytelling tool. Every iteration is a new sentence on the same beat.
steps: Pick a short rhythmic cell (one beat or half-bar in length).
Iterate it four to eight times across the four bars.
Change the pitches each iteration while keeping the rhythm locked.
Shape the overall pitch contour as an arch: climb, peak, descend.
Land the final iteration on the root.
examples: Cell: eighth-eighth-quarter (one beat). Sixteen iterations — too many. Use eight by making cell span two beats. Pitches climb bar 1: root-2-3, 3-2-root. Bar 2: 3-5-3, 5-3-2. Bar 3: 5-6-5, 6-5-3. Bar 4: 5-3-2, 2-root-root.
Cell: sixteenth-sixteenth-eighth. Eight iterations across 4 bars. Pitches trace: root, 2, 3, 5, 6, 5, 3, root — one iteration per half-bar.
Cell: dotted-quarter-eighth-quarter (a full bar). Four iterations total. Pitch shape across bars: root-3, 3-5, 5-6, 3-root.
rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_phraseLength.watchOut: Four bars of the same rhythmic cell can feel hypnotic or monotonous — the difference is entirely in how thoughtfully you shape the pitches. Plan the contour before you start or you'll drift.

rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_phraseLength.interaction: Eight bars of a rhythmic seed is a full minimalist etude — the same tiny rhythmic cell repeats eight, twelve, or sixteen times while the pitches tell an entire melodic story on top. Steve Reich and Philip Glass live here. The listener's ear locks into the rhythm in bar one and then follows every pitch change closely because the rhythm gives it a stable frame.
steps: Choose a rhythmic cell that fits cleanly into one or two beats.
Iterate it consistently for all eight bars — no rhythm changes.
Bars 1-2: establish the motif near the root.
Bars 3-4: climb the pitches toward the fifth.
Bars 5-6: peak pitches around the sixth or high root.
Bars 7-8: descend and resolve to the root on the final iteration.
examples: Cell: eighth-eighth-quarter. Sixteen iterations across 8 bars. Pitches: root-2-3, 3-2-root, 3-5-3, 5-3-2 (bars 1-2), continuing climb to 6-5 peak in bars 5-6, descent to root by bar 8.
Cell: four sixteenths (one beat). Thirty-two iterations. Pitch trace: slowly climbing root through 6 across bars 1-5, holding peak in 5-6, descending to root by bar 8.
Cell: dotted-eighth-sixteenth-quarter. Eight iterations (one per bar). Bars 1-8 pitches: root, 3, 5, 6, 6, 5, 3, root.
rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_phraseLength.watchOut: By bar five your rhythmic cell will start degrading — add ghost notes, lose the swing, shift the accent. Record the first bar, loop it in your head as a template, and check every iteration against that mental recording.

rhythmConstraint:triplets_phraseLength:1.interaction: Triplets in a 1-bar fragment gives you twelve notes — three per beat — in a rolling, rounded pulse. The fragment is tiny but dense. You barely have room for a melodic idea, so the rhythm itself becomes the expression. It's one gesture of rolling triplets that opens and closes in four beats.
steps: Play three notes per beat for all four beats — twelve notes total.
Keep the triplets perfectly even, no leaning.
Contour the line as a small arch: climb on beats 1-2, descend on beats 3-4.
Start and end on the root for a clean fragment.
examples: Beat 1: root-2-3. Beat 2: 3-5-3. Beat 3: 5-3-2. Beat 4: 2-root-root.
Beat 1: 3-2-root. Beat 2: root-2-3. Beat 3: 3-5-3. Beat 4: 2-root-root.
All twelve triplet notes pentatonic: root-2-3 | 5-6-5 | 3-2-root | 2-3-root.
rhythmConstraint:triplets_phraseLength:1.watchOut: Triplets at faster tempos want to turn into sixteenths in your body. Count 'tri-pa-let, tri-pa-let' under your breath or tap the quarter pulse with your foot to stay honest.

rhythmConstraint:triplets_phraseLength:2.interaction: Two bars of triplets gives you twenty-four notes in a rolling wave — a full sentence in that classic 6/8 or shuffle-feel texture. You can now set up a real question-and-answer: six triplets climb in bar one, six triplets descend in bar two. The rolling pulse is what makes the phrase feel flowing instead of frantic.
steps: Play three notes per beat, every beat, for two full bars.
Use bar 1 to climb from the root toward the fifth or sixth.
Use bar 2 to descend back to the root.
Keep accent on the first note of each triplet group, light on the other two.
Aim for a waltz-like lilt, not a sprint.
examples: Bar 1 triplets: root-3-5 | 5-3-2 | 3-5-6 | 5-3-2. Bar 2 triplets: 3-2-root | 2-3-2 | root-2-root | root-root-root.
Bar 1: stepwise climb root-2-3, 2-3-5, 3-5-6, 5-6-5. Bar 2: descent 6-5-3, 5-3-2, 3-2-root, 2-root-root.
rhythmConstraint:triplets_phraseLength:2.watchOut: Triplets tend to bunch — the first two notes rush and the third comes late. Slow the tempo until you can place all three evenly, then build speed.

rhythmConstraint:triplets_phraseLength:4.interaction: Four bars of triplets is forty-eight notes of rolling pulse — a long, churning melodic paragraph. You have real room to develop an idea now: bars 1-2 set it up, bars 3-4 answer and resolve. This is the territory of triplet-based solos in blues, shuffle, and 6/8 feels. The trick is making forty-eight notes feel like one long gesture, not forty-eight separate events.
steps: Commit to three notes per beat across all four bars.
Bars 1-2: build a motif near the root and climb toward the fifth.
Bar 3: peak around the sixth or high root with the densest triplet runs.
Bar 4: descend and resolve to the root on the final triplet.
Accent the downbeats lightly so the listener can still hear the quarter-note frame.
examples: Bar 1: root-2-3 climbing through three triplets to 3-5-3. Bar 2: 5-3-5 | 5-6-5 peak. Bar 3: 6-5-6 | 5-6-5 | descending. Bar 4: 3-2-root | 2-root-root.
Bars 1-2: stepwise triplet climb root through 5. Bar 3: 6-5-6 triplets peaking. Bar 4: descending triplets 5-3-2, 2-root-root.
Bar 1 pentatonic triplets root-2-3 | 3-5-3 | 5-3-2 | 2-root-root. Bars 2-3 climb motif a third higher. Bar 4 resolves back to root.
rhythmConstraint:triplets_phraseLength:4.watchOut: Around bar three the triplets will try to become sixteenths in your body, especially if the tempo is quick. Keep the foot on the quarter pulse and count 'one-trip-let, two-trip-let' internally.

rhythmConstraint:triplets_phraseLength:8.interaction: Eight bars of triplets is a rolling torrent — ninety-six notes in a continuous wave. This is a full epic in triplet feel, the kind of extended passage you hear in a jazz shuffle solo or a 6/8 fiddle tune. Pacing matters enormously: the rolling pulse never stops, so you need to shape the pitches across a long arc to keep the listener engaged. Stamina and planning are both required.
steps: Commit to three notes per beat for all 32 beats without breaks.
Bars 1-2: low register motif introduction near the root.
Bars 3-4: climbing triplet runs toward the fifth.
Bars 5-6: peak region — triplet runs around the sixth, seventh, and high root.
Bars 7-8: descend and land the final triplet on the root.
Accent beat 1 of every bar very slightly to keep the eight-bar structure audible.
examples: Bars 1-2: triplet motif root-2-3, 3-2-root repeating. Bars 3-4: climb motif to 5-6-5, 6-5-3. Bars 5-6: peak 6-7-6, high root runs. Bars 7-8: descend 6-5-3, 3-2-root, 2-root-root.
Bars 1-2: low pentatonic triplets root-3-5 looping. Bars 3-4: same shape up a third. Bars 5-6: high peak triplets 6-high-root-6. Bars 7-8: full descent in triplets back to root.
Eight bars of continuous triplet waves, pitches tracing a slow arch root-2-3-5-6-6-5-3-2-root across the bars.
rhythmConstraint:triplets_phraseLength:8.watchOut: Ninety-six triplets in a row will expose every weakness in your pulse. If you drop the feel in bar four, the whole epic collapses. Practice bars 1-4 and 5-8 separately until clean, then join them — and always keep the foot tapping quarters.

rhythmConstraint:river_register:chest.interaction: Even quarter-note flow meets grounded chest register. One note per beat, all of them vibrating in the sternum. The challenge is keeping the chest weight from getting heavy and dragging the tempo — the river has to stay moving even though the tone is thick.
steps: Plant your feet and feel the breath drop into the low belly before you start
Sing steady quarter notes on scale degree 1, feeling the vibration in the sternum and ribs
Keep the abdominal support constant — every quarter note gets the same push from below
Move through 1-2-3-2-1 without letting the chest weight pull you behind the beat
Check: if you feel throat squeeze, the support has collapsed — reset the belly engagement
examples: Four quarters on tonic, four on the second, rocking between them
Walking quarters up to the fifth and back down by step
1-3-5-3-1-3-5-3 in steady quarters, chest anchored
rhythmConstraint:river_register:chest.watchOut: Chest register makes singers sit on the beat instead of moving through it. If the sternum buzz turns into jaw tension, your support stopped working and you're muscling the tone. Let the breath do the work.

rhythmConstraint:river_register:head.interaction: Quarter-note flow in the light head register. Every beat gets a floaty note placed in the mask — cheeks, bridge of nose, behind the eyes. The trick is keeping the airstream steady enough that the head tone doesn't thin out or wobble between notes.
steps: Start with a gentle 'ng' to find the buzz behind the upper teeth and cheekbones
Sing quarter notes on the fifth degree, feeling placement stay high in the mask
Keep the soft palate lifted — the vibration should feel like it's floating above the roof of the mouth
Step through 5-6-5-4-5 in even quarters, same lightness on every beat
Notice if the tone drops into the throat — reset by sighing downward into the mask again
examples: Quarter notes circling the fifth, stepping up to the sixth and back
A steady walk from the fifth up to the tonic above and back down
Repeated quarters on the third, feeling the mask buzz on each one
rhythmConstraint:river_register:head.watchOut: Head voice in steady quarters tempts singers to push for volume. If you feel the larynx rising or the cheeks tightening, you've left head placement and started yelling lightly. Sigh back into the mask.

rhythmConstraint:river_register:mixed.interaction: Even quarters in the mixed register — bridging chest and head without an audible flip. The river rhythm makes every transition visible because each beat lands exposed. You have to keep the blend consistent note to note, with no gear shifts.
steps: Find the mixed placement first: hum a slide from low to high, feeling the vibration travel from ribs up into the cheeks
Sing quarter notes crossing the fifth degree, holding the same blend of chest ring and head float
Keep abdominal support steady — the blend lives on breath pressure, not throat manipulation
Walk 3-4-5-6-5-4-3 in quarters, listening for any gear change through the passaggio
If you hear a bump, slow the tempo and feel where the body stopped supporting
examples: Quarter-note walk from the third up to the sixth and back
Rocking quarters between the fourth and fifth through the passaggio
Steady quarters on a 1-3-5-3-1 arpeggio shape
rhythmConstraint:river_register:mixed.watchOut: Mixed voice collapses into chest pushing or head thinning under sustained quarters. If one beat sounds thick and the next sounds airy, the support switched off mid-phrase. Keep the ribs expanded through all four beats of the bar.

rhythmConstraint:river_register:falsetto.interaction: Quarter notes in pure falsetto — airy, detached from chest resonance, floating on top of the breath. The steady rhythm exposes any wobble in airflow because there's no rhythmic trick to hide behind. Every beat has to have the same feathery quality.
steps: Begin with a soft 'hoo' sigh to find the disconnected falsetto placement
Sing quarters on the sixth degree, feeling the breath flow steadily from the belly outward
Keep the throat completely open — the vibration should feel like it's happening beyond the soft palate, not in the neck
Step through 5-6-7-6-5 in even quarters with identical airiness on each note
If the tone tightens, stop and sigh — falsetto dies when you grip
examples: Quarters rocking between the fifth and sixth high in the range
A descending walk from the tonic above down to the fifth
Repeated quarters on the seventh degree, feeling the air move on every beat
rhythmConstraint:river_register:falsetto.watchOut: Falsetto on steady quarters almost always loses breath flow by beat three. If the tone goes from airy to squeezed, your ribs collapsed and you're pinching the cords together. Re-expand and let the breath carry the sound.

rhythmConstraint:river_register:breathy.interaction: Quarter-note flow with more breath than tone — intimate, close-mic quality on every beat. The rhythm has to stay steady while the airflow is doing most of the work. It's harder than it sounds because breathy tone drains the breath fast.
steps: Take a low, wide breath that expands the ribs and back before starting
Sing quarters on the third degree, letting audible air mix with every note
Keep the support active — breathy does not mean collapsed; the belly still engages
Walk 3-4-5-4-3 in even quarters, same air-to-tone ratio on each beat
Check the ribs at beat four — if they've dropped, breathe and restart
examples: Whispered quarters rocking between the third and fourth
A gentle descending walk from the fifth down to the tonic
Repeated quarters on the second degree, close and airy
rhythmConstraint:river_register:breathy.watchOut: Breathy tone burns through air fast. By the third or fourth beat, untrained singers run out and start pushing or disconnecting completely. Keep the ribs wide and meter the air like you're sipping it out.

rhythmConstraint:burst_register:chest.interaction: Four to six fast notes punched out in chest register, then two or three beats of silence. The bursts need sternum weight and solid support to land with authority, and the silences have to feel intentional — not like you're catching your breath.
steps: Set the support low and wide before the burst fires
Release 4-5 fast notes on scale degrees 1-2-3-2-1, all anchored in the sternum vibration
Stop cleanly — the chest tone should cut off with the abdominal engagement still active
Use the silence to reset breath without collapsing the ribs
Feel the body stay 'loaded' during the rest, ready for the next burst
examples: Fast 1-2-3-2-1 burst, two beats of silence, repeat
Six notes climbing 1-2-3-4-3-1 then rest
Four punched notes on the tonic and third, then three beats of rest
rhythmConstraint:burst_register:chest.watchOut: Chest bursts tempt singers to yell. If your throat feels scratchy after a burst, you pushed from the neck instead of firing from the core. The belly throws the notes — the throat just lets them through.

rhythmConstraint:burst_register:head.interaction: Quick flurry in head voice, then silence. The bursts are light, mask-placed notes that dart out and stop, like pebbles skipping on water. Speed plus lightness is tricky — most singers either slow down to stay light or get heavy to go fast.
steps: Warm the mask with a few light sighs before the first burst
Fire 4-5 quick notes around the fifth and sixth degrees, all sitting in the cheekbones
Keep the breath pressure gentle — head bursts need flow, not force
Cut off cleanly and feel the placement stay high during the rest
On the next burst, check: is the vibration still behind the nose, or did it drop into the throat?
examples: Quick 5-6-5-4-5 flurry, two beats rest, repeat
Five fast notes walking up from the fifth to the tonic above, then silence
A rapid 6-5-4-5-6 burst high in the mask
rhythmConstraint:burst_register:head.watchOut: Speed pulls head voice down into chest. If the bursts start light and finish with a thicker, lower tone, the placement dropped mid-flurry. Keep imagining the sound floating out above the roof of the mouth.

rhythmConstraint:burst_register:mixed.interaction: Fast notes through the bridge register, then space. The bursts cross the passaggio at speed, which means the blend has to be locked in before you start — you can't adjust mid-burst. The silences are for resetting support, not panicking.
steps: Pre-set the mixed placement with a slow hum crossing the fifth
Fire a 4-6 note burst through the passaggio, ribs wide the whole time
Keep the blend consistent — no beat should sound thicker or thinner than the others
End the burst cleanly and feel the support hold during the rest
Re-check the blend on every new burst; the first note sets the tone for all of them
examples: Fast 3-4-5-6-5 through the bridge, rest, repeat
Six quick notes climbing 4-5-6-7-6-5 then silence
A burst rocking 5-6-5-6-5 right in the passaggio zone
rhythmConstraint:burst_register:mixed.watchOut: Mixed bursts expose every weakness in the blend because there's no time to finesse. If you hear a crack, the support wasn't set before the first note. The fix is always earlier — prepare before you start.

rhythmConstraint:burst_register:falsetto.interaction: Airy, disconnected flurries at speed, then silence. Falsetto bursts feel like puffs of air carrying pitch — fast and feathery. The challenge is keeping the breath flowing during a quick run; most singers pinch halfway through.
steps: Sigh a soft 'hoo' to find the disconnected falsetto placement
Release 4-5 quick notes around the sixth and seventh degrees, breath flowing through all of them
Feel the ribs stay open — do not collapse on the burst
Cut the tone cleanly and keep the throat relaxed during the silence
On each new burst, check that the first and last notes have the same airy quality
examples: Fast 5-6-7-6-5 flurry in upper range, rest, repeat
Five quick notes walking from the sixth up and back, then silence
A burst on 6-7-6-5-6 high and airy
rhythmConstraint:burst_register:falsetto.watchOut: Falsetto bursts die when singers grip. If the burst starts airy and ends squeezed, you ran out of flow and compressed the cords. Shorter bursts first, longer only when breath support holds up.

rhythmConstraint:burst_register:breathy.interaction: Whispered flurries, then silence — almost like quick sighs with pitch. The close-mic quality has to survive the speed, which is unusual because breathy tone normally wants to be slow. The silences are for re-oxygenating.
steps: Take a low, wide breath that fills the ribs and back
Release 4-5 fast notes on the third and fourth degrees, more air than tone
Engage the belly to meter the air — breathy bursts drain fast
Stop cleanly and feel if the ribs held during the burst
On the next burst, listen for consistent air-to-tone ratio across all the notes
examples: Whispered 3-4-5-4-3 flurry, two beats rest, repeat
Five quick intimate notes walking up from the second to the fifth and back
A close burst on 4-3-2-3-4, soft and airy
rhythmConstraint:burst_register:breathy.watchOut: Breathy bursts are the hardest breath management in vocals. If the last note of a burst has no air left, you didn't ration the airflow. Expand the ribs before each one and sip the breath out like you're painting with it.

rhythmConstraint:space_register:chest.interaction: Half of every bar is silence, and the notes that do sound are anchored in chest register. The rest becomes a feature — it frames the chest weight. You have to resist filling the space with fidget and let the sternum buzz hang in the air.
steps: Take a grounded breath and feel the support settle low
Sing two chest-anchored notes on the tonic and third, then rest for two beats
Let the silence breathe — keep the body 'on' but not singing
Feel the ribs stay wide during the rest; do not collapse
Re-enter the next note from the same sternum vibration, not from a fresh breath grab
examples: Two quarters on 1-3, two beats rest, repeat
A single held note on the fifth in chest, then two beats of silence
Notes on 3 and 1 grounded in chest, then rest, then 5 and 3
rhythmConstraint:space_register:chest.watchOut: Space tempts singers to take loud breaths or bail out of support during the rest. If your next entrance sounds weaker than the last, the body stopped working during the silence. The rest is active.

rhythmConstraint:space_register:head.interaction: Floating single notes or small clusters in head voice, surrounded by silence. This is one of the most exposed combinations — every note is naked, and the head register is delicate. The silences frame each note like a single brushstroke.
steps: Find mask placement with a quiet 'ng' hum before the phrase
Sing one or two head-voice notes on the sixth degree, feeling them float in the cheekbones
Let the note bloom and then release cleanly into the rest
Keep the soft palate lifted during the silence — placement does not reset
Re-enter the next note from the same high buzz, not from a new attack
examples: A single note on the sixth, two beats of silence, repeat
Two head-voice notes on 5 and 6, then rest
A held note on the tonic above in head voice, then silence
rhythmConstraint:space_register:head.watchOut: Floating head notes in silence expose every tiny wobble. If the note bloomed and then wavered, the breath stopped flowing. Head voice needs constant air, even on a sustained single note.

rhythmConstraint:space_register:mixed.interaction: A note or two in the blend, then silence. The space gives you time to reset — but also exposes whether your blend was solid on the note that just sounded. You can't hide a weak bridge in a phrase with this much rest.
steps: Pre-set the mixed placement with a hum across the fifth
Sing one or two notes in the passaggio zone, feeling ring and float at once
Release cleanly and let the ribs stay expanded during the rest
Listen during the silence — did the note sustain evenly, or did it drift?
Re-enter with the same blend, not a slightly different one
examples: A single note on the fifth in mixed, two beats rest, repeat
Two notes on 4 and 5 in the blend, then silence
A held note crossing 5 to 6 in mixed voice, then rest
rhythmConstraint:space_register:mixed.watchOut: Space makes a weak blend obvious. If the held note started mixed and ended thin, head took over because support dropped. Engage the belly through the full length of every note.

rhythmConstraint:space_register:falsetto.interaction: A single airy falsetto note or short cluster, then silence. The disconnected quality needs steady airflow even during a lone note, and the rest has to hold the mood without letting your support slump. It's meditative and surprisingly difficult.
steps: Sigh softly to find the disconnected falsetto placement
Sing one or two notes on the sixth or seventh degrees, pure air carrying pitch
Let the note fade and feel the ribs stay open through the silence
Keep the throat relaxed — falsetto dies with any grip
Re-enter the next note from the same open, airy setup
examples: A single falsetto note on the sixth, two beats rest, repeat
Two airy notes on 6 and 7, then silence
A held disconnected note on the tonic above, then rest
rhythmConstraint:space_register:falsetto.watchOut: Falsetto in silence tempts singers to reset too hard and start the next note with a pressed sound. The breath is always flowing, even between notes. Keep sipping air, keep ribs wide.

rhythmConstraint:space_register:breathy.interaction: Whispered notes or phrases with half the bar in silence. Intimate, almost spoken, with long pauses. The breathy tone has to feel like a secret — and the silences have to feel as present as the notes themselves. This is a patience exercise.
steps: Take a slow low breath that expands ribs and back
Sing one or two breathy notes on the third degree, more air than tone
Let the note fade into the rest without tightening
Meter the air during the note — do not dump it all at once
Enter the next phrase from the same calm, grounded support
examples: A single whispered note on the third, two beats rest, repeat
Two intimate notes on 2 and 3, then silence
A held breathy note on the fifth, then rest
rhythmConstraint:space_register:breathy.watchOut: Breathy tone in space exposes breath management more than any other combination. If the note runs out before the bar does, you didn't ration the air. Start with less than you think you need.

rhythmConstraint:offbeat_register:chest.interaction: Notes land between the beats — never on 1 or 3 — and they're all in grounded chest register. The body has to feel the downbeat without singing it, which creates a powerful push-and-pull. Chest weight plus syncopation is where funk and soul live.
steps: Tap the downbeats with your foot while keeping the voice quiet
Sing chest-anchored notes on the 'and' of 1 and the 'and' of 2, feeling sternum vibration
Use the support to punch the offbeats — the belly fires on every 'and'
Keep the foot solid on 1 and 3 so you can feel where you're NOT singing
Release each offbeat cleanly, ready for the next one
examples: Chest notes on the 'ands' of 1 and 2 on the tonic and third
A syncopated line on 'and-2-and-and-4' using degrees 1 and 5
Offbeat punches on the third degree between beats 2 and 3
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_register:chest.watchOut: Chest plus offbeats tempts singers to anticipate and land on the beat accidentally. If your note keeps sliding back to the downbeat, your foot isn't really keeping time — it's following the voice. Reverse that.

rhythmConstraint:offbeat_register:head.interaction: Light head-voice notes landing between beats. The floaty placement doesn't care about groove, so you have to manufacture it with support and body feel. Syncopated head voice is rare and lovely when it works.
steps: Find mask placement with a quiet 'ng' hum
Tap the downbeats and sing head-voice notes on the 'ands'
Place the notes on the sixth and seventh degrees, feeling cheek vibration
Use belly engagement to time the offbeats — placement stays high
Keep the foot solid on 1 and 3; the voice plays in the gaps
examples: Head notes on the 'and' of 1 and 'and' of 2 on the sixth degree
A syncopated line floating on 5 and 6 between beats
Offbeat entries on the tonic above, light and placed
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_register:head.watchOut: Head voice in syncopation tends to drift off the rhythm because the light placement feels disconnected from the body. Anchor the groove in your feet, not your ears.

rhythmConstraint:offbeat_register:mixed.interaction: Syncopated notes in the blend, landing between beats across the passaggio. The mixed register has to stay locked in while the rhythm is playing games. It's the combination you hear in a lot of soul and R&B phrasing.
steps: Pre-set the mixed placement with a slow hum
Tap 1 and 3 with your foot while staying silent
Sing blended notes on the 'ands' crossing the fifth degree
Keep ribs wide — support carries both the blend and the offbeat timing
Feel the body fire on every syncopation, not just the vocal cords
examples: Blended notes on 'and-2-and-3' using degrees 4, 5, and 6
A syncopated phrase through the passaggio on 5 and 6
Offbeat entries on the fifth degree in mixed voice
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_register:mixed.watchOut: Mixed syncopation falls apart if the blend slips even once. A missed offbeat often comes with a gear change. Keep support constant — rhythm lives in the belly.

rhythmConstraint:offbeat_register:falset.interaction: Airy, disconnected notes landing between beats. The falsetto quality plus syncopation gives a ghostly, playful feel — like the voice is skipping in and out of the track. Timing is the challenge because falsetto feels floaty.
steps: Sigh softly into falsetto placement
Tap the downbeats with your foot
Fire light airy notes on the 'ands' on the sixth and seventh degrees
Keep the breath flowing between notes — do not pinch to place the rhythm
Feel the body mark time while the voice plays the gaps
examples: Falsetto notes on 'and' of 1 and 'and' of 3 on the sixth
A syncopated airy line on 6 and 7 high in the range
Offbeat entries on the tonic above, disconnected and playful
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_register:falset.watchOut: Falsetto plus offbeats is disorienting because both elements feel weightless. If you lose the beat, stop and clap quarters out loud before trying again. The body has to know the downbeat.

rhythmConstraint:offbeat_register:breath.interaction: Whispered notes landing in the cracks between the beats. Intimate and rhythmic at once — close-mic pop and neo-soul territory. The tone is soft but the timing has to be razor sharp, which is counterintuitive.
steps: Take a low wide breath and set the support
Tap the downbeats with your foot
Sing breathy notes on the 'ands' on the third and fourth degrees, air mixing with tone
Meter the breath — short offbeats drain air fast
Feel the belly fire on each offbeat even though the tone is soft
examples: Whispered offbeats on 'and-2-and-3' using 3 and 4
Breathy syncopated line on the third degree
Close, intimate offbeats on 4 and 5
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_register:breath.watchOut: Breathy tone on offbeats makes timing mushy because the attack is soft. If the rhythm gets vague, the body isn't committing to each 'and'. Sharp support, soft tone.

rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_register:che.interaction: A short rhythmic cell repeats with changing pitches, all grounded in chest register. The consistency of the rhythm lets you focus on keeping the sternum vibration steady across different notes. It's a great way to feel the body without the brain working overtime on rhythm.
steps: Choose a short cell like 'dum da-dum' and internalize it before singing
Sing the cell first on the tonic, feeling chest weight in the sternum
Repeat the same cell on the third, keeping identical body engagement
Move to the fifth and back, cell unchanged, chest anchor unchanged
Notice if any pitch change broke the support — reset if so
examples: A 'long-short-short' cell on 1, then 3, then 5
A 'short-short-long' cell walking up degrees 1-2-3-4
A syncopated cell repeated on 1, 3, and 5 in chest
rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_register:che.watchOut: Chest weight plus pitch changes tempts singers to lift the larynx on higher notes. If the second or third repetition sounds tighter, support dropped as the pitch rose. Keep the belly low and the sternum open.

rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_register:hea.interaction: A rhythmic cell repeats at different pitches in head voice. Light, placed, consistent. The rhythm becomes a frame for exploring mask placement across the upper range. It's a meditative, almost hypnotic exercise.
steps: Hum to find mask placement before the first cell
Sing the cell on the fifth degree, feeling cheek and nose buzz
Repeat on the sixth — same placement, same lightness
Move to the tonic above, cell unchanged
Check every repetition: is the buzz still behind the upper teeth?
examples: A 'long-short' cell on 5, then 6, then the tonic above
A three-note cell walking up 5-6-7 at each pitch level
A repeated rhythmic figure on the sixth and then the seventh
rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_register:hea.watchOut: Head voice across repetitions drifts downward into the throat. If the third or fourth repetition sounds heavier, you let the placement drop. Re-sigh up into the mask.

rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_register:mix.interaction: A rhythmic cell repeated at shifting pitches through the passaggio. The blend has to stay consistent through every repetition — which is the hardest part of the combination. Any wobble in support on repetition three will sound like a crack.
steps: Set mixed placement with a slow hum
Sing the cell on the fourth degree, ribs wide
Repeat on the fifth, blend identical, support identical
Move to the sixth — still in the blend, still ringing and floating
Listen for any repetition that felt different; that's where support slipped
examples: A 'long-short-short' cell on 4, then 5, then 6
A syncopated cell repeated through the passaggio degrees
A three-note cell walking up 3-4-5-6 at each pitch level
rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_register:mix.watchOut: Mixed repetition is where the blend breaks because support dips between cells. If the third repetition cracks, the belly stopped engaging during the micro-breaths. Keep the core active across the whole exercise.

rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_register:fal.interaction: A cell repeated at different pitches in airy falsetto. Disconnected and consistent. The breath has to flow the same way through every repetition, which is where most singers fail — they grip by repetition four.
steps: Sigh into falsetto placement
Sing the cell on the sixth, ribs open and wide
Repeat on the seventh with identical airflow
Move to the tonic above, breath still flowing, throat still loose
Feel the ribs — if they collapsed, that's why the last cell squeezed
examples: A 'short-short-long' cell on 6, then 7, then the tonic above
A rhythmic figure repeated on degrees 5, 6, and 7
A three-note cell at different falsetto pitches
rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_register:fal.watchOut: Falsetto repetition drains breath fast. By the third cell the ribs usually collapse. Take a small, low breath between cells — don't gasp, just re-expand.

rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_register:bre.interaction: A whispered cell repeated at different pitches. Intimate and rhythmic. The close-mic quality has to stay consistent across repetitions, and the airflow has to be metered carefully because breathy cells eat breath.
steps: Take a low wide breath before the first cell
Sing the cell on the third degree, air mixing with tone
Repeat on the fourth with identical air-to-tone ratio
Move to the fifth, still soft, still supported
Feel the belly work across every repetition — breathy does not mean relaxed
examples: A 'long-short' cell on 3, then 4, then 5
A repeated intimate rhythmic figure on the third and fourth
A three-note cell whispered on degrees 2, 3, and 4
rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_register:bre.watchOut: Breathy repetition runs out of breath by cell three if you don't ration. Start each cell like you barely have enough air — that discipline keeps the last one as intimate as the first.

rhythmConstraint:triplets_register:chest.interaction: Three notes per beat in chest register — a rolling, urgent feel. Fast chest triplets are a wall of sound when they work, because every note carries sternum weight. The danger is the tongue or jaw trying to do the work instead of the support.
steps: Anchor the support low and wide before the first triplet
Roll triplets across the tonic, second, and third, all grounded in sternum vibration
Let the belly fire each triplet — the throat stays passive
Keep the jaw loose; the tongue is the only thing that moves quickly
After each bar, check: is the sternum still buzzing, or did I switch to neck muscles?
examples: Triplets rolling 1-2-3-1-2-3 on a steady beat
A chest-weighted run on 1-2-1-2-3-2 in triplets
Fast triplet figures crossing degrees 1 through 5
rhythmConstraint:triplets_register:chest.watchOut: Chest triplets are the easiest way to hurt yourself. If your throat feels tight or scratchy, you stopped supporting and started yelling fast. Slow the tempo until the belly can do the work again.

rhythmConstraint:triplets_register:head.interaction: Rolling triplets in head voice — three per beat, light and placed in the mask. The combination of speed and lightness is genuinely hard because most singers either slow down to stay light or get heavy to go fast. Both ruin it.
steps: Hum softly to set mask placement before starting
Roll triplets around the fifth and sixth degrees, each note floating in the cheekbones
Keep the breath pressure gentle — fast head voice runs on airflow, not force
Feel the soft palate stay lifted across the entire run
If the tone thickens, stop and sigh back up into the mask before continuing
examples: Triplet rolls on 5-6-5-6-5-6 high in the range
A light triplet figure climbing 5-6-7-6-5-6
Fast triplets circling the sixth degree in head voice
rhythmConstraint:triplets_register:head.watchOut: Speed drags head voice down into chest. If the triplets started light and ended pressed, the placement dropped. Lightness first, speed second — do not trade one for the other.

rhythmConstraint:triplets_register:mixed.interaction: Fast triplets through the passaggio in the blend. The rolling rhythm crosses the break at speed, which means the support has to hold absolutely steady. Any dip in the belly and you hear a crack in the middle of a triplet — ugly and unmistakable.
steps: Pre-set the mixed placement with a slow hum across the fifth
Roll triplets across degrees 4, 5, and 6, ribs wide
Keep the blend consistent across every triplet — no beat should shift color
Feel the body fire on every group of three, supporting through the break
Listen for cracks; they are always a support drop, not a technique flaw
examples: Triplet rolls 4-5-6-5-4-5 through the passaggio
A blended triplet figure on 5-6-7-6-5-4
Fast triplets crossing the fifth in mixed voice
rhythmConstraint:triplets_register:mixed.watchOut: Mixed triplets expose every tiny support lapse. A crack in the middle of a run means the belly disengaged for one micro-moment. Slow it down until the support never wavers.

rhythmConstraint:triplets_register:false.interaction: Airy triplets in the upper range — genuinely one of the hardest combinations in this whole matrix. Fast, disconnected, and breath-dependent. Most singers either pinch to go fast or collapse to stay airy. The sweet spot requires serious breath control.
steps: Sigh into falsetto placement and expand the ribs wide
Roll triplets around the sixth and seventh degrees, pure airflow carrying pitch
Keep the throat completely open — any grip and the run dies
Feel the belly meter the air steadily across every group of three
If the tone tightens or breaks, stop immediately and reset — do not push through
examples: Airy triplet rolls on 6-7-6-7-6-7 high in the range
A light falsetto triplet figure on 5-6-7-6-5-6
Fast disconnected triplets circling the seventh degree
rhythmConstraint:triplets_register:false.watchOut: Falsetto triplets are technique Kryptonite. The second you grip, the run collapses into noise. Work this slowly — start at half tempo and only speed up when the breath holds perfectly.

rhythmConstraint:triplets_register:breat.interaction: Whispered triplets at speed — intimate rolls, like quick rustling. The combination is unusual because breathy tone normally wants to be slow and triplets want to drive. Makes a close-mic, rushed-secret kind of quality when it works.
steps: Take a very low, very wide breath before the first triplet
Roll triplets on the third and fourth degrees, air mixing with tone
Engage the belly hard — breathy triplets drain air faster than anything
Keep the tongue loose and fast; the body is working, not the throat
Feel the ribs stay open through the entire run
examples: Whispered triplet rolls on 3-4-3-4-3-4
A soft intimate triplet figure on 2-3-4-3-2-3
Close breathy triplets circling the fourth degree
rhythmConstraint:triplets_register:breat.watchOut: Breathy triplets run out of air in under a bar if you don't ration. If the last triplet sounds strangled, the rib expansion was too small. Start from a bigger breath and sip the air out slowly.

rhythmConstraint:river_pickingHand:downs.interaction: Even quarter-note river meets all-downstroke attack. Every beat gets a firm downward pick-hand motion, no alternate picking allowed. The challenge is building wrist endurance while keeping each note evenly spaced and consistently voiced across the bar.
steps: PICKING HAND: lock into strict downward pendulum, reset above the string between beats 2 and 3.
FRETTING HAND: stay in one position and emphasize the tonic on beat 1 of every bar.
Anchor each beat to a scale degree and count 1-2-3-4 out loud with the downstrokes.
Keep wrist loose; let gravity do half the work on the return motion.
examples: Position 5 minor pentatonic: 1-b3-4-5 across one bar, tonic on beat 1.
Major scale in 7th position: 5-6-7-1 climbing with one downstroke per beat.
Dorian shape at the 5th fret: 1-2-b3-5 landing on the tonic each downbeat.
rhythmConstraint:river_pickingHand:downs.watchOut: Wrist tension creeps in around bar 3 and the tone gets thin as the pick digs in harder. Reset the PICKING HAND between phrases and let the forearm breathe.
rhythmConstraint:river_pickingHand:downs.deeperInsight: Downstroke rivers teach the same endurance pulse that drives reggae upstrokes and punk eighths — steady, undeniable, metronomic.

rhythmConstraint:river_pickingHand:finge.interaction: Quarter-note river with fingers only — no pick in sight. Each beat gets a clean plucked note from thumb, index, middle, or ring, one at a time. The softness of fingertip attack changes the feel from driving to conversational.
steps: PICKING HAND: assign thumb to the lowest string in play and index/middle to the upper two.
FRETTING HAND: stay positional and resolve to the tonic at the top of each bar.
Pluck one note per beat and let it ring fully before the next.
Vary finger assignment every two bars to avoid lopsided tone.
examples: Position 5 pentatonic: 1-5-b7-1 walking up with thumb-i-m-i.
Major scale at 7th fret: 5-6-7-1 with p-i-m-a.
Dorian in 5th position: 1-b3-4-5 alternating thumb and index.
rhythmConstraint:river_pickingHand:finge.watchOut: Volume imbalance between thumb and fingers — thumb tends to dominate. Match the thumb's attack to the softer index/middle or the groove goes lopsided.
rhythmConstraint:river_pickingHand:finge.deeperInsight: Fingerpicked rivers are the backbone of nylon-string ballads and classic folk — each beat carries full tone without needing dynamics to stay interesting.

rhythmConstraint:river_pickingHand:muted.interaction: Even quarter rivers played as palm-muted pulses — the guitar becomes a percussion section with pitch peeking through. The PICKING HAND rests its heel on the bridge saddles for a tight chunky thud on every beat of the bar.
steps: PICKING HAND: plant heel lightly on the bridge so strings still speak but sound choked.
FRETTING HAND: work inside a 4-fret window and lean on the tonic downbeat.
Keep every note the same muted length — no ringing, no sudden opens.
Accent beat 1 slightly to mark the bar without breaking the even feel.
examples: Low register pentatonic: 1-5-b7-5 chugging on the bottom three strings.
Position 3 minor shape: 1-2-b3-4 walking up in quarters.
Open position root pulse: 5-1-5-1 alternating on the bass strings.
rhythmConstraint:river_pickingHand:muted.watchOut: Palm drifts too far forward and the pitch disappears entirely — you want muted, not silent. Keep just enough bridge contact to dampen ring, not strangle the note.

rhythmConstraint:river_pickingHand:hybri.interaction: Quarter-note river split between pick and fingers simultaneously. Pick handles the low anchor notes on the bass strings, middle and ring fingers handle upper voices — creating a two-voice river from one hand.
steps: PICKING HAND: pick holds the low root on beats 1 and 3, m and a finger the upper notes on beats 2 and 4.
FRETTING HAND: hold a chord-tone shape so both voices ring from one position.
Keep the pick quiet and the fingers bright so the two layers stay distinct.
Count aloud to keep both hands locked in time.
examples: Am triad at the 5th fret: 1 on bass then 5 and b3 on upper strings.
Dm shape in 5th position: 1-5-b3-1 across two strings.
Position 7 major chord tones: 1 on the 4th string with 3 and 5 on top.
rhythmConstraint:river_pickingHand:hybri.watchOut: Pick attack overpowers the finger notes and the upper voice disappears. Softer pick grip and firmer finger plant keeps both rivers audible.

rhythmConstraint:river_pickingHand:slide.interaction: Quarter-note river played entirely with a slide — every beat is a gliding arrival, no frets pressed. The slide moves in quarter-note jumps, landing precisely over the target fret for each scale degree.
steps: FRETTING HAND: rest slide lightly on the strings and damp behind it with the index finger.
PICKING HAND: pluck or pick on the downbeat as the slide lands over the target fret wire.
Move in short deliberate jumps rather than long glides between beats.
Anchor to the tonic on beat 1 of every bar so the ear stays oriented.
examples: On the 3rd string: slide 1-2-b3-1 in positions centered around fret 5.
On the 2nd string: 5-6-b7-1 climbing in small slide hops.
On the 4th string: b3-4-5-1 between frets 5 and 10.
rhythmConstraint:river_pickingHand:slide.watchOut: Slide lands slightly sharp or flat of the fret wire and the whole river goes out of tune. Aim directly over the wire, not behind it.

rhythmConstraint:river_pickingHand:bends.interaction: Quarter-note river where every pitch change happens via bending, not fretting a new note. Stay on one fret and bend up to each target scale degree — this limits how far the river can travel but forces pristine pitch control.
steps: FRETTING HAND: anchor the index behind the bending finger for support.
PICKING HAND: pluck once at the beat, then let the fretting hand raise the pitch to target.
Pre-bend to the tonic on beat 1 to establish the home pitch.
Alternate bent notes with released notes to create the melodic motion.
examples: Around the 7th fret of the 3rd string: plucked 4 bent up to 5, released, plucked 5 bent to 6.
On the 2nd string 8th fret: b7 bent to 1, released, bent again.
3rd string 5th fret: b3 bent to 4 then full bend to 5 and back.
rhythmConstraint:river_pickingHand:bends.watchOut: Bend overshoots or undershoots and the steady river feel collapses. Sing the target scale degree before you bend so the ear leads the hand.

rhythmConstraint:burst_pickingHand:downs.interaction: Rapid downstroke bursts of 4-6 notes at the top of the bar, then 2-3 beats of silence. The PICKING HAND has to fire fast downward strokes and then freeze mid-swing without accidentally hitting the strings during the rest.
steps: PICKING HAND: start each burst with a strong tonic downstroke, then rip the next 3-5 down in tight succession.
FRETTING HAND: drop fingers in sync with each downstroke, release completely when the burst ends.
Freeze the pick above the strings during rests — both hands go silent.
Recover rhythm by counting the empty beats out loud.
examples: Position 5 pentatonic burst: 1-2-b3-4-5 on beat 1, silence for 2-3-4.
Dorian climb at 7th fret: 5-6-b7-1 burst, then silence.
Major scale burst: 1-2-3-5 fired at the top of the bar.
rhythmConstraint:burst_pickingHand:downs.watchOut: Wrist stays tense through the silence and the next burst drags. Actively relax the PICKING HAND during the rest — it resets faster that way.

rhythmConstraint:burst_pickingHand:finge.interaction: Rapid finger-rolled bursts followed by long silence. Thumb and fingers fire off 4-6 plucked notes in a tight flurry, then both hands freeze. This rewards independent finger control and punishes sloppy string crossing.
steps: PICKING HAND: plan finger order — p-i-m-a-m-i or similar — before the burst starts.
FRETTING HAND: set the shape before the burst and release cleanly after.
Fire the burst from a relaxed hand, not a tense one.
Let the final note ring into the silence, then damp it before the next bar.
examples: 5th fret pentatonic: 1-b3-4-5-b7 rolled across three strings.
Position 7 major: 1-3-5-6-5-3 as a six-note cascade.
Dorian arpeggio: 1-b3-5-b7-1 walking up.
rhythmConstraint:burst_pickingHand:finge.watchOut: Fingers collide or mis-order mid-burst and the flurry turns into noise. Slow the burst way down first and only speed up once the sequence is automatic.

rhythmConstraint:burst_pickingHand:muted.interaction: Percussive muted bursts — 4-6 fast chugged notes followed by dead air in the bar. The combination sounds like a machine gun with pitch underneath, and the dramatic contrast between flurry and silence is the whole point of the exercise.
steps: PICKING HAND: keep heel planted firmly at the bridge for all burst notes.
FRETTING HAND: tap through a short scale-degree sequence in sync with the burst.
Release fretting pressure during rests so strings go fully silent.
Anchor each burst to the tonic and return there on repeat.
examples: Low pentatonic burst: 1-1-b3-1 chugged on the bass strings.
Root-5 pulse burst: 1-5-1-5 four times fast.
Position 3 minor: 1-2-b3-2-1 muted flurry.
rhythmConstraint:burst_pickingHand:muted.watchOut: Bursts blur together if the palm mute is too heavy — you lose pitch identity. Keep just enough attack to hear the scale degree inside the chug.

rhythmConstraint:burst_pickingHand:hybri.interaction: Hybrid picking bursts where pick and fingers fire simultaneously in a compressed flurry. Pick grabs a bass note while m and a finger pluck upper strings in the same motion — a 4-6 note cascade that fills the first beat and vanishes.
steps: PICKING HAND: pick on the low root, m and a on the top two strings as a tight cluster.
FRETTING HAND: pre-plant the chord-tone shape before the burst.
Fire all notes in a single compressed motion, not separate strokes.
Damp everything during the rest — no ringing sustain.
examples: Am voicing at 5th fret: 1 on bass, 5 and b3 on top as simultaneous burst.
Position 7 major triad: 1-3-5 fired as a three-voice pluck.
Dm at the 5th fret: 1-b3-5 as rapid hybrid chord burst.
rhythmConstraint:burst_pickingHand:hybri.watchOut: Pick and fingers fire out of sync and the burst sounds smeared. Practice the coordinated strike slowly as a single chord grab before speeding up.

rhythmConstraint:burst_pickingHand:slide.interaction: Slide bursts are tricky — you can only reach so many notes in a single beat before the glide gets sloppy and pitch-drifty. Keep bursts limited to 3-4 slide-arrivals across a small fret range, then freeze the slide completely for the rest of the bar.
steps: FRETTING HAND: rest the slide lightly so it can move quickly without scraping.
PICKING HAND: pluck each slide arrival on a separate beat fraction.
Plan a small fret window — 3-5 frets max — for the burst.
End on the tonic so the silence afterward resolves.
examples: 3rd string burst: 1-b3-4-5 slid between frets 5 and 10.
2nd string burst: 5-6-b7-1 climbing quickly.
4th string burst: b3-4-5 over three frets.
rhythmConstraint:burst_pickingHand:slide.watchOut: Slide drags between notes instead of landing cleanly and the burst smears into one long slur. Short controlled hops, not long glides.

rhythmConstraint:burst_pickingHand:bends.interaction: Bend-bursts are the hardest combo here — each burst note is a fresh bend executed in rapid succession. Since bends limit you to 1-2 steps of pitch motion at most, the bursts stay short and focus on one string at a time to avoid fatigue.
steps: FRETTING HAND: reinforce with middle and index behind the bending finger.
PICKING HAND: pluck on each bend attack, not between.
Burst 3-4 quick bends up to target scale degrees, then rest.
Release each bend fully before starting the next one.
examples: 3rd string 7th fret: burst of 4-5-4-5 via repeated bends.
2nd string 8th fret: b7-1-b7-1 bent back and forth.
3rd string 5th fret: b3-4 full bends in rapid succession.
rhythmConstraint:burst_pickingHand:bends.watchOut: Fingers fatigue within the first burst and pitch accuracy collapses. Rest thoroughly in the silence and actively shake out the FRETTING HAND.

rhythmConstraint:space_pickingHand:downs.interaction: Half the bar is silence, the other half is downstrokes. The downstroke's weight suits the sparse feel — each struck note rings into the empty space with authority instead of disappearing into clutter.
steps: PICKING HAND: hit only on the chosen beats, pick stays above strings elsewhere.
FRETTING HAND: mute immediately after each note if sustain interferes with the rest.
Count the rests out loud so the silence stays measured.
Anchor every phrase to a scale-degree target on the struck beats.
examples: Position 5 pentatonic: 1 on beat 1, 5 on beat 2, then silence beats 3-4.
Dorian in 7th position: 1 and b3 hit, then two beats empty.
Major scale: 5 on beat 1, 1 on beat 3, rest between.
rhythmConstraint:space_pickingHand:downs.watchOut: Sustain from downstrokes bleeds into the rest and fills the space you're trying to create. Damp with the FRETTING HAND or PICKING HAND heel between notes.

rhythmConstraint:space_pickingHand:finge.interaction: Fingerpicked sparse playing — each plucked note rings into half a bar of silence. The softness of fingertip tone makes the space feel contemplative rather than aggressive, and the sustain naturally fills the air.
steps: PICKING HAND: assign thumb or index per note and let each one ring.
FRETTING HAND: keep pressure on after the pluck so the note sustains into the rest.
Plant the next finger on the string early to prepare the next attack.
Resolve phrases to the tonic across the silence.
examples: 5th fret pentatonic: 1 plucked on beat 1, 5 on beat 2, silence.
Position 7 major: 3 and 5 plucked on beats 1-2, rest through 3-4.
Dorian: b3 then 1 fingered, then long space.
rhythmConstraint:space_pickingHand:finge.watchOut: Thumb overpowers the fingers and the sparse phrase sounds lopsided. Match thumb volume to the lightest finger note in the phrase.

rhythmConstraint:space_pickingHand:muted.interaction: Muted sparse hits — half the bar is dead silence and the other half is chunky palm-muted stabs. The muted tone creates a beatbox-like pulse where each hit punches the empty space around it without ringing through the rests and filling the pocket.
steps: PICKING HAND: heel planted firm for every stab.
FRETTING HAND: release pressure after each note to end the chug cleanly.
Count the silent beats internally to keep placement rock-solid.
Hit the tonic on the first struck beat of every bar.
examples: Low pentatonic stabs: 1 on beat 1, 5 on beat 2, rest beats 3-4.
Root pulse: 1 and b7 stabbed muted on beats 1 and 2.
Position 3: 1-b3 struck muted, then two beats silence.
rhythmConstraint:space_pickingHand:muted.watchOut: Muted hits blur with the silence if they're too quiet — they should punch. Dig in more on the attack to carve the rhythm out of the rest.

rhythmConstraint:space_pickingHand:hybri.interaction: Hybrid picking in sparse phrases — each struck beat is a simultaneous pick-and-finger grab, then silence. Perfect for sparse chord-tone comping where a bass note and upper voice hit together and ring into the rest.
steps: PICKING HAND: pick the bass note while m or a finger grabs the upper voice at the same instant.
FRETTING HAND: hold the shape through the sustain, release before the next bar.
Let the hybrid hit ring fully into the rest — don't rush to the next one.
Anchor the low note to the tonic or 5 on every beat 1.
examples: Am at 5th fret: 1 and b3 hit on beat 1, silence.
Position 7 major: 1 and 5 struck together, then rest.
Dm at 5th fret: 1 and b3 hybrid grab, silent two beats.
rhythmConstraint:space_pickingHand:hybri.watchOut: Bass and top note don't strike at exactly the same instant and the hit sounds flammed. Line them up as a single coordinated grab.

rhythmConstraint:space_pickingHand:slide.interaction: Sparse slide playing — one or two gliding notes per bar, with half the bar empty. Slide's natural sustain and glide feel perfectly at home in wide open space, and you only need a single sliding arrival to fill a whole rest.
steps: FRETTING HAND: slide into the target pitch slowly from a fret below.
PICKING HAND: pluck once as the slide starts moving.
Let the slide note ring through the silent beats.
Damp behind the slide to keep the sustained note clean.
examples: 3rd string: slide into 5 from fret below, let it ring two beats.
2nd string: slide into 1 from b7, sustain.
4th string: slide into 4 from b3, hold through rest.
rhythmConstraint:space_pickingHand:slide.watchOut: The slide doesn't arrive on a clean fret wire and the sustained note drifts flat. Check the destination pitch with a clean pluck once it lands.

rhythmConstraint:space_pickingHand:bends.interaction: Sparse bends — one bend per half-bar, stretched out into a slow expressive gesture across the rest. The surrounding space gives you room to milk the bend's rise, add vibrato, and fully resolve the pitch, which is exactly what bending is built for.
steps: FRETTING HAND: bend slowly from the starting scale degree to the target across one full beat.
PICKING HAND: pluck once as the bend begins.
Hold the bent pitch into the silent beats with vibrato.
Release the bend as the next bar begins.
examples: 3rd string 7th fret: 4 bent slowly to 5, held two beats.
2nd string 8th fret: b7 bent to 1, sustained with vibrato.
3rd string 5th fret: b3 bent to 4, let ring.
rhythmConstraint:space_pickingHand:bends.watchOut: Bent pitch sags during the sustain and the sparse phrase sounds out of tune. Reinforce the FRETTING HAND grip and listen hard through the rest.

rhythmConstraint:offbeat_pickingHand:dow.interaction: Downstrokes strictly on the offbeats (the 'and' of each beat) — never on 1 or 3. The PICKING HAND has to resist the natural downbeat pull and place every attack in the gap. This is the essence of ska and reggae upstroke feel, but powered by down-pick weight.
steps: PICKING HAND: swing the pick down on every 'and' while the beat itself stays silent.
FRETTING HAND: tap the scale-degree target on each offbeat.
Count '1 AND 2 AND' out loud, downstrokes only on the ANDs.
Keep the pick reset motion relaxed so the offbeat stays punchy.
examples: Position 5 pentatonic: 1, b3, 4, 5 landing on each 'and'.
Dorian in 7th position: 5, 6, b7, 1 all offbeat.
Major scale: 3, 5, 6, 1 on the 'and' of 1, 2, 3, 4.
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_pickingHand:dow.watchOut: Pick drifts onto the downbeat and the feel collapses into regular quarters. Tap your foot on the downbeats so the offbeats stay in contrast.

rhythmConstraint:offbeat_pickingHand:fin.interaction: Fingerpicked offbeats — soft finger plucks landing only between the downbeats, never on 1 or 3. The fingertip tone gives the offbeat a gentler lift than a pick stroke and naturally suits folk, bossa nova, and nylon-string syncopated accompaniment.
steps: PICKING HAND: assign one finger per offbeat for consistency.
FRETTING HAND: prepare each scale-degree target in advance.
Count the downbeats silently, pluck only on the 'ands'.
Let each plucked offbeat ring into the next downbeat space.
examples: 5th fret pentatonic: 1, 4, 5, b7 plucked on each offbeat.
Position 7 major: 3, 5, 6, 1 fingered on the ANDs.
Dorian: 1, b3, 5, b7 offbeat pattern.
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_pickingHand:fin.watchOut: Fingers land early because the downbeat pull is strong — the phrase collapses into straight quarters. Count out loud until the offbeat feel sticks.

rhythmConstraint:offbeat_pickingHand:mut.interaction: Palm-muted chugs landing only on the offbeats — the classic reggae and ska upstroke sound, but executed percussively with a muted attack. The silent downbeats create a huge pocket for an imaginary bass drum to live in, and the mute lets the rhythm pop.
steps: PICKING HAND: heel on the bridge, hit only on each 'and'.
FRETTING HAND: tap scale degrees in sync with the offbeat hits.
Count the downbeats aloud, strike only the ANDs.
Anchor to the tonic on the first offbeat of every bar.
examples: Low pentatonic: 1, b3, 4, 5 chugged muted on offbeats.
Root-5 pulse: 1, 5, 1, 5 on the ANDs.
Dorian at 5th fret: 1, b3, 5, b7 offbeat.
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_pickingHand:mut.watchOut: Muted hits drift onto the downbeat under pressure and the groove loses its reggae lift. Foot taps on 1 and 3 to keep the downbeat anchored internally.

rhythmConstraint:offbeat_pickingHand:hyb.interaction: Hybrid grabs on the offbeats — pick plus finger landing together on each 'and' while the downbeats stay silent. The two-voice texture gives syncopated comping a much richer harmonic sound than a single note hit alone, and works beautifully for country and funk comping.
steps: PICKING HAND: pick bass and m finger upper voice simultaneously, only on ANDs.
FRETTING HAND: hold the chord-tone shape through the bar.
Count the downbeats silently to keep the offbeat placement true.
Vary which upper note you finger to add melody to the syncopation.
examples: Am at 5th fret: 1 with b3 on each offbeat.
Dm in 5th position: 1 with 5 on the ANDs.
Position 7 major: 1 with 3 hybrid-plucked offbeat.
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_pickingHand:hyb.watchOut: Pick and finger come in at slightly different times and the offbeat smears. Practice the grab slowly as a single strike before putting it in context.

rhythmConstraint:offbeat_pickingHand:sli.interaction: Slide arrivals on the offbeats only — each glide lands precisely between downbeats, never on 1 or 3. The slide's natural slur actually helps here because the lift-and-land motion into the 'and' perfectly mimics the offbeat push you want in a reggae feel.
steps: FRETTING HAND: start the slide motion just before each 'and'.
PICKING HAND: pluck as the slide arrives on the offbeat target.
Damp between slides so downbeats stay silent.
Anchor the first offbeat of each bar to a scale-degree target.
examples: 3rd string: slide to 5 on each 'and' from fret below.
2nd string: slide to 1 on offbeat from b7.
4th string: slide to 4 and b7 alternating on ANDs.
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_pickingHand:sli.watchOut: The slide arrival lands late (on the next downbeat) and the offbeat pocket is lost. Start the glide motion earlier to place the arrival exactly on the AND.

rhythmConstraint:offbeat_pickingHand:ben.interaction: Pre-bent notes released onto the offbeats — a distinctive syncopation where the pitch is already raised before the pluck, and the string gets attacked on each 'and'. Limited pitch travel but a huge expressive payoff and a genuinely unusual rhythmic feel.
steps: FRETTING HAND: pre-bend to the target scale degree before plucking.
PICKING HAND: pluck on each offbeat while the bend is held.
Release the bend between ANDs to set up the next pre-bend.
Count downbeats aloud so the offbeat pocket stays honest.
examples: 3rd string 7th fret: pre-bend 4 to 5, pluck on each AND.
2nd string 8th fret: pre-bend b7 to 1, release, repeat.
3rd string 5th fret: pre-bend b3 to 4 on offbeats.
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_pickingHand:ben.watchOut: The pre-bend pitch sags before the offbeat pluck lands and the note is flat. Reinforce the FRETTING HAND with all three fingers behind the bend.

rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_pickingHand:.interaction: A short rhythmic cell (2-4 notes) looped with all downstrokes, pitches varying from loop to loop. The downstroke attack keeps the cell consistently punchy while your FRETTING HAND changes scale degrees around it.
steps: PICKING HAND: lock the cell rhythm in with strict down motion first.
FRETTING HAND: repeat the cell with pitches 1-b3-5 first loop, then 2-4-5 next.
Keep the rhythmic shape identical across pitch variations.
Return to the tonic cell every 4 bars as a landmark.
examples: Cell: long-short-short on 1-b3-4, then 1-5-b3.
Cell: short-short-long on 5-6-1, then 5-b7-1.
Cell: dotted 1-2-b3, repeated with 1-4-5.
rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_pickingHand:.watchOut: Cell rhythm warps as the pitches change — the loop stops being a loop. Internalize the rhythm first with a single pitch before varying.

rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_pickingHand:.interaction: A looped rhythmic cell played with fingerpicking, each loop revisiting the same rhythm with fresh scale degrees rotated around the tonic. Fingers give each note its own tonal identity so the pitch variations within the cell stay crystal clear to the ear.
steps: PICKING HAND: choose a fixed finger order for the cell (p-i-m or i-m-a).
FRETTING HAND: vary the scale degrees each repetition while keeping the rhythm intact.
Start with the cell on the tonic, then rotate the pitches around it.
Use the thumb for any low anchor note in the cell.
examples: Cell: three plucks short-short-long on 1-b3-5, then 1-4-5.
Cell: long-short on 5-1, then 5-b7.
Cell: four plucks on 1-2-b3-4, then 1-2-4-5.
rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_pickingHand:.watchOut: Finger order drifts as the cell pitches change and the loop sounds inconsistent. Lock the finger assignment to the rhythm, not the pitches.

rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_pickingHand:.interaction: A looped rhythmic cell played as palm-muted chugs with pitch variation hiding inside the mute. The percussive edge of the mute makes the cell feel like a drum pattern that also happens to carry pitch information — groove-first, melody-second.
steps: PICKING HAND: palm planted, strict rhythm for every loop.
FRETTING HAND: tap through pitch variations 1-5-b3, 1-4-b7, etc.
Keep the mute heavy enough that rhythm dominates.
Anchor the cell to the tonic on the first note of every loop.
examples: Cell: dotted-eighth pattern on 1-5-b7, then 1-5-b3.
Cell: three chugs on 1-4-5, then 1-b3-5.
Cell: short-short-long 1-2-b3 muted, then 1-2-4.
rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_pickingHand:.watchOut: Heavy mute kills all pitch difference and the seed becomes a single drum loop. Lighten the heel just enough for the scale degree to speak through.

rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_pickingHand:.interaction: A looped cell played with hybrid picking — pick on the low note, fingers on the upper notes, rhythm identical each loop. This is essentially a repeating pick-and-pluck cell that comps through a scale.
steps: PICKING HAND: pick the bass note of the cell, m and a the upper notes.
FRETTING HAND: rotate the upper pitches each loop while keeping the bass on the tonic.
Lock the cell rhythm by practicing with one chord shape first.
Vary between chord tones 1-b3-5, 1-4-5, 1-b3-b7.
examples: Cell: pick 1, then m-a on b3-5, loop with 4-5.
Cell: pick 1 on bass, a finger on 3 high, cycle with 5.
Cell: pick 5, fingers on 1 and b3 upper.
rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_pickingHand:.watchOut: Pick and finger cell goes out of alignment when you rotate pitches. Keep the bass rock steady even when the upper voice moves.

rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_pickingHand:.interaction: A rhythmic cell played entirely with a slide — the rhythm shape repeats every loop but the glide destinations rotate around the tonic. Limited pitch travel per loop since the slide can only move so far in a tight rhythmic cell without dragging the tempo.
steps: FRETTING HAND: slide into each cell note from a half step below.
PICKING HAND: pluck at the start of each note in the cell.
Keep the cell inside a 3-5 fret window.
Return to the tonic slide on every loop start.
examples: Cell: slide to 1, slide to 5, slide to b3 on 3rd string.
Cell: slide to 1, slide to 4, slide to b7.
Cell: slide to 5, slide to 6, slide to 1 short glides.
rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_pickingHand:.watchOut: Slide movement slows the cell down and the rhythm drags. Keep slide motions short and crisp to stay in tempo.

rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_pickingHand:.interaction: A rhythmic cell where every single note in the cell is a bend. The cell rhythm stays locked across loops but the target bend pitches rotate around the tonic, forcing precise pitch memory and stamina across multiple consecutive bends on the same string.
steps: FRETTING HAND: pre-plan bends to 4-5, b7-1, b3-4 for each loop.
PICKING HAND: pluck on the start of each bend.
Release between bends so the next one starts from the unbent pitch.
Anchor the cell to a tonic bend at the start of every loop.
examples: Cell: bend 4 to 5, release, bend b3 to 4 on 3rd string.
Cell: bend b7 to 1, release, bend 1 to 2 on 2nd string.
Cell: bend b3 to 4 twice, then bend 4 to 5.
rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_pickingHand:.watchOut: Bend pitches drift as the hand fatigues and the cell goes out of tune. Take rest bars between loops to recover strength.

rhythmConstraint:triplets_pickingHand:do.interaction: Three notes per beat, every single one a downstroke. This is the wrist-killer combo — the PICKING HAND has to fire 12 down strokes per 4/4 bar with no alternate-pick relief. Build up slowly; speed is the reward, not the starting point.
steps: PICKING HAND: pendulum wrist in strict down-down-down motion, no upstrokes anywhere.
FRETTING HAND: place three scale-degree targets per beat cleanly in rhythm.
Count 'trip-o-let trip-o-let' out loud with each downstroke.
Start at 60 BPM and only speed up once 30 seconds feels easy.
examples: Position 5 pentatonic: 1-b3-4 on beat 1, 5-b7-1 on beat 2 all downstrokes.
Dorian in 7th position: 5-6-b7 then 1-2-b3 triplet downstrokes.
Major scale at fret 5: 1-2-3 then 4-5-6 as downstroke triplets.
rhythmConstraint:triplets_pickingHand:do.watchOut: Wrist locks up within 2 bars and the triplet feel collapses into sludge. Stop the second tension appears — this combo is an endurance build, not a speed test.

rhythmConstraint:triplets_pickingHand:fi.interaction: Triplet feel fingerpicked — three plucks per beat cascading across the strings in a rolling arc. A classic p-i-m or i-m-a finger roll per beat, rolling through 12 notes per bar, and one of the most satisfying ways to internalize the triplet pulse physically.
steps: PICKING HAND: fix a rolling finger order like p-i-m for each triplet.
FRETTING HAND: land on three scale-degree targets per beat.
Keep each finger strike the same volume to preserve triplet clarity.
Count 'one-trip-let two-trip-let' aloud.
examples: 5th fret pentatonic: 1-b3-5 then 1-4-5 triplet rolls.
Position 7 major arpeggio: 1-3-5 then 3-5-1 rolling triplets.
Dorian at fret 5: 1-b3-5 then 4-5-b7 as finger triplets.
rhythmConstraint:triplets_pickingHand:fi.watchOut: Third finger in the triplet consistently drops volume and the pattern limps. Match a-finger strength to i and m with isolated finger drills.

rhythmConstraint:triplets_pickingHand:mu.interaction: Palm-muted triplets — three chugged notes per beat with pitch information lurking underneath the mute. A galloping, almost cinematic feel when done right, where the palm mute makes each triplet feel machine-like and surgically precise — think spaghetti western.
steps: PICKING HAND: heel planted, three strikes per beat in tight succession.
FRETTING HAND: tap three scale degrees per beat in rhythm.
Anchor each beat's first chug to the tonic or 5.
Keep the attack even across all three triplet notes.
examples: Low pentatonic: 1-1-5 then 1-1-b3 muted triplets.
Root pulse: 1-5-1 three times per beat.
Position 3: 1-b3-4 chugged muted triplets.
rhythmConstraint:triplets_pickingHand:mu.watchOut: Middle note of each triplet gets swallowed by the mute. Dig in slightly harder on that note to balance the triplet.

rhythmConstraint:triplets_pickingHand:hy.interaction: Hybrid triplets — pick on one note, middle and ring fingers on the other two notes inside each triplet grouping. A natural and efficient way to break up 3-per-beat phrases across adjacent strings without the alternate-picking chaos that triplets normally create.
steps: PICKING HAND: pick on note 1, m finger on note 2, a finger on note 3 of each triplet.
FRETTING HAND: target three scale degrees per beat spread across two or three strings.
Keep the bass pick note locked in tempo as the anchor.
Alternate chord tones 1-b3-5 then 5-b7-1 across bars.
examples: Am at 5th fret: pick 1, m on b3, a on 5 triplet.
Position 7 major: pick 1, m on 3, a on 5.
Dm at 5th fret: pick 1, m on b3, a on 5.
rhythmConstraint:triplets_pickingHand:hy.watchOut: a finger is weaker than pick and m, so note 3 drops out of the triplet. Isolate a-finger strength drills before combining.

rhythmConstraint:triplets_pickingHand:sl.interaction: Slide triplets are narrow by necessity — you physically can't glide too far in a single beat without losing pitch accuracy. Three small slide hops per beat across adjacent frets produce a fluid triplet arc that swoops melodically through the scale degrees you're targeting.
steps: FRETTING HAND: slide in three short hops per beat, minimal travel.
PICKING HAND: pluck on each triplet note as the slide lands.
Keep hops inside a 4-5 fret window max.
Anchor beat 1 to a tonic slide.
examples: 3rd string: slide 1-b3-4 then 5-4-b3 over 5 frets.
2nd string: slide 5-6-b7 as tight triplet hops.
4th string: slide b3-4-5 and back.
rhythmConstraint:triplets_pickingHand:sl.watchOut: Slide motion bleeds between triplet notes and smears the rhythm. Pluck sharply on each hop to articulate the triplet.

rhythmConstraint:triplets_pickingHand:be.interaction: Three bent pitches per beat is extremely demanding and severely pitch-limited by hand stamina. Often this becomes a bend-release-bend shape or a bend-held-and-plucked-again shape instead of three completely separate bends. This combo is expressive and slow, not fast.
steps: FRETTING HAND: plan a bend-release-bend pattern for each beat.
PICKING HAND: pluck on each triplet pulse without over-strumming.
Keep the bend travel to one step max per motion.
Rest between bars to avoid pitch fatigue.
examples: 3rd string 7th fret: bend 4 to 5, release to 4, bend back to 5.
2nd string 8th fret: bend b7 to 1, release, bend again.
3rd string 5th fret: bend b3 to 4, release, back to 4.
rhythmConstraint:triplets_pickingHand:be.watchOut: Pitch sags within a beat and the triplet sounds out of tune. Shorten the bend distance and rest frequently — this combo punishes tired fingers.

dynamics:swell_articulation:legato.interaction: This is the classic expressive arc: smooth connected notes that bloom from nothing, peak in the middle, and fade back to silence.
