rhythmConstraint:river_harmonicTarget:sc.watchOut: The river tempo is so hypnotic that your pitch choices go on autopilot and the line becomes random. Fight this by consciously phrasing in groups of four — every four beats should feel like a small sentence.

rhythmConstraint:river_harmonicTarget:ar.interaction: Quarter notes over a triad palette is a walking-bass exercise — one chord tone per beat, any order, full range of octaves. Every note is root, third, or fifth, and the pulse turns those three pitches into the backbone of the line. This is the jazz bass player's bread and butter and the Bach chorale's foundation.
steps: Set a calm quarter-note pulse.
Beat 1: play the root. Beat 2: leap or step to the third. Beat 3: leap to the fifth. Beat 4: the root up an octave.
Vary the order on the next bar — maybe fifth, root, third, root.
Use octave displacement to keep the line moving rather than repeating the same register.
Close on the root downbeat to resolve the round.
examples: root → third → fifth → root up an octave → fifth → third → root → fifth (walking triad)
fifth → root → third → fifth → root up an octave → fifth → third → root
rhythmConstraint:river_harmonicTarget:ar.watchOut: The river pulse plus limited palette can turn this into a mechanical drill. Use rhythm accents and dynamics to phrase in four-beat chunks, not just eight identical notes.
rhythmConstraint:river_harmonicTarget:ar.deeperInsight: A quarter-note walking line over a triad is exactly how early jazz bassists like Walter Page invented the modern bass role — functional harmony in perfect motion.

rhythmConstraint:river_harmonicTarget:ch.interaction: Quarter-note flow with chord-tone landing is how jazz players practice navigating changes — the steady pulse forces you to think ahead, and the landing rule punishes any phrase that drifts past the chord change without resolving. This is the Aebersold drill: four-beat phrases over II-V-I where every fourth beat lands on a chord tone.
steps: Set a medium quarter-note tempo and lock in the pulse.
Plan your phrase so its last note falls on the fourth beat of each bar.
Walk or leap freely through the scale for beats 1, 2, 3.
On beat 4 land on a chord tone of the current chord.
When the chord changes on the next bar, start fresh and aim your new landing.
examples: Over I: fifth → fourth → third → root (landing on root beat 4), then over IV: second → third → fourth → root (root of IV)
Over II: fourth → fifth → seventh → root (landing on root of II), then over V: third → second → fourth → fifth (landing on fifth of V)
rhythmConstraint:river_harmonicTarget:ch.watchOut: The river tempo makes you think in equal beats, so the landing becomes just another quarter note without weight. Slightly emphasize beat 4 with dynamics or a tiny breath so the landing is audible as an arrival, not a continuation.

rhythmConstraint:river_harmonicTarget:co.interaction: Steady quarter notes with color tones is a walking meditation through the extensions of a chord — one 9th here, one 11th there, each tasted for exactly one beat. The calm pulse gives the ear time to hear each color tone distinctly, and the 12 months of practice this builds is what separates players who understand chord color from players who only know triads.
steps: Set a slow quarter-note pulse so you can hear each note ring out.
On each beat choose a color tone or a chord tone, with at least half the beats being extensions.
Rotate through the 7th, 9th, and 11th across the round so no single extension dominates.
Let the line walk and leap between extensions and triad tones.
Resolve the last bar on the root in plain quarter notes.
examples: root → third → seventh → ninth → seventh → third → fifth → eleventh (walking extensions)
ninth → seventh → fifth → eleventh → ninth → seventh → fifth → root
rhythmConstraint:river_harmonicTarget:co.watchOut: With even quarter-note weight, a color tone and a triad tone sound the same emphasis-wise. The extensions need room — stretch onto them with a slightly longer duration or wait to play them on a strong beat.

rhythmConstraint:burst_harmonicTarget:sc.interaction: Bursts fire a cluster of notes at the start of each phrase then sit in silence — and scale-shape freedom lets the burst be any shape through the palette. This is exactly the gesture of a gospel organ fill or a Middle Eastern ornament: a quick flurry of scale notes followed by breathing room where the ear digests what just happened.
steps: Imagine the first beat of each phrase as a launching pad.
Fire 4-6 quick notes through any scale shape — up, down, jagged, smooth.
Let the burst rest on a stable scale note — usually the root, third, or fifth.
Hold the silence for 2-3 beats and actually listen to the decay.
Begin the next burst from a different register so the two bursts feel like different voices.
examples: root → second → third → fourth → fifth (burst up) ... rest ... sixth → fifth → fourth → third → root (burst down)
third → fifth → seventh → sixth → fifth (burst arc) ... rest ... fourth → second → root → second → third
rhythmConstraint:burst_harmonicTarget:sc.watchOut: Under speed pressure the bursts all end up running straight up or down the scale. Mix directions mid-burst — up two, down one, up three — so each burst has character.

rhythmConstraint:burst_harmonicTarget:ar.interaction: A burst through a triad is a broken-chord flurry — a classic keyboard cadenza ornament, a jazz horn player's upward sweep through the tonic. Because there are only three chord tones, every burst is an arpeggio exploding up or down, then resting in silence. It has the clean explosive feel of an electric piano fill or a harp glissando.
steps: Imagine the burst as an upward or downward arpeggio.
Fire 4-6 notes alternating among root, third, fifth, using octave displacements to cover range.
Let the burst end on the root or fifth for a stable landing.
Rest 2-3 beats and let the triad ring in the room.
The next burst should sweep the opposite direction or start from a different triad tone.
examples: root → third → fifth → root up an octave → third up an octave (rising burst) ... rest
fifth → third → root → fifth → third (descending burst) ... rest
rhythmConstraint:burst_harmonicTarget:ar.watchOut: Every burst will want to be the same rising arpeggio — you played it once, it felt great, you did it again. Vary the direction, the start note, and the ending so the bursts function as question and answer.

rhythmConstraint:burst_harmonicTarget:ch.interaction: A burst fires 4-6 quick notes and chord-tone landing forces the final note onto a chord tone. This is the gospel pianist's runaway-train fill: a flurry of scale motion screaming up to a perfect chord tone landing on the downbeat of the next bar. The silence afterward amplifies the landing, and the burst gives you the energy to reach it.
steps: Identify the chord tones of the current chord before starting.
Launch the burst on the downbeat with 4-6 quick notes through the scale.
Aim the final note of the burst to land squarely on a chord tone of that chord.
Rest for 2-3 beats, letting the chord tone ring.
When the chord changes, plan the next burst's landing for the new chord.
examples: second → third → fourth → fifth → sixth → fifth (burst landing on fifth)
seventh → root up an octave → second → third (burst landing on third)
rhythmConstraint:burst_harmonicTarget:ch.watchOut: Under the speed of a burst it is easy to overshoot the landing and end on a scale tone rather than a chord tone. Practice each burst slowly first so you know which note is the fifth-burst-note and aim it.

rhythmConstraint:burst_harmonicTarget:co.interaction: Bursts loaded with extensions are the fast-fill vocabulary of fusion players — think Joe Zawinul or Herbie Hancock spinning through 9ths and 11ths in a quick cluster. The burst gives you enough notes to actually feature an extension, and the rest afterward lets the tart color ring before the next flurry arrives.
steps: Plan which extensions are available over the current chord.
Launch the burst with a mix of triad tones and extensions — at least one color tone per burst.
Let the last note of the burst be a 7th, 9th, or 11th so the silence rings with extended harmony.
Rest 2-3 beats and actually feel the unresolved color.
Next burst: feature a different extension for variety.
examples: root → third → fifth → seventh → ninth (burst resolving up to ninth)
eleventh → ninth → seventh → fifth → third (burst descending through extensions)
rhythmConstraint:burst_harmonicTarget:co.watchOut: The burst will race past the extensions without featuring them — they need to be at the peak or the resting point of the burst. A color tone buried in the middle of the flurry sounds like a passing tone, not a feature.

rhythmConstraint:space_harmonicTarget:sc.interaction: Half-rest-per-bar space with free scale choice is how Miles Davis played after 1959 — maybe two or three notes in a whole bar, each one chosen carefully. The silence is the other half of the music, and the few notes you do play need to carry the whole meaning. It is a meditation on which scale degrees say the most when they are not surrounded by noise.
steps: Commit to playing only in the second half of each bar (or any half you pick) — the rest is rest.
Choose each note deliberately — two or three per bar is the ceiling.
Let each note ring into the silence so you can hear the decay.
Use register changes between notes to keep the sparseness from feeling monotonous.
End the round with one note on beat 1 of the final bar and nothing else.
examples: (rest rest) third ... (rest) fifth ... (rest rest rest) second (one note per gap)
(rest) root ... (rest rest) sixth ... fourth ... (rest) root
rhythmConstraint:space_harmonicTarget:sc.watchOut: The silence is uncomfortable and you will want to fill it. Every note you add past the half-bar threshold breaks the space rule. Count the rests in real beats, not in vibes.

rhythmConstraint:space_harmonicTarget:ar.interaction: A sparse tonic-triad meditation — three chord tones, half the bar is silence. This is the sound of Arvo Pärt's tintinnabuli style or a lone church bell: just root, third, or fifth, each note ringing into vast air. Every note is an entire event, and because only chord tones are legal, every event is anchored to the tonic gravity.
steps: Pick a slow inner pulse and hear the full bar before each note.
In each bar, play one to three triad tones — at least half the bar is rest.
Let each note ring into the silence; do not play the next until the first has decayed.
Alternate which triad tone you feature across bars — one bar on root, next on fifth, next on third.
Use octave displacement to turn three pitch classes into a wide range.
examples: (rest rest) root up an octave ... (rest rest) fifth ... (rest rest) third (single notes in wide space)
(rest) third → fifth ... (rest rest rest) root (a tiny pair then a solo)
rhythmConstraint:space_harmonicTarget:ar.watchOut: Sparse plus only three pitches can feel static if you do not vary the register. A bar of high root and a bar of low root are two different events — use that to sculpt motion.

rhythmConstraint:space_harmonicTarget:ch.interaction: When you barely play anything and every phrase must end on a chord tone, the ending IS the phrase. The two or three notes you play are really a setup for one perfect landing. This is the Bill Frisell ballad sensibility — a handful of notes per bar, each one landing like a small stone dropped into water, always on the right harmony.
steps: Choose a slow internal pulse.
Each phrase is two or three notes ending on a chord tone of the current chord.
Let the rest between phrases be at least as long as the notes themselves.
When the chord changes, wait through a full bar of silence before your next phrase begins.
End the round with one chord tone on beat 1 of the final bar.
examples: (rest) fourth → root (landing, rest) (rest) sixth → fifth (landing)
(rest rest) second → third (landing, rest) (rest) seventh → root (landing)
rhythmConstraint:space_harmonicTarget:ch.watchOut: In space mode the ear hears everything — a botched landing stands out. If the chord tone you aimed for is wrong, the silence will amplify the miss. Double-check the chord before playing.

rhythmConstraint:space_harmonicTarget:co.interaction: Long silences and every note a color tone — this is the whole ECM-label aesthetic, the sound of Pat Metheny or Keith Jarrett sustaining a single 9th across a bar of rest. Each color tone rings unresolved into empty air and the listener lives inside the tension. Space removes the pressure to resolve and makes every extension feel like its own complete statement.
steps: Pick a slow inner pulse — the silence must be audible as a real duration.
In each bar, play one or two color tones and let them ring across half the bar or more.
Feature a different extension in each successive bar — 7th, then 9th, then 11th.
Let at least half the bar be rest before the next color tone enters.
End the round on a plain chord tone for final resolution after the extended meditation.
examples: (rest rest) ninth ... (rest rest rest) seventh (sparse extensions)
(rest) eleventh → seventh ... (rest rest) ninth (tiny phrase, long rest)
rhythmConstraint:space_harmonicTarget:co.watchOut: A held extension in silence is exquisite; a held extension rushed through is just a mistake. Let the color tone actually decay before you do anything else, or the whole point of sparse color is lost.

rhythmConstraint:offbeat_harmonicTarget:.interaction: Off-the-beat notes plus free scale choice is the ska-reggae guitar feel — every attack lands between beats and every pitch is a fresh decision. The rhythm itself creates lift because the downbeat is conspicuously empty, and the free scale choice lets you place any melodic character onto that floating pulse. It is inherently buoyant.
steps: Feel the downbeat clearly in your body even though you will not play it.
Play on the and of each beat instead of on the beat itself.
Use any scale note on each offbeat — let the pitch be whatever the line calls for.
Alternate direction and interval size so the offbeat line has melodic shape, not just rhythmic insistence.
End the round by holding a note that begins on an offbeat and sustains through the final downbeat.
examples: (rest) root → (rest) third → (rest) fifth → (rest) fourth (offbeat stepwise)
(rest) third → (rest) fifth → (rest) second → (rest) sixth (offbeat mixed)
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_harmonicTarget:.watchOut: Under tempo pressure your attacks will drift back onto the downbeat out of reflex. Tap the downbeat in your foot so you physically feel where you are NOT playing.

rhythmConstraint:offbeat_harmonicTarget:.interaction: Offbeat triads are the reggae upstroke distilled — chord tones floating on the and, no downbeat support, everything lifting. Because the palette is only root/third/fifth, each offbeat hit is inherently stable harmonically even though it is unstable rhythmically. The contrast is exactly what makes reggae feel like it is breathing.
steps: Feel the downbeat but do not play on it.
On each and, play a chord tone — root, third, or fifth.
Rotate which chord tone you hit — do not park on the fifth every time.
Use octave displacement to give the offbeat hits different colors.
End with one downbeat strike on the root to break the offbeat spell and resolve.
examples: (rest) root → (rest) third → (rest) fifth → (rest) root up an octave (rising triad offbeats)
(rest) fifth → (rest) root → (rest) third → (rest) fifth
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_harmonicTarget:.watchOut: The triad palette is so stable that the offbeat placement can feel redundant — you get lift but no forward motion. Vary the octave and the chord tone so each and feels like a new event.

rhythmConstraint:offbeat_harmonicTarget:.interaction: Offbeat attacks with chord-tone landings are a rhythmic puzzle: if every note is on an offbeat and the phrase must end on a chord tone, then the last offbeat of the phrase must be a chord tone. That placement is unstable rhythmically but stable harmonically, giving the line an airborne quality with clean harmonic resolution.
steps: Feel the downbeat — your phrase endings will NOT land there.
Play all notes on offbeats through the scale.
As the phrase approaches its end, aim the final offbeat note at a chord tone of the current chord.
Let the phrase ending sustain through the next downbeat — the tail is the resolution.
When the chord changes, update your target chord tone for the next phrase's final offbeat.
examples: (rest) fourth → (rest) fifth → (rest) seventh → (rest) root (final offbeat on root)
(rest) sixth → (rest) fifth → (rest) fourth → (rest) third (final offbeat on third)
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_harmonicTarget:.watchOut: The natural instinct is to resolve on the downbeat — after all, that is where resolution happens in most music. Resist, and let the offbeat itself carry the landing. Sustain it through the downbeat if you need to hear it hang.

rhythmConstraint:offbeat_harmonicTarget:.interaction: Offbeat color tones is the reggae-jazz hybrid — think Ernest Ranglin or Monty Alexander, placing 9ths and 11ths exclusively on the and-of-the-beat so the chord rings with extended tension in pure syncopation. The rhythmic lift plus the harmonic lift creates this uniquely buoyant, tangy feeling.
steps: Feel the downbeat and do not play on it.
On each offbeat, choose between a triad tone and an extension, with at least half being color tones.
Let a color tone land on the final and of the phrase and sustain through the downbeat.
Rotate through 7th, 9th, 11th across the round.
Close on a triad tone on a downbeat to restore gravity after the round of floating extensions.
examples: (rest) third → (rest) seventh → (rest) ninth → (rest) seventh (offbeat color arc)
(rest) ninth → (rest) fifth → (rest) eleventh → (rest) ninth
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_harmonicTarget:.watchOut: Color tones plus offbeats can feel slippery — both rhythm and pitch are destabilized. Ground the ear by briefly featuring plain chord tones every few phrases as breathing room.

rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_harmonicTarg.interaction: A repeated rhythm cell with free pitch choice is the Steve Reich mantra — the rhythm becomes a fixed lattice that the melody threads through. Because the rhythm never changes, your ear and your hand both lock onto the pulse, and the pitch choices become the only thing you are composing. This is one of the purest melodic exercises there is.
steps: Choose a short rhythm cell — two or three beats of a distinctive pattern.
Play the cell once through the scale, choosing pitches freely.
Repeat the exact same rhythm with different pitches.
Let the pitch choices gradually develop a narrative — go up, reach a peak, come back down.
Close with one final repetition of the rhythm landing on the root.
examples: Cell: quarter eighth-eighth quarter. Round 1: root third-fifth seventh. Round 2: third fifth-sixth fourth. Round 3: second fourth-third root
Cell: dotted-quarter eighth. Round 1: fifth sixth. Round 2: fourth third. Round 3: second root
rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_harmonicTarg.watchOut: You will drift from the rhythm cell without noticing — the cell must be a strict template, not a vibe. Tap the cell out loud before each phrase to stay honest.

rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_harmonicTarg.interaction: A repeated rhythm cell threaded through root/third/fifth is a mini-composition exercise — every iteration of the rhythm has exactly three pitches to choose from, and reordering them creates the variation. Think of a bass ostinato or a classical accompaniment figure that cycles a rhythmic pattern through a triad endlessly.
steps: Pick a short rhythm cell — two beats is ideal.
Play it using any ordering of root, third, fifth.
Repeat the cell using a different ordering of the same three tones.
Cycle through every permutation possible given the cell's note count.
Close on a repetition whose last note is the root.
examples: Cell: quarter-quarter-quarter. Round 1: root third fifth. Round 2: fifth root third. Round 3: third fifth root (triad permutations)
Cell: eighth-eighth quarter. Round 1: root-third fifth. Round 2: fifth-third root
rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_harmonicTarg.watchOut: Three pitches plus a fixed rhythm run out of permutations fast. Use octave displacement and dynamic variation to extend the exercise beyond its mathematical floor.

rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_harmonicTarg.interaction: A fixed rhythm cell over changing chords with chord-tone landings is the tightest kind of motivic development — the same rhythmic idea, the same phrase ending, but re-aimed for each new chord. This is how Paul Simon and Rickie Lee Jones shape verses: a repeating rhythmic hook whose final note subtly shifts with the harmony.
steps: Compose a short rhythm cell whose last note is a chord tone of chord I.
Play it; now when the chord changes, replay the SAME rhythm with a different final pitch that is a chord tone of the new chord.
Let every iteration keep the rhythm exact and the non-landing notes mostly constant.
Over time let the interior pitches also mutate, but only one change per iteration.
End the round with the cell landing on the root of chord I.
examples: Cell: quarter-quarter eighth-eighth quarter. Over I: fourth-third second-fourth root. Over IV: fourth-third second-fourth fourth (landing on root of IV)
Cell: dotted-eighth sixteenth quarter. Over II: fourth-third root. Over V: fourth-third fifth (landing on root of V)
rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_harmonicTarg.watchOut: You will be tempted to change the rhythm when the chord changes — fight it. The rhythm is the constant, the pitches are the variable. That tension is the whole point of the exercise.

rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_harmonicTarg.interaction: A fixed rhythm cell featuring extended chord tones is how jazz players develop flavored motifs — the rhythm is the fingerprint and the 7ths/9ths/11ths are the spice. Think of the way Herbie Hancock spins one rhythmic idea through a chord progression and makes it feel new because each repetition features a different extension of the current chord.
steps: Invent a short rhythm cell — two beats long works well.
On each iteration, let at least one of the notes be a 7th, 9th, or 11th of the current chord.
Preserve the cell's rhythm exactly while rotating which extension you feature.
Breathe triad tones into the cell on every third iteration so the color does not become saturated.
End the round with the cell landing on a plain root.
examples: Cell: quarter eighth-eighth. Round 1: third seventh-ninth. Round 2: fifth ninth-seventh. Round 3: root seventh-eleventh
Cell: dotted-eighth sixteenth quarter. Round 1: ninth seventh root. Round 2: eleventh ninth third
rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_harmonicTarg.watchOut: If you put the extension on the downbeat of the cell every time, the motif feels stiff. Rotate where in the cell the color tone appears — sometimes the first note, sometimes the last, sometimes the middle.

rhythmConstraint:triplets_harmonicTarget.interaction: Triplets gives you three notes per beat — that is exactly enough to form a micro-scale fragment in every beat. Free scale choice lets each triplet be a different shape: ascending, descending, a tiny arc, a curl. This is the violin shuffle feel, the Irish fiddle feel, the gospel piano trill — threeness carving the beat into balanced motion.
steps: Internalize a triplet pulse — 'one-two-three, one-two-three' across each beat.
Play three scale notes per beat, moving by step or skip as you choose.
Let each beat be a tiny melodic event — up three, or up-down-up, or down three.
Alternate directions across beats so the line does not just spiral up or down.
Close the final beat with a triplet that lands on the root on the next downbeat.
examples: root-second-third, fourth-third-second, fifth-fourth-third, root (rising triplet line)
fifth-fourth-third, second-fourth-third, fifth-sixth-seventh, root up an octave
rhythmConstraint:triplets_harmonicTarget.watchOut: Triplets pull strongly toward running scales. Break that gravity with direction changes within the triplet itself — up, down, up — not just one ascending scale.

rhythmConstraint:triplets_harmonicTarget.interaction: Three notes per beat through a three-note triad palette is a perfect mathematical fit — every beat is exactly one full arpeggio. This is the Bach Toccata rolling-triad feel or the bluegrass banjo roll: triplet beats each spelling the tonic triad, then rotating the starting note so it does not feel mechanical.
steps: Triplet pulse in the body; three notes per beat.
Beat 1: play root-third-fifth as a rising triplet.
Beat 2: play the same triad in a different order — say third-fifth-root.
Each beat rotates the ordering or octave placement so no two consecutive beats have the same shape.
End on a beat whose last triplet note is the root.
examples: root-third-fifth, third-fifth-root, fifth-root-third, root-third-fifth (rotating triad triplets)
fifth-third-root, root-third-fifth, third-root-fifth, root (mixed direction rotations)
rhythmConstraint:triplets_harmonicTarget.watchOut: Six triplet notes per two beats using only three pitch classes will get hypnotic fast. Use octave displacement between beats to prevent the line from becoming a closed loop.

rhythmConstraint:triplets_harmonicTarget.interaction: Three notes per beat and a chord-tone landing at the phrase end creates the classic jazz-horn triplet fill — cascading triplet motion through the scale that lands cleanly on the chord's target note. The triplet energy is forward momentum; the chord-tone landing is the brake that gives the whole phrase a shape.
steps: Triplet pulse in the body.
Play triplets through the scale for the first few beats of the phrase.
On the final beat, aim the last triplet note at a chord tone of the current chord.
Hold or breathe after the landing so the resolution registers.
Begin the next phrase from a different register so the triplet cascade feels fresh.
examples: root-second-third, fourth-fifth-sixth, seventh-sixth-fifth, third (cascading into third)
fifth-fourth-third, second-third-fourth, fifth-sixth-seventh, root up an octave
rhythmConstraint:triplets_harmonicTarget.watchOut: Triplets create so much momentum that you miss the landing and keep cascading. Plan the target note before the phrase starts and count backward from it to know which triplet note must hit the chord tone.

rhythmConstraint:triplets_harmonicTarget.interaction: Triplets plus color tones is the Coltrane 'Giant Steps' feel — cascading triples through extended chord territory, where every triplet has at least one 7th, 9th, or 11th adding spice. The sheer density of notes means the color is everywhere, and the three-against-two rhythm already feels sophisticated without any melodic help.
steps: Triplet pulse — three notes per beat.
Each triplet must contain at least one color tone of the current chord.
Rotate through the extensions across beats — 7th in one beat, 9th in the next, 11th after.
Let triad tones fill the remaining triplet positions so the line has both spice and anchor.
End on a beat whose last triplet note is a plain chord tone.
examples: root-third-seventh, fifth-ninth-seventh, eleventh-ninth-seventh, fifth (triplets featuring extensions)
third-seventh-ninth, fifth-seventh-ninth, seventh-ninth-eleventh, root
rhythmConstraint:triplets_harmonicTarget.watchOut: Triplet speed blurs individual notes, so if the color tone is buried in the middle of a beat it will be unheard. Put the extension on the first or third triplet note where it gets natural emphasis.

rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_harmonicTarget:sc.interaction: The 3-3-2 tresillo pattern is the backbone of habanera, reggaeton, and most of Latin-diasporic popular music — three eighths, three eighths, two eighths packed into a bar. With free scale shape you can fill those positions with any melody you like, turning the rhythm into a hypnotic loop and the pitches into the variation layer.
steps: Internalize the tresillo — clap it before you play: one-and-a, two-and-a, three-and.
On each of the eight positions, choose any scale note.
Try grouping pitches with the rhythm — three notes that rise, then three that fall, then two that hold.
Vary which of the three rhythm cells carries the melodic peak.
Close with the tresillo landing on the root on the last cell.
examples: root-third-fifth (3), fourth-third-second (3), third-root (2)
third-fifth-seventh (3), sixth-fourth-second (3), second-root (2)
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_harmonicTarget:sc.watchOut: If you do not actively feel the 3-3-2 grouping, the line slides into straight eighth-note groove and the tresillo dies. Emphasize the first note of each cell with a slight accent.

rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_harmonicTarget:ar.interaction: A tresillo rhythm with a triad palette is the bomba or plena feel — Caribbean dance music built on a root/third/fifth backbone pushed through 3-3-2. Because the rhythm has exactly eight notes per bar and the palette has three pitches, you cycle through triad orderings inside the tresillo shape creating that driving Afro-Caribbean pulse.
steps: Clap the 3-3-2 pattern to lock it in.
Assign each rhythm cell a triad ordering — first three, next three, last two.
Let the cells contrast — rising triad, descending triad, holding pair, for example.
Rotate which triad tone starts each cell across bars so the tresillo does not get stuck.
End on the root on the last note of the final 2-cell.
examples: root-third-fifth (3), root up an octave-fifth-third (3), fifth-root (2)
fifth-root-third (3), fifth-root up an octave-fifth (3), third-root (2)
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_harmonicTarget:ar.watchOut: The tresillo wants a dance pulse — if you play three identical quarter-note-feel triads in a row, the 3-3-2 subdivision flattens into generic eighth-note motion. Accent the first of every group.

rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_harmonicTarget:ch.interaction: Tresillo rhythm with chord-tone landings is exactly how reggaeton bass lines work — a driving 3-3-2 with the phrase ending on a chord tone of the current chord. Because the tresillo naturally ends on the 2 cell (beats 7 and 8 of the eight-eighth-note bar), the landing hits right there, giving the progression motion that you can dance to.
steps: Clap the tresillo pattern to lock it in.
Play through the 3-3-2 with any scale notes for the first two cells.
On the final 2 cell, aim the last note at a chord tone of the current chord.
When the chord changes on a new bar, relock your landing for the new chord.
Close the round with the tresillo landing on the root.
examples: fourth-third-second (3), fifth-sixth-fifth (3), fourth-third (2, landing on third)
seventh-fifth-third (3), fourth-second-root (3), third-root (2, landing on root)
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_harmonicTarget:ch.watchOut: The 2 cell is short — only two notes — so landing on a chord tone requires tight aiming. Pre-plan which of the two positions will be the target and practice landing there specifically.

rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_harmonicTarget:co.interaction: A tresillo rhythm featuring color tones is Latin jazz in concentrated form — Eddie Palmieri or Michel Camilo spinning 9ths and 11ths through a habanera pulse. The dance rhythm plus the extended harmony creates that bilingual sophisticated-but-primal feel where the body moves while the ear lingers on the tartness.
steps: Lock in the 3-3-2 tresillo.
On each cell, include at least one 7th, 9th, or 11th of the current chord.
Let the triad tones fill the remaining positions.
Rotate which cell carries the color peak — sometimes the first 3, sometimes the 2.
End on a plain chord tone on the last note of the final cell.
examples: root-third-seventh (3), ninth-fifth-eleventh (3), ninth-root (2)
ninth-seventh-fifth (3), eleventh-ninth-seventh (3), fifth-root (2)
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_harmonicTarget:co.watchOut: Packing extensions into a fast tresillo can flatten them into passing tones. Give the extensions slight agogic emphasis — a tiny lean on them — so the ear catches their color within the dance pulse.

dynamics:swell_harmonicTarget:scaleShape.interaction: The swell — barely audible up to full voice and then dropping instantly to silence — plus free scale choice means every phrase is a long crescendo through whatever melodic shape you want. This is how Sarah Vaughan opens a ballad: the melody emerges from nothing, rises in both pitch and volume together, and then the ending cuts to silence instead of fading. It is theatrical and precise.
steps: Start the first note of the phrase at the quietest controllable volume.
Build volume gradually across the notes of the phrase, regardless of melodic direction.
Do not peak the crescendo until the penultimate note or so.
Cut to silence instantly on the final note — no fade.
Take a full beat of silence before the next swell begins.
examples: root → third → fifth → sixth → seventh (crescendo barely-to-full, then silence)
second → fourth → fifth → sixth → fifth (crescendo on an arch, final note silent)
dynamics:swell_harmonicTarget:scaleShape.watchOut: Most players peak the swell too early — the crescendo should reach full volume right at the end. Pace the volume deliberately, not just by feel, or you will arrive at fortissimo on the second note and coast.
dynamics:swell_harmonicTarget:scaleShape.deeperInsight: The instant cutoff at the end of the swell is what makes it dramatic — a normal phrase fades out, a swell punches a hole in the texture.

dynamics:swell_harmonicTarget:arpeggio13.interaction: A swell through the tonic triad — starting whisper-quiet on root, climbing through third, peaking on fifth or root-up-an-octave, then cutting — is the most essential classical gesture there is. It is the cello phrase at the start of a slow movement, the opera singer's first entrance. The triad gives the swell a perfectly stable harmonic arc while the dynamic gives it the whole dramatic effect.
steps: Begin on the root at the quietest controllable volume.
Move through the triad tones — third and fifth — climbing in both pitch and volume together.
Peak volume on the highest note, which should also be the loudest.
Cut instantly to silence.
Breathe before the next swell, which should begin from the quietest volume again.
examples: root → third → fifth → root up an octave (crescendo, peak on octave root, instant silence)
third → fifth → root up an octave → third up an octave (crescendo through triad, then cut)
dynamics:swell_harmonicTarget:arpeggio13.watchOut: With only three pitch classes the swell can feel formulaic — the pacing of the volume is the only variable. Do not rush the quiet notes; spend most of the phrase in the soft range.

dynamics:swell_harmonicTarget:chordToneL.interaction: A phrase that rises from silence to full voice and then lands on a chord tone is the arrival gesture every gospel singer knows — the build, the peak, the landing on the right note of the chord before the silence hits. The swell gives you the emotional ascent, the chord-tone landing gives you the harmonic resolution, and together they are pure vocal theater.
steps: Plan the last note of the phrase first — a chord tone of the current chord.
Begin the phrase barely audible on any scale note below the target.
Crescendo across the phrase as you approach.
Peak volume on the landing chord tone.
Cut to silence immediately after the landing note has sounded its full length.
examples: fourth → fifth → sixth → fifth (crescendo landing on fifth at peak volume, then silence)
second → third → fourth → fifth → third (crescendo landing on third at peak volume, then silence)
dynamics:swell_harmonicTarget:chordToneL.watchOut: Players often crescendo on the approach and coast on the landing — then the target chord tone is the soft one. The peak volume MUST be on the landing or the whole gesture collapses.

dynamics:swell_harmonicTarget:colorTone7.interaction: A slow swell into a color tone is the most aching gesture in jazz — you rise out of silence, climb through the scale, and peak on the 9th or the 11th instead of a safe chord tone. The volume and the pitch and the harmony all tense up together, and then you cut, leaving the extension ringing in the ear. This is how Jim Hall and Bill Evans phrased.
steps: Pick a color tone as the target peak — a 7th, 9th, or 11th.
Begin barely audible on a plain chord tone below the target.
Crescendo slowly as the line climbs.
Peak volume on the extension, hold it briefly, then cut.
Feel the unresolved color ring into the silence.
examples: third → fifth → seventh → ninth (crescendo from whisper, peak on ninth, silence)
fifth → seventh → eleventh → ninth (crescendo climbing, peak on eleventh then landing on ninth)
dynamics:swell_harmonicTarget:colorTone7.watchOut: The cut at the end is crucial — do not resolve the extension down to a chord tone at the last moment out of reflex. The silence is the resolution, not the melody.

dynamics:terraces_harmonicTarget:scaleSh.interaction: Terraces means jumping between distinct volume plateaus — loud bar, quiet bar, loud bar — with no fades. Combined with free scale choice, you can sculpt the melodic line independently from the volume shifts, and the two systems interact at the bar boundaries where the terrace changes. This is Baroque organ technique, where you pull a stop and the volume simply is what it is.
steps: Pick two or three distinct volume levels and commit to them as plateaus.
Play the first bar at level 1 with any scale notes.
At the next bar, jump suddenly to level 2 — no crescendo, no fade.
Let the melody continue naturally across the volume change.
Alternate levels by bar for the whole round.
examples: Bar 1 (loud): root → third → fifth → fourth. Bar 2 (soft): sixth → fifth → third → second
Bar 1 (soft): fifth → fourth → third. Bar 2 (loud): second → root → seventh → root
dynamics:terraces_harmonicTarget:scaleSh.watchOut: The plateaus must be distinct — if level 1 is just slightly louder than level 2 you get sloppy dynamics instead of terraces. Exaggerate the gap.

dynamics:terraces_harmonicTarget:arpeggi.interaction: Triad-only terraces is the classical concerto grosso effect — tutti loud for one bar of rolled triad, solo soft for the next bar of rolled triad. Because the palette is the same across terraces, the only thing changing is the volume plateau. It isolates dynamics as a pure expressive tool.
steps: Pick two volume levels — loud and soft — with a big gap.
Bar 1 (loud): play the triad tones in some order.
Bar 2 (soft): play the triad tones in a different order, instantly at the lower plateau.
Alternate bars, keeping the pitch content matched and the volume leap obvious.
End with a soft bar that trails off on the root.
examples: Bar 1 (loud): root → third → fifth → root up an octave. Bar 2 (soft): fifth → third → root → fifth
Bar 1 (soft): third → fifth → root → third. Bar 2 (loud): fifth → root up an octave → fifth → third
dynamics:terraces_harmonicTarget:arpeggi.watchOut: Since pitch material repeats, the terrace effect is all you have — if the volume gap blurs, the whole exercise disappears. Over-commit to the loud plateau and the soft plateau.

dynamics:terraces_harmonicTarget:chordTo.interaction: Volume plateaus that shift at bar boundaries combined with chord-tone landings creates a call-and-response in volume that is synchronized with the chord progression. Each terrace coincides with a chord change, so the volume shift and the harmonic shift arrive together, giving a strong sense of 'new thing happening' at each bar line.
steps: Set up two or three volume plateaus.
When the chord changes, also change the terrace volume.
Shape the melody so the phrase ending of each bar lands on a chord tone of that bar's chord.
Synchronize the volume change and the chord-tone landing so they arrive together.
Close with a soft terrace landing on the tonic root.
examples: Bar 1 loud over I: third → fifth → root → fifth (landing on fifth). Bar 2 soft over IV: fifth → third → fourth → root (landing on root of IV)
Bar 1 soft over I: second → root → third. Bar 2 loud over V: fourth → fifth → root (landing on root of V)
dynamics:terraces_harmonicTarget:chordTo.watchOut: If the volume shift happens one note late or one note early, the terrace is smeared into a fade. Shift on the downbeat of the new bar, precisely.

dynamics:terraces_harmonicTarget:colorTo.interaction: Volume plateaus with color tones is a way to spotlight the extensions — loud terrace features a color tone, soft terrace features another. The dynamics organize which extension gets attention in each bar, turning the 7th, 9th, and 11th into distinct characters that rotate in and out of the foreground.
steps: Set up two volume plateaus.
In the loud bar, feature one extension — say the 9th.
In the soft bar, feature a different extension — say the 11th.
Rotate across the round so each bar showcases a different color.
End on a soft bar with a plain chord tone for resolution.
examples: Bar 1 loud: ninth → seventh → fifth → ninth. Bar 2 soft: eleventh → ninth → seventh → eleventh
Bar 1 soft: seventh → fifth → third → seventh. Bar 2 loud: ninth → third → fifth → ninth
dynamics:terraces_harmonicTarget:colorTo.watchOut: Do not let the same extension appear in both terraces — the whole point is that each plateau features a distinct color. Cross-contamination dulls the contrast.

dynamics:whisper_harmonicTarget:scaleSha.interaction: Whispered volume with free scale choice forces intimate listening — the line is almost inaudible so every pitch decision must earn its place. This is the Elliott Smith quiet bedroom voice or the Bill Frisell single-coil breath. Dynamics disappear as a variable and melody becomes the entire foreground.
steps: Find the quietest volume you can produce while still controlling pitch.
Play any scale notes at that exact level across the whole round.
Do not let any note poke above the whisper — no accents, no crescendos.
Let the melodic shape do all the work of phrasing.
Close on the root at the same whispered volume.
examples: root → second → third → fifth → fourth → root (whispered wandering)
fifth → fourth → third → second → third → fourth → fifth (whispered return)
dynamics:whisper_harmonicTarget:scaleSha.watchOut: At whisper volume any sloppy note feels huge. Slow down if pitch clarity suffers — a clean whisper is worth more than a busy mumble.

dynamics:whisper_harmonicTarget:arpeggio.interaction: A whispered triad run is meditative tonic outlining — root, third, fifth spoken at the quietest possible volume, every note fragile. It is a concentration exercise: can you play the simplest possible material cleanly under the hardest possible dynamic control? This is nylon-string classical technique distilled.
steps: Sink into whisper volume before any note.
Play only root, third, fifth in any order and any octave.
Give each note full attention and let each ring before the next.
Keep the volume perfectly flat — no note louder than another.
End on a whispered root.
examples: root → third → fifth → root up an octave (whispered rising triad)
fifth → third → root → fifth → third (whispered rolling triad)
dynamics:whisper_harmonicTarget:arpeggio.watchOut: Even three notes feel like a lot at whisper volume if any one of them jumps out. Consistency is the whole exercise.

dynamics:whisper_harmonicTarget:chordTon.interaction: Whispered phrases that each land on a chord tone is the bedroom ballad approach — barely-there volume, but every line resolves correctly to the harmony. The chord-tone landing anchors the round even though the dynamics would let it float away. This is the Nick Drake sensibility.
steps: Sink to whisper volume.
Play freely through the scale and aim each phrase ending at a chord tone of the current chord.
Let the landing note be as quiet as the rest — no emphasis.
When the chord changes, adjust your next phrase's target accordingly.
End on a whispered root.
examples: third → fifth → sixth → fifth (whispered phrase, landing on fifth)
root → second → fourth → third (whispered phrase, landing on third)
dynamics:whisper_harmonicTarget:chordTon.watchOut: The landing is the pedagogical point but at whisper volume it wants to be just another note. Mark it with a tiny duration bump, never a volume bump.

dynamics:whisper_harmonicTarget:colorTon.interaction: Whispered color tones are the most delicate possible sound — an almost-silent 9th or 11th hanging in the air. This is the slow-ballad jazz pianist at 2am or the classical harpist featuring an unresolved extension. Extremely hard to play cleanly, extremely beautiful when it works.
steps: Sink to whisper volume.
Feature 7ths, 9ths, and 11ths of the current chord at that flat quiet level.
Let each color tone ring cleanly before moving on.
Use plain chord tones sparingly as breathing room.
End on a whispered plain chord tone for contrast after the extensions.
examples: third → seventh → ninth → seventh (whispered color arc)
eleventh → ninth → seventh → fifth (whispered descent through colors)
dynamics:whisper_harmonicTarget:colorTon.watchOut: Whispered extensions are easy to accidentally bend or wobble in pitch. Absolute stability of volume AND pitch is required — this is a virtuoso exercise in restraint.

dynamics:accentMap_harmonicTarget:scaleS.interaction: Accent Map means everything is quiet except one sudden loud note per bar — and free scale choice lets that accent fall on any pitch. The quiet texture is the wallpaper and the accent is the punctuation. Gospel piano players use this constantly: soft rolling chords, then one pointed high note that stabs through the texture.
steps: Play the bar mostly at low volume with any scale notes.
Pick one note per bar to be dramatically louder than the rest.
Vary which beat the accent falls on — bar 1 on beat 2, bar 2 on the and of 3, etc.
Let the accent note be a surprising pitch — a leap, a color, something the ear notices.
Close the round with an accent on the root.
examples: root → second → (ACCENT) sixth → fifth → fourth → root
fifth → (ACCENT) seventh → fourth → third → second → root
dynamics:accentMap_harmonicTarget:scaleS.watchOut: More than one accent per bar dilutes the effect — the accent is a single event, not a pattern. Pick one note and commit.

dynamics:accentMap_harmonicTarget:arpegg.interaction: One sudden loud note per bar inside a triad palette is a classic Motown bass fill — mostly quiet root-and-fifth walking, then a surprise accent on a specific triad tone. Because the pitch material is only three notes, the accent placement and its dynamic contrast do all the expressive work.
steps: Play the bar quietly through the triad tones in any order.
Choose one note per bar to be dramatically louder.
Make the accent land on a different triad tone each bar.
Let octave displacement amplify the accent — the loud note can also be an octave leap.
End on a quiet root.
examples: root → third → (ACCENT) fifth → root (accent on fifth)
fifth → root → (ACCENT) third up an octave → root (accent on high third)
dynamics:accentMap_harmonicTarget:arpegg.watchOut: Three pitches repeated softly can lull your ear to sleep so the accent does not feel like an accent anymore. Keep the soft material genuinely soft — the contrast is everything.

dynamics:accentMap_harmonicTarget:chordT.interaction: Quiet playing with one loud accent per bar combined with chord-tone landing — what if the accent IS the landing? That is exactly the exercise: the quiet material wanders, and on the final beat of each bar, the accent fires on a chord tone of the current chord. Each bar has an explicit climax that is both dynamic and harmonic.
steps: Play the bar quietly through any scale notes.
On the last beat, fire a loud accent that lands on a chord tone of the current chord.
Make the accent the peak of both volume and landing.
When the chord changes, the next bar's accent targets the new chord's tones.
Close the round with a quiet accent-free resolution on the root.
examples: third → second → fourth → (ACCENT) fifth (accent landing on fifth)
sixth → seventh → sixth → (ACCENT) root up an octave (accent landing on root)
dynamics:accentMap_harmonicTarget:chordT.watchOut: The quiet-wander-then-accent structure will get predictable if you always accent on beat 4. Occasionally accent on beat 2 or 3 for variety even though the chord tone still has to be the target.

dynamics:accentMap_harmonicTarget:colorT.interaction: A quiet bar punctuated by one loud extension is how Steely Dan writes horn lines — the section plays quietly, then one sudden fortissimo 9th or 11th jumps out. The dynamic accent plus the harmonic color creates a double surprise: the volume moves AND the pitch is tart. Very memorable.
steps: Play the bar quietly through chord and scale tones.
On one beat, fire a loud accent that is specifically a 7th, 9th, or 11th of the current chord.
Rotate which extension takes the accent each bar.
Let the rest of the bar remain quiet around the stabbing extension.
End the round quietly on a plain chord tone.
examples: root → third → fifth → (ACCENT) ninth (accent on the ninth)
fifth → seventh → fifth → (ACCENT) eleventh (accent on the eleventh)
dynamics:accentMap_harmonicTarget:colorT.watchOut: If the accent note is not loud enough, the extension just sounds like a passing tone and the whole concept collapses. Really commit to the volume leap — the contrast must be obvious.

dynamics:forte_harmonicTarget:scaleShape.interaction: Full volume throughout with free scale choice is the arena-rock solo feel — everything loud, everything present, every pitch choice exposed. There is no place to hide a weak note because the volume is fixed. Your only expressive tool is which scale degree to play next, and that decision drives the entire round.
steps: Commit to full volume from the first note.
Do not let any note soften — forte all the way through.
Play freely through the scale, making each pitch choice feel definitive.
Use rhythm and register as your variation tools since dynamics are locked.
Close on a strong downbeat root at the same full volume.
examples: root → third → fifth → seventh → sixth → fourth (forte throughout)
fifth → fourth → third → second → root (forte descent)
dynamics:forte_harmonicTarget:scaleShape.watchOut: At constant forte, ear fatigue sets in fast and your pitch choices get sloppy. Move deliberately and let each forte note be a confident decision, not a wall of noise.

dynamics:forte_harmonicTarget:arpeggio13.interaction: A constant-forte triad run is the mariachi trumpet feel or a brass-band fanfare — root, third, fifth hammered at full volume. Because the pitch set is small and the dynamic is fixed, the only expressive variable is the ordering and octave placement of the three triad tones. It is a short, sharp declaration of tonic.
steps: Play full volume from the downbeat.
Use only root, third, fifth in any order and octave.
Keep the energy constant — no apologetic softening.
Vary the order of the triad tones across phrases to avoid repetition.
End on a full-volume root as the final declaration.
examples: root → fifth → third → root up an octave → fifth (forte triad)
third → root → fifth → third up an octave → root up an octave
dynamics:forte_harmonicTarget:arpeggio13.watchOut: Forte plus triad is a recipe for macho monotony. Vary the rhythm and the articulation to keep the line musically interesting — the volume and pitch set cannot help you.

dynamics:forte_harmonicTarget:chordToneL.interaction: Full volume with chord-tone landings is how a jazz big-band lead trumpet plays — everything loud, every phrase nails the chord tone at the end. The constant forte requires confident pitch choices and the landing requires confident harmonic awareness. Both tools are forcing you to be decisive.
steps: Play full volume throughout.
Freely wander through the scale on the interior of each phrase.
Aim the final note of every phrase at a chord tone of the current chord.
When the chord changes, the next phrase's landing retargets.
Close on a forte tonic root.
examples: fourth → fifth → sixth → fifth (forte landing on fifth)
second → third → fourth → root (forte landing on root)
dynamics:forte_harmonicTarget:chordToneL.watchOut: At forte the landings can get pounded flat — every note is equally loud. Emphasize the landing with duration and weight rather than volume so it stands out even without a dynamic contrast.

dynamics:forte_harmonicTarget:colorTone7.interaction: Full-volume extended-chord playing is the Maceo Parker saxophone sound — everything loud, every note a 9th or an 11th or a 7th. The constant forte amplifies the color tones so they cut through like paint splashes. Extremely declarative, extremely tart.
steps: Play full volume throughout.
Feature 7ths, 9ths, and 11ths across the round — at least half the notes are extensions.
Use plain chord tones as breathing-room foil.
Rotate which extension dominates each phrase.
End on a forte plain chord tone for final resolution.
examples: third → seventh → ninth → fifth → eleventh (forte color parade)
ninth → seventh → eleventh → ninth → root (forte descent through extensions)
dynamics:forte_harmonicTarget:colorTone7.watchOut: At constant high volume a color tone and a chord tone sound equally aggressive — the color gets lost. Use articulation and duration to mark the extensions as the featured notes even while the volume is flat.

articulation:legato_harmonicTarget:scale.interaction: Smooth connected playing with free scale choice is the long bel canto line — every note pours into the next with no gap, and any scale degree is available. This is the Puccini aria feeling, or the Stan Getz ballad tone: pure melodic connection, pitch flowing like water. The legato makes every pitch choice feel like a natural consequence of the one before it.
steps: Play the first note and hold it right up until the attack of the next.
No gaps between notes — each note ends exactly when the next begins.
Use any mix of steps and skips through the scale.
Let the melody breathe through phrasing rather than through articulation gaps.
Close on the root with a long sustained tail.
examples: root → second → third → fifth → fourth → sixth → fifth (connected stepwise)
third → fifth → sixth → fifth → third → root (connected arc)
articulation:legato_harmonicTarget:scale.watchOut: Even tiny gaps between notes break legato. On guitar, use hammer-ons and pull-offs for stepwise motion and slides for leaps. On voice, do not close the vowel between notes.

articulation:legato_harmonicTarget:arpeg.interaction: Legato triad motion is the classical cellist's bowed arpeggio — root slurs into third slurs into fifth, every note connected into the next. Because the triad tones are a skip apart, the legato connection must cross the interval cleanly; this is exactly the bowing or slurring challenge that defines good string and wind technique.
steps: Begin on the root with a full firm tone.
Slur directly to the third with no gap — on guitar, a hammer-on; on voice, no restart.
Slur onward to the fifth equally cleanly.
Continue up to the root up an octave if the range allows.
Slur back down by the same path to the starting root.
examples: root → third → fifth → root up an octave → fifth → third → root (legato triad arch)
fifth → third → root → fifth → third → root (legato descending triad)
articulation:legato_harmonicTarget:arpeg.watchOut: Legato across a skip is the hardest — on guitar a hammer-on by a third sometimes produces an uneven second note. Practice the interval until the second note is as loud as the first.

articulation:legato_harmonicTarget:chord.interaction: Smooth connected playing that lands on chord tones is how jazz horn phrases work — the line slurs through scalar passages and arrives cleanly on a chord tone of the current chord. The legato means nothing interrupts the line's flow; the landing means the line has a target. Together they produce the long melodic arc that defines Lester Young or Stan Getz phrasing.
steps: Plan the chord tone you will land on.
Begin the phrase somewhere below or around the target.
Slur through the interior of the phrase with no gap between notes.
Slur directly onto the landing chord tone.
Sustain the landing for its full value before starting the next phrase.
examples: fourth → fifth → sixth → fifth → third (legato landing on third)
second → third → fourth → root (legato landing on root)
articulation:legato_harmonicTarget:chord.watchOut: As you approach the landing the temptation is to articulate it harder for emphasis. Resist — the legato must hold through the final note or the connection is broken.

articulation:legato_harmonicTarget:color.interaction: Legato color tones is the Herbie Hancock or Wayne Shorter ballad voicing — slurred connected phrases featuring 9ths and 11ths. The smooth connection makes the extensions sound inevitable rather than jarring, and the sustained legato tail lets each color tone ring into the ear.
steps: Slur connected notes through the scale.
Pass through 7ths, 9ths, and 11ths of the current chord without articulating them separately.
Let the color tones emerge as natural stopping points in the legato flow.
Sustain the extensions slightly longer than the plain triad tones within the same slur.
End on a sustained color tone legato from the previous note.
examples: third → fifth → seventh → ninth (slurred into the ninth)
root → third → fifth → seventh → eleventh → ninth (legato color sweep)
articulation:legato_harmonicTarget:color.watchOut: If the legato wobbles in pitch on a color tone, the extension sounds wrong rather than tart. Legato does not mean slack — it means connected with accurate pitch control.

articulation:staccato_harmonicTarget:sca.interaction: Staccato plus free scale is the Nutcracker Dance of the Sugar Plum Fairy feel — every note short, every note detached, any pitch choice. The space between notes makes each pitch into a discrete event that the ear can isolate. It is the opposite of legato: maximum attention to each individual note.
steps: Play each note short — about half its normal duration.
Leave a clean gap of silence between every note.
Use any scale notes in any order.
Let the spacing be consistent so the staccato feels pulse-like.
End on a short root with a final silence.
examples: root . third . fifth . fourth . sixth (all short, all gapped)
fifth . third . second . fourth . root (descending staccato)
articulation:staccato_harmonicTarget:sca.watchOut: Staccato can drift into accents — each short note equally hit. Keep the attacks consistent; staccato is about note length, not attack force.

articulation:staccato_harmonicTarget:arp.interaction: Staccato triad tones is the pizzicato string bass feel — short detached root, third, fifth plucked with space between. It is the most digestible form of a chord outline because each pitch gets isolated and the listener can hear the triad as a sequence rather than a blur.
steps: Play each triad tone short with a clean gap after.
Vary which triad tone comes next so the line is not a repeating pattern.
Use octave displacement between notes to keep the staccato interesting.
Keep every attack equally articulate.
End on a short, clean root.
examples: root . third . fifth . root up an octave . fifth (short, gapped triad)
fifth . root . third . fifth . third . root (descending staccato triad)
articulation:staccato_harmonicTarget:arp.watchOut: With only three pitches, the staccato rhythm becomes the main variable. Put some phrasing in the note lengths — some very short, some slightly longer — so the line is not robotic.

articulation:staccato_harmonicTarget:cho.interaction: Short detached notes landing on chord tones is the funk-guitar-chord-hit feel — stabs of sound, each one ending on a target chord tone, with clean silence between. It is the opposite of smooth legato phrasing: every phrase is a dart, not a line.
steps: Play short detached notes through the scale.
Aim the last short note of each phrase at a chord tone of the current chord.
Leave a full gap of silence after the landing.
When the chord changes, the next phrase retargets.
Close with a crisp staccato root.
examples: fourth . third . fifth (short notes landing on fifth)
second . fourth . root (short notes landing on root)
articulation:staccato_harmonicTarget:cho.watchOut: The staccato attack can obscure whether the landing note is actually the chord tone — it goes by too fast. Slow down if needed to verify the target.

articulation:staccato_harmonicTarget:col.interaction: Short detached color tones is the piccolo or piano-jab feel — stabs of 9ths and 11ths in fast succession, each one isolated enough that the ear registers its tartness before the next. The staccato prevents the color tones from blurring into a legato soup where the tension is smoothed over.
steps: Play short detached notes with clean gaps.
Feature 7ths, 9ths, and 11ths in the staccato pattern.
Each short note should land cleanly on its pitch — no smears.
Rotate which extension is featured so each stab is a different color.
End on a staccato plain chord tone.
examples: third . seventh . ninth . seventh (stabs of extensions)
eleventh . ninth . seventh . fifth (descending staccato colors)
articulation:staccato_harmonicTarget:col.watchOut: A mistimed staccato attack on an extension will sound like a mistake rather than a feature. Crispness and pitch accuracy must both be there.

articulation:slurredPairs_harmonicTarget.interaction: Slurred pairs — two notes smoothly connected, then a tiny gap — with free scale choice gives you the Rossini vocal fioritura feel or the baroque violin bow pattern: a constant flow of two-note sighs, each pair a miniature breath. The structure is rhythmic but the pitches are free, which lets you thread any melodic line through the articulation.
steps: Play two notes smoothly connected, then a tiny gap, then two more.
Vary which scale notes fill each pair.
Let some pairs be steps and others be skips.
Make the gap between pairs audible but brief — not a full silence.
End on a pair whose second note is the root.
examples: root-third . fifth-fourth . sixth-fifth . third-root (paired phrasing)
second-fourth . third-fifth . fourth-sixth . fifth-root (rising paired phrasing)
articulation:slurredPairs_harmonicTarget.watchOut: If the gap disappears, the pairs blur into one long legato line; if the gap is too big, they become disconnected staccato. The gap should be a tiny breath — audible but not broken.

articulation:slurredPairs_harmonicTarget.interaction: Paired triad notes is the baroque keyboard ornament pattern — root-third pair, then fifth-root pair, each with a tiny breath between. Because the triad tones are skips apart, every pair is a leap smoothed together, giving a distinctive angular-but-elegant sound.
steps: Play two triad tones smoothly connected, then gap, then two more.
Mix orderings — root-third, fifth-root, third-fifth, and their descents.
Use octave displacement between pairs so the line has range.
Keep the paired slurs tight and the gaps between pairs consistent.
End with a pair whose second note is the root.
examples: root-third . fifth-root up an octave . third-fifth . root (paired triad)
fifth-third . root-fifth . third-root up an octave . fifth-root
articulation:slurredPairs_harmonicTarget.watchOut: With paired slurs there is a tendency to make the first of each pair louder, which can turn into an accent pattern. Keep both notes of each pair equally weighted.

articulation:slurredPairs_harmonicTarget.interaction: Slurred pairs ending on chord tones is the classical phrasing of Mozart and Haydn — every pair is a miniature cadence, with the second note of each pair acting as a micro-resolution. When the phrase ends on a chord tone, the final pair's second note IS the chord tone. It is phrasing at the smallest possible scale.
steps: Play pairs through the scale.
As the phrase end approaches, aim the second note of the final pair at a chord tone of the current chord.
The first note of that final pair is the upbeat leading to the landing.
Let each pair have a subtle sense of tension-release.
Close the round with a pair landing on the root.
examples: fourth-third . second-root (pair landing on root)
sixth-fifth . fourth-third (pair landing on third)
articulation:slurredPairs_harmonicTarget.watchOut: The internal logic of a pair — first note upbeat, second note arrival — has to match the chord-tone landing rule. If the target is the second of a pair, the first note is the approach; do not accent the landing or you break the pair.

articulation:slurredPairs_harmonicTarget.interaction: Slurred pairs featuring color tones is the baroque suspension figure updated with jazz harmony — two notes tied together, the second one a 7th or 9th creating a tart sigh that the gap punctuates. This is how Ravel wrote piano ornaments: the color tone is always the second of the pair, so the ear hears a tiny catch of dissonance with each breath.
steps: Pair two notes smoothly, letting the second be a color tone of the current chord.
Gap briefly, then start the next pair.
Rotate extensions across pairs — one pair features the 9th, the next the 11th.
Keep the first note of each pair a plain tone so the color contrast is sharp.
End on a pair whose second note is a plain chord tone.
examples: third-seventh . fifth-ninth . root-eleventh (pair sighs into colors)
fifth-ninth . seventh-eleventh . ninth-seventh (rolling color pairs)
articulation:slurredPairs_harmonicTarget.watchOut: If the first note of a pair is also a color tone, the contrast between the two halves of the pair disappears. The structure is plain-tone-into-color-tone, not color-to-color.

articulation:tenuto_harmonicTarget:scale.interaction: Tenuto means every note held for its full value — emphasized, sustained, but still distinct from the next. Combined with free scale choice, it produces the weight of a Bach chorale melody or a Sacred Harp hymn: each note fully present, each transition audible, nothing rushed. It is the articulation of solemnity.
steps: Play each note for its full rhythmic value — do not clip short.
Let the next note begin exactly when the previous one ends.
Give every note slight emphasis through sustained weight, not through accent.
Use any scale notes in any shape.
End on a tenuto root held long enough to feel like final.
examples: root → third → fourth → fifth → fourth → third → root (tenuto wandering)
fifth → fourth → third → second → third → fourth → fifth (tenuto return)
articulation:tenuto_harmonicTarget:scale.watchOut: Tenuto is easy to confuse with legato — the difference is that tenuto notes are distinct, legato notes are connected. Tenuto has tiny gaps; legato does not.

articulation:tenuto_harmonicTarget:arpeg.interaction: Tenuto triad tones is the brass-chorale feel — each root, third, fifth held at its full value with weight and presence. Because the triad palette is small, the held weight of each note is exactly what makes the line feel monumental rather than monotonous. Think of a Bruckner brass passage: three notes, deeply sustained.
steps: Play each triad tone for its full duration with full emphasis.
Transition between tones without rushing.
Use octave displacement to give the tenuto notes varied color.
Let each note breathe before the next begins.
End on a long tenuto root.
examples: root → third → fifth → root up an octave (tenuto rising triad)
fifth → third → root → fifth (tenuto rolling triad)
articulation:tenuto_harmonicTarget:arpeg.watchOut: With only three pitches, tenuto phrasing can feel static. Use rhythmic variation — some notes last longer than others — to avoid creating a block of identical sustained notes.

articulation:tenuto_harmonicTarget:chord.interaction: Tenuto playing where each phrase lands on a chord tone is the sustained-melody approach of a good ballad singer — every note has weight, and every phrase resolves cleanly. The tenuto means no note is rushed, and the chord-tone landing means each phrase has a definite goal. It is phrasing for clarity and emotional weight.
steps: Play tenuto notes through the scale with full duration each.
Plan the landing chord tone and shape the phrase toward it.
Let the landing note be held with particular weight.
When the chord changes, retarget the next phrase's landing.
End on a tenuto root with a long sustain.
examples: fourth → fifth → sixth → fifth (tenuto landing on fifth)
second → third → fourth → root (tenuto landing on root)
articulation:tenuto_harmonicTarget:chord.watchOut: Under tenuto emphasis every note can feel equally important, which flattens the sense of arrival. Give the landing note slightly more sustain than the others — it is the held point.

articulation:tenuto_harmonicTarget:color.interaction: Tenuto color tones is the slow-jazz-ballad emphasis — every note has full weight, and the 7ths, 9ths, and 11ths get held long enough that their tart character becomes unmistakable. This is how Coltrane plays 'In a Sentimental Mood': sustained extensions, full presence, no rushing through the color.
steps: Play tenuto notes through the scale.
Feature color tones with the same full-value emphasis as plain chord tones.
Let the extensions breathe — their tartness needs time to register.
Use plain chord tones between extensions as contrast.
End on a tenuto plain chord tone so the resolution is heard.
examples: third → seventh → ninth → seventh (tenuto color arc)
eleventh → ninth → seventh → fifth (tenuto descent through extensions)
articulation:tenuto_harmonicTarget:color.watchOut: Sustained color tones can slip in pitch if the instrument or voice is not stable. Tenuto on extensions requires absolute pitch control — wobble ruins the flavor.

phraseLength:1bar_harmonicTarget:scaleSh.interaction: One-bar phrases with free scale choice are the haiku of melody — a tiny fragment, then a breath, then another fragment. Each bar is a complete small statement, not a run-on. This is how Thelonious Monk constructs his improvised lines: terse, isolated, each four-beat bar its own self-contained idea.
steps: Think of each bar as one complete sentence.
Play three to six notes in the bar using any scale shape.
Stop playing at the bar line — let the silence be audible.
The next bar should contrast the previous — new direction, new register, new shape.
End the round with a one-bar phrase that cadences on the root.
examples: Bar 1: root → third → fifth → fourth. Bar 2: (rest) seventh → sixth → fourth
Bar 1: fifth → fourth → third → second. Bar 2: (rest) second → root → seventh → root
phraseLength:1bar_harmonicTarget:scaleSh.watchOut: The temptation is to let the line bleed across the bar line into a continuous melody. One-bar means the bar is the boundary — you stop and start, not flow.

phraseLength:1bar_harmonicTarget:arpeggi.interaction: One-bar triad fragments are the most condensed tonic statement possible — four beats to spell out a complete root-third-fifth idea, then silence. Because the palette is only chord tones and the phrase length is only a bar, every fragment is a perfect miniature tonic declaration. Think of a classical development section's motivic fragments.
steps: Think of each bar as a complete triad statement.
Play three or four chord tones in some order during the bar.
Stop at the bar line for at least a beat of silence.
Next bar: a different triad ordering or octave shape.
End with a one-bar phrase landing on the root on beat 1 of the final bar.
examples: Bar 1: root → third → fifth → root up an octave. Bar 2: (rest) fifth → third → root
Bar 1: third → root → fifth → third. Bar 2: (rest) fifth → root up an octave → fifth → root
phraseLength:1bar_harmonicTarget:arpeggi.watchOut: With the tiny palette, each bar can end up identical. Force variation — different starting notes, different rhythms, different octaves — so the one-bar statements feel like distinct ideas.

phraseLength:1bar_harmonicTarget:chordTo.interaction: A 1-bar fragment that must land on a chord tone is the tightest possible harmonic discipline — you have four beats to say one thing and that thing has to resolve exactly. The short length forces you to pre-hear the target before the bar begins, because there's no room for a scenic route. Think of Steely Dan horn stabs or a punchy Stax guitar fill — tiny but chord-committed.
steps: Watch the backing chord changes and identify the root, third, and fifth of each upcoming chord in scale-degree terms.
For each bar, pick ONE target tone (root, third, or fifth) before you play anything.
Improvise three or four notes that lead into that target on beat 4 of the bar.
Use a rest on beat 1 of the next bar to confirm the landing and hear whether the target locked to the new chord.
Rotate target choice every bar so you don't default to the root every time.
examples: second → fourth → third (lands on the third of the bar's chord on beat 4)
fifth → sixth → fifth → root (fragment ending on the root as a resolution)
phraseLength:1bar_harmonicTarget:chordTo.watchOut: The biggest trap is running out of bar before you hit the target — you try to say too much in four beats and the landing arrives early or late. Leave the last half-beat silent if you need it; the target tone wants air around it.
phraseLength:1bar_harmonicTarget:chordTo.deeperInsight: Short chord-committed fragments are the engine of call-and-response blues and jazz comping — each bar is a mini thesis statement.

phraseLength:1bar_harmonicTarget:colorTo.interaction: A 1-bar fragment pointed at a seventh, ninth, or eleventh is a pure color flash — you don't have time to build context, so the extension just rings out naked and has to speak for itself. This is the harmonic equivalent of a hot-pink accent in a grey outfit. The short length makes the dissonance land hard, which is either thrilling or grating depending on how confidently you pick it.
steps: Pick your color tone for the bar from the seventh, ninth, or eleventh scale degree.
Start the bar with a neutral scale tone (root, fifth, or chord tone) so the ear has a reference.
Place the color tone on a strong beat — beat 2 or beat 3 — and let it ring.
End the bar either on the color tone itself or on a neighboring tone that slides into it next bar.
Alternate between 7, 9, and 11 across successive bars so you feel the difference in flavor.
examples: root → third → seventh (the seventh rings on beat 3 and holds)
fifth → ninth → root (ninth sits on beat 2 and tilts the bar into jazz color)
phraseLength:1bar_harmonicTarget:colorTo.watchOut: One bar is not enough time to explain a ninth — if you bury it inside fast movement the ear loses it and it just sounds like a wrong note. Rhythmically isolate the color tone with space on either side.
phraseLength:1bar_harmonicTarget:colorTo.deeperInsight: This is the Joe Henderson or Grant Green trick — one bar, one color tone, everything else in service of framing it.

phraseLength:2bar_harmonicTarget:scaleSh.interaction: Two bars of free scale movement is the most common sketching unit in improvisation — enough room for a real sentence but short enough to stay disciplined. With no harmonic targeting, the scale becomes a playground and the only structure is contour and rhythm. This is where you practice breathing — say a complete musical thought, then stop and listen.
steps: Decide the overall shape of your 2 bars before you start — rising, falling, arch, or valley.
Start on any scale degree except the root to avoid the default 'I'm starting from home' reflex.
Use beat 1 of bar 2 as the midpoint pivot — this is where the contour should turn.
End the phrase somewhere on bar 2 and leave the last beat or two empty as a period.
Vary the starting note every repetition so you don't loop the same gesture.
examples: second → third → fifth → sixth → fifth → third → second (arch shape across 2 bars)
seventh → sixth → fourth → third → fifth → fourth → second (descending then one lift)
phraseLength:2bar_harmonicTarget:scaleSh.watchOut: Without harmonic targeting you'll drift into pure scale runs — up, down, up, down — and the 2-bar length will feel like half of a bigger line instead of a complete thought. Force yourself to finish at a resting point and take silence before continuing.
phraseLength:2bar_harmonicTarget:scaleSh.deeperInsight: Great melodic writers like Paul Simon build entire songs out of 2-bar free-scale fragments strung together — the constraint teaches economy.

phraseLength:2bar_harmonicTarget:arpeggi.interaction: Two bars of triad-only playing forces the tonic chord to ring clearly in the listener's ear — you have room to state the triad, depart briefly, and return. This is bluegrass fiddle territory, gospel piano intro territory, Ennio Morricone trumpet line territory. The 2-bar container is long enough to avoid sounding like a warm-up exercise but short enough to keep the triad from blurring.
steps: Commit to only the root, third, and fifth of the key — no passing tones, no approach notes.
In bar 1, state the triad in one direction (ascending or descending) using rhythmic variety.
In bar 2, rearrange the same three notes in a different order — this is where it stops sounding like an exercise.
Land on any one of the three triad tones on the downbeat of the next bar as your resolution.
Rotate which triad tone you start on across repetitions to explore all three inversions.
examples: root → third → fifth → root up an octave → fifth → third (2-bar arch)
fifth → root → third → fifth → root → fifth → third (rearranged triad through bar 2)
phraseLength:2bar_harmonicTarget:arpeggi.watchOut: Two bars of only three notes tempts you to speed up to fill space — resist it. Use rhythmic variety (eighths, quarters, dotted figures) to fill the time instead of repeating the triad faster.
phraseLength:2bar_harmonicTarget:arpeggi.deeperInsight: Cole Porter and George Gershwin both wrote killer melodies from triad-only 2-bar cells — the triad is the strongest building block in Western music.

phraseLength:2bar_harmonicTarget:chordTo.interaction: Two bars with chord-tone landings is the classic bebop sentence length — long enough to tell a story but short enough that every landing matters. You have 8 beats to make a shape and glue it to the current chord with a single well-chosen final note. This is how Charlie Parker and Sonny Rollins built solos one brick at a time.
steps: Study the next two chord changes and pick the target chord tone for the end of bar 2 first.
Work backwards: plan the approach note (scale step above or below) that leads into that target.
Fill bars 1 and most of bar 2 with freer scale movement that prepares the ear for the landing.
Place the target chord tone exactly on beat 4 (or beat 4-and) of bar 2 so the next bar's chord can arrive clean.
Repeat with a different target tone on the same changes to feel how different landings reshape the phrase.
examples: second → third → fifth → fourth → third → second → root → third (two-bar line landing on the third)
sixth → seventh → root up an octave → seventh → fifth → fourth → fifth (landing on the fifth of the next chord)
phraseLength:2bar_harmonicTarget:chordTo.watchOut: The danger is running the same shape and just changing the final note — that makes the landings sound disconnected from what came before. Vary the approach each time so the landing feels like a consequence, not a correction.
phraseLength:2bar_harmonicTarget:chordTo.deeperInsight: This is the exact discipline that turns scale knowledge into bebop — the landing is not the target, it is the reward for a well-prepared phrase.

phraseLength:2bar_harmonicTarget:colorTo.interaction: Two bars with color tones gives you room to frame the extension — you can state a plain triad, then push into the seventh or ninth, then let it hang. This is Herbie Hancock writing Maiden Voyage, Wayne Shorter on Speak No Evil. The phrase length is perfect for 'statement plus color commentary' shapes.
