steps: Compose a theme that lands on a chord tone (say, the root of the chord).
State the theme plainly.
Variation 1: same contour, same rhythm, but shift the landing to the third of the chord.
Variation 2: same contour, shift the landing to the fifth.
Variation 3: ornament the theme while keeping the landing on a different chord tone.
examples: Theme: fifth → fourth → third → root || Var: fifth → fourth → third → third (landing shifted to the third)
Theme: second → third → root → third (landing on the third) || Var: second → third → root → fifth (landing shifted to the fifth)
harmonicTarget:chordToneLanding_phraseSt.watchOut: The contour must stay recognizable through each variation — if the shape changes as much as the landing, you've lost the theme-and-variation feel.
harmonicTarget:chordToneLanding_phraseSt.deeperInsight: Parker solos are built from exactly this: one shape, many landings. It creates an architectural coherence that feels like compositional thinking.

harmonicTarget:chordToneLanding_phraseSt.interaction: Chord-tone landings in an ostinato is a modal jazz trick — the ostinato repeats the same shape but, because the chord changes underneath, the landing target changes. So your fingers repeat the same phrase, but your ears hear it land differently on each chord. This is Miles Davis's So What, this is Herbie's Cantaloupe Island, this is how you make one ostinato feel harmonically narrative.
steps: Compose a short phrase (3-5 notes) that lands on the root of the tonic.
Repeat the same phrase exactly, even as the chord changes.
Notice that the 'landing' note now functions as a different chord tone (maybe the fifth of the new chord, or the third).
Stay committed to the exact pitches of the ostinato through all the changes.
Let your ear track how the ostinato note changes meaning as the chord shifts underneath.
examples: Ostinato: second → third → second → root (the root is the target, but it becomes different chord tones as changes move)
Ostinato: fifth → fourth → third → fifth (the fifth as target shifts meaning with each chord)
harmonicTarget:chordToneLanding_phraseSt.watchOut: The temptation is to change the ostinato to match each new chord — don't. The point is that the ostinato stays fixed while the harmonic meaning of the landing shifts.
harmonicTarget:chordToneLanding_phraseSt.deeperInsight: This is literally how Herbie Hancock built Cantaloupe Island — one repeated phrase that changes meaning as the chords cycle underneath it.

harmonicTarget:colorTone79_phraseStructu.interaction: Color tones in question-and-answer form is sophisticated pop songwriting — Joni Mitchell, Steely Dan, D'Angelo. The question ends on an extension (ninth, eleventh) which creates an unresolved upward pull, and the answer resolves the extension stepwise to a chord tone. The ninth doesn't just feel unresolved — it feels curious, inquisitive.
steps: Plan the question to end on a color tone (ninth, eleventh, or seventh).
Plan the answer to end on a chord tone (root, third, or fifth).
Play the question with the color tone as the last note — let it hang.
Play the answer, stepping down from the question's color tone to a chord tone.
Repeat the pattern, rotating which color tone ends the question.
examples: Q: third → fifth → seventh → ninth (lands on the ninth) || A: ninth → seventh → fifth → third (resolves to the third)
Q: fourth → fifth → seventh → eleventh (lands on the eleventh) || A: eleventh → ninth → seventh → fifth (resolves to the fifth)
harmonicTarget:colorTone79_phraseStructu.watchOut: A ninth at the end of a question must have space to ring — if you rush into the answer, the ninth doesn't get to hang. Leave a clear rest so the question's color registers.
harmonicTarget:colorTone79_phraseStructu.deeperInsight: This is the Donald Fagen songwriting move — unresolved extensions at the end of question phrases are what gives Steely Dan its sophisticated melodic character.

harmonicTarget:colorTone79_phraseStructu.interaction: Color tones in call-and-response is sophisticated modern jazz — the call statement features an extension, and the response echoes or rebuts with a different extension. Think Kenny Garrett, think Tigran Hamasyan. You're creating dialogue between two different colors (seventh and ninth, or ninth and eleventh) and the ear reads it as two voices in conversation.
steps: Plan which extension features in the call and which features in the response.
Play the call phrase featuring your first extension — let it ring.
Play the response phrase featuring a different extension.
The two colors should contrast — seventh in the call, eleventh in the response, for example.
Repeat with a new pair of colors each time.
examples: Call: fifth → seventh → root up an octave → seventh || Response: fourth → ninth → third → ninth (call features seventh, response features ninth)
Call: third → ninth → root (call features ninth) || Response: fifth → eleventh → fifth (response features eleventh)
harmonicTarget:colorTone79_phraseStructu.watchOut: Using different extensions in call and response creates great color contrast but can feel academic if not played with conviction. Commit to each extension as if you meant it.
harmonicTarget:colorTone79_phraseStructu.deeperInsight: This is how sophisticated jazz builds whole solos — each phrase features a specific extension, and the sequence of extensions becomes a narrative of harmonic colors.

harmonicTarget:colorTone79_phraseStructu.interaction: Color tones in theme-and-variation is advanced jazz composition — you state a theme featuring a particular extension, then each variation replaces that extension with a different one. The contour stays consistent but the harmonic color shifts with each pass. This is how Wayne Shorter wrote pieces like Infant Eyes and Speak No Evil — the variation is harmonic, not melodic.
steps: Compose a theme that prominently features a color tone (say, the ninth).
State the theme plainly.
Variation 1: same contour, same rhythm, but replace the ninth with a seventh.
Variation 2: same contour, replace with the eleventh.
Variation 3: same contour but layer multiple extensions together within the phrase.
examples: Theme: root → third → ninth → seventh || Var: root → third → seventh → sixth (replaces ninth with seventh)
Theme: fifth → seventh → ninth → fifth || Var: fifth → seventh → eleventh → fifth (replaces ninth with eleventh)
harmonicTarget:colorTone79_phraseStructu.watchOut: If the variation changes the rhythm or contour too much, the theme gets lost — keep the structural skeleton identical and let only the color tone vary.
harmonicTarget:colorTone79_phraseStructu.deeperInsight: Wayne Shorter's approach to melodic writing often uses exactly this — a single contour shape that gets rehashed with different extensions in each appearance.

harmonicTarget:colorTone79_phraseStructu.interaction: Color tones as an ostinato is mesmerizing — a short phrase that repeats forever and features a seventh, ninth, or eleventh. Because the extension never resolves, the phrase has an unresolved, hovering quality that creates a meditative trance. This is Pharoah Sanders territory, this is Alice Coltrane, this is the spiritual-jazz aesthetic.
steps: Compose a short 4-6 note ostinato that prominently features an extension.
Play it through once.
Repeat it exactly for at least 16 cycles.
Focus on the unresolved hovering quality of the color tone.
Let the repetition create its own kind of resolution through pure familiarity.
examples: fifth → ninth → seventh → ninth (trance ostinato with ninth)
root → third → ninth → root (triad plus ninth ostinato)
harmonicTarget:colorTone79_phraseStructu.watchOut: An ostinato featuring an extension can feel dissonant after many repetitions — resist changing it. The point is that your ear eventually accepts the extension as the new home.
harmonicTarget:colorTone79_phraseStructu.deeperInsight: Spiritual jazz and modal jazz both use extension-heavy ostinatos to bypass tonal gravity — the repeated phrase becomes the new center of gravity, and the extension becomes home.

harmonicTarget:scaleShape_neckZone:open.interaction: Free scale playing in open position is folk-guitar fluency — open strings ringing, scale shapes fingered against open strings for extra resonance. This is Doc Watson territory, this is Nick Drake territory, this is Leo Kottke territory. The open position gives you a natural bass drone (especially the low open strings) under any melodic line you play.
steps: Stay below the 5th fret — this is open position territory.
Start on a scale degree that's available as an open string (depending on the key).
Run free scalar phrases using both fretted notes and open strings as pivot points.
Let the open strings ring under fretted notes to create a natural drone.
Experiment with shifting between 1st and 2nd fret alternate fingerings for slight timbral change.
examples: root (open) → second (2nd fret) → third (4th fret) → fifth (open string on a different string)
fifth → fourth → third → second → root (open position scale descent)
harmonicTarget:scaleShape_neckZone:open.watchOut: Open position is limited in range — if you try to go beyond 5 frets, you leave the zone. Keep the melodic range realistic for the position.
harmonicTarget:scaleShape_neckZone:open.deeperInsight: Open position is where guitarists learn their first scales — free scale playing here is the foundational folk-guitar improvisation skill.

harmonicTarget:scaleShape_neckZone:mid.interaction: Free scale playing in the mid-neck zone is where the guitar feels fattest — frets 5-9 are barre-chord territory, rock rhythm territory, the fattest tonal range of the instrument. Free scale playing here gives you runs that sit in the rock and blues sweet spot. This is Hendrix rhythm territory, this is the pentatonic box, this is where SRV lives.
steps: Stay between frets 5 and 9 — no open strings, no upper register.
Start on any scale degree within the position and run free scalar phrases.
Use the full width of the fretboard (all 6 strings) within this fret range.
Let the mid-neck compression give the tone its characteristic fat, warm quality.
Experiment with string bending within the position to add expressive shape.
examples: fifth → sixth → seventh → sixth → fifth (mid-neck arch)
root → third → fifth → third → root (barre-area triad flow)
harmonicTarget:scaleShape_neckZone:mid.watchOut: Mid-neck position can feel cramped when you try to stretch — if you need to reach a note 5 frets away from your index finger, you're stretching out of position. Either shift or use a different note.
harmonicTarget:scaleShape_neckZone:mid.deeperInsight: Mid-neck is where most rock and blues solos live — it's the compromise between open-position folk and high-neck soloing, and it has its own sonic character.

harmonicTarget:scaleShape_neckZone:high.interaction: Free scale playing in the high-neck zone is solo guitar territory — frets 9-15 where the tone thins out and the notes sound piercing and vocal. This is SRV's Scuttle Buttin, this is Allan Holdsworth, this is Jerry Garcia at peak. The thin, clear quality of high-neck notes gives free scale playing a soaring, vocal character.
steps: Stay between frets 9 and 15 — upper neck territory.
Start on a scale degree in the position and run free scalar phrases using all 6 strings.
Let the thin, clear high-neck tone ring — this is where the guitar sounds most like a singer.
Bends are especially effective up here — the strings are tight and the pitches move.
Experiment with slides between positions for expressive glides.
examples: fifth → sixth → seventh → sixth → fifth (high-neck arch)
seventh → highest scale note → seventh → sixth (vocal upper-neck hover)
harmonicTarget:scaleShape_neckZone:high.watchOut: High-neck notes can sound brittle if over-picked — use a light touch so the thin tone stays musical rather than strident.
harmonicTarget:scaleShape_neckZone:high.deeperInsight: The high neck is where the guitar earns its reputation as a solo-melodic instrument — the thin upper register sounds closer to a human voice than any other part of the neck.

harmonicTarget:scaleShape_neckZone:fullR.interaction: Free scale playing across the full range of the neck is jazz and fusion territory — you can start low in open position and end high past the 12th fret, or vice versa, within a single phrase. This is Pat Metheny, this is Kurt Rosenwinkel, this is every great jazz guitarist. The full-neck approach lets your phrases have real dynamic and tonal range.
steps: Start a phrase at one end of the neck (say, low in open position).
Use scale shifts to travel up the neck as the phrase develops.
Use slides, legato, or position shifts to connect one fret zone to the next.
End the phrase at the opposite end of the neck (high, past the 9th fret).
Return journey: next phrase travels back down the neck the opposite direction.
examples: root (open) → second → third → fifth (mid-neck) → seventh → highest scale note (high-neck)
fifth (high-neck) → third (mid-neck) → root (open) — descending full-range line
harmonicTarget:scaleShape_neckZone:fullR.watchOut: Connecting fret zones requires clean position shifts — if you jump without a slide or glue note, the phrase sounds chunked. Use slides or legato to smooth the transitions.
harmonicTarget:scaleShape_neckZone:fullR.deeperInsight: Full-range playing is what separates journeyman guitarists from masters — knowing the whole neck means never being boxed into one shape.

harmonicTarget:arpeggio135_neckZone:open.interaction: Triad outlining in open position is where every guitarist starts — the open chord shapes give you triads that include open strings, which means the triad rings with extra resonance. This is folk strumming territory, this is songwriter territory, this is where most guitarists first discover the triad as a concept. The open strings give the triad a bell-like clarity.
steps: Stay in frets 0-5 and use open strings wherever possible.
Identify the root, third, and fifth on the available strings in the open position.
Arpeggiate the triad — one note at a time — starting on the root.
Let open strings ring where possible to create a natural drone under the triad.
Rotate through different inversions (root-third-fifth, third-fifth-root, fifth-root-third).
examples: root (open or low fret) → third (2nd or 4th fret) → fifth (open or 2nd fret)
fifth → third → root → fifth (open position triad rearrangement)
harmonicTarget:arpeggio135_neckZone:open.watchOut: Open position triads can sound too folky if every shape is the cowboy-chord version. Experiment with less-common open voicings for variety.
harmonicTarget:arpeggio135_neckZone:open.deeperInsight: Open position is where guitarists learn that a triad is just 3 notes — and that you can find them almost anywhere on the first 5 frets.

harmonicTarget:arpeggio135_neckZone:mid.interaction: Triad outlining in mid-neck is barre chord territory — the triad fingered against the fatter, compressed mid-neck tone. Each of the three notes has a beefy, rock-ready character. This is how hard rock and metal intros get their triadic weight. The mid-neck compression makes even a simple 1-3-5 outline sound authoritative.
steps: Stay between frets 5 and 9.
Identify the root, third, and fifth in a barre-chord-compatible shape.
Arpeggiate the triad one note at a time, using the fatter mid-neck tone to give each note weight.
Experiment with picking vs fingerpicking to vary attack quality.
Rotate through different inversions — triad across strings, same string in sequence.
examples: root (5th fret bass) → third (4th fret next string up) → fifth (5th fret another string)
fifth → root → third (triad starting on the fifth)
harmonicTarget:arpeggio135_neckZone:mid.watchOut: The mid-neck triad can feel heavy and slow if every note sustains — use rhythmic variety so the triad has life.
harmonicTarget:arpeggio135_neckZone:mid.deeperInsight: Every metal power chord intro is effectively a 1-5 or 1-3-5 outlined in mid-neck — the fat, compressed tone is what gives the riff its authority.

harmonicTarget:arpeggio135_neckZone:high.interaction: Triad outlining in high-neck is solo guitar territory — the triad gets thin, clear, and vocal up here. Each of the three notes rings like a bell or a voice. This is where jazz guitarists and fusion players outline chord changes in the upper register, where pedal-steel players live, where George Benson shines. The triad becomes angelic instead of muscular.
steps: Stay between frets 9 and 15.
Identify root, third, and fifth in the upper neck position.
Arpeggiate the triad one note at a time — the thin high-neck tone gives each note its own identity.
Let the notes ring slightly into each other for a bell-like harmonic bloom.
Rotate through inversions.
examples: root (high-neck, say 12th fret) → third (nearby string) → fifth (higher string, same fret area)
fifth → third → root up an octave (high triad)
harmonicTarget:arpeggio135_neckZone:high.watchOut: High-neck triads can sound piercing if picked too hard — use a lighter touch to preserve the bell-like quality.
harmonicTarget:arpeggio135_neckZone:high.deeperInsight: The upper neck is where triads ring with the most harmonic purity — the partials align better in the upper octaves than lower down.

harmonicTarget:arpeggio135_neckZone:full.interaction: Triad outlining across the full neck range is jazz guitar virtuosity — Pat Metheny, Bill Frisell, Julian Lage. You take three pitches (root, third, fifth) and span them from open position all the way up past the 12th fret, creating a triad that covers 3-4 octaves. The gesture is dramatic — a triad that starts in your lap and ends in the stratosphere.
steps: Start on the root in open position (lowest available octave).
Move to the third one octave up, in mid-neck.
Move to the fifth another octave up, in high-neck.
Reach the root up two octaves at the highest available fret.
Descend through the triad back to the starting root, potentially picking up the octaves you skipped.
examples: root (low, open) → third (mid-neck, octave up) → fifth (higher mid-neck) → root up an octave (high-neck)
fifth (open) → root (next octave up) → third (mid-neck) → fifth (high-neck) → root up two octaves
harmonicTarget:arpeggio135_neckZone:full.watchOut: Full-range triad requires smooth position shifts — if you jump chunks the phrase stops sounding continuous. Use slides or legato to glue the octaves together.
harmonicTarget:arpeggio135_neckZone:full.deeperInsight: Pat Metheny's signature solos are often full-range triad outlining — he uses the entire neck as his canvas for one simple triadic idea.

harmonicTarget:chordToneLanding_neckZone.interaction: Chord-tone landings in open position is folk-country guitar soloing — Norman Blake, Doc Watson, Jerry Reed in acoustic mode. The open strings provide ringing resonance and the chord-tone targeting keeps the improvising locked to the changes. This is how country-flatpick soloists traverse fiddle tunes over rapidly changing chord progressions.
steps: Stay in open position (frets 0-5).
Identify chord-tone targets in scale-degree terms for each chord change.
Improvise short phrases using both fretted notes and open strings.
Land each phrase on a chord tone using open strings where available for extra ring.
Rotate which chord tone you target.
examples: second → third → root → third (lands on the third in open position)
fifth → fourth → third → root (resolves to open-string root)
harmonicTarget:chordToneLanding_neckZone.watchOut: Open position limits your access to chord tones — some targets will only be available on certain strings. Plan your landings around which open strings can carry them.
harmonicTarget:chordToneLanding_neckZone.deeperInsight: Doc Watson's flatpicking is almost entirely open-position chord-tone landing work — the resonance of the open strings is what gives the style its signature sound.

harmonicTarget:chordToneLanding_neckZone.interaction: Chord-tone landings in mid-neck is rock and blues improvisation with harmonic discipline — you're in the barre chord zone where the power is, and every phrase commits to the chord changes. This is where SRV's solos live when he's thinking bebop, where Hendrix lands when he's being precise, where every Nashville session guitar player earns their paycheck.
steps: Stay in frets 5-9 (mid-neck).
Identify chord tones for each chord change in scale-degree terms.
Run scalar phrases using the fat mid-neck tone, building toward landings.
The mid-neck gives you good bending access — use string bends to reach targets expressively.
Rest between phrases so the landings have time to ring.
examples: fourth → fifth → fourth → third (mid-neck, lands on the third)
sixth → fifth → fourth → fifth (blues resolution to the fifth)
harmonicTarget:chordToneLanding_neckZone.watchOut: Mid-neck bends can overshoot targets — make sure the bend lands exactly on the chord tone, not a quarter tone above or below.
harmonicTarget:chordToneLanding_neckZone.deeperInsight: This is bread-and-butter blues and rock lead guitar — mid-neck is where the instrument speaks most confidently and chord-tone discipline is what makes the line feel musical instead of aimless.

harmonicTarget:chordToneLanding_neckZone.interaction: Chord-tone landings in high-neck is upper-register jazz and fusion guitar — Wes Montgomery's octaves, Pat Martino, John Scofield. The thin clear upper neck gives each landing a bell-like clarity, and the chord-tone discipline makes every high-register choice feel deliberate. This is how you solo up top without sounding aimless or shreddy.
steps: Stay in frets 9-15 (high-neck).
Identify chord-tone targets in scale-degree terms.
Run scalar phrases in the upper range, building toward landings.
Use the thin upper-neck tone to let each landing ring with clarity.
Small bends up here are effective — the high strings are tight and bendable.
examples: sixth → seventh → highest scale note → seventh (high-neck, lands on the seventh)
fifth → fourth → fifth → third (upper neck resolution to the third)
harmonicTarget:chordToneLanding_neckZone.watchOut: High-neck notes can sound piercing if picked hard — let the natural thin tone carry the landing with a light touch.
harmonicTarget:chordToneLanding_neckZone.deeperInsight: Wes Montgomery's upper-register solo work was chord-tone landing discipline in the high neck — the clarity of the upper register and the harmonic precision of the landings combined to make him sound effortless.

harmonicTarget:chordToneLanding_neckZone.interaction: Chord-tone landings across the full neck is master-class jazz guitar — Wes, Pat Metheny, Kurt Rosenwinkel. You move freely between open position and upper neck within a single phrase, always landing on chord tones no matter where you end up. This is what separates a good improviser from a great one — the harmonic discipline holds whether you're down by the nut or up at the 15th fret.
steps: Identify chord-tone targets in scale-degree terms.
Start a phrase in one neck zone (say, mid-neck).
Let the phrase travel up or down the neck through slides and position shifts.
Land on a chord tone in a different neck zone than you started (say, high-neck).
Return phrases travel back the opposite direction, also ending on chord tones.
examples: root (open) → third (mid-neck) → fifth (high-neck) → third (high-neck, landing on the third)
seventh (high-neck) → fifth (mid-neck) → third (mid-neck) → root (open, landing)
harmonicTarget:chordToneLanding_neckZone.watchOut: Traveling the neck tempts you to focus on the position shift and forget the landing. Set the target first, then let the position shifts serve the target — not the other way around.
harmonicTarget:chordToneLanding_neckZone.deeperInsight: This is the full flowering of jazz guitar technique — the ability to solo anywhere on the neck and still hit the changes with precision is the aspiration of every serious improviser.

harmonicTarget:colorTone79_neckZone:open.interaction: Color tones in open position is a clever trick — you find the seventh, ninth, or eleventh as an open string or low fretted note, and the extension rings naturally alongside the open triad. This creates lush open voicings that feel both folky and sophisticated. Think Nick Drake's use of DADGAD, John Fahey's modal tuning work, contemporary classical guitar.
steps: Stay in frets 0-5.
Identify where your extension (7, 9, or 11) lives in open position.
Play scalar phrases that approach the color tone using open strings and nearby fretted notes.
Let open strings ring under the extension for a natural harmonic bloom.
Resolve stepwise to a chord tone, letting the extension die out slowly.
examples: root (open) → second → third → ninth (open string, octave up) ringing together
fifth → sixth → seventh (open or low fret) → fifth (resolves through the seventh)
harmonicTarget:colorTone79_neckZone:open.watchOut: Open position limits which extensions are easily available — some might require awkward stretches. Choose the extension based on what your position actually supports.
harmonicTarget:colorTone79_neckZone:open.deeperInsight: DADGAD and other open tunings are often designed specifically to put extensions on open strings — the whole point is to make sophisticated harmony easy in the open position.

harmonicTarget:colorTone79_neckZone:mid.interaction: Color tones in mid-neck is modern jazz guitar rhythm territory — the extensions sit comfortably in barre chord shapes, and the fat mid-neck tone gives the extension real weight. This is Ed Bickert territory, this is Lenny Breau, this is contemporary neo-soul comping. The mid-neck compression makes the ninth sound like it matters.
steps: Stay in frets 5-9.
Identify extensions in scale-degree terms relative to the barre-chord shape.
Play scalar phrases through the extension using the fat mid-neck tone.
Let the extension sustain a full beat to register as color.
Resolve stepwise back to a chord tone or let the extension hang.
examples: root → third → ninth → seventh (mid-neck ninth feature)
fifth → sixth → eleventh → ninth (mid-neck eleventh)
harmonicTarget:colorTone79_neckZone:mid.watchOut: Mid-neck extensions can get muddy if multiple strings ring at once — use selective muting so only the intended note sounds.
harmonicTarget:colorTone79_neckZone:mid.deeperInsight: Mid-neck is where jazz guitar's rhythm-section voicings live — extension-heavy barre voicings in this zone are the sound of modern jazz comping.

harmonicTarget:colorTone79_neckZone:high.interaction: Color tones in high-neck is fusion and modern jazz single-note soloing — the thin upper register gives each extension a piercing clarity that's impossible anywhere else on the instrument. This is Allan Holdsworth, this is George Benson, this is modern players like Gilad Hekselman. The upper register is the color tone's natural home because the harmonic structure is so exposed.
steps: Stay in frets 9-15.
Identify the extension in scale-degree terms.
Run single-note scalar phrases that feature the color tone at the top of the line.
Let the thin upper-neck tone give the extension its distinctive clarity.
Resolve stepwise back down through the position.
examples: fifth → seventh → highest scale note → ninth (upper-neck ninth feature)
sixth → eleventh → seventh → sixth (upper-neck eleventh)
harmonicTarget:colorTone79_neckZone:high.watchOut: Upper-neck extensions are tuning-exposed — a slightly mistuned ninth sounds awful up here. Check tuning and string intonation before committing to high-neck color tones.
harmonicTarget:colorTone79_neckZone:high.deeperInsight: The upper neck is where color tones ring with the most harmonic purity — the partials align cleanly, which is why fusion players love the high register for extension work.

harmonicTarget:colorTone79_neckZone:full.interaction: Color tones across the full neck is master-class jazz and fusion guitar — an extension can start as an open string, travel up through mid-neck fretted, and end as a high-neck melodic note, all within one phrase. This is how Pat Metheny outlines harmony across the whole instrument. The extension becomes a through-line that connects the neck zones.
steps: Identify your target extension in scale-degree terms.
Start the phrase in one neck zone with the extension available.
Travel up the neck via slides, legato, or position shifts.
End in a different neck zone, still featuring the same extension.
Return phrases travel the opposite direction.
examples: root (open) → third (mid-neck) → fifth (mid-neck) → ninth (high-neck) (the ninth as destination)
eleventh (high-neck) → seventh (mid-neck) → fifth (mid-neck) → root (open, ninth lingering)
harmonicTarget:colorTone79_neckZone:full.watchOut: Full-range color tone work requires you to know where each extension lives in every neck zone — this is advanced fretboard knowledge. Map the extensions before you play.
harmonicTarget:colorTone79_neckZone:full.deeperInsight: This is full jazz-guitar fluency — knowing where every extension lives across the whole neck and being able to traverse them in a single thought.

harmonicTarget:scaleShape_noteTransition.interaction: Free scale playing with clean attack is the disciplined picking exercise — every note freshly fretted and freshly struck, no connection sounds, no legato. This is country flatpicking, this is Django Reinhardt, this is classical guitar. The clean attack gives every scale tone its own identity and forces rhythmic accuracy because there's no smearing to hide behind.
steps: Pick every note with a distinct attack — no slurs, no legato.
Fret every note freshly — lift your finger off the previous note before fretting the next.
Run free scalar phrases at a tempo where every note is clean.
Listen for any notes that connect accidentally — those are mistakes.
Build tempo only when every note is cleanly separated.
examples: root → second → third → fourth → fifth (clean attack scale climb)
seventh → sixth → fifth → fourth → third (clean descent)
harmonicTarget:scaleShape_noteTransition.watchOut: Clean attack at fast tempo exposes every weakness — any fingering sloppiness in the fretting hand becomes audible. Start slow and build up only when every note is perfect.
harmonicTarget:scaleShape_noteTransition.deeperInsight: This is the foundation of country and bluegrass flatpicking — the clean attack is what gives the style its crystalline articulation.

harmonicTarget:scaleShape_noteTransition.interaction: Free scale playing with hammer-ons and pull-offs is legato territory — the scale flows as if sung, with only the first note of each group actually picked. This is Eddie Van Halen, this is Allan Holdsworth, this is classical guitar slur work. The scale feels liquid instead of percussive, and the fretting hand does most of the work.
steps: Pick only the first note of each 3- to 5-note group.
Execute the rest of the group with hammer-ons (ascending) and pull-offs (descending).
Let the notes connect seamlessly — you should barely hear individual attacks after the first.
Run scalar phrases using legato exclusively.
Experiment with varying the pickup points — pick on beat 1 and 3, or just beat 1, etc.
examples: pick root → hammer to third → hammer to fifth → pick, hammer to seventh → hammer to highest scale note
pick fifth → pull off to fourth → pull off to third → pick, pull off to second → pull off to root
harmonicTarget:scaleShape_noteTransition.watchOut: Hammer-ons and pull-offs get weak fast — if the notes after the picked one are too quiet, the legato line sounds uneven. Strike the fretting hand harder to generate tone.
harmonicTarget:scaleShape_noteTransition.deeperInsight: This is how fusion guitarists like Holdsworth get their wind-instrument fluidity — legato playing creates a continuous line that feels more like a horn than a plucked string.

harmonicTarget:scaleShape_noteTransition.interaction: Free scale playing with slides is desert blues, Hawaiian steel, and reggae guitar territory — every transition is a glide. The scale stops being a set of discrete pitches and becomes a continuous landscape you travel through. This is Tinariwen, this is surf-guitar tremolo, this is slide guitar without the bottleneck. Each move is a gesture, not an attack.
steps: Pick only the starting note of each scale segment.
Slide from note to note — your finger never leaves the fretboard between pitches.
Let the slide be audible — the glide is part of the music, not a side effect.
Vary slide speed — some should be fast flicks, others slow swoops.
Run phrases where every transition is a slide.
examples: pick third → slide to fifth → slide to fourth → slide to third (slide-connected descent)
pick root → slide to third → slide to fifth → slide to sixth (slide climb)
harmonicTarget:scaleShape_noteTransition.watchOut: Slides are only musical if the destination note is held — if you slide through a pitch without resting on it, the note disappears. Arrive and rest, then slide again.
harmonicTarget:scaleShape_noteTransition.deeperInsight: Tinariwen's guitar style is almost entirely slide-connected scalar playing — the technique is the genre's defining sonic feature.

harmonicTarget:scaleShape_noteTransition.interaction: Free scale playing with bend-to-target is blues and rock guitar at its most expressive — you reach target scale tones by bending up from a lower fret rather than fretting them directly. This is B.B. King, SRV, Clapton, Hendrix. The bent approach gives each target a vocal, expressive entry that fretting directly can never match.
steps: Pick a target scale tone you want to reach.
Instead of fretting it directly, fret a lower pitch and bend up to reach the target.
Make sure the bend lands exactly on the target — not a quarter tone above or below.
Release the bend and let the string return.
Run phrases where each target is reached via a bend.
examples: pick second, bend to third (reaches the third via bend)
pick fourth, bend to fifth (reaches the fifth via bend)
harmonicTarget:scaleShape_noteTransition.watchOut: Bends that don't quite reach the target sound out of tune — the bend must be exact. Practice bending against a reference pitch so you can hear when the target arrives.
harmonicTarget:scaleShape_noteTransition.deeperInsight: Bend-to-target is the heart of blues guitar expression — every legendary blues solo is built from a few expressive bends rather than a hundred picked notes.

harmonicTarget:arpeggio135_noteTransitio.interaction: Triad outlining with clean attack is the classical guitar arpeggio exercise — every note of the triad freshly picked, freshly fretted, no connective sounds. Each of the three chord tones gets its own pristine entry. This is Carcassi, this is Giuliani, this is Bach partita movement territory. The triad becomes a set of three bells rung in sequence.
steps: Commit to picking every note with a distinct, clean attack.
Fret each triad tone freshly and release it before the next.
Play the triad one note at a time using only root, third, and fifth.
Listen for any slurs or connection sounds — those are mistakes.
Rotate through inversions to vary the shape.
examples: root → third → fifth → root up an octave (clean triad climb)
fifth → third → root → third → fifth (clean triad rearrangement)
harmonicTarget:arpeggio135_noteTransitio.watchOut: Clean attack exposes every fingering weakness — a triad played unevenly sounds worse than a scale played unevenly because each note is so exposed.
harmonicTarget:arpeggio135_noteTransitio.deeperInsight: Classical guitar students spend years on clean-attack triad exercises — it's the foundation of the technique.

harmonicTarget:arpeggio135_noteTransitio.interaction: Triad outlining with hammer-ons and pull-offs is electric guitar tapping ancestor material — you hammer through the triad tones, which works especially well on one string where root, third, and fifth fall within reach. This is Eddie Van Halen's single-string tapping setup, this is country legato fills, this is classical slur exercises applied to triads.
steps: Find the triad on a single string — root, third, fifth all within a few frets of each other.
Pick only the root.
Hammer on to the third without picking.
Hammer on to the fifth without picking.
Pull off back to the root to complete the cycle.
examples: pick root → hammer to third → hammer to fifth → pull off to root (single-string triad legato)
pick fifth → pull off to third → pull off to root (triad descent legato)
harmonicTarget:arpeggio135_noteTransitio.watchOut: Hammer-ons and pull-offs produce weaker notes than picked ones — make sure the triad still rings out evenly by striking the fretting hand firmly.
harmonicTarget:arpeggio135_noteTransitio.deeperInsight: Eddie Van Halen's tapping is essentially triad-outline legato extended to two hands — the technique is built directly on single-string hammer-pull triad work.

harmonicTarget:arpeggio135_noteTransitio.interaction: Triad outlining with slides is pedal steel territory and slide guitar territory — root to third to fifth to root up an octave, every transition a glide rather than a strike. This is Hawaiian slack-key, this is Ry Cooder, this is every weepy country ballad intro. The slides make the triad feel liquid and expressive, like a vocal melody.
steps: Find the triad on a single string so slides between tones are natural.
Pick the root.
Slide to the third — audible glide, don't lift the finger.
Slide to the fifth.
Slide to the root up an octave or back down to the root.
examples: pick root → slide to third → slide to fifth → slide to root up an octave (slide triad climb)
pick fifth → slide to third → slide to root (slide triad descent)
harmonicTarget:arpeggio135_noteTransitio.watchOut: Slides between triad tones often traverse multiple frets — the slide will audibly pass through notes that aren't in the triad. That's fine, but rest on the destination so the triad still reads.
harmonicTarget:arpeggio135_noteTransitio.deeperInsight: Pedal steel guitar is essentially triad slides for a living — the technique defines the instrument's sound.

harmonicTarget:arpeggio135_noteTransitio.interaction: Triad outlining with bend-to-target is the most expressive triad treatment on guitar — you reach each of the three chord tones by bending up from a lower fret. This is blues guitar turnaround territory, this is SRV on a ballad, this is the way you make a simple 1-3-5 triad feel vocal and emotional. Each bent arrival feels like a singer reaching a note.
steps: Pick a lower fret and bend up to the root.
Pick another lower fret and bend up to the third.
Pick another lower fret and bend up to the fifth.
Release the bends slowly for expressive effect.
Experiment with pre-bending (bending before the pick attack) for vocal landings.
examples: bend to root → bend to third → bend to fifth (all three triad tones reached via bends)
pick second, bend up to third → pick fourth, bend up to fifth (reaching triad tones via bends)
harmonicTarget:arpeggio135_noteTransitio.watchOut: Bends on triad tones must land exactly — any quarter-tone error and the triad doesn't feel like a triad anymore. Train the bends against a reference.
harmonicTarget:arpeggio135_noteTransitio.deeperInsight: This is how blues guitarists make a simple triad sound like a human voice — the bend to each target note creates a vocal quality that fretting directly can never achieve.

harmonicTarget:chordToneLanding_noteTran.interaction: Chord-tone landing demands harmonic precision; clean attack demands sonic precision. Together they force absolute accountability — every phrase ends on a target note, and that target arrives freshly picked with no smear, no hammer cushion, no slide fudge. This is the Steely Dan / studio-session discipline: the note is either right or it isn't, and you can hear both fret and pick at the moment of truth.
steps: Loop a slow two-chord vamp and let each bar's phrase end on a chord tone of that bar's chord — root, third, fifth, or seventh.
Pick the landing note with a clean downstroke, fretting hand planted a fraction ahead so the attack is pitch-first and noise-free.
Plan one beat ahead: say the target degree out loud before you play it, so the picking hand isn't guessing.
Once the landings are reliable, shorten the lead-in so you have to find the chord tone with less runway.
Finish each round by landing on the root of the tonic chord to cement the home feeling.
examples: second → third → fifth → third (landing on the third of chord one, clean pick)
fourth → fifth → sixth → root (landing on the root of chord two, cold downstroke)
harmonicTarget:chordToneLanding_noteTran.watchOut: The biggest trap is arriving at the target note a sixteenth late because you're still thinking about it — that late landing kills the clean-attack feel by turning it into a slur. If you catch yourself ghosting into the target, stop and reset the tempo slower.

harmonicTarget:chordToneLanding_noteTran.interaction: Legato wants flow; chord-tone landing wants commitment. The interesting tension is that the LANDING note can't be hammered — it has to ring clearly enough to register as a resolution — so you build flowing hammer/pull runs UP TO the target and then let the target sit. Think Allman Brothers or Jerry Garcia: liquid approach, then the chord tone lands with full voice.
steps: Set a slow vamp and identify a chord tone target one beat before the bar ends.
Lead into it with a two- or three-note legato approach: pick once, hammer or pull the rest, letting the final target sustain.
Feel the fretting-hand finger strike into the fret as the 'pick replacement' — no buzz, no dead note.
Alternate approaches: ascending hammer-on into the target, descending pull-off into the target.
End the round with a held chord-tone landing that rings for the full bar.
examples: second h→ third h→ fourth p→ third (pull-off lands on the third of chord one)
fifth p→ fourth p→ third h→ fifth (hammer-on lands on the fifth of chord two)
harmonicTarget:chordToneLanding_noteTran.watchOut: Pulled landings die quickly because the pull-off robs volume — if your target note can't be heard over the backing, use a harder pick attack on the lead note so the subsequent legato has enough energy, or land via hammer-on instead of pull-off.
harmonicTarget:chordToneLanding_noteTran.deeperInsight: Chord tones landed via legato feel inevitable rather than announced — the harmony arrives like a fact, not a decision.

harmonicTarget:chordToneLanding_noteTran.interaction: Sliding into chord tones is the secret sauce of desert blues, Tinariwen, and classic reggae lead — the target note has harmonic gravity, and the slide supplies the aching approach that says 'I had to reach for this.' The combo teaches you to hear chord tones as destinations worth an expressive journey, not just stop-off points on a scale.
steps: Pick a target chord tone a fret or two above your starting finger position.
Fret a lower note, pick it cleanly, then slide up into the target precisely on the downbeat of the chord change.
Keep picking-hand palm muting light so the slide sings rather than scrapes.
Alternate short slides (one fret) and long slides (three or four frets) into the same chord tone to hear the difference in drama.
Rest on the target for a full beat before moving on — let the chord tone prove it's the landing.
examples: second / third → fifth → third (one-fret slide into the third, held)
fourth / / seventh → root (long slide into the seventh of a dominant chord)
harmonicTarget:chordToneLanding_noteTran.watchOut: The most common failure here is sliding PAST the target because the fretting finger is traveling too fast — plant the target fret with your eyes before you start the slide, so your finger has somewhere definite to stop.

harmonicTarget:chordToneLanding_noteTran.interaction: Bending up to a chord tone is the most emotionally charged move on the guitar — it's the whole reason the instrument gets a lead voice in blues, soul, and country. The combo forces you to BEND IN TUNE, because the target isn't just 'the next scale note,' it's a structural harmony point that will sound wrong if you under- or over-bend. Think Albert King, Gilmour, Hendrix aiming for the fifth over a IV chord.
steps: Identify a chord tone you can reach with a whole-step bend from one scale note below.
Pre-hear the target pitch in your head — sing it if you can — before the fretting finger starts pushing.
Bend slowly on the beat, using the ring finger with index and middle reinforcing behind it for control.
Hold at the peak for a full count to prove the intonation.
Release back down and re-land on the target by picking it straight the second time to compare.
examples: fourth ↑(bend) fifth → third (whole-step bend into the fifth of the one chord)
sixth ↑(bend) seventh → root (bending into the flat seven of a dominant chord)
harmonicTarget:chordToneLanding_noteTran.watchOut: Under-bending is the big leak — a chord-tone target bent only ninety percent of the way sounds mournful in a bad way. Train with the drone on and verify every bend against it.

harmonicTarget:colorTone79_noteTransitio.interaction: Seventh-and-ninth color tones are the jazz and bossa language — they add sophistication but they also SOUND like mistakes if you're not committing to them. A clean attack on a ninth is the Steely Dan, Antônio Carlos Jobim move: the pick declares 'I meant that,' and the extension glows instead of wavering. No legato cushion, no slide alibi.
steps: Over a static chord drone, find the seventh, ninth, and eleventh of that chord on the fretboard.
Pick each color tone as a clean eighth-note attack on a strong beat, letting it ring for the whole beat.
Plant the fretting finger just ahead of the pick so the attack is pitch-first — no chirp.
Alternate chord tones (root/third/fifth) with color tones so the extensions feel like chosen peaks.
Finish with a deliberate ninth landing held through the bar change.
examples: root → ninth → third → seventh (color tones picked dead clean on beats two and four)
fifth → eleventh → ninth → third (rising extensions over one held chord)
harmonicTarget:colorTone79_noteTransitio.watchOut: Half-committed extensions read as out-of-key mistakes. If you feel yourself wincing at a ninth, it's usually because the pick attack was tentative — double the pick-hand force on color tones so they land with authority.

harmonicTarget:colorTone79_noteTransitio.interaction: Hammering and pulling into ninths and elevenths is the George Benson / Wes Montgomery trick — extensions glide in as though the hand discovered them, so the sophistication never sounds studied. The challenge: legato drops volume, and color tones NEED volume to register as intentional rather than accidental. You're hunting for a liquid feel that still sells the harmonic statement.
steps: Identify a scale note near a color tone (seventh, ninth, or eleventh) where a one- or two-fret hammer/pull can reach it.
Pick the non-color neighbor loudly, then hammer firmly into the extension so it rings with authority.
Practice the reverse: start on the color tone and pull off INTO the chord tone so the ninth feels like a moment of tension resolving.
Cycle through seventh, ninth, and eleventh, spending four bars on each to let the ear register each flavor.
Finish every phrase on a plain chord tone so the extensions feel like color, not destination.
examples: root h→ ninth p→ root (hammer into the ninth, pull back to root)
fifth h→ seventh h→ ninth (stacked legato climb into extensions)
harmonicTarget:colorTone79_noteTransitio.watchOut: Hammering onto an extension with weak finger force makes it a ghost note that the listener reads as a blurred chord tone — you need a hard hammer on color tones specifically, even harder than on triad notes.

harmonicTarget:colorTone79_noteTransitio.interaction: Slide plus color tones is the pedal-steel-and-Khruangbin language — the slide turns every extension into a sigh. The ninth wants to resolve, the eleventh wants to hang, and sliding INTO either one makes the harmonic tension audible in the physical motion. This is where 'pretty' and 'interesting' finally agree.
steps: Find the ninth of a chord two to three frets above a chord tone.
Pick the chord tone, slide up into the ninth slowly, and let it ring — the slide rate should match the feel of the tempo.
Try sliding DOWN into the seventh from above (a classic bluesy descent).
Pair a slide-into-eleventh with a held chord tone below — drone against color for color-music contrast.
Finish by sliding from a ninth back down into the root to show how gravity still wins.
examples: third / / ninth → root (slide up a whole step into the ninth)
root / seventh → third (short downward slide into the flat seventh)
harmonicTarget:colorTone79_noteTransitio.watchOut: Landing a slide ON a color tone requires more precision than landing on a chord tone because there's no safety net — if the slide stops a quarter-fret short, the extension sounds dissonant instead of lush. Mark the target fret visually before starting.

harmonicTarget:colorTone79_noteTransitio.interaction: Bending up to a ninth or an eleventh is the high art of expressive lead playing — it's how Derek Trucks, David Gilmour, and Jeff Beck conjure the sigh that turns a scale into a sentence. The bend must land BOTH in pitch and in harmonic taste; a slightly flat ninth sounds like a mistake, a slightly sharp ninth sounds like crying. Perfect is one single pitch, and you have to hear it before you bend.
steps: Drone the chord quality while you locate the ninth two frets above a note you can bend from.
Pre-sing the pitch of the target color tone.
Bend slowly and hold at the apex — compare against the droned chord to verify intonation.
Release back to the starting fret and re-bend three times to confirm muscle memory.
Resolve by releasing the bend and landing on a plain chord tone.
examples: root ↑(bend whole step) ninth → fifth (bend into the ninth, resolve to the fifth)
fifth ↑(bend half step) flat-sixth → fifth (microbend adding temporary color)
harmonicTarget:colorTone79_noteTransitio.watchOut: Color tones are the one place where under-bending is absolutely fatal — a ninth a few cents flat sounds worse than a plain wrong note. If you can't reach the target pitch reliably, move down the neck where the strings are less stiff.

harmonicTarget:scaleShape_vibrato:straig.interaction: Pure scale running with zero vibrato is the severest honesty test on the guitar — Bach, classical, flamenco rest position. You can't hide behind finger wobble, so every note's pitch, duration, and volume is naked. It teaches what vibrato actually adds by removing it entirely and forcing you to feel the difference.
steps: Choose one scale pattern and loop it slowly with a drone going.
Plant each fretting finger dead still — no wrist tremor, no accidental vibrato from nerves.
Hold every note for its full beat and confirm the pitch against the drone.
Listen for volume consistency: straight tones expose volume irregularities from the picking hand.
Only after five clean passes, speed up slightly.
examples: root → second → third → fourth (ascending tetrachord, held dead-straight)
fifth → fourth → third → second → root (descending run, no wobble)
harmonicTarget:scaleShape_vibrato:straig.watchOut: Nervous vibrato sneaks in on held notes because the hand wants to 'do something.' If you catch it, freeze the entire hand and count out loud — the counting distracts the micro-motor impulse.

harmonicTarget:scaleShape_vibrato:slow.interaction: Slow wide vibrato over a scale run is the B.B. King / Gilmour voice — it's not decoration, it's how the instrument actually SINGS. The scale provides the melodic material and the vibrato makes each sustained note feel alive. You're not vibrating every note, you're choosing which ones deserve breath.
steps: Loop a slow scale run and pick ONE note per phrase to sustain.
On that note, swing the fretting finger in a wide slow wrist-driven arc — two to three cycles per second.
Keep the other notes in the scale short and straight so the vibrato note stands out as the sung one.
Alternate which scale degree gets the vibrato — try the third, the sixth, the seventh.
Finish by sustaining the root with slow vibrato as the resolve.
examples: root → third → fifth~ (slow vibrato only on the fifth, held four counts)
second → fourth → sixth~ (slow vibrato on the sixth, the aching color note)
harmonicTarget:scaleShape_vibrato:slow.watchOut: Slow vibrato driven from the fingers alone sounds tight and weak — the motion MUST come from the wrist or forearm rotation. If your tone sounds like a shiver instead of a sigh, the wrist is locked.

harmonicTarget:scaleShape_vibrato:fast.interaction: Fast narrow vibrato over free scale running is the classical / flamenco shimmer — Paco de Lucía, Julian Bream. The scale wants motion, the vibrato adds a shimmer on top of that motion, and together they create a kind of forward-leaning intensity that slow vibrato can't match. It's a different emotional register entirely.
steps: Choose a scale shape and run it at medium tempo.
On any note held longer than a beat, apply fast narrow vibrato — five to seven cycles per second, small excursion.
Keep the vibrato width tight: it's a shimmer, not a bend.
Vary WHICH notes get the shimmer — some runs with vibrato on every held note, some with only one.
Anchor the final note with sustained fast vibrato for three full beats.
examples: root → second → third ≈ (fast vibrato on the held third)
fifth → sixth → seventh ≈ (quivering leading tone pulling toward the root)
harmonicTarget:scaleShape_vibrato:fast.watchOut: Fast vibrato easily turns into an uncontrolled tremor, which listeners hear as nervousness rather than intensity. Keep the motion coming from a specific joint (wrist or finger) and count the cycles — six per second is right, ten per second is panic.

harmonicTarget:scaleShape_vibrato:lateEn.interaction: Late-entry vibrato over a scale run is the most vocal move on the instrument — it mirrors exactly what a great singer does, starting each sustained note pure and letting the vibrato bloom in only after the pitch has settled. The combo trains your ear to value note STABILITY as the foundation from which expression grows, not a thing you skip past on the way to decoration.
steps: Loop a scale and pick any one note per phrase to sustain.
Hold that note completely still for the first half of its duration.
Let vibrato enter gradually in the second half, starting small and widening.
Use a drone so you can hear the bloom against a reference pitch.
Try the same note with immediate vibrato vs. late-entry — feel the difference in emotional color.
examples: root → third → fifth__~ (straight held fifth, vibrato blooming in at the end)
seventh → sixth → root__~ (final root sung then shimmered)
harmonicTarget:scaleShape_vibrato:lateEn.watchOut: Entering the vibrato TOO EARLY is the classic failure — you think you waited but the bloom started on beat two instead of beat three. Count the rhythm out loud: 'one, two, three-AND-a-shimmer.'
harmonicTarget:scaleShape_vibrato:lateEn.deeperInsight: Late-entry vibrato is the guitar equivalent of confidence — the player doesn't NEED to decorate the note immediately, so they don't.

harmonicTarget:arpeggio135_vibrato:strai.interaction: Root-third-fifth arpeggios with zero vibrato is pure classical etude territory — Bach, Segovia, fundamentals. The triad tells you exactly what the harmony is, the straightness forces you to own the tuning of EVERY chord tone, and together they build a kind of skeletal honesty that shows up any bad intonation in the fretting hand.
steps: Pick a scale and outline the tonic triad: root, third, fifth, and back to root on top.
Play each note dead straight with the drone running, holding each for its full count.
Verify every note against the drone — especially the third, which is the color tone of the triad.
Once clean, try the inversions: third-fifth-root, fifth-root-third.
Finish on a held root octave with no vibrato at all.
examples: root → third → fifth → root (ascending triad, straight)
fifth → third → root (descending close of triad, no shimmer)
harmonicTarget:arpeggio135_vibrato:strai.watchOut: Straight triads expose a minor third vs. major third mismatch instantly if your finger is a fret off — play the third against the drone and pause to confirm quality every single time.

harmonicTarget:arpeggio135_vibrato:slow.interaction: Triad arpeggios with slow vibrato on the landing note is the classic Gilmour 'Great Gig in the Sky' move — the chord is stated via the arpeggio, then the last note blooms into a slow wide sigh that holds the whole room still. It's how you turn a technical exercise into a ballad.
steps: Pick a triad shape and arpeggiate it slowly: root, third, fifth, and beyond to the octave if you can reach it.
The last note of the arpeggio gets long sustain and slow vibrato — wrist-driven, two to three cycles per second.
The notes BEFORE the landing should be straight and even so the vibrato surprise lands with contrast.
Try landing the slow vibrato on different triad tones — root, then third, then fifth — and notice how the emotion changes.
Finish with an octave root held and swung slowly for eight counts.
examples: root → third → fifth~ (slow vibrato bloom on the fifth, four-count hold)
fifth → third → root~ (descending arpeggio resolving into shimmering root)
harmonicTarget:arpeggio135_vibrato:slow.watchOut: If the slow vibrato comes in immediately with the target note, the contrast with the straight approach notes is lost. Let the target sound dead still for a full beat before you begin to wobble.

harmonicTarget:arpeggio135_vibrato:fast.interaction: Triad arpeggios with fast narrow vibrato is flamenco and classical guitar territory — Paco de Lucía, Sabicas. The arpeggio states the harmony with crystal clarity, and the fast shimmer on held notes feels like the string is humming with energy rather than being strummed. It's intensity without volume.
steps: Arpeggiate the tonic triad at a medium tempo with clean alternate picking.
On each longer note — typically the top or bottom of the arpeggio — apply narrow fast vibrato, five to seven cycles per second.
Keep the vibrato excursion small; this is shimmer, not bend.
Try the arpeggio as root-third-fifth-third-root with fast vibrato only on the peak fifth.
Finish with fast vibrato on the octave root held for four counts.
examples: root → third → fifth ≈ → third → root (fast vibrato on the held fifth)
fifth → root → third ≈ (fast vibrato on the final third)
harmonicTarget:arpeggio135_vibrato:fast.watchOut: Fast vibrato on a minor third can sound whiny if the rate is uneven — lock the rate to your sense of internal pulse and count 'one-two-one-two' at six cycles per second.

harmonicTarget:arpeggio135_vibrato:lateE.interaction: Triads stated cleanly with vibrato blooming in only at the end of the landing note — this is the vocalist's arpeggio. Think opera arias, Nina Simone, Jeff Buckley. The harmony arrives with perfect clarity, and only THEN does the expression decide to show up. The combo trains you to trust that pure pitch alone carries weight.
steps: Arpeggiate the tonic triad with completely straight, clean notes.
Hold the final note of the arpeggio dead still for at least half a beat.
Let the vibrato enter gradually — starting narrow and small, widening as it grows.
Compare with an identical arpeggio where the vibrato starts immediately — the late entry should feel more 'said,' less 'sung at.'
Finish on the octave root with the bloom reaching full width by beat four.
examples: root → third → fifth → octave-root__~ (straight arpeggio, vibrato blooming late on the octave)
third → root → fifth__~ (triad inversion resolving into a slowly blooming fifth)
harmonicTarget:arpeggio135_vibrato:lateE.watchOut: The delay must be rhythmically counted, not felt — vague 'eventually wobble' sounds indecisive. Decide exactly where the vibrato enters (beat three, beat four) and count it in every time.

harmonicTarget:chordToneLanding_vibrato:.interaction: Landing every phrase on a chord tone with NO vibrato is the soul-session discipline — Steve Cropper at Stax, Cornell Dupree on any session. The landing note is the whole harmonic statement, and straight sustain says 'I'm confident this is the right note and I don't need to decorate it to prove it.' Vibrato would actually dilute the conviction.
steps: Start a vamp with two chord changes and plan chord-tone targets for each bar.
Lead into each target with scale notes, then land on the chord tone and hold it STRAIGHT through the bar.
Verify each landing against a drone or the backing chord.
Rotate targets so you land on root, third, fifth, and seventh over the same chord.
Finish with a straight root-of-tonic held through two full bars.
examples: second → third → fourth → third__ (held third of chord one, straight)
sixth → fifth → fourth → root__ (held root of chord two, no shimmer)
harmonicTarget:chordToneLanding_vibrato:.watchOut: Nervous vibrato sneaks in on long-held landings because silence feels uncomfortable. Freeze the fretting finger and let the note ring for its entire duration — resist the urge to 'finish' it with a wobble.

harmonicTarget:chordToneLanding_vibrato:.interaction: This is the blues ballad vocabulary — B.B. King, Albert King, Gilmour on 'Comfortably Numb.' The chord tone provides the harmonic certainty, the slow vibrato provides the emotional weight, and together they form the single most expressive move in rock/blues lead playing. You're learning how to put one note in the right place and make the room hold its breath.
steps: Loop a slow chord vamp and pre-plan one chord-tone target per bar.
Approach the target with scale notes and land on it precisely on the chord change.
Apply wrist-driven slow vibrato at two to three cycles per second, wide and deliberate.
Hold the note for at least two beats with vibrato before moving on.
Try different chord tones — root, third, fifth — and listen to how the slow vibrato changes the color of each.
examples: fourth → fifth → third~ (wide slow vibrato on the third of the one chord)
second → flat-seventh~ (slow bloom on the flat seventh of a dominant chord)
harmonicTarget:chordToneLanding_vibrato:.watchOut: Slow vibrato driven from the finger alone is weak and uneven. It MUST come from the wrist or forearm — the whole hand should rock, not just one digit.

harmonicTarget:chordToneLanding_vibrato:.interaction: Fast vibrato on chord-tone landings is the classical-meets-rock, Yngwie Malmsteen / Randy Rhoads aesthetic — the harmony is stated with complete clarity and the shimmer adds intensity rather than sadness. It's assertive singing instead of sighing singing. The combo teaches the difference between emotional vibrato and ENERGETIC vibrato.
steps: Loop a vamp and target a chord tone per bar.
On landing, apply narrow fast vibrato — five to seven cycles per second, small excursion.
Keep the motion localized in the fingertip or wrist joint — don't let the shimmer drift wide.
Compare against a slow-vibrato version of the same phrase to feel the emotional difference.
End the round with a fast-vibrato landing on the root of the tonic, held four counts.
examples: third → fifth → seventh ≈ (fast shimmer on the seventh of a dominant chord)
sixth → fifth → third ≈ (fast vibrato on the major third landing)
harmonicTarget:chordToneLanding_vibrato:.watchOut: Fast vibrato turns nervous fast — if the cycle rate starts drifting upward you've lost control. Lock to a metronome at 120 bpm and count two sixteenth-note cycles per beat to train consistency.

harmonicTarget:chordToneLanding_vibrato:.interaction: Landing on a chord tone, letting it sit dead still, then letting vibrato bloom in late is the singer's approach to instrumental phrasing — think Nina Simone, Sinatra, Jeff Buckley. It says 'the harmony is what matters first; the feeling is what matters second.' No rush to emote. This combo rewires your sense of WHEN expression is supposed to happen.
steps: Set a vamp and target a chord tone on the bar change.
Land the target, pick cleanly, and hold the note dead still for the first full beat.
On beat two or three of the sustain, let vibrato bloom in — starting narrow, widening.
Play the same phrase twice back-to-back: once with immediate vibrato, once with late entry. Feel the difference.
Finish with a chord-tone landing where the vibrato enters only on the last beat of the measure.
examples: second → third → fifth__~ (held fifth, vibrato entering on beat three)
sixth → seventh → root__~ (tonic root landing, slow bloom on beat four)
harmonicTarget:chordToneLanding_vibrato:.watchOut: Decide exactly when the bloom starts before you play — ambiguous entry reads as hesitation, not patience. Count the beats out loud the first few passes.

harmonicTarget:colorTone79_vibrato:strai.interaction: Straight ninths and elevenths is the jazz fusion / Pat Metheny approach — Metheny plays a held ninth completely dead still and it sounds like a statement of intent. The extension is already doing the emotional work; vibrato would be painting the Mona Lisa. Straightness on color tones is actually the hardest committed move because you have nowhere to hide from intonation.
steps: Drone a chord and locate its ninth, eleventh, and seventh.
Pick each extension cleanly and hold it completely straight for a full count.
Verify intonation against the drone — color tones make flatness or sharpness obvious.
Cycle through seventh, ninth, eleventh at slow tempo, building ear familiarity with each.
Finish by holding a ninth dead still for four full counts.
examples: third → fifth → ninth__ (held ninth, no vibrato)
root → seventh__ (flat seventh held dead straight over a dominant chord)
harmonicTarget:colorTone79_vibrato:strai.watchOut: Nervous vibrato on a held extension is more noticeable than on a chord tone — the ear already senses tension in the color tone and any wobble reads as 'the player isn't sure.' Freeze the hand.

harmonicTarget:colorTone79_vibrato:slow.interaction: Slow wide vibrato on a ninth or eleventh is the Khruangbin / BALTHVS / cosmic soul sound — the extension is already a sigh, and the slow wobble is the exhale after the sigh. Used sparingly, this is the most emotionally charged single-note move available on a guitar, because you're taking the harmonically sophisticated note and giving it the vocal treatment on top.
steps: Drone a chord and find its ninth or eleventh.
Pick the extension cleanly, let it sit straight for a beat, then apply wide slow vibrato from the wrist.
Keep the vibrato WIDE but SLOW — think two cycles per second, not six.
Try the same thing on the seventh and compare — the seventh with slow vibrato tends to sound bluesier, the ninth tends to sound dreamier.
Finish with a slow-vibrated ninth held for eight beats.
examples: fifth → ninth~ (slow wide vibrato on the ninth)
third → eleventh~ (eleventh shimmering slow, held four counts)
harmonicTarget:colorTone79_vibrato:slow.watchOut: Color tones with slow vibrato can drift out of tune because the wide swing takes you past the target; keep the center of the oscillation nailed to the correct pitch and return there on the downbeat.

harmonicTarget:colorTone79_vibrato:fast.interaction: Fast narrow vibrato on ninths and elevenths is the flamenco jazz sound — Al Di Meola / Paco / John McLaughlin. The extension provides the harmonic sophistication, the fast shimmer provides the Mediterranean intensity, and together they feel like the note is lit from within. It's the exact opposite of lazy playing.
steps: Drone a chord and locate the ninth.
Pick it and apply fast narrow vibrato, five to seven cycles per second, small excursion.
Verify the center pitch is accurate — fast vibrato can mask flat intonation.
Alternate seventh, ninth, and eleventh, applying fast vibrato to each.
Finish with a fast-vibrated eleventh held for four counts.
examples: root → third → ninth ≈ (fast narrow vibrato on the ninth)
fifth → seventh ≈ (fast shimmer on the dominant seventh)
harmonicTarget:colorTone79_vibrato:fast.watchOut: Fast vibrato on extensions is where tremor masquerades as intensity. Count cycles — six per second is right. If it feels out of control, it is.

harmonicTarget:colorTone79_vibrato:lateE.interaction: Extension held straight first, then vibrato blooming in late is the Jeff Beck / Bill Frisell vocabulary — the color tone arrives as a pure statement and only becomes 'expressive' once it's proven it belongs. This is the most adult move in the whole combo family: a ninth that waits to be emoted is a ninth that knows it's right.
steps: Pick a ninth or eleventh over a droned chord.
Hold it completely straight for the first beat — no motion in the fretting finger.
Let vibrato bloom in starting on beat two, narrow and widening.
Try the same note with immediate vibrato and feel how late entry sounds more confident.
Finish with a late-blooming ninth over a full four-beat measure.
examples: third → fifth → ninth__~ (ninth dead still, blooming on beat three)
fifth → eleventh__~ (eleventh held straight then shimmered late)
harmonicTarget:colorTone79_vibrato:lateE.watchOut: The pre-vibrato silence must be DELIBERATE, not just slow reaction. Decide in advance exactly which beat the bloom starts on and count to that beat every time.

harmonicTarget:scaleShape_onset:hardAtta.interaction: Free scale running with hard vocal attack is the rock and pop default — Ann Wilson, Freddie Mercury, Aretha. Every note starts full-voiced and cleanly fretted on the pitch, no air, no scoop. The scale gives you melodic freedom, the hard attack makes every note matter. Combined, it's the most assertive possible phrasing: 'here I am, here is the note, here we go.'
steps: Choose a scale and a comfortable pitch range and stand grounded with feet shoulder-width apart.
Palm on the sternum — feel the chest resonance on each attack.
Sing ascending and descending scale phrases, each note initiated with a clean pitch-and-volume onset — no slide up, no breath lead.
Keep the vowel open ('ah' or 'oh') so the attack isn't choked by consonants.
Finish with a sustained note at full volume, attacked clean, held for a full breath.
examples: root → third → fifth → third (each note hard-attacked on the beat)
sixth → fifth → fourth → third (descending scale phrase, every note fresh)
harmonicTarget:scaleShape_onset:hardAtta.watchOut: Hard attack slips into glottal shock — a clicky 'uh' before each note — if you're gripping the throat. The attack should come from chest compression, not throat closure. If you hear the click, relax the jaw and re-attack with palm pressure on the sternum guiding you.

harmonicTarget:scaleShape_onset:soft.interaction: Free scale running with soft classical-default onset is the choral / art song approach — each note starts with breath just ahead of pitch, so the tone blooms in rather than arriving with a punch. The scale is your material, the soft onset is your touch. This is how legato singing begins: the note rises out of breath rather than striking the air.
steps: Stand relaxed, palm resting in front of the mouth to feel breath.
Before each note, release a small puff of breath, then let the pitch rise into the center of that breath.
Sing the scale with every note having this breath-to-pitch gradient.
Keep the vowel open and the soft palate lifted — think 'yawn space' in the back of the throat.
Finish with a soft-onset held root that takes a full second to reach peak volume.
examples: root → second → third (each note rising softly out of breath)
fifth → fourth → third → root (descending scale, each note breath-led)
harmonicTarget:scaleShape_onset:soft.watchOut: Soft onset drifts into flat pitch — the breath gets there before the pitch does, and the pitch can arrive late and low. Pre-hear the exact pitch before releasing any breath so the tone blooms on the correct note, not under it.

harmonicTarget:scaleShape_onset:breathy.interaction: Breathy onsets over free scale runs is Norah Jones, Billie Eilish, Beth Gibbons territory — whispered pitch that resolves into tone. The scale provides the notes, the breathy onset provides the intimacy, and the combination creates a sound that feels like someone is telling you a secret. Used sparingly, it's devastating.
steps: Stand relaxed with the palm close to the lips.
Start each note with more air than tone — an audible whisper of pitch — and let the tone resolve in over half a second.
Keep the breath flowing continuously between notes so the phrase doesn't stop and start.
Sing ascending and descending scale passages with every note breath-led.
Finish with a breathy-onset held note that slowly firms up into full voice over two counts.
examples: root → third → fifth (each note whispered in before firming)
seventh → sixth → fifth (descending scale, breathy throughout)
harmonicTarget:scaleShape_onset:breathy.watchOut: Breathy singing burns through breath fast and leaves you gasping by the end of the phrase. Take deeper breaths than you think you need, and pace: a breathy onset doesn't have to last the whole note — let the tone firm up so you're not leaking air forever.

harmonicTarget:scaleShape_onset:slideIn.interaction: Slide-in onsets over free scale runs is the blues / R&B / country vocabulary — Otis Redding, Dolly Parton, Amy Winehouse. Instead of arriving at the pitch, you approach it from below and slide up into it. The scale gives you notes, the slides turn the spaces between them into emotion. It's how singers make a major scale sound like it's been lived in.
steps: Pick a scale and a slow tempo and stand with palm on sternum.
Start each note a half step or whole step BELOW the target and slide up into the scale degree, landing on the beat.
Keep the slide audible but brief — this is a scoop, not a glissando.
Vary which notes get the slide — try slides into every scale degree and notice which ones feel most emotional (usually thirds and sixths).
Finish with a slow slide up into the root and hold it.
examples: (scoop up to) third → fifth → third (third scooped into, others clean)
(scoop up to) sixth → fifth → third (classic blues turn of phrase)
harmonicTarget:scaleShape_onset:slideIn.watchOut: Slides arriving late is the main trap — the target pitch must LAND on the beat, not after it. Start the slide ahead of the beat so the landing is rhythmically exact.

harmonicTarget:arpeggio135_onset:hardAtt.interaction: Triad arpeggios with hard onset is rock-anthem Freddie / Steven Tyler territory — every chord tone punched clean, full voice, no apology. The triad states the harmony and the hard attack states the attitude. This is how you sing a big hook or a gospel climax: every note a declaration.
steps: Stand grounded, palm on sternum, open vowel.
Outline the triad — root, third, fifth, back to root — with each note hard-attacked.
Feel the chest compression drive each onset from the diaphragm.
Alternate ascending and descending arpeggios, keeping the full-voice energy.
Finish by hard-attacking the octave root and holding it for a full breath.
examples: root → third → fifth → octave-root (each note punched clean)
fifth → third → root (descending triad, all full voice)
harmonicTarget:arpeggio135_onset:hardAtt.watchOut: Hard attack on arpeggios can skid into glottal shock on every single note because you're attacking three in a row. Keep the breath FLOWING underneath — the attack is chest, not throat. Air continues even between notes.

harmonicTarget:arpeggio135_onset:soft.interaction: Triad arpeggios with soft classical onset is lieder / choral tradition — Schubert, Fauré, Eva Cassidy. The triad is your architecture, the soft onset makes each pillar bloom in like it grew out of the breath. It's the opposite of punching the harmony; it's letting the harmony emerge. The combo teaches you to feel a chord as a sequence of breath-born pitches.
steps: Stand relaxed, palm in front of mouth to feel the breath lead.
Arpeggiate the tonic triad slowly with each note starting as breath and blooming into tone.
Keep the breath flowing continuously across all three notes.
Try different inversions — starting from the third, from the fifth — with the same soft-onset bloom on each note.
Finish with a softly-onset octave root held through a full breath.
examples: root → third → fifth (each note rising out of breath gently)
third → fifth → octave-root (ascending to octave with bloom onsets)
harmonicTarget:arpeggio135_onset:soft.watchOut: Soft-onset arpeggios drift flat on the third because the third is where singers most often let pitch slide in from below. Pre-hear the third exactly before releasing breath — better to front-load the pitch than back-load it.

harmonicTarget:arpeggio135_onset:breathy.interaction: Breathy arpeggios is the lullaby / indie-folk voice — Feist, Sufjan Stevens, Jeff Buckley in quiet moments. The triad outlines the chord structure, the whispered onsets make it intimate. The combination feels like you're humming a chord to yourself rather than singing one out. It's tiny, private, and deeply effective in the right context.
steps: Stand relaxed with palm close to mouth.
Arpeggiate the triad with each note starting as more air than tone.
Let the whisper resolve into full pitch over half a count per note.
Keep breath flowing continuously between notes — don't stop the air.
Finish with a breathy-onset octave root that slowly firms into quiet full voice.
examples: root → third → fifth (each note whispered in)
fifth → third → root → lower-octave-root (descending triad in whisper)
harmonicTarget:arpeggio135_onset:breathy.watchOut: Breathy arpeggios burn breath fast — by the third note of an ascending triad you can be out of air. Take twice the breath you think you need and don't whisper the whole duration; let each note firm up so you're not leaking air continuously.

harmonicTarget:arpeggio135_onset:slideIn.interaction: Triad arpeggios with slide-in onsets is the gospel / soul vocabulary — Aretha, Stevie Wonder, D'Angelo. Each chord tone is approached from below via a scoop, so the triad is both stated AND emoted at every note. The arpeggio gives structure, the slides give feeling, and the whole thing sounds like the harmony is being pulled out of the air rather than simply vocalized.
steps: Stand grounded, palm on sternum.
Arpeggiate the tonic triad with each note preceded by a half-step-below slide.
The slide must be brief and the pitch must LAND exactly on the beat.
Alternate: sometimes slide into only the third, sometimes into every triad note.
Finish with a slow slide up into the octave root held for two counts.
examples: (slide) root → (slide) third → (slide) fifth (each triad note scooped)
root → (slide) third → fifth (selective scoop on the third only)
harmonicTarget:arpeggio135_onset:slideIn.watchOut: Scooping every note makes the triad sound slurry and unclear. If you want the harmony to still READ as a triad, limit scoops to one or two of the three notes — usually the third or the top note — and sing the others clean.

harmonicTarget:chordToneLanding_onset:ha.interaction: Hard-attacking chord-tone landings over changes is the Tower of Power / Chaka Khan / Earth Wind & Fire discipline — every phrase ends on a chord tone of the new chord, punched clean. The harmony lands with both rhythmic force AND harmonic rightness, which is why good funk and soul phrasing feels so decisive. Nothing is left to chance; nothing arrives quietly.
steps: Set a two-chord vamp and plan a chord-tone target for each bar.
Lead into each target with a short melodic phrase and land on the chord tone exactly on the downbeat, hard-attacked.
Feel the chest compression drive the landing note from the diaphragm.
Rotate through different chord tones on the same chord — root, third, fifth — and hit each with equal force.
Finish with a hard-attacked root-of-tonic held for a full breath.
