examples: second → third → fourth → third (hard-attacked third of chord one)
sixth → fifth → fourth → root (hard-attacked root of chord two)
harmonicTarget:chordToneLanding_onset:ha.watchOut: The hard attack must hit exactly on the downbeat of the chord change — a fraction late and the landing sounds like it's chasing the harmony instead of nailing it. Count the bar change out loud in rehearsal until the landing is automatic.

harmonicTarget:chordToneLanding_onset:so.interaction: Soft onsets landing on chord tones over changes is the jazz ballad / bossa vocabulary — João Gilberto, Astrud Gilberto, Diana Krall. Each phrase ends on the harmonically correct note, but the arrival is a bloom, not a punch. The combo teaches the rare balance of harmonic discipline and emotional softness — you KNOW where you're going and you get there like a whisper.
steps: Set a slow two-chord vamp with long bar lengths.
Approach each chord-tone target with flowing scale notes.
Let the landing note start as breath and bloom into tone over the first half-beat of sustain.
Verify each landing against the backing chord — soft onsets can hide flat pitch.
Finish with a softly-landed root of tonic held for a full breath.
examples: third → fourth → fifth (softly-onset third of the chord, held)
second → root (flowing descent with a softly-blooming root landing)
harmonicTarget:chordToneLanding_onset:so.watchOut: Soft onset on a chord-tone landing drifts flat because the bloom takes time and the pitch arrives late. Pre-hear the exact pitch before you release breath so the tone is centered from the moment it becomes audible.

harmonicTarget:chordToneLanding_onset:br.interaction: Breathy chord-tone landings is the Norah Jones / Nina Simone / Madeleine Peyroux zone — the harmony lands precisely, but the arrival is whispered. Every phrase ends on a structural chord note, and that note is sung with more air than tone, so the listener feels the harmony settling into place rather than being announced. It's sophisticated intimacy.
steps: Set a slow vamp and target a chord tone on each bar change.
Approach the target, then whisper the landing with deliberate breath flow.
Hold the breathy landing for a full beat, letting it slowly firm into tone.
Try landing on the seventh for maximum tension — the combination of color tone plus whisper is hypnotic.
Finish with a breathy landing on the tonic root that takes two counts to reach full voice.
examples: second → third → fifth (breathy landing on the fifth of the chord)
sixth → flat-seventh (whispered chord-tone landing on the dominant seventh)
harmonicTarget:chordToneLanding_onset:br.watchOut: Breathy landings over changing chords go flat fast because the air volume is high and the pitch support is low. Maintain strong diaphragm pressure UNDER the whisper so the pitch holds steady.

harmonicTarget:chordToneLanding_onset:sl.interaction: Sliding into chord-tone landings is the classic soul / R&B / blues phrasing — Otis Redding, Sam Cooke, Al Green. You approach the target harmony from a half-step below and scoop up into it on the bar change. The harmony is still right but the arrival has history to it — the note feels like it was reached for rather than simply placed. This is the single most soulful move in vocal phrasing.
steps: Set a vamp with distinct chord changes and plan chord-tone targets for each bar.
Begin each target note from a half step or whole step below and slide up into it.
The slide must land the target pitch exactly on the bar downbeat.
Try sliding into the third (sweet and sad), the fifth (strong and bright), and the seventh (pained and pleading) and feel the different flavors.
Finish with a slow slide up into the tonic root held for two counts.
examples: second → third → (slide) fifth (scoop up into the fifth of the chord)
(slide) flat-third → root (blues scoop into a minor third over the tonic)
harmonicTarget:chordToneLanding_onset:sl.watchOut: The slide must RESOLVE the target on the downbeat. If the scoop is still traveling when the beat arrives, the harmony feels late and the phrase drags. Start the slide ahead of the beat.

harmonicTarget:colorTone79_onset:hardAtt.interaction: Hard-attacking color tones over changes is rare and powerful — think Rufus Wainwright, Jacob Collier, Stevie Wonder on the outro of 'Living for the City.' You're punching a ninth or eleventh with full voice on the downbeat, and the listener's ear has to both hear the hit AND accept the sophisticated harmony. Done right, it's electrifying; done wrong, it sounds like a missed pitch. Commit or don't try.
steps: Drone a chord and practice the ninth, eleventh, and seventh in isolation until they feel consonant.
Sing phrases that END on a color tone, hard-attacking the extension on the downbeat.
Pre-hear the target pitch before the onset — you cannot afford to chase the pitch on a hard-attacked extension.
Verify with the drone that each landing is centered exactly on the correct pitch.
Finish with a hard-attacked ninth held for a full breath.
examples: root → third → ninth (hard-attacked ninth on the downbeat)
fifth → seventh (punched flat seventh of a dominant chord)
harmonicTarget:colorTone79_onset:hardAtt.watchOut: Half-commitment is lethal here. A tentative hard attack on a ninth sounds worse than a confident wrong note. If you're not SURE of the pitch, sing a soft-onset version until the pitch is in your ear, then switch to hard attack.

harmonicTarget:colorTone79_onset:soft.interaction: Soft-onset color tones is the art-song / modern jazz ballad world — Ella Fitzgerald's ballad phrasing, Dianne Reeves, Gregory Porter in quiet moments. The extension blooms into place and the sophistication is delivered gently, so the listener's ear has time to catch up with the harmony. This is the safest way to introduce complex harmony into a vocal phrase without shocking the listener.
steps: Drone the chord and sing a phrase that ends on a ninth or eleventh.
Let the landing color tone start as breath and bloom into full tone over the first beat.
Verify pitch against the drone — soft onsets hide flatness.
Alternate ninth, eleventh, and seventh landings and feel the different colors.
Finish with a softly-blooming ninth held for four counts.
examples: third → fifth → ninth (soft bloom into the ninth)
root → fourth → eleventh (soft eleventh landing)
harmonicTarget:colorTone79_onset:soft.watchOut: Color tones pitched flat because of soft-onset drift sound dissonant in a bad way. Pre-hearing the exact pitch is absolutely required — the bloom must arrive centered, not sliding up from below.

harmonicTarget:colorTone79_onset:breathy.interaction: Breathy color-tone landings is the Fiona Apple / Björk / Rickie Lee Jones territory — whispered extensions over shifting harmony. The color tone provides the harmonic surprise, the breathiness provides the emotional vulnerability, and together they make every landing feel like a confession. It's one of the most distinctive sounds in contemporary singing and it takes serious pitch control.
steps: Drone a chord and sing a soft phrase that ends on a ninth or eleventh.
Whisper the landing color tone — more air than tone — and let it firm up slowly.
Support the whisper with strong diaphragm pressure to keep pitch centered.
Alternate extensions so you build familiarity with each color.
Finish with a breathy ninth landing held for four counts.
examples: root → third → ninth (whispered ninth landing)
fifth → eleventh (breathy eleventh, held)
harmonicTarget:colorTone79_onset:breathy.watchOut: Breathy color tones burn breath fast AND are most prone to drifting flat. Take a bigger breath than you think you need and nail the pitch center before you start the whisper.

harmonicTarget:colorTone79_onset:slideIn.interaction: Sliding into color tones is the gospel-jazz vocabulary — think Lalah Hathaway, Stevie Wonder scooping into a ninth, Al Jarreau reaching for an eleventh. The scoop adds emotional pull, the extension adds harmonic sophistication, and the combination sounds like somebody stretching for the most beautiful possible version of a phrase's ending. It's maximum expression with maximum vocabulary.
steps: Drone a chord and find the ninth or eleventh above a comfortable starting pitch.
Start a whole step below the extension and scoop up into it, landing on the beat.
Verify the target pitch with the drone — slides onto color tones are easy to under- or over-shoot.
Alternate scooping into the ninth and the eleventh and feel the different emotional weights.
Finish with a slow slide up into a ninth held for two full counts.
examples: root → third → (slide) ninth (scooping up a whole step into the ninth)
fifth → (slide) eleventh (wide slide into the eleventh of a chord)
harmonicTarget:colorTone79_onset:slideIn.watchOut: Slides into color tones must LAND ON the target pitch exactly — under-shooting leaves you on a chord tone by mistake, over-shooting leaves you in the next scale note. Pre-hear the target and use the drone as a reference anchor.

pitchConstraint:leaps_neckZone:open.interaction: Leaps demand drama and open position offers the most dramatic leap in the whole guitar: a fretted note on a wound string jumping across to an open high string. The ringing open strings become free anchor points that let your skips resonate like bluegrass cross-picking or a Richard Thompson intro — one note hands off to another while the previous one is still sounding.
steps: Map your scale shape across frets 0-5 and star every open-string note that belongs to the scale — those are your free high-leverage targets.
Start a phrase on a fretted low-string note and immediately leap to an open string at least a third away, letting both ring.
Alternate which hand initiates the leap: sometimes the fretting hand moves first (to a new fret), sometimes you jump to an untouched open string and let the fretting hand catch up.
Resolve each phrase by leaping down to the root on a low open string if it's available, letting it drone.
Record one pass and listen for bloom — a good leap in open position should make the guitar sound three strings wider than it is.
examples: fifth → root up an octave → third → fifth (low fretted fifth, jump to open-string root, drop to fretted third, back up to fifth)
root → sixth → second → fifth (fretted root, leap up to high sixth, drop across to a middle string second, land on fretted fifth)
pitchConstraint:leaps_neckZone:open.watchOut: The biggest trap is muting the ring. Lifting the fretting finger too soon or letting the picking-hand palm drift onto the bridge kills the open-string shimmer that makes this combo work. Also watch for accidental stepwise motion — if your leap lands on a neighbor, redo it.
pitchConstraint:leaps_neckZone:open.deeperInsight: Open position is the only place on guitar where the instrument itself plays some of your notes for free — leaps here are as much about which strings you don't touch as which you do.

pitchConstraint:leaps_neckZone:mid.interaction: Mid neck is barre-chord country — fat, compressed, focused. Leaping here trades the sparkle of open strings for the meatiness of fretted unisons and tight interval shapes that sit right under your hand. This is the territory of Tony Iommi riffs, Albert King bends, and funk double-stops where a big skip still feels physically contained.
steps: Anchor your index finger across frets 5-9 like a floating barre reference — that's your horizon line.
Pick any string and leap from a note under one finger to a note two frets away on the next higher string, staying inside the 5-9 box.
Let the leap come from finger replacement rather than position shift: train your ring and pinky to be launch points for skips up, index and middle for skips down.
Palm-mute the picking hand lightly so the fat mid-neck tone gets a percussive edge on each leap instead of smearing.
Close every phrase by skipping down to the root on the fourth or fifth string inside the 5-9 window.
examples: third → fifth → root up an octave → sixth (compact leap up across two strings, big skip to the octave, drop back to sixth)
root → seventh → third → fifth (leap down a step-and-skip, jump up a fourth, land on fifth — all within a four-fret span)
pitchConstraint:leaps_neckZone:mid.watchOut: Mid neck makes every leap look easy because everything is close, so the failure mode is accidentally playing adjacent notes on the same string — that's stepwise, not a leap. Verify each move crosses at least one scale degree before you commit the shape.
pitchConstraint:leaps_neckZone:mid.deeperInsight: Leaps that feel small physically but sound like big intervals are the hallmark of a player who has memorized fretboard intervals instead of shapes.

pitchConstraint:leaps_neckZone:high.interaction: High neck is where the guitar sings like a soprano, and leaps up there feel like vocal runs in a soul ballad — a Prince solo, a Nile Rodgers punctuation, a mariachi trumpet imitation. The thin strings stretch the ear more per millimeter of fret travel, so a single scale-degree leap sounds larger and brighter than the same interval elsewhere on the neck.
steps: Park your index finger around fret 10 and use it as the low anchor for everything — consider nothing below that fret legal.
Leap between strings more than along a single string up here, because cross-string skips at this range ring out cleanest.
Use your pinky aggressively for the top notes of each leap — this is the zone where pinky strength decides whether a phrase speaks.
After each leap, let the note breathe for a beat before the next one — the high register rewards space.
End every phrase on the root up an octave or the brightest note in your palette for a clear vocal landing.
examples: fifth → root up an octave → third → root up an octave (quick leap up to the octave, drop a third, back up — like a fiddle hook)
seventh → second → fifth → third (tight cross-string leaps tracing an upper-structure triad)
pitchConstraint:leaps_neckZone:high.watchOut: Thin strings squeal and choke easily up here. The common failure is squeezing too hard, which sharps every landing note — keep the fretting pressure light and let the pick attack do the accent, not the fret hand.
pitchConstraint:leaps_neckZone:high.deeperInsight: The fret spacing above fret 12 is actually narrower than the distance between scale degrees in your ear, so leaps feel physically smaller than they sound.

pitchConstraint:leaps_neckZone:fullRange.interaction: Full range unlocks the whole neck for your leaps, so the real lesson is learning which strings and frets to cross for the biggest musical payoff. Leaps that also travel physically — from fret 3 all the way to fret 14, not just across a string — give you the dramatic contour of a Django solo or a Tal Farlow run where the listener can hear the guitar player reaching.
steps: Divide the neck mentally into three zones — low (0-5), mid (5-9), high (9-15) — and aim to leave your current zone inside every 2-bar phrase.
Plan one 'big reach' per phrase: a leap of at least a sixth that also shifts your hand at least four frets.
Fill around that big reach with smaller leaps that stay inside whichever zone you just landed in, so the ear has something to process before the next journey.
Use thumb-over bass notes and pinky tops to make the zone jumps physically feasible at speed.
Close each phrase by leaping back down to a root, ideally in a different octave than where you started.
examples: root (low zone) → fifth (mid zone) → root up an octave (high zone) → third (back in mid) → root (low zone)
third → fifth → seventh → root up an octave → sixth (leaping upward through three zones, then drifting back)
pitchConstraint:leaps_neckZone:fullRange.watchOut: The failure mode is playing 'pattern leaps' that all live inside a single box shape, then sliding the box up the neck. That isn't full range — verify that at least one leap per phrase actually crosses a fret-number boundary, not just a string boundary.
pitchConstraint:leaps_neckZone:fullRange.deeperInsight: The players we admire for 'covering the fretboard' usually make two big zone jumps per phrase, not ten tiny ones — drama beats density.

pitchConstraint:stepwise_neckZone:open.interaction: Stepwise motion is the language of folk melody and hymnody, and open position is where folk music lives on guitar. The combination is practically sacred: every scale run you play in frets 0-5 can pass through open strings that ring under the fingered notes like an Irish pipe drone. This is where Nick Drake, Elizabeth Cotten, and early Dylan all lived.
steps: Lay out your scale fingering across strings 1-6 in frets 0-5 and identify which scale degrees fall on open strings — those are your effortless passing tones.
Play a straight ascending scale, but let every open-string note ring into the next fretted note instead of cutting it off.
Now descend the scale and deliberately re-pick the open strings even when they're already ringing, so each neighbor gets a tiny reattack.
Vary direction mid-phrase: three steps up, two down, one up, two down — keep it always stepwise but never predictable.
End every phrase by stepping down to the root on the lowest open-string version of it you have available.
examples: root → second → third → fourth → third → second → root (slow climb and settle, letting open strings ring through)
fifth → sixth → seventh → root up an octave → seventh → sixth (a gentle peak-and-return folk cadence)
pitchConstraint:stepwise_neckZone:open.watchOut: Open position invites you to accidentally leap across strings when changing positions — if you cross from string 4 to string 2 and skip the note on string 3, that's no longer stepwise. Watch the string you're on and move one scale-degree at a time, even if that means more string crossings.
pitchConstraint:stepwise_neckZone:open.deeperInsight: Stepwise in open position is the one case where the guitar sounds most like a keyboard — all the passing tones sustain instead of dying the moment you move.

pitchConstraint:stepwise_neckZone:mid.interaction: Mid neck stepwise is classical-etude territory: Bach preludes transcribed for guitar, Sor studies, those fat rising scale runs in a Gilmour solo. The compressed fret spacing lets you run stepwise at speed without stretching, and the fuller body of frets 5-9 makes the line feel sung rather than picked.
steps: Set up a three-note-per-string scale pattern rooted around fret 5 so you have an obvious physical lane for the run.
Walk up one string at a time, moving one scale-degree per pick stroke, letting the fretting hand glide rather than hop.
When you cross to a new string, keep the last finger you used down for a split-second so the two adjacent notes briefly overlap — that is where legato ears start to appear.
Reverse direction every two bars, not every bar, so the ear gets to settle into the climb before the descent.
End each phrase by walking down to the nearest root and holding it with tenuto weight.
examples: third → fourth → fifth → sixth → fifth → fourth → third (symmetric rise and fall inside the box)
root → second → third → fourth → fifth → fourth → third → second → root (classic one-octave scale turn-around)
pitchConstraint:stepwise_neckZone:mid.watchOut: Mid neck tempts shortcut fingerings where you skip a scale degree to make the fingering easier — resist that. If the shape forces a leap, change the shape, not the constraint. Also check that you aren't accidentally chromatic when shapes are unclear — stay inside the scale.
pitchConstraint:stepwise_neckZone:mid.deeperInsight: Mid-neck scales played stepwise are what most listeners hear as 'guitar virtuosity' — the fluidity is an illusion created by tiny physical motions.

pitchConstraint:stepwise_neckZone:high.interaction: Stepwise motion up high is the sound of a blues diva improvising over a slow 12/8 — Bonnie Raitt in ballad mode, a Warren Haynes climb, a George Benson turn. The narrow high frets make step motion feel almost like a slide, and the thin strings let you phrase with vocal delicacy that would disappear in open position.
steps: Get above fret 9 and find your scale shape across the top three strings — that's your runway.
Play each step with a tiny finger roll instead of a full lift-and-place, keeping the motion horizontal along the string.
Accent every fourth note with a pick attack — the rest should feel almost pulled out of the string by the fret finger.
When you reach the top of your runway, pivot and walk back down with even tinier steps — up there, every fret matters more.
Resolve by stepping onto the root up an octave or the nearest octave-of-root note and holding it with vibrato.
examples: fifth → sixth → seventh → root up an octave → seventh → sixth → fifth (a slow climb to the octave and gentle return)
root up an octave → second → third → second → root up an octave (two-step probe around the upper tonic)
pitchConstraint:stepwise_neckZone:high.watchOut: The high register exposes intonation cruelly. Stepwise motion there must be clean — if your fingers are landing half-between frets because you're squeezing too hard, every note goes sharp and the 'step' becomes a microtonal mush. Keep fret-pressure minimal and check with a tuner.
pitchConstraint:stepwise_neckZone:high.deeperInsight: Stepwise runs in the upper register are where most players sound like singers for the first time — the physical gesture is finally small enough to track your breath.

pitchConstraint:stepwise_neckZone:fullRa.interaction: A stepwise line that travels the whole neck is essentially a scale played across positions — the classic classical guitar exercise, but put to musical use. The payoff is a single unbroken melodic gesture that starts in the bassiest zone and arrives at the brightest one, or vice versa, the way a string section builds through a tutti unison climb.
steps: Pick a scale and map a contiguous one-octave run from low open strings all the way up through fret 12 — mark in your mind every position shift.
Walk the entire run as slowly as you need to, one step at a time, shifting position only on the same-string neighbor note (so the shift is silent inside the step).
Once the shifts are invisible, speed up and let the line become a single rising gesture — no pause at the position changes.
Reverse and descend the run, this time with tenuto weight on every fourth note so the phrase has pulse.
Build a two-bar phrase that uses most of the run but turns around before the top, then a second phrase that completes the climb — the contrast makes the full sweep feel earned.
examples: root (low) → second → third → fourth → fifth → sixth → seventh → root up an octave (full ascending scale across three positions)
root up an octave → seventh → sixth → fifth → fourth → third → second → root (return journey, same steps, reversed)
pitchConstraint:stepwise_neckZone:fullRa.watchOut: Position shifts during a stepwise climb are where leaps sneak in — if you shift and accidentally skip a scale note during the transition, the line breaks. Practice each shift in isolation and verify the neighbor note was actually sounded before moving on.
pitchConstraint:stepwise_neckZone:fullRa.deeperInsight: The way you hide a shift inside a stepwise line is the same way a cellist hides a bow change — it is the most basic and the hardest test of legato phrasing.

pitchConstraint:arch_neckZone:open.interaction: An arch in open position is the shape of every campfire melody ever written — the rise-and-fall of 'Blackbird', 'Dust In The Wind', 'Scarborough Fair'. Open strings let the ascent ring into the peak and then back down again, which means you can linger at the top without the low notes dying underneath. It's the most natural guitar contour there is.
steps: Sketch the peak of your arch on a high fretted note in the 0-5 range — ideally the third or fifth of the scale for maximum brightness.
Start a phrase on a low root or low open string, climb toward the peak in three or four steps, and let every climbing note ring.
Hit the peak with slight extra weight and let it hang for a beat before the descent starts.
Descend faster than you ascended — the return should feel like gravity, not another climb.
Land on a low open root or the low-octave equivalent, letting the whole phrase resonate until it fades naturally.
examples: root → third → fifth → sixth (peak) → fifth → third → root (a pure campfire arch with a single peak note)
root → second → fourth → fifth → seventh (peak) → fifth → third → root (a gentle climb to a higher peak and a slow return)
pitchConstraint:arch_neckZone:open.watchOut: The most common failure is an arch without a clear peak — the line wanders up and down without ever committing to a single highest note. Pick the peak before you start and promise yourself you will play it exactly once per phrase.
pitchConstraint:arch_neckZone:open.deeperInsight: Open-position arches sound like hymns because hymn melodies were designed for untrained voices, and they resemble what an open-tuned guitar naturally wants to do.

pitchConstraint:arch_neckZone:mid.interaction: Mid-neck arches have the sculpted quality of a Mark Knopfler solo or a Paul Simon lead line — the peak is a fretted note you have to work for, and the descent has weight because you're coming back through compressed fret spacing. No open strings propping anything up; every note is on your fingers.
steps: Pick a peak note around fret 9 or 10 that sits under your pinky in the scale box — that's your target.
Start from a low note in the 5-7 range and climb in steps or small leaps toward the pinky peak, spacing the climb across 2-3 beats.
Hit the peak with your pinky and bend it slightly for extra emotional weight, or hold it with vibrato — do not let it speak without intention.
Descend with a combination of pull-offs and picked notes so the return feels lighter than the climb.
Close on a middle-register root inside the 5-7 zone, giving the phrase an answered feeling.
examples: root → third → fifth → seventh (peak with bend) → fifth → third → root (classic blues-rock arch inside the box)
fifth → sixth → root up an octave (peak) → seventh → fifth → third (small rising arch with a high tonic peak)
pitchConstraint:arch_neckZone:mid.watchOut: Mid-neck arches can turn into shape-memorization: you climb the box, touch the top, climb back down, same notes every time. Make the peak a chosen destination, not a mechanical high point of the fingering.
pitchConstraint:arch_neckZone:mid.deeperInsight: A good mid-neck arch is defined by how it leaves the peak, not how it arrives — the release is what makes the phrase feel composed.

pitchConstraint:arch_neckZone:high.interaction: High-neck arches are the sound of a singer reaching for a money note — the Stevie Wonder bridge climb, the Aretha Franklin top-of-the-chorus belt translated to guitar. Every note is already bright and the peak is extreme, so the descent has to work hard to feel like gentle gravity rather than collapse.
steps: Lock yourself above fret 10 and pick a peak note as high as your pinky can stretch comfortably — that is your ceiling.
Begin the phrase on a low-for-the-zone note and climb in just 3-4 notes, because the high register arches ask for efficiency.
Arrive at the peak with a bent or vibrato-ed note — at this height, a straight pinky poke sounds frozen.
Release the peak with a slide or a gradual bend release, then step down to finish the phrase on the middle of your zone, not the bottom.
Let the whole phrase take less than two bars — high-neck arches reward compression.
examples: fifth → seventh → root up an octave (peak with bend release) → seventh → fifth (three-note climb, big peak, two-note descent)
third → fifth → second-above-octave (peak) → root up an octave → seventh → fifth (quick climb, slow settle)
pitchConstraint:arch_neckZone:high.watchOut: The ceiling problem: if your peak is a note you can barely reach with your pinky, it will squeal, choke, or go sharp. Pick a peak that is actually playable with control. Also avoid a flat descent that sounds like a scale running out of runway.
pitchConstraint:arch_neckZone:high.deeperInsight: Arches up high only sound like singing when the peak is held long enough to be a note — otherwise they sound like a pick-scrape gesture dressed up as melody.

pitchConstraint:arch_neckZone:fullRange.interaction: A full-range arch is cinematic — it starts in the dark basement and climbs all the way to the sky before descending, like the Hans Zimmer score of an action-movie build or a slow Wilco outro solo. This combo asks you to think in inches of neck-travel, not just scale degrees, and to map the arc physically before you play it.
steps: Plan the low note (open strings or frets 1-3) and the high peak (frets 12+) as two fixed dots before you start.
Sketch the climb as a series of 3-note bursts that each shift the hand up one zone — low to mid, mid to high.
Hit the peak and hold it with big vibrato or a slow bend — this is the moment the audience has been climbing toward.
Descend faster than you climbed, using slides to skip large chunks of neck and still stay inside the scale.
Resolve in a zone that is different from where you started — either one octave down from the peak, or right back at the original low note, but make the choice consciously.
examples: root (low) → third → fifth → root up an octave → fifth up two octaves (peak) → third → root up an octave → fifth → root (climb through three zones, descend through two)
fifth (low) → root up an octave → third → fifth → seventh → second-above-octave (peak) → root up an octave → fifth → root (slow burn climb, bright peak, quick return)
pitchConstraint:arch_neckZone:fullRange.watchOut: A full-range arch becomes a blur if you treat it as 'play the scale top to bottom'. The peak must be a chosen event the listener can hear coming. Also, position shifts during the climb can smear pitches — be cleaner than you think you need to be.
pitchConstraint:arch_neckZone:fullRange.deeperInsight: The difference between a scale run and a full-range arch is that an arch has a single peak the listener remembers, while a scale run has none.

pitchConstraint:seed_neckZone:open.interaction: A three-note seed in open position naturally picks up open strings as anchor pitches — which means your seed often has one 'free' note that doesn't move across iterations even when you're officially varying one note. That constraint-within-a-constraint gives open-position seeds the sound of minimalist folk, like Will Oldham or a Bill Frisell intro where a tiny motif rotates.
steps: Choose a three-note seed where at least one note is an open string — that open string becomes your anchor across all variations.
Play the seed three times exactly as written to burn it into your ear.
For the fourth iteration, change exactly one non-open note by a single scale degree up or down.
Play the new version three times, then change one note again — maybe the same note, maybe a different one.
After 5-6 iterations, return to the original seed and feel how much the journey has transformed what used to sound neutral.
examples: root → third → fifth → root → third → fifth → root → third → sixth (original seed, then variation changes fifth to sixth)
open-string root → second → fourth → open-string root → second → fifth → open-string root → third → fifth (two single-note changes over three iterations)
pitchConstraint:seed_neckZone:open.watchOut: The open-string anchor can tempt you to change the open note itself — don't. Keep the open string as an immovable reference so the ear hears the variations against a stable backdrop. Also, changing two notes at once kills the seed concept.
pitchConstraint:seed_neckZone:open.deeperInsight: A seed with an open-string anchor is how folk traditions pass songs orally — the anchor is the only thing you have to remember.

pitchConstraint:seed_neckZone:mid.interaction: A mid-neck seed is pure Steve Reich — a three-note cell in a tight physical box, rotating through micro-variations. The fat mid-neck tone makes every variation sound like a deliberate compositional choice rather than a doodle, and the barre-chord proximity lets you build entire motifs around chord tones without moving your hand.
steps: Pick a three-note seed entirely within one finger-per-fret box around frets 5-9 — that's your laboratory.
Play the seed three times straight to get the ear locked.
On the fourth iteration, change exactly one note by a single scale degree, keeping all three fingers inside the same box.
Play that new version three times, then vary again — now you have two 'sibling' seeds to alternate between.
At the end of the round, play all your variations in sequence so the development becomes audible as a single composition.
examples: root → third → fifth → root → third → fifth → root → second → fifth (seed with third becoming second)
third → fifth → seventh → third → fifth → seventh → third → fifth → sixth (seed with seventh becoming sixth)
pitchConstraint:seed_neckZone:mid.watchOut: Mid-neck seeds get boring fast if you keep varying the same note. Rotate which note you change — sometimes the first, sometimes the last, sometimes the middle. Also don't change a note and then immediately change it back; let variations breathe.
pitchConstraint:seed_neckZone:mid.deeperInsight: A seed is a tiny composition class — you're learning how one note's change recolors the meaning of the two that don't.

pitchConstraint:seed_neckZone:high.interaction: High-neck seeds sound like Indian raga ornamentation or a sarod-influenced Ry Cooder phrase — tiny three-note cells up in the stratosphere that reveal new micro-colors each time you shift a single pitch. Because the upper register is already so bright, every variation feels louder in your ear even when the volume is unchanged.
steps: Get above fret 10 and pick a three-note seed using only the top two strings — thin strings, thin seed.
Play the seed three times with light picking so the thin register doesn't fatigue the ear.
Change one note by a scale degree, ideally moving toward or away from the peak of the seed rather than the middle note.
Play three more iterations, varying the attack slightly — pull-off one, pick the next, hammer-on the next — to keep the cell alive.
End on the original seed's root note held with vibrato so the round resolves.
examples: root up an octave → third → fifth → root up an octave → third → fifth → root up an octave → fourth → fifth (third becoming fourth)
fifth → seventh → root up an octave → fifth → seventh → root up an octave → fifth → sixth → root up an octave (seventh drops to sixth)
pitchConstraint:seed_neckZone:high.watchOut: Small seeds on thin strings die easily — if your seed is all whispered, you lose the sense of variation. Make sure at least one note per cell is clearly picked so the ear can hear the change register.
pitchConstraint:seed_neckZone:high.deeperInsight: High-neck seeds work best when the seed has a built-in resolution point that the variations keep returning to — a home note inside the cell.

pitchConstraint:seed_neckZone:fullRange.interaction: A seed played across the whole neck is a displacement study — same three notes, different octaves. This is a kind of practice that jazz pianists do at a keyboard when they drop a right-hand motif into the left hand and then back again. On guitar, you're physically moving the seed through zones while keeping its melodic shape identical.
steps: Pick a three-note seed and play it in open position as written.
Move the same seed to mid neck — different octave or different string set — and play it three times.
Move it to high neck and play it three times.
Now start applying one-note variations, but rotate which zone gets the variation each iteration.
End by playing all three zones in sequence with the same variation — same motif, three octaves of color.
examples: root → third → fifth (open) → root → third → fifth (mid) → root → third → fifth (high) → root → third → sixth (high, with variation)
second → fourth → fifth (open) → second → fourth → fifth (mid) → second → third → fifth (mid, variation) → second → fourth → fifth (high, back to original)
pitchConstraint:seed_neckZone:fullRange.watchOut: The temptation is to let the seed morph every time you change zones. Don't — keep it literal for the first three zone-plays, then start varying. Also, beware of varying the note and the zone simultaneously; change one thing at a time.
pitchConstraint:seed_neckZone:fullRange.deeperInsight: Seeds that survive zone displacement teach your ears that a motif is a shape independent of register, which is the core skill of any improviser.

pitchConstraint:forbidden_neckZone:open.interaction: Forbidding notes in open position immediately reshapes the guitar's default resonance — if one of your forbidden notes is an open string, you'll feel like a hand has been tied behind your back. That restriction forces fingerings most players never touch and produces the sound of alternate-tuned fingerstyle, Joni Mitchell's DADGAD-adjacent colors, or an Elliott Smith outtake where familiar chords sound strange.
steps: Pick 1-3 forbidden scale degrees before you start — and commit them to memory, not just a piece of paper.
Play a slow scale ascent and skip each forbidden note, substituting the nearest legal neighbor to keep the shape moving.
Write a 4-note phrase using only legal notes and play it three times — it should sound stranger than it looks.
Now try to play an arch or a run across the open strings while maintaining the forbidden set; notice which shapes become impossible and let that drive new fingerings.
Close each phrase on a legal root — or, if the root is forbidden, on the most stable legal note, usually the fifth.
examples: second → fourth → fifth → seventh → fifth → fourth → second (forbidden: root and third — phrase circles around secondary colors)
root → third → fifth → seventh → fifth → third (forbidden: fourth and sixth — pentatonic-shaped phrase with hollow pockets)
pitchConstraint:forbidden_neckZone:open.watchOut: The forbidden-note trap in open position is sliding onto an open string by accident — your hand falls onto a default shape and an off-limits note rings out. Watch the open strings like hawks, and palm-mute if you catch yourself crossing over them.
pitchConstraint:forbidden_neckZone:open.deeperInsight: Removing two scale degrees in open position often converts a major key into a pentatonic or a hexatonic color that belongs to a completely different tradition.

pitchConstraint:forbidden_neckZone:mid.interaction: Forbidding notes in mid-neck barre territory forces you off your default box-shape autopilot — the fingering you've muscle-memorized suddenly has holes in it. The result sounds like a Bill Frisell improvisation or a jazz-fusion guitarist re-harmonizing over static changes: familiar shapes, unfamiliar colors, constant surprise.
steps: Pick 1-3 forbidden notes and name them by scale degree aloud before you play.
Start a slow mid-neck phrase and each time your finger wants to fall on a forbidden note, lift it and play the nearest legal neighbor instead.
After a few phrases, your ear will start to 'hear the holes' — lean into them by resting briefly on the legal note that replaces each forbidden one.
Use finger replacement rather than hand shifts to find new legal paths within the same box.
End on the tonic if it is legal, or on the fifth if the tonic is forbidden — either way, a clear landing.
examples: root → third → fifth → seventh → fifth → third (forbidden: fourth and sixth — pure upper-structure motion)
second → fourth → fifth → root up an octave → fifth → second (forbidden: third and seventh — sus-like color throughout)
pitchConstraint:forbidden_neckZone:mid.watchOut: Mid neck fingerings are so patterned that a forbidden note can be played by accident when you shift fingers mid-box. Slow the tempo enough that you can veto every finger placement consciously. Speed kills the exercise.
pitchConstraint:forbidden_neckZone:mid.deeperInsight: Forbidding the third creates an instant modal-jazz quality; forbidding the seventh creates a folk quality — the 'banned' note is the one doing all the genre work.

pitchConstraint:forbidden_neckZone:high.interaction: High-neck forbidden-note work is where you can hear an improviser learning to compose in real time — each phrase has to dodge an audible pothole, and the forbidden note's absence becomes a kind of negative-space melody of its own. Think of Chico Buarque walking a vocal line around a missing note, or a mid-career Mark Knopfler solo avoiding the blues cliché.
steps: Choose your forbidden notes and name them by degree out loud.
Above fret 10, play a two-bar improvised line that avoids the forbidden notes, stopping and restarting if a forbidden one slips out.
Repeat the most successful fragment and extend it by two notes — still dodging the forbidden degrees.
Try bending a legal note up towards the forbidden pitch and then back down without landing on it — that creates the tension of the absence.
Resolve to a legal root or fifth, holding it with vibrato so the phrase closes.
examples: fifth → sixth → root up an octave → seventh → sixth → fifth (forbidden: third and fourth — floats around upper degrees)
second → fourth → fifth → root up an octave → fifth → fourth → second (forbidden: third and sixth — phrase becomes sus-colored)
pitchConstraint:forbidden_neckZone:high.watchOut: High-neck fingerings up around the twelfth fret are bendy — a legal note you bend too far can become a forbidden note by accident. Decide in advance how far you can bend before you break a rule.
pitchConstraint:forbidden_neckZone:high.deeperInsight: Absence is a kind of melody; up high, the listener can almost hear the missing note as a ghost note.

pitchConstraint:forbidden_neckZone:fullR.interaction: Forbidden notes across the full range is the exercise most improvisers avoid because it reveals how much of their vocabulary is muscle memory in a single zone. When you have to avoid a note everywhere on the neck, you learn fingerings and voicings you've never touched — which is exactly the point. This is Kurt Rosenwinkel working on his lines, or a session player prepping for a tricky horn chart.
steps: Pick 1-3 forbidden notes and commit them to memory.
Play the same short motif in open position, mid neck, and high neck — all three zones must dodge the forbidden notes.
Build a phrase that travels through all three zones in a single gesture while still avoiding the forbidden set.
When you reach a shape that wants a forbidden note, note which finger was tempted and train an alternate to replace it.
Close the round by improvising freely across all zones for two bars under the same forbidden constraint — the shift from exercise to music is the goal.
examples: root (low) → fifth (mid) → root up an octave (high) → seventh → fifth → root (forbidden: third and fourth — zone-traversing outline)
fifth (low) → root up an octave (mid) → third → fifth → second (high) → root (forbidden: sixth and seventh — pure pentatonic color)
pitchConstraint:forbidden_neckZone:fullR.watchOut: The zone transitions are where forbidden notes sneak back in, because you're thinking about the hand shift rather than the constraint. Slow the transitions until the avoidance becomes automatic across all three zones.
pitchConstraint:forbidden_neckZone:fullR.deeperInsight: Forbidding a note across the whole neck exposes which zone of your playing is weakest — the zone where the forbidden note leaks is the one you've been leaning on.

pitchConstraint:targetLanding_neckZone:o.interaction: Target landing in open position is the heart of every folk-song melody ever written — each phrase meanders but everyone knows where it's going. Picking a target in the 0-5 zone often means landing on an open-string note, which gives your arrivals a natural sustain and the sense of arriving home. This is the 'tag' at the end of a Gillian Welch verse.
steps: Pick your target scale degree and find its lowest open-string or low-fret version — that's where every phrase lands.
Improvise a short wandering line in frets 0-5, shaped however you like, and deliberately do not go near the target until the end.
When the target appears, land it with extra emphasis — let the ringing open strings around it amplify the arrival.
Pause for one beat of silence after each landing to mark the cadence — this is not a rapid-fire exercise.
Vary the path each phrase takes but never vary the destination; after a few rounds your ear will start hearing the target coming from farther and farther away.
examples: third → fifth → second → seventh → fourth → root (six-note wander landing on the low open-string root)
fifth → seventh → second → third → sixth → fifth (wander landing on the fifth as target)
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_neckZone:o.watchOut: The main failure is landing on the target too early — if the target note appears in the middle of your wander, the landing is no longer a landing. Plan the phrase so the target only appears at the final beat.
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_neckZone:o.deeperInsight: The ear measures 'arrival' not by pitch alone but by position in time — a target played in the middle is just another note, but the same pitch at the end is a homecoming.

pitchConstraint:targetLanding_neckZone:m.interaction: Target landing in mid neck is how jazz players think about solos — pick a target pitch, wander melodically, and arrive on the target in time with a chord change. Because mid-neck has no open-string anchors, the target must earn its status through placement alone. This builds the sound of a Wes Montgomery chorus or a Kurt Rosenwinkel improvisation.
steps: Set a metronome and pick your target degree on the 4th or 3rd string in the 5-9 zone.
Improvise a line for seven beats and place the target on beat eight — nothing else can land there.
Alter the path each round: one phrase arrives by stepwise descent, one by leap up, one by chromatic approach from below.
When you hit the target, hold it with a subtle bend or vibrato so it sits like a final word.
Alternate between targeting on beat eight and targeting on beat four to train your ear on two different cadence lengths.
examples: root → third → fifth → sixth → fifth → fourth → third → second (landing on the second as target)
seventh → root up an octave → fifth → third → fourth → second → fifth (landing on the fifth as target)
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_neckZone:m.watchOut: The biggest trap is accidentally playing the target note during the approach, which robs the landing of impact. Be as vigilant about NOT playing the target as you are about playing it at the right moment.
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_neckZone:m.deeperInsight: Target-landing practice is the single fastest way to develop 'horizontal' hearing — thinking about where a line is going, not just what note is next.

pitchConstraint:targetLanding_neckZone:h.interaction: High-neck target landing is where you learn vocal-style phrasing on guitar — every phrase is built to arrive on one climactic note, like a belted resolution at the end of a soul chorus. The thin high-register strings make the target ring like a bell, and the approach becomes an elaborate setup for that single moment. Think of Stevie Wonder's voice reaching a held peak, translated to bent strings.
steps: Choose your target above fret 10, ideally the root up an octave or the fifth up an octave.
Improvise a short line in the same zone that deliberately circles the target without touching it — use approach notes from above and below.
On the final beat of each phrase, land on the target with a bend or a slide so the arrival is physically visible in the gesture.
Hold the target for a full beat or more with vibrato — high-register targets need time to bloom.
Vary your approach direction each round: above, below, leap from below, chromatic from above.
examples: seventh → fifth → sixth → root up an octave → second → fourth → third (landing on the root up an octave with bend)
third → fifth → fourth → seventh → fourth → sixth → fifth (landing on the fifth as target with vibrato)
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_neckZone:h.watchOut: The high-neck target can vanish if you don't give it enough length — a picked-and-released target note in the thin register is just another pluck. Make the target count for at least a beat of sustain.
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_neckZone:h.deeperInsight: A target up high feels like a singer hitting a high note precisely because the ear fills in the vocal metaphor when the pitch is in singing range.

pitchConstraint:targetLanding_neckZone:f.interaction: Target landing across the full neck is how composed solos work — the target is a pitch you return to from everywhere on the instrument, and the phrase before each landing can roam through every zone. This is the shape of a great John Mayer live solo or a Lindsey Buckingham improvised extension: wandering that nonetheless feels purposeful because every wander resolves in the same place.
steps: Pick one target note and find it in three octaves across the neck — low, mid, high.
Improvise a wandering phrase that crosses zones, then land on any octave of the target at the end of the phrase.
Change which octave of the target you land on each round — low this time, mid next, high after — while keeping the target pitch constant.
Try a phrase that starts in the low zone and lands on the high-octave target, and another that does the reverse.
End the round with a phrase that visits all three zones in one line and still arrives on the target.
examples: root (low) → third → fifth (mid) → seventh → root up an octave → third up an octave → fifth up an octave → root up an octave (landing on the high-octave root as target)
fifth (high) → root up an octave → fifth → third → root → fifth (low, landing on the low-octave fifth as target)
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_neckZone:f.watchOut: Zone-crossing is seductive and can make you forget the target — if you are enjoying the wander too much, you might sail past the landing. Keep one ear on the count so the target always arrives on time.
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_neckZone:f.deeperInsight: The best improvisers treat the target as a gravity well — no matter how far the phrase wanders, you can feel it being pulled back.

rhythmConstraint:river_neckZone:open.interaction: A river of quarter notes in open position is the sound of American primitive guitar — John Fahey, Robbie Basho, the folk minimalism where every tick is even and the harmonic magic comes from which open strings happen to ring under the pulse. The ear starts hearing the rhythm as inevitable, which frees you to play almost anything melodically and still feel grounded.
steps: Set the metronome to a slow tempo — 70-85 BPM — and establish a relentless quarter-note flow in frets 0-5.
Let the first full bar be a pure scale walk, one note per beat, so the river locks in your hand.
Starting in the second bar, introduce melodic variation without ever changing the quarter-note grid — the pulse never stutters.
Watch for passages where an open string can ring under a moving fretted line and exploit them — that's where the combo sings.
Close every four bars by landing on the root on beat one and letting it ring for the rest of the bar without breaking the quarter grid in your mind.
examples: root → third → fifth → third → root → fifth → seventh → root up an octave (two bars of steady quarters tracing a scalar path)
fifth → fourth → third → second → root → second → third → fifth (descending-rising quarters pivoting on the tonic)
rhythmConstraint:river_neckZone:open.watchOut: Rivers die the moment you rush a note or add an extra sixteenth in a transition. A river is not about what you play — it is about what you don't play between notes. Keep the picking hand mechanical.
rhythmConstraint:river_neckZone:open.deeperInsight: A quarter-note river in open position teaches you that melodic interest and rhythmic stability are independent — the steadier the rhythm, the freer the melody can be.

rhythmConstraint:river_neckZone:mid.interaction: Mid neck rivers have the weight of a post-rock build or a Robert Fripp cross-picking study — compressed and inexorable. With no open strings to carry the pulse, every quarter note must come from an intentional pick stroke, which builds the precise attack control that Steve Howe and Fripp are famous for.
steps: Set a moderate tempo (90-110 BPM) and lock the metronome to the click — no leading, no lagging.
Anchor a scale shape in frets 5-9 and play a straight quarter-note climb, one note per beat, for one bar.
For the next three bars, vary the pitch contour freely while never breaking the quarter-note grid.
Focus on the picking hand — each downstroke must sound exactly like the last, with no dynamic wobble unless you choose it.
Land on a root every fourth bar to reset the phrase without changing the grid.
examples: root → fifth → third → root → fifth → seventh → root up an octave → fifth (steady quarters outlining a triad then an octave leap)
third → fifth → fourth → third → second → fourth → fifth → third (quarter-note meandering inside the box)
rhythmConstraint:river_neckZone:mid.watchOut: Mid neck tempts you to palm-mute the quarters into a chunk-chunk feel — that's a different exercise. For a river, keep the notes ringing their full value until the next one starts, so the flow is audibly continuous.
rhythmConstraint:river_neckZone:mid.deeperInsight: A mid-neck river teaches that 'groove' is not about fills — it's about the uniformity of every downstroke, which is the hardest thing a picking hand learns.

rhythmConstraint:river_neckZone:high.interaction: A high-neck quarter-note river is a chime pattern — minimalist post-punk, a Johnny Marr arpeggio figure, or a classical guitar étude. The thin strings turn steady quarters into something almost percussive, and the high register makes each note sound like a distinct bell. Restraint is the instrument here.
steps: Tempo on the metronome, fretting hand above the 9th fret, picking hand relaxed.
Play straight quarter notes on the top three strings, moving one scale-degree at a time for the first bar.
For bars two through four, vary the melodic contour but never the grid.
Alternate between picked notes and hammer-ons/pull-offs inside the river, so the picking hand doesn't have to fire on every beat — this teaches independence between the hands under a steady pulse.
Resolve to a high-octave root and let it ring into the next bar even as the pulse continues in your mind.
examples: root up an octave → seventh → sixth → fifth → sixth → seventh → root up an octave → second (gentle quarter-note hover above the octave)
third → fifth → third → fifth → third → fifth → third → root (hypnotic oscillation between two pitches on the beat)
rhythmConstraint:river_neckZone:high.watchOut: High notes on thin strings are squealy and die quickly. Rivers up here require the pick to be softer than usual or the notes won't sustain their full beat length. Also, pinky fatigue is real — rest between rounds.
rhythmConstraint:river_neckZone:high.deeperInsight: A high-neck river reveals that the top strings are actually the hardest place on the guitar to play rhythmically clean, because your hand is smaller but the precision demands are larger.

rhythmConstraint:river_neckZone:fullRang.interaction: A river across the whole neck is what great classical guitarists do in an étude — one quarter note per beat, but the fingers are traveling all over the instrument. The constraint forces position shifts to happen inside the pulse, meaning every shift must be silent. This is the sound of a Sor study or a Bach prelude transcribed to six strings.
steps: Set a moderate tempo and play a simple scale that spans low to high neck across three position shifts.
Walk the scale in straight quarter notes, making sure each position shift happens between beats, not on a beat.
The shifted note must arrive exactly on its click — no rushing, no anticipating.
Once the shifts are clean, vary the direction of the scale: climb for two bars, descend for two, climb again for one.
Resolve on a root in a zone different from where you started, with the quarter grid still ticking in your head.
examples: root (low) → third → fifth (mid) → seventh → root up an octave (mid-high) → third up an octave → fifth up an octave → root up two octaves (high) — one quarter each, climbing through zones
root up an octave (high) → seventh → fifth (mid) → third → root (low) → fifth → third → root — descending river
rhythmConstraint:river_neckZone:fullRang.watchOut: Position shifts are where the river breaks — you slow down unconsciously to nail the new position, and the grid stutters. Practice the shift alone until it is silent, then put it back into the stream.
rhythmConstraint:river_neckZone:fullRang.deeperInsight: A full-range river is the fastest way to discover which position shifts your hands can't do cleanly — the pulse reveals every weakness.

rhythmConstraint:burst_neckZone:open.interaction: Bursts in open position are the sound of a Doc Watson fiddle-tune break or an Irish flute player dropping a fast run between verses — a torrent of notes exploding out of silence, then resting on a ringing open chord. Open strings amplify both the burst and the silence, because the final note of each burst can hang over the rest.
steps: At a moderate tempo, set a phrase length of four beats — the first two are the burst, the last two are silence.
Play 4-6 fast notes in the first half-bar using mostly fretted notes with occasional open strings for speed cheats.
Stop the fretted notes with a light palm touch, but let the last note (especially if open) ring across the silence.
Resist the urge to fill the silence — it is the point. Count it out loud if you have to.
Vary the pitch content of each burst but keep the rhythmic shape identical: 4-6 quick notes, then rest.
examples: root → third → fifth → seventh → fifth → third (burst) — rest — root → second → fourth → sixth → fourth → second (burst)
fifth → fourth → third → second → root (burst) — rest — fifth → sixth → seventh → root up an octave (burst)
rhythmConstraint:burst_neckZone:open.watchOut: The silence is half the exercise. If you let the open strings carry you into a new burst before the rest ends, you've flattened the phrase into a river. The rest must be absolute and felt, even if open strings are ringing.
rhythmConstraint:burst_neckZone:open.deeperInsight: Bursts with open-string endings are how trad-folk players 'breathe' through speed — the open string is the breath, not a note.

rhythmConstraint:burst_neckZone:mid.interaction: Mid-neck bursts are the sound of a Paul Gilbert lick or a flatwound jazz-bop line — tight, fast, box-contained explosions followed by deliberate silence. The compressed fret spacing lets you fire 6 notes in a single motion of the hand, and the silence after forces you to actually commit to the phrase rather than let it dribble on.
steps: Set a metronome at a quick tempo (around 110-130 BPM) and decide on a 2-bar phrase length, with the burst inside beats 1-2 of each bar.
Pick a box shape in frets 5-9 and fire 4-6 notes in a single alternate-picking burst.
Mute the strings cleanly with the fretting hand the instant the burst ends — the silence must be silent, not ringing.
Repeat the burst with a different melodic shape but the same physical gesture — this builds speed control.
Leave the final beat of each bar completely empty before starting the next burst.
examples: root → second → third → fourth → fifth (burst in 1 beat) — silence — fifth → fourth → third → second → root (burst) — silence
third → fifth → seventh → root up an octave → seventh → fifth (burst) — silence — fifth → third → root → second → third → fifth (burst)
rhythmConstraint:burst_neckZone:mid.watchOut: Mid-neck bursts tend to bleed into the silence because the fretting hand lingers on the last note. Practice stopping the burst with a deliberate mute — the silence is the lesson.
rhythmConstraint:burst_neckZone:mid.deeperInsight: A burst is really a compression test — how many notes can you fit in a short gesture without losing clarity? The silence afterward is the answer the ear needs to hear.

rhythmConstraint:burst_neckZone:high.interaction: High-neck bursts are the signature of hard bop and Indian-classical cadenzas — sudden bright flurries that peak for a second and then vanish, like a shooting-star lick at the top of a solo. The thin high strings speak fast but die fast, so the silence after a burst up here is natural and dramatic.
steps: Set a fast tempo and decide on phrase length — bursts of 4-6 notes, rests of 2-3 beats.
Above fret 10, fire your burst using only the top two strings — picking hand precise, fretting hand light.
Use pull-offs to accelerate the burst mid-flight, then stop it with a hard mute.
Let the silence sit — in the high register, silence after a burst sounds like a held breath.
Vary the burst's shape each phrase but always peak on the brightest note before the rest.
examples: root up an octave → second-above-octave → third-above-octave → fourth-above-octave → fifth-above-octave (burst peaking on the fifth-above-octave) — silence
fifth → seventh → root up an octave → second-above-octave → root up an octave → seventh (burst) — silence
rhythmConstraint:burst_neckZone:high.watchOut: Thin strings choke if you grip too hard at speed — the burst turns into a squeal. Keep the fretting hand light and let the picking hand do the work. Also, the rest after is not optional; a burst without silence is just a run.
rhythmConstraint:burst_neckZone:high.deeperInsight: High-neck bursts are the musical equivalent of a photo flash — the listener doesn't need them long, just bright.

rhythmConstraint:burst_neckZone:fullRang.interaction: A burst across the full neck is a lightning-bolt solo fragment — starts somewhere low or mid, shoots up the neck in a single flurry, and ends with silence before anything else happens. This is the shape of every great rock solo signature lick, the Page or Van Halen or Lynch gesture that covers the whole guitar in one breath.
steps: Plan the starting zone and ending zone of your burst before you play — this is a two-waypoint run.
Fire the burst as one continuous picking motion that physically moves your hand up the neck.
Pass through at least two zones during the burst — that's what makes it full-range, not a box burst.
End the burst with a hard mute or a final landing note that rings into the silence.
Count out the rest afterward — if it feels too long, you haven't truly rested yet.
examples: root (low) → third → fifth → seventh → root up an octave → third up an octave (burst climbing through three zones) — silence — same again
root (mid) → fourth → seventh → root up an octave → third → fifth (high burst) — silence — resolve to low root
rhythmConstraint:burst_neckZone:fullRang.watchOut: Full-range bursts are where position shifts fail — the hand can't physically travel three zones in one burst without smearing. Practice the shift motion slowly first, then apply the burst speed on top.
rhythmConstraint:burst_neckZone:fullRang.deeperInsight: A full-range burst is the fastest way to discover how much of the neck you actually know — the zones you stumble through are the ones you haven't mapped yet.

rhythmConstraint:space_neckZone:open.interaction: Space in open position is the sound of Ry Cooder on a slow ballad — long open-string chords ringing into nothing, each note so precious that silence becomes a character in the phrase. Open strings and open space are natural allies: the ringing sustains carry you through the rest bars without feeling empty.
steps: Set a moderate tempo and decide that every bar will have at least two beats of rest.
Play just one or two notes per bar, mostly on open or low-fret strings so they can ring.
Let the notes decay naturally — don't chase them with another attack.
When you do play, choose scale degrees with the most harmonic weight (root, fifth, third) rather than passing tones.
Count the silence as if it were a note — every rest beat has a felt pulse.
examples: root (beat 1, ring for 3 beats of rest) — fifth (beat 1 of bar 2, ring for 3 beats)
third (beat 1) — fifth (beat 3, half a bar apart) — silence until beat 1 of next bar (root)
rhythmConstraint:space_neckZone:open.watchOut: The single biggest trap with space is re-attacking a note while its predecessor is still ringing. Let the open string fade — the whole point is to train your patience as a player.
rhythmConstraint:space_neckZone:open.deeperInsight: In open position, silence is actually never silent — open strings are always doing some low-level work, which is why this combo sounds so full despite being so sparse.

rhythmConstraint:space_neckZone:mid.interaction: Mid-neck space is a jazz ballad — Jim Hall with Bill Evans, Pat Metheny on a slow tune, a Bill Frisell reverie. Without open strings to sustain for you, every sustained note has to be pushed out of the fretting hand on purpose. The silences become heavier because the hand is actively holding the note, not letting it ring.
steps: Pick a moderate tempo and commit to half a bar of rest between every melodic event.
Play a single note in the 5-9 zone with tenuto weight, then lift the finger at the last second to end it cleanly.
Sit in the silence without moving the hands — stillness is part of the exercise.
On the next beat one, play one or two more notes, again with weight, then rest.
Vary the pitch and placement of each sparse note, but never exceed two notes per bar.
examples: root (held for half a bar) — rest — third (held for half a bar)
fifth (beat 1, held) — seventh (beat 3, quickly lifted) — rest until next bar
rhythmConstraint:space_neckZone:mid.watchOut: Mid neck without space support from open strings can feel dead, and the temptation is to fill. Don't. If the note truly dies too fast, add vibrato to keep it alive — but don't re-attack.
rhythmConstraint:space_neckZone:mid.deeperInsight: The silence after a single mid-neck note is where listeners actually hear tone — the attack fools them, the decay reveals the player.

rhythmConstraint:space_neckZone:high.interaction: Space up high is the most fragile combo on the guitar — a single high note and then quiet. This is the sound of a David Gilmour hold, a Pat Metheny outro, the slow end of a jazz ballad. Every note must be emotionally worth the silence around it, and because the high strings decay quickly, the silence is often almost-complete.
steps: Above fret 10, pick one note per bar and play it with vibrato from the moment it speaks until it fades.
Let the full decay of the note happen — do not touch the string until silence returns.
Rest for the rest of the bar and the beginning of the next.
Choose scale degrees with maximum weight — the root, the third, the seventh — so every note says something.
Resolve the round with a held high-octave root that carries into silence as the final gesture.
examples: root up an octave (with vibrato, full decay) — rest — third up an octave (with vibrato, full decay)
fifth (full bar of decay) — rest half a bar — seventh (quick held note) — rest
rhythmConstraint:space_neckZone:high.watchOut: High-neck notes die fast, so space up there can sound like you stopped playing. The solution is vibrato, which keeps the note alive even as it fades. Also, don't let the silence seduce you into playing too soon.
rhythmConstraint:space_neckZone:high.deeperInsight: Silence in the upper register is the closest guitar gets to pure breath — it is what distinguishes a great ballad player from someone filling space.

rhythmConstraint:space_neckZone:fullRang.interaction: Space across the full neck is the most composerly constraint on this list — you must choose each precious note knowing it may come from anywhere on the instrument, and the space between notes is often filled with hand travel. This is the sound of a Jim Hall solo where a low-register phrase answers a high-register phrase with bars of silence between them.
steps: Plan each note's location before you play it — every sparse note has a deliberate zone.
Play one note in the low zone, let it ring, then rest for a full bar before playing one note in the mid zone.
Use the silence to physically shift your hand to the next zone, but don't rush — the travel is part of the rest.
Alternate zones for each note: low, mid, high, high, mid, low — the zones themselves become part of the melody.
End the round with a single note in the zone you started, closing the arc.
examples: root (low, beat 1 — full bar of rest) — fifth (mid, beat 1 of bar 2 — full bar) — root up an octave (high, beat 1 of bar 3)
third (high, held) — rest — seventh (low, held) — rest — fifth (mid, held)
rhythmConstraint:space_neckZone:fullRang.watchOut: Full-range space is where you can forget to play at all — the silences start feeling meditative and you lose the grid. Keep a metronome running and place each note exactly on a beat, even if it's beat 1 of the next bar.
rhythmConstraint:space_neckZone:fullRang.deeperInsight: Zones are like characters in a dialogue — space across the neck lets you hear each one speak in turn, with real pauses between speakers.

rhythmConstraint:offbeat_neckZone:open.interaction: Offbeat notes in open position are the DNA of reggae and ska — the offbeat chop is literally defined by what lands between beats 1 and 3. When you solo offbeat in the 0-5 zone, you're playing the way Ernest Ranglin or Skatalites horn lines work: upstrokes that feel like the 'skank' instead of the downbeat. The open strings make the offbeats bounce because there's no downbeat dragging the ear.
steps: Set a metronome that only clicks on 1 and 3 — those are your forbidden beats.
Place every note on the 'and' between clicks, so the click is silent to your guitar.
Use mostly upstrokes to reinforce the offbeat feel, letting open strings ring when possible.
Vary the pitch freely but keep the placement absolutely strict — no note can land on the click.
Close the phrase with a last offbeat note that rings across the following click, creating a suspended feel.
examples: rest on 1 → root on 'and' of 1 → rest on 2 → third on 'and' of 2 → rest on 3 → fifth on 'and' of 3
rest → seventh → rest → root up an octave → rest → fifth → rest → third (four-beat bar of pure offbeats)
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_neckZone:open.watchOut: The biggest failure is drifting onto the click — as the phrase gets more complex, it's tempting to land on a downbeat for emphasis. You must stay strict: if the click sounds, your guitar does not. Also, don't confuse offbeat with upbeats of a faster grid — these are the full eighth-note offbeats.
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_neckZone:open.deeperInsight: Offbeat playing is the opposite of what the body wants, which is why reggae drummers had to invent the 'one drop' — to give the offbeat guitar somewhere to hang.

rhythmConstraint:offbeat_neckZone:mid.interaction: Mid-neck offbeat is funk territory — sliding pentatonic licks that land off the beat, like Prince or Nile Rodgers but chromatic and soloed. The compressed fret spacing makes fast offbeat notes easy, and the weighty mid-neck tone gives each offbeat a punch that the open-string version can't.
steps: Set a metronome clicking only on 1 and 3.
Play a single note on every 'and' beat in the 5-9 zone, starting with just the root.
Vary the pitches across bars while keeping strict offbeat placement.
Use pick-hand muting between notes to make the offbeats pop — they should feel like stabs.
End each phrase on a strong offbeat note held just long enough to bridge the next click.
examples: rest → root → rest → fifth → rest → third → rest → seventh (four offbeat notes in a bar)
rest → fifth → rest → sixth → rest → fifth → rest → third (tight offbeat funk fragment)
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_neckZone:mid.watchOut: Mid-neck offbeat is easy to drift onto the click when the melodic line pulls you into a natural resolution — resist. If a phrase wants to land on beat 1, re-shape it so it lands on 'and' of 4 instead.
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_neckZone:mid.deeperInsight: Offbeat mid-neck playing is the core of every funk and R&B guitar part you've ever admired — displacing the pulse is most of the style.

rhythmConstraint:offbeat_neckZone:high.interaction: High-neck offbeats are a Parliament-Funkadelic high lick or a ska-punk upper lead — thin, bright, and punching the spaces between downbeats. This is the sound of a horn-line guitar where the guitar imitates a trumpet or trombone section stabbing on the 'ands'.
steps: Metronome on 1 and 3 only, tempo moderate to fast.
Above fret 10, place short staccato notes on every offbeat — use the top two strings.
Mute between notes aggressively with the palm of the picking hand so the offbeats are percussive.
Use pull-offs or slides to hit consecutive offbeats without re-picking each time.
Close on an offbeat with a short bend upward to mimic a horn hit.
examples: rest → fifth → rest → root up an octave → rest → fifth → rest → third (four offbeat horn stabs)
rest → seventh → rest → second-above-octave → rest → fifth → rest → root up an octave
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_neckZone:high.watchOut: Thin high notes on offbeats can disappear if they aren't percussive enough. The picking-hand mute is the sound — don't leave it out. Also, offbeats can drift toward a triplet feel in the high register if you're not locked in.
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_neckZone:high.deeperInsight: High-neck offbeats almost always sound like brass because horns naturally play sharp attack/quick decay notes, which is what the upper register of the guitar defaults to.

rhythmConstraint:offbeat_neckZone:fullRa.interaction: Full-range offbeats give you a conversational solo — low zone offbeats answer high zone offbeats across the beats, like a funk bassist and horn player trading fills. The physical travel between zones happens entirely on silent downbeats, which means your shifts are hidden inside the rest.
steps: Metronome on 1 and 3. Your downbeats are for moving the hand; your offbeats are for playing.
Play one offbeat note in the low zone, then use the next downbeat to shift to the mid zone, then play an offbeat note there.
Continue this pattern, using each silent downbeat as a hand shift — the offbeats become a call-and-response across zones.
After a few bars, speed up the alternation so the zones are trading every offbeat.
Resolve on a final offbeat in whichever zone you started.
examples: rest on 1 → root low on 'and' → rest on 2 → fifth mid on 'and' → rest on 3 → root high on 'and' → rest on 4 → fifth mid on 'and'
rest → third low → rest → seventh high → rest → fifth mid → rest → root low
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_neckZone:fullRa.watchOut: The hand has exactly one downbeat to travel a zone, which is not always enough time. If your shifts are sloppy, the offbeat note arrives late — practice the shift without playing first, then add the note.
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_neckZone:fullRa.deeperInsight: Offbeat-only with zone travel is the same physical discipline as a drum-kit player moving between cymbals on the 'ands' — both are improvisations inside silence.

rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_neckZone:ope.interaction: A rhythmic seed in open position is how folk melodies are actually built — one short rhythmic cell (like 'long-short-short') repeated with new notes every bar. The open strings carry the pulse under the melodic variations, and the rhythmic cell becomes the signature the ear remembers. This is 'House of the Rising Sun' or 'Streets of Laredo' cell-building.
steps: Pick a short 1-beat rhythm cell — for example, dotted-eighth + sixteenth, or triplet + rest — and commit to it for the whole round.
Play the cell on the tonic three times to lock your ear on the rhythm.
Keeping the cell rhythm identical, change the pitches each repetition — first the root, then the third, then the fifth.
Use open strings as filler inside or at the end of each cell to keep the pulse ringing.
Close by playing the cell one final time with the root and letting the open strings decay.
examples: root (cell) → third (cell) → fifth (cell) → third (cell) → root (cell) — five iterations of the same rhythmic shape
second (cell) → fourth (cell) → sixth (cell) → fifth (cell) — pitch contour walks up and settles
rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_neckZone:ope.watchOut: The rhythm cell must stay literally identical — if a phrase wants a new rhythm, resist. Any deviation from the cell breaks the seed. Also watch that open strings don't add accidental subdivisions inside the cell.
rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_neckZone:ope.deeperInsight: Rhythmic seeds are the engine of every trance-like folk tune — the repeating cell turns the song into a drone even when the pitches keep changing.

rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_neckZone:mid.interaction: Mid-neck rhythmic seeds are pure riff writing — the cell you repeat becomes a hook, and the pitch variations are the verses and choruses around it. This is Black Sabbath constructing a song from a single rhythmic idea, or Radiohead's 'Reckoner' guitars trading the same rhythmic figure with different notes.
steps: Pick a short, punchy rhythm cell — something like two sixteenths + eighth, repeated.
Establish the cell on a single mid-neck note so the rhythm is the only variable.
Move the cell to a new pitch within the 5-9 zone every two bars, keeping the rhythm exact.
After four variations, alternate pitches within a single cell — same rhythm, first cell on root, next on fifth, back on root.
Close the round with the cell on the root, held a moment past its normal length.
examples: root (cell) → root (cell) → third (cell) → third (cell) → fifth (cell) — pitch climbing while rhythm stays identical
fifth (cell) → seventh (cell) → root up an octave (cell) → seventh (cell) → fifth (cell) — an arch built from one cell
rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_neckZone:mid.watchOut: The easiest failure is to 'improve' the cell after a few repetitions — adding a note, stretching a beat. Don't. The seed is the cell, and the cell is non-negotiable.
rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_neckZone:mid.deeperInsight: Rhythmic seeds teach you that memorability in music is almost entirely a function of pattern repetition — the brain loves knowing what's coming next.

rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_neckZone:hig.interaction: High-neck rhythmic seeds sound like jingling bells in a minimalist Steve Reich figure, or the repeating high-register motif in Radiohead's 'Videotape' — a short rhythm cell with shifting pitches that creates a hypnotic, chime-like pattern. Because the high register is thin, the rhythm has to carry the weight of the phrase, not the pitches.
steps: Choose a rhythm cell that fits cleanly in one beat — short and bright.
Above fret 10, play the cell with the root of the scale three times.
Shift the cell to a new pitch every iteration, staying above fret 10.
Use finger-roll fingerings so the pitches change but the pick stroke pattern stays identical.
Close on the cell played once on the high-octave root with a slight ring-out.
examples: root up an octave (cell) → third (cell) → fifth (cell) → root up an octave (cell) — same rhythm, four pitches
fifth (cell) → sixth (cell) → seventh (cell) → root up an octave (cell) — rising seed
rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_neckZone:hig.watchOut: High strings decay fast, so the cell can sound empty if the rhythm has a long note in it — pick cells with short, punchy subdivisions up here. Also, the picking hand must stay consistent or the cell loses identity.
rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_neckZone:hig.deeperInsight: In the upper register, a rhythmic seed is often more memorable than the pitch content — you'll forget the notes before you forget the rhythm.
