neckZone:open_vibrato:slow.watchOut: In open position the string is short enough that wide vibrato pulls the note sharp of its target — your vibrato sounds like it's oscillating around a pitch slightly above what you intended. Start the vibrato from the target pitch and let the oscillation drift above and below equally.
neckZone:open_vibrato:slow.deeperInsight: Slow vibrato in open position is the acoustic blues player's rarest gesture — when it appears it always means something, because the zone doesn't need it.

neckZone:open_vibrato:fast.interaction: Fast vibrato in open position is classical and flamenco territory — the narrow, quick vibrato of a flamenco nylon-string player or a classical guitarist on a held chord tone. Combined with the open-string ringing of cowboy-chord positions, fast vibrato adds a shimmer to an already-bright zone, like the glint on water. Think Paco de Lucía on a held note over an E drone, or a classical player's trillo on the high string.
steps: Stay in frets 2-5 and target a note on one of the higher strings (G, B, or high E) where the narrow vibrato reads clearest.
Use rapid, narrow oscillation — 5-7 cycles per second — generated by a tight wrist action, not wide forearm motion.
Keep the vibrato narrow: the pitch should oscillate within a 10-cent range around the target, not a wide quarter-tone sweep.
Anchor the fretting hand's thumb on the back of the neck for stability — fast vibrato requires a stable base.
Let the ringing open strings underneath create a stable drone against which the fast vibrato shimmers.
examples: (fast vibrato on held chord tone, open ringing) third [held with fast vibrato for 2 beats] → fifth → root (open) (flamenco shimmer)
(fast vibrato on melody note) fifth (picked) → sixth (picked) → fifth [held with fast vibrato] (classical-style held note)
neckZone:open_vibrato:fast.watchOut: Fast vibrato in open position turns into a nervous twitch if the wrist isn't controlled — the oscillation becomes irregular and the note sounds unstable instead of shimmery. Practice the wrist action without the guitar first to get the rhythm consistent.
neckZone:open_vibrato:fast.deeperInsight: Fast vibrato works in open position only when it's clearly an ornament, not a default — because the zone is already so resonant, fast vibrato must be reserved for a single gesture per phrase.

neckZone:open_vibrato:lateEntry.interaction: Late-entry vibrato in open position is the perfect balance of folk restraint and blues expression — you play the note straight (folk style) and then let vibrato bloom in at the end (blues style). This is how Bert Jansch and Nick Drake used vibrato: sparingly, late, and always as an emotional release on a resolution. The open-string ringing below the vibrato note becomes the stable context against which the bloom is audible.
steps: Stay in frets 2-5 and identify a target note — usually the resolution point of a short phrase.
Pick the target note and hold it completely straight for at least a full beat.
As you approach the end of the held note, let vibrato bloom in: start narrow, widen gradually, cycle 3-4 times per second.
Let the vibrato fade back to straight tone before the note dies, or let it die while still vibrating — both are valid.
Contrast with open strings ringing underneath so the vibrato bloom reads clearly against a stable backdrop.
examples: (note held straight, then vibrato blooms) root (picked) → fifth → third [held straight 2 beats → vibrato blooms] (folk resolution with expressive tail)
(late-entry vibrato on fretted note, open below) fifth (picked, open root ringing) → [held straight 1 beat → vibrato bloom for 1 beat] (delayed expressive gesture)
neckZone:open_vibrato:lateEntry.watchOut: The temptation is to start the vibrato too early — the whole point is the delay. If your vibrato begins during the first half of the note, it's just regular slow vibrato, not late-entry.
neckZone:open_vibrato:lateEntry.deeperInsight: Late-entry vibrato is the folk-player's secret ornament — it lets you sound traditional for most of a phrase and expressive only at the resolution point, which is exactly how singers phrase.

neckZone:mid_vibrato:straight.interaction: Straight, vibrato-free playing at frets 5-9 is the sound of modern rock rhythm and funk — Nile Rodgers, Prince on rhythm guitar, any barre-chord-heavy pop arrangement. The mid-neck's compressed, weighted tone carries the harmonic authority on its own, and adding vibrato would smudge the precision. Straight tone in the mid-neck is where rhythmic intention lives.
steps: Anchor at fret 5 or 7 with a barre-chord shape and commit to zero fretting-finger movement during sustained notes.
Use firm, centered fretting pressure — every note's pitch must be rock-solid for the full duration.
Focus on rhythmic consistency instead of pitch expression — this is a picking-hand workout.
Mute aggressively with the index finger of the fretting hand so the held notes have clean beginnings and clean ends.
Practice a repetitive rhythm figure for 30 seconds with zero pitch modulation — the challenge is restraint.
examples: (straight tone rhythm figure) root [held 2 beats] → fifth [held 2 beats] → root (oct) [held 2 beats] (barre-chord line with zero vibrato)
(straight melody) third → fifth → root (oct) → fifth [all held dead straight] (rock rhythm melody)
neckZone:mid_vibrato:straight.watchOut: The mid-neck's natural sustain fools you into thinking your note is vibrating when it's actually decaying — the decay has a slight pitch drift that sounds like vibrato. Record and listen: is the pitch truly stable?
neckZone:mid_vibrato:straight.deeperInsight: Straight tone in the mid-neck is the sound of rhythmic conviction — funk and rock rhythm guitarists build their entire styles on never adding vibrato, because it would dilute the pulse.

neckZone:mid_vibrato:slow.interaction: Slow vibrato in the 5-9 fret zone is the iconic blues-rock sound — B.B. King, Gilmour, Gary Moore, Clapton. The mid-neck's string tension and position make slow, wide vibrato biomechanically optimal: your wrist and fretting finger are at the right angle, the string is the right tension. This is the home of THE rock vibrato, and it's the zone where most players first learn to vibrato properly.
steps: Anchor at fret 7 or 9 with the ring finger on a chord tone, index and middle fingers supporting behind.
Hold the note for a beat before starting vibrato — the delay gives weight to the vibrato entrance.
Rock the wrist side to side (not the fingers) at 2-3 cycles per second to generate a wide, slow vibrato.
Let the pitch oscillate equally above and below the target — not just up, not just down.
Practice the 'B.B. King hand shake': wrist rotation with the hand loose enough that the vibrato happens naturally from the wrist motion.
examples: (slow vibrato on resolution) third → fifth → root (oct) [held 4 beats with slow vibrato] (classic blues resolution)
(slow vibrato on bent note) fourth → [bent half step to fifth, held with slow vibrato] → third → root (blues bend with vibrato)
neckZone:mid_vibrato:slow.watchOut: Slow vibrato in the mid-neck feels so natural that players add it to every held note, which dilutes its impact. Save slow vibrato for 2-3 notes per phrase, not every note.
neckZone:mid_vibrato:slow.deeperInsight: Mid-neck slow vibrato is the single most imitated guitar sound in the world — everyone from teenage bedroom players to world-class touring musicians is trying to master this one gesture.

neckZone:mid_vibrato:fast.interaction: Fast vibrato in the 5-9 fret zone is the flamenco-meets-fusion sound — Paco de Lucía's electric moments, Al Di Meola's mid-neck held notes, classical-to-rock crossover players like Steve Vai at his most ornamented. Fast narrow vibrato in the mid-neck gives a note a nervous energy it couldn't have up high or down low. The combination rewards tight wrist control and light finger pressure.
steps: Anchor at fret 5 or 7 and target a held note — usually a chord tone for contextual stability.
Use narrow, rapid wrist oscillation — 5-7 cycles per second — with the vibrato width kept within a 10-15 cent range.
Keep the fretting finger lightly pressed, not gripping — fast vibrato requires finger-release as well as finger-push.
Anchor the thumb on the back of the neck for stability — without a thumb anchor, fast vibrato wobbles into slow vibrato.
Contrast fast and slow vibrato: play one held note with fast vibrato, the next with slow, so the ear hears the difference.
examples: (fast vibrato on held chord tone) root → third → fifth [held 2 beats with fast vibrato] → third → root (flamenco-fusion line)
(fast vibrato on sustain) root (oct) [held 4 beats with fast, narrow vibrato] (held note shimmer)
neckZone:mid_vibrato:fast.watchOut: Fast vibrato in the mid-neck morphs into wobble — the wrist gets lazy and the oscillation slows without you noticing. Use a metronome: 7 cycles per second is 7 cycles per second, not 'approximately fast.'
neckZone:mid_vibrato:fast.deeperInsight: Fast vibrato in the mid-neck is the least-used vibrato style in rock and blues, which is exactly why it stands out — a narrow, quick shimmer on a held note instantly signals classical training or fusion sensibility.

neckZone:mid_vibrato:lateEntry.interaction: Late-entry vibrato in the 5-9 fret zone is the signature move of the mature rock and blues lead player — SRV holding a note straight for a beat, then letting the wide slow vibrato bloom in. The mid-neck's natural sustain lets the straight-tone opening and the vibrato tail both read clearly, and the combination is emotionally devastating when used on a resolution point. This is vibrato as dramatic entrance.
steps: Anchor at fret 7 or 9 with ring finger supported by index and middle.
Pick the target note and hold it completely straight for at least one full beat — maybe two.
Let the vibrato bloom in gradually: start narrow, widen, slow down to classic 2-3 cycle per second wide vibrato.
Time the bloom so the vibrato is in full swing just before the note ends — the full cycle is straight → narrow vibrato → wide vibrato → release.
Practice the bloom on its own: pick a note, count 'one-and-two' straight, then start vibrating on 'three' — the timing is the whole gesture.
examples: (straight then vibrato bloom on resolution) fifth → root (oct) [held straight 2 beats → vibrato blooms wide for 2 beats] (classic resolution)
(vibrato bloom on a bend) third → [bent half step to fourth, held straight 1 beat, vibrato blooms 1 beat] → third (bend with delayed vibrato)
neckZone:mid_vibrato:lateEntry.watchOut: Players bloom the vibrato too abruptly — instead of a gradual widening, they flip from straight to wide vibrato instantly, which sounds like a mistake rather than an expression. The bloom must be a crescendo, not a switch.
neckZone:mid_vibrato:lateEntry.deeperInsight: Late-entry vibrato is the most vocal of all vibrato styles — singers do exactly this on held notes, holding the pitch straight before letting vibrato into the tail of the note. When guitarists mimic it, they sound like singers.

neckZone:high_vibrato:straight.interaction: Straight, vibrato-free playing in the 9-15 fret zone is unusual and deliberate — the high neck naturally invites vibrato because the strings are bendy and the tone is bright. Playing up here with zero vibrato sounds like a vibraphone or a bell, or like Jeff Beck holding a note with total control to expose the pure pitch. This is the rarest combo in the rock vocabulary — when used it's always a pointed choice.
steps: Plant at fret 12 or 14 and commit to zero vibrato for the entire round.
Use firm, centered fretting pressure; any sideways wobble up here will vibrato the note without you intending it.
Let held notes decay naturally — the bright high-neck tone means the decay itself is expressive, no vibrato needed.
Anchor the thumb behind the neck so your fretting finger can stay perfectly still under the string.
Practice holding a single high note for 8 beats with zero pitch modulation — harder than it sounds.
examples: (straight held high note) root (oct+) [held 4 beats dead straight] → seventh (oct) → fifth (oct) (vibraphone-like statement)
(straight high melody) fifth (oct) → sixth (oct) → seventh (oct) [all held straight, no vibrato] (bell-tone phrase)
neckZone:high_vibrato:straight.watchOut: The bendy high-neck strings wobble under even the slightest fretting-finger tension — you'll add vibrato without realizing it. If the note sounds 'alive,' you're probably already vibrating.
neckZone:high_vibrato:straight.deeperInsight: Straight tone in the high neck is a direct challenge to the zone's natural expressiveness — it asks you to suppress a default, which is harder than adding an ornament.

neckZone:high_vibrato:slow.interaction: Slow, wide vibrato in the 9-15 fret zone is the emotional summit of rock lead guitar — Gilmour on the end of 'Comfortably Numb,' Gary Moore on 'Still Got the Blues,' Santana on 'Europa.' The high neck's short, loose strings make wide vibrato effortless, and the slow oscillation combined with the zone's bright tone creates a singing-voice quality that is the most iconic lead sound in recorded music. This combo IS the climactic rock guitar moment.
steps: Plant at fret 12-14 with the ring finger on the target note, index and middle supporting.
Hold the note straight for one full beat before starting vibrato — the delay gives the vibrato entrance weight.
Rock the wrist side to side at 2-3 cycles per second — the motion comes from the forearm, not the fingers.
Let the vibrato be wide — up to a quarter-tone above and below the target — the high-neck strings support it easily.
Hold the vibrato for as long as the note sustains — up high, the decay is slow, so the vibrato can go on for 4+ beats.
examples: (slow wide vibrato on peak note) fifth (oct) → seventh (oct) → root (oct+) [held 4 beats, slow wide vibrato] (ballad solo peak)
(slow vibrato after bend) third (oct) → [bent whole step to fourth (oct), held with slow vibrato] → root (oct) (rock ballad bend + vibrato)
neckZone:high_vibrato:slow.watchOut: The wide vibrato pulls the average pitch sharp of the target up high because the strings stretch more than they compress. Aim slightly below the target when the vibrato is in full swing so the average lands on pitch.
neckZone:high_vibrato:slow.deeperInsight: High-neck slow vibrato is the most imitated emotional gesture in rock guitar — every student who has ever reached for a Gilmour solo starts with this single technique.

neckZone:high_vibrato:fast.interaction: Fast vibrato in the 9-15 fret zone is shred-era classical-rock crossover — Yngwie's narrow mid-string vibrato, Steve Vai's held-note shimmers, every neoclassical player's high-neck signature. The short strings and bright tone combine to make fast vibrato glitter up here, and the narrow width reads as refinement rather than nervousness. This is the flamenco-meets-metal vibrato of the 1980s guitar generation.
steps: Plant on a triad shape at fret 12-14 with firm thumb anchor behind the neck.
Target a held note — usually the root or third up an octave.
Use rapid narrow wrist oscillation — 5-7 cycles per second — with the vibrato width kept tight (10-15 cents).
Keep fretting-finger pressure light and consistent — up high, heavy pressure turns fast vibrato into pitch warp.
Practice narrow fast vibrato on a single held note for 4 beats straight before trying to play melodic lines with it.
examples: (fast narrow vibrato on peak note) fifth (oct) → seventh (oct) → root (oct+) [held 2 beats with fast narrow vibrato] (neoclassical peak)
(fast vibrato on sustained chord tone) third (oct+) [held 4 beats with fast narrow vibrato] (classical-style shimmer)
neckZone:high_vibrato:fast.watchOut: Fast vibrato up high slips out of tune easily because the wrist fatigues quickly — the narrow oscillation widens into a wobble as you lose control. Stop as soon as you feel the wrist lock up.
neckZone:high_vibrato:fast.deeperInsight: Fast vibrato in the high neck is the most technically demanding vibrato style on the guitar — it requires simultaneous narrow width AND fast cycle AND pitch stability, three constraints that rarely coexist.

neckZone:high_vibrato:lateEntry.interaction: Late-entry vibrato in the 9-15 fret zone is the signature sound of the mature rock ballad solo — Gilmour, Satriani's emotional ballads, Gary Moore's extended held notes. You play a high-neck target note straight for a full beat (or two) to expose the pure pitch, then let a wide slow vibrato bloom in. The delay is what makes the vibrato feel earned, and the high-neck zone gives the entrance maximum drama.
steps: Plant at fret 12-14 with ring finger on the target, full support from behind.
Pick the target note and hold it rock-solid straight for 1-2 beats — no pitch modulation whatsoever.
Start the vibrato narrow — barely noticeable — and widen it gradually into full-slow-wide vibrato over another 2 beats.
Let the final phase of the held note be in full wide vibrato before the note decays or the phrase moves on.
Practice the timing of the bloom separately: count 'one-and' straight, then 'two-and' narrow, then 'three-and' wide.
examples: (straight 2 beats, vibrato bloom 2 beats) root (oct+) [held straight 2 beats → vibrato blooms wide 2 beats] (ballad peak)
(vibrato bloom after bend) seventh (oct) → [bent whole step to root (oct+), held straight 1 beat, vibrato blooms 2 beats] (emotional bend + delayed vibrato)
neckZone:high_vibrato:lateEntry.watchOut: Up high, the temptation to start vibrato immediately is irresistible — the zone begs for vibrato, and your hand adds it before you mean to. The whole point is the delay; if you vibrato before beat 2, you're not doing late-entry.
neckZone:high_vibrato:lateEntry.deeperInsight: Late-entry vibrato in the high neck is the closest a guitarist gets to an opera singer's sustained note — straight pitch first, then expressive tail, then release.

neckZone:fullRange_vibrato:straight.interaction: Straight, vibrato-free playing across the full neck is the sound of rigorous melodic discipline — Mike Stern's cleaner passages, Pat Metheny's straight-tone lines, every jazz-guitar-as-horn imitation. When you commit to zero vibrato across every zone, the melody has to carry itself with no expressive cheats, and the zone travel becomes the expressive element instead. This combo is for players who want the melody to speak for itself.
steps: Plan a melodic line that travels through at least three zones and commit to zero vibrato across the entire round.
Every note must be fretted firmly with zero sideways motion, across every zone.
Use phrasing, rhythm, and position shifts as your expressive tools since vibrato is off the table.
Pay particular attention in the high neck where bendy strings will want to vibrato themselves — firm fretting is the only defense.
Record and listen: is the melody strong enough to carry without vibrato? If not, the line itself needs rewriting, not vibrato added.
examples: (no vibrato, full neck) (open) root → third → (mid) fifth → (high) root (oct) → (mid) fifth → (open) root (zone travel, straight tone)
(straight melody across zones) (mid) fifth → (high) root (oct) → (open) root → (mid) fifth [all held straight] (zone-traveling melody with zero vibrato)
neckZone:fullRange_vibrato:straight.watchOut: Across the full neck the fretting hand's micro-tension varies with position, and you'll add unintended vibrato in the high neck without realizing. Monitor each zone separately for straight-tone accuracy.
neckZone:fullRange_vibrato:straight.deeperInsight: Straight tone across the full neck reveals whether your melodic line is actually strong — vibrato often masks weak melody writing, and removing it shows you what you've really composed.

neckZone:fullRange_vibrato:slow.interaction: Slow wide vibrato across the full neck is the sound of a guitar confidently walking through its entire range while singing — Gilmour on a long solo, Santana across multiple positions, Steve Lukather holding notes in different zones. The vibrato is the unifying expression, the zone travel is the journey, and each zone colors the vibrato differently. Open position slow vibrato sounds rootsy, mid-neck sounds bluesy, high-neck sounds transcendent.
steps: Plan a melodic line that hits 3-4 zones and identify the held notes in each zone where slow vibrato will apply.
Commit to slow wide vibrato (2-3 cycles per second) on every held note, regardless of zone.
Adjust vibrato-generation technique per zone: more finger support in open position, wrist rotation in mid, loose wrist up high.
Keep the vibrato cycle rate constant across zones — the 'slow' quality must be uniform, even as the zone-character changes.
Use the fastest position shifts on non-vibrato notes and save the vibrato for the arrival points in each zone.
examples: (slow vibrato per zone) (open) root [slow vibrato] → third → (mid) fifth [slow vibrato] → seventh → (high) root (oct) [slow vibrato] (three-zone vibrato journey)
(full-neck slow vibrato melody) (high) third (oct+) [held slow vibrato 2 beats] → (mid) fifth → (open) root [held slow vibrato 2 beats] (descending vibrato arc)
neckZone:fullRange_vibrato:slow.watchOut: Your vibrato cycle rate changes zone to zone without you noticing — slower in the mid-neck, faster up high because the strings respond differently. If a listener can tell what zone you're in by the vibrato speed, you've lost the unifying quality.
neckZone:fullRange_vibrato:slow.deeperInsight: Slow vibrato across the full neck is the mark of a player who has internalized vibrato as a singing instinct rather than a zone-specific technique — the voice is the same everywhere, only the room changes.

neckZone:fullRange_vibrato:fast.interaction: Fast narrow vibrato across the full neck is a demanding technical study — Al Di Meola's intricate fusion lines, neoclassical players like Jason Becker on held notes across positions. Fast vibrato is harder to execute cleanly as you travel, because the fretting finger's optimal angle for fast vibrato changes with every zone. This combo is a workout for fretting-hand dexterity across the entire instrument.
steps: Plan a line that travels through at least three zones and identify the held notes where fast vibrato applies.
Use rapid narrow wrist oscillation (5-7 cycles per second) on every held note.
Re-anchor the thumb on the back of the neck at each new zone so the vibrato base stays stable.
Focus on keeping the vibrato width narrow even in the high neck where loose strings want to wobble wide.
Record and check: is the cycle rate consistent across zones? If the fast vibrato slows in the open position, your finger angle is wrong there.
examples: (fast narrow vibrato across zones) (mid) fifth [fast vibrato] → (high) root (oct) [fast vibrato] → (open) root [fast vibrato] (fast vibrato across three zones)
(held notes in each zone, fast vibrato) (open) third [fast vibrato] → (mid) fifth [fast vibrato] → (high) root (oct+) [fast vibrato] (rising fast vibrato series)
neckZone:fullRange_vibrato:fast.watchOut: Fast vibrato is physically exhausting across multiple zones — your wrist tires quickly, and the cycle rate slows into slow vibrato without you noticing. Take breaks and reset the wrist position before fatigue sets in.
neckZone:fullRange_vibrato:fast.deeperInsight: Fast narrow vibrato across the full neck is an ear-training exercise as much as a technique — the listener hears whether the oscillation rate is consistent, and any wobble in consistency reveals zone-specific weaknesses.

neckZone:fullRange_vibrato:lateEntry.interaction: Late-entry vibrato across the full neck is the most sophisticated vibrato combination on the forge — every zone gets a held-note bloom, and the delay before each vibrato entrance is the signature of a mature player. This is Gilmour across a full solo, Gary Moore's ballad work, every player who has learned that vibrato is most powerful when it is earned by patience. The full-neck dimension means each zone's bloom is physically different but emotionally identical.
steps: Plan a melodic line across 3-4 zones with one sustained held note per zone.
On every held note, pick the note, hold it straight for 1-2 beats, then bloom slow wide vibrato in.
Keep the bloom rate (how quickly vibrato widens) consistent across zones — the bloom is your unifying gesture.
Adapt the finger technique to the zone: in open position the bloom needs finger support, in high neck it needs only loose wrist motion.
The final held note of the round (wherever it lands on the neck) should have the longest bloom — the climactic gesture of the whole piece.
examples: (bloom vibrato per zone) (open) root [held straight 2 beats → vibrato bloom 2 beats] → (mid) fifth [held straight 1 beat → bloom 2 beats] → (high) root (oct+) [held straight 2 beats → bloom 4 beats] (three-zone bloom arc)
(bloom on final note in high neck) (mid) fifth → (high) third (oct+) [held straight 2 beats → bloom wide 4 beats] (climactic delayed vibrato)
neckZone:fullRange_vibrato:lateEntry.watchOut: The blooms get mechanical — every held note has exactly the same 2-beats-straight-then-bloom timing, and the gesture stops feeling expressive. Vary the delay slightly from note to note so the blooms feel organic.
neckZone:fullRange_vibrato:lateEntry.deeperInsight: Late-entry vibrato across the full neck is the guitar's closest analog to a singer's phrasing in a long aria — straight tone establishes pitch, the bloom adds expression, and the listener learns to expect both across every register.

pitchConstraint:leaps_noteTransition:cle.interaction: Leaps want drama and clean attack wants accountability. Every skip you throw becomes a naked statement — no legato to smear the distance, no slide to cheat the interval. This is the honesty pairing: each target note arrives freshly picked, so the ear measures the jump with ruler-like precision. Think Telecaster country double-stops, Wes Montgomery's fearless octave leaps, or the stabbed intervals of a James Jamerson bass line where silence and strike define the shape.
steps: Set metronome to 70 BPM and pick any three non-adjacent scale degrees as your leap alphabet for the round.
Pick each note with a fresh downstroke and release the fretting-hand pressure the instant before the next attack so there is no accidental ring-through.
Listen for the microsecond of silence between pick attacks — that gap is the proof of clean attack; if you hear a smudge, slow down 10 BPM.
After each leap, name the interval out loud (third, fourth, sixth) while the note is still ringing to bind the sound to the distance.
Finish every phrase with a picked landing on the root up an octave so the ear resolves the drama.
examples: root → fifth → third → root up an octave (wide arpeggio, every note picked)
sixth → second → fourth → root (zigzag leaps, four clean downstrokes)
pitchConstraint:leaps_noteTransition:cle.watchOut: The most common failure is unintended legato when your fretting finger arrives before the pick hand releases pressure on the old note — you get a hammered ghost between the leaps. Also watch for picking-hand tension creeping in as the intervals widen; clean attack is not the same as loud attack.

pitchConstraint:leaps_noteTransition:ham.interaction: This pair is a physics problem disguised as music. Leaps want distance but hammer-ons and pull-offs want fingers already on the string — so most 'impossible' leaps become possible only when you pre-plant the far finger and trust the ring of the first note to carry you across the gap. The feel is Eddie Van Halen tapping-adjacent, Allan Holdsworth legato fusion, or the slithery wide-interval runs of a sitar player who never re-strikes.
steps: Pick the first note of every leap with intention — it must ring long enough to bridge the silence while your fretting hand travels.
For every upward leap, pre-plant your landing finger in the air above the target fret so the hammer-on lands with mechanical confidence.
For every downward leap, pull off with a slight sideways flick so the lower note speaks clearly without re-picking.
Alternate ascending and descending leaps in pairs so the hand learns both directions evenly.
Finish phrases by pulling off to the open root or to the lowest available scale tone so gravity takes over.
examples: root (picked) → fifth (hammer) → third (pull-off) → seventh (hammer across strings)
second (picked) → sixth (hammer) → fourth (pull-off) → root up an octave (hammer, long ring)
pitchConstraint:leaps_noteTransition:ham.watchOut: Leaps usually cross strings, and hammer-pull is designed for single-string travel — the fretting finger will mute the new string if it lands flat. Land on the fingertip, not the pad, and accept that your leap interval is limited by how clearly the first picked note sustains.
pitchConstraint:leaps_noteTransition:ham.deeperInsight: When a leap crosses strings, a hammer-on is really a tap in disguise — the fretting finger must strike hard enough to excite the new string without help from the pick.

pitchConstraint:leaps_noteTransition:sli.interaction: Slides turn leaps into gestures. Instead of a discrete jump, the ear hears the journey — the pitch rises or falls through every fret between your start and your target, so a sixth becomes a swoop and a fifth becomes a cry. This is pedal-steel territory, Ry Cooder desert-blues territory, George Harrison slide territory, and the glassy portamento of a surf guitar line where the distance is the point of the musical sentence.
steps: Pick the first note firmly and keep constant fretting pressure through the entire slide so the pitch never dies mid-journey.
Target the far note with your ear, not your eye — let the hand find the fret by stopping when the pitch matches the scale degree you wanted.
Vary the slide speed: a fast slide sounds like a bend, a slow slide sounds like a lament; pair each leap with one of each.
Land the slide exactly on the beat so the target pitch has a clean moment of repose before the next leap begins.
Release pressure between leaps so the target note fades into silence before the next pick attack.
examples: root → (slide up) fifth → (slide down) second → (slide up) third up an octave
fourth → (slow slide) seventh → (fast slide) root → (slow slide) fifth
pitchConstraint:leaps_noteTransition:sli.watchOut: The danger is overshooting. With a wide leap your hand wants to go 'approximately there' and the slide becomes sloppy. Slide slightly past the target by a hair and settle back — a small vocal-style correction is more musical than arriving flat and staying flat.

pitchConstraint:leaps_noteTransition:ben.interaction: Bending to leap is the most vocal thing a guitar can do. Instead of fretting the far note, you fret a lower note and strain the string up to meet the target — so every leap gains a crying quality, as though the guitar is reaching for something it almost can't grab. Think Albert King's monstrous interval bends, David Gilmour's soaring fourth-bends, or a Tuareg desert blues line that treats every leap as an emotional plea.
steps: Pick the starting low note, then pre-set your fretting fingers behind the bend finger for reinforcement and bend up until the pitch matches your target scale degree.
Sustain the bent note fully so the ear accepts it as the destination, not a passing gesture.
Release the bend slowly to return to the home note, then leap again from a new starting point.
Alternate whole-step bends (to the next-but-one scale degree) and step-and-a-half bends (to the next-but-two) to train interval accuracy in the fretting hand.
Anchor the phrase by ending on a bent arrival at the root up an octave — the highest ache in the phrase.
examples: fourth (picked) → (bend up to) sixth → (release down to) fourth → (leap picked) root up an octave
second (picked) → (bend up a whole step to) third → (leap picked) fifth → (bend up to) seventh
pitchConstraint:leaps_noteTransition:ben.watchOut: The most common failure is undershot bends that arrive a quarter-tone flat of the target — your ear hears it as a leap missed, not a leap made. Always reference the target pitch first by picking it cleanly before the round, so the bend has a known destination.

pitchConstraint:stepwise_noteTransition:.interaction: Stepwise and clean attack is the exposed scale test — nowhere to hide, no slurs to blur wobbly fingers, no slides to cover imprecise fretting. It's the way classical guitarists practice scales, the way Django Reinhardt drilled his early picking, the way mandolin players build cross-picking accuracy. The music that emerges sounds like bouzouki runs, fingerstyle etudes, or a horn player's neatly tongued scale passage.
steps: Play each consecutive scale note with its own dedicated pick attack — alternate down-up strictly so the picking hand builds symmetry.
Release fretting-hand pressure between notes just enough to cut the old note but not enough to lose the new one's pitch.
Count out loud as you go: every beat is a stepping stone and there is no cheating the distance.
Reverse direction every two bars so you alternate ascending and descending stepwise motion without falling into scale-running autopilot.
End each phrase with a picked landing on a stable tone (the root, the third, or the fifth).
examples: root → second → third → fourth → fifth → fourth → third → second (up-and-back)
third → fourth → fifth → sixth → seventh → root up an octave (ascending ladder)
pitchConstraint:stepwise_noteTransition:.watchOut: Fretting-hand asymmetry is the trap — your strongest fingers want to play louder than your pinky and ring finger, so some steps punch through while others mumble. Equalise attack volume by watching the weakest finger's note closely.

pitchConstraint:stepwise_noteTransition:.interaction: Stepwise legato is the river itself — once you pick the first note, the entire scale passage flows from your fretting hand alone. This is the foundation of shredder speed and also the core of every Celtic fiddle-transcribed-to-guitar run, every bossa nova single-string fill, every Allan Holdsworth breath-long legato phrase. Notes-per-second climbs dramatically because the picking hand is no longer the bottleneck.
steps: Pick only the first note of each group (every three to six notes) and let hammer-ons climb, pull-offs descend.
Press harder with the hammering finger than you think necessary — the new note's volume comes entirely from finger impact, not pick help.
Keep the fretting fingers close to the frets at all times; every millimeter of lift is a missed note.
Tap your foot on beat one of each group to stay locked to the pulse, since you lose the natural beat-pickup of alternating pick strokes.
End each phrase with a pull-off to the root or fifth so the final note rings without a pick attack.
examples: root (picked) → second → third → fourth → fifth (hammer ladder, one pick)
fifth (picked) → fourth → third → second → root (pull-off cascade, one pick)
pitchConstraint:stepwise_noteTransition:.watchOut: Volume crater — later notes in a long legato run die away because the first pick's energy is all the sound the string has. Refresh the attack every four to six notes, and use more fretting-hand force on the notes buried in the middle of the run.

pitchConstraint:stepwise_noteTransition:.interaction: Stepwise sliding is the raga of the guitar — every scale step becomes a tiny glissando and the ear no longer hears discrete pitches so much as a continuous pitch ribbon. It evokes Indian slide guitar, Hawaiian steel, or a pedal-steel scale exercise where the music breathes between every fret. Because the intervals are small, each slide is short and intimate — the antithesis of a leaping slide.
steps: Pick the first note of the run with firm pressure, then slide one fret to the next scale step without re-picking.
Keep the slide extremely short — you're moving one or two frets, so the movement is a flick of the wrist, not a journey of the arm.
Maintain pressure so the pitch arcs continuously; if you hear the string go silent mid-slide, you've lifted.
Land the slide precisely on the next beat so the target scale degree is rhythmically anchored.
After four to six slides, re-pick and reset so the ear gets a crisp event to measure against.
examples: root → (slide) second → (slide) third → (re-pick) fourth → (slide) fifth
sixth → (slide) fifth → (slide) fourth → (re-pick) third → (slide) second → (slide) root
pitchConstraint:stepwise_noteTransition:.watchOut: Tiny slides want to become finger-lifts in disguise — if the pitch arrives without the smear, you cheated. Audible glide is the whole point of this pairing; silent note changes mean you're actually playing stepwise clean attack.

pitchConstraint:stepwise_noteTransition:.interaction: Stepwise bending is vocal mimicry at its purest — imagine a singer gently scooping up to every note. Because the steps are tiny (often a half-step or whole-step), the bends are small and precise, which trains microtonal hearing. This is the sound of BB King's delicate mid-register runs, Chet Atkins' vocal-style bends, and the gentle half-step grace notes that thread through Carnatic violin passages transcribed to guitar.
steps: Identify which steps in your scale are whole-steps and which are half-steps — the bend distance must match or the target is wrong.
Fret one scale step below the target, pick, and bend up the exact distance to the next scale degree.
Release halfway down, then re-bend to the target to train the ear to find the pitch on command.
Chain bends together: after the first bend arrives at a step, fret the next lower neighbor and bend to the next step.
Rest on the root or fifth at phrase ends so the ear confirms tonal center.
examples: root (picked) → (half-step bend to) flat second → (release) root → (whole-step bend to) second
third (picked) → (half-step bend to) fourth → (whole-step bend to) fifth → (release to) fourth
pitchConstraint:stepwise_noteTransition:.watchOut: Half-step versus whole-step distinction — in most scales the steps alternate, and a whole-step bend where a half-step belongs overshoots badly. Reference the target note by fretting it cleanly before the round so your ear locks the pitch.

pitchConstraint:arch_noteTransition:clea.interaction: Arches plus clean attack is the classical-melody structure — think Bach invention phrasing, a pentatonic folk melody, or a Segovia scale etude. Every note is its own declaration, and the phrase draws a clean hill in the air: rise to a peak, fall back to rest. The cleanliness forces each note to carry structural weight; no slurs paper over the climb.
steps: Decide your peak note before you start — a high scale tone like the root up an octave or the third up an octave.
Pick every note with alternating down-up strokes, counting the beats on the way up and then letting the descent mirror the climb.
Place the peak note on a strong beat (beat one or beat three) so the arch has architectural clarity.
Descend symmetrically — if you rose in five notes, fall in five notes, even if the pitches differ.
End on the root or fifth, picked clearly, so the arch resolves.
examples: root → third → fifth → root up an octave → fifth → third → root (symmetric arch, all picked)
second → fourth → sixth → root up an octave → sixth → fourth → second (offset arch, all picked)
pitchConstraint:arch_noteTransition:clea.watchOut: Most failed arches are crooked — students rush the ascent and drag the descent, or place the peak on a weak beat so the shape feels limp. Count through the whole phrase and treat the peak as the rhythmic center of gravity.

pitchConstraint:arch_noteTransition:hamm.interaction: Arch legato is the classic ascending-descending shred run — the kind of phrase that made Randy Rhoads and Yngwie Malmsteen famous, but also lives in bossa nova fills and Hindustani alap descents. You pick once at the bottom, hammer up to the peak, pull off all the way down, and the entire hill comes out of one pick stroke. It teaches hand coordination across a wide span.
steps: Pick the first note firmly — it must carry the entire ascent's worth of sustain.
Hammer each rising note with equal force, targeting the peak as the loudest point of the phrase.
At the peak, hold for a beat so the ear registers the apex clearly.
Pull off downward with slight sideways flicks so each descending note speaks as cleanly as its ascending twin.
End on a lower pulled-off note (root or fifth) that rings to silence.
examples: root (picked) → third → fifth → root up an octave (peak, held) → fifth → third → root (all legato)
second (picked) → fourth → sixth (peak) → fourth → second → root (hammer up, pull down)
pitchConstraint:arch_noteTransition:hamm.watchOut: The peak dies. By the time you reach the top of the arch the original pick attack's energy is gone and the apex — the most important note in the shape — whispers. Consider a re-pick at the peak if the sustain is genuinely dying, or strike the first note harder than feels musical.

pitchConstraint:arch_noteTransition:slid.interaction: An arch drawn with slides is pure pedal-steel vocabulary — the melody becomes a single continuous gesture that rises and falls like breath. You can hear this in Ben Harper's lap steel phrasing, in the slide turnarounds of a Blind Willie Johnson lament, and in the lyrical swoops of a Hawaiian guitar melody. The arch shape survives, but every transition is a glide instead of a jump.
steps: Pick the starting low note firmly and begin the slide climb immediately — the whole phrase is one breath.
Accelerate gently toward the peak so the rise has energy, then decelerate on the descent so the fall feels like a sigh.
Let the peak note rest for a beat with no slide movement — a moment of stillness at the top.
On the descent, lighten fretting pressure slightly so each slide sounds softer than its ascending counterpart.
Land the final slide on the root with a tiny fade to silence.
examples: root → (slide up) third → (slide up) fifth → (slide up) root up an octave (peak) → (slide down) fifth → (slide down) root
second → (slide) fourth → (slide) sixth (peak) → (slide) fourth → (slide) second → (slide) root
pitchConstraint:arch_noteTransition:slid.watchOut: Slide arches tend to collapse into one long smear with no internal structure. Mark the scale-degree stops by briefly pausing the slide at each target note; otherwise you just play a siren rise-and-fall with no melodic content.

pitchConstraint:arch_noteTransition:bend.interaction: Bending your way up an arch is BB King at his most vocal — the phrase climbs through bent arrivals and falls through released bends, and the entire hill feels wrung out of the guitar. The musical effect is intensely emotional because the ascent has real physical effort behind it and the descent feels like exhaling. This is the most vocal contour combo on the board.
steps: Start on a low scale tone picked firmly; bend up to the next target degree and sustain.
Without re-picking, release the bend (if moving down) or hold while re-picking to set up the next ascending bend.
The peak note should be held the longest — consider a subtle vibrato on the apex bend to make it sing.
Descend by releasing bends, re-fretting lower notes when needed to reach below the bend's starting point.
End on the root, picked cleanly with no bend — the release of tension.
examples: root (picked) → (bend to) third → (re-pick, bend to) fifth → (re-pick, bend to) root up an octave (peak, vibrato) → (release to) seventh → (release to) fifth → (pick) root
fourth (picked) → (bend to) fifth → (bend to) seventh (peak) → (release to) fifth → (release to) fourth → (pick) root
pitchConstraint:arch_noteTransition:bend.watchOut: The peak arrives flat. A big bend at the top of a climb is physically tiring, and the string-bend distance is greatest for the highest target — your fretting hand runs out of gas. Plan the peak bend carefully and consider supporting the bending finger with two fingers behind it.

pitchConstraint:seed_noteTransition:clea.interaction: A three-note seed plus clean attack is Steve Reich-minimalism territory — or the opening riff of a Philip Glass etude. Each iteration is exposed; the ear listens for exactly which note changed. Because every note is freshly picked there's no slur to hide behind, and the transformation from iteration to iteration becomes a puzzle the listener solves in real time.
steps: Choose a three-note seed from your scale — a clear shape like root-third-fifth or second-fourth-sixth.
Pick the seed four times identically to plant it in the listener's ear.
For iteration five, change exactly one note — usually the middle or the highest — and pick the new seed cleanly.
Continue morphing one note per iteration, always fully repicking so the change is unambiguous.
After eight iterations, play the original seed once more as a return bookend.
examples: [root → third → fifth] × 4 → [root → fourth → fifth] → [root → fourth → sixth] → [root → second → sixth]
[second → fourth → sixth] × 4 → [second → fourth → seventh] → [third → fourth → seventh] → [third → fifth → seventh]
pitchConstraint:seed_noteTransition:clea.watchOut: The mutation drift — students change two notes at once because they lose track of which was the 'current' seed. Say the three scale degrees out loud each iteration so you know exactly what you're altering.

pitchConstraint:seed_noteTransition:hamm.interaction: A legato seed is how most electric-guitar licks are actually built — a three-note shape repeated with tiny hand-position shifts. You pick once per iteration and the seed tumbles out of the fretting hand. As the seed mutates, the hand learns to reshape fingerings on the fly, which is exactly how players like Eric Johnson build endless variation from a tiny nucleus.
steps: Pick only the first note of each seed iteration; hammer-on and pull-off handle the other two.
Keep the fretting fingers pre-positioned for all three notes before the pick attack.
When a note changes in the next iteration, pre-plant the new fingering in the air before picking.
Keep the rhythmic envelope identical from iteration to iteration — same duration, same pick-to-hammer gap.
Return to the original seed at the end to bookend the shape.
examples: [root (pick) → third (hammer) → fifth (hammer)] × 4 → mutate middle to fourth
[fifth (pick) → third (pull-off) → root (pull-off)] × 4 → mutate low to second → mutate to seventh
pitchConstraint:seed_noteTransition:hamm.watchOut: Because you're only picking once per iteration, a missed hammer creates an embarrassing gap exactly where the mutation should be audible. Overhammer slightly during mutations until the new fingering feels settled.

pitchConstraint:seed_noteTransition:slid.interaction: A seed connected by slides is like a microscope on Hindustani alap — three-note motifs shifted incrementally with glides between. It's also the DNA of Ali Farka Touré's desert blues phrasing, where a short melodic figure slides its way across a scale over minutes. The slides bind each seed into a single gesture, so the mutations feel like a shape being stretched in space.
steps: Pick the first note of the seed, then slide into notes two and three so the full iteration is one picked event.
Keep the slides short and deliberate — not wild portamento, but purposeful glides of one to three frets each.
When you mutate the seed, change only one of the three slide targets and keep the others identical.
Between iterations allow a beat of silence so the listener can register the shift.
Return to the original seed at the round's end.
examples: [root (pick) → (slide) third → (slide) fifth] × 4 → change third to fourth → change fifth to sixth
[second (pick) → (slide) fourth → (slide) sixth] × 4 → change sixth to seventh → change second to third
pitchConstraint:seed_noteTransition:slid.watchOut: Slide seeds blur together because you can't tell when one iteration ended and the next began. Add a tiny re-pick or a breath of rest between iterations so the ear gets a reset point, and make the mutation always land on the beat.

pitchConstraint:seed_noteTransition:bend.interaction: A seed reached through bends is the backbone of a blues guitar solo — three-note micro-motifs where one note is always a bent cry. Think BB King's famous box, where a seed of root/third/fifth mutates iteration to iteration with bent arrivals. The bends give the seed emotional weight; each mutation feels like the same plea said slightly differently.
steps: Start with a seed that contains at least one bent note — e.g. root, third, bent fifth.
Play the seed four times with the same bend target and the same release speed.
Mutate by changing one of the non-bent notes, or by shifting the bend target up a scale degree.
After each mutation, stabilize the new seed by playing it twice before mutating again.
End the round by returning to the first seed with a final dramatic bend-and-hold on the apex note.
examples: [root → third → (bend to) fifth] × 4 → mutate third to fourth → mutate fifth bend to sixth
[fourth → (bend to) fifth → seventh] × 4 → mutate fourth to third → mutate seventh to root up an octave
pitchConstraint:seed_noteTransition:bend.watchOut: Bends fatigue the fretting hand, so by iteration six the bend target starts arriving flat. Rest the hand between iterations or reduce the bend distance; a flat bend turns a seed mutation into an error even if the notes were chosen correctly.

pitchConstraint:forbidden_noteTransition.interaction: Forbidden notes plus clean attack is a focus exercise — every note you strike is deliberate, and the gaps in the scale force you to rethink fingerings you use on autopilot. Picking every note cleanly makes the forbidden-note constraint visible: if you hit a banned tone, it rings out in isolation and can't be hidden in a slur. It's the opposite of muscle memory.
steps: Write the forbidden notes on paper by scale degree before you start (e.g. 'no third, no seventh').
Start slowly and pick every note with a fresh attack, saying the scale degree out loud as you play.
When you instinctively go toward a forbidden note, stop, rest a beat, and restart the phrase.
Build phrases of four to six notes that completely avoid the forbidden set.
End each phrase with a picked landing on an allowed stable tone like the root or fifth.
examples: (forbidding third and seventh) root → second → fourth → fifth → sixth → fifth → root
(forbidding second and sixth) root → third → fourth → fifth → seventh → root up an octave
pitchConstraint:forbidden_noteTransition.watchOut: Muscle memory is the enemy. Your hand has shapes it loves to play — pentatonic boxes and arpeggio templates — and they will drag you right into the forbidden tones. Slow down enough that every note is chosen consciously, not played reflexively.

pitchConstraint:forbidden_noteTransition.interaction: Legato phrases built around forbidden notes force you to rethink where your fingers land on the fretboard — because hammer-pull lines default to adjacent frets, and if the next fret is a banned note your whole finger pattern breaks. This is how jazz players like Allan Holdsworth invent new fingerings: by artificially banning one or two pitches and seeing what the hand discovers.
steps: Identify your forbidden notes and locate them on the fretboard — every one of those frets is a no-go zone.
Pick the first note, then hammer-on only toward allowed targets, often skipping one fret to reach them.
When a pull-off wants to go to a forbidden note, pull off to a further allowed note instead — this creates wide-interval legato.
Repeat each phrase twice so the hand memorizes the alternate fingering.
End the phrase by pulling off to the root or fifth for resolution.
examples: (forbidding fourth) root (pick) → third → fifth (hammer skip) → seventh → fifth (pull skip) → third → root
(forbidding sixth) fifth (pick) → seventh → root up an octave → seventh → fifth → third (pull-off ladder)
pitchConstraint:forbidden_noteTransition.watchOut: Muscle memory will have your pinky hammering onto a forbidden fret before your brain catches up. Pre-plant only the fingers you're allowed to use and let the banned fingers hang away from the strings so they physically can't betray you.

pitchConstraint:forbidden_noteTransition.interaction: Forbidden notes with slides is a special challenge because slides physically pass through every fret between your start and your target — including the forbidden ones. You must either slide so fast that the banned tone is inaudible, or choose slide targets that don't cross forbidden fret zones. It's an exercise in fretboard cartography and slide control.
steps: Mark the forbidden notes' frets mentally as danger zones on each string.
For each slide, pick a start and target where no banned fret sits between them — often this means slide on a string that skips the forbidden tone entirely.
When you must cross a banned tone, use a fast slide that spans the forbidden fret in under a sixteenth-note — too short to register as a melodic note.
Repeat each slide-phrase twice to lock in the route.
Finish on a slid arrival at the root or fifth.
examples: (forbidding third) root → (slide) second → (slide fast through forbidden to) fourth → (slide) fifth
(forbidding fourth) second → (slide up) third → (slide past the banned fret quickly to) fifth → (slide) sixth
pitchConstraint:forbidden_noteTransition.watchOut: The banned note sneaks out of the slide's middle. If you slide slowly across it, the ear hears the forbidden pitch and you've failed. Speed up the slide segments that cross forbidden frets and slow down the ones that don't — deliberate asymmetry is the craft.

pitchConstraint:forbidden_noteTransition.interaction: Forbidden plus bendTarget is a conceptual twist: bends reach their target by traveling through intermediate pitches, so if the forbidden note lies between your start and your bend target, your bend passes through it. The workaround is to choose bend distances that land on allowed notes from allowed starts — turning the constraint into a scale-degree arithmetic problem.
steps: List your forbidden scale degrees and calculate which whole-step and half-step bends from which starting notes will not accidentally park on a banned tone.
Pick an allowed low note and bend up the exact distance needed to reach an allowed target.
Hold the bend at the target long enough for the ear to register the arrival.
Release slowly and re-target a different allowed pair for variety.
End with a bent arrival at the root or fifth up an octave.
examples: (forbidding fourth) third (pick) → (bend whole-step to) fifth → (release) third → (bend to) fifth
(forbidding seventh) fifth (pick) → (bend whole-step to) sixth → (release) fifth → (bend to) root up an octave
pitchConstraint:forbidden_noteTransition.watchOut: A half-step bend from a note adjacent to a forbidden tone will often arrive right on the banned note. Always verify the bend's destination scale degree before you play the phrase — intuition is unreliable here.

pitchConstraint:targetLanding_noteTransi.interaction: Target landing plus clean attack is the textbook jazz bebop phrase-builder — wander where you want, then pick your arrival note with intention. The clean attack makes the target unmistakable because it's freshly struck; the ear can't miss the destination. This is how Charlie Parker ended phrases on guide tones, how horn players aim at chord tones, and how every great soloist lands a phrase.
steps: Choose your target note before the phrase — commit to it so the ear hears the inevitability.
Improvise freely for three to five notes, picking each one cleanly.
Aim the last note of the phrase at the chosen target and pick it with clear intention on a strong beat.
Rest for a beat after the landing so the target note rings and settles.
For the next phrase, choose a different target and repeat.
examples: (targeting the third) fifth → seventh → root → second → third (clean arrival on beat 1)
(targeting the fifth) root → third → fourth → sixth → fifth (clean arrival on beat 3)
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_noteTransi.watchOut: Target drift. You start the phrase with one target in mind and by the third note you've forgotten what you aimed at. Say the target note's scale degree in your head before every phrase, and place it on a strong beat so your body knows when to arrive.

pitchConstraint:targetLanding_noteTransi.interaction: A legato phrase that lands precisely on a target tone is the fusion-solo archetype — you fly freely with hammer-pull runs, then deliberately pick the landing note so the target punctuates the blur. Think Guthrie Govan or Tom Quayle phrases where a flurry of legato notes snaps to a single conclusive arrival. The texture contrast between legato body and picked landing is the musical point.
steps: Pick the first note, then improvise a short hammer-pull run across three to five notes.
Re-pick the target note with deliberate clean attack so it stands apart from the legato tail.
Hold the target for at least a beat before starting the next phrase.
Vary the route to the same target so the landing stays fresh.
Rotate targets every two phrases between root, third, and fifth.
examples: root (picked) → second → third → fourth (hammer run) → (re-pick) fifth (target landing)
seventh (picked) → sixth → fifth → fourth (pull-off descent) → (re-pick) third (target landing)
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_noteTransi.watchOut: The legato run runs out of sustain before you reach the target, and your 're-pick' is really the first audible note after silence. Keep the run short enough that the first pick's energy survives, or deliberately re-pick mid-run to refresh.

pitchConstraint:targetLanding_noteTransi.interaction: Sliding into a target is the pedal-steel signature move — the guitar arrives at its destination by sliding up or down from somewhere lower or higher. Ry Cooder, David Lindley, and Duane Allman all used this as a phrasal punctuation. The slide makes the target feel inevitable, because the ear hears the journey completing.
steps: Choose your target note and decide whether the approach will come from above or below.
Improvise a short free phrase that lands on a note one to three frets from the target.
Slide into the target on the beat, sustaining pressure until the target pitch speaks clearly.
Hold the target with optional slight vibrato before releasing to silence.
Alternate upward and downward approaches for variety.
examples: (targeting the fifth) second → fourth → third → fourth → (slide up) fifth (target)
(targeting the root) fifth → seventh → sixth → second → (slide down) root (target)
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_noteTransi.watchOut: The approach note is too far from the target, so the slide becomes a long smear instead of a targeted arrival. Keep the slide within a whole-step or minor-third of distance, and place the target on a strong beat so the slide timing is unambiguous.

pitchConstraint:targetLanding_noteTransi.interaction: Bending up to a landing note is the blues guitar phrase's eternal gesture — every BB King, Buddy Guy, and Albert Collins phrase resolves with a bent arrival that cries at the target. The phrase wanders through free notes, then reaches up to the target with physical effort, and the bend's emotional weight makes the target feel earned rather than merely chosen.
steps: Choose a target that sits a whole-step or step-and-a-half above a comfortable bending start note.
Improvise a short wandering phrase that lands on the bend's start note on a strong beat.
Bend up to the target and hold with slight vibrato.
Release slowly, then rest for a beat so the phrase breathes.
Rotate targets — root, third, fifth, and sometimes the flat seventh for blues color.
examples: (targeting the fifth) root → third → fourth → (bend start) fourth → (bend to) fifth (target, held)
(targeting the root up an octave) sixth → fifth → seventh → (bend start) seventh → (bend to) root up an octave
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_noteTransi.watchOut: The bend arrives flat — a very common failure because the target is the phrase's most important note and the pressure of trying to nail it causes under-bending. Reference the target by picking it cleanly once before the round begins, so your ear has a known pitch to match.

rhythmConstraint:river_noteTransition:cl.interaction: Even quarter notes with clean attack is the metronome made musical — every note a measured step, every attack a fresh commitment. It's the sound of a Bach prelude transcribed for guitar, a walking bass line in a jazz standard, or a Steve Reich 'Electric Counterpoint' interlock. The constraint forbids shortcuts; the river is slow enough that every wobble is audible.
steps: Set the metronome to 80 BPM and play one scale tone per click with alternating downstrokes and upstrokes.
Release fretting pressure on every beat so the old note dies cleanly before the new note speaks.
Listen for the gap between pick attacks — it must be identical from beat to beat.
Play four bars ascending, four bars descending, so the flow has a built-in shape.
End on a long quarter note landing on the root or fifth, held through one extra beat of silence.
examples: root → second → third → fourth | fifth → fourth → third → second (two bars, all clean quarters)
root → third → fifth → root up an octave | seventh → fifth → third → root (arpeggio river)
rhythmConstraint:river_noteTransition:cl.watchOut: Volume and time drift go together. As you play each successive note the attack weakens and the beat slips late. Lock your foot-tap to the click and check that every pick attack carries the same weight — no ghost notes, no accidental accents, no rushing.

rhythmConstraint:river_noteTransition:ha.interaction: A legato river is a strange constraint because hammer-pull naturally wants to be fast (sixteenths or triplets), but here you must keep quarter notes. You pick the first note and hammer or pull at quarter-note speed — which exposes every fretting-hand flaw. This is how classical-guitar teachers drill left-hand independence: slow, unadorned, unforgiving.
steps: Set the metronome to 60 BPM and pick every first beat; hammer the next three quarter notes.
Each hammer must land exactly on the click — no rushing, no late arrivals.
Release the previous note with a pinkie flick as the new finger hammers down so the note changes cleanly.
Alternate picked and legato notes for the first half of the round, then go full legato for the second half.
End on a held pulled-off note on the root or fifth.
examples: root (pick) → third → fifth → root up an octave (all hammered on quarters)
fifth (pick) → third → root → third (mix of pulls and hammers on quarters)
rhythmConstraint:river_noteTransition:ha.watchOut: Hammers at quarter-note speed go flat volume-wise because the fretting hand has time to relax between notes. Strike harder than feels natural — your finger is doing the entire job a pick normally does, and at slow tempo you have nothing to hide behind.

rhythmConstraint:river_noteTransition:sl.interaction: A river of slides is the Hawaiian steel guitar's signature — every quarter-note is a slide, and the music breathes through the glides between pitches. It's also the pedal-steel lament pattern, or the gentle swoops of an Indian slide guitar meditation. The constraint shapes quarter-notes into a series of short journeys, each landing on the beat.
steps: Set the metronome to 70 BPM and pick every downbeat — slides fill the space between pick attacks.
Slide one or two frets per quarter note; keep the glide short but audible.
Each slide must land exactly on the beat — the rhythmic flow cannot collapse into free-time gliding.
Vary direction — up-slides on beats 1 and 3, down-slides on beats 2 and 4 — to sketch a gentle zigzag.
End on a long slide up to the root for resolution.
examples: root (pick, beat 1) → (slide up on beat 2) second → (slide up on beat 3) third → (slide down on beat 4) second
fifth (pick) → (slide) fourth → (slide) fifth → (slide) sixth (zigzag at quarter-note pace)
rhythmConstraint:river_noteTransition:sl.watchOut: The slides arrive late because you left the fretting finger at the old note too long before starting the glide. Begin the slide on the beat before the target, so the target lands right on the next click.

rhythmConstraint:river_noteTransition:be.interaction: A river of bends is unusual and beautiful — every quarter note arrives via a bend-up-and-hold gesture, so the music feels like a slow continuous question. It's a cousin of the country pedal-steel bend idiom and the slow vocal phrasing of Ella Fitzgerald. The quarter-note grid forces the bend to be timed precisely, which is technically demanding.
steps: Pick a low starting note on beat 1 and begin bending up immediately; the bend's target pitch must land on beat 2.
Hold the bend on beat 2 fully, then release during beat 3 so the return-pitch lands on beat 4.
Pick the next bend-start on beat 1 of the following bar.
Alternate whole-step bends and half-step bends to train bend-distance sensitivity.
End on a held bend at the root up an octave that fills the whole final bar.
examples: third (pick, beat 1) → (bend arriving on beat 2) fifth → (release on beat 3) → (pick on beat 4) fourth
fifth (pick) → (bend to) sixth → (release) → (pick) fourth → (bend to) fifth
rhythmConstraint:river_noteTransition:be.watchOut: Bend timing is the hard part. A bend that reaches target pitch in the middle of the beat instead of on the beat destroys the river flow. Practice the bend's 'travel time' as a separate exercise until it lands reliably on the click.

rhythmConstraint:burst_noteTransition:cl.interaction: A burst of cleanly picked notes followed by silence is the spaghetti-western guitar motif — a machine-gun volley, then nothing. The clean attack makes the burst crystalline: you can count each note in the flurry. Think of Ennio Morricone's tremolo-picked bursts, or surf guitar's alternate-picked sixteenth-note stabs, or the kind of picking Steve Morse trains at.
steps: Set the metronome to 90 BPM and pack four to six notes into beat 1 with strict alternate picking.
Rest completely through beats 2, 3, and 4 — fretting hand off the strings, no ring-through.
Let the silence feel as intentional as the burst; the contrast is the point.
Start the next burst on beat 1 of the next bar with the same note count.
Finish the round with one final burst and a silent bar to let the ear settle.
examples: [root → second → third → fourth → fifth] (burst beat 1) → silence (beats 2-4)
[fifth → fourth → third → second → root] (burst) → silence → [root → third → fifth → root up an octave] (burst)
rhythmConstraint:burst_noteTransition:cl.watchOut: The burst bleeds into the silence because you forget to mute. Practice the mute as deliberately as the burst — palm the strings with the heel of your picking hand at the exact moment the last burst note ends.

rhythmConstraint:burst_noteTransition:ha.interaction: Bursts of hammer-pull legato are how fusion and progressive rock players drop unsung machine-gun runs — pick once, let the legato pour out a flurry, then stop cold. Think John Petrucci's bursts or Allan Holdsworth's legato clusters. The silence makes the legato feel like an explosion in a room that was previously empty.
steps: Pick the first note of the burst hard; the flurry's volume depends on that single attack.
Hammer and pull through four to six notes in one continuous legato cell.
At the end of the burst, lift the fretting fingers completely and mute the string with the picking hand.
Rest for two to three beats, fully silent.
Start the next burst with a re-pick and a new melodic shape.
examples: root (pick, burst) → third → second → fourth → third (all legato, beat 1) → silence
fifth (pick, burst) → sixth → seventh → sixth → fifth → fourth (legato pour) → silence
rhythmConstraint:burst_noteTransition:ha.watchOut: The legato burst runs out of energy before the sixth note because the first pick is already fading. Either shorten the burst to four notes or strike the first note hard enough that the whole flurry rides its sustain.

rhythmConstraint:burst_noteTransition:sl.interaction: A burst shaped by slides feels alien — sliding wants space but a burst wants density. The resolution is a rapid zigzag of tiny slides, each one covering only one or two frets, so the burst sounds like a guitar inside a vacuum tube being rapidly modulated. It evokes shimmery reverb-soaked surf guitar licks and the most pointed parts of a Bollywood sitar flourish.
steps: Pick the first note of the burst, then slide into four to five consecutive scale tones in fast succession.
Keep each slide to one or two frets — larger slides won't fit the burst's time budget.
Re-pick mid-burst if the sustain dies, but keep the pick attacks aligned with the rhythmic grid.
After the burst, damp the string with the picking hand and rest for two to three beats.
Start a new burst with a new pick attack and a different slide shape.
examples: root (pick) → (slide) second → (slide) third → (slide) fourth → (slide) fifth (burst, beat 1) → silence
sixth (pick) → (slide down) fifth → (slide down) fourth → (slide down) third → (slide down) second (burst) → silence
rhythmConstraint:burst_noteTransition:sl.watchOut: At burst speed, slides turn into finger-lifts that lose the glide character. If the notes come out with no audible smear between them, slow down the burst by one note and commit to hearing the pitch travel.

rhythmConstraint:burst_noteTransition:be.interaction: Bending within a burst is a strange athletic move — you have one beat to pack multiple bent arrivals, which means half-second bends stacked together. It feels like Hendrix's fastest vocal-style phrases, or the hyper-compressed blues bursts of Buddy Guy on a wild night. Each bend must be lightning-fast and still land on pitch.
steps: Pick a starting low note and bend up rapidly to the first target within the burst.
Release and re-pick a new low note within the same burst, bending to a new target.
Fit three to four bend events into the burst, then mute for the silent beats.
Rest fully so the fretting hand recovers — bends are exhausting.
Start a new burst with fresh targets.
examples: third (pick) → (quick bend to) fifth → (pick) fourth → (quick bend to) fifth → (pick) fifth → (quick bend to) seventh (burst) → silence
root (pick) → (quick bend to) second → (pick) second → (quick bend to) third (burst) → silence
rhythmConstraint:burst_noteTransition:be.watchOut: Fast bends arrive flat because you don't have time to complete the pitch travel. Shorten the bend distance to half-step moves inside the burst — save the big whole-step bends for the end where you have more time.

rhythmConstraint:space_noteTransition:cl.interaction: Space plus clean attack is the minimalist guitar statement — think Bill Frisell's most sparse playing, or the intervals of a Japanese shakuhachi transcription. Each note is its own event surrounded by silence, and the clean attack gives it a sculptural clarity. There is nowhere for a wobbly note to hide because there's nothing before or after it.
steps: Set the metronome to 60 BPM and place one or two notes per bar, nothing more.
Pick each note firmly with a fresh attack, then lift the fretting finger so the silence is complete.
Count through the rest beats out loud so the silence has structure.
Choose notes that contrast with each other — a low note one bar, a high note the next — so each placement feels deliberate.
End with two bars of silence after the final note to honor the space.
examples: root (beat 1) → silence (beats 2-4) → silence (bar 2) → fifth (beat 3) → silence
third (beat 1) → silence → root up an octave (beat 2 of bar 2) → silence
rhythmConstraint:space_noteTransition:cl.watchOut: The urge to fill the silence is the biggest enemy. Every extra note you sneak in ruins the space constraint. Physically lift your hands off the strings during rests if you don't trust your restraint.

rhythmConstraint:space_noteTransition:ha.interaction: Legato with spacious placement is a contradiction that rewards patience — hammer-pull usually lives inside bursts, but here you pick once and let a tiny legato cell ring out into a vast silence. It evokes ECM-jazz atmospheric phrasing, Pat Metheny's most patient lines, or the isolated hammer-ons of a Nick Drake fingerpicking passage.
steps: Pick a low note on beat 1 and let it ring for two full beats.
Hammer-on a single note and let the combined two-note event sustain into the silence.
Rest the fretting hand completely once the notes have died.
In the next bar, start a new pick-and-hammer cell from a different scale degree.
End with a pick-and-pull-off cell that leaves the fretted note ringing to silence.
examples: root (pick, beat 1) → (hammer on beat 2) third → silence (beats 3-4) → fifth (pick, bar 2 beat 2) → (hammer) seventh
fifth (pick) → (pull-off) third → silence → root up an octave (pick, next bar) → (hammer) second up an octave
rhythmConstraint:space_noteTransition:ha.watchOut: The hammer is inaudible because the first pick's sustain is already dying by the time you hammer on. Hit the first pick much harder than a typical legato attack so the hammered note has something to ride on.

rhythmConstraint:space_noteTransition:sl.interaction: Slides in wide-open space is the sound of a pedal-steel in a cathedral — one long glide per bar, and the rest is empty. The spacious context lets you savor the pitch travel, making each slide a kind of musical sigh. This evokes Daniel Lanois atmospherics, Ry Cooder's most patient Paris, Texas solos, and the gentle lap-steel breath of a Calexico interlude.
steps: Pick one low note per bar on beat 1 and begin a slow slide immediately.
Let the slide take three beats to complete — hear every fret it passes through.
Rest for the remainder of the bar after the slide arrives.
Vary the slide direction and distance so consecutive slides don't form a predictable pattern.
End with one long slow slide up to the root, fading to silence.
examples: root (pick, beat 1) → (slide slowly through beats 2-3) fifth (arriving beat 4) → silence (bar 2)
fourth (pick) → (slow slide down) root → silence → seventh (pick) → (slow slide up) second up an octave
rhythmConstraint:space_noteTransition:sl.watchOut: The slide dies mid-journey because fretting pressure lightens. In a slow slide the string's sustain is already marginal and any pressure loss kills the pitch. Press slightly harder than normal during space-context slides.

rhythmConstraint:space_noteTransition:be.interaction: A sparse, space-honoring landscape with bends is the most vocal the guitar gets — each bent note hangs in open silence like a cry. This is the most exposed bend context on the board, and it's the sound of David Gilmour on 'Marooned,' or a Pink Floyd slow-burn solo. Every bend must land true because there is nothing around it to distract the ear.
steps: Pick a low note on beat 1 and immediately begin bending up to the target.
The target pitch should arrive around beat 2 and sustain through beat 3 with slight vibrato.
Release the bend during beat 4 or hold the bend into the silence.
Rest the full next bar completely.
In bar 3, start a new bent phrase from a different scale degree.
examples: third (pick, beat 1) → (bend to) fifth (beat 2, hold) → (release beat 4) → silence (bar 2)
fifth (pick) → (bend to) root up an octave (held, vibrato) → silence → seventh (pick) → (bend to) second up an octave
rhythmConstraint:space_noteTransition:be.watchOut: Without fast notes to distract the ear, any flatness in the bend is obvious and embarrassing. Reference the target pitch once at the start of the round so you have an aural anchor, and commit to over-bending slightly rather than arriving flat.

rhythmConstraint:offbeat_noteTransition:.interaction: Offbeat placement plus clean attack is the reggae skank made melodic, or a ska lead line, or the upstroke-emphasized accent of a funk rhythm guitar player mining the 'ands.' Every note lands between the beats, and the clean attack makes each one crisply audible so the listener can feel the absence of downbeat pulse and the presence of the upbeat.
steps: Set the metronome to 90 BPM in four and imagine the click as the downbeats; your job is to play on the 'and' of every beat.
Pick each offbeat note cleanly with an upstroke to reinforce the feel.
Mute the downbeats with your picking hand so the silence on 1 and 3 is audible.
Alternate scale degrees on successive offbeats — don't let the pattern become a monotone rhythm.
End the round by placing one note on beat 1 as a cadence — the only downbeat note of the round.
examples: (silence on 1) root (and) → (silence on 2) third (and) → (silence on 3) fifth (and) → (silence on 4) root up an octave (and)
(silence) fifth (and) → (silence) fourth (and) → (silence) third (and) → (silence) second (and) — descending skank
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_noteTransition:.watchOut: The feel collapses back onto the downbeat. If you stop feeling the click as the beat, your offbeats drift into straight eighths and the reggae character disappears. Tap your foot on the clicks so the body lives the downbeat even as the hand plays the up.

rhythmConstraint:offbeat_noteTransition:.interaction: Legato offbeats is the funk-fusion lick — the picked event lands on the 'and' and the hammer-on notes trail into and through the next downbeat silence. It evokes Scofield's funk phrasing and the offbeat legato fills of a Meters-style New Orleans groove guitar part. The rhythmic kick comes from the picked onset, the hammered notes ride the pulse.
steps: Set the click to 80 BPM and pick only on 'and'; hammer-ons happen on the following downbeat.
Plan each offbeat pick as a fresh rhythmic event — the downbeat notes are implicit, hammered from the offbeat strike.
Keep a two-note legato cell per offbeat: pick on 'and,' hammer on the beat.
Alternate ascending and descending legato cells so the groove doesn't feel static.
End on a picked offbeat that hammers to the root.
examples: (silence beat 1) root (pick on and-of-1) → (hammer on beat 2) third → (silence rest of 2) fifth (pick on and-of-3) → (hammer on beat 4) seventh
(silence) fifth (pick on and-of-1) → (hammer) sixth → (silence) fourth (pick on and-of-3) → (hammer) fifth
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_noteTransition:.watchOut: Hammered notes on the downbeat fade into inaudibility because they're not picked, and offbeat-heavy listeners can't hear them. Strike the offbeat pick hard enough that the hammered downbeat is genuinely audible — otherwise you've played silence on the beat, not a legato note.

rhythmConstraint:offbeat_noteTransition:.interaction: Slides on offbeats is how a pedal-steel player makes a reggae turnaround sing — the slide lands on the 'and' and glides into the downbeat silence. It also evokes Hawaiian slack-key on the upstroke feel, and the drifting offbeat lines of a Khruangbin psychedelic lead. The slides shape the 'and' into something that swings across the unplayed downbeat.
steps: Pick a low note on the 'and' of beat 1 and immediately begin a short slide.
Let the slide arrive at its target between beats — so the target pitch hangs through the next downbeat's silence.
Rest the picking hand on the downbeats; the fretting hand holds pitch.
Alternate slide direction per bar so the groove has variation.
End with a long slide up to the root on the final 'and.'
examples: (silence beat 1) root (pick on and-of-1) → (slide up, arriving beat 2) third → (silence beat 2) fifth (pick on and-of-3) → (slide) fourth
(silence) sixth (pick on and-of-1) → (slide down) fifth → (silence) fourth (pick on and-of-3) → (slide up) fifth
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_noteTransition:.watchOut: The slide's arrival drifts onto the downbeat instead of landing before it.
