The rhythmic point of offbeat is that the ear feels the downbeat as silence — if your slide's target pitch changes ON the downbeat, you've accidentally made a straight line.

rhythmConstraint:offbeat_noteTransition:.interaction: Bending on offbeats is the Clapton-meets-Marley vocabulary — bent arrivals on the 'and,' with the downbeats as silent rest. It's an unusual combination because bends take time to travel, and the offbeat constraint means the bend's arrival pitch must hit the 'and' exactly, not the next beat. This trains bend-timing precision on an unusual grid.
steps: Start bending a low note just before the 'and' so the target pitch arrives exactly on the 'and.'
Sustain the bent note through the following downbeat silence.
Release slowly so the release completes by the next 'and' of a subsequent beat.
Alternate bent-arrival notes across scale degrees for melodic variety.
End on a bent arrival at the root up an octave on the final 'and.'
examples: (silence beat 1) (bend finishing on and-of-1) fifth → (held through beat 2) → (silence) (bend on and-of-3) seventh → (held)
(silence) (bend to) third on and-of-1 → (silence beat 2) (bend to) fifth on and-of-3 → (held)
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_noteTransition:.watchOut: The bend's target arrives on the downbeat or a fraction late, which kills the offbeat character. Start the bend a hair earlier than feels natural — the string needs lead-time to reach pitch, and the 'and' must be the arrival moment, not the beginning of the pitch travel.

rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_noteTransiti.interaction: A repeating rhythm cell with clean attack is the Steve Reich minimalism pattern — a short rhythmic motif played over and over with changing pitches. Every attack is fresh and equal, so the ear latches onto the rhythm as a constant while the pitches morph. It's also the structural heart of a countless African mbira patterns and of a Philip Glass ostinato line.
steps: Choose a rhythm cell — e.g. dotted-eighth / sixteenth / eighth / eighth — and drum it on a table until it's internalized.
Play the cell on the root for two bars with clean alternate picking.
Change to a new scale degree and play the same cell again; do not modify the rhythm.
Cycle through four to six different pitches using the identical rhythm cell each time.
End on the root with one final clean statement of the cell.
examples: [rhythm cell on the root] × 2 → [same cell on the third] × 2 → [same cell on the fifth] × 2
[dotted-eighth/sixteenth cell on the fifth] × 2 → [same on the fourth] × 2 → [same on the second] × 2
rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_noteTransiti.watchOut: The rhythm cell morphs without you noticing. Students unconsciously adjust the rhythm to fit the new pitch position, which breaks the seed. Record yourself or use a drum track to lock the rhythm in place — the point is the rhythm is the constant.

rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_noteTransiti.interaction: A repeated rhythm cell executed with legato is the foundation of a countless fusion motif and the DNA of Allan Holdsworth phrases. You pick once per rhythm cell and let the hammer-pull notes handle the rest — so the picked notes become the rhythmic anchors while the legato fills the cell. The rhythm stays fixed; the pitches drift upward or downward.
steps: Choose a rhythm cell of three to four notes.
Pick the first note of every iteration and let hammer-pull handle notes two, three, and four.
Keep the picked notes on the beat; the hammered notes ride the sustain.
Shift the pitches by one scale degree per iteration without changing the rhythm.
End on a cell where the picked root lands on beat 1 of the final bar.
examples: [root (pick) → second → third → second] (rhythm cell) → [second (pick) → third → fourth → third] → [third (pick) → fourth → fifth → fourth]
[fifth (pick) → fourth → third → fourth] × 2 → [fourth (pick) → third → second → third]
rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_noteTransiti.watchOut: The hammered notes are inaudible because the picked note's sustain is already decaying. Strike the picked note harder than the legato feels like it needs — especially for short sustaining instruments or lower-output pickups.

rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_noteTransiti.interaction: A repeating rhythm with slides is the African highlife or Tuareg desert-blues motif — the rhythm cell stays locked while the slides reshape the melody. Tinariwen's guitar lines live in this territory. The slides create a sense of continuous motion over a rigid rhythmic grid, and the result is music that grooves without ever feeling mechanical.
steps: Choose a rhythm cell — say, an eighth followed by two sixteenths repeated.
Pick the first note of each cell and slide into the other notes so the glide is audible.
Keep the slide distances short — a fret or two — so they fit inside the rhythmic cell.
Shift the pitches upward or downward by a scale degree per cell without changing the rhythm.
End with a final statement of the cell landing on the root.
examples: [root (pick) → (slide) second → (slide) root] (rhythm cell) → [second (pick) → (slide) third → (slide) second] → [third (pick) → (slide) fourth → (slide) third]
[fifth (pick) → (slide) fourth → (slide) fifth] × 2 → [fourth (pick) → (slide) third → (slide) fourth]
rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_noteTransiti.watchOut: The slides stretch beyond the rhythm cell's boundary and destroy the rhythmic integrity of the seed. Use short, tight slides so they finish inside the rhythmic time budget, not dragged past the next attack.

rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_noteTransiti.interaction: A fixed rhythm with bends is the trick Buddy Guy and Jimi Hendrix played constantly: the rhythmic cell is locked, but each iteration's target note is bent up from below, giving the repeating figure a vocal quality. It's not a rhythm change, it's a pitch treatment applied rhythmically — the result is a phrase that grooves while crying.
steps: Choose a rhythm cell of three to four notes.
Include a bend as one of the notes in the cell — usually the longest note.
Repeat the cell with the same bend, shifting the other notes up or down the scale while the bent target moves too.
Let the bend's vibrato add its own micro-rhythm on top of the cell.
End on a final cell with a held bend on the root up an octave.
examples: [third (pick) → fourth → (bend to) fifth (held)] (cell) → [fourth (pick) → fifth → (bend to) sixth] → [fifth (pick) → sixth → (bend to) seventh]
[fifth (pick) → (bend to) sixth → fifth] × 2 → [fourth (pick) → (bend to) fifth → fourth]
rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_noteTransiti.watchOut: The bend's held duration shifts from iteration to iteration, which breaks the rhythm cell. Count beats carefully and make sure the bend occupies exactly the same fraction of the rhythm every time.

rhythmConstraint:triplets_noteTransition.interaction: Triplets with clean attack is the country and western swing shuffle, the classical guitar étude, and the picking drill of Jerry Reed — three evenly spaced attacks per beat, nothing sloppy. Every triplet attack must land at exactly one-third of the beat, and that precision is the whole exercise. It's how pickers build cross-picking accuracy at speed.
steps: Set the metronome to 70 BPM and internalize the triplet feel by counting 'one-and-a, two-and-a' out loud.
Pick three notes per beat with strict down-up-down alternate picking.
Keep the volume even across all three notes — no accidental accent on the first of each triplet.
Ascend in triplets for two bars, descend for two bars.
End on a held note on the root or fifth on the first triplet of the final beat.
examples: [root → second → third] [fourth → fifth → sixth] [seventh → root up an octave → seventh] [sixth → fifth → fourth]
[root → third → fifth] [third → fifth → seventh] [fifth → seventh → root up an octave] (arpeggio triplets)
rhythmConstraint:triplets_noteTransition.watchOut: The triplet feel collapses into straight sixteenths when you add even one extra note. Listen to the click — if the third note of each triplet starts arriving too late, your sense of threes is drifting into fours.

rhythmConstraint:triplets_noteTransition.interaction: Legato triplets is the shredder's bread and butter — three-notes-per-string patterns played with one pick per string, hammer and pull filling the rest. It's Yngwie Malmsteen, Eric Johnson, John Petrucci territory, and it's also the core of every Celtic fiddle tune transcribed for guitar. The triplet grid forces rhythmic discipline on an otherwise free-flowing technique.
steps: Set the metronome to 80 BPM and pick the first note of each three-note triplet group; hammer and pull the other two.
Each triplet's three notes must be spaced evenly — the hammered notes cannot rush or drag.
Stay on a single string for each triplet group so the pattern is consistent.
Ascend through four triplets, then descend through four.
End on a held final note on the root or fifth.
examples: [root (pick) → second → third] [third (pick) → fourth → fifth] [fifth (pick) → sixth → seventh]
[seventh (pick) → sixth → fifth] [fifth (pick) → fourth → third] [third (pick) → second → root]
rhythmConstraint:triplets_noteTransition.watchOut: The third note of each triplet shrinks in volume and timing. Because the pick only gives energy to note 1, the distant hammered notes struggle — you must exaggerate the fretting-hand force on notes 2 and 3 to keep volume consistent.

rhythmConstraint:triplets_noteTransition.interaction: Triplet slides is a strange and beautiful combination — three sliding notes per beat, each one a tiny glide. The result sounds like a chicken-pickin' country lick, a Hindustani fiddle run, or a pedal-steel triplet-based melodic turn. The triplet feel forces the slides to be very short and tightly timed, which trains slide-control at speed.
steps: Pick the first note of each triplet and slide into the other two notes.
Each slide covers one or two frets — any further and the triplet's rhythm collapses.
Keep the slides audible by maintaining constant fretting pressure through the glides.
Play four triplet groups ascending and four descending.
End on a long slide into the root.
examples: [root (pick) → (slide) second → (slide) third] [third (pick) → (slide) fourth → (slide) fifth] [fifth (pick) → (slide) sixth → (slide) seventh]
[seventh (pick) → (slide) sixth → (slide) fifth] × 3 (descending slide triplets)
rhythmConstraint:triplets_noteTransition.watchOut: Short slides at triplet speed become indistinguishable from hammer-pull in the ear. Commit to the glide audibly — if the pitch jumps cleanly between frets with no smear, you've lost the slide character and played legato triplets instead.

rhythmConstraint:triplets_noteTransition.interaction: Triplets with bends is the hardest combination in the rhythm category — a bend is slow by nature but triplets are fast, so you must pack a tiny bend into a third-of-a-beat window. The result, when it works, is the kind of hyper-vocal triplet phrase you hear in blues-rock guitar at its most expressive — Clapton's 'Crossroads' territory.
steps: Pick a note on the first triplet of a beat, then bend up to the target on the second triplet.
Release or re-pick on the third triplet so the next beat's first triplet starts fresh.
Keep the bend distance small — half-step bends only — so the target can be reached inside a third-of-a-beat.
Alternate bending and non-bending triplets so the hand isn't in constant strain.
End with one final triplet that lands on a bent root or fifth.
examples: [third (pick) → (bend to) fourth → third] [third (pick) → (bend to) fourth → third] (triplets beat 1-2)
[fifth (pick) → (bend to) sixth → fifth] × 2 (triplets with half-step bends)
rhythmConstraint:triplets_noteTransition.watchOut: The bend arrives after the target triplet instead of on it. A half-step bend at triplet speed is athletic — if you can't reach the target in time, widen the bend window by making the first note longer and the bend a grace note rather than a metered triplet.

rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_noteTransition:cl.interaction: The tresillo rhythm plus clean attack is the bedrock of Afro-Cuban and reggaeton — three-plus-three-plus-two eighth notes with every attack crisp. The rhythm is the point; the melody rides on top. Clean attack keeps the tresillo structure unmistakable, so the listener can clap along and feel the asymmetric lurch of 3+3+2 even in a single-note guitar context.
steps: Set the metronome to 90 BPM in eighths and count 'one-two-three / four-five-six / seven-eight' as the 3+3+2 grouping.
Play a picked note on the 1, the 4, and the 7 of each bar, leaving all other eighths silent.
Let each note's sustain be cut by the fretting hand right before the next tresillo attack.
Vary pitches — a different scale degree per attack — so the rhythm has melodic interest.
End on a long final attack on the root on the 7 of the final bar.
examples: root (on 1) → silence → third (on 4 = dotted quarter later) → silence → fifth (on 7 = half-note later)
fifth (on 1) → fourth (on 4) → third (on 7) → root up an octave (on 1 of next bar)
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_noteTransition:cl.watchOut: The tresillo flips into 2+3+3 or straight eighths because the asymmetric grouping is unfamiliar. Clap the rhythm on a table for a full minute before picking up the guitar — your body must know the feel before your hands can play it.

rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_noteTransition:ha.interaction: Tresillo with legato is how a jazz-fusion player implies a clave without strumming it — the picked events land on the 1, 4, and 7 of the 3+3+2 grid, and the hammer-pull notes fill around them. The rhythmic feel stays clavé-like but the texture is smooth. It's a rare and sophisticated combination — think Lionel Loueke or Kurt Rosenwinkel subtle rhythmic phrasing.
steps: Pick on the 1 of the bar and let a two-note hammer-pull legato cell ring out.
Pick on the 4 and repeat another tiny legato cell.
Pick on the 7 and end with a third legato cell.
Vary pitches across the three picked events so the tresillo has melodic contour.
End on a final picked note on the 1 of the next bar with a long hammer-on sustain.
examples: root (pick on 1) → (hammer) third → fourth (pick on 4) → (hammer) fifth → fifth (pick on 7) → (pull-off) fourth
fifth (pick on 1) → (pull-off) fourth → third (pick on 4) → (hammer) fourth → second (pick on 7) → (hammer) third
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_noteTransition:ha.watchOut: Legato cells bleed past the next tresillo attack, destroying the 3+3+2 feel. Keep each cell very short — two notes max — and let the pick attack on 4 and 7 cut off the legato from the previous group.

rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_noteTransition:sl.interaction: Tresillo with slides is a Cuban-tres transplant — the 3+3+2 groove lives in the picked attacks, and slides fill the space between with melodic motion. It also evokes the Afrobeat lead guitar of Fela Kuti's band, where rhythmic groupings hold firm while the melody glides through them. Rare, sophisticated, and deeply musical.
steps: Pick on the 1, 4, and 7 of the bar and slide into the next picked note across each grouping.
The slide's journey must finish before the next pick attack — so the 3+3+2 groupings are preserved.
Short slides work best; save long glides for other contexts.
Rotate the pitches per bar so the slide direction alternates.
End on a final pick-and-hold on the root.
examples: root (pick on 1) → (slide) second → fourth (pick on 4) → (slide) fifth → sixth (pick on 7) → (slide) seventh
fifth (pick on 1) → (slide) fourth → third (pick on 4) → (slide) second → root (pick on 7) → (slide) second up an octave
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_noteTransition:sl.watchOut: Slides drift past the next tresillo attack, washing out the rhythmic grouping. Treat the 4 and 7 attacks as hard cut-offs — any slide still in progress must finish before those beats or the groove collapses into free-time.

rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_noteTransition:be.interaction: Bending through a tresillo grid is an extraordinarily musical combination because the bend's emotional arrival can land on any of the three tresillo pulses — 1, 4, or 7 — and each placement has a different groove implication. It evokes the slow-burn blues leads of a Santana Latin-rock solo where bends float over a clavé-based rhythm section.
steps: Pick a low note on the 1 of the bar and begin bending up to a target that lands on the 4.
Hold the bent note through to the 7 pulse.
Release on the 7 or re-pick a new low note and begin a new bend.
Rotate which pulse (1, 4, or 7) carries the bent arrival each bar.
End on a bent root up an octave held through the final bar's 7.
examples: root (pick on 1) → (bend arriving on 4) fifth → (held to 7) → (re-pick, bend to) seventh
third (pick on 1) → (bend to) fourth on 4 → (held) → (bend to) fifth on 7
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_noteTransition:be.watchOut: The bend's arrival timing drifts because tresillo grid is already unusual and adding bend-travel-time layers ambiguity. Clap the 3+3+2 first, then rehearse the bend's arrival on each pulse separately before combining.

dynamics:swell_noteTransition:cleanAttac.interaction: The swell with clean attack is a physical contradiction — a swell wants continuous volume rise but clean attack wants separate note-by-note events. The resolution is to start each picked note quieter and progressively strike harder, then cut everything silent on the final attack. It evokes a classical guitarist's hairpin crescendo or a Segovia tremolo swell.
steps: Start the first note with a whisper-soft pick attack using just the tip of the pick.
Each successive note increases pick force noticeably — your ear must track the growth.
Keep fretting pressure equal so the volume change comes entirely from the picking hand.
At the peak volume, strike the final note with full force on a strong beat.
Cut the string silent immediately — palm-mute or lift fingers — so the drop from peak to silence is instant.
examples: root (ppp) → second (pp) → third (p) → fourth (mf) → fifth (f) → root up an octave (fff) → silence
root (whisper) → third → fifth → seventh → root up an octave → fifth (fff) → silence
dynamics:swell_noteTransition:cleanAttac.watchOut: The volume increases in steps instead of smoothly, so the swell sounds like stairs. Pick a longer run of notes (eight or more) and increase force by only a tiny amount per note — the smoothness comes from tiny increments, not big jumps.

dynamics:swell_noteTransition:hammerPull.interaction: A legato swell is the volume-pedal ghost of an electric guitar — you pick once softly and the hammer-pull notes crescendo via the sustain growing under a volume swell. But without a volume pedal, the swell must come entirely from pick attack. The resolution is several re-picks that progressively get louder, while legato fills in between. Think Allan Holdsworth-style legato crescendos.
steps: Pick the first note softly and hammer a cell of three notes.
Re-pick the next cell slightly louder and hammer through again.
Progress through four to six cells, increasing pick force each time.
The final cell strikes at full force and ends on a sustained high note.
Cut to silence immediately after the final legato cell.
examples: [root → third → fifth] (ppp) → [third → fifth → seventh] (p) → [fifth → seventh → root up an octave] (mf) → [seventh → root up an octave → second up an octave] (fff) → silence
[second → fourth → sixth] (ppp) → [fourth → sixth → root up an octave] (p) → [sixth → root up an octave → third up an octave] (fff) → silence
dynamics:swell_noteTransition:hammerPull.watchOut: The legato cells within each swell-step fluctuate in their internal volume, masking the intended overall crescendo. Keep the pick force constant within each cell and change it only between cells — the ear reads 'steps-with-ramp,' not 'chaos with a trend.'

dynamics:swell_noteTransition:slideConne.interaction: Swelling slides is the pedal-steel crescendo — every note is a slide and every slide gets louder than the last. The guitar sounds like it's being pulled from behind a curtain into the spotlight. It's the dynamic shape of a Calexico interlude or an Ennio Morricone cinematic slide build-up. The combination makes the volume and the pitch-travel merge into a single gesture of growing intensity.
steps: Start with a whisper-soft pick attack and a slow slide.
Each subsequent picked event is louder AND carries a longer slide.
Let the slide direction alternate up and down to keep melodic variety during the crescendo.
The penultimate slide arrives at a high target at near-full volume.
The final slide is a dramatic full-force glide to the peak note, then instant silence.
examples: root (ppp pick) → (slide soft to) second → third (p pick) → (slide) fifth → sixth (mf pick) → (slide) root up an octave → (fff pick) → (slide) fifth (peak) → silence
root (pp pick) → (slide) third → fifth (mf pick) → (slide) seventh → (fff pick) → (slide) root up an octave → silence
dynamics:swell_noteTransition:slideConne.watchOut: Slides are naturally quieter than picked notes because they have no re-attack, so your swell drops every time a slide happens. Compensate by making the pick attacks progressively louder, knowing the slide's sustain will be quieter by physics.

dynamics:swell_noteTransition:bendTarget.interaction: Bending swells is the most vocal dynamic possible on guitar — each bend is an emotional reach, and each one crescendos louder than the last, so the whole phrase sounds like a singer growing from whisper to scream. It's the shape of a David Gilmour extended bend sequence, or the climactic outro of a power ballad solo. The pick force and bend intensity grow in lockstep.
steps: Start with a soft pick and a small half-step bend on a low note.
Each successive bend uses slightly louder pick force and slightly larger bend distance.
Hold each bent target longer than the last to let the swell breathe.
The final bend is struck at full force and bends a full step-and-a-half or more to a climactic target.
Cut to silence immediately after the peak bend.
examples: root (ppp pick) → (half-step bend to) flat second → third (p pick) → (bend to) fourth → fifth (mf pick) → (bend to) sixth → seventh (fff pick) → (bend to) root up an octave → silence
fifth (pp pick) → (bend to) sixth → seventh (mf pick) → (bend to) root up an octave → (fff pick) → (bend to) third up an octave → silence
dynamics:swell_noteTransition:bendTarget.watchOut: The final bend arrives flat because your hand is tired from the crescendo and the biggest bend is the last one. Save some fretting-hand strength for the peak bend, and practice just that final bend in isolation before combining it with the swell.

dynamics:terraces_noteTransition:cleanAt.interaction: Terraced dynamics with clean attack is Baroque keyboard music transferred to guitar — think Scarlatti sonatas, or the sudden 'echo' effect in a Vivaldi violin concerto. Every note is freshly picked, and the volume jumps abruptly at group boundaries. No fades, only plateaus, and the clean attack makes every level change unmistakable.
steps: Pick a four-note phrase at mezzo-forte — even, confident, not loud.
Play the same phrase again at piano — soft but clear, with the same pick technique at reduced force.
Return to mezzo-forte for four notes, then jump to forte for four.
Keep transitions sudden — no crescendo between levels, just a hard step up or down.
End on a single forte note on the root.
examples: [root → third → fifth → third] (mf) → [same] (p) → [root → fifth → root up an octave → fifth] (f)
[fifth → fourth → third → second] (f) → [same] (p) → [root → second → third → fourth] (mf)
dynamics:terraces_noteTransition:cleanAt.watchOut: The levels blur into a gradual fade because you don't commit to the sudden change. Count beats and mark the exact moment the volume level switches — your brain must issue a 'now' command and your pick hand must obey instantly.

dynamics:terraces_noteTransition:hammerP.interaction: Terraced dynamics with legato means the picked onsets carry different force levels — pick a cell softly, pick the next cell loudly, pick the next softly again. The legato cells stay internally consistent but jump between levels. It's a strange texture, reminiscent of Baroque keyboard echoes translated through fusion guitar phrasing.
steps: Pick a three-note legato cell at mezzo-forte and let the hammers ring.
Pick the next three-note cell at piano — the picked onset is much softer, which makes the whole cell softer.
Return to mezzo-forte for a third cell, then jump to forte for a fourth.
Keep the fretting-hand force constant — the dynamic shift must come from the pick only.
End with a fortissimo picked cell that lands on the root.
examples: [root (mf pick) → third → fifth] → [root (p pick) → third → fifth] → [root (mf pick) → third → fifth] → [root (f pick) → third → fifth]
[fifth (f pick) → fourth → third] → [fifth (p pick) → fourth → third] → [fifth (mf pick) → fourth → third]
dynamics:terraces_noteTransition:hammerP.watchOut: The softer cells have almost no hammered sustain because the picked onset is too quiet to drive the legato. You'll need to strike slightly harder than 'piano' feels, so the hammer-ons are audible — otherwise the soft cells become silent cells.

dynamics:terraces_noteTransition:slideCo.interaction: Terraced slides is a rare hybrid — each slide within a dynamic level is consistent, but the volume jumps at the boundaries. It creates a plateaued landscape of glides, like a pedal-steel player alternating between a whisper melody and a declarative melody on the same phrase. The sudden volume contrast highlights the slides' expressive character.
steps: Play a four-slide phrase at mezzo-forte, picking each slide's start note with even force.
Play the same phrase again at piano — same slide distances and directions, but softer picks.
Return to mezzo-forte, then jump to forte for the final phrase.
The fretting hand uses equal pressure during all slides regardless of level; only the pick force changes.
End on a forte slide up to the root.
examples: [root (mf) → (slide) third → fifth (mf) → (slide) seventh] → [same pitches at p] → [same pitches at f]
[fifth (f) → (slide) fourth → third (f) → (slide) second] → [same at p] → [same at mf]
dynamics:terraces_noteTransition:slideCo.watchOut: Slides lose audibility at piano level because the pick was too soft to give the string enough energy for the glide's sustain. Press harder with the fretting hand during soft-level slides — the compensation must come from the left side.

dynamics:terraces_noteTransition:bendTar.interaction: Terraced bends — some bends loud, some soft, no fades — creates a startling effect because bends usually ride dynamic shaping. When you terrace them instead, the ear hears 'whispered cries' alternating with 'shouted cries' on identical phrases. It's sophisticated, unusual, and deeply expressive when executed precisely. Think of a Robben Ford ballad passage with quiet bent sobs answered by loud bent declarations.
steps: Pick a bend phrase at mezzo-forte — a low note bent up to a target.
Play the same bend again at piano — same start, same target, but a much softer pick.
Alternate mezzo-forte and piano bend statements, then jump up to forte for a loud answer.
The bend distance must be identical across levels — only the pick force changes.
End with a fortissimo bend to the root up an octave.
examples: [third (mf) → (bend to) fifth] → [same (p)] → [same (mf)] → [third (f) → (bend to) fifth] (loud answer)
[fifth (mf) → (bend to) seventh] → [same (p)] → [same (f)]
dynamics:terraces_noteTransition:bendTar.watchOut: Soft bends arrive flat because a quiet pick gives the string less initial tension, and the bend travels further than intended under your fretting hand's constant force. Calibrate — you may need to bend slightly less at piano level to hit the same target pitch.

dynamics:whisper_noteTransition:cleanAtt.interaction: Whispered clean attack is the classical guitarist's pianissimo test — each note picked with the barest pressure, yet every attack fully formed. Every flaw is audible because there's no loud volume to mask imperfection. It's the sound of Julian Bream at his quietest, or a Baroque lute passage, or the ghostly pianissimo passages of Segovia's slow movements.
steps: Pick with just the pad of the pick barely touching the string — feel the string move under minimal force.
Every note must still be a full note, not a whisper that fades out halfway.
Keep the fretting hand light — any extra pressure creates buzz at whisper volume.
Play each note fully formed and let the silence between be equally quiet.
End on a held whisper note on the root, decaying naturally to silence.
examples: root → third → fifth → third → root (all whispered, steady pulse)
second → fourth → sixth → fourth → second (whispered, wave-like shape)
dynamics:whisper_noteTransition:cleanAtt.watchOut: Whispered notes become ghost notes — so soft they can't be heard at all. The pick must still make a clear string contact; otherwise you're playing silence. The challenge is finding the minimum force that still produces a clean, audible note.

dynamics:whisper_noteTransition:hammerPu.interaction: Whispered legato is almost impossible — a soft picked onset barely has sustain, and legato depends on that sustain carrying the hammered notes. The resolution is to pick slightly harder than the whisper target and let the fretting hand do most of the dynamic shaping, or to use very short legato cells where the picked energy doesn't need to last long. It's Pat Metheny's softest playing.
steps: Pick the first note with a soft-but-committed attack — softer than 'normal' but firmer than true whisper.
Hammer or pull no more than two notes before re-picking — a long legato run will die.
Keep the fretting hand firm despite the quiet context — the hammered notes depend on it.
Alternate picked and legato notes to refresh sustain constantly.
End on a held pulled-off note on the root.
examples: root (soft pick) → (hammer) second → third (soft pick) → (hammer) fourth → fifth (soft pick) → (pull-off) fourth
fifth (soft pick) → (pull-off) third → fourth (soft pick) → (hammer) fifth → sixth (soft pick) → (pull-off) fifth
dynamics:whisper_noteTransition:hammerPu.watchOut: The hammered notes are inaudible because the picked sustain was too quiet to carry them. The fundamental tension of this combo is that legato needs energy and whisper denies it. Accept that 'whisper legato' is slightly louder than 'whisper picking' — that's the honest answer.

dynamics:whisper_noteTransition:slideCon.interaction: Whispered slides is the most intimate guitar sound possible — a soft pick, a quiet slide that sustains itself on fretting-hand pressure, and a landing note that barely speaks. It's the texture of a Japanese koto transcription, or a Nick Drake fingerstyle passage on a nylon-string, or the quiet interludes of Brian Eno's 'Apollo.' The slides are breathy and ghostlike.
steps: Pick each note with the barest touch of the pick.
Slide with firm fretting-hand pressure so the pitch survives the whisper's low sustain.
Keep slides short — one or two frets — so the quiet note doesn't die mid-journey.
Rest between slides to let the ear settle into the whisper atmosphere.
End with a long slow slide up to the root, fading to silence.
examples: root (whisper pick) → (slide) second → third (whisper pick) → (slide) fourth → fifth (whisper pick) → (slide) sixth
fifth (whisper) → (slide) fourth → third (whisper) → (slide) second → root (whisper, long hold)
dynamics:whisper_noteTransition:slideCon.watchOut: The slide dies halfway through because the whisper pick didn't give the string enough energy. Fretting-hand pressure must compensate heavily — press harder during whisper-context slides than you would at normal volume.

dynamics:whisper_noteTransition:bendTarg.interaction: Whispered bends is the most delicate blues guitar move — a tiny pick attack, a bend that barely bends, but an arrival that somehow still carries emotion. It's BB King in his quietest passages, Robben Ford in a ballad verse, or the micro-bent melodies of a Bonnie Raitt slow number. The challenge is making a near-silent note still feel like a cry.
steps: Pick the starting note with a whisper-soft attack.
Bend slowly to the target — the slowness matters because a quick bend would destroy the whisper character.
Hold the bent note as long as its quiet sustain allows.
Release quietly; no audible click or finger-drag.
End on a whispered bent root, held to natural silence.
examples: third (whisper pick) → (slow bend to) fifth → (slow release) third
fifth (whisper pick) → (slow bend to) sixth → (hold) → (slow release) fifth
dynamics:whisper_noteTransition:bendTarg.watchOut: Whispered bends lose pitch stability because the string has so little energy. Any unintended finger twitch causes audible pitch wobble. Breathe deliberately and steady the fretting hand against the neck before every bend.

dynamics:accentMap_noteTransition:cleanA.interaction: Accent map with clean attack is how Kenny Burrell plays a jazz standard — a whispered baseline of quarter notes with one unexpected pop of volume per bar. The clean attack makes the accent unmistakable because the surrounding notes are all identically quiet by comparison. The accent is the punchline; everything else is the setup.
steps: Pick a four-note phrase at piano — quiet, even, controlled.
On a pre-chosen beat (often beat 3 of each bar), strike one note at forte — loud and sudden.
Return immediately to piano for the remaining notes of the bar.
Vary which scale degree carries the accent so the phrase stays interesting.
End the round with one final accent on the root at fortissimo.
examples: root (p) → second (p) → third (f — accent) → second (p) | fourth (p) → fifth (p) → third (f — accent) → second (p)
fifth (p) → fourth (p) → fifth (f — accent) → fourth (p) | third (p) → fourth (p) → third (f — accent) → second (p)
dynamics:accentMap_noteTransition:cleanA.watchOut: The accents creep quieter over time because fatigue or habituation makes you forget to hit them hard. The quiet baseline must stay quiet — if the baseline drifts louder, the accent loses its contrast and the whole idea dies.

dynamics:accentMap_noteTransition:hammer.interaction: Accent map with legato is an unusual pairing because accents want sudden force and legato wants the picking hand to be silent. The resolution is that the accent happens exactly on a picked event within a legato line — the legato notes are quiet, and one picked event per bar is the loud accent. It evokes the sudden 'pop' of a fusion guitarist's emphatic note inside an otherwise smooth line.
steps: Play a legato phrase at piano — soft picks, soft hammers.
On a pre-chosen beat, re-pick at fortissimo with a sudden loud attack.
Immediately return to piano for the legato notes that follow.
The accent always happens on a picked event, never a hammered or pulled one.
End on a final accent landing on the root.
examples: root (p pick) → third (hammer p) → fifth (f pick — accent) → fourth (pull-off p) → third (hammer p)
fifth (p pick) → sixth (hammer p) → seventh (f pick — accent) → sixth (pull-off p) → fifth (pull-off p)
dynamics:accentMap_noteTransition:hammer.watchOut: The accent dies because the re-pick's volume is absorbed by the legato surrounding it — the ear's baseline is so soft that even a modest pick attack doesn't feel like an accent. Hit the accent pick attack with real committed force, well above mezzo-forte.

dynamics:accentMap_noteTransition:slideC.interaction: Accent map with slides is a pedal-steel player's game — quiet slide phrases with one sudden loud glide per bar. The loud slide usually travels further and starts from a fresh hard pick. The effect is a gentle atmospheric landscape punctuated by a single declarative gesture. It evokes the ambient-country of Daniel Lanois or a Calexico instrumental's standout moment.
steps: Play three or four quiet slides at piano, each one or two frets in distance.
On a chosen beat, re-pick at fortissimo and slide a longer distance — three frets or more.
Return to quiet slides immediately after the accent.
The accent slide must be picked with sudden force; no crescendo into it.
End with a final accent slide up to the root.
examples: root (p pick) → (slide) second (p) → third (p pick) → (slide) fourth (p) → fifth (f pick) → (long slide) root up an octave (accent) → sixth (p pick)
fifth (p pick) → (slide) fourth → third (p pick) → (slide) second → root (f pick) → (long slide) fifth (accent)
dynamics:accentMap_noteTransition:slideC.watchOut: The accent slide's increased distance makes it naturally slower than a quiet slide, and the volume drop during travel can erase the accent. Strike the accent pick attack hard enough that the whole slide length stays audible.

dynamics:accentMap_noteTransition:bendTa.interaction: Accent map with bends is the blues guitarist's perfect pairing — quiet baseline, one bent cry per bar. Each bent arrival IS the accent, and the phrase has a built-in emotional peak per measure. It's BB King's bar structure distilled: whispered notes setting up a single loud wail. The clarity of this combo is why it's a blues staple.
steps: Pick a three-note phrase at piano, each note clearly audible but soft.
On the last beat of the bar, pick a new note at forte and bend up to the accent target.
Hold the bent accent note with slight vibrato.
Release the bend into the next bar's quiet baseline.
End with a final bent accent on the root up an octave.
examples: root (p) → third (p) → fourth (p) → (f pick, bend to) fifth (accent, held) | (release) fourth (p) → fifth (p) → (f pick, bend to) seventh (accent)
fifth (p) → sixth (p) → fifth (p) → (f pick, bend to) root up an octave (accent) | (release) seventh (p) → (f pick, bend to) second up an octave (accent)
dynamics:accentMap_noteTransition:bendTa.watchOut: The bent accent arrives flat because your bending energy is drained from the contrast with the quiet baseline — a loud pick and a big bend together tire the hand. Rest briefly on the piano notes and recharge the fretting hand so the accent bend has full power.

dynamics:forte_noteTransition:cleanAttac.interaction: Constant forte plus clean attack is the electric rhythm player's driving phrase — every note hit hard, every attack clearly defined, no legato smudging. It's the sound of a punk lead break, a Chuck Berry solo at its most emphatic, or a Dick Dale surf guitar lick where each note is a picked explosion. The full-volume constraint means fatigue is your biggest enemy.
steps: Set the metronome moderately and commit every pick attack to full force from note one.
Use the whole forearm, not just the wrist, to sustain the pick energy across the round.
Keep the fretting hand firm; any pressure drop at forte volume creates buzz.
Ascend and descend through scale passages with unwavering volume.
End on a final forte strike on the root with vibrato.
examples: root → second → third → fourth → fifth → fourth → third → second (all forte, clean picked)
root → third → fifth → seventh → root up an octave → seventh → fifth → third (arpeggio forte)
dynamics:forte_noteTransition:cleanAttac.watchOut: Forte-picking fatigue sets in quickly and your attack degrades to mezzo-forte without your noticing. Record the round and listen — if note 20 is softer than note 1, you've failed the constraint. Build stamina by playing shorter rounds at true forte.

dynamics:forte_noteTransition:hammerPull.interaction: Legato at constant forte is the shred challenge — every hammered and pulled note must be as loud as a picked note, which is physically difficult because legato depends on string sustain that's already decaying from the first pick. It evokes the highest-intensity passages of Allan Holdsworth, John Petrucci, or a Guthrie Govan shred solo where every legato note still punches through the mix.
steps: Pick every first note of a legato cell at fortissimo — more than forte, so the cell has energy to spare.
Hammer with maximum finger force — the fretting hand must strike the fretboard like a hammer.
Pull off with a sharp sideways flick rather than a gentle lift.
Re-pick every two to three notes to refresh sustain; long legato runs won't hold forte.
End on a picked fortissimo note on the root.
examples: root (fff pick) → second → third (f pick) → fourth → fifth (fff pick) → fourth → third (f pick) → second
fifth (fff pick) → sixth → seventh (f pick) → root up an octave → second up an octave (fff pick) → root up an octave
dynamics:forte_noteTransition:hammerPull.watchOut: Despite your best effort, the hammered notes are quieter than the picked notes at forte volume — physics prevents perfect parity. Accept a small volume dip on legato notes and compensate by re-picking more often than you would at lower volumes.

dynamics:forte_noteTransition:slideConne.interaction: Loud slides is a power slide — every glide happens at high volume, which is unusual because slides are typically quiet. The picked onset must be hard enough to sustain the full-volume glide. Think of a Duane Allman electric slide passage at peak intensity, or a George Harrison slide lead hitting its most emphatic statement. The forte context makes the glides feel urgent and declarative.
steps: Strike every pick attack at fortissimo to maximize the string energy.
Slide with firm fretting pressure so the glide doesn't lose volume to friction.
Keep the slides short when possible — long slides at forte volume require huge pick attacks.
Re-pick every slide rather than chaining them, so each glide has a fresh strong onset.
End on a loud slide up to the root with sustained vibrato.
examples: root (fff pick) → (slide) second → third (fff pick) → (slide) fifth → sixth (fff pick) → (slide) root up an octave
fifth (fff pick) → (slide) fourth → third (fff pick) → (slide) second → root (fff pick) → (slide) third
dynamics:forte_noteTransition:slideConne.watchOut: Slides lose most of their volume during the glide because pick energy can't sustain them at forte level for long distances. Re-pick on every slide event or risk the glide ending in silence.

dynamics:forte_noteTransition:bendTarget.interaction: Forte bends are the rock-solo archetype — every note hit hard, every bend a full-force cry. It's Angus Young, Jimmy Page, Slash at their most emphatic. The combination maximizes expressive impact but destroys the fretting hand quickly, so economy of movement matters. This is the sound of the guitar solo's climax.
steps: Pick each bend's start note at fortissimo.
Bend with maximum fretting-hand force to reach the target pitch fast and loud.
Vibrato the target note with wide, confident oscillations.
Release each bend firmly — no fade into silence.
End on a sustained bent forte note on the root up an octave.
examples: third (fff pick) → (bend to) fifth → (release) third → (fff pick) (bend to) fifth (vibrato) → (release) third
fifth (fff pick) → (bend to) seventh → (release) fifth → (fff pick) (bend to) root up an octave (vibrato)
dynamics:forte_noteTransition:bendTarget.watchOut: Fretting-hand fatigue after three or four big forte bends will make bend pitches arrive flat. Build stamina gradually, and save the biggest bends for the end of the round so you have the hand strength when it matters most.

articulation:legato_noteTransition:clean.interaction: All-legato articulation paired with clean-attack note transition is a paradox because legato wants smooth connection and clean attack wants fresh re-striking. The resolution is a connection by sustain: pick every note cleanly, but let each note ring long enough to overlap slightly with the next so the ear hears a connected line even though the picking hand is independent per note. It's classical guitar legato phrasing.
steps: Set the metronome slowly and pick every note with a fresh attack but release fretting pressure late, so the old note rings into the new.
Do not let any silence exist between notes — the slight sustain overlap is what creates legato.
Pick with a softer force than usual so the attacks don't puncture the smooth line.
Play a four-bar scale passage feeling the connected flow despite each note being picked.
End on a long held note on the root.
examples: root → second → third → fourth → fifth (each picked, each overlapping the next via sustain)
fifth → fourth → third → second → root (descending, every note overlapping)
articulation:legato_noteTransition:clean.watchOut: The 'overlap' creates accidental chords when the sustain doesn't die fast enough on the old note. Time the fret lift carefully — release just as the new pick strikes, so there's a microsecond overlap but not a full ring-through.

articulation:legato_noteTransition:hamme.interaction: Legato articulation with hammer-pull transition is the purest legato on guitar — smooth by nature, connected by physics, flowing by intent. It's the Allan Holdsworth ideal, the bossa nova single-line lead, the classical slur gesture. Because both constraints pull the same direction, the challenge isn't making it smooth — it's making it rhythmically precise and musically shaped.
steps: Pick only the first note and let hammer-pull handle everything else.
Keep fretting pressure high and timing precise — every hammered note lands on the beat.
Shape the phrase dynamically even though the picking hand is silent after the first note.
Vary hammer-pull direction so the phrase has melodic variety, not just ascending shapes.
End on a held pulled-off note on the root.
examples: root (pick) → second → third → fourth → fifth → fourth → third → second → root (all legato, one pick)
fifth (pick) → sixth → seventh → root up an octave → seventh → sixth → fifth → fourth (all legato, one pick)
articulation:legato_noteTransition:hamme.watchOut: Volume degradation is the classic legato problem — by note eight, the original pick's sustain is exhausted and the line whispers. Re-pick subtly every four to six notes if the phrase is long, or keep the phrase short enough to live on one pick attack.

articulation:legato_noteTransition:slide.interaction: Legato with slides is pedal-steel liquidity — the phrase becomes one long connected gesture of glides. It evokes Hawaiian slack-key, Indian slide guitar, or the dreamiest Ry Cooder interludes. Every note feels merged with the next, and the constraint doubles down on smoothness — there is absolutely no separation anywhere in the phrase.
steps: Pick the first note firmly and slide through the rest of the phrase with constant fretting pressure.
Let each slide's destination be the next melodic note — no hammer-ons, no pull-offs, only slides.
Keep the glide speeds consistent or rhythmic (e.g. one slide per beat).
Shape dynamics by varying fretting pressure, since picking is happening only once.
End with a final slow slide up to the root, fading to silence.
examples: root (pick) → (slide) third → (slide) fifth → (slide) root up an octave → (slide) seventh → (slide) fifth → (slide) third
fifth (pick) → (slide down) fourth → (slide) third → (slide) second → (slide) root → (slide up) third
articulation:legato_noteTransition:slide.watchOut: The pitch dies mid-slide at slow tempi because fretting pressure drifts during the long glide. You must maintain constant, slightly exaggerated pressure — the string has to keep moving the whole time.

articulation:legato_noteTransition:bendT.interaction: Legato with bends is a continuous vocal phrase — every target is reached through a bend, and the phrase is one connected gesture of rising and falling pitch tension. It's the extreme of vocal phrasing on guitar, evoking David Gilmour's most lyrical passages or a Santana ballad's most emotive bent line. Every note bleeds into the next through bend releases and fresh bends.
steps: Pick the first note and immediately bend up to the first target.
Release the bend smoothly — the release itself is the transition to the next note.
Re-pick only when necessary to set up a new bent target; otherwise the fretting hand carries the whole phrase.
Vary bend distances — half-step, whole-step, step-and-a-half — for melodic interest.
End on a held bent root up an octave with sustained vibrato.
examples: third (pick) → (bend to) fifth → (release to) third → (bend to) fifth → (re-pick, bend to) seventh
fifth (pick) → (bend to) seventh → (release to) fifth → (bend to) root up an octave → (release to) fifth
articulation:legato_noteTransition:bendT.watchOut: The phrase decays in volume across multiple bend-releases because the original pick's sustain runs out. Re-pick surreptitiously after every two to three bend events so the line stays audible.

articulation:staccato_noteTransition:cle.interaction: All-staccato with clean attack is the mandolin tremolo of punctuation — every note short, every attack fresh, and the space between notes is part of the rhythm. It's the sound of a Chet Atkins staccato fingerpicking passage, or a tight funk lead line where every note pops. The constraint rewards wrist precision and picking-hand control.
steps: Pick each note with a fresh downstroke and mute the string instantly with your fretting finger.
The rest after each note must be clearly audible — if you can't hear a gap, you're not staccato.
Keep the note durations equal; don't let some notes ring longer than others.
Ascend and descend through scale passages maintaining the pop-and-rest pattern.
End on one final staccato note on the root.
examples: root (pick, mute) → second (pick, mute) → third (pick, mute) → fourth (pick, mute) — each a pop with a gap
fifth (pop) → fourth (pop) → third (pop) → second (pop) — descending staccato line
articulation:staccato_noteTransition:cle.watchOut: The mute arrives late and one or two notes ring out like normal notes. Your fretting finger must lift the moment the pick has struck — the muscle coordination is subtle. Drill it slowly at first.

articulation:staccato_noteTransition:ham.interaction: Staccato with legato is a contradiction — legato wants connected notes and staccato wants separated notes. The resolution is brief hammered cells where you mute the string immediately after the cell — so the notes inside the cell are connected, but the cells themselves are staccato events. It's an unusual, punchy texture reminiscent of fusion players placing short bursts.
steps: Pick a two-note legato cell (pick-then-hammer) and mute immediately after the hammered note speaks.
Leave a clear rest, then pick a new two-note cell.
Keep the cells very short — two notes max — so they can be muted cleanly.
Alternate ascending and descending cells for variety.
End on a picked staccato note on the root.
examples: root (pick) → third (hammer, mute) — rest — fifth (pick) → seventh (hammer, mute) — rest
fifth (pick) → fourth (pull-off, mute) — rest — third (pick) → second (pull-off, mute)
articulation:staccato_noteTransition:ham.watchOut: The mute cuts the hammered note too fast and you lose the second note entirely. Let the hammer speak for a tiny fraction of a second before muting — just long enough for the ear to register both notes.

articulation:staccato_noteTransition:sli.interaction: Staccato sliding is a paradox that resolves into short, choppy glides — each slide is one or two frets and is muted immediately after the target is reached. It's the sound of a pedal-steel percussive gesture, or a surf guitar staccato lead with pitch wobbles. The combination feels rhythmically jagged and pitch-smooth simultaneously.
steps: Pick a note, slide one or two frets, and mute the string the instant the slide arrives at target.
The rest after the muted slide must be clearly audible.
Vary slide directions bar by bar — up, down, up, down — to create rhythmic interest.
Don't let any slide ring beyond its target note.
End on a staccato slide up to the root.
examples: root (pick) → (slide) second (mute) — rest — third (pick) → (slide) fourth (mute) — rest
fifth (pick) → (slide down) fourth (mute) — rest — third (pick) → (slide down) second (mute)
articulation:staccato_noteTransition:sli.watchOut: The slide's pitch travel isn't audible because you muted too fast. The listener needs to hear the glide, even briefly, before the mute. Slow the slide slightly so the pitch-change is part of the event.

articulation:staccato_noteTransition:ben.interaction: Staccato bending is a hyper-punctuated blues lick — each bent arrival is immediately muted and followed by silence. It's the texture of a tightly phrased BB King line or an Albert Collins funk-blues lead where every bent note is a sudden emphatic event. The combination feels like the guitar is shouting single words instead of singing phrases.
steps: Pick a starting note, bend up to the target, and mute the string immediately at the bent arrival.
Leave a clear rest before the next pick event.
Keep the bend distance short — half-step bends work best for fast staccato pops.
Alternate bent and non-bent staccato events for rhythmic variation.
End with a staccato bent root up an octave.
examples: third (pick) → (bend to) fifth (mute) — rest — fourth (pick) → (bend to) sixth (mute) — rest
fifth (pick) → (bend to) seventh (mute) — rest — sixth (pick) → (bend to) root up an octave (mute)
articulation:staccato_noteTransition:ben.watchOut: The mute cuts the bend before it reaches target pitch, so the listener hears a flat partial bend. Wait the tiniest fraction of a second at the bend's arrival before muting — the target must be audible even if only briefly.

articulation:slurredPairs_noteTransition.interaction: Slurred pairs with clean attack is the classical guitar 'portato' shape — two notes connected, a tiny gap, two more notes connected. It's the phrasing of Sor and Tárrega études, the subtle shaping of Segovia's scale work. The clean attack means each note is picked, but the pairing creates a breathing pattern that shapes the phrase at the sub-beat level.
steps: Pick every note cleanly, but release pressure after every second note to create a tiny gap.
The first note of each pair rings slightly longer than it would in a continuous flow.
The second note of each pair is followed by an audible micro-rest.
Move through scale passages in grouped twos, so the phrase breathes at regular intervals.
End on a single held note on the root after a final pair.
examples: [root → second] (pair) — (gap) — [third → fourth] (pair) — (gap) — [fifth → sixth] (pair)
[fifth → fourth] — (gap) — [third → second] — (gap) — [root → seventh] (descending pairs)
articulation:slurredPairs_noteTransition.watchOut: The gap between pairs drifts into silence that's too long, and the phrase starts sounding like staccato pairs rather than slurred pairs with micro-breaths. Keep the gap tiny — a true grace-of-silence, not a full rest.

articulation:slurredPairs_noteTransition.interaction: Slurred pairs with hammer-pull is the textbook legato exercise — pick-hammer, tiny gap, pick-hammer, tiny gap. It's how classical guitar students first learn to slur, and how jazz players subtly shape their lines without picking everything. Each pair is a single legato gesture, and the gaps between pairs give the phrase breath.
steps: Pick the first note of each pair and hammer the second.
After the second note, lift the fretting finger to create a tiny silence.
Pick a new first note for the next pair and hammer its second.
Move through scale passages in grouped twos, always pick-then-hammer.
End on a pick-hammer pair that lands on the root.
examples: [root (pick) → third (hammer)] — (gap) — [third (pick) → fifth (hammer)] — (gap) — [fifth (pick) → seventh (hammer)]
[fifth (pick) → third (pull-off)] — (gap) — [fourth (pick) → second (pull-off)] — (gap) — [third (pick) → root (pull-off)]
articulation:slurredPairs_noteTransition.watchOut: The pairs become uneven — some pairs' hammered note is loud, others inaudible, depending on pick strength. Practice each pair identically so the pairing feel stays consistent.

articulation:slurredPairs_noteTransition.interaction: Slurred pairs with slides is a rare combination — pick-slide pair, tiny gap, pick-slide pair. Each pair is a single glide gesture, and the gaps create a breathing architecture. It evokes the phrasing of a pedal-steel melody where each pair is a short swoop. The resulting texture is pedagogically valuable because it trains slide-and-rest coordination.
steps: Pick a note and slide one or two frets to its pair partner; lift briefly so there's a tiny gap.
Pick a new note and slide again; another tiny gap.
Keep the slides short so they fit inside a 'pair' gesture.
Move through scale passages in grouped twos, each pair being a pick-and-slide.
End on a final pair that lands on the root.
examples: [root (pick) → (slide) second] — (gap) — [third (pick) → (slide) fourth] — (gap) — [fifth (pick) → (slide) sixth]
[fifth (pick) → (slide down) fourth] — (gap) — [third (pick) → (slide down) second] — (gap) — [root (pick) → (slide up) third]
articulation:slurredPairs_noteTransition.watchOut: The slides within each pair lose audibility because the pick attack is soft and the slide is fast. Commit fully to the glide even inside a short pair — otherwise the pair sounds like two picked notes.

articulation:slurredPairs_noteTransition.interaction: Slurred pairs with bends — pick-bend pair, tiny gap, pick-bend pair — is the blues guitarist's 'crying pairs' vocabulary. Each pair is a single bent gesture, and the pauses between create a conversational phrasing. It's how BB King builds slow-burn solos: small bent statements separated by breath. Pedagogically it trains bend-and-release rhythmic precision.
steps: Pick a note and bend up to the pair target; hold briefly.
Release the bend and lift for a tiny rest.
Pick a new note and bend to a new pair target.
Keep the pairs consistent in their bend distance and timing.
End on a final bent pair that lands on the root up an octave.
examples: [third (pick) → (bend to) fifth] — (gap) — [fourth (pick) → (bend to) sixth] — (gap) — [fifth (pick) → (bend to) seventh]
[fifth (pick) → (bend to) sixth] — (gap) — [sixth (pick) → (bend to) seventh] — (gap) — [seventh (pick) → (bend to) root up an octave]
articulation:slurredPairs_noteTransition.watchOut: The bend pairs become uneven — some bends arrive true, others flat — because fretting-hand fatigue accumulates. Practice each pair with the same bend-distance and rhythmic timing, and rest briefly if the hand starts to fail.

articulation:tenuto_noteTransition:clean.interaction: Tenuto with clean attack is the maximally weighted phrase — every note held for its full beat, every attack fresh. It's the sound of a solemn classical guitar passage, a slow jazz ballad's single-note melody, or a Bach chorale transcription. Each note carries equal gravity, and the clean attack gives each its own individual commitment.
steps: Pick every note with conviction and hold the fret through its full rhythmic value.
No note dies before its beat ends — sustain is key.
Let each note bleed slightly into the next via overlap, but don't let it ring into the following beat's territory.
Feel the weight of each note as if the phrase were a series of weighted steps.
End on a long held note on the root.
examples: root (held full beat) → second (held) → third (held) → fourth (held) → fifth (held, long)
fifth (held) → fourth (held) → third (held) → second (held) → root (held, long)
articulation:tenuto_noteTransition:clean.watchOut: Notes start dying before their beat is over because fretting pressure relaxes. Tenuto demands sustained pressure — check that the note is still ringing when the next attack arrives, and press slightly harder if it isn't.

articulation:tenuto_noteTransition:hamme.interaction: Tenuto legato is a slow, heavy line where every note is held fully and connected smoothly. It's the sound of a classical slow movement, a ballad's most lyrical phrase, or a meditative Eastern-music transcription. The picking hand is mostly silent; the fretting hand carries the whole weighted melody.
steps: Pick the first note with firm pressure and hold it through its full beat.
Hammer on the next note just as the beat changes, and hold it through its full beat.
Continue hammering and pulling in rhythm, each note fully sustained for its allotted duration.
Shape the phrase dynamically via fretting-hand pressure variations.
End on a long pulled-off final note on the root.
examples: root (pick, held) → third (hammer, held) → fifth (hammer, held) → third (pull-off, held) → root (pull-off, held long)
fifth (pick, held) → sixth (hammer, held) → seventh (hammer, held) → sixth (pull-off, held) → fifth (pull-off, held long)
articulation:tenuto_noteTransition:hamme.watchOut: The held notes fade because the initial pick can't sustain multiple full-beat notes of legato. Re-pick on every second or third note to refresh the sustain and preserve the tenuto weight.

articulation:tenuto_noteTransition:slide.interaction: Tenuto slides is the pedal-steel's slow-motion melody — every note held for its full duration, and the transitions are long slow glides. It evokes a Daniel Lanois atmosphere, a Hawaiian slack-key ballad, or the opening bars of a Calexico desert-noir soundtrack cue. The slides fill the held-beat architecture with pitch travel.
steps: Pick a note and let it ring for most of its beat before starting the slide.
Slide into the next note so the slide arrives right on the next beat.
Hold the arrived note through its full beat, then slide again.
Every slide must fit inside the final part of a beat, no earlier and no later.
End on a final held slide up to the root.
examples: root (pick, held) → (late-beat slide up) third (held) → (late-beat slide up) fifth (held) → (late-beat slide up) root up an octave
fifth (pick, held) → (late-beat slide down) third (held) → (late-beat slide down) root (held long)
articulation:tenuto_noteTransition:slide.watchOut: The slides start too early and the previous note gets cut short, violating tenuto. Time the slide initiation carefully — the pitch travel should happen in the last third of each beat, not the middle.

articulation:tenuto_noteTransition:bendT.interaction: Tenuto with bends is the most vocal slow phrase possible on guitar — every note held for its full duration, each note reached via a bend that takes most of the beat to complete. It's the archetype of an Albert King slow-blues phrase, or a David Gilmour 'Shine On' climactic bend. The combination demands both fretting-hand endurance and precise bend timing.
steps: Pick the starting low note with force and begin a slow bend that reaches target pitch by the end of the beat.
Hold the bent target through its full beat.
Release slowly or re-pick a new start note and bend again.
Let each phrase-bend breathe — the weight comes from the duration.
End on a held bent root up an octave with wide vibrato.
examples: third (pick, begin bend) → (arriving on beat 2) fifth (held beat 2) → (pick, begin bend) → (arriving on beat 3) sixth (held)
fifth (pick, begin bend) → (arriving) seventh (held) → (pick, begin bend) → (arriving) root up an octave (held long)
articulation:tenuto_noteTransition:bendT.watchOut: The bend arrives too fast and the held portion of the note is too long, which sounds static. Calibrate the bend speed so the travel and the hold have roughly equal weight within the beat — that's what gives tenuto bends their drama.

phraseLength:1bar_noteTransition:cleanAt.interaction: Single-bar phrases with clean attack is the haiku of guitar playing — say one thing in one bar, then stop. Every note is picked cleanly so the phrase is perfectly legible, and the brevity forces you to choose content carefully. It evokes the sparse phrasing of Miles Davis' coolest ballads translated to guitar, or a Bill Frisell minimalist single-bar melody.
steps: Plan the phrase in your head before playing — four to six notes in one bar.
Pick each note cleanly with clear articulation; no slurs or slides or bends.
End the phrase on a strong beat and let the final note ring until silent.
Rest completely between bars — no overflow into the next bar.
Change the phrase for each bar so no two are identical.
examples: [root → third → fifth → third] (1-bar phrase, clean) — silence — [next 1-bar phrase]
[second → fourth → sixth → fourth] (1-bar phrase) — silence — [fifth → fourth → third → fourth]
phraseLength:1bar_noteTransition:cleanAt.watchOut: The phrase overruns the bar because you weren't counting. One bar is a strict container — if the phrase has six notes at a medium tempo, some of them will be rushed. Plan the note count to fit the tempo, not the other way around.

phraseLength:1bar_noteTransition:hammerP.interaction: A single-bar legato phrase is the shred fragment — pick once, legato through a compact one-bar run, rest completely. It's the density-and-silence contrast that makes shred licks feel punchy. Think of a Yngwie Malmsteen micro-lick, a Buckethead one-bar flourish, or a Guthrie Govan compact fusion statement. The constraint is the bar boundary.
steps: Pick the first note of the bar and let hammer-pull carry the rest of the phrase.
Plan the number of notes to fit exactly one bar — usually five to eight legato notes at medium tempo.
End the phrase before the bar boundary; the bar-end must be silent.
Rest completely in the next bar — no legato bleed-over.
Change the phrase for each bar so the micro-licks stay fresh.
examples: [root (pick) → second → third → fourth → fifth → fourth → third] (1-bar legato) — silence — [new 1-bar phrase]
[fifth (pick) → sixth → seventh → root up an octave → seventh → sixth] (1-bar legato) — silence
phraseLength:1bar_noteTransition:hammerP.watchOut: The legato run's sustain runs out before the bar ends and the final notes whisper into the silence. Either shorten the run or strike the first pick harder than normal — the entire bar's worth of notes must survive on that single attack.

phraseLength:1bar_noteTransition:slideCo.interaction: One bar is almost too short for a slide to breathe — so the slide itself becomes the phrase's whole identity. Instead of playing a motif that happens to contain a slide, you're playing a slide that happens to land on a note. Think Tinariwen or a single Khruangbin lick tossed off in the space between vocal lines: one gesture, one arrival, done.
steps: Set the metronome to a slow-to-medium tempo and commit to dropping your hand off the neck every bar — the silence is part of the phrase.
Choose a target degree (start with the third or the fifth) and begin your finger two or three frets below it on the same string.
Pick once, let your fretting finger slide up to the target, and land cleanly on the downbeat or the and-of-two — the destination note must arrive in time, not the departure.
Vary the slide distance round to round: short half-step creeps vs long four-fret sweeps, and notice how the phrase character shifts from vocal sigh to pedal-steel swoop.
Anchor the last phrase of the round by sliding UP into the root from below and letting it ring — that's your bar-by-bar period.
examples: (silent pickup) → slide into the third → rest (single gesture, voice-like entry)
the second → slide up into the fifth → the fourth → rest (wet landing on the bright note)
phraseLength:1bar_noteTransition:slideCo.watchOut: The trap is sliding AROUND inside the bar instead of sliding INTO the bar's one target. If you hear three notes inside the bar, the phrase length is fighting you. Also: don't let the slide's start pitch become the 'real' note — the arrival is what matters.
phraseLength:1bar_noteTransition:slideCo.deeperInsight: On guitar the slide is the closest thing we have to portamento in a singer's voice — a one-bar fragment with one slide is essentially a sung syllable.

phraseLength:1bar_noteTransition:bendTar.interaction: A one-bar phrase with a bend is a haiku about tension and release. There's no room to set it up, follow it through, and also land — so the bend IS the phrase. This is classic B.B. King 'speech' phrasing: pick, bend, arrive, shut up. The silence around it lets the ear hear the bend as a sentence rather than a fill.
steps: Pick a target degree inside your scale (the third and the fifth are the most expressive to bend into) and identify a fret one whole step lower on the same string.
Put down the metronome click and practice the bend itself unpitched first — match the target by ear against the drone before you add timing.
Now with click: pick the lower note on beat one, bend up to the target and hold until the next downbeat, then stop.
Alternate two round shapes: 'bend and hold' vs 'bend, arrive, immediately release back down' — both fit in one bar and feel totally different.
End each bar with total silence — let the ear feel the phrase's edge.
examples: (bend) the second → the third → rest (B.B. King whole-step arrival)
the fourth → (bend) the fourth → the fifth → rest (half-step bend, pleading quality)
phraseLength:1bar_noteTransition:bendTar.watchOut: The most common failure is under-bending — your hand puts in the effort but the pitch arrives flat of the target, which sounds like intonation weakness instead of expression. Sing the target note before you bend, then match with your fingers. Also: don't cram a second lick into the bar because the bend felt 'too simple'.
phraseLength:1bar_noteTransition:bendTar.deeperInsight: A one-bar bend is a test of your internal pitch memory — there's no other note to check against within the phrase, only the drone and the silence.

phraseLength:2bar_noteTransition:cleanAt.interaction: Two bars of complete sentences with every note freshly struck is the old-school surf and country school of playing — Dick Dale, James Burton, Chet Atkins. No legato cheating, no sliding over the seams. The phrase gets its personality from rhythm and picking articulation, not from connective tissue. You're forced to rely on timing and note choice to carry meaning.
steps: Set the metronome and internally count two-bar units — land the final note of each phrase on a strong beat, then leave the second bar's ending breathable.
Pick every single note fresh: no hammer-ons, no pull-offs, no slides. Each finger arrives on the fret before the pick hits.
Front-load the sentence: get the key idea out in bar one, then answer or resolve it in bar two (question, answer).
Vary the shape: first pass, ascending statement then descending answer; second pass, arch shape that peaks mid-phrase-two.
Listen for the gaps between notes — if two notes overlap because the left hand was lazy lifting off, you lost the clean attack.
examples: the root → the third → the fifth → the sixth | the fifth → the third → the second → the root (classic antecedent-consequent)
the fifth → the seventh → the root up an octave → rest | the sixth → the fifth → the third → the root (peak then fall)
phraseLength:2bar_noteTransition:cleanAt.watchOut: Without legato to lean on, poor timing gets exposed immediately. Every late note is naked. Also watch for unintentional ringing — cleanly struck means cleanly MUTED between notes, so use your fretting fingertips to lift cleanly and your palm to dampen adjacent strings.
phraseLength:2bar_noteTransition:cleanAt.deeperInsight: Clean attack forces the fretting and picking hands to synchronize to the millisecond; it's one of the hardest tests of guitar timekeeping.

phraseLength:2bar_noteTransition:hammerP.interaction: Legato turns a two-bar sentence into a single exhaled breath. Because one pick attack can feed four or five sounded notes, your phrase gets vocal continuity — think Allman Brothers, Jerry Garcia, or the smoother corners of Jerry Reed. The trick is that the TIMING of hammers and pulls is sloppy by default, so the constraint is as much about rhythmic discipline as it is about economy.
steps: Tune the metronome in and divide the two bars into clear eighth-note or triplet grids — you'll play against that grid with legato.
Pick only the first note of each small group (three-note or four-note cells are typical); let hammer-ons and pull-offs sound the rest.
Make the hammer-on and pull-off equally loud as the picked note — if the picked note is 100 and the hammered note is 60, the phrase sags.
Shape the phrase across both bars: maybe bar one rises through hammered groups, bar two falls through pulled groups.
End the phrase with a clean, picked note on the root or fifth to give the two-bar sentence a closing consonant.
examples: (pick) the root → h→the second → h→the third | (pick) the fifth → p→the fourth → p→the third → the root (ascending hammer then descending pull)
(pick) the third → h→the fifth → p→the third | (pick) the second → h→the fourth → p→the second → the root (pull-off resolving to home)
phraseLength:2bar_noteTransition:hammerP.watchOut: Legato notes always get quieter on the way down — your pull-offs will be whispers unless you actively 'pluck' the string with the lifting finger. And don't let hammer-ons become a timing loophole; the beat a hammer lands on must be as honest as a picked one.
phraseLength:2bar_noteTransition:hammerP.deeperInsight: A two-bar legato phrase is where guitarists first learn that fretting-hand force has to match picking-hand force in dB, not just in effort.

phraseLength:2bar_noteTransition:slideCo.interaction: Two bars gives a slide room to actually travel. Now you're in the territory of Duane Allman country-blues, Ry Cooder bottleneck, or Bibio's hazy Khruangbin-adjacent lines — phrases where the slide is the connective tissue between one idea and its answer. Because you have two bars, you can set up a note, slide to a second idea, and still have time to land home.
steps: Loop two bars on the click and plan a three-note sketch: starting point, slide target, resolution.
Pick the starting note clean, slide from it to the target across a measurable beat (usually beat 3 or beat 4 of bar one is the most vocal spot for the glide).
Let bar two land the resolution — it can be a new picked note or another short slide to the root.
Vary the slide duration: a fast scoop vs a slow glissando paint very different moods in the same phrase length.
Round out by using the last beat of bar two as breath — a slide phrase needs its silence as much as a sung one does.
examples: the root → slide up to the fifth (across beats 3-4 of bar 1) → the fourth → the third → the root (lap-steel arc)
the fifth → the sixth → slide down to the third → the second → the root (descending sigh)
phraseLength:2bar_noteTransition:slideCo.watchOut: The biggest pitfall is slides that don't land on a beat — they smear into the middle of a measure and the listener loses the phrase grid. Set the slide's arrival to a strong beat, not a random moment. Also: watch for pressure dropping mid-slide; the finger must stay planted or the pitch noise turns into dead string.
phraseLength:2bar_noteTransition:slideCo.deeperInsight: The sliding gesture mimics vocal consonants more than vowels — the 'attack' of a slide is the departure note, not the arrival.

phraseLength:2bar_noteTransition:bendTar.interaction: Two bars is where bending starts to tell stories instead of just punctuating. You have time for a setup note, a bend into a target, a hold to let it breathe, and a resolution. This is Gilmour territory, or early Clapton — phrases where the bend isn't a lick but a whole line's center of gravity. The two bars give the bent target time to MEAN something.
steps: Sketch the phrase on paper first: pickup note, bent target, what happens after the release.
Pick the setup note on beat 1, bend into the target, land the target on beat 2 or 3, and hold through to the bar line.
In bar two, either release the bend and descend through the scale, or rebend into a higher target for a double-climb.
Do one pass with a whole-step bend (into the third from the second, or into the root from the seventh) and one with a half-step bend — very different emotional temperatures.
Close each two-bar unit with a clean picked resolution so the bend doesn't eat the phrase's ending.
examples: the root → the second → (bend) the second → the third (held) | the third → the second → the root (release and home)
the fourth → the fifth → (bend) the sixth → the seventh (held) | the root up an octave → the fifth → the third (Gilmour ascent)
phraseLength:2bar_noteTransition:bendTar.watchOut: Bends that are too fast (whip-snap) sound rushed inside a two-bar phrase — the target should arrive on the beat but the travel TIME matters. Also: once you release a bend, the re-pitched open note often sounds flat because your finger is already tired. Practice holding the bent pitch against the drone for three full counts.
phraseLength:2bar_noteTransition:bendTar.deeperInsight: A two-bar phrase centered on one bend is one of the oldest blues rhetorical forms: 'statement → cry → answer'.

phraseLength:4bar_noteTransition:cleanAt.interaction: Four bars of all-picked notes is where guitar starts to feel like speech rather than gesture. Every note is its own word. Think bebop heads, Django's gypsy lines, or a Kacey Musgraves guitar solo — where every note is intentional and nothing is smeared. The constraint exposes motor control and demands you be a good melodist, because you can't hide behind a slick slide.
steps: Chart the four bars as a paragraph: bar 1 states, bar 2 develops, bar 3 peaks, bar 4 resolves.
Commit to picking every note and use your fretting hand as a clean on-off switch — no pitch bleeds between notes.
Keep tempo medium — cleanliness at speed comes later; for now, give every note its own beat or subdivision.
Use space: leaving a beat of silence in bar 2 or bar 3 will make the final bar feel like an arrival rather than a collapse.
Finish on the root, third, or fifth on a strong beat — four bars without legato needs a strong landing to feel finished.
examples: the root → the second → the third → the fourth | the fifth → the third → the root → rest | the second → the third → the fifth → the sixth | the fifth → the third → the second → the root (four-bar arch)

the fifth → the sixth → the seventh → the root up an octave | the fifth → the third → the root → rest | the fourth → the fifth → the sixth → the fifth | the third → the second → the root → rest (rise, fall, rise, home)
phraseLength:4bar_noteTransition:cleanAt.watchOut: With no connective tissue, four bars is long enough to lose the grid — the third bar is where timing usually sags. Practice to a metronome and specifically check beat one of every bar. Also watch for the classic 'I ran out of ideas at bar 3' collapse; plan the whole paragraph before you play it.
phraseLength:4bar_noteTransition:cleanAt.deeperInsight: Four bars of clean-attack lines is the fundamental unit of bebop vocabulary — it's why every jazz method book eventually demands it.

phraseLength:4bar_noteTransition:hammerP.interaction: Four bars of legato is where the cascading Allman/Derek Trucks sound lives — long sinuous lines that seem like one continuous breath. The constraint teaches endurance in the fretting hand and evenness across many notes per pick. This is the shape where your left-hand 'touch' starts to become a recognizable voice.
steps: Outline the four bars as legato groups of 3, 4, or 6 notes — plan which notes get picked and which get hammered/pulled.
Pick only the first note of each group; keep fretting-hand velocity high so every sounded note matches the picked note in volume.
Stretch the line: if bar 1 is a three-note rising cell, bar 2 is a four-note rising cell, bar 3 peaks, bar 4 descends with pull-offs.
Keep your picking hand relaxed — its job is just to refresh volume at the start of each group, not to carry the phrase.
End with a picked root or fifth on a strong beat so the paragraph has a period instead of a fade-out.
examples: (pick) the root → h→the second → h→the third | (pick) the third → h→the fifth → p→the third | (pick) the fourth → h→the sixth → p→the fourth | (pick) the third → p→the second → the root (rising groups, descending home)
