Pay attention to the slur across the 2-cell — because it's shorter, the pair will feel rushed. Good — that's the tension this combo teaches.
Vary the contour of the pairs: step-up, step-down, skip-up, neighbor-back.
On the final bar, slur all six notes into one continuous line to feel the release of the pair constraint.
examples: (root-second) (third-second) (fourth-third) — three slurred pairs descending and settling
(fifth-sixth) (seventh-root up an octave) (seventh-fifth) — ascending pair pattern with a descending resolution pair
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_articulation:slur.watchOut: Slurred pairs become indistinct at speed — the tiny gap between pairs is what makes them PAIRS and not a legato blur. If you can't hear the gap, slow down until you can.
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_articulation:slur.deeperInsight: The pair-vs-triple friction is exactly the source of Brazilian samba's melodic pull — straight pairs over triplet rhythm — except tresillo makes it even more uneven.

rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_articulation:tenu.interaction: Tenuto asks every note to hold its full length with weight; tresillo asks for rhythmic asymmetry. When you hold each note for exactly its slot's duration — so the first 3-cell note rings for 3/8, the second rings for 3/8, and the 2-cell note rings for 2/8 — the resulting phrase feels like a chorale sung over a clave. Pedagogically this is about honoring rhythm cells with sustained commitment, not just articulating their attacks.
steps: Set the drone and assign yourself to hold each note exactly through its tresillo cell — no note leaves until the next cell begins.
Focus on the '2' cell: it's the shortest, and the temptation is to cut it off early or let it ring longer. Match its duration exactly.
Play the same three-pitch shape for eight bars until the hold-lengths feel automatic.
Then introduce tiny micro-dynamics: push slightly louder into the middle of each tenuto hold, ease off just before the next cell begins.
Finish with a final bar where you sustain only the 2-cell note into silence — the long goodbye.
examples: root [hold 3] → third [hold 3] → fifth [hold 2] (bracketed numbers are cell durations in eighths)
fifth [hold 3] → third [hold 3] → root [hold 2] (descending tenuto tresillo with final cell cut to match)
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_articulation:tenu.watchOut: Tenuto collapses into legato if you don't breathe between notes — there should still be a micro-moment where the old note ends and the new one begins, even though the old note filled its full slot. Rearticulate cleanly.
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_articulation:tenu.deeperInsight: Tenuto is the hidden opposite of staccato: instead of carving out silence, it makes you commit to every millisecond of sound — and tresillo rewards that commitment with immediate groove.

rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_phraseLength:1bar.interaction: A single tresillo bar is already a complete statement — three cells, one idea. Constraining phrases to one bar removes all narrative sprawl and forces you to make each bar self-contained, like a one-line haiku. This is the logic of a J Dilla beat loop: the whole world inside a two-second cell, repeated for effect.
steps: Play one full tresillo bar with three notes, then stop completely for the next bar.
In the silence, DO NOT mentally replay what you just did — let the space be empty.
Play a different one-bar phrase, then stop again. The silence is half of the exercise.
Aim for six one-bar phrases in the round, each different from the last in at least one dimension (pitch shape, register, or direction).
End by repeating your favorite of the six across a final bar, then silence.
examples: root → fifth → third (a single declarative bar, then rest)
seventh → sixth → fifth (one descending statement, complete in itself)
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_phraseLength:1bar.watchOut: The instinct to chain phrases together is enormous — the first phrase makes you want to answer it immediately. Treat each bar as if it stands alone; the silence is the punctuation.
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_phraseLength:1bar.deeperInsight: Learning to let a one-bar phrase breathe is the hardest lesson in Afro-Cuban phrasing — musicians are trained to fill time, and this exercise un-trains them.

rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_phraseLength:2bar.interaction: Two bars of tresillo gives you a question-and-answer shape — bar one is the call, bar two is the response, and the asymmetric 3+3+2 pulse drives forward momentum between them. This is the natural size for Afro-Cuban montunos, Brazilian forró hooks, and most reggaetón pre-choruses. The second bar can echo, invert, or resolve the first.
steps: Play a tresillo phrase in bar one — three notes on the 3+3+2 accents with a clear melodic shape.
In bar two, RESPOND to it — same rhythm grid, but pitches that answer the first bar (step down if bar one stepped up, land on a stable tone if bar one ended on an unstable one).
The second bar should arrive at a resolution point — usually the root or the fifth on the short 2-cell.
Play four such two-bar sentences in a round, each one a distinct call-and-response pair.
For the final pair, make the response an inversion of the call — same contour upside down.
examples: bar 1: root → third → fifth; bar 2: sixth → fourth → second (ascending call, descending response)
bar 1: fifth → seventh → root up an octave; bar 2: seventh → fifth → root (reaching call, settling response)
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_phraseLength:2bar.watchOut: The response bar usually just copies the call bar with slightly different pitches — too similar. The response should feel like it FINISHES the call, not parrot it. Aim for clear resolution.
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_phraseLength:2bar.deeperInsight: Two-bar call-and-response is the pedagogical unit of all Afro-diasporic music — it's the smallest container for genuine musical conversation.

rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_phraseLength:4bar.interaction: Four bars of tresillo is room for real development — a setup, a complication, a climax, a release. This is the phrase length of a verse line in a reggaetón song or a full Khruangbin guitar riff. With four bars you can finally build dynamic and melodic arcs on top of the rhythmic grid instead of just running scales through it.
steps: Plan a four-bar shape before playing: bar 1 = introduction (simple), bar 2 = development (more movement), bar 3 = climax (peak note or widest leap), bar 4 = resolution (back to root).
Play the shape keeping three notes per bar on the 3+3+2 accents.
In bar 3 put your peak or your highest contour point on the short 2-cell so the climax syncopates.
In bar 4 land the final note of the 2-cell on the root or the fifth to feel the closure.
Repeat the same four-bar shape twice, then try the same arc inverted (starting high and ending high with a dip in the middle).
examples: bars 1-4: root-third-fifth → third-fifth-seventh → fifth-root-third → root-second-root (build, develop, peak, resolve)
bars 1-4: sixth-fifth-fourth → fourth-third-second → second-fourth-seventh → seventh-fifth-root (descent, further descent, upward turn, home)
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_phraseLength:4bar.watchOut: Four bars is long enough that players run out of ideas around bar 3 and start repeating themselves. Plan your peak BEFORE starting — knowing where you're going lets bar 3 actually land.
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_phraseLength:4bar.deeperInsight: The four-bar arc is the smallest container in which a tresillo phrase can genuinely tell a story — shorter ones are fragments, longer ones need sub-arcs.

rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_phraseLength:8bar.interaction: Eight bars of tresillo is a full narrative — opening, development, climax, denouement — and it's the length of a Khruangbin instrumental verse or a full reggaetón hook. At this scale, the 3+3+2 pulse becomes texture rather than feature; you can stop thinking about each cell and start shaping a real melodic architecture with multiple internal peaks and valleys.
steps: Map out your eight bars before playing: bars 1-2 introduce the idea, bars 3-4 develop it, bars 5-6 peak, bars 7-8 resolve.
Set your highest pitch (your melodic climax) on the short 2-cell of bar 6 — that creates the biggest 'lift' moment in the arc.
Use bars 1-2 low in the register and bars 7-8 returning to a middle register; let the arc do the work.
Allow ONE moment of rhythmic deviation — e.g., a held note across a cell — in bars 5-6 to mark the climax as special.
The final bar 8 should end on the root on the short 2-cell so the phrase bleeds into a natural silence.
If it all goes off the rails, restart from the top; eight-bar arcs need to be heard whole.
examples: bars 1-2 low root/third/fifth; bars 3-4 rising fourth/fifth/sixth; bars 5-6 peak root up an octave; bars 7-8 returning third → root
bars 1-2 fifth/fourth/third; bars 3-4 second/root/seventh; bars 5-6 peak leap to sixth/root up octave/fifth; bars 7-8 third/second/root
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_phraseLength:8bar.watchOut: The middle bars (4-5) are where players lose the thread and revert to noodling. Keep the arc alive through those bars by thinking of them as the 'rising action' phase — they're not filler, they're the build.
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_phraseLength:8bar.deeperInsight: Eight bars is the container where rhythm stops being the subject and becomes the stage on which melody performs — tresillo practitioners learn to treat the rhythm as furniture rather than focus.

rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_register:chest.interaction: Chest voice is low, weighted, and locked into sternum vibration; tresillo's groove is propulsive and rhythmically exposed. Singing chest voice on the 3+3+2 accents teaches you to commit bodily to each rhythmic cell — the weight of the voice has to be present AND the rhythm has to punch. This is the Tinariwen desert-blues vocal posture, or the Caetano Veloso porch-baritone approach.
steps: Rest your palm flat on your sternum; sing one sustained chest-voice root note and feel the vibration under your hand.
Now sing three chest-voice notes per bar on the 3+3+2 accents, keeping that sternum buzz constant across all three.
If the vibration fades on any note, you've drifted out of chest — rest your palm and re-sing the bar until the hand feels it on every attack.
Keep the pitch range low (root to fifth) so chest voice is the natural choice, not a forced one.
Close with a long sustained chest root on the final downbeat — let the hand feel it for 4-5 seconds.
examples: root → third → fifth (all in low chest register, sternum engaged on every attack)
fifth → fourth → root (descending chest phrase, each note grounded in the body)
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_register:chest.watchOut: Rhythmic excitement pulls the voice upward into the throat — the tresillo groove will tempt you to 'perform' out of chest resonance. Keep the hand on the sternum; let the vibration be your truth.
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_register:chest.deeperInsight: Chest voice locked into a funky rhythm is how traditional African and Iberian vocal styles blend presence with groove — the weight of the voice becomes part of the percussion.

rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_register:head.interaction: Head voice floats above the throat with light mask resonance; tresillo drives hard and low. The combo is paradoxical — the voice feels weightless while the rhythm is heavy, and that contrast creates a 'ghost above the groove' effect used constantly in Balearic pop, Kelsey Lu string writing, and Caroline Polachek's high-register hooks. Your job is to keep the head voice absolutely clean while the rhythm stays precise.
steps: Place your fingertips lightly on your cheekbones and find the buzzing resonance point at the front of your face.
Sing three head-voice notes per bar on the 3+3+2 accents, keeping that cheekbone buzz constant.
Pitch range should sit at the upper end of your comfortable — think fifth, sixth, seventh, root up an octave.
If any note drops into throat tension, restart the bar; head voice in tresillo is only useful if the floating quality survives the rhythmic attacks.
Close with a high held root up an octave on the final downbeat, letting it ring lightly until the breath runs out.
examples: fifth → sixth → root up an octave (ascending head-voice tresillo, all mask placement)
seventh → fifth → root up an octave (zigzag high-register shape, cheekbone buzz throughout)
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_register:head.watchOut: Head voice collapses into falsetto under rhythmic pressure — if the tone goes breathy and disconnected, you've lost the mask placement. Fingertips on cheekbones; feel the buzz.
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_register:head.deeperInsight: Head voice attacks on syncopated accents are harder than sustained head voice because each attack is a mini-onset — this combo trains the onset muscle specifically.

rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_register:mixed.interaction: Mixed voice bridges chest and head with no audible break; tresillo exposes transitions because each cell is a fresh attack. If there's ANY weakness in your mix — a tiny flip at your passaggio, a shift in tone color — the rhythmic accents will spotlight it. This combo is specifically designed to smooth the chest-to-head bridge under rhythmic pressure, which is the technical core of every good pop chorus vocal.
steps: Identify your passaggio note (the spot where chest wants to flip into head) — for most it's around the fourth to fifth of the scale.
Sing a tresillo shape that sits RIGHT on the passaggio: three notes per bar, one in chest territory, one at the passaggio, one in head territory.
Use mixed voice throughout — the chest note should have light head, the head note should have light chest, the passaggio note should be the purest mix.
Check yourself with one finger below the sternum and one on the cheekbone — both should feel light vibration on every note.
Run four bars, rest one, run four more. Listen for ANY flip or color change.
examples: third → fifth → seventh (tresillo that crosses the passaggio mid-bar, all in mix)
fourth → sixth → root up an octave (ascending mixed-voice shape, no tone change between notes)
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_register:mixed.watchOut: The short '2' cell note is usually where the break happens — because it's rhythmically squeezed, you grab tension to produce it, and tension breaks the mix. Keep the 2-cell as relaxed as the other cells.
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_register:mixed.deeperInsight: Every great modern pop vocal chorus is built on mixed voice over syncopated rhythm — this combo is the technical foundation of that sound.

rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_register:falsetto.interaction: Falsetto is airy and disconnected from chest resonance; tresillo is grounded and pulse-driven. Combining them produces a signature 'ghostly groove' — falsetto hooks over a syncopated beat is the sound of early Bee Gees, D'Angelo's Voodoo album, and every great disco top-line. The rhythmic precision has to be surgical because falsetto gives you no volume or weight to hide behind.
steps: Find your clean falsetto — high, airy, completely disconnected from chest. No strain.
Sing three falsetto notes per bar on the 3+3+2 accents, pitched in the upper reaches of your range.
Keep the air flow constant — falsetto collapses the moment breath support wavers, and tresillo's short 2-cell will try to starve you of breath.
Take a quick, silent breath right at the bar line so every new bar starts with full support.
Close with a single long falsetto root up an octave held across two bars of silence.
examples: root up an octave → sixth → fifth (descending falsetto figure, constant air flow)
seventh → root up an octave → third up an octave (high falsetto arc across the tresillo cells)
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_register:falsetto.watchOut: Falsetto gets 'pinched' when you try to add rhythmic attack — the instinct is to tighten the throat for clarity, but that kills the airy character. Keep the throat open; let the rhythm come from breath pulses, not constriction.
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_register:falsetto.deeperInsight: Falsetto over syncopated rhythm works because the voice is so disembodied that it hovers ABOVE the groove rather than riding it — creating the psychedelic vertical separation great funk vocalists chase.

rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_register:breathy.interaction: Breathy register is intimate, close-mic, more air than tone; tresillo is rhythmically assertive. The combo should sound like a whispered confession over a dancehall beat — Billie Eilish's mix engineering instincts in audio form. The breathiness has to be consistent across all three tresillo cells, and the rhythm has to remain audible through all that air.
steps: Hold your palm about two inches in front of your mouth; breathe out and feel the air on your palm. Now sing an 'ah' with the same air flow — palm feels the breath.
Sing three breathy notes per bar on the 3+3+2 accents. On each note, your palm should feel warmth from the air.
Keep volume very low — breathy loses its character past mezzo-piano. Close-mic intimacy means close-mic volume.
The rhythmic accents come from tiny puffs of extra air, not from pitch stabs. Each tresillo cell is a breath pulse.
Close with one sustained breathy root, palm in front of mouth, feeling the warm air for the full duration.
examples: root → third → second (close-mic breathy tresillo, each note with a warm air pulse)
fifth → fourth → third (descending breathy phrase, palm feels air on every cell)
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_register:breathy.watchOut: Breathy singing tempts you to push more air for rhythmic clarity, but more air makes the tone flatten and lose pitch. Stay at low volume; let the rhythm come from tiny onsets, not bigger breath gusts.
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_register:breathy.deeperInsight: Breathy over tresillo works because the ear processes air-noise and rhythm on separate channels — the groove remains clear even when the pitch is almost whispered.

rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_pickingHand:downs.interaction: All downstrokes over tresillo is pure driving energy — it's ska rhythm guitar, early punk, Fela Kuti's rhythm section when the horn line is on top. The picking hand moves like a pendulum, but because tresillo is uneven, the arm has to fight back against the beat 2 and beat 3 alternate-picking instincts. This is forearm discipline training disguised as groove practice.
steps: Rest your palm lightly on the bridge for a partial mute so the downstrokes sound punchy, not ringing.
Play three downstrokes per bar on the 3+3+2 accents — every note a committed downward motion from the wrist.
Between notes, LIFT the picking hand back to its starting height — don't drift in a floating arc, return fully each time.
The '2' cell downstroke is the hardest because the timing is tighter; focus breath there.
Play four bars, then try it muted more heavily (palm deeper on the bridge) so the downstrokes become percussive chucks — feel the shift from melodic to rhythmic function.
examples: root ↓ third ↓ fifth ↓ (three downstrokes per bar across a rising phrase)
fifth ↓ seventh ↓ root up an octave ↓ (ascending downstroke attack pattern)
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_pickingHand:downs.watchOut: The forearm starts cheating into alternate picking under speed pressure — especially on the '3-3' transition where an up-stroke 'feels right'. Force the full reset; speed is a side effect of discipline, not a goal.
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_pickingHand:downs.deeperInsight: All-downstrokes on uneven rhythms is how ska and early punk generated their signature forward pressure — every attack is a declaration, not a continuation.

rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_pickingHand:alter.interaction: Strict alternate picking wants even subdivisions; tresillo refuses to be even. When you force down-up-down-up picking onto a 3+3+2 grid, you get continuous eighth notes with accents on the tresillo cells — a hypnotic shimmer that sounds like Khruangbin rhythm guitar or an Afro-fusion groove. The PICKING is metronomic, the ACCENTS are syncopated.
steps: Play continuous eighth notes on a single pitch with strict down-up-down-up picking and the drone underneath — eight notes per bar.
Now accent the tresillo cells: beat 1 (down), the 'and' of beat 2 (down), and beat 4 (down if landing tresillo on beats 1 / 2and / 4). Let every other note ring quieter.
Maintain the alternate picking no matter what — even the accented notes are just part of the continuous up-down flow.
Add melodic movement: change pitch every tresillo accent, keep the un-accented eighths on the same note as the previous accent.
Run four bars, speed up gradually, and notice how the picking hand becomes a separate entity from the accent hand (your brain).
examples: continuous eighths with accents: ROOT third third THIRD fifth fifth FIFTH fifth (caps = accented tresillo hits)
continuous eighths with accents: FIFTH fifth fifth SEVENTH seventh seventh ROOT up an octave seventh
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_pickingHand:alter.watchOut: Players drop un-accented notes in an attempt to 'only play the tresillo' — but the whole exercise depends on the continuous alternate stream running underneath. If the in-between notes disappear, you've lost the paradox.
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_pickingHand:alter.deeperInsight: This is the exact technique behind the steady 'shimmer' of great fusion guitar — continuous subdivision picked evenly, with accents placed wherever the rhythm calls for them.

rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_pickingHand:finge.interaction: Fingerpicking a tresillo is the most graceful version of the rhythm — thumb and fingers can trigger different strings simultaneously, so the 3+3+2 cells can be independent voices rather than single attacks. This is the nylon-string Brazilian guitar approach, or a Tommy Guerrero laid-back loop, where the thumb keeps a bass pulse and the fingers sprinkle the melody on the upper strings.
steps: Set the drone and assign your thumb to play the root on every tresillo cell start — three thumb hits per bar on the 3+3+2 grid.
Assign your index and middle fingers to play melody notes on the upper strings between or on top of the thumb hits.
Start with just the thumb (bass pulse) for two bars until the groove is settled, then layer the fingers in on the next two bars.
The key is independence: the thumb should keep its pulse even when the fingers do something surprising.
On the final bars, let the fingers trace a full three-note phrase on top of the thumb bass line, landing the highest note on the short 2-cell.
examples: thumb: root-root-root (on each tresillo accent); fingers: fifth → seventh → root up an octave (on top)
thumb: root-fifth-root (alternating bass); fingers: third → sixth → fifth (top voice)
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_pickingHand:finge.watchOut: Finger independence collapses under rhythmic pressure — the thumb tends to follow the fingers' timing instead of holding its own. Focus on the thumb as the rhythmic authority; the fingers are decoration.
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_pickingHand:finge.deeperInsight: Fingerpicked tresillo with independent thumb is the foundation of Caribbean and Brazilian guitar styles — it lets one guitarist sound like a full rhythm section.

rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_pickingHand:hybri.interaction: Hybrid picking (pick + middle/ring fingers) lets you play a bass pulse and an upper melody with different attack characters — the pick is sharp, the fingers are soft. Over tresillo, this means you can get the driving rhythm of downstrokes on the bass AND the graceful upper melody of fingerstyle at the same time. It's Brent Mason country-funk territory, or a modern Blake Mills approach to rhythmic comping.
steps: Use the pick on the low strings for the bass pulse: hit the root on each tresillo accent with a downstroke.
Use your middle and ring fingers on the upper strings for the melodic content: three notes per bar following the 3+3+2 grid.
The pick attacks should sound sharper and louder than the finger attacks — two tonal layers stacked.
Run four bars of bass-plus-melody, then isolate: two bars of just pick (bass), two bars of just fingers (melody), then back together.
For the final minute, add a string skip in the fingered melody so the upper voice leaps between non-adjacent strings while the pick keeps the bass pulse locked.
examples: pick: root-root-root (tresillo bass pulse); fingers: third → fifth → seventh (melody on top)
pick: root-fifth-root (alternating bass); fingers: seventh → root up an octave → fifth (high melody)
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_pickingHand:hybri.watchOut: The pick and finger volumes usually don't balance — either the pick is way too loud (buried melody) or the fingers dominate (lost groove). Listen for two distinct voices, not one blended attack.
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_pickingHand:hybri.deeperInsight: Hybrid picking over tresillo is how a solo guitarist sounds like a band — separate attacks for separate rhythmic functions, all in one hand.

pitchConstraint:leaps_texture:singleLine.interaction: Leaps want drama and single-line texture gives them nowhere to hide — without a chord cushioning the skip, each jump is a tiny cliff the ear hears in isolation. This combo is how players like Jerry Garcia or Marc Ribot build angular country-blues lines: one voice, bold intervals, every pitch auditioning for its place. Leaps become melodic decisions rather than embellishments.
steps: Set a slow metronome and commit to one note per beat so you can hear every skip clearly.
Start from the root and force yourself to skip at least a scale step in both directions, keeping your fretting hand relaxed between jumps.
Land each leap with a deliberate pick attack — no slides, no pull-offs disguising the interval.
Every four beats, anchor back to a strong tone (root, third, or fifth) so the shape of the wandering becomes audible.
Record one pass and listen: can you hum it back? If not, your leaps are random rather than shaped.
examples: root → fifth → third → seventh → root (down a fifth, up a third, up a fifth, down a seventh — cowboy lope)
second → sixth → fourth → root (wide to narrow to home, like a question answered curtly)
pitchConstraint:leaps_texture:singleLine.watchOut: The trap is making every leap the same width — octave, octave, octave — which turns the phrase into a pattern drill instead of a melody. Vary your interval sizes; a fifth, a third, and a seventh in one phrase feel like conversation, three octaves in a row feel like a machine.

pitchConstraint:leaps_texture:doubleStop.interaction: Leap motion plus double-stops doubles the drama — you're moving two voices in parallel across skip intervals, which is exactly how Chuck Berry, Keith Richards, and Southern soul rhythm guitar get their swagger. The bottom voice and top voice both jump, which means your ear has to track two leaping lines that still need to sound like one instrument.
steps: Pick two adjacent strings and build thirds or sixths on them so your hand already has a shape to leap with.
Move the whole shape by scale leaps — never step — sliding the framework up or down, keeping the interval between the two notes consistent.
Pick both strings cleanly with a down-pick or light pinch so neither voice is louder than the other.
After every leap, freeze for half a beat and check: are both notes in the scale? Small leaps can hide wrong tones.
Finish each phrase with both notes landing on a chord tone of the current key (root-third, third-fifth, or fifth-root).
examples: third/fifth → seventh/second → root/third (rising sixths skipping a scale step each time)
fifth/seventh → second/fourth → root/third (a descending sixth figure in three leap-steps)
pitchConstraint:leaps_texture:doubleStop.watchOut: Your muscle memory will want to slide the shape stepwise because that's comfortable. Force yourself to lift the fingers fully between each skip — a true leap sounds different from a smeared glide, and that difference is the whole point of this constraint.

pitchConstraint:leaps_texture:triadParti.interaction: Leaping between three-string triad partials is the signature move of Curtis Mayfield, Steve Cropper, and classic reggae upstroke comping — you're not just changing chords, you're throwing small harmonic pictures at each other across scale skips. Every partial is a color, and leaping means you're mixing bold, contrasting colors rather than blending neighbors.
steps: Build three-string partials off the root, fourth, and fifth chords of your scale so you have ready-made shapes.
Leap between them by skipping a scale step each voicing change — root to third-chord, third to fifth-chord, never adjacent.
Mute with your palm between leaps so each voicing lands crisp, not blurred into the next.
Keep your picking hand at the same strumming angle — uniform attack across leaping voicings so the contrast is harmonic, not dynamic.
Anchor the phrase by returning every four beats to a root-position partial as a home base.
examples: root-triad → fifth-triad → second-triad → root-triad (skipping through related voicings)
third-triad → seventh-triad → root-triad (a tense leap resolved home)
pitchConstraint:leaps_texture:triadParti.watchOut: Three-string shapes are so close together on the neck that stepwise motion feels natural. Watch your fretting hand — if you're moving only one or two frets between voicings, you're probably stepping, not leaping. Force bigger geographic jumps.

pitchConstraint:leaps_texture:fullChord.interaction: Leaping between full chord voicings turns you into an arranger — this is how Ernest Ranglin reharmonizes on the fly, or how a big-band pianist builds horn-section stabs. Every skip is a full harmonic shift, so the texture is maximal and the motion has to be decisive. There's no melting one voicing into the next; each chord lands as its own statement.
steps: Grab four or more notes per chord and cycle through chord shapes built off non-adjacent scale degrees — root chord, then fourth chord, then second chord.
Strum once per chord cleanly and let each voicing ring its full length before the leap.
Breathe between chords; give your fretting hand a half-beat to reset before the next skip.
Vary the leap direction — up, down, up again — so the harmony doesn't march in one direction.
End on a root or fifth chord so the ear hears the journey land.
examples: root-chord → fifth-chord → third-chord → root-chord (a leap-based cadence)
fourth-chord → seventh-chord → second-chord → fifth-chord (four non-adjacent chords — descending leaps)
pitchConstraint:leaps_texture:fullChord.watchOut: Full chord leaps expose any fretting-hand sloppiness — if even one string buzzes, the whole voicing falls apart. Slow the tempo enough that every chord rings perfectly before you leap, then speed up only when the leaps are clean.

pitchConstraint:leaps_texture:mixed.interaction: Leaps plus mixed texture is the language of soul and funk rhythm playing — think Jimmy Nolen's stabs and Cornell Dupree's fills, where chord punches skip to single-note answers which skip back to chord punches. The leap constraint forces you to not walk between the stab and the fill; every transition is a jump, every texture change is a moment of contrast.
steps: Alternate four-bar sections: bars 1-2 are chord stabs, bars 3-4 are single-note fills.
When moving from stab to fill, leap a scale step or more from the top chord tone to your first single note — never land adjacent.
When moving back to the chord, leap to a chord tone that's not next to your last single-note.
Keep picking attack consistent so the texture change, not the volume, is what the ear notices.
End every four-bar loop with a chord stab on the root to re-anchor the key.
examples: root-chord (stab) → fifth → seventh → third (fills) → fourth-chord (stab, leap) → sixth → root (fills)
fifth-chord (stab) → root → third → root (fills) → root-chord (stab)
pitchConstraint:leaps_texture:mixed.watchOut: The most common slip is hiding the leap inside a fast fill — if the first single note after a stab is the adjacent scale tone to the chord's top note, you've stepped, not leaped. Slow down the transitions; they're the whole point of this card.

pitchConstraint:stepwise_texture:singleL.interaction: Stepwise single-line work is where most great melodies live — every classic vocal hook, every Baroque violin line, every Khruangbin guitar curl. One voice, always walking to a neighbor, means every note is inevitable but every note is also a choice about direction. This combo trains your ear to find the melody inside the scale by letting go of leaps altogether.
steps: Place your fretting hand in one position so all the scale neighbors are under your fingers — no stretching for a skip.
Start from the root and walk up or down one scale step at a time, staying within a five-note window.
Every four beats, change direction — up to down or down to up — so the line breathes rather than marches.
Vary your step rhythm (quarter, eighth, held note) so the melody has phrasing, not just pitches.
End each phrase by walking toward the nearest strong tone (root, third, or fifth) so the line resolves rather than stopping mid-step.
examples: root → second → third → fourth → third → second → root (a simple arch in stepwise motion)
fifth → sixth → seventh → root up an octave → seventh → sixth → fifth (walking to the octave and back)
pitchConstraint:stepwise_texture:singleL.watchOut: Stepwise melodies turn into scales when you forget to phrase. If you're just running up five notes and back down, you're practicing technique, not melody. Insert rests, change direction mid-phrase, and let some steps linger.

pitchConstraint:stepwise_texture:doubleS.interaction: Stepwise double-stops are the foundation of parallel-harmony playing — Duane Allman slide lines, Mark Knopfler's dual voicings, classic country pedal-steel moves. Both voices walk in parallel thirds or sixths, neighbor to neighbor. The constraint is tight: nothing jumps, everything glides, which means your ear needs to follow two lines moving inchworm-style up and down the scale.
steps: Pick two strings and build a third or sixth as your starting shape on the root.
Move the whole shape one scale step at a time, keeping the interval constant (stay in diatonic thirds or sixths).
Use hammer-ons or legato to emphasize how smoothly the two voices walk together.
Reverse direction every few beats so the stepwise line breathes — up three steps, down two, up one.
End by walking both voices onto a chord tone pair (root+third, third+fifth, or fifth+root).
examples: root/third → second/fourth → third/fifth → second/fourth → root/third (diatonic thirds walking)
fifth/root-up-octave → sixth/second → seventh/third → sixth/second (parallel sixths up and back)
pitchConstraint:stepwise_texture:doubleS.watchOut: The two voices can go out of sync — one walks while the other sits — and you end up with random intervals instead of parallel stepwise motion. Check after every beat: did BOTH notes move one step? If not, back up and redo.

pitchConstraint:stepwise_texture:triadPa.interaction: Stepwise motion with triad partials gives you the sliding chord vocabulary of reggae-soul players like Lynn Taitt or modern neo-soul comping — three-string shapes walking neighbor to neighbor so the harmony moves smoothly through diatonic chord qualities. Every step is a full chord change, but the voice-leading is so tight it sounds like one melted line of color.
steps: Start with a triad partial rooted on the root of the scale — top three or middle three strings.
Move the whole shape up one scale step so you land on the triad partial of the second degree (minor if you're in major, etc.).
Continue walking diatonically: root, second, third, fourth chord partials, never skipping.
Use a clean upstroke on each voicing so the texture stays even as the harmony shifts.
Return stepwise back down to the root partial — the round trip is the point.
examples: root-partial → second-partial → third-partial → fourth-partial → third-partial → root-partial
fifth-partial → sixth-partial → seventh-partial → fifth-partial (a short stepwise loop)
pitchConstraint:stepwise_texture:triadPa.watchOut: Triad partials tempt you to slide a shape and call it stepwise when you've skipped a diatonic chord quality. Every move must land on the NEXT scale-degree partial — don't skip the minor or half-diminished shapes just because they're harder.

pitchConstraint:stepwise_texture:fullCho.interaction: Stepwise full-chord motion is diatonic walk-up territory — think gospel piano, Nashville country turnarounds, or the descending chord-walk at the end of a Beatles bridge. Moving a full voicing one scale step is mechanically hard on guitar (different shapes for different qualities), which is exactly why this combo builds your chord vocabulary the fastest.
steps: Choose four diatonic chords that sit next to each other on the scale (root, second-degree, third-degree, fourth-degree).
Strum each chord for one bar, moving one scale step between each change.
When the chord quality changes (major → minor → minor → major), let your fingers adapt the shape rather than forcing a parallel move.
Walk the chords up four steps, then back down the same four steps — the return trip reinforces the diatonic ear.
End on the root chord so the walk feels like a journey with a home base.
examples: root-chord → second-chord → third-chord → fourth-chord → third-chord → second-chord → root-chord
fifth-chord → sixth-chord → seventh-chord → root-chord (resolving up a step at a time)
pitchConstraint:stepwise_texture:fullCho.watchOut: The urge is to treat this as a chord progression exercise and ignore the stepwise constraint by jumping to 'the good chords.' Every move must be exactly one scale step — even if that means playing the awkward half-diminished seventh chord.

pitchConstraint:stepwise_texture:mixed.interaction: Stepwise motion with mixed texture is the language of soulful rhythm playing — a chord stab followed by a single-note fill that walks a neighbor-to-neighbor line back to the next chord stab. Cornell Dupree, Steve Cropper, and neo-soul rhythm guitarists live here. The constraint keeps everything smooth and conjunct even as the texture bounces between dense and sparse.
steps: Strum a chord on beat 1, then play three single notes moving one scale step at a time to land on beat 4.
Make sure the first single note is adjacent to the top chord tone (no leap when the texture changes).
Let the next chord stab sit on the scale step you walked to — voice-leading continuity.
Alternate direction: one phrase walks up to the next chord, the next walks down.
Record a loop and listen: does the single-note fill feel like it 'melted' from the chord, or does it sound disconnected?
examples: root-chord (top note: fifth) → fourth → third → second → second-chord (top note: second)
third-chord (top note: seventh) → root-up-octave → seventh → sixth → sixth-chord
pitchConstraint:stepwise_texture:mixed.watchOut: The hardest part is keeping the chord's top note connected to the first single note by one scale step. If your chord's highest note is the fifth and your first fill note is the root, that's a leap — start the fill on the fourth or sixth instead.

pitchConstraint:arch_texture:singleLine.interaction: Arch contour with a single line is the oldest melody shape in the world — Gregorian chant, Appalachian fiddle tunes, nursery rhymes. Every phrase rises to a peak and falls. With single-note texture, the arch is fully exposed; you can hear exactly where the peak lives and whether the descent answers the ascent or just fades. This combo trains your sense of phrase architecture above all else.
steps: Plan where the peak will land before you start — usually the third note of the top half of the scale (sixth or seventh degree).
Start from a low scale tone and walk or skip upward, spending most of your time climbing.
Hit the peak on a strong beat and let it ring slightly longer than the surrounding notes.
Descend back to a resting tone — ideally lower than you started — using motion that mirrors but doesn't exactly repeat the ascent.
Test the phrase by singing it: a good arch feels like a breath.
examples: root → third → fifth → sixth → fifth → third → root (a classic five-note arch)
second → fourth → sixth → seventh → fifth → third → second (offset arch with asymmetric descent)
pitchConstraint:arch_texture:singleLine.watchOut: Beginners make the peak too early or too short-lived. The peak should arrive near the middle of the phrase and linger just enough to feel like an arrival — not a drive-by.

pitchConstraint:arch_texture:doubleStops.interaction: Arch contour with double-stops is pedal-steel and slide-guitar country — two voices climbing together to a high peak and falling. The harmony deepens the drama of the peak because both voices arrive at the top together. Think Santo and Johnny 'Sleep Walk,' or the soaring double-stop bends in a Richard Thompson solo.
steps: Pick your starting pair (third+fifth of the scale is a warm place to begin) and plan the peak as a pair of chord tones (usually root-up-octave paired with the third above).
Walk or skip the double-stop shape upward across three or four moves, staying diatonic.
Hold the peak pair for a full beat longer than any other — the arrival is the whole phrase.
Descend using parallel motion (both notes moving down together) to a pair below where you started.
Check both voices hit the peak at exactly the same time — any unevenness flattens the arch.
examples: third/fifth → fourth/sixth → fifth/seventh → sixth/root-up-octave → fourth/sixth → root/third
root/third → third/fifth → fifth/seventh → fourth/sixth → second/fourth
pitchConstraint:arch_texture:doubleStops.watchOut: Double-stop arches can feel cluttered at the peak because both voices are competing for the listener's ear. Make sure the top voice carries the melodic shape and the bottom voice supports — don't let the bottom voice leap around while the top walks.

pitchConstraint:arch_texture:triadPartia.interaction: Arch contour with triad partials is cinematic — rising waves of three-voice color that climb to a peak chord and fall back. This is how film-scorers build tension with a single guitar, or how modern soul guitarists (think Tom Misch, Mac DeMarco chord movement) shape a verse. Each partial is a miniature stage in the climb.
steps: Pick three diatonic triad partials that form a rising sequence up the scale.
Play each partial one bar at a time, gradually raising the geographic position on the neck.
Let the peak partial (often the fourth or fifth chord partial in a high neck position) ring for an extended beat.
Descend through three more triad partials back to a low-neck resting position.
Keep your strumming hand volume even — the arch is geographic/harmonic, not dynamic.
examples: root-partial → third-partial → fifth-partial → sixth-partial (peak) → fourth-partial → root-partial
second-partial → fourth-partial → root-partial up octave (peak) → fifth-partial → root-partial
pitchConstraint:arch_texture:triadPartia.watchOut: Triad partials can all sit in the same neck zone, which flattens the arch into a plateau. The peak partial must be geographically higher on the neck than everything else — let your fretting hand climb, not just change shapes.

pitchConstraint:arch_texture:fullChord.interaction: Arch contour through full chord voicings is the rise-and-fall of a hymn, a power ballad bridge, or a Bon Iver slow-burn — harmonic drama where each chord steps closer to a climactic peak voicing and then the tension drains through the descent. Full chords make the peak sound enormous, so the release has to be planned.
steps: Sketch a four-chord climb (root-chord → third-chord → fifth-chord → sixth-chord) where each chord is higher in register than the last.
Strum each chord slowly, letting it ring into the next — the climb should feel inevitable.
The peak chord gets an extra beat and maybe a slightly harder strum so the ear registers the arrival.
Descend through three more chords back to the root chord, using chords in lower neck positions.
End with the root chord ringing out quietly — the breath after the climb.
examples: root-chord → third-chord → fifth-chord → sixth-chord (peak) → fourth-chord → second-chord → root-chord
second-chord → fourth-chord → sixth-chord (peak) → fifth-chord → third-chord → root-chord
pitchConstraint:arch_texture:fullChord.watchOut: Full chord arches can accidentally become 'loud in the middle, soft at the ends' by pure volume — but the constraint is PITCH contour. Keep dynamics flat and let the chord register and voicing position do the arch work.

pitchConstraint:arch_texture:mixed.interaction: Arch contour with mixed texture — chord stabs climbing and single-note fills falling — is classic verse-chorus architecture in miniature. The chord stabs build the climb, the single-note fills release tension on the way down. This is how soul rhythm guitarists punctuate a bridge, or how a jazz comping guitarist sets up a horn soloist's entry.
steps: Use chord stabs for the ascending half of the arch, each stab on a higher scale-degree chord than the last.
On the peak bar, play the highest chord stab as a held strum rather than a short stab — mark the arrival.
Drop into single-note fills for the descending half, walking or skipping downward.
End the fills on a low-register note (root or fifth) to complete the fall.
Loop the phrase: the climb should always feel taller than the fall is deep.
examples: root-chord → third-chord → fifth-chord (peak, held) → fourth → third → second → root (fills)
second-chord → fourth-chord → sixth-chord (peak) → fifth → third → root
pitchConstraint:arch_texture:mixed.watchOut: The texture change can mask the arch because chord stabs already feel 'bigger' than single notes. Make sure the pitch arch is real — the peak chord must contain notes HIGHER than the start AND higher than the end-fill note.

pitchConstraint:seed_texture:singleLine.interaction: Seed plus single line is pure melodic algebra — you plant a three-note cell and transform it one pitch at a time while keeping a single voice. This is Bach invention thinking, or how Pat Metheny develops a motif across a solo. The texture keeps everything transparent so each variation is audibly different from the one before.
steps: Invent a three-note seed using any three scale tones — for example, the root, third, and fifth.
Play the seed cleanly and let it settle in your ear before changing anything.
For the next iteration, change EXACTLY ONE note — maybe the third becomes the fourth — and play the whole seed again.
Continue for eight iterations, each time changing one pitch (not the same one every time).
Finish by playing the original seed again to show how far you've traveled.
examples: root-third-fifth → root-third-sixth → root-fourth-sixth → second-fourth-sixth → second-fourth-seventh
second-fourth-sixth → second-fifth-sixth → third-fifth-sixth → third-fifth-seventh
pitchConstraint:seed_texture:singleLine.watchOut: The seduction is to change two notes at once because it sounds 'more different.' Resist. The discipline of one-note-at-a-time is what teaches your ear to hear voice-leading in real time — if you swap two, you've broken the exercise.

pitchConstraint:seed_texture:doubleStops.interaction: Seed variations with double-stops turns the seed into a three-chord micro-progression — three dyads, each a harmonic moment, and you change one note across the whole cell per iteration. It's like writing a hymn tune in miniature: tiny shifts inside a fixed framework. Gospel guitarists and hymn-tune arrangers lived in this headspace.
steps: Build a three-dyad seed — for example, root/third, fourth/sixth, fifth/seventh.
Play the seed once in rhythm so the three double-stops feel like one gesture.
Change ONE note in ONE of the three dyads for the next iteration (e.g., the fourth in the second dyad becomes the third).
Play the modified seed, noticing how one small change shifts the whole chord-color of the cell.
Keep iterating — pick a different note to modify each time so the variations don't pile up in the same voice.
examples: (root/third) → (fourth/sixth) → (fifth/seventh) becomes (root/third) → (fourth/sixth) → (fifth/root-up-octave)
(root/fifth) → (second/sixth) → (third/seventh) becomes (root/fifth) → (second/fifth) → (third/seventh)
pitchConstraint:seed_texture:doubleStops.watchOut: With two voices per cell, it's tempting to move the whole dyad up a step and call that 'one change.' That's actually two changes. Keep one note of every dyad anchored across iterations so the ear tracks the exact micro-shift.

pitchConstraint:seed_texture:triadPartia.interaction: Seed plus triad-partial texture is how a reggae or soul comping guitarist develops a groove without changing the whole chord — the three-string partial is the seed, and you're changing one note inside the voicing to shift the color subtly. Think Bob Marley's subtle chord embellishments, or Nile Rodgers hinting at extensions without abandoning the core voicing.
steps: Lock in a single triad partial on the root chord (three strings, three notes).
Play the partial in rhythm for a bar.
Change ONE of the three notes — for example, the third becomes the fourth (creating a suspension).
Play that modified partial for a bar, then change a DIFFERENT note next iteration (the fourth becomes the fifth, or the root becomes the seventh).
After eight iterations, return to the original partial as a resolution.
examples: (root+third+fifth partial) → (root+fourth+fifth) → (root+fourth+sixth) → (second+fourth+sixth)
(third+fifth+seventh) → (third+fifth+root-up-octave) → (fourth+fifth+root-up-octave)
pitchConstraint:seed_texture:triadPartia.watchOut: Triad partials are easy to 'slide' into a whole new chord without realizing it — if you change TWO notes, you've moved to a new voicing, not varied the seed. After each iteration, stop and count: exactly how many notes are different from the previous bar?

pitchConstraint:seed_texture:fullChord.interaction: Seed plus full chord turns each iteration into a little piece of harmonic engineering — you're changing one note inside a four-plus-note voicing, which might turn a major into a major-seven, a minor into a minor-nine, a diminished into a minor-seven-flat-five. This is jazz reharmonization in action: Bill Evans-style chord-scale thinking built one note at a time.
steps: Pick a full chord voicing on a root-position chord (at least four strings).
Strum it and let it ring for a full bar.
Change ONE note in the voicing — for example, raise the seventh to the root above, or drop the third to the second (creating a sus).
Strum the altered voicing and hear the new quality.
Keep changing one note per iteration; make sure the modified note isn't the same one you changed last time.
examples: (root+third+fifth+seventh chord) → (root+third+fifth+sixth) → (root+third+fifth+root-up-octave)
(root+third+fifth+seventh) → (root+fourth+fifth+seventh) → (root+fourth+fifth+sixth)
pitchConstraint:seed_texture:fullChord.watchOut: Full chords tempt you to 'just grab a new shape' and pretend one note changed. Be strict: write down your voicing, change exactly one pitch, and grab the new shape that still has every other original note. If that new shape doesn't exist on guitar, pick a different note to change.

pitchConstraint:seed_texture:mixed.interaction: Seed variations in mixed texture is how a great rhythm guitarist develops a groove across a verse — the chord stab is one cell of the seed, the single-note fill is another, and each iteration changes ONE note somewhere. The result is a groove that evolves in tiny audible steps, the way James Brown's guitarists (Jimmy Nolen, Catfish Collins) would shift a riff without breaking the feel.
steps: Set up a two-bar seed: bar 1 is a chord stab, bar 2 is a three-note single-line fill.
Loop the seed twice so it feels settled.
For the third iteration, change ONE note — either in the chord voicing OR in the fill, not both.
Play the new version, notice the shift, and keep looping.
Every fourth iteration, pick a different note (chord vs fill) to vary so changes don't pile up in one place.
examples: (root-chord, fifth→fourth→third fill) → (root-chord, fifth→fourth→second fill) → (root-seven-chord, fifth→fourth→second fill)
(second-chord, root→seventh→sixth fill) → (second-chord, root→seventh→fifth fill)
pitchConstraint:seed_texture:mixed.watchOut: Because mixed texture already gives you two 'moments' per iteration, the urge is to change both the chord AND the fill every time and call it one big idea. No — the whole point of seed discipline is surgical incremental change. One note per round. Count them.

pitchConstraint:forbidden_texture:single.interaction: Forbidden notes with a single line is the fastest way to discover the melodic personality of a scale — when you lock out, say, the third of a major scale, suddenly you're sketching something almost pentatonic-but-not, and your ear has to invent new melodic paths. Miles Davis famously soloed with 'forbidden' tones; Jimmy Giuffre built whole compositions around them.
steps: Choose one to three scale notes to forbid — avoid the root if you want the exercise to be hard, forbid the third if you want a modal-sounding challenge.
Play a single-note line using only the permitted scale tones, walking or skipping freely.
When your ear wants the forbidden note, pause instead — let the silence mark where the lockout happened.
Find paths around the forbidden tone: if the third is off-limits, try going root → fourth instead of root → third → fourth.
Finish by landing on any strong tone that isn't forbidden.
examples: (forbid the fourth) root → second → third → fifth → sixth → fifth → third → root
(forbid the third and seventh) root → second → fourth → fifth → sixth → fifth → fourth → second → root
pitchConstraint:forbidden_texture:single.watchOut: You will play the forbidden note by muscle memory — it happens to every player, especially on the first pass. When you slip, stop and replay the phrase without it. Treat each slip as data: the more often your fingers reach for that note, the more it reveals about how much you rely on it.

pitchConstraint:forbidden_texture:double.interaction: Forbidden notes plus double-stops is harder than it sounds — now BOTH voices have to avoid the forbidden tone, which eliminates whole interval families from the scale. If you forbid the fourth, you can't play root/fourth, second/fifth, fifth/root-up-octave — the lockouts cascade. This teaches diatonic dyad vocabulary at a cellular level.
steps: Forbid one scale note before you start and write down every double-stop in your key that contains that note — those are off-limits.
Play double-stops using only the permitted pairs, walking or skipping between them.
When you accidentally hit the forbidden note in either voice, stop and rewind to the previous valid dyad.
Try both thirds and sixths voicings so you see the lockout affect different interval structures.
End on a dyad that contains a strong, permitted tone (root or fifth if they're not forbidden).
examples: (forbid the sixth) root/third → second/fourth → fourth/root-up-octave → fifth/seventh → root/third
(forbid the seventh) root/third → third/fifth → fourth/sixth → fifth/root-up-octave → third/fifth
pitchConstraint:forbidden_texture:double.watchOut: Double-stop shapes often contain the forbidden note in the 'other' voice while you focus on the top. Check BOTH notes of every dyad before you commit. A single-string check after each move is slow but necessary.

pitchConstraint:forbidden_texture:triadP.interaction: Forbidden notes plus triad partials forces you to rethink which chord shapes even exist in your key — when you lock out one scale degree, any partial containing that note is dead. The result is a kind of modal comping where certain colors are simply unavailable, and you have to find new partials that work within the constraint. This is how Miles Davis's Kind of Blue bandmates limited themselves into modal brilliance.
steps: Forbid one scale note. List the diatonic triad partials that AVOID this note — these are your allowed voicings.
Cycle through the allowed partials in rhythm, one per bar.
Notice which chord qualities became unavailable and which became more prominent.
Experiment with substitutions: if the forbidden note usually lives in the 'fourth-chord partial,' try the 'two-chord partial' in its place.
Return to a permitted root partial at the end of the round.
examples: (forbid the sixth) root-partial → third-partial → fifth-partial → second-partial → root-partial (skipping any partial containing the sixth)
(forbid the second) root-partial → fourth-partial → fifth-partial → sixth-partial → root-partial
pitchConstraint:forbidden_texture:triadP.watchOut: Triad partials often sneak the forbidden note in as a top-string color tone. Check every voicing string by string — 'this shape is fine because the root and third are clean' doesn't cut it if the top string is the forbidden note.

pitchConstraint:forbidden_texture:fullCh.interaction: Forbidden notes with full chord voicings turns into full-on diatonic reharmonization — you're building chord voicings that deliberately skip one scale degree. This is how jazz arrangers voice a chord with omitted color tones (no-third voicings, no-fifth shell chords) to create a specific modal flavor. The constraint sharpens your knowledge of exactly which chord tones are doing what.
steps: Forbid one scale note and think about which chord qualities contain it — those chords need to be re-voiced without that tone.
Play a chord progression (root → fourth → fifth → root) but omit the forbidden note from every voicing, even if it's normally a chord tone.
Use wider string gaps to spread voicings when your normal shape contains the forbidden note.
Let each voicing ring so you can hear what the chord sounds like with the tone missing.
Resolve the progression to a permitted-tone-based voicing.
examples: (forbid the third) root-chord with no third → fourth-chord with no third → fifth-chord with no third → root-chord with no third
(forbid the seventh) root-chord with no seventh → second-chord → fifth-chord with no seventh → root-chord
pitchConstraint:forbidden_texture:fullCh.watchOut: Full chord shapes have a forbidden note lurking on one string nine times out of ten. You'll play through without noticing the violation. Slow down and finger-check every voicing; you're practicing the rule as much as the music.

pitchConstraint:forbidden_texture:mixed.interaction: Forbidden notes with mixed texture — chord stab and fill alternating — means your lockout applies to BOTH the chord voicings and the melodic fills. Miss a forbidden note in either and the round resets. This builds ferocious diatonic awareness because the forbidden tone has to be audibly absent in two different musical modes.
steps: Forbid one scale note. Plan a chord stab voicing that omits it, and plan a fill that routes around it.
Play the chord stab on beats 1-2, then the fill on beats 3-4, staying within the allowed tones.
Loop for four bars, varying the fill shape but keeping the lockout intact.
Every time you slip, stop and replay that bar without the forbidden tone.
End on a chord stab with a clean, permitted-only voicing.
examples: (forbid the fourth) root-chord-no-fourth → fifth → sixth → third fill → root-chord-no-fourth
(forbid the second) root-chord-no-second → fourth → fifth → third fill → root-chord-no-second
pitchConstraint:forbidden_texture:mixed.watchOut: The fill section is where the forbidden note usually slips in — a stepwise walk through the scale inevitably hits it unless you plan around it. Write out your fill notes in advance for the first few passes.

pitchConstraint:targetLanding_texture:si.interaction: Target landing with a single line is the Coltrane exercise — wander anywhere in the scale, but every phrase lands precisely on the target note. The single-voice texture means there's nothing to hide behind; the target has to be hit cleanly and feel inevitable. This trains your ear to calculate 'where am I now, how do I get to the target, how many beats do I have.'
steps: Pick your target note — the third or fifth are classic choices for melodic weight.
Play a four-bar phrase wandering anywhere in the scale, using both steps and skips as you like.
Plan the last two beats of every phrase as the approach to the target — usually from a step above or a step below.
Land the target on beat 4 of the phrase, not beat 1 or 3 — the resolution must feel like an arrival.
Loop the exercise, changing your meandering path each time but always landing on the same target.
examples: (target: third) fifth → sixth → fourth → third (wandering then landing)
(target: fifth) root → third → second → fourth → fifth (approach from below)
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_texture:si.watchOut: The trap is crashing into the target from a leap — it arrives but doesn't feel like a landing. Always set up the target with one adjacent step (the note above or below it) so the resolution is audible.

pitchConstraint:targetLanding_texture:do.interaction: Target landing with double-stops changes the game: now the 'target' is a dyad, not a single note, and both voices have to arrive in the same breath. Country pedal-steel players and Allman Brothers slide-guitar parts live in this world — wandering through parallel dyads then snapping home to a pre-set double-stop pair.
steps: Choose a target dyad (root/third, third/fifth, or fifth/root-up-octave work well).
Wander freely through diatonic double-stops — thirds, sixths, whatever fits.
Plan the final dyad of each phrase as a one-step approach to the target pair (both voices stepping together).
Land the target dyad on the last beat of the phrase with a deliberate, slightly louder pick attack.
Loop and vary the wandering path, but the target dyad stays identical.
examples: (target: root/third) sixth/root-up-octave → fifth/seventh → second/fourth → root/third
(target: fifth/seventh) third/fifth → fourth/sixth → sixth/root-up-octave → fifth/seventh
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_texture:do.watchOut: One voice often lands the target a beat before the other — the top voice hits root on beat 3 while the bottom voice hits third on beat 4. Both voices must arrive together. Practice the landing by itself first, then rebuild the wandering around it.

pitchConstraint:targetLanding_texture:tr.interaction: Target landing with triad partials makes the target a full three-voice voicing — you wander through diatonic chord partials and every phrase resolves to the same 'home' partial. This is how a reggae skank builds a groove: the listener knows the target root-chord partial is coming, so the wandering around it feels like setup for an inevitable arrival.
steps: Pick your target triad partial — usually the root chord partial in root position.
Wander through other diatonic partials (second-chord, fourth-chord, fifth-chord) for three bars.
On the final bar, walk or leap one partial away from the target, then land it on beat 4.
The target partial should ring slightly longer than the wandering partials.
Loop the whole phrase, changing the wandering path but keeping the target partial identical.
examples: (target: root-partial) fifth-partial → fourth-partial → second-partial → root-partial
(target: fifth-partial) root-partial → third-partial → sixth-partial → fourth-partial → fifth-partial
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_texture:tr.watchOut: The target partial can blend into the wandering if all your partials are in the same neck zone. Play the target at a slightly different geographic location (higher or lower on the neck) so its arrival is visible to the ear.

pitchConstraint:targetLanding_texture:fu.interaction: Target landing with full chords is a cadence-construction exercise — wander through diatonic chord voicings and every phrase resolves to the same home chord. This is the root of every tonal song ever written: tonic is the target, and the other chords exist to make the tonic return feel earned. Gospel and folk guitarists do this instinctively.
steps: Pick your target chord — the root chord (tonic) is classic but the fifth chord works for half-cadences.
Wander through a two-bar progression using any diatonic chords EXCEPT the target.
On the last beat of the second bar, play a chord one step away from the target (usually the fifth or second chord).
Strum the target chord on beat 1 of bar 3 — the landing.
Let the target chord ring for a full bar before looping.
examples: (target: root chord) fourth-chord → sixth-chord → fifth-chord → root-chord
(target: fourth chord) root-chord → second-chord → fifth-chord → fourth-chord
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_texture:fu.watchOut: You'll find yourself landing the target too often during the wandering — every other bar — which makes the target stop feeling like an arrival. Discipline: the target chord appears ONCE per phrase, only on the landing.

pitchConstraint:targetLanding_texture:mi.interaction: Target landing with mixed texture means the wandering happens in two worlds — some of it chord stabs, some of it single-note fills — but every phrase still resolves to the same target moment. The target can be a single note or a chord, and the whole exercise sharpens your sense of 'every path leads home' regardless of texture.
steps: Decide the target: either a single note (resolve the fill to it) or a chord (resolve the stab to it).
Alternate chord stabs and fills through the phrase — texture can change anywhere.
Plan the last two beats as the approach to the target, regardless of which texture you're in.
Land the target on beat 4 of the phrase with extra attention.
Loop with different paths but identical target.
examples: (target: root note) second-chord → third → fourth → root-chord → seventh → root (landing on root note fill)
(target: fourth chord) root-chord → fifth → sixth → seventh → fourth-chord (landing on fourth chord)
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_texture:mi.watchOut: Mixed texture lets you sneak up on the target in whichever mode is most comfortable — always chord, never single-note, or vice versa. Force yourself to alternate whether the target arrives in chord or single-note form across repetitions.

rhythmConstraint:river_texture:singleLin.interaction: River rhythm with a single line is raga-like — even quarter notes, one voice, endless patient flow. Think Ry Cooder's solo slide meditations or a Tinariwen riff. When every note occupies exactly one beat and there's no harmony to lean on, the pitch choices do all the expressive work. This combo trains your melodic IQ by stripping away every other variable.
steps: Set the metronome to a slow-to-medium tempo and commit to one note per click.
Start on the root and walk or skip through the scale, changing pitch on every beat.
Never rush into the next note — let each quarter feel complete before moving on.
Breathe with the phrase: if you're tempted to double-time, slow your whole body down.
Finish by landing on a chord tone when the loop ends, so the flow has resolution.
examples: root → third → fifth → sixth → fifth → third → second → root (eight patient quarters)
fifth → fourth → second → root → seventh → root → second → third (descending then ascending)
rhythmConstraint:river_texture:singleLin.watchOut: The river rhythm is so calm that your brain will want to decorate — an extra grace note here, a quick pickup there. Those are sneaky violations. Every beat gets exactly one note attack. If you want to be expressive, do it through pitch choice, not rhythm.

rhythmConstraint:river_texture:doubleSto.interaction: River rhythm with double-stops is country-gospel territory — two voices walking together one quarter at a time, patient and hymnal. The constraint builds muscle memory for parallel harmony because every beat is an opportunity to place two notes together cleanly. Think of the hymn-tune double-stop work in a Gillian Welch or David Rawlings duet.
steps: Set a slow metronome; each click gets exactly one double-stop.
Build a two-voice pattern (thirds or sixths) and walk it through the scale, one quarter per move.
Pick both strings together cleanly so the two voices arrive as one sound on every click.
Reverse direction every eight beats — up, then down — so the river flows both ways.
Land on a strong dyad (root/third or root/fifth) at the end of each pass.
examples: root/third → second/fourth → third/fifth → fourth/sixth (walking up in quarters)
fifth/seventh → fourth/sixth → third/fifth → second/fourth → root/third (walking down)
rhythmConstraint:river_texture:doubleSto.watchOut: Two-note attacks on every beat get tiring on the picking hand and lead to uneven volume — one dyad loud, the next weak. Keep your picking motion identical on every quarter so the river flows with steady weight.

rhythmConstraint:river_texture:triadPart.interaction: River rhythm plus triad partials is straight-ahead reggae upstroke or folk-comping — one partial per beat, never rushing, never pausing. Every click is a chord. This is how a rhythm guitarist builds a steady pulse underneath a singer without ever calling attention. Bob Marley's rhythm guitarists and folk accompanists both live here.
steps: Lock the metronome at a slow tempo and commit: one partial per click, no exceptions.
Use a clean downstroke or upstroke on every beat so the attack stays uniform.
Walk through four diatonic triad partials — root, fourth, fifth, sixth — one per quarter for four beats.
Repeat the four-bar cycle without breaking the pulse.
Mute between beats with your palm so each partial is distinct, not bleeding into the next.
examples: root-partial → fourth-partial → fifth-partial → root-partial (quarter pulse)
second-partial → fifth-partial → sixth-partial → fourth-partial (diatonic wander in quarters)
rhythmConstraint:river_texture:triadPart.watchOut: Triad partials invite 'extra' strums — a quick double-hit, an off-beat upstroke. Resist. The river means ONE strum per beat, nothing in between. Your right hand should feel lazy by comparison to normal playing.

rhythmConstraint:river_texture:fullChord.interaction: River rhythm with full chord voicings is processional — one big chord per beat, hymn-like, prayer-like. It's how solo fingerstyle arrangements of folk songs breathe, or how a singer-songwriter grounds a verse. The patience of quarter-note chords forces you to make every voicing count because nothing moves fast enough to hide a weak choice.
steps: Set a slow metronome; commit to one chord strum per beat.
Cycle through a four-chord diatonic progression (root → fourth → fifth → root) playing one chord per beat.
Let each chord ring through its beat — don't kill it before the next one enters.
Keep the strum dynamic even: every chord arrives at the same volume.
After a few cycles, swap the progression order to test your chord vocabulary at slow tempo.
examples: root-chord → fourth-chord → fifth-chord → root-chord (slow quarter-note hymn)
second-chord → fifth-chord → root-chord → fourth-chord (diatonic progression in quarters)
rhythmConstraint:river_texture:fullChord.watchOut: Full chord strums on every beat are physically tiring, which causes tempo drift. Watch the metronome's click — if you're arriving early or late, you're fatiguing. Lighten the strum, save the energy.

rhythmConstraint:river_texture:mixed.interaction: River rhythm with mixed texture alternates chord stabs and single-note notes one-per-beat, so you're effectively walking a conversation between textures while keeping the quarter-note flow perfect. This is how a classic Motown rhythm guitarist plays — Curtis Mayfield and Steve Cropper build entire grooves from patient quarter-note mixing of chord stabs and fills.
steps: Set a slow metronome; decide which beats are chord stabs and which are single notes in advance (e.g., beats 1 and 3 are chords, 2 and 4 are single notes).
Play the pattern strictly, one attack per beat.
Keep both textures at the same volume so the texture switch is the feature, not a dynamic change.
Change the pattern every four bars — shift which beats are chords — but never break the quarter pulse.
End on a chord stab so the loop resolves texturally.
examples: root-chord → fifth → fourth-chord → third (quarter-pulse mix)
second-chord → root → fifth-chord → seventh (beat-by-beat texture swap)
rhythmConstraint:river_texture:mixed.watchOut: Single notes are quicker to articulate than chords, so you'll tend to squeeze the chord beats late and the single-note beats early. Force both textures to land square on the click — use the metronome as the arbiter.

rhythmConstraint:burst_texture:singleLin.interaction: Burst rhythm with a single line is flamenco picado or Django Reinhardt gypsy-jazz territory — a flurry of fast notes at the start of the phrase, then silence. The single-voice texture puts all the weight on pitch choice during the burst, and the silence afterward gives the burst room to echo in the listener's memory. The contrast is the music.
steps: Set the metronome to a moderate tempo.
Play 4-6 quick single notes (sixteenths work well) starting on beat 1 — stepwise or with skips, your choice.
Stop dead on beat 3 at the latest — the rest of the phrase is silence.
Let the silence last two beats minimum before the next burst.
Vary the pitch path of each burst but keep the rhythm shape identical: flurry + silence.
examples: (burst) root → third → fifth → seventh → (silence for 2 beats) → (next burst)
(burst) fifth → sixth → fourth → third → second → (silence)
rhythmConstraint:burst_texture:singleLin.watchOut: The temptation is to fill the silence with 'just one more little note.' Don't. The silence is the other half of the phrase and it has to feel long — if you're dropping quick fills in the rest, you're not bursting, you're rambling.

rhythmConstraint:burst_texture:doubleSto.interaction: Burst rhythm with double-stops is the signature of Chuck Berry intros and classic R&B punches — a quick flurry of two-voice stabs followed by silence. Because both voices are firing rapidly, the burst feels bigger and more physical than a single-line burst. Think of the opening to 'Johnny B. Goode' or a Little Richard piano-transcribed guitar punch.
steps: Build a dyad shape (thirds or sixths) you can play fast without looking.
On beat 1, start a burst of 4-5 quick double-stop stabs moving stepwise or skipwise through the scale.
End the burst by beat 2 or 3, then sit in silence until beat 1 of the next bar.
Make sure both voices of every dyad arrive together — any lag kills the burst energy.
Vary the burst's pitch contour each loop but keep the rhythm shape identical.
examples: (burst) root/third → second/fourth → third/fifth → fourth/sixth → (silence)
(burst) fifth/seventh → fourth/sixth → third/fifth → second/fourth → (silence)
rhythmConstraint:burst_texture:doubleSto.watchOut: Double-stop bursts can have one voice lagging a millisecond behind the other, especially at speed. Slow the burst down at first and check: both strings arriving simultaneously? Only then bring the tempo up.

rhythmConstraint:burst_texture:triadPart.interaction: Burst rhythm plus triad partials is the Motown stab — three or four quick partial voicings at the top of the phrase followed by silence. Marvin Gaye's rhythm guitarists, Jerry Jemmott's bass-plus-guitar Stax records, and modern neo-soul punches all live here. The partial texture gives the burst harmonic density without the physical heft of a full chord.
steps: Pick three or four diatonic triad partials in advance — root, fourth, fifth partials work well.
On beat 1, stab them in quick succession (sixteenth or fast eighth rhythm).
Stop by beat 2 and rest until the next bar.
Mute between partials with your fretting hand or palm so each stab is distinct.
Loop and change the partial sequence each repetition for variety.
examples: (burst) root-partial → fourth-partial → fifth-partial → root-partial → (silence)
(burst) second-partial → fifth-partial → sixth-partial → (silence)
rhythmConstraint:burst_texture:triadPart.watchOut: Fast partial stabs tend to bleed into each other harmonically — you hear a smeared chord instead of four distinct punches. Palm-mute aggressively or release fretting-hand pressure between stabs so each partial is surgical.

rhythmConstraint:burst_texture:fullChord.interaction: Burst rhythm with full chords is a Pete Townshend windmill or a Keith Richards slash-chord — four or five full chord hits jammed at the top of the bar, then silence. The physicality is enormous: you're strumming hard and often for a fraction of the bar, then locking the strings still. Rock guitar history is full of this shape.
steps: Pick a full chord voicing you can strum cleanly.
On beat 1, hit the chord 4-5 times in quick succession (sixteenths or fast eighths).
Palm-mute or lift fretting pressure on beat 3 so the chord dies instantly into silence.
Hold the silence for at least two beats.
Loop and change the chord each bar — walking through root, fourth, fifth over a four-bar cycle.
examples: (burst of root-chord × 5) → (silence) → (burst of fourth-chord × 5) → (silence)
(burst of fifth-chord × 4) → (silence) → (burst of root-chord × 4) → (silence)
rhythmConstraint:burst_texture:fullChord.watchOut: Full chord bursts exhaust the strumming arm and blur the silence because the strings keep ringing. The silence is as important as the burst — use your fretting hand to fully mute the strings the instant the burst ends.

rhythmConstraint:burst_texture:mixed.interaction: Burst rhythm with mixed texture is a James Brown horn-section stab followed by a quick single-note fill answer — the burst contains both a chord punch and a single-note response before the silence. This is the tightest funk rhythm guitar move, and it trains the lightning-quick alternation between chord and melody that defines 'chicken pickin' and rhythm-and-blues lead playing.
steps: Start the burst with a chord stab on beat 1.
Immediately follow with 2-3 fast single notes (stepwise or skipwise) in the same burst window.
End the burst by beat 2 and hold silence until next bar.
Keep both textures articulated clearly — the chord and the fills should each be audible within the burst.
Loop with different fill paths but the same burst-and-silence shape.
examples: (burst) root-chord → fifth → fourth → third → (silence)
(burst) second-chord → root → seventh → sixth → (silence)
rhythmConstraint:burst_texture:mixed.watchOut: When the burst has to contain both a chord and single notes, the chord usually eats too much time and the single notes become a mumble. Shorten the chord strum (one quick down-strum only) so the fills have room inside the burst.

rhythmConstraint:space_texture:singleLin.interaction: Space rhythm with a single line is Jim Hall, Bill Frisell, Ry Cooder's sparest work — each note floats in silence, and the ear has time to savor its placement and its decay. Because half the bar is rest, every note is precious, and the single-voice texture means there's nowhere for weakness to hide. Pitch choice and timing become everything.
steps: Set a slow-to-medium metronome.
Play ONE note per half-bar (two beats on, two beats off) using only scale tones.
Let each note ring its full half-bar; don't dampen it early.
Choose each next note deliberately — this is the opposite of improvising fast.
End on a root or third that can hold sustain into the silence.
examples: root (held 2 beats) → (rest 2 beats) → fifth (held 2 beats) → (rest 2 beats)
third (held) → (rest) → sixth (held) → (rest) → fifth (held) → (rest) → root (held)
rhythmConstraint:space_texture:singleLin.watchOut: Silence is terrifying for improvisers — you'll want to fill it. Sit with the rests and count them out loud if necessary. Every rest is a note too; treat it with the same weight.

rhythmConstraint:space_texture:doubleSto.interaction: Space rhythm plus double-stops is hymnal, devotional — two voices arriving together on a single beat, then vanishing into silence. Classical guitar transcriptions of Bach chorales have this quality; so does the sparest Americana fingerstyle work.
