The combination asks for perfect dyad balance (both voices loud enough, ringing together) because there's no other sound to distract.
steps: Set a slow metronome.
Play ONE double-stop per bar, on beat 1, and let it ring for a full two beats.
Rest for the other two beats — total silence, no muting needed if the dyad has decayed naturally.
The next bar gets a new dyad, stepping or leaping to a different scale position.
Keep the attack on both strings perfectly simultaneous — no flam, no roll.
examples: root/third (ring 2 beats) → (rest 2 beats) → third/fifth (ring 2 beats) → (rest)
fifth/seventh (ring) → (rest) → fourth/sixth (ring) → (rest)
rhythmConstraint:space_texture:doubleSto.watchOut: The space rhythm tempts you to add decorative notes inside the rest because 'it's empty.' No — the rest is part of the music. If you're adding grace notes or quick fills during the silence, you've abandoned the constraint.

rhythmConstraint:space_texture:triadPart.interaction: Space rhythm plus triad partials is ambient-cinematic — one three-voice chord per bar, hanging in air, then silence. Think the sparest moments of a Mark Knopfler soundtrack or Bill Frisell's film work. The partial texture gives enough harmonic color to make each chord feel like an arrival, and the space lets each voicing breathe.
steps: Set a slow metronome.
Play one diatonic triad partial per bar, on beat 1, letting it ring until the natural decay.
Let the silence between chords feel genuinely empty — minimum two beats.
Move through 4-6 partials across the round, one per bar, at a patient pace.
End on a root-chord partial with the longest hold of the round.
examples: root-partial (hold 2 beats, rest 2) → fourth-partial (hold 2, rest 2) → fifth-partial (hold 2, rest 2) → root-partial (hold)
second-partial (hold, rest) → sixth-partial (hold, rest) → fourth-partial (hold, rest)
rhythmConstraint:space_texture:triadPart.watchOut: Triad partials can ring into the rests and blur the silence. Use your fretting hand to lift slightly and mute the strings at the start of the rest — the silence must be crisp and intentional, not a slow fade.

rhythmConstraint:space_texture:fullChord.interaction: Space rhythm with full chords is monumental — one huge voicing per bar, ringing into silence like a church bell. Solo acoustic arrangements of film-score ballads, hymn arrangements for solo guitar, and slow-core rock verses all work this way. The combination asks you to treat each chord as an event, not a beat in a progression.
steps: Set a slow metronome.
Play one full chord voicing per bar, strumming on beat 1 with a firm but controlled attack.
Let the chord ring through to beat 3, then naturally decay into silence.
Rest for the second half of the bar, counting the beats silently.
Move to the next chord on the next downbeat, cycling through a diatonic progression.
examples: root-chord (ring 2 beats, rest 2) → fourth-chord (ring, rest) → fifth-chord (ring, rest) → root-chord (ring)
second-chord (ring, rest) → fifth-chord (ring, rest) → sixth-chord (ring, rest) → root-chord
rhythmConstraint:space_texture:fullChord.watchOut: Full chords sustain long enough to bleed into the rest — that's fine, but it means the silence is actually shorter than you think. Count the beats of sustain and rest carefully, or you'll drift early into the next chord.

rhythmConstraint:space_texture:mixed.interaction: Space rhythm with mixed texture is a solo fingerstyle device — one chord stab followed by a single-note answer, then silence. Think of a Tommy Emmanuel or Tuck Andress arrangement where the chord speaks, then the melody replies, then both rest together. The space gives each texture room to land and decay before the next event.
steps: Play a chord stab on beat 1 and let it ring for one full beat.
On beat 2, play a single scale note as a response, letting it ring briefly.
Beats 3 and 4 are silence — total rest.
Next bar, new chord and new single-note answer, same rhythm shape.
End on a chord stab that rings into total silence at the end of the round.
examples: root-chord (beat 1) → fifth (beat 2) → (rest beats 3-4) → fourth-chord → third → (rest)
second-chord → root → (rest) → fifth-chord → root → (rest)
rhythmConstraint:space_texture:mixed.watchOut: The single-note response tends to sneak toward beat 2.5 or 3 when you want it to breathe more, which shrinks the silence. Place the single-note firmly on beat 2 and stop — let the rest do its work.

rhythmConstraint:offbeat_texture:singleL.interaction: Offbeat rhythm with a single line is ska-reggae upstroke territory translated into melody — every note lands between the beats, never on 1 or 3. The single-voice texture makes the offbeat placement maximally audible. Think of a Toots Hibbert guitar fill or a Khruangbin melodic line that dodges the downbeats entirely.
steps: Set a metronome clicking on beats 1 and 3 only (or count them silently).
Play every note on the AND of a beat — 1-and, 2-and, 3-and, 4-and — never on the click.
Use both stepwise and leap motion through the scale, but every attack must be offbeat.
Keep the picking hand pulse physical — your arm knows where beat 1 is even though you're not playing it.
End the phrase on an offbeat that resolves to a strong scale tone (root or fifth).
examples: (and-of-1) root → (and-of-2) third → (and-of-3) fifth → (and-of-4) third
(and-of-1) fifth → (and-of-2) fourth → (and-of-3) third → (and-of-4) root
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_texture:singleL.watchOut: The strongest beat in your muscle memory is 1, and you will accidentally land on it. The fix: count '1-and' and always pick on the 'and.' If you slip, stop and restart the bar — don't let a wrong-beat note live.

rhythmConstraint:offbeat_texture:doubleS.interaction: Offbeat double-stops is reggae skank applied to two voices — every upbeat gets a dyad stab, every downbeat is silence or a muted chuck. Lynn Taitt, Ernest Ranglin, and modern ska-punk rhythm guitarists all build grooves this way. The double-stop texture thickens the offbeat punch without filling out a full chord.
steps: Set the metronome on 1 and 3.
Play a dyad (third or sixth interval) on the and of every beat — 1-and, 2-and, 3-and, 4-and.
Use short, crisp upstrokes so each dyad is a punch, not a ring.
Palm-mute or lift the fretting hand on the downbeats so there's true silence between stabs.
Walk the dyads diatonically through the scale while keeping the offbeat rhythm perfect.
examples: (and-1) root/third → (and-2) second/fourth → (and-3) third/fifth → (and-4) fourth/sixth
(and-1) fifth/seventh → (and-2) fourth/sixth → (and-3) third/fifth → (and-4) second/fourth
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_texture:doubleS.watchOut: Offbeat double-stops start drifting toward the downbeat the moment you get comfortable — your hand wants to fall into 1. Record and check: every stab should be exactly halfway between clicks, not slightly before.

rhythmConstraint:offbeat_texture:triadPa.interaction: Offbeat triad partials is the core of reggae rhythm guitar — the skank. Every upbeat gets a three-voice partial stab, every downbeat is silent or muted. Bob Marley's rhythm guitarists, Lee Perry's productions, and modern dub-soul artists all use this exact combination. The texture and rhythm together ARE reggae.
steps: Set metronome on beats 1 and 3.
Choose a triad partial (top three strings work best) on the root chord.
On every AND, play a short, muted upstroke of the partial.
Keep the downbeats completely silent — your fretting hand lifts or palm mutes.
Cycle through different triad partials (root → fourth → fifth partials) while maintaining the perfect offbeat pulse.
examples: (and-1) root-partial → (and-2) root-partial → (and-3) fourth-partial → (and-4) fourth-partial
(and-1) fifth-partial → (and-2) fifth-partial → (and-3) root-partial → (and-4) root-partial
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_texture:triadPa.watchOut: The classic reggae mistake is the partial ringing into the next downbeat and muddying the offbeat. Cut each stab short with your fretting hand immediately after the upstroke — the silence is as important as the stab.

rhythmConstraint:offbeat_texture:fullCho.interaction: Offbeat full chord stabs is the most physically demanding version of the skank — a full voicing punched on every upbeat. This is how The Clash played reggae-influenced songs, or how Willie Nelson's rhythm player drives a country waltz. Full chords on the upbeat require precise muting because the voicing rings longer than a partial.
steps: Metronome on 1 and 3.
Hit a full chord voicing on the AND of every beat with a crisp upstroke.
Immediately mute with fretting hand pressure release so the chord doesn't bleed into the next click.
Walk through diatonic chords (root → fourth → fifth → root) keeping the offbeat rhythm perfect.
Vary the voicing position on the neck across the round to keep the texture alive.
examples: (and-1) root-chord → (and-2) root-chord → (and-3) fourth-chord → (and-4) fourth-chord
(and-1) second-chord → (and-2) fifth-chord → (and-3) root-chord → (and-4) sixth-chord
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_texture:fullCho.watchOut: Full chord offbeats are exhausting and tempo-drifting — your arm tires, the stabs start landing early. Shorten the strum motion: a small, tight upstroke, not a big arc. Efficiency is the only way to stay on the offbeat for a full round.

rhythmConstraint:offbeat_texture:mixed.interaction: Offbeat rhythm with mixed texture is 70s funk rhythm guitar at its most precise — a chord stab on one offbeat, a single-note answer on the next offbeat, then back to chord. Jimmy Nolen's James Brown work is the textbook example. Every attack lives on an upbeat; the downbeats are silent holes that the band's bassist and drummer will fill.
steps: Metronome on 1 and 3.
Decide a pattern: chord stab on 1-and and 3-and, single notes on 2-and and 4-and.
Play it strictly, one attack per offbeat, nothing on the downbeats.
Single notes should walk diatonically between chord stabs — creating a tiny melody inside the offbeat grid.
Loop for four bars without breaking the pattern.
examples: (and-1) root-chord → (and-2) fifth → (and-3) fourth-chord → (and-4) third
(and-1) second-chord → (and-2) root → (and-3) fifth-chord → (and-4) seventh
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_texture:mixed.watchOut: The chord stab eats picking time and the single note after it lands late — or vice versa. Use a metronome that clicks on the ANDS for a few passes so you can hear whether you're hitting the offbeat perfectly for both textures.

rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_texture:sing.interaction: Rhythm seed with single line is classical-theme-and-variations thinking — pick a rhythm cell (say, dotted-quarter + eighth + two sixteenths) and keep repeating it while the pitches evolve. Beethoven built whole movements this way; Bach built inventions on a single rhythmic idea. The single-voice texture keeps the rhythm cell transparent.
steps: Invent a short rhythm cell (2-3 beats long) and clap it until it's in your body.
Play the cell with any scale pitches for one bar — the rhythm is fixed, the pitches are free.
Next bar, repeat the EXACT same rhythm cell but change the pitches — different melodic shape, identical timing.
Continue for 6-8 bars, varying pitch each time while the rhythm never budges.
End by playing the cell with a resolving pitch path (landing on root or third).
examples: (rhythm: dotted-quarter + eighth + quarter) root → fifth → third / second → fourth → seventh / third → sixth → root
(rhythm: eighth + eighth + quarter + quarter) root-third-fifth-seventh / fifth-third-root-second / seventh-fifth-fourth-root
rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_texture:sing.watchOut: The rhythm cell drifts when you get excited about pitch choices. Tap the cell on your thigh before every bar to reset — if the rhythm moves even one sixteenth, you've broken the seed.

rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_texture:doub.interaction: Rhythm seed with double-stops is country-gospel theme-and-variations — the rhythm cell repeats while the dyad shapes walk through the scale in parallel. This is how David Rawlings or Tony Rice builds a chorus variation without changing the underlying groove. The two-voice texture thickens the cell while the fixed rhythm holds the shape.
steps: Pick a rhythm cell 2-3 beats long and a starting dyad interval (thirds or sixths).
Play the cell with a double-stop on every note — the rhythm is identical, the dyads walk.
Move the dyad shape stepwise through the scale across iterations, keeping the rhythm locked.
Vary the dyad direction (up, down) across repetitions while the rhythm stays the same.
End on a root/third or root/fifth dyad played with the seed rhythm for closure.
examples: (rhythm: quarter + eighth + eighth + quarter) root/third → second/fourth → third/fifth variations
(rhythm: dotted-eighth + sixteenth + quarter) fifth/seventh → fourth/sixth → third/fifth
rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_texture:doub.watchOut: Double-stops on a fixed rhythm cell tempt you to modify the cell when a dyad is awkward — 'I'll add a sixteenth here so it lines up.' No. The rhythm is sacred. Pick a dyad shape you can play cleanly IN the cell or pick a different dyad shape.

rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_texture:tria.interaction: Rhythm seed plus triad partials is reggae-soul variation writing — a rhythm cell that repeats with different chord partial voicings each iteration. Nile Rodgers' Chic work has this quality: a relentless rhythmic idea with shifting harmonic colors above it. The three-voice partial gives enough harmony for the cell to feel like a chord progression in miniature.
steps: Set a short rhythm cell (2-3 beats, with at least one subdivision smaller than quarter).
Pick three triad partials in advance — root, fourth, fifth partials, for example.
Play the rhythm cell with the root partial, then exact same rhythm with the fourth partial, then the fifth partial.
Keep the rhythm unchanged across partials — only the harmonic voicing shifts.
Cycle through the partials across 4-6 bars, maintaining the cell perfectly.
examples: (rhythm: dotted-eighth + sixteenth + quarter + eighth + eighth) root-partial / fourth-partial / fifth-partial / root-partial
(rhythm: eighth + eighth + quarter + eighth + eighth) second-partial / fifth-partial / sixth-partial
rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_texture:tria.watchOut: Triad partials have different finger shapes, and you'll unconsciously shorten the rhythm cell when switching to a harder shape. Slow the tempo until every partial can be played cleanly with the EXACT seed rhythm, then build speed.

rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_texture:full.interaction: Rhythm seed with full chords is the funk-rhythm-guitar signature — a short rhythm cell that repeats identically while the chord voicing underneath changes from bar to bar. Jimmy Nolen, Nile Rodgers, and Prince all built songs this way. The full chord fills the cell with maximum harmonic information, so the rhythm cell's shape has to be crystal clear.
steps: Write out a 2-bar rhythm cell with mixed subdivisions (e.g., dotted-quarter, two sixteenths, eighth, eighth).
Play the cell with the root chord, strumming each rhythmic attack as a full voicing.
Next bar, play the EXACT same cell with the fourth chord — every attack identical, just a new voicing.
Next bar, fifth chord, same cell.
After the progression cycles once, vary the chord sequence (root → sixth → fourth → fifth) while keeping the cell rigid.
examples: (rhythm cell) with root-chord → fourth-chord → fifth-chord → root-chord
(rhythm cell) with second-chord → fifth-chord → root-chord → sixth-chord
rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_texture:full.watchOut: Full chord strums tire the picking hand, and when tired you unconsciously shorten the cell's slow notes. Check tempo and cell length after every bar — if the dotted-quarter has become a plain quarter, you've fatigued the seed.

rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_texture:mixe.interaction: Rhythm seed with mixed texture is where rhythm guitarists become arrangers — the rhythm cell stays constant, but one iteration the cell is all chord stabs, the next iteration it's all single notes, the next it's a mix. The rhythm shape holds the identity while the texture morphs, which is how a verse-chorus transition often works without a chord change.
steps: Pick a short rhythm cell (2-3 beats, with one strong short note).
First bar: play the cell as all chord stabs.
Second bar: play the EXACT same cell as all single notes (any scale pitch).
Third bar: play the cell with mixed texture — chord on the longest note, single notes on the shorter subdivisions.
Loop the three-bar cycle with slight variations; the rhythm never changes.
examples: (rhythm: dotted-quarter + eighth + two sixteenths) root-chord all-stabs / fifth + third + fourth + sixth single-line / root-chord + fifth + third + fourth mix
(rhythm: eighth + eighth + quarter + eighth + eighth) second-chord all-stabs / root + third + fifth + seventh single / second-chord + root + fifth + seventh mix
rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_texture:mixe.watchOut: When switching textures inside a fixed rhythm cell, the physical time required for a chord vs a single note is different, so the rhythm feels 'heavier' when chord-based. Maintain strict metronomic accuracy — the cell is defined by when, not by what.

rhythmConstraint:triplets_texture:single.interaction: Triplets with a single line is the swing-jazz language — the lilting three-per-beat feel of Wes Montgomery, Charlie Christian, Kenny Burrell. The single-voice texture puts all the focus on how the triplet subdivisions flow through the scale, and your picking hand has to find a consistent three-notes-per-beat pulse that feels natural, not mechanical.
steps: Set the metronome to a moderate tempo and count 'one-trip-let, two-trip-let' aloud.
Play three scale tones per click, walking stepwise or with small skips through the scale.
Use alternate picking (down-up-down, then up-down-up) so the triplets flow smoothly.
Change direction every two beats so the triplet line has phrasing, not just running motion.
End on the 'one' of a bar with a strong scale tone (root or third) for a clean stop.
examples: (beat 1) root-second-third (beat 2) fourth-fifth-sixth (beat 3) fifth-fourth-third (beat 4) second-root
(beat 1) fifth-sixth-seventh (beat 2) root-up-octave-seventh-sixth (beat 3) fifth-fourth-third (beat 4) second-root
rhythmConstraint:triplets_texture:single.watchOut: Triplets collapse into sixteenths the moment you lose the three-per-beat pulse — you'll play four notes where three belong. Count aloud or tap your foot on every beat to anchor the triplet feel.

rhythmConstraint:triplets_texture:double.interaction: Triplet double-stops is an unusual but powerful combination — three dyad attacks per beat, which is physically demanding on the picking hand but sonorous and thick. Bluegrass mandolin tremolo players work this territory on strings; on guitar, think of Duane Allman's fastest double-stop runs or Doc Watson's gospel-triplet work.
steps: Set a moderate metronome and count three per beat.
Play dyads (thirds or sixths) with three attacks per click, walking the dyad through the scale.
Use alternate strumming or down-up-down picking to keep all three triplet notes even.
Move the dyad stepwise across beats so the triplet flow has melodic direction.
End on a strong double-stop (root/third) at the 'one' of a bar.
examples: (beat 1) root/third × 3 (beat 2) second/fourth × 3 (beat 3) third/fifth × 3 (beat 4) second/fourth × 3
(beat 1) fifth/seventh × 3 (beat 2) fourth/sixth × 3 (beat 3) third/fifth × 3 (beat 4) root/third
rhythmConstraint:triplets_texture:double.watchOut: Three double-stops per beat tires the picking arm fast; when tired, the triplets unevenly space. Lighten the pick attack — just enough to sound both strings — and let the forearm relax between attacks.

rhythmConstraint:triplets_texture:triadP.interaction: Triplet triad partials is the jazz-comp lilt of a Joe Pass accompaniment or a Lenny Breau fingerstyle passage — three chord-partial attacks per beat, which gives the groove a rolling forward momentum while keeping the harmony flowing. The three-voice texture and three-per-beat rhythm create a natural match.
steps: Set a moderate metronome and count three-per-beat.
Play a triad partial three times in quick succession on beat 1, then move to a different partial on beat 2, etc.
Use an alternating up-down-up picking or thumb-finger pattern so the triplets stay even.
Walk through four diatonic partials over four beats, three attacks each.
End on a root partial at the 'one' of the next bar with a longer held strum.
examples: (beat 1) root-partial × 3 (beat 2) fourth-partial × 3 (beat 3) fifth-partial × 3 (beat 4) root-partial × 3
(beat 1) second-partial × 3 (beat 2) fifth-partial × 3 (beat 3) sixth-partial × 3 (beat 4) root-partial × 3
rhythmConstraint:triplets_texture:triadP.watchOut: Three partial strums per beat merges into a buzz if the strums aren't clearly articulated. Palm-mute between attacks or release fretting pressure so each of the three attacks is distinct.

rhythmConstraint:triplets_texture:fullCh.interaction: Triplet full chord strumming is waltz-on-steroids, or Spanish-gypsy flamenco-strum energy — three full strums per beat, huge sound, cascading forward. This is how flamenco guitarists build rasgueado patterns, or how a bluegrass rhythm guitarist drives a waltz. The physicality is intense but the result is unstoppable momentum.
steps: Set a moderate metronome and count three per beat.
Play a full chord voicing with three strum attacks per click — down-up-down or rolling-finger pattern.
Keep the strums even in volume so the triplet feel is clean, not a loud-soft-loud stumble.
Change chords every beat, cycling through a diatonic progression.
End with a held strum of the root chord at the next bar's 'one.'
examples: (beat 1) root-chord × 3 (beat 2) fourth-chord × 3 (beat 3) fifth-chord × 3 (beat 4) root-chord × 3
(beat 1) second-chord × 3 (beat 2) fifth-chord × 3 (beat 3) root-chord × 3 (beat 4) sixth-chord × 3
rhythmConstraint:triplets_texture:fullCh.watchOut: Full chord triplets exhaust the strumming arm within a few bars, and the triplet spacing stretches. Use a small wrist motion rather than a big arm swing, and keep the pick light enough that all six strings ring but nothing is chopped.

rhythmConstraint:triplets_texture:mixed.interaction: Triplet rhythm with mixed texture is where jazz-funk fusion rhythm guitarists shine — a chord triplet burst followed by single-note triplet fill, maintaining the three-per-beat pulse across the texture change. This is how Herbie Hancock's guitarists or George Benson in comping mode move between harmony and melody without ever breaking the swing.
steps: Set a moderate metronome with triplet feel.
On beats 1 and 3, play a chord with three triplet strums.
On beats 2 and 4, play three single notes in triplet time (stepwise or skipwise).
Keep the three-per-beat pulse perfectly even across the texture change.
Loop with different chord and fill combinations but the same texture pattern.
examples: (beat 1) root-chord × 3 (beat 2) fifth-fourth-third (beat 3) fourth-chord × 3 (beat 4) third-second-root
(beat 1) second-chord × 3 (beat 2) root-seventh-sixth (beat 3) fifth-chord × 3 (beat 4) seventh-root-second
rhythmConstraint:triplets_texture:mixed.watchOut: Triplet feel is fragile across texture changes — the chord triplets want to be slower, the single-note triplets want to rush. Count the triplet subdivisions aloud across the whole bar and make every beat contain exactly three attacks, whether chord or single-note.

rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_texture:singleLin.interaction: Tresillo (3+3+2) with a single line is the rhythmic backbone of Afro-Cuban, reggaeton, and habanera music rendered as pure melody — the eight eighth-notes of a bar divided into 3+3+2 creates a subtle syncopation that single-note texture exposes beautifully. Every pitch lands on a tresillo accent, and the uneven spacing gives the line an infectious forward lean.
steps: Count the bar as '1-2-3, 1-2-3, 1-2' (three + three + two eighth-notes).
Play one note on each tresillo attack (three attacks per bar, not eight).
Walk or skip through the scale, landing each attack on a diatonic tone.
Feel the forward push of the 3+3+2 — it naturally wants to cycle.
Loop for four bars with different pitches, but the rhythm stays fixed.
examples: (3) root (3) fifth (2) third / (3) second (3) sixth (2) fourth
(3) fifth (3) root-up-octave (2) sixth / (3) third (3) seventh (2) fifth
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_texture:singleLin.watchOut: The tresillo is often mis-felt as '1, 2+, 4' (dotted-quarter + dotted-quarter + quarter). That's correct, but beginners accent it wrong — the third attack on beat 4 feels weak. Give every tresillo attack equal weight; the internal syncopation does the work.

rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_texture:doubleSto.interaction: Tresillo with double-stops is the classic reggaeton-dembow dyad punch — three offset attacks per bar, each a two-voice chord stab. This rhythm-texture combination is how a lot of modern Afro-Caribbean rhythm guitar works, and it's the heartbeat of songs from 'El Watusi' to any contemporary reggaeton track translated to guitar.
steps: Count '1-2-3, 1-2-3, 1-2' so the tresillo pulse is locked in your body.
Play a diatonic dyad (thirds or sixths) on each tresillo attack — three dyads per bar.
Use a crisp downstroke on both strings so the two voices punch together.
Walk the dyads stepwise or with small skips across attacks while keeping the tresillo rhythm perfect.
End on a root/third dyad at the start of the next bar to close the loop.
examples: (3) root/third (3) third/fifth (2) fourth/sixth / (3) fifth/seventh (3) fourth/sixth (2) third/fifth
(3) second/fourth (3) fifth/seventh (2) root/third / (3) sixth/root-up-octave (3) fifth/seventh (2) fourth/sixth
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_texture:doubleSto.watchOut: Double-stops on the second tresillo attack (the 'and of 2') tend to drift early because that attack has no downbeat to anchor it. Tap your foot on every beat and make sure the second attack lands exactly on the and-of-2.

rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_texture:triadPart.interaction: Tresillo plus triad partials is the dembow-skank hybrid — three chord-partial stabs per bar in the 3+3+2 pattern. This is how Jamaican one-drop guitar players subtly shift between reggae skank and dancehall feel, and how modern Latin-pop rhythm guitarists build grooves under reggaeton vocals. The partial texture keeps the harmony mobile within the rhythmic framework.
steps: Internalize '1-2-3, 1-2-3, 1-2' by tapping it on your thigh.
Pick three diatonic triad partials to cycle through — root, fourth, fifth.
Play one partial on each tresillo attack, three per bar.
Keep the picking-hand strum uniform (crisp, short upstrokes).
Walk the partials diatonically across bars while the tresillo rhythm repeats exactly.
examples: (3) root-partial (3) fourth-partial (2) fifth-partial / (3) root-partial (3) sixth-partial (2) fourth-partial
(3) second-partial (3) fifth-partial (2) sixth-partial / (3) fourth-partial (3) root-partial (2) fifth-partial
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_texture:triadPart.watchOut: The natural tendency with triad partials is to fall back into straight quarter notes — the tresillo collapses into 'chord-rest-chord-rest.' Fight this by counting eighth notes aloud and placing the second and third attacks exactly on the off-positions of the 3+3+2 count.

rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_texture:fullChord.interaction: Tresillo with full chord voicings is the habanera-bolero guitar part — three big strummed voicings per bar in the 3+3+2 pattern. Cuban son musicians, classic bolero guitarists, and modern Latin-jazz accompanists all use this. The full chord texture gives each tresillo attack maximum harmonic weight, which makes the internal syncopation feel grand rather than nervous.
steps: Count the 3+3+2 pattern aloud until it's in your body.
Strum a full chord voicing on each tresillo attack — three strums per bar.
Let each chord ring through its allotted eighth-note window without cutting it short.
Move through a diatonic chord progression (root → fourth → fifth) with one chord per bar, three strums each.
End on a held root chord at the next bar's downbeat.
examples: (3) root-chord (3) root-chord (2) root-chord / (3) fourth-chord (3) fourth-chord (2) fourth-chord
(3) second-chord (3) second-chord (2) second-chord / (3) fifth-chord (3) fifth-chord (2) fifth-chord
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_texture:fullChord.watchOut: Full chord tresillos tempt you to keep the same chord for three attacks without change, which is correct — but beginners get bored and change the chord on the second attack, breaking the rhythm-feel. Stick with one chord per bar; the tresillo is the rhythmic interest.

rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_texture:mixed.interaction: Tresillo with mixed texture is the tightest Afro-Caribbean rhythm guitar move — a chord stab on the first tresillo attack, single notes on the second and third, or vice versa. Every attack in the 3+3+2 is either a chord or a note, and the texture variation maps onto the rhythm's uneven spacing. This is how modern Latin-rock rhythm guitarists (think Santana's chord+fill vocabulary) build grooves.
steps: Lock the tresillo count ('1-2-3, 1-2-3, 1-2') in your body.
Play chord stabs on the first and third tresillo attacks, single notes on the middle attack.
Keep both textures at the same volume so the texture change is the feature.
Walk the single-note fills diatonically between the chord stabs.
Loop with different fill pitches but the same texture pattern.
examples: (3) root-chord (3) fifth (2) root-chord / (3) fourth-chord (3) third (2) fourth-chord
(3) second-chord (3) root (2) fifth-chord / (3) sixth-chord (3) fourth (2) root-chord
rhythmConstraint:3-3-2_texture:mixed.watchOut: The middle tresillo attack (the single note) is the hardest to place — it's offbeat and surrounded by chord stabs. Isolate it by playing the rhythm as 'chord, REST, chord' first to feel where the silence lives, then fill the silence with the single note.

dynamics:swell_texture:singleLine.interaction: Swell dynamics with a single line is violin-bow territory on guitar — building from near-silence to full voice across a melodic phrase and cutting off sharply. The single-voice texture means every note in the line carries a different volume, and you're sculpting a 3D shape through time. Think Jeff Beck's melodic swells or a volume-pedal ambient solo.
steps: Start the phrase at the quietest volume your pick can produce cleanly.
Play a single scale line walking up or down, slightly increasing pick attack on each note.
Peak on the last note or two of the phrase at full volume — that's where the swell arrives.
Cut the sustain dead on the last note (palm mute or lift fretting hand).
Leave a silence before the next swell begins, so the shape is clear.
examples: root (pp) → third (p) → fifth (mp) → sixth (mf) → seventh (f) → root-up-octave (ff) → (silence)
fifth (pp) → fourth (p) → third (mp) → second (mf) → root (f) → (silence)
dynamics:swell_texture:singleLine.watchOut: Linear swells often plateau in the middle — soft, soft, soft, LOUD — because incremental volume is hard to hear in single notes. Force every note to be ONE click louder than the previous; if you can't hear the difference, decrease your starting volume.

dynamics:swell_texture:doubleStops.interaction: Swell with double-stops is cinematic — two voices building together from whisper to roar, then silence. Classical guitar has this as 'crescendo con moto,' pop ballads use it in the chorus lift, Santo and Johnny lived in this territory. Because both voices are crescendoing together, the volume build feels twice as dramatic as a single-line swell.
steps: Start the phrase with a dyad at the quietest pick pressure you can produce.
Walk the dyad stepwise or leap-wise through the scale, increasing pick attack on every dyad.
Keep both voices balanced at each volume level — don't let one string get louder than the other.
Peak at the final dyad of the phrase at maximum volume.
Kill the sustain instantly after the peak dyad.
examples: root/third (pp) → third/fifth (p) → fifth/seventh (mp) → sixth/root-up-octave (mf) → root-up-octave/third-up (f) → (silence)
second/fourth (pp) → fourth/sixth (p) → sixth/root-up-octave (mp) → fifth/seventh (mf) → root/third (f) → (silence)
dynamics:swell_texture:doubleStops.watchOut: Double-stops can develop one-voice-louder-than-the-other imbalance as you push volume — the bass string tends to dominate at high attack. Check your pick angle: flat to both strings, not angled toward the lower one.

dynamics:swell_texture:triadPartial.interaction: Swell with triad partials is a classic build-up device — three voices in parallel, each growing louder together across the phrase. Think of the opening bars of a Sigur Rós song or a Radiohead guitar wash rendered harmonically. The partial texture gives enough density that the swell feels like the whole instrument is rising.
steps: Start with a very quiet triad partial strum — barely audible.
Walk the partial stepwise up the scale across 4-6 bars, increasing strum intensity each bar.
Peak on the highest partial with a full strum, letting it ring at maximum volume.
Cut the partial dead after the peak with a fretting-hand mute.
Silence follows the peak for at least two beats before looping.
examples: root-partial (pp) → second-partial (p) → third-partial (mp) → fourth-partial (mf) → fifth-partial (ff) → (silence)
third-partial (pp) → fourth-partial (p) → fifth-partial (mp) → sixth-partial (mf) → root-up-octave partial (ff)
dynamics:swell_texture:triadPartial.watchOut: Partial voicings swell well but the three-string strum can muddy at high volume — individual strings blur together. At the peak, make sure each of the three strings is still audible, not smashed into a wall of sound.

dynamics:swell_texture:fullChord.interaction: Swell with full chords is power-ballad bridge territory — building from a whispered strum to a wall-of-sound chord, then drop to silence. Every pop rock song from 'Bohemian Rhapsody' to U2 anthems uses this. The full chord texture means the swell has maximum harmonic information at peak, so the contrast from quiet to loud is shattering.
steps: Start with the quietest possible chord strum — fingertips only, barely touching the strings.
Repeat the same chord across 4-8 bars, gradually increasing strum intensity.
Peak on the final strum at maximum volume with a full, hard downstroke.
Kill the strings instantly after the peak with a fretting-hand mute.
Let the silence be total for 2 full beats before looping.
examples: root-chord (pp) × 4 → root-chord (p) × 2 → root-chord (mp) × 2 → root-chord (f) × 1 → (silence)
fourth-chord (pp) × 2 → fourth-chord (mf) × 2 → fourth-chord (ff) × 1 → (silence)
dynamics:swell_texture:fullChord.watchOut: Full chord swells always feel dramatic enough in the middle, so you stop building — then the peak isn't a peak, it's a plateau. Force the final strum to be noticeably louder than the one before it; there should be a clear 'climax' attack.

dynamics:swell_texture:mixed.interaction: Swell with mixed texture is theatrical — a quiet chord stab, a single-note fill, another slightly louder chord stab, another fill, building together in volume toward a climax chord. This is how a cinematic fingerstyle arranger builds tension in a solo piece, or how a Bon Iver/Sufjan arrangement crescendos over an acoustic guitar.
steps: Start the phrase with a barely-audible chord stab, then a soft single-note fill.
Each subsequent chord stab + fill pair is slightly louder than the last.
Keep the texture pattern constant — always chord then fill — while volume climbs.
Peak on the final chord stab at full volume.
Cut everything to silence after the peak.
examples: root-chord (pp) → third (pp fill) → fourth-chord (p) → second (p fill) → fifth-chord (mf) → root (mf fill) → root-chord (ff) → (silence)
second-chord (pp) → seventh (pp) → fifth-chord (p) → third (p) → root-chord (f) → (silence)
dynamics:swell_texture:mixed.watchOut: Mixed texture swells tend to have the chord stabs getting louder while the single-note fills plateau — you're swelling one texture and not the other. Both textures must climb in parallel; every pair should be louder than the previous pair.

dynamics:terraces_texture:singleLine.interaction: Terraces dynamics with a single line is Baroque-harpsichord thinking applied to guitar — sudden jumps between volume plateaus, no fades. Bach wrote this into his keyboard music because the harpsichord couldn't crescendo, only switch manuals. On guitar, it trains precise volume control without any gradual transition, which is harder than it sounds.
steps: Pick two volume plateaus — loud and soft.
Play a short melodic phrase (4 notes) entirely at the soft plateau.
Play another short phrase (4 notes) entirely at the loud plateau — no transition between plateaus.
Alternate the plateaus bar by bar, never crescendoing or decrescendoing.
Mark the switch with a pick-attack change: soft uses fingertips or light pick, loud uses a firm full-pick stroke.
examples: (soft) root → third → second → root / (loud) fifth → sixth → fourth → fifth / (soft) second → third → root → second
(soft) fourth → fifth → third → second / (loud) sixth → seventh → fifth → fourth
dynamics:terraces_texture:singleLine.watchOut: The sudden volume switch is hard to execute cleanly — you'll accidentally fade between the plateaus. Every note within a plateau must be the same volume; every transition between plateaus must be instantaneous.

dynamics:terraces_texture:doubleStops.interaction: Terraces with double-stops is how classical duo arrangements handle contrast — two voices at a loud plateau, then two voices at a soft plateau, no fades. Transcriptions of Scarlatti harpsichord sonatas to guitar use this. The challenge is keeping both voices at exactly the same plateau simultaneously when the switch happens.
steps: Pick two volume plateaus and stick to them.
Play a sequence of double-stops at the soft plateau for one bar.
Switch instantly to the loud plateau for the next bar — both voices jump together.
Keep the dyad texture constant across plateaus; only volume changes.
Alternate plateaus bar by bar for the whole round.
examples: (soft) root/third → third/fifth → fourth/sixth → fifth/seventh / (loud) sixth/root-up-octave → fifth/seventh → fourth/sixth → root/third
(soft) second/fourth → third/fifth → fourth/sixth / (loud) fifth/seventh → sixth/root-up-octave
dynamics:terraces_texture:doubleStops.watchOut: On the plateau-switch beat, one voice often jumps to the new plateau a fraction before the other — especially if your pick favors one string. Both voices must switch simultaneously. Practice the transition beat in isolation until both strings jump together.

dynamics:terraces_texture:triadPartial.interaction: Terraces with triad partials is how a soul organist fakes a Leslie rotor shift — sudden plateau jumps between loud and soft three-voice chords. On guitar, this is the Motown punch-and-release trick: loud partial-stab, silent-soft response, loud again. The three-voice texture gives each plateau full harmonic presence.
steps: Pick two plateaus.
Play a triad partial at the soft plateau for one bar — clean, quiet strums.
Switch to the loud plateau immediately for the next bar — same partial, fuller strums.
Alternate bar-by-bar, no fades.
Walk through diatonic partials across the round to add harmonic variety.
examples: (soft) root-partial × 4 / (loud) root-partial × 4 / (soft) fourth-partial × 4 / (loud) fourth-partial × 4
(soft) second-partial × 4 / (loud) fifth-partial × 4 / (soft) sixth-partial × 4 / (loud) root-partial × 4
dynamics:terraces_texture:triadPartial.watchOut: Triad partials on loud plateaus often smear into distortion-like blurs because the strums are rushed. Keep the partial strums clean and controlled; loud doesn't mean sloppy.

dynamics:terraces_texture:fullChord.interaction: Terraces with full chords is theatrical — loud chord, soft chord, loud chord, soft chord, with no transitions. This is how Beethoven-style piano chord writing maps onto guitar, or how a folk-rock singer-songwriter accents a chorus without any crescendo. Full chord texture makes the plateau contrast feel enormous because there's so much sound at the loud level.
steps: Pick two plateaus — very soft and very loud.
Strum a full chord voicing at the soft plateau for one bar.
Switch instantly to the loud plateau for the next bar, same chord or new chord.
Alternate plateaus bar-by-bar throughout the round.
End on a soft plateau strum so the contrast is freshest in the ear.
examples: (soft) root-chord × 4 / (loud) root-chord × 4 / (soft) fourth-chord × 4 / (loud) fifth-chord × 4
(soft) second-chord × 4 / (loud) fifth-chord × 4 / (soft) root-chord × 4 / (loud) sixth-chord × 4
dynamics:terraces_texture:fullChord.watchOut: Full chord terraces are physically jarring — you're constantly resetting the strumming hand between pressure levels. Practice the plateau switch as a pure physical motion first, without worrying about the chord quality.

dynamics:terraces_texture:mixed.interaction: Terraces with mixed texture is a tightly composed rhythm-guitar arrangement — soft chord stab, loud single-note fill, soft chord stab, loud fill, alternating texture AND plateau at each change. This is how a Jeff Buckley or Bon Iver rhythm guitar part builds dynamics inside a verse — textural and volume contrast working together.
steps: Decide: chord stabs are soft plateau, single-note fills are loud plateau (or vice versa).
Play a chord stab at the chosen plateau.
Immediately switch to the opposite plateau for the fill.
Back to the first plateau for the next chord stab.
Keep both the texture and the plateau alternation strict for the whole round.
examples: (soft) root-chord → (loud) fifth → fourth → third → (soft) fourth-chord → (loud) third → second → root
(soft) second-chord → (loud) root → seventh → sixth → (soft) fifth-chord → (loud) root → fifth → third
dynamics:terraces_texture:mixed.watchOut: Alternating both texture AND plateau every beat is complex — you might accidentally crescendo across the texture change instead of switching instantly. Practice the transition as 'chord-soft, fill-LOUD' with an audible plateau jump.

dynamics:whisper_texture:singleLine.interaction: Whisper dynamics with a single line is Bill Frisell late-night, or an ECM-jazz guitarist building an entire mood at the edge of audibility. Every note has to be articulated cleanly while staying barely-there, which means your pick attack is fingertip-gentle and your fretting hand has to place every note perfectly or the note won't sound at all. It's the hardest volume to control.
steps: Set the room quiet and your amp (or acoustic mic) to pick up small dynamics.
Play a single scale line using only the lightest fingertip pick motion possible.
Check that every note sounds — whisper volume reveals any fretting-hand sloppiness.
Walk through the scale using steps and skips, staying at whisper throughout.
End on a root or third at the whisper volume, letting the note decay naturally.
examples: root → third → fifth → sixth → fifth → third → root (all pp)
fifth → fourth → third → second → root → second → third → fifth (all pp)
dynamics:whisper_texture:singleLine.watchOut: Whisper volume exposes dead notes — any finger not pressed firmly enough makes no sound at all. If a note is missing from your line, you're not playing it loud enough to overcome the string's weight; press harder with the fretting hand WITHOUT picking harder.

dynamics:whisper_texture:doubleStops.interaction: Whisper double-stops is hymn-like and intimate — two voices at the edge of audibility, the listener leaning in. This is how a solo classical guitarist might begin a slow movement, or how a folk duo builds a reverent moment. The challenge is both voices arriving at the same (whisper) volume — one string louder and you've lost the intimacy.
steps: Set up a dyad shape (third or sixth).
Use fingertip or flat-pick light-touch technique to produce the quietest clean dyad.
Walk the dyad through the scale staying at whisper volume.
Check both voices equally: one dominating means re-adjusting your pick angle.
End on a root/third or root/fifth dyad at whisper volume.
examples: root/third → third/fifth → fifth/seventh → sixth/root-up-octave (all pp)
second/fourth → third/fifth → fourth/sixth → fifth/seventh (all pp)
dynamics:whisper_texture:doubleStops.watchOut: Whisper dyads always have one voice slightly louder — usually the bass string because it has more mass. Compensate by angling your pick very slightly toward the treble string, or playing the dyad fingerstyle (thumb softer than index).

dynamics:whisper_texture:triadPartial.interaction: Whisper with triad partials is ambient-folk accompaniment — three voices barely audible, floating above a hushed singer or underneath a spoken-word passage. Mark Knopfler's quietest fingerstyle work has this quality, as does Nick Drake's accompaniment. The three-voice texture needs enough volume to ring, but not enough to dominate.
steps: Set up a triad partial on the root chord.
Strum very softly — fingertip or light pick only — so all three strings sound but at whisper volume.
Walk through diatonic partials, one per bar, keeping the volume consistent.
Check that no partial is louder than another — whisper must be uniform.
End on a root partial at whisper, letting the three voices decay together.
examples: root-partial (pp) → fourth-partial (pp) → fifth-partial (pp) → root-partial (pp)
second-partial (pp) → fifth-partial (pp) → sixth-partial (pp) → root-partial (pp)
dynamics:whisper_texture:triadPartial.watchOut: Triad partials at whisper volume can have one or two strings not sounding at all — your strum is too light to activate all three. Use fingerstyle (thumb + two fingers) to ensure each string gets its own gentle attack.

dynamics:whisper_texture:fullChord.interaction: Whisper full chord is the opening of a ballad, or a solo-guitar intro before a singer enters — the whole room leans in because the chord is almost inaudible. Full chord texture means six strings have to ring simultaneously at whisper volume, which is a serious test of strumming-hand control and fretting-hand accuracy.
steps: Set up a clean chord voicing.
Strum with the lightest possible downstroke that still causes all six strings to vibrate.
Let the chord ring its full natural decay; don't cut it short.
Move to the next diatonic chord with the same gentle strum.
Cycle through 4-6 chords at strict whisper volume.
examples: root-chord (pp) → fourth-chord (pp) → fifth-chord (pp) → root-chord (pp)
second-chord (pp) → fifth-chord (pp) → sixth-chord (pp) → root-chord (pp)
dynamics:whisper_texture:fullChord.watchOut: Full chord whisper is hardest because any fretting-hand gap means that string produces no sound at all — and a missing string is obvious even at whisper volume. Press every fret firmly; the fretting hand works hard, the strumming hand works gently.

dynamics:whisper_texture:mixed.interaction: Whisper with mixed texture is the most intimate rhythm-guitar mode — chord stab at whisper, single-note fill at whisper, alternating textures but never crossing the whisper volume ceiling. This is how Jeff Buckley or James Blake's acoustic moments work: hushed, detailed, every note audible in the quiet.
steps: Set up a quiet room or quiet-mode amp.
Play a chord stab at whisper volume.
Play a single-note fill at whisper volume — match the chord's level exactly.
Keep both textures equally quiet; the texture change should not change the volume.
Loop for the round, varying pitches but never volume.
examples: root-chord (pp) → fifth (pp) → fourth-chord (pp) → third (pp) → root-chord (pp)
second-chord (pp) → root (pp) → fifth-chord (pp) → seventh (pp)
dynamics:whisper_texture:mixed.watchOut: Single notes at whisper are easier to produce than whisper chords, so the single-note fills will accidentally become slightly louder. Check every attack — match the fill's pick pressure to the chord's strum pressure, not the other way around.

dynamics:accentMap_texture:singleLine.interaction: Accent map with a single line is how a jazz soloist places rhythmic punctuation — most of the phrase is quiet and even, but one note per bar is suddenly much louder. It's the musical equivalent of a bold word in a sentence. The single-voice texture makes the accent unmissable; nothing else can compete with it.
steps: Play a single-note phrase at a consistent soft volume.
On one chosen note per bar (usually beat 1 or a syncopated spot), suddenly pick much harder.
Return immediately to the soft volume for the next notes.
Vary where the accent falls across bars — beat 1, then and-of-2, then beat 3 — so the accent placement becomes expressive.
End the round on an accented note for closure.
examples: root → third → [FIFTH-accent] → fourth → second → root (soft-soft-LOUD-soft-soft-soft)
fifth → [FOURTH-accent] → third → second → root → third (soft-LOUD-soft-soft-soft-soft)
dynamics:accentMap_texture:singleLine.watchOut: Accents become predictable and lose impact if they're always on beat 1. Move them around — the 'and of 2' is especially powerful because it's unexpected — and keep the surrounding volume genuinely soft so the accent pops.

dynamics:accentMap_texture:doubleStops.interaction: Accent map with double-stops is country pedal-steel or Allman-Brothers slide-guitar punctuation — most of the phrase is gentle parallel dyads, and one sudden hard-picked dyad per bar hits like a stinger. The two-voice texture gives the accent extra punch because both strings are firing hard simultaneously.
steps: Play a series of dyads walking stepwise through the scale at a soft volume.
One dyad per bar, pick both strings significantly harder — accent that dyad.
Return to soft for the next dyads.
Vary which beat gets the accent across bars (beat 1, beat 3, and-of-2, etc.).
End with an accented dyad for closure.
examples: root/third → third/fifth → [FOURTH/SIXTH-accent] → fifth/seventh → root/third (soft-soft-LOUD-soft-soft)
second/fourth → [THIRD/FIFTH-accent] → fourth/sixth → fifth/seventh → root/third
dynamics:accentMap_texture:doubleStops.watchOut: Double-stop accents need both voices to jump in volume together — often one string jumps harder than the other because of pick angle. The accent should sound like 'one loud punch,' not 'one loud and one medium-loud.'

dynamics:accentMap_texture:triadPartial.interaction: Accent map plus triad partials is the Motown rhythm guitar stab buried in a soft groove — the rhythm is mostly quiet ticks, and then one moment per bar the partial cracks out loud. This is how a soul rhythm guitarist punctuates a verse or marks the snare hit without abandoning the skank groove.
steps: Play a triad partial rhythm at soft volume (upstrokes on the ands, for example).
Pick one attack per bar to hit much harder — the accent.
Return to soft for the next attacks in the pattern.
Move the accent's position in the bar across iterations (and-of-2, then and-of-4, then beat 1).
Walk through diatonic partials across bars while maintaining the accent pattern.
examples: root-partial (p) × 3 → [root-partial ACCENT] → fourth-partial (p) × 3 → [fourth-partial ACCENT]
second-partial (p) × 2 → [ACCENT] → second-partial (p) → fifth-partial (p) × 3 → [ACCENT]
dynamics:accentMap_texture:triadPartial.watchOut: Triad partial accents tend to bleed into the next soft partial because the strum is harder and the strings ring longer. Mute immediately after the accent so the contrast with the following soft attacks is crisp.

dynamics:accentMap_texture:fullChord.interaction: Accent map with full chords is the backbeat chord stab — most of the bar is soft comping, then one huge loud strum hits on beat 2 or 4 (or wherever the accent maps to). This is how a country or gospel rhythm guitarist emphasizes a snare hit, or how a folk singer-songwriter punctuates a chorus without changing the underlying strum pattern.
steps: Strum a chord voicing quietly for three attacks in a bar.
On one strum (usually beat 2 or 4), hit the chord much harder — full volume.
Return to soft strumming for the remaining attacks.
Keep the chord quality and voicing constant; only volume changes.
Cycle through diatonic chords across bars, one accent per bar each time.
examples: root-chord (p) on 1 → [root-chord ACCENT] on 2 → root-chord (p) on 3 → root-chord (p) on 4
fourth-chord (p) × 2 → [fourth-chord ACCENT] → fourth-chord (p)
dynamics:accentMap_texture:fullChord.watchOut: Full chord accents can be so much louder than the surrounding soft strums that they distort the sound. The accent should be loud relative to the bar, not 'maximum possible'; the ratio is what creates the punch, not the absolute volume.

dynamics:accentMap_texture:mixed.interaction: Accent map with mixed texture — soft chord stabs, soft fills, one accent per bar — is how a classic rhythm-and-blues rhythm guitarist marks a section. The accent could be a hard chord stab or a sudden loud fill note, and either choice reshapes how the bar feels. This is one of the most musical, arrangement-oriented Practice Forge combos.
steps: Play a bar of mixed chord stabs and single-note fills all at soft volume.
Choose one attack per bar to accent — it can be the chord OR a fill note.
Hit that accent much louder than the rest, then return immediately to soft.
Alternate across bars whether the accent is a chord or a single note.
End the round on a loud accented chord for closure.
examples: root-chord (p) → fifth (p) → [FOURTH-CHORD-ACCENT] → third (p) / fourth-chord (p) → third (p) → [SECOND-ACCENT] → root (p)
second-chord (p) → root (p) → fifth-chord (p) → [SEVENTH-ACCENT] / sixth-chord (p) → fourth (p) → [FIFTH-CHORD-ACCENT] → root (p)
dynamics:accentMap_texture:mixed.watchOut: Mixing which texture gets accented is the whole point — if you always accent the chord stabs, you're just doing 'loud chord, soft fill.' Deliberately alternate: one bar the chord is the accent, the next bar a single note is the accent.

dynamics:forte_texture:singleLine.interaction: Constant forte with a single line is electric blues lead playing — every note at full volume, no mercy, no dynamics variation. B.B. King in his prime could carve a whole solo at constant forte and make every note sing. The challenge is keeping pick attack consistent while the scale meanders; fatigue creates volume drop, which breaks the constraint.
steps: Set your pick attack at 'full commitment' — the volume a blues lead break would use.
Play a single-note scale line walking through various pitches.
Match the attack volume on every note; no dropping off as the phrase continues.
Use both steps and leaps; the pitch contour can vary, but the volume stays locked.
End on a held fermata note at full volume, letting it ring until natural decay.
examples: root (f) → third (f) → fifth (f) → sixth (f) → fifth (f) → third (f) → root (f)
fifth (f) → fourth (f) → third (f) → second (f) → root (f) → second (f) → third (f)
dynamics:forte_texture:singleLine.watchOut: Sustained forte playing tires the picking arm in under a minute, and the last four notes of a phrase end up weaker than the first four. Practice in short bursts — four bars at a time — and rest; quality forte beats sustained mush-forte.

dynamics:forte_texture:doubleStops.interaction: Constant forte with double-stops is country-rock rhythm guitar at full tilt — every dyad hit hard, both voices ringing at maximum. Think of Keith Richards' open-tuning rhythm work or the Allman Brothers' dual-guitar harmonies at full volume. The dual-voice attack at forte is physically demanding but produces the biggest sound a guitar can make outside of a full strum.
steps: Choose a dyad interval (thirds or sixths) that sits comfortably under your fingers.
Walk the dyads through the scale playing every dyad at full volume.
Match both string volumes — no bias toward bass or treble.
Use either hard downstrokes or a hard pinch (index + middle fingerstyle) so both voices fire simultaneously.
End on a sustained dyad at full volume.
examples: root/third (f) → second/fourth (f) → third/fifth (f) → fourth/sixth (f) → fifth/seventh (f)
fifth/seventh (f) → fourth/sixth (f) → third/fifth (f) → second/fourth (f) → root/third (f)
dynamics:forte_texture:doubleStops.watchOut: Forte double-stops can have one string buried under the other, making the dyad sound like a single-note solo with a buzz. Check your pick angle and strike-point so both strings receive equal attack.

dynamics:forte_texture:triadPartial.interaction: Constant forte with triad partials is 70s funk rhythm guitar at its loudest — three-voice stabs hammered at full volume through an entire groove. The Meters, Cameo, Earth Wind & Fire rhythm guitarists all worked this territory. The partial texture gives the forte plenty of harmonic content, and the sustained volume creates relentless forward momentum.
steps: Lock in a triad partial and strum it at full volume in rhythm.
Walk through diatonic partials (root, fourth, fifth, sixth) across bars, each strum at forte.
Use a firm downstroke that makes all three strings ring loudly.
Never drop below the forte level — even the off-beat strums stay loud.
End on a sustained root partial at forte.
examples: root-partial (f) × 4 → fourth-partial (f) × 4 → fifth-partial (f) × 4 → root-partial (f) × 4
second-partial (f) × 4 → fifth-partial (f) × 4 → sixth-partial (f) × 4 → root-partial (f) × 4
dynamics:forte_texture:triadPartial.watchOut: Forte triad-partial rhythm turns into a wall of sustained sound if you don't mute between strums — the three voices bleed together into mud. Use palm-mute or fretting-hand release between attacks so every partial is distinct even at full volume.

dynamics:forte_texture:fullChord.interaction: Constant forte full chord is arena-rock rhythm — every strum at full volume, six strings roaring, no breathing room. Pete Townshend, Billie Joe Armstrong, Johnny Ramone all built entire careers on this combination. The physicality is enormous and the sound is unstoppable, but sustaining the volume and cleanness together is a real test.
steps: Grab a full chord voicing.
Strum with full-arm motion at maximum clean volume (not overdriven into distortion).
Cycle through diatonic chords, one per bar, at strict forte.
Keep the strum length and attack consistent across chords — even the hard-shape chords get the same volume.
End on a held root chord at full volume until natural decay.
examples: root-chord (f) × 4 → fourth-chord (f) × 4 → fifth-chord (f) × 4 → root-chord (f) × 4
second-chord (f) × 4 → fifth-chord (f) × 4 → sixth-chord (f) × 4 → root-chord (f) × 4
dynamics:forte_texture:fullChord.watchOut: Forte full-chord strumming exhausts the arm within 30 seconds, and the volume drops unconsciously. Take short breaks between rounds — sustained forte is about physical efficiency (small motion, hard impact) not big motion.

dynamics:forte_texture:mixed.interaction: Constant forte with mixed texture is peak-intensity rhythm playing — chord stabs and single-note fills all at full volume, no soft moments, no dynamic variation. This is how a rock or funk rhythm guitarist plays a choruses and solos: everything loud, all the time, but still varying texture to keep it interesting. The dynamics never drop, but the ear gets variety through chord vs fill.
steps: Play a chord stab at full volume.
Immediately play a fill line at the same full volume — no softening on the texture change.
Back to the next chord stab at full volume.
Keep the texture alternation while the volume stays locked at forte.
Loop for four bars and end on a loud chord stab.
examples: root-chord (f) → fifth (f) → fourth (f) → third (f) → fourth-chord (f) → third (f) → second (f) → root (f)
second-chord (f) → root (f) → seventh (f) → sixth (f) → fifth-chord (f) → root (f) → fifth (f) → third (f)
dynamics:forte_texture:mixed.watchOut: Single notes at forte naturally sound quieter than chord stabs at forte because chords have more strings vibrating. Compensate by hitting single notes with a harder pick attack than you'd use for an equivalent volume in isolation — the fills need to MATCH the chord's perceived loudness, not match the chord's pick pressure.

articulation:legato_texture:singleLine.interaction: Legato on a single line is the guitar playing like a singer — no gaps between syllables, one breath from first note to last. The picking hand has to stop being a drummer and start being a bow, letting each note bleed seamlessly into the next. Think Carlos Santana on a sustained line, or David Gilmour phrasing over slow changes — the goal is a continuous ribbon of pitch where the listener can't hear where you picked and where you slurred.
steps: Set a slow tempo and play a simple four-note scale fragment ascending, picking every note but letting each ring until the next one starts.
Now replace half your pick attacks with hammer-ons and pull-offs — any note you can reach without picking, don't pick.
Listen for any micro-gap between notes; if you hear silence, your fretting hand is lifting too early or too late.
Record one pass and play it back at half speed to check if the line truly sounds like a single breath.
Finish on the root or the fifth and let that final note decay naturally without damping it.
examples: root → second → third → fourth (ascending with hammer-ons, no re-pick)
fifth → fourth → third → second → root (descending pull-offs, legato resolve to tonic)
articulation:legato_texture:singleLine.watchOut: The most common failure is 'fake legato' — picking every note softly and calling it connected. True legato means the strings never stop vibrating between pitches. If you can hear the attack of each note, you're still playing detached.
articulation:legato_texture:singleLine.deeperInsight: On electric guitar, compression and slight overdrive help sustain bridge the gaps — but on a clean acoustic line, only precise fretting-hand timing can create true legato.

articulation:legato_texture:doubleStops.interaction: Legato double stops are the sound of pedal steel or soul-ballad rhythm guitar — two voices moving as one, sliding from interval to interval without ever breaking contact. Because you're fretting two strings at once, the connection has to happen through slides, barre rolls, or finger substitution rather than hammer-ons. Think the creamy thirds in a Curtis Mayfield verse, or the smeared sixths under a Steely Dan chorus.
steps: Pick a pair of adjacent strings and play parallel thirds on the top two strings, one stop per beat.
Move between stops exclusively by sliding — never lift both fingers off the fretboard at once.
When you must jump too far to slide, 'walk' it by substituting fingers while keeping at least one note fretted.
Play the whole sequence with a single long downstroke sweep feel — the pick only rearticulates when a new stop needs a fresh attack.
End on a stop built from the root and the third, and let it ring a full beat.
examples: third+fifth → fourth+sixth → fifth+seventh → root+third (sliding thirds climbing to the octave)
root+third → seventh+second → sixth+root → fifth+seventh (descending sixths with micro-slides)
articulation:legato_texture:doubleStops.watchOut: Both notes must arrive at the new fret simultaneously and leave together — if one finger is late, you'll hear a tiny glissando that breaks the illusion. Also watch your pick: strumming too hard re-articulates both strings and kills legato.
articulation:legato_texture:doubleStops.deeperInsight: Legato double stops teach you that interval 'color' is about simultaneity — a third that arrives late is no longer a third for a beat, it's a grace note.

articulation:legato_texture:triadPartial.interaction: Legato triad partials is the secret weapon of reggae and neo-soul rhythm guitar — three-string voicings on the top strings that glide between chords without ever lifting the hand. The picking hand should feel like it's gently brushing the strings continuously while the fretting hand slides from shape to shape. This is the Dennis Bovell/Ernest Ranglin territory: chords that sound like they're swimming.
steps: Fret a three-string voicing of the tonic triad on the top three strings and let it ring.
Slide the whole shape up a whole step without lifting, then back down — feel the barre glide under your fingers.
Now alternate between the tonic partial and the four-chord partial, sliding between them instead of jumping.
Add a very soft constant strum — fingertip brush, not a pick attack — so the chord is never silent between changes.
Finish by sliding into the tonic partial from a half-step below, landing right on the root in the bass of the voicing.
examples: tonic partial → fourth-chord partial → fifth-chord partial → tonic partial (sliding around the I-IV-V circle)
tonic partial → sixth-chord partial → second-chord partial → fifth-chord partial (sliding through a pop turnaround)
articulation:legato_texture:triadPartial.watchOut: The fretting hand tends to lift on chord changes out of habit — that kills legato. Practice sliding even when the next voicing is in a totally different shape: sometimes you have to 'fake-slide' one finger while silently repositioning the others.
articulation:legato_texture:triadPartial.deeperInsight: On electric guitar with a touch of chorus, legato triad partials become the defining texture of Ernest Ranglin's rocksteady tone — the chord isn't strummed, it's exhaled.

articulation:legato_texture:fullChord.interaction: Legato full chords ask the impossible: make a strummed voicing sound connected to the next one even though the strum itself is an attack. The answer is in the transition — never let the strings go fully silent. This is the sound of classic ballad comping: Bill Withers's guitarist keeping the harmony flowing under his voice, or a slow gospel pianist on guitar translating the sustained vibe.
steps: Pick a four-chord progression and play it slowly, one strum per bar.
During each chord change, keep at least one common finger planted and let the open strings ring while you shift.
Use very light, slow downstrokes — your strum should feel like pouring water, not hitting a drum.
At the moment of the chord change, brush across the strings with your thumb instead of the pick so the transition is gentler than the chord itself.
On the final chord, strum once and let the whole voicing ring out for at least two beats before the next bar.
examples: tonic voicing → six-chord voicing → fourth-chord voicing → fifth-chord voicing (sustained strum-pouring with common-tone pivots)
tonic voicing → third-chord voicing → sixth-chord voicing → fourth-chord voicing (long legato changes anchored on the root string)
articulation:legato_texture:fullChord.watchOut: The classic failure is the 'stop-strum-stop' pattern where you mute the strings during the change out of nerves. That silent fraction of a second destroys legato instantly. Train yourself to let previous chord's ring bridge the change.
articulation:legato_texture:fullChord.deeperInsight: On acoustic guitar, holding a capoed chord and lifting only the moving fingers (leaving open strings to ring) is how fingerstyle ballads like James Taylor's rhythm parts achieve legato full-chord movement.

articulation:legato_texture:mixed.interaction: Legato mixed textures is the art of never letting the listener hear the seam between chord and line. When a single-note fill emerges from a chord and returns to the next chord, the transition should feel like a river widening into a pool and narrowing again. This is Jeff Beck on 'Cause We've Ended as Lovers' — the chord is the fill is the chord, no border.
steps: Start with a three-note chord partial on the top strings, strummed softly.
Pull the top finger off into an open string to begin a single-note descent — no pick attack on the first line note, let the pull-off launch it.
Walk the line down two or three notes and hammer the final note back into a new chord partial.
Keep the picking hand brushing lightly throughout so the texture shift is in the fretting hand, not the pick.
Loop the phrase and check that you cannot tell by ear where 'chord' ends and 'line' begins.
examples: tonic partial → third (pull-off) → second → root → fourth-chord partial (chord bleeds into line, line bleeds back into chord)
fifth-chord partial → seventh (line starts on a hammer from the chord) → root → second → tonic partial (descending line resolves into a fresh voicing)
articulation:legato_texture:mixed.watchOut: The temptation is to pick the first note of the single-line fill as a fresh attack — that creates an audible seam. Force yourself to launch every fill with a hammer or pull-off from a note that's already ringing from the chord.
articulation:legato_texture:mixed.deeperInsight: This is how Motown rhythm guitarists (Robert White, Joe Messina) created the illusion that the rhythm part and the fills were one continuous instrument.

articulation:staccato_texture:singleLine.interaction: Staccato on a single line is funk lead playing, surf guitar melody, or bebop heads — each note a separate dot in space. The challenge is precise muting: the note must ring just long enough to register its pitch, then die cleanly. This is the Meters' Leo Nocentelli territory, or the punchy heads of Charlie Parker transcribed to guitar. Silence between notes is a rhythm instrument in itself.
steps: Pick a simple scale fragment and play it quarter notes with the metronome.
Immediately after each pick attack, use the side of your picking-hand palm or a fretting-hand touch to kill the string.
Aim for each note to last about 50% of its notated value — half sound, half silence.
Record and listen: if the note names blur into each other, you're not muting fast enough.
Finish with a staccato root followed by a full beat of silence, letting the dot sit in the air.
examples: root · third · fifth · root (staccato tonic arpeggio climbing, each dot muted)
root · second · third · root · fifth · root (funk-style single-line hook with crisp gaps)
articulation:staccato_texture:singleLine.watchOut: Most students either don't mute at all (letting notes ring through each other and losing the staccato feel) or clamp so fast that the pitch doesn't register. The note needs enough ring to be heard, then instant death.
articulation:staccato_texture:singleLine.deeperInsight: On fretted instruments, true staccato is a two-hand event — picking-hand palm-mute the fat strings, fretting-hand finger-lift the thin strings.

articulation:staccato_texture:doubleStop.interaction: Staccato double stops are the sound of horn-section stabs translated to guitar — two notes punching together and vanishing. Chuck Berry, Steve Cropper at Stax, Nile Rodgers on a chord stab: each double stop is an exclamation mark. The picking hand has to deliver both strings with perfect simultaneity and then cut them off without choking the pitch.
steps: Fret a pair of strings with a third or sixth on the top two or three strings.
Strike both strings exactly together with a crisp downstroke, then rest your palm back on the bridge to kill them.
Play four staccato stabs per beat, alternating between two different intervals (tonic third, fourth-chord third).
Check that both notes of each stab cut off at the exact same instant — one hanging string ruins the chop.
Land the final stab on a double stop containing the root and give it a full beat of silence.
examples: tonic+third · tonic+third · fourth-chord third · fifth-chord third (Stax-style horn stabs, each dot cleanly chopped)
third+fifth · fourth+sixth · fifth+seventh · third+fifth (ascending thirds as staccato punches)
articulation:staccato_texture:doubleStop.watchOut: Because two strings are vibrating, muting is twice as hard — the lower string especially tends to ring on after the upper string dies. Practice dropping your palm flat across both strings at the same instant.
articulation:staccato_texture:doubleStop.deeperInsight: Nile Rodgers teaches that staccato double stops only sound 'tight' when the gap after the stab is as precisely timed as the stab itself — the silence is a note.

articulation:staccato_texture:triadParti.interaction: Staccato triad partials are the raw engine of funk rhythm guitar — three-string voicings chopped into sixteenth-note slivers. Nile Rodgers's right hand is doing this constantly on 'Good Times'; James Brown's guitarists live here. The partial is small so the chop can be fast, and the tight voicing means every stab lands like a single percussive event rather than a strum.
steps: Fret a triad partial on the top three strings in the tonic shape.
Chop the strings with quick downstrokes, using your fretting-hand pressure to kill the strings between chops — press hard on strike, relax to mute.
Play on the up-beats only (the 'e's and 'a's of the count), leaving hard silences on the down-beats.
Move to a fourth-chord partial and a fifth-chord partial, keeping the exact same chop pattern.
Land the final chop on the tonic partial, cut cleanly, then give two full beats of silence.
examples: tonic partial · tonic partial · fourth-chord partial · fifth-chord partial (Nile-Rodgers-style sixteenth chops)
tonic partial · silence · sixth-chord partial · silence · fourth-chord partial · silence (James Brown ghost-note groove)
articulation:staccato_texture:triadParti.watchOut: The partial has to choke completely between chops — if any string leaks, the staccato becomes a wash. Light constant pressure release in the fretting hand is the secret, not trying to mute with the picking hand alone.
articulation:staccato_texture:triadParti.deeperInsight: This texture is why funk guitarists barely use overdrive — too much sustain and the chop geometry disappears.

articulation:staccato_texture:fullChord.interaction: Staccato full chords are the Latin tumbao, the ska upstroke, the James Brown horn-section-on-guitar sound. The entire voicing has to land and vanish as one object. Because you're striking more strings than in a partial, the chop is more athletic — the fretting hand must release pressure across all four-plus strings simultaneously the instant after the strum.
steps: Fret a full barre chord of the tonic.
Strum a single hard downstroke and immediately release fretting-hand pressure while keeping fingers in contact with the strings — this is the chop mute.
Play four chops per bar, alternating tonic, fourth, tonic, fifth.
Make sure every chord cuts to silence before the next one — if you hear any decay, the mute is late.
On the final bar, play one chop and let two full beats of silence follow — then a last staccato tonic as a period.
examples: tonic chord · fourth-chord · tonic chord · fifth-chord (ska/reggae up-chop pattern, each chord fully muted between)
tonic chord · silence · fourth-chord · silence · fifth-chord · silence (Latin tumbao style with rests as rhythm)
articulation:staccato_texture:fullChord.watchOut: The barre is the villain — if you squeeze it hard, you can't release fast enough to chop. Learn the 'dead-chord' trick: the moment after the strike, your barre finger just unweights without lifting off the strings.
articulation:staccato_texture:fullChord.deeperInsight: Ska guitarists often keep the picking hand still and do almost all the staccato work by releasing fretting-hand pressure — the 'chop' lives in the left hand.

articulation:staccato_texture:mixed.interaction: Staccato mixed textures is the architecture of a funk guitar part — chord stabs punctuate the groove while short single-note fills insert between them, everything clipped and tight. Cory Wong does this; so does Prince on rhythm. Both the chord and the line are chopped, but the chops need to speak in two different voices: the chord is percussive, the line is dotted.
steps: Play four bars: bars 1-3 alternate a tonic chord stab and a fourth-chord stab on beats 2 and 4.
On bar 4, replace the chords with a three-note staccato single-line fill climbing from the root to the fifth.
Mute the fill with the picking-hand palm so each note has the same dot-length as the chord stabs.
Return to the chord stabs on the next loop and make sure the transition has no bleed.
Record and listen — the chord stabs and the line stabs should feel like they're made of the same material.
examples: [ tonic stab · fourth-chord stab ] × 3 → root · third · fifth (staccato line answering the stabs)
[ tonic stab · sixth-chord stab ] × 3 → fifth · third · second · root (descending staccato fill)
articulation:staccato_texture:mixed.watchOut: The danger is that the single-note line turns out softer and longer than the chord stabs, breaking the uniform 'dot size.' Use the same wrist motion and same palm-mute angle for both.
articulation:staccato_texture:mixed.deeperInsight: In Prince's rhythm parts, the staccato line is often played exactly one fret away from the chord shape — minimal movement, maximal punch.

articulation:slurredPairs_texture:single.interaction: Slurred pairs on a single line teach you the fundamental gravity of melody — every two notes belong together, and the second of each pair wants to release downward. This is how classical guitar etudes teach legato, and how bebop phrasing organizes eighth-note lines into natural breath groupings. The first note of each pair is an arrival, the second a relaxation.
steps: Play an ascending scale fragment in eighth notes, slurring each beat's pair with a hammer-on, then letting a tiny gap appear before the next beat.
Feel the shape: attack-release, attack-release — like a voice saying two syllables per breath.
Vary the line with pulls-offs going down, hammers going up — never pick the second note of a pair.
Add a light dynamic dip on the second note of each pair to reinforce the group.
End on a slurred pair of seventh-to-root to make the final gesture feel like an exhalation home.
examples: (root→second) · (third→fourth) · (fifth→sixth) · (seventh→root) (ascending slurred pairs up the scale)
(fifth→fourth) · (third→second) · (root→seventh) · (sixth→fifth) (descending pull-off pairs)
articulation:slurredPairs_texture:single.watchOut: The gap between pairs is the hardest part — students tend to make it too big (turning slurred pairs into staccato couplets) or too small (making it sound fully legato). The gap should be the width of a breath, not a rest.
articulation:slurredPairs_texture:single.deeperInsight: Charlie Parker's eighth-note lines almost always imply slurred pairs even when tongued on saxophone — the first note is the target, the second is the passing release.

articulation:slurredPairs_texture:double.interaction: Slurred pairs on double stops is piano-like — two voices moving together in two beats of breath. Think gospel thirds moving from chord to chord in pairs, or Chet Atkins phrasing sixths in pair-wise slides. Because both notes move, the slur has to happen on both strings at once — usually via a coordinated slide rather than a hammer-on.
steps: Fret a pair of parallel thirds on two adjacent strings and strike them once.
Slide both fingers together up one scale step to the next pair of thirds — that's one slurred pair, no re-pick on the second stop.
Re-pick and slide into the next pair, then release. Now alternate: picked stop, slid stop, tiny gap, picked stop, slid stop.
Focus on the simultaneity of the slide — both fingers must leave and arrive together.
End on a slurred pair that resolves into a tonic-and-third double stop.
examples: (root+third →slide→ second+fourth) · (third+fifth →slide→ fourth+sixth) (ascending thirds, sliding the pair's second note)
(fifth+seventh →slide→ fourth+sixth) · (third+fifth →slide→ root+third) (descending sixths slurring down)
