texture:singleLine_harmonicTarget:colorT.watchOut: Landing on a color tone while the chord underneath is a plain triad creates a specific tension — don't flinch from it. Students tend to 'correct' to a triad tone at the last second because the color tone feels 'wrong,' but it's the whole point.
texture:singleLine_harmonicTarget:colorT.deeperInsight: In bebop harmony, the seventh is considered a 'guide tone' and lines often target it — it's why 'dominant seventh' is the default chord color in jazz.

texture:doubleStops_harmonicTarget:scale.interaction: Double stops through scale shape is the freedom of two-voice playing over a single harmony — parallel thirds or sixths moving up and down the scale without chord-tone targeting. This is the sound of early rock and roll rhythm leads, or a country rhythm player filling behind a singer. The two voices give you interval color, but the free movement means you can focus on shape and motion rather than harmonic targeting.
steps: Pick an interval (parallel thirds on two strings) and play them ascending through the scale.
Reverse and play them descending.
Vary the rhythm with a mix of quarter and eighth notes.
Switch to sixths (wider interval) and repeat.
End on any scale pair — the tonic-plus-third is fine but not required.
examples: root+third → second+fourth → third+fifth → fourth+sixth → fifth+seventh (parallel thirds up the scale)
fifth+seventh → fourth+sixth → third+fifth → second+fourth → root+third (thirds down the scale)
texture:doubleStops_harmonicTarget:scale.watchOut: The pair of strings you use matters — stick to two adjacent strings so the hand shape stays consistent. Moving across string pairs introduces a new challenge and breaks the 'free' feel.
texture:doubleStops_harmonicTarget:scale.deeperInsight: Chuck Berry's entire rhythm-lead vocabulary is built on scale-shape double stops — parallel thirds and sixths moving freely through the key, no chord-tone targeting.

texture:doubleStops_harmonicTarget:arpeg.interaction: Double stops built from tonic arpeggio tones (root, third, fifth) give you the chord in two-voice form — harmonized melody where every stop is a piece of the triad. Root-plus-third, third-plus-fifth, fifth-plus-root-up — each is a different double-stop flavor of the same chord. This is how rhythm-lead guitarists comp over a static tonic: play the chord as a series of two-voice arpeggiations.
steps: Identify the three tones of the tonic arpeggio: root, third, fifth.
Build three double stops from them: root+third, third+fifth, fifth+root-up.
Play them in order, holding each for a beat.
Re-order them and vary the rhythm: maybe root+third → fifth+root-up → third+fifth.
End on root+third as the final resolved stop.
examples: root+third → third+fifth → fifth+root-up → third+fifth → root+third (arpeggio double-stop cycle)
root+fifth → third+root-up → fifth+third → root+third (interval variations of the same triad)
texture:doubleStops_harmonicTarget:arpeg.watchOut: With only three pitches available, the double stops can feel repetitive. Vary the register (play some stops an octave higher) and the rhythm to keep it alive.
texture:doubleStops_harmonicTarget:arpeg.deeperInsight: Three chord tones can be combined into three distinct two-note stops — root/third, third/fifth, root/fifth — and rotating between them is a complete double-stop vocabulary for a triad.

texture:doubleStops_harmonicTarget:chord.interaction: Double stops with chord-tone landing means each phrase ends on a stop built from chord tones of the current chord. Because double stops have TWO notes, you have to pick two chord tones for each landing — like two singers both resolving to the chord. This is how rhythm-lead country and blues players align their parallel thirds with the harmony: the line wanders, but the resolution is always on the chord.
steps: Over bar 1 (tonic chord), play scale-shape double stops but land on a chord-tone pair like root+third or fifth+root-up at beat 4.
Over bar 2 (fourth-chord), land on a chord-tone pair of the fourth-chord: fourth+sixth or sixth+root-up.
Continue for bars 3 and 4, each bar's landing built from chord tones of its chord.
Keep the middle of each bar free — use any scale pair you want, just land correctly.
End on a tonic-plus-third landing over the final tonic chord.
examples: Over tonic: root+third → second+fourth → fifth+root-up (land on fifth+root-up = chord tones of tonic). Over fourth-chord: sixth+root-up → seventh+second → fourth+sixth (land on fourth+sixth = chord tones of four)
Over tonic: fifth+seventh → fourth+sixth → root+third (chord-tone landing). Over fifth-chord: second+fourth → root+third → fifth+seventh-of-five (chord tones of the five-chord)
texture:doubleStops_harmonicTarget:chord.watchOut: Picking two chord tones simultaneously is harder than picking one — sometimes the two chord tones aren't on adjacent strings. Be flexible with which chord tones you target on each landing.
texture:doubleStops_harmonicTarget:chord.deeperInsight: Country rhythm-lead guitarists practice chord-tone double-stop landings as scales — they memorize which double-stop shape goes with each chord in the key, like a vocabulary.

texture:doubleStops_harmonicTarget:color.interaction: Double stops built on color tones (seventh, ninth, eleventh) give you the most sophisticated harmonic sounds in two-voice form — a seventh-plus-ninth stop is a fragment of a chord extension, and landing on it adds serious jazz color. This is how Wes Montgomery and Kenny Burrell comped behind their own solos: fragments of thirteen-chord and nine-chord voicings harmonized as double stops.
steps: Identify the seventh and ninth of your current chord.
Build a double stop from them: seventh+ninth.
Play a phrase ending on this color-tone stop — let it ring and feel the tension.
Next phrase, build a stop from the ninth and eleventh and land on that.
Alternate between triad-tone landings and color-tone landings to hear the difference.
examples: root+third → third+fifth → seventh+ninth (land on the color tone pair)
fifth+seventh → ninth+eleventh → seventh+ninth (color-tone exploration)
texture:doubleStops_harmonicTarget:color.watchOut: Color-tone stops are harmonically 'spicy' and can sound wrong to unaccustomed ears. Trust the constraint — the tension is the point, not a mistake.
texture:doubleStops_harmonicTarget:color.deeperInsight: Wes Montgomery often built entire choruses out of color-tone double stops — the seventh-plus-ninth stop became his signature sound, heard in every solo.

texture:triadPartial_harmonicTarget:scal.interaction: Triad partials moving through scale shape means three-string chords gliding up and down the scale in parallel — reggae-funk comping where each scale step brings a new partial voicing. Because you're not targeting specific chord tones, the voicings can be any partial shape, and the ear hears a moving wall of harmony that doesn't necessarily stay on the tonic chord. This is Ernest Ranglin territory.
steps: Fret a tonic triad partial on the top three strings.
Move the whole shape up one scale step — every note now shifted up a tone.
Continue up through the scale one step at a time — each scale step gives you a new parallel partial.
Then come back down.
End on the tonic partial as the final resolution.
examples: tonic partial → partial on second → partial on third → partial on fourth → partial on fifth (parallel scale partials ascending)
fifth partial → fourth partial → third partial → second partial → tonic partial (descending parallel partials)
texture:triadPartial_harmonicTarget:scal.watchOut: The parallel partials may clash with a chord progression underneath — because you're NOT targeting chord tones, some partials will be 'outside' the current chord. Use this constraint over a tonic vamp, not a changing progression.
texture:triadPartial_harmonicTarget:scal.deeperInsight: Jazz comping uses scale-shape triad partials over a modal vamp — the partials become a smear of parallel harmony that doesn't need to follow chord changes.

texture:triadPartial_harmonicTarget:arpe.interaction: Triad partials outlining the tonic arpeggio means playing ONLY voicings built from root, third, and fifth — so every partial is a version of the tonic chord in a different register or inversion. This creates a 'meditative' harmonic feel: the chord never changes, but the voicing keeps rotating through root-position, first-inversion, second-inversion, and octave-up shapes.
steps: Fret the tonic triad partial in root position (root on bottom).
Move to first-inversion tonic partial (third on bottom).
Move to second-inversion tonic partial (fifth on bottom).
Move to root-position tonic an octave higher.
Come back down and end on the original root-position partial.
examples: tonic partial (root position) → tonic partial (first inversion) → tonic partial (second inversion) → tonic partial (octave up) (arpeggio inversions)
tonic root-position → second-inversion → first-inversion → root-position (varying voicing order)
texture:triadPartial_harmonicTarget:arpe.watchOut: With only one chord's worth of notes, the harmony can feel static — but that's the constraint. Use rhythmic variation and strumming differences to keep the single-chord texture alive.
texture:triadPartial_harmonicTarget:arpe.deeperInsight: Classical guitar accompaniment often cycles through triad inversions of a single chord — it's how a single chord gets a whole bar of voicing variety without harmonic motion.

texture:triadPartial_harmonicTarget:chor.interaction: Triad partials landing on chord tones means each new partial arrives when the chord changes, and it must be built from chord tones of the new chord. This is essentially 'accurate comping' — the reggae or jazz rhythm guitarist who always plays the right voicing for the right chord. Unlike scale-shape, here the voicing literally IS the chord in three-string form, and the landing is where the change happens.
steps: Over bar 1 (tonic), play a tonic triad partial on beats 2 and 4.
On bar 2 (fourth-chord), switch to a fourth-chord triad partial on beats 2 and 4 — the chord tones are now the four-chord's root, third, fifth.
On bar 3 (fifth-chord), switch to a fifth-chord triad partial.
On bar 4, return to the tonic partial.
Loop the 4-bar cycle and check that each bar's partial clearly represents its chord.
examples: Bar 1: tonic partial. Bar 2: fourth-chord partial. Bar 3: fifth-chord partial. Bar 4: tonic partial (standard reggae comping with chord-tone landings)
Bar 1: tonic. Bar 2: sixth-chord partial. Bar 3: fourth-chord partial. Bar 4: fifth-chord partial (pop turnaround with proper chord-tone landings)
texture:triadPartial_harmonicTarget:chor.watchOut: The transition between partials should happen exactly on the chord change — not early, not late. If the old partial bleeds into the new bar, the chord tone landing is lost.
texture:triadPartial_harmonicTarget:chor.deeperInsight: Reggae rhythm guitar is essentially chord-tone-landing triad partials — every offbeat chop is a new chord tone arrival aligned with the underlying harmony.

texture:triadPartial_harmonicTarget:colo.interaction: Triad partials with color tones instead of triad tones — this is where three-string voicings become jazz chord extensions. A 'triad partial' here is really a color-voicing on three strings: root-plus-seventh-plus-ninth, or third-plus-ninth-plus-eleventh. This is the sound of Joe Pass or Jim Hall comping — tight three-string voicings that happen to contain a seventh and a ninth, giving the chord its jazz color.
steps: Identify the seventh and ninth of the tonic chord.
Build a three-string partial that includes root, seventh, and ninth (instead of root, third, fifth).
Strum it softly and let the color tones ring.
Move to a similar extension partial of the fourth-chord and play it.
End on a color-tone partial of the tonic — let the ninth ring out.
examples: root + seventh + ninth → fourth + tenth + twelfth → fifth + eleventh + thirteenth (extension partials)
third + ninth + thirteenth (tonic extension partial) → fourth + sixth + eleventh (four-chord extension) → fifth + seventh + ninth (five-chord extension)
texture:triadPartial_harmonicTarget:colo.watchOut: These voicings are spicy and can sound 'wrong' if played over a plain triad backing. Use them over modal vamps or a jazz progression that supports extensions.
texture:triadPartial_harmonicTarget:colo.deeperInsight: Jazz comping is almost exclusively color-tone partials — the 'plain triad' sound is too harmonically neutral for jazz harmony.

texture:fullChord_harmonicTarget:scaleSh.interaction: Full chord voicings moving through scale shape is the weirdest constraint in this set — full six-string voicings gliding up and down the scale in parallel. This is rare in most genres but common in modal jazz: McCoy Tyner's block-chord ascents, or a gospel piano line translated to guitar where entire voicings shift by a whole step. The ear hears a wall of harmony that isn't tied to any one chord.
steps: Fret a tonic full voicing.
Move the whole voicing up one whole step (or one scale step).
Continue up the scale one step at a time with the full voicing — each bar is a new chord.
Descend back to the tonic voicing.
End by letting the tonic voicing ring out.
examples: tonic voicing → voicing on the second → voicing on the third → voicing on the fourth → voicing on the fifth (parallel scale voicings ascending)
tonic voicing → voicing up a fifth → voicing up a third (non-adjacent scale voicings)
texture:fullChord_harmonicTarget:scaleSh.watchOut: Parallel full voicings can sound 'wrong' over a tonic vamp because they're NOT chord tones — they're a harmonic wash. This constraint works best without a backing chord, just over a drone or a modal vamp.
texture:fullChord_harmonicTarget:scaleSh.deeperInsight: McCoy Tyner and Herbie Hancock used parallel block chords extensively — the technique creates a modal, floating harmonic feel that's impossible with chord-tone-targeted playing.

texture:fullChord_harmonicTarget:arpeggi.interaction: Full chord voicings outlining the tonic arpeggio means rotating through different full voicings of the SAME chord — root position, first inversion, second inversion, octave versions. The chord never changes, but the voicing constantly shifts. This creates the meditation-on-a-chord feel of Satie, or a modern jazz guitarist's 'rubato intro' where a single chord is explored from every angle.
steps: Fret the tonic voicing in root position.
Shift to a first-inversion tonic voicing (third in the bass).
Shift to a second-inversion tonic voicing (fifth in the bass).
Shift to an octave-higher tonic voicing.
End on the root-position tonic voicing.
examples: root-position tonic voicing → first-inversion → second-inversion → octave-up root-position (inversion cycle)
tonic voicing at low register → tonic voicing at middle register → tonic voicing at high register (register exploration)
texture:fullChord_harmonicTarget:arpeggi.watchOut: With one chord held constant, the ear can zone out. Use rhythm, dynamics, and strum variation to keep the single-chord texture alive and engaging.
texture:fullChord_harmonicTarget:arpeggi.deeperInsight: Bill Frisell and Pat Metheny sometimes spend entire minutes on a single chord, rotating through voicings — the technique creates a hypnotic, meditative 'chord study' feel.

texture:fullChord_harmonicTarget:chordTo.interaction: Full chord voicings with chord-tone landing is just standard rhythm guitar playing at its core: each new chord is a new voicing, and the voicing is built from chord tones of the new chord. The constraint sounds trivial but it's really about CLEAN chord changes — the new voicing arrives exactly when the chord changes, and every note of the new voicing belongs to the new chord.
steps: Over bar 1, strum a tonic full voicing throughout.
On bar 2, change to a four-chord full voicing — every note of the new voicing is from the four-chord's notes.
On bar 3, change to a five-chord full voicing.
On bar 4, return to the tonic.
Loop and verify every chord change is clean — no notes from the previous chord bleeding into the next.
examples: Bar 1: tonic voicing. Bar 2: fourth-chord voicing. Bar 3: fifth-chord voicing. Bar 4: tonic voicing (standard I-IV-V-I comping)
Bar 1: tonic voicing. Bar 2: six-chord voicing. Bar 3: fourth-chord voicing. Bar 4: fifth-chord voicing (pop vi-IV-V turnaround)
texture:fullChord_harmonicTarget:chordTo.watchOut: The chord change should happen on beat 1 of each bar — if any strings from the previous chord ring into the new bar, the landing is fuzzy. Mute cleanly at the end of each bar.
texture:fullChord_harmonicTarget:chordTo.deeperInsight: Clean chord-tone-landing is what separates beginner rhythm guitar from intermediate — when every chord change is precise, the harmony sounds 'right' even in simple music.

texture:fullChord_harmonicTarget:colorTo.interaction: Full chord voicings with color tones means playing jazz chords: seventh chords, ninth chords, thirteenth chords — voicings that include chord extensions, not just triad tones. This is Joe Pass, Jim Hall, Wes Montgomery comping territory. The full voicing has five or six notes, and the color tones are baked into the shape.
steps: Instead of a plain tonic triad, fret a tonic major-seventh or tonic-ninth voicing.
Strum it softly and let all notes including the seventh and ninth ring.
Move to a fourth-chord seventh or ninth voicing.
Move to a fifth-chord thirteenth or ninth voicing.
End on a tonic voicing with the ninth in the top voice.
examples: tonic major seventh voicing → fourth-chord major seventh → fifth-chord ninth → tonic major seventh (jazz I-IV-V with extensions)
tonic ninth → six-chord minor ninth → fourth-chord major ninth → fifth-chord thirteenth (rich pop-jazz voicings)
texture:fullChord_harmonicTarget:colorTo.watchOut: Color tones in a full voicing can sound dissonant if the top note is the seventh or ninth without resolution. Listen to how the extension sits and decide whether to resolve it or let it hang.
texture:fullChord_harmonicTarget:colorTo.deeperInsight: In jazz standards, the 'right' chord is almost always a color-tone voicing — a plain major triad in a jazz context sounds naive, while a major seventh sounds authentic.

texture:mixed_harmonicTarget:scaleShape.interaction: Mixed textures through scale shape means both the chord stabs AND the line fills use any scale notes, not targeted chord tones. The chord stabs might be random parallel partials, the line fills might be scale runs — it's a free-form comping texture where the constraint is only 'stay in the scale.' This creates the modal-comping sound: chord and line merge into a single scale-colored texture.
steps: Play a tonic triad partial and a short scale-shape line answer.
Play a parallel partial a scale step up and another scale-shape line.
Continue with partials on any scale degree and line fills using any scale notes.
Make sure the lines and chords all stay within the scale.
End on a tonic partial strike with a scale-shape resolve.
examples: tonic partial + [second → third → fourth line] → partial on second + [fifth → sixth → seventh line] → partial on third + [root-up → seventh → sixth line]
tonic partial + [root → third → fifth] → partial on fourth + [sixth → seventh → root-up] → tonic partial + [fifth → third → root]
texture:mixed_harmonicTarget:scaleShape.watchOut: Without chord-tone targeting, it's easy to sound aimless. Even in scale-shape mode, each phrase should have a clear rhythm and a clear destination — just not a specific harmonic one.
texture:mixed_harmonicTarget:scaleShape.deeperInsight: Modal jazz comping (Miles Davis's 'Kind of Blue' era) is essentially scale-shape mixed textures over a single mode — the scale is the harmony, no chord-tone targeting needed.

texture:mixed_harmonicTarget:arpeggio135.interaction: Mixed textures using only tonic arpeggio tones means the chord partials are built from root/third/fifth, and the line fills use only root/third/fifth. Every element of the comping texture is a piece of the tonic triad. This creates a harmonic 'lockdown' on a single chord, but with rhythmic and textural variety. It's the perfect constraint for learning triadic thinking in a rhythm-guitar context.
steps: Play a tonic triad partial on beat 1.
Answer with a line fill using only root, third, fifth: say, third → fifth → root.
On the next bar, another tonic partial (maybe in a different inversion) and another line fill from the same three notes.
Continue for several bars, all over a static tonic chord.
End on a tonic partial strike.
examples: tonic partial + [fifth → third → root] → tonic first-inversion partial + [root → third → fifth] → tonic second-inversion partial + [third → fifth → root]
tonic partial + [root → fifth → third] → tonic partial (different voicing) + [fifth → third → root]
texture:mixed_harmonicTarget:arpeggio135.watchOut: Only three pitches means the line fills can sound repetitive. Vary the octave and rhythm to keep the triad-only texture alive.
texture:mixed_harmonicTarget:arpeggio135.deeperInsight: This is the 'three notes' constraint that Miles Davis sometimes used in rehearsal — limiting improvisation to the triad tones forces rhythmic and textural creativity instead of harmonic noodling.

texture:mixed_harmonicTarget:chordToneLa.interaction: Mixed textures with chord-tone landing means every phrase's line fill ends on a chord tone of the current chord, AND the chord stabs are voicings of the current chord. This is essentially accurate soul-jazz comping: Hendrix's 'Little Wing' written down as a set of constraints. Every element aligns with the chord beneath, but the texture shifts constantly between chord and line.
steps: Over bar 1 (tonic), play a tonic partial stab and a line fill that lands on a tonic chord tone.
Over bar 2 (fourth-chord), play a fourth-chord partial stab and a line fill that lands on a four-chord chord tone.
Over bar 3 (fifth-chord), play a fifth-chord partial stab and a line fill landing on a five-chord tone.
Over bar 4, return to tonic.
Loop and verify every line fill lands on a chord tone of its bar's chord.
examples: Bar 1: tonic partial + [second → third → root] (root is chord tone). Bar 2: fourth-chord partial + [sixth → seventh → fourth] (fourth is chord tone). Bar 3: fifth-chord partial + [second → root → fifth] (fifth is chord tone)
Bar 1: tonic partial + [fifth → root → third]. Bar 2: six-chord partial + [root → fifth → sixth-of-six (chord tone of the six)]. Bar 3: fourth-chord partial + [sixth → fourth].
texture:mixed_harmonicTarget:chordToneLa.watchOut: The chord tones of bar 2's chord are DIFFERENT from bar 1's. Students forget this and land on tonic chord tones even in the fourth-chord bar, which sounds wrong. Always target the current chord, not the tonic.
texture:mixed_harmonicTarget:chordToneLa.deeperInsight: Jimi Hendrix's 'Little Wing' rhythm part is a textbook example of mixed-texture with chord-tone landings — every fill answers the chord beneath it.

texture:mixed_harmonicTarget:colorTone79.interaction: Mixed textures with color tones means BOTH the chord partials AND the line fills emphasize seventh, ninth, and eleventh tones. The voicings are jazz partials (extension chords in three-string form), and the line fills target the color tones as landings. This is Wes Montgomery's comping-and-soloing-at-once technique: every element is sophisticated jazz harmony in miniature.
steps: Build a tonic extension partial (root plus seventh plus ninth) and strum it.
Play a short line fill ending on the ninth or eleventh of the chord.
Move to an extension partial of the fourth-chord and answer with a line ending on its seventh.
Continue with color-tone partials and color-tone line landings.
End on a tonic ninth-chord partial with a ninth in the line fill.
examples: tonic seventh-ninth partial + [root → third → ninth] (line lands on ninth = color tone). Four-chord seventh partial + [fourth → seventh-of-four] (line lands on seventh of four)
tonic ninth partial + [seventh → ninth → root-up] (line ends on ninth). Fifth-chord ninth partial + [fourth → sixth → ninth-of-five]
texture:mixed_harmonicTarget:colorTone79.watchOut: Color-tone targeting in both the chord and the line creates a very 'outside' sound — if it sounds too harsh, some elements can be standard triad tones while others are color tones. The full constraint is challenging.
texture:mixed_harmonicTarget:colorTone79.deeperInsight: Pat Metheny's rhythm parts often blur the line between comping and soloing — both parts emphasize color tones, so the chord AND the line sound like jazz vocabulary.

texture:singleLine_phraseStructure:quest.interaction: A single-line question-and-answer phrase pair is the most natural melodic shape in human music — the first phrase rises or stays suspended (the question), the second phrase descends or resolves (the answer). Think of nursery rhymes, folk ballads, bluegrass fiddle tunes. On single-line guitar, the phrase is pure melody, and the question-answer structure gives it the arc of a small spoken sentence.
steps: Play a short phrase that ends on a tense note — the seventh, second, or fourth — as the question.
Immediately answer with a second phrase that ends on the root or fifth — the resolved note.
Keep the contour of the question rising or suspended, the contour of the answer descending.
Match the rhythmic length of the two phrases (both two bars, or both one bar).
Loop the question-and-answer pair and check that it sounds like a small conversation.
examples: Question: root → second → third → fourth (ends on tense fourth). Answer: third → second → root → seventh → root (resolves home)
Question: fifth → sixth → seventh → root-up (ends on octave with lift). Answer: seventh → sixth → fifth → fourth → third → root (descends to tonic)
texture:singleLine_phraseStructure:quest.watchOut: The answer tends to get too decorative — students 'answer' with a longer or more complex phrase, which defeats the symmetry. Keep the answer simple: it should be a direct response to the question, not a new statement.
texture:singleLine_phraseStructure:quest.deeperInsight: Every child's melody — 'Twinkle Twinkle,' 'Mary Had a Little Lamb' — is built on question-and-answer phrases, which is why this is the most universally recognized musical shape.

texture:singleLine_phraseStructure:call-.interaction: Single-line call-and-response is the backbone of gospel, blues, and West African music — one phrase makes a statement (the call), another echoes or argues with it (the response). The response is often a transformation of the call: same rhythm but different notes, or same notes but different rhythm. This trains you to hear phrases as a dialogue, not just consecutive sentences.
steps: Play a simple one-bar phrase — the call. Make it memorable: three or four notes, a clear rhythm.
Play the same rhythm with different notes — the response echoes the call's shape but varies the pitch.
Next pair: different rhythm, same notes — the response transforms the call's pitch into a new rhythm.
Alternate: sometimes the response agrees (similar contour), sometimes it challenges (opposite contour).
End with a call and a resolving response that lands on the root.
examples: Call: root → third → second (rising to the second). Response: third → second → root (descending to the root — argues)
Call: fifth → fifth → fifth → root (rhythmic statement on the fifth). Response: fifth → fourth → third → root (descends — echoes)
texture:singleLine_phraseStructure:call-.watchOut: The response must clearly reference the call — it's not just 'a different phrase.' If the response doesn't relate to the call rhythmically or melodically, it's just two unrelated phrases.
texture:singleLine_phraseStructure:call-.deeperInsight: In West African drumming, call and response is literal: the lead drummer plays a pattern and the ensemble plays back a transformed echo. Translated to guitar, it's the most effective way to structure improvisation.

texture:singleLine_phraseStructure:theme.interaction: Single-line theme and variation means: play a simple theme, then vary it each time you repeat it — keep the contour or rhythm, evolve the details. This is classical variation form in miniature. You learn to hear a theme as a skeleton that can be dressed differently every time. Think of 'Twinkle Twinkle' transformed by Mozart into twelve variations, or a jazz musician reharmonizing a hymn.
steps: Play a simple four-note theme — for instance, root → third → fifth → root.
Play the theme a second time, adding one passing note: root → second → third → fifth → root.
Play the theme a third time with different rhythm: the same notes in syncopated eighth notes.
Play the theme a fourth time with an ornamental turn: root → second → third → second → third → fifth → root.
End with the simplest version — the unembellished theme — as the resolution.
examples: Theme: root → third → fifth → root. Variation 1: root → third → second → third → fifth → root. Variation 2: root → fifth → third → root
Theme: fifth → third → second → root. Var 1: fifth → fourth → third → second → root. Var 2: fifth → third → fourth → third → second → root
texture:singleLine_phraseStructure:theme.watchOut: The theme has to be recognizable in each variation — students over-decorate and the original is lost. If the listener can't hear the skeleton anymore, the variation has drifted too far.
texture:singleLine_phraseStructure:theme.deeperInsight: Bach's 'Goldberg Variations' starts with a simple aria and varies it 30 ways — each variation preserves the theme's harmonic skeleton while dressing it differently.

texture:singleLine_phraseStructure:ostin.interaction: A single-line ostinato is a hypnotic repeating phrase that doesn't change — think of a Philip Glass minimalist line, or the opening of 'Satisfaction' by the Rolling Stones. The constraint forces you to commit to a single phrase and repeat it until it transcends its own notes. The ear starts to hear it as a texture rather than a melody. Ostinato is meditation on loop.
steps: Pick a short four- or five-note phrase that feels good.
Play it once. Then play it again exactly the same way. Then again.
Repeat for at least 16 bars with zero variation in the notes or rhythm.
Focus on your tone, feel, and breath — the phrase stays constant, but YOU should deepen with each rep.
End by letting the phrase decay naturally — don't add a flourish to 'finish.'
examples: root → fifth → third → second → root (hypnotic five-note loop)
third → second → root → seventh → root (descending-resolving ostinato)
texture:singleLine_phraseStructure:ostin.watchOut: After four or five repeats, students get bored and start varying the phrase. Resist — the whole point is the transcendence that comes from non-variation. Variation kills the trance.
texture:singleLine_phraseStructure:ostin.deeperInsight: Steve Reich and Philip Glass built careers on the observation that a repeated phrase transforms perceptually without changing — your ear fills in variety the 30th time even though the notes are identical.

texture:doubleStops_phraseStructure:ques.interaction: Double-stop question-and-answer is two-voice dialogue — the question phrase ends on a tense interval (a second or seventh), the answer resolves to a stable interval (the tonic third or fifth). Because double stops have two voices, the tension and resolution are harmonized, giving the dialogue a richer color. This is how rhythm-lead country or early rock phrases conversation.
steps: Play a double-stop phrase rising from a stable third to a tense second+fourth — this is the question.
Answer with a descending double-stop phrase resolving to the tonic-plus-third.
Match the rhythmic length of the two phrases.
Make sure the answer's final interval is clearly more stable than the question's.
Loop the Q-and-A pair and listen for the small harmonic conversation.
examples: Question: root+third → second+fourth → third+fifth (ends on the tense 'scale top'). Answer: third+fifth → second+fourth → root+third (descends to the tonic pair)
Question: fifth+seventh → sixth+root-up → seventh+second (tense upper extension). Answer: sixth+root-up → fifth+seventh → root+third (resolves to tonic third)
texture:doubleStops_phraseStructure:ques.watchOut: Double-stop phrases are 'denser' than single-line phrases — resist the urge to make them too long. A two-measure Q and a two-measure A is more than enough.
texture:doubleStops_phraseStructure:ques.deeperInsight: Chuck Berry's signature rhythm-lead fills are essentially double-stop question-answer pairs — rising thirds followed by descending sixths to resolve.

texture:doubleStops_phraseStructure:call.interaction: Double-stop call-and-response is gospel two-voice territory — a call rises with thirds, the response answers with sixths (or vice versa). The harmonized dialogue evokes a choir of two voices trading phrases. This is how Curtis Mayfield's rhythm parts, or Steve Cropper's Stax fills, built their 'conversational' feel. The two voices of the double stops add harmonic color to the call-and-response shape.
steps: Play a call phrase using parallel thirds rising up the scale.
Respond with a parallel sixths phrase descending back down the scale.
Keep the rhythm of the call and response similar so the pair feels coherent.
On the next pair, reverse: sixths call, thirds response.
End with a call-response pair resolving to a tonic-plus-third stop.
examples: Call (thirds): root+third → second+fourth → third+fifth. Response (sixths): third+root-up → second+seventh → root+sixth
Call (sixths): fifth+third-up → sixth+fourth-up → seventh+fifth-up. Response (thirds): fifth+seventh → fourth+sixth → root+third
texture:doubleStops_phraseStructure:call.watchOut: The two voices of the call and the two voices of the response need to connect clearly — if they're in different registers, the conversation is hard to hear. Keep both phrases in the same general area of the neck.
texture:doubleStops_phraseStructure:call.deeperInsight: Curtis Mayfield built Impressions-era Chicago soul on double-stop call-response — the technique is how a single rhythm guitarist can sound like a chord-and-fill section at once.

texture:doubleStops_phraseStructure:them.interaction: Double-stop theme and variation means state a simple two-voice theme (say, a rising third pair), then vary it on each repeat — add a passing stop, change the rhythm, invert the interval. Because the theme is harmonized, variations have twice the dimensions to play with: rhythm AND interval color can change independently. This is a rhythm-lead player's classical-variation form in miniature.
steps: State a simple theme: root+third → third+fifth → fifth+root-up (three rising double stops).
Variation 1: add a passing stop between each — root+third → second+fourth → third+fifth → fourth+sixth → fifth+root-up.
Variation 2: change thirds to sixths — root+sixth → third+root-up → fifth+third-up.
Variation 3: invert the rhythm — same stops, but in descending order.
End with the original theme as the final resolution.
examples: Theme: root+third → third+fifth → fifth+root-up. Var 1: root+third → second+fourth → third+fifth → fourth+sixth → fifth+root-up
Theme: root+fifth → third+seventh → fifth+root-up. Var: root+fifth → fourth+root-up → third+seventh → fifth+root-up
texture:doubleStops_phraseStructure:them.watchOut: With two voices to vary, it's tempting to over-decorate. Change ONE dimension at a time — rhythm OR interval — so the variation stays recognizable.
texture:doubleStops_phraseStructure:them.deeperInsight: Baroque 'figured bass' variations are essentially theme-and-variation on harmonized intervals — the theme is a two-voice skeleton and each variation adds decoration to one voice at a time.

texture:doubleStops_phraseStructure:osti.interaction: A double-stop ostinato is a repeating two-voice pattern — think of a cyclical parallel-thirds phrase that loops hypnotically. Steve Cropper did this under soul verses; Bo Diddley built a whole sound from the 'Bo Diddley beat' played as double-stop ostinato. The two voices give the ostinato a richer texture than a single-line loop, but the constraint of no variation still creates trance.
steps: Pick a three- or four-stop ostinato: for instance, root+third → third+fifth → root+third → second+fourth.
Play it once, then repeat exactly.
Loop for at least 16 bars with zero variation — no passing notes, no changes.
Focus on feel and groove — your body motion should deepen while the notes stay constant.
End by letting the final stop decay without flourish.
examples: root+third → fifth+root-up → root+third → third+fifth (four-stop ostinato pattern)
fifth+seventh → fourth+sixth → fifth+seventh → fourth+sixth (two-stop alternation)
texture:doubleStops_phraseStructure:osti.watchOut: Two-voice ostinatos start to sound crowded faster than single-line ones — the harmonic density can feel claustrophobic. If so, space out the rhythm with small rests inside the loop.
texture:doubleStops_phraseStructure:osti.deeperInsight: Steve Cropper's rhythm-lead fills on 'Dock of the Bay' use a double-stop ostinato under the verse — the same three stops repeat hypnotically for the entire song.

texture:triadPartial_phraseStructure:que.interaction: Triad-partial question-and-answer is chord-level dialogue — the question is a partial that ends on a tense chord (maybe the fifth-chord partial, which wants to resolve), and the answer is a partial that resolves to the tonic. Unlike single-line Q-and-A, here you're answering with a HARMONY, not a melody note. This is how reggae and soul rhythm guitarists construct their two-phrase 'comp conversations.'
steps: Play a one- or two-bar question phrase ending on the fifth-chord partial on beat 4.
Answer with a one- or two-bar phrase ending on the tonic partial on beat 4.
Match the rhythms of question and answer so the pair feels balanced.
Use different rhythms for the middle of each phrase but land at the same rhythmic point.
Loop the pair and check that the final tonic partial sounds like resolution of the fifth-chord partial.
examples: Question: tonic partial → fourth-chord partial → fifth-chord partial (unresolved). Answer: fifth-chord partial → fourth-chord partial → tonic partial (resolves home)
Question: tonic partial → sixth-chord partial → fourth-chord partial → fifth-chord partial (suspended). Answer: tonic partial → sixth-chord partial → fourth-chord partial → tonic partial (resolved)
texture:triadPartial_phraseStructure:que.watchOut: If the answer ends on anything other than the tonic partial, it doesn't feel like a resolution. The ear is waiting for the 'home' chord — give it to them.
texture:triadPartial_phraseStructure:que.deeperInsight: Gospel keyboard playing uses partial chord question-and-answer extensively — the pianist comps a question with a five-chord voicing and answers with a tonic voicing, and the congregation hears it as a spoken sentence.

texture:triadPartial_phraseStructure:cal.interaction: Triad-partial call-and-response is rhythm-section dialogue — a call phrase is a rhythmic pattern on one partial, the response is the same pattern on a different partial or the same partial with a twist. This is the skeleton of West African polyrhythms translated to guitar, or a James Brown rhythm guitarist comping with two voicings in conversation.
steps: Play a one-bar rhythmic call on the tonic partial — say, a specific funk chop pattern on beats 2 and 4.
Respond with the SAME rhythm but on the fourth-chord partial.
Next pair: call on the tonic with a different rhythm. Response on the sixth-chord partial with the same new rhythm.
Let the 'call' be the rhythmic statement and the 'response' be the harmonic echo.
End with a call on the tonic and a response on the tonic — the same partial, as the final unity.
examples: Call: [tonic partial on beats 2, 4-and]. Response: [fourth-chord partial on beats 2, 4-and] (same rhythm, new chord)
Call: [tonic partial triplets on beat 2]. Response: [fifth-chord partial triplets on beat 2] (rhythm echoed on the V)
texture:triadPartial_phraseStructure:cal.watchOut: The rhythm of the call and response should be identical — the only change is the chord. If the rhythms differ, it's not call-and-response, it's two unrelated phrases.
texture:triadPartial_phraseStructure:cal.deeperInsight: James Brown's rhythm section often used triad-partial call-and-response — the guitarist played a 'call' rhythm on one chord and the response came from the bass or another guitarist with the same rhythm on a different chord.

texture:triadPartial_phraseStructure:the.interaction: Triad partials on the top three strings are already a miniature — a compact, portable chord shape that fits inside one beat. Theme-and-variation asks you to state that miniature as a clear two-bar idea, then redecorate it each time through without breaking the shape. The interaction turns you into a reggae-jazz rhythm player who comps the same voicing but finds endless small ways to make it speak differently.
steps: Pick one triad partial shape on the top three strings rooted near the fifth fret and plant it as your anchor — do not move it during the round.
State the theme in the first two bars: strum the partial on beat two and beat four, Bob Marley upstroke style, so the pulse is unmistakable.
On the next pass, vary ONE dimension only — swap the on-beat for a sixteenth push, or ghost one string, or mute for one hit.
Keep stacking one fresh variation per pass while the theme shape stays locked; the ear should always recognize the original even at variation four.
Close the round by returning to the unadorned theme statement once — prove to yourself the variations were decoration, not drift.
examples: third → fifth → root (top-string triad, skank on two and four, plain)
third → fifth → root (same shape with a sixteenth-note push into beat three and a muted scratch on beat four)
texture:triadPartial_phraseStructure:the.watchOut: Variations tend to quietly migrate the shape up or down the neck. Keep a fingertip planted as a pivot so the partial voicing itself stays still — it is the rhythm and articulation that change, not the fret position.

texture:triadPartial_phraseStructure:ost.interaction: Triad partials plus ostinato is the heartbeat of Khruangbin, reggae one-drop, and Afrobeat guitar — a tiny three-string voicing looped until it stops being a chord and becomes furniture in the room. The point is not to play more, it is to play the same thing so consistently that the listener forgets you are there, which is precisely when the groove takes over.
steps: Choose a single top-three-string triad partial and lock the picking hand into one repeating rhythmic cell — two bars max.
Loop that two-bar cell without any variation for at least eight passes, eyes closed, letting the metronome disappear into the pattern.
Use palm-on-bridge muting at exactly the same pressure every cycle so the tone of the ostinato does not drift brighter or darker.
When boredom hits — and it will around pass six — do NOT add a fill; instead lean into the repetition and notice how the groove deepens.
End by listening for the one string that wants to go sharp under tension and relax the fretting pressure there to keep the voicing pure.
examples: root → third → fifth (closed triad, chick on beats two and four, sixteen bars straight)
fifth → root → third (first-inversion partial, offbeat skank every eighth, never varied)
texture:triadPartial_phraseStructure:ost.watchOut: The temptation is to 'rescue' the ostinato by sneaking in a grace note or an accidental hammer-on. Resist. The pedagogical payoff is staying identical for longer than feels musical — that is where the hypnosis lives.
texture:triadPartial_phraseStructure:ost.deeperInsight: Great reggae rhythm players practice ostinato triad partials specifically because the consistency is the sound — any variation you add subtracts funk.

texture:fullChord_phraseStructure:questi.interaction: Full chords have weight and finality built in — every strum is already a sentence. Question-and-answer asks you to use that weight deliberately: the first chord phrase lifts and hangs unresolved, the second lands home. Think of a gospel piano player punctuating a preacher, or the push-pull of a Motown rhythm guitar working the verse toward the chorus.
steps: Pick two full-chord voicings: one 'open' or unstable (suspended, inverted, or the five-chord) as your question, one 'home' (the one-chord) as your answer.
Play the question chord across two bars with a strum pattern that rises in intensity and leaves space at the end — the hanging comma.
Answer in the next two bars with the home chord and a settling strum that lands squarely on beat one of the second bar, lower volume than the question.
Alternate four question-answer pairs without shifting tempo; the dynamic arc must do the asking and answering, not speed.
On the final pair, let the answer ring out full length and keep your picking hand still — silence is part of the answer.
examples: (question: fifth-chord full voicing, rising strum) → (answer: root chord, settling strum resolving on the downbeat)
(question: suspended second-chord, unresolved hang) → (answer: root chord with added ninth, soft landing)
texture:fullChord_phraseStructure:questi.watchOut: Players tend to strum the answer just as hard as the question, which flattens the dialogue into a monologue. Drop the right-hand force by a clear notch on every answer or the question stops being a question.

texture:fullChord_phraseStructure:call-r.interaction: Call-and-response with full chords is how gospel and West African guitar congregations talk to each other — the call is a chord punched with conviction, the response is the same chord echoed back softer, or a sibling chord answering in agreement. Unlike question-and-answer, the response is not resolution, it is affirmation. Both halves belong to the same community.
steps: Set up two related full-chord voicings — one punchy comp voicing, one shell or lighter voicing of a closely related chord (relative minor, the four-chord, or an inversion).
Play the call as a single accented strum on beat one with the heavier voicing, letting it ring a full bar.
Deliver the response on beat three of the next bar with the lighter voicing — a little softer, a little shorter, acknowledging but not overpowering.
Repeat the call-response phrase exactly four times so the audience (you) internalizes the shape, then swap which voicing calls and which responds.
Keep both hands relaxed: the call uses wrist-driven power strumming, the response uses fingertip brush strumming — two different motor programs.
examples: (call: root chord full voicing, beat one accent) → (response: sixth-chord shell voicing, beat three echo)
(call: four-chord barre voicing, heavy strum) → (response: root chord with added second, fingertip brush)
texture:fullChord_phraseStructure:call-r.watchOut: Do not let the response collapse into a filler strum just because it comes second. The response has to sound like it chose to answer — not like you rushed to fill the gap.

texture:fullChord_phraseStructure:theme-.interaction: A full-chord theme-and-variation is how a singer-songwriter keeps four bars of the same chord progression interesting for three minutes. The theme is a simple strum pattern over your voicings; variations redecorate by changing rhythm, voicing, muting, or ornament — never all at once. This is the Paul Simon, Joni Mitchell, Jack Johnson discipline.
steps: Pick a two-chord progression in full voicings and set a simple theme strum pattern — quarter notes down, or an easy 'down-down-up-up-down-up' per bar.
Play the theme twice, exactly the same, so the ear prints it as the baseline.
For the first variation, change only the strum pattern (e.g., add a sixteenth push on the 'and of three') while keeping both voicings identical.
For the second variation, keep the new strum and swap one voicing for a different inversion of the same chord — the bass note moves, the character changes.
For the third variation, mute selected strings with the fretting hand to create a percussive gap in the middle of the bar — same chords, new groove.
Return to the plain theme at the end so the journey is audible.
examples: (theme: root chord → four-chord, down-up-down-up, full ring) → (variation: same chords with sixteenth push and first-inversion four)
(theme: plain quarter strum) → (variation: percussive dead-string chunk on beat three, everything else unchanged)
texture:fullChord_phraseStructure:theme-.watchOut: Changing two things at once in a variation is cheating — the ear loses the through-line. Limit yourself to one altered dimension per pass so the theme still pokes through.

texture:fullChord_phraseStructure:ostina.interaction: Full-chord ostinato is the trance engine of AC/DC, Tom Petty, and The Strokes — the same handful of chords played with the same strum pattern for the entire song. It teaches endurance, pocket, and the hardest thing in guitar: letting a groove be enough without adding anything. The brain wants to decorate; the body has to learn to refuse.
steps: Pick a two-to-four-chord progression in full voicings and lock a single strum pattern — one bar max before it repeats.
Play the ostinato for at least sixteen bars without any change to rhythm, voicing, or dynamic shape.
Use wrist-driven strumming (not elbow) so your right arm can sustain the repetition without tightening up.
Every four bars, check in with your left thumb — it should stay relaxed behind the neck; ostinato practice is where chord-hand tension accumulates fastest.
When the loop starts to feel 'boring,' treat that as the doorway — the groove is about to unlock. Play eight more bars.
examples: (root chord → four-chord → five-chord → four-chord, plain down-up strum, repeated sixteen bars)
(root chord → six-chord minor, two bars each, identical strum pattern, no fills)
texture:fullChord_phraseStructure:ostina.watchOut: Full-chord ostinato invites sneaky dynamic creep — each pass gets slightly louder. Use the volume meter to keep the average level flat across the whole round, or the hypnosis breaks.
texture:fullChord_phraseStructure:ostina.deeperInsight: A song's entire energy can rise through arrangement alone (drums, voice, instrumentation) while the guitar ostinato stays flat — that is the Tom Petty secret.

texture:mixed_phraseStructure:question-a.interaction: Mixed textures — chord stabs alternating with single-note fills — are already a dialogue at the microphrase level. Wrapping that inside question-and-answer turns the round into a two-level conversation: the chords ask within each phrase, the fills answer; and across phrase pairs, one whole gesture questions and the next whole gesture answers. Think Jimi Hendrix verse rhythm or Curtis Mayfield fills between comp chords.
steps: Set up a full-chord comping voicing and a short single-note fill cell that uses the top two strings.
For the question phrase: strum two stabs, leave a gap, then play an unresolved single-note fill ending on an upper scale tone (seventh or second) that hangs in the air.
For the answer phrase: same rhythmic shape, but the fill lands on the root or third — the home tones — at the end.
Alternate four question-answer pairs, keeping the stab placement identical so the fill is clearly the voice that is speaking.
Use palm muting on the picking hand during the fills to differentiate them sonically from the chord stabs.
examples: (stab - stab - gap - fill: fourth → fifth → sixth, hanging on the sixth) → (stab - stab - gap - fill: fifth → third → root, landing on the root)
(full chord stab - fill: seventh → root → second, unresolved) → (full chord stab - fill: third → second → root, answering home)
texture:mixed_phraseStructure:question-a.watchOut: When you switch from chord to single-note and back, the fretting hand often loses grip on the chord voicing. Practice the shape hold without releasing so the stab sounds fresh every return.

texture:mixed_phraseStructure:call-respo.interaction: Mixed-texture call-and-response is textbook funk-soul guitar — a chord stab calls out, a single-note line responds, and the whole band leans into the gap between them. Think Nile Rodgers comping with Bernard Edwards's bass reply, or Curtis Mayfield's right hand answering his own left. The call is chordal weight; the response is melodic commentary.
steps: Choose a sharp, short chord stab voicing — often a ninth or thirteenth chord on the middle strings — as your call.
Build a two-to-four-note single-line response using scale tones from the same chord's mode.
Play the call on beat one with a staccato right-hand cut (palm lands on strings immediately after the strum).
Deliver the response in the second half of the bar, picked, softer, on the top two strings.
Repeat the exact call-response cell four times, then change ONLY the response line while keeping the call chord the same.
Alternate whose turn it is — sometimes the chord stab speaks alone, sometimes the fill comments back.
examples: (call: ninth-chord stab, staccato) → (response: fifth → sixth → root on top string, picked)
(call: thirteenth-chord punch) → (response: third → second → root, descending reply)
texture:mixed_phraseStructure:call-respo.watchOut: Timing the stab cutoff is the whole game. If the chord rings into the response, you have blurred the call and the conversation turns into mumble. Make the silence between them audible.

texture:mixed_phraseStructure:theme-vari.interaction: A mixed-texture theme-and-variation is the John Mayer / Derek Trucks / Hendrix playground — state a compact rhythm-fill cell as the theme, then vary either the chord placement, the fill shape, or the dynamic without losing the bone structure. The challenge is that mixed texture already has two voices (stab and fill), so every variation has to decide which voice gets to change.
steps: Lock a two-bar theme: a chord stab pattern in bar one plus a short single-note fill in bar two, nothing fancy.
State the theme cleanly twice so the shape is established — imagine writing it in pencil first.
For variation one, keep the chord stabs identical and vary ONLY the fill — use the same rhythm but new scale tones.
For variation two, keep the fill identical and vary ONLY the stab — add muted ghost strums between hits, or change the strum direction.
For variation three, shrink the gap between stab and fill so they overlap rhythmically — the voices start finishing each other's sentences.
Return to the plain theme once more as a cadence.
examples: (theme: two stabs + fill of root → third → fifth) → (variation: same stabs + fill of root → fourth → second, same rhythm)
(theme: plain chord jabs + ascending fill) → (variation: ghost-strummed chord jabs + same fill)
texture:mixed_phraseStructure:theme-vari.watchOut: Do not vary both voices in the same pass — the theme dissolves and you are just improvising. Constrain yourself to change one side at a time.

texture:mixed_phraseStructure:ostinato.interaction: Mixed-texture ostinato is the Nile Rodgers 'Good Times' chassis, or Bill Withers's 'Use Me' — a tight chord-plus-fill cell that loops until the pocket is unbreakable. Because mixed textures have built-in rhythmic interest, they make ostinato practice easier to sustain; because you are looping, every detail of placement matters enormously.
steps: Construct a one-bar cell: two staccato chord stabs + a three-note single-line fill in the second half of the bar.
Loop the cell without variation for at least sixteen passes — set a timer if necessary.
Use palm-on-bridge muting for the stabs and release it for the fill, and train that transition to be automatic.
Every four bars, check your right-hand wrist — it should be making small circles, not chopping from the elbow.
When the cell feels fully autopilot, close your eyes and keep looping; the goal is to stop tracking it consciously.
examples: (two muted stabs + fill: fifth → sixth → root, repeated sixteen bars)
(chord stab on beat one + fill: seventh → root → second on the back half, identical every bar)
texture:mixed_phraseStructure:ostinato.watchOut: Mixed-texture ostinatos drift because the fill is the seductive part — players start 'improving' it. Keep the fill locked; the groove lives in the sameness.

texture:singleLine_neckZone:open.interaction: Single-line melody in open position is the sound of folk, country flatpicking, and early blues — fretted notes ringing alongside open strings, creating a drone-like sustain under the line. The interaction is that open position gives you 'free sustain' because open strings keep ringing: your melodic choices become decisions about when to let the previous note bleed into the next.
steps: Stay entirely within frets zero through five and pick one scale rooted there with at least two open strings available as drone notes.
Play a descending line that deliberately lands on an open string every three notes, letting it ring.
Alternate pick with relaxed wrist motion — the fretting hand is the active voice, the picking hand is just the delivery mechanism.
Try the same line but damp each open string with your fretting-hand fingertip the moment the next note sounds — notice how much drier it feels.
Return to letting the open strings ring. The 'open position' identity is the ring, not the fret numbers.
examples: root → second → third → open fifth (let fifth ring through the next phrase)
fifth → fourth → third → second → open root (descending line settling on the ringing tonic)
texture:singleLine_neckZone:open.watchOut: Open-position single lines can get muddy when every open string is allowed to ring indefinitely. Choose one or two drone-friendly strings and mute the others with the heel of your palm so the melody stays legible.

texture:singleLine_neckZone:mid.interaction: Single-line in mid-neck (frets five through nine) lives in barre-chord territory — the strings are tenser, the notes fatter, and the ringing sympathetic sustain of open position is gone. This is where blues solos sit, where the horn-like singing tone lives, and where you are forced to rely on fretting-hand phrasing for expression because the open strings cannot help you.
steps: Choose a scale box rooted at the fifth or seventh fret and do not leave frets five through nine for the whole round.
Play a single-note phrase using only adjacent strings (string crossing is harder in this zone), letting each note sing its full value.
Use the fretting-hand fingertip as a damper on the string you just left — there are no open strings to hide behind.
Focus on the attack: slightly harder pick strokes here bring out the fat compressed tone this zone is known for.
End phrases on the chord tones (root, third, fifth) which sit at specific fingertips in the box — learn their fret positions by feel.
examples: third → fourth → fifth → seventh → root up an octave (ascending mid-neck pentatonic line)
fifth → fourth → third → root (bluesy descent within one box, every note ringing full length)
texture:singleLine_neckZone:mid.watchOut: Mid-neck is where finger independence falls apart — the ring finger and pinky get sloppy under string tension. If a note chokes or buzzes, press just behind the fret, not on top, and relax between notes.

texture:singleLine_neckZone:high.interaction: Single lines above the twelfth fret are weightless and vocal — think Jeff Beck, late Santana, the soaring top end of a Derek Trucks solo. The interaction is that high-neck single-line practice trains melodic commitment: there is no room for extra notes up here, the strings are short and unforgiving, and every note has to justify itself.
steps: Keep the whole round between frets nine and fifteen — set a rule and physically resist dropping back down.
Play sparse, vocal phrases — fewer notes, longer durations, each one sung like a held vowel.
The fretting hand has to squeeze less: high frets need light pressure because the string length is short; excess force pulls the pitch sharp.
Use slight fingertip vibrato on sustained notes to keep them alive — high-neck notes die fast without movement.
Target landing notes that are chord tones of the tonic — the root, third, and fifth in this zone are your vocal anchors.
examples: fifth → sixth → seventh → root up an octave (rising phrase floating into the high register)
root up an octave → seventh → fifth (descending three-note phrase, each note sustained a full beat)
texture:singleLine_neckZone:high.watchOut: High-neck notes pull sharp when you fret too hard — it is the number-one intonation bug up here. Lighten your left hand by fifty percent compared to mid-neck and the notes will sit in tune.

texture:singleLine_neckZone:fullRange.interaction: Single-line over the full range is the cinematic sweep — Allman Brothers solos, Tinariwen desert-blues lines, the full vocabulary of a player who owns the whole neck. The interaction trains you to think of the fretboard as one continuous instrument instead of a series of boxes, and to use vertical leaps between zones as expressive punctuation.
steps: Decide on one scale and identify the root note in three different zones (open, mid, high) before you play anything.
Start a phrase in open position, climb to mid-neck within four notes, and land the phrase high-neck on the root up an octave.
Use position shifts ONLY on scale tones — a slide or a planted jump on a chord tone is invisible; a jump on a passing tone is a glitch.
Every phrase should traverse at least two zones — no staying put.
Listen for the tonal change as you climb — the strings get thinner and brighter; use that shift as the phrase's built-in dynamic arc.
examples: root (open) → third → fifth → root up an octave (mid) → third up an octave (high) — rising arpeggio across three zones
root up an octave (high) → fifth → third → root (mid) → fifth (open) — descending phrase using the neck's vertical gradient
texture:singleLine_neckZone:fullRange.watchOut: Full-range players fall into the trap of box-to-box leaping without connecting tissue. Practice passing through the transition zones (fret five, fret nine) with scale motion, not jumps.

texture:doubleStops_neckZone:open.interaction: Double stops in open position are the backbone of country chicken-pickin' and Delta blues — pairs of notes where at least one is often an open string, giving you that ringing, bell-like, old-timey character. The interaction is that open position lets you voice double stops with one fretted note and one open, which means you get sustain for free while the fretted note does the melodic work.
steps: Stay in frets zero through five and find double stops where one note is an open string and the other is fretted on an adjacent string.
Pick both strings together using either pick-and-middle-finger (hybrid) or a firm downstroke that hits both at once.
Move the fretted note through the scale while the open string stays the same — this creates parallel movement against a drone.
Try thirds (two strings apart) and sixths (three strings apart) separately; they have very different characters.
Let each double stop ring full length before moving — the open string sustain is the whole point.
examples: (fretted third + open root on the adjacent string) → (fretted fourth + same open root) — parallel melody over a drone
(fretted sixth on one string + open second on the adjacent string) → (fretted root + open third) — sliding sixths in open position
texture:doubleStops_neckZone:open.watchOut: Open-position double stops expose buzzing fretted notes because the open string is perfectly clean by comparison. Fret just behind the fret wire, not in the middle of the fret, and the buzz disappears.

texture:doubleStops_neckZone:mid.interaction: Mid-neck double stops are where Chuck Berry, Keith Richards, and classic rock rhythm guitar live — thirds and sixths voiced in the fat middle register, played with swagger. The interaction is that mid-neck double stops sound chunkier and more compressed than open-position ones, which is why they dominate rock rhythm comping.
steps: Park yourself in a mid-neck box (around fret five or seven) and work only with thirds on the top two strings or the middle string pair.
Practice sliding into each double stop from one fret below — the quick slide is the classic rock articulation.
Use a slight palm mute so the double stops have bite without ringing into each other when you change shapes.
Alternate between thirds (narrow, tight) and sixths (wider, sweeter) to feel the difference in character within the same zone.
Target double stops that frame the root, third, and fifth of the home chord — those are your landing points.
examples: (slide from a fret below → third + fifth on the top strings) → (slide → fifth + seventh)
(sixth + root double stop, struck cleanly) → (sixth + fifth on adjacent pair, palm muted)
texture:doubleStops_neckZone:mid.watchOut: Mid-neck double stops buzz when the fretting hand does not press both strings evenly — the index finger must roll flat across the pair. If one note chokes, flatten the finger slightly and try again.

texture:doubleStops_neckZone:high.interaction: High-neck double stops are surf, psychedelia, and the shimmering top of a Dick Dale, Santo & Johnny, or Allah-Las solo. Up here the strings are short and bright, and two-note voicings take on a bell-like clarity that cuts through any mix. The interaction pushes you to voice double stops with extreme lightness — the high frets amplify every micro-tension in the fretting hand.
steps: Stay above the ninth fret and find thirds and sixths on the top two string pairs only.
Play them with a soft pick attack and let them ring out — at this height, overplaying kills the shimmer.
Fret with featherlight pressure — the string length is short, so pressing hard pulls both notes sharp.
Add a slow fingertip vibrato on the sustained double stops for that surf / slide-guitar halo.
Target the octave notes (root and fifth up an octave) and use them as landing points; they ring longest up here.
examples: (third + fifth high on the top strings, ringing) → (fourth + sixth, quick bend up a semitone)
(root up an octave + third up an octave double stop) → (same shape slid up two frets and back)
texture:doubleStops_neckZone:high.watchOut: High-neck double stops go sharp the instant you fret too hard. Use the lightest pressure you can manage without buzz — the thinner high strings reward a delicate touch.

texture:doubleStops_neckZone:fullRange.interaction: Double stops across the full range is how Derek Trucks plays, or Ry Cooder — two-note melodic thinking that uses the whole neck to get from parlor-blues sweetness up to slide-guitar soaring. The interaction is you are not playing chords, you are not playing single notes, you are playing a harmonized melody whose harmony happens to move vertically across the entire fretboard.
steps: Map the same double-stop shape (say, sixths) in three zones before you play: open, mid, high.
Play a melodic phrase in sixths that starts open, climbs to mid-neck, and tops out high-neck on a chord tone.
Use slides between zones — a full-range phrase rarely jumps cleanly; the slide is your connective tissue.
Listen for the harmonic color change as you rise — the same interval sounds different in each zone.
End the phrase on a high-neck double stop and let it ring with vibrato — the full-range arrival has to feel earned.
examples: (open-position third + fifth) → slide → (mid-neck third + fifth) → slide → (high-neck third + fifth) — vertical climb
(mid-neck sixth + root) → descending slide to (open sixth + root) → finish on a ringing high root up an octave
texture:doubleStops_neckZone:fullRange.watchOut: The biggest trap is losing the harmonic interval as you move zones — the shape might shift physically, but the interval should stay the same (third stays a third, sixth stays a sixth). Track the interval, not the finger pattern.

texture:triadPartial_neckZone:open.interaction: Triad partials in open position are the sound of folk, acoustic pop, and early reggae comping — three-string voicings that incorporate open strings as chord tones, giving you sustain without effort. Think Jack Johnson, early Bob Marley, James Taylor. The interaction is that open strings lock you into specific voicings dictated by the key; the practice is learning to embrace those constraints rather than fight them.
steps: Stay in frets zero through five and find top-three-string triad partials where at least one note is an open string.
Strum only the top three strings with a gentle fingertip brush so the open strings ring clean.
Move between two partials that share one open string — the shared note is your pivot, glue between shapes.
Let each partial ring its full length; open-position partials are meant to sustain, not stab.
Shift between two partials every two bars to feel how the shared open string stitches them together.
examples: (root + third + fifth partial with open fifth on top) → (four-chord partial sharing the same open fifth)
(fifth-chord top-string triad) → (relative minor partial, open second-degree string rings through both)
texture:triadPartial_neckZone:open.watchOut: Open-position triad partials often include accidentally ringing lower strings. Mute the unused strings with the side of your thumb or a ghosted left-hand fingertip so only the partial sounds.

texture:triadPartial_neckZone:mid.interaction: Mid-neck triad partials are the heart of reggae, funk, and jazz comping — movable top-three-string shapes that can slide anywhere and always voice the chord cleanly. Think Bob Marley's upstrokes, Cory Wong's sixteenth-note funk, or Grant Green's jazz comp. The interaction is that mid-neck is where partial voicings become infinitely transposable; the practice is moving them as a unit without the voicing falling apart.
steps: Pick one triad partial shape and commit to moving it to three different mid-neck positions (frets five, seven, and nine).
Strum only the top three strings with a palm-muted staccato chick on beats two and four.
When sliding between positions, the fretting hand should move as a single unit — the shape stays locked, the whole hand travels.
Keep the picking hand completely still in terms of motion — every slide lands on the same offbeat.
Alternate between two related partials (root chord and four-chord) and feel the physical memory of each landing fret.
examples: (root-chord partial at fret five, chick on two and four) → slide → (four-chord partial at fret seven, same chick)
(first-inversion root partial at fret seven) → (second-inversion partial at fret nine, same strum)
texture:triadPartial_neckZone:mid.watchOut: Mid-neck partial voicings collapse when the fretting hand grips too hard during slides — the fingers catch and one note chokes. Keep the grip just tight enough to sound the notes, then loosen it during the slide.

texture:triadPartial_neckZone:high.interaction: High-neck triad partials are the upper-register jangle of Khruangbin, the psychedelic shimmer of Tame Impala, and the soul comping of Grant Green — three-string voicings above the ninth fret that chime like a music box. The interaction is that up here, partials are physically tiny (frets are narrow) but sonically soaring; the practice is fretting with a featherweight touch so every note speaks clean.
steps: Stay above the ninth fret and find top-three-string partials voiced high — often around frets ten through fifteen.
Use a fingerstyle or fingertip brush rather than a pick — high-neck partials reward touch.
Fret light: high-neck string lengths are short, and excess pressure pulls all three notes sharp at once.
Let the partial ring for at least a full beat before moving; up here, the sustain is the character.
Target partials that land on the root, third, and fifth of the home chord in this octave — they are the sparkle.
examples: (high-neck root-chord partial at fret twelve, fingertip brush) → (same partial slid to fret fifteen)
(triad partial voiced as root + third + fifth up an octave) → ring for two beats, then quick grace-note shift
texture:triadPartial_neckZone:high.watchOut: High-neck partials lose clarity when the fingertips are not arched — any flesh touching an adjacent string deadens the voicing. Fret on the very tips, relaxed but precise.

texture:triadPartial_neckZone:fullRange.interaction: Triad partials across the full range is how Nile Rodgers, Cory Wong, and Khruangbin's Mark Speer build entire songs out of comping — same three-note shapes, moved vertically from open position up to high-neck, turning a single chord into a whole journey. The interaction trains you to see partials as a movable unit and to use zone changes as the song's built-in dynamic arc.
steps: Map the same triad partial shape in three zones (open, mid, high) before playing anything.
Start a groove with the partial in open position for two bars, then slide it up to mid-neck for two bars, then up to high-neck for two bars.
Keep the strum pattern identical in every zone — only the position changes.
The rise through zones should feel like an arrangement crescendo, not a technical exercise.
Return to open position for the final two bars to close the arc — the round becomes a song form.
examples: (open partial, two bars) → (mid-neck partial, two bars, same strum) → (high-neck partial, two bars) → (back to open)
(fret three partial, skank) → slide → (fret seven partial, identical skank) → slide → (fret twelve partial, identical)
texture:triadPartial_neckZone:fullRange.watchOut: The biggest slip is changing strum intensity as you climb — players unconsciously strum harder in the high zone. Force yourself to keep the dynamics flat so the vertical motion is the only change.

texture:fullChord_neckZone:open.interaction: Full chords in open position is the cowboy-chord foundation — first-position voicings where open strings are ringing through every shape. Folk, campfire pop, early country, acoustic singer-songwriter. The interaction is that open chords have a specific sonic weight you cannot get anywhere else on the neck: open strings add sympathetic sustain, brightness, and a chime that is impossible with barre chords.
steps: Use only open-position full-chord voicings — no barres, no movable shapes.
Strum each chord fully with a relaxed wrist and let every open string ring — that ring IS the open-position sound.
Switch between two chords that share at least one open string (common in diatonic progressions) and let that shared string bridge them seamlessly.
Use the heel of your picking-hand palm to control any low string that shouldn't ring — open position requires selective muting.
End each chord by letting it ring its full length — you are practicing the sustain as much as the strum.
examples: (open root chord, full strum) → (open four-chord, full strum) — shared open strings bleeding between
(open five-chord) → (open six-chord minor) — contrasting bright major and darker minor voicings
texture:fullChord_neckZone:open.watchOut: Open chords get muddy when every string rings indiscriminately. Decide which open strings you actually want to sound and lightly damp the rest with your palm or thumb.

texture:fullChord_neckZone:mid.interaction: Full chords in mid-neck is barre-chord and power-chord country — the thick, compressed strumming tone of rock rhythm, seventies singer-songwriter, and pop-punk. The interaction is that mid-neck full chords have no open strings to help them; your fretting hand has to do all the work, and your endurance becomes the real limitation.
steps: Stay in mid-neck (frets five to nine) and use only barre-chord or movable full-voicing shapes.
Strum with a steady wrist motion — mid-neck strumming rewards consistency over power.
Check your fretting-hand thumb every four bars; it should stay relaxed behind the neck, not clamped over the top.
Use the clean barre technique: the index finger should press hardest at the knuckle joint, not the pad.
Alternate between two barre voicings (root-position and sixth-string-root chord) without taking the barre finger off the strings — it slides position without lifting.
examples: (mid-neck root-chord barre, steady down-up strum) → (four-chord barre two frets up, identical rhythm)
(five-chord barre voicing, strummed) → (relative minor barre, same strum)
texture:fullChord_neckZone:mid.watchOut: Barre-chord buzz happens when the index finger rolls onto its side or fingers cramp up. Relax the thumb, re-seat the barre just behind the fret, and take a breath between chord changes to release tension.

texture:fullChord_neckZone:high.interaction: Full chords above the ninth fret are rare and specific — they appear in jazz voicings, John Mayer-style upper-register comping, and the shimmering top end of a reverb-soaked indie song. The interaction is that high-neck full chords have to be voiced carefully because the frets are narrow and the strings are thin; crammed fingers and sympathetic ringing make them tricky.
steps: Use full four-note-plus voicings played entirely above the ninth fret — rootless jazz voicings work perfectly here.
Strum with a soft fingerstyle brush or an extremely light pick — high-neck full chords cannot take heavy strumming.
Fret with the tips of your fingers, very arched, and use minimum pressure — at this height, excess grip pulls every note sharp.
Let each chord ring for a full bar before moving — high-neck full chords are more about statement than groove.
Target voicings that include the root and third in the upper register — that is where the shimmer lives.
examples: (high-neck rootless root-chord voicing, fingerstyle, full ring) → (rootless four-chord voicing two frets up)
(high-neck sixth-chord shell voicing) → (high-neck root chord with added second)
texture:fullChord_neckZone:high.watchOut: High-neck full chords will feel cramped — that is normal. The fix is not more force, it is more finger arch. Curl the knuckles tighter and the notes will speak even at light pressure.

texture:fullChord_neckZone:fullRange.interaction: Full chords across the whole neck is the moving-voicings discipline of jazz, pop songwriting, and cinematic rhythm playing — the same progression voiced differently in each register, creating a vertical arrangement inside a single guitar part. The interaction is that you are not just playing chords, you are orchestrating them, using the neck as a range of timbres from open warmth to high-neck shimmer.
steps: Pick a two-chord progression and map three voicings for each chord — open, mid-neck barre, and high-neck rootless.
Cycle through the progression three times: first with open voicings, then mid-neck, then high-neck.
Each octave-zone should feel like a dynamic gear — open is warm baseline, mid is driving body, high is sparkling top.
Use slides between zones rather than jumps — the connection is part of the musicality.
End on the high-neck voicing letting it ring — the full-range arc closes upward.
examples: (open root chord → open four-chord) → (mid-neck barre root → mid-neck barre four) → (high-neck root → high-neck four)
(open five-chord → six-chord minor) → (mid-neck voicings of same pair) → (rootless high voicings of same pair)
texture:fullChord_neckZone:fullRange.watchOut: Transitioning between zones is where the chords lose clarity — you strum the old shape and fret the new. Finish the ring of the previous voicing before starting the next and the transitions become musical rather than muddled.

texture:mixed_neckZone:open.interaction: Mixed textures in open position is the acoustic singer-songwriter vocabulary — a full open chord ringing, then a single-note fill that weaves around it using nearby open strings as hidden drones. Think James Taylor, Nick Drake, Paul Simon. The interaction is that the open strings give your fills free sustain, so even a two-note melodic tag rings like a phrase.
