steps: Use an open-position full chord as your stab and weave single-note fills using scale tones in frets zero through five.
The fill should walk away from the chord and come back — typically two to four notes that land on a chord tone.
Let open strings ring through the fill; do not damp them just because you moved to single-note mode.
Use hybrid picking (pick plus middle finger) to switch seamlessly between the chord stab and the line.
Keep the stab-to-fill transition smooth: the picking hand barely changes position; only the targets change.
examples: (open root chord strum) → (fill: open root → third → second → root, sustained open strings ringing)
(open four-chord strum) → (fill: fourth → fifth → third → open root)
texture:mixed_neckZone:open.watchOut: Open-position mixed textures get muddy because everything wants to ring — including notes you didn't play. Use the left-hand fingertips to lightly damp unwanted open strings as you move into the fill.

texture:mixed_neckZone:mid.interaction: Mixed textures in mid-neck is the funk-soul-R&B playground — a palm-muted chord stab followed by a compressed single-note fill, all inside the fat mid-register. Think Curtis Mayfield, Prince, Jimi Hendrix verse rhythm. The interaction is that mid-neck has no open strings to help, so both the chord and the fill have to earn their clarity through fretting-hand precision.
steps: Lock yourself into a mid-neck box around fret five or seven. No leaving it for the round.
Use a ninth- or thirteenth-chord voicing on the middle strings as your stab — classic funk palette.
After each chord stab, play a two-to-three-note single-line fill inside the same box using chord tones.
Palm-mute the stab with the heel of your picking hand and release the mute for the fill — the tonal contrast matters.
Cycle: stab - fill - stab - fill, each pair filling one bar, for at least eight bars straight.
examples: (mid-neck ninth-chord stab, palm muted) → (fill: fifth → third → root in the same box)
(mid-neck thirteenth-chord stab) → (fill: seventh → root → second, tight)
texture:mixed_neckZone:mid.watchOut: Mid-neck mixed textures fall apart when the fretting hand has to reshape too fast — the stab voicing collapses before the fill lands. Choose a fill that shares a finger position with the chord so the transition is minimal.

texture:mixed_neckZone:high.interaction: Mixed textures in high-neck is the shimmering soul-jazz vocabulary — upper-register chord stabs answered by delicate single-note lines on the top strings. Think Grant Green, George Benson, Phoebe Bridgers's guitarist — bell-like and intimate. The interaction is that high-neck is fragile; both the chord and the fill require extremely light touch or they pull sharp.
steps: Stay above the ninth fret. Use a small rootless voicing or triad partial as your chord stab, and build fills on the top two strings.
Pick everything gently — high-neck mixed texture rewards a fingertip or hybrid approach, never aggressive picking.
The chord stab should be a quick brush, letting it ring briefly before the fill answers.
Fills should land on chord tones in the upper octave — root and third up high are your targets.
Fret the fills with barely any pressure — up here, the strings respond to the lightest touch and punish anything heavier.
examples: (high-neck triad-partial stab) → (fill on top string: fifth → sixth → root up an octave, soft)
(rootless high chord voicing, brushed) → (fill: third up an octave → second → root up an octave)
texture:mixed_neckZone:high.watchOut: High-neck mixed textures sound cramped because you are working in a tiny physical space. Fret with arched knuckles, relax the thumb, and use fingertip precision — force is the enemy of this zone.

texture:mixed_neckZone:fullRange.interaction: Mixed textures across the full range is the cinematic guitar approach — a chord stab low, a melodic fill high, and the vertical journey between them creating the arrangement. Think Gilmour's 'Comfortably Numb,' John Mayer live solos, the Allman Brothers dual-guitar conversation condensed into one player. The interaction is that you use zone changes as the emotional arc: low chord grounds, high fill lifts.
steps: Start with a full chord stab in open or mid position — solid, grounding, weighty.
Answer it with a single-note fill that climbs from the chord zone up to the high neck.
Return to the low-zone chord for the next phrase — the fill is the high voice, the chord is the bass.
Use slides during the fill to show the zone transition — jumps lose the musicality.
After four cycles, try the inverse: high-neck chord stab answered by a low-position fill — feel the different gravity.
examples: (open root chord stab) → (fill climbing: third → fifth → root up an octave → third up an octave, ending high-neck)
(mid-neck barre chord stab) → (fill descending to open: root → seventh → fifth → third, ending open)
texture:mixed_neckZone:fullRange.watchOut: The biggest slip is letting the chord and fill become the same loudness. The chord should be the frame; the fill is the voice. Drop the strum intensity slightly so the fill can float above it.

texture:singleLine_noteTransition:cleanA.interaction: Single-line with clean attack is textbook jazz / country / bluegrass picking — every note individually articulated, no legato shortcuts, no ringing connections. The interaction forces both hands to coordinate perfectly: the picking hand places the attack, the fretting hand releases cleanly before the next note, and there is no blur. It is one of the hardest disciplines in guitar and one of the most useful.
steps: Use alternate picking (down-up-down-up) with strict relaxation — clean attack is a wrist motion, not an arm motion.
The fretting-hand finger lifts OFF its note the instant the picking hand hits the next note — no early release, no late release.
Focus on the synchronization: every pick stroke meets the fretting-hand finger as it presses down. Mismatch equals blur.
Practice slowly first; clean attack at tempo is the payoff of clean attack slow.
End each note fully — no sympathetic ringing from adjacent strings. Use the fretting-hand fingertips to damp.
examples: root → second → third → fourth → fifth (ascending scale, every note picked cleanly, no connection)
third → root → fifth → root → seventh → root (arpeggio with clean attack, each note crisp)
texture:singleLine_noteTransition:cleanA.watchOut: The failure mode is sympathetic ringing — an adjacent string buzzing because your fretting fingers lifted lazily. Practice damping with the fingertip of the finger that just played so each note is a precise event.

texture:singleLine_noteTransition:hammer.interaction: Single-line legato (hammer-ons and pull-offs) is the sound of Eric Johnson, Allan Holdsworth, and any bluesy fluidity — pick one note, let the fretting hand do the rest. The interaction trains fretting-hand independence: the hammering finger has to land with enough force to sound the note without picking, and the pulling finger has to flick sideways, not just lift.
steps: Pick one note per beat and fill the rest with hammer-ons and pull-offs — the picking hand works less than the fretting hand.
Hammers: drop the finger hard and fast at the fret wire; weight comes from the big knuckle, not the fingertip.
Pull-offs: flick the finger sideways (toward the floor for treble strings) so the string rings as you release.
Keep the picking hand in constant motion rhythmically even when it is not striking — the motion keeps the pulse locked.
Alternate between hammer-pull patterns and pure picked patterns to feel the difference in motor load.
examples: (picked root) → hammer → third → pull → second → hammer → third (legato four-note cell per beat)
(picked fifth) → hammer → seventh → pull → fifth → hammer → sixth (legato through a pentatonic scale)
texture:singleLine_noteTransition:hammer.watchOut: Hammer-on notes sound weaker than picked notes unless the finger drops with real speed. If legato notes are ghost-quiet, it is not about fret pressure — it is about attack velocity. Drop fast, not hard.

texture:singleLine_noteTransition:slideC.interaction: Single-line with slide connections is the desert-blues, Tinariwen, Allah-Las, surf-guitar sound — gliding pitches that blur the boundary between notes. The interaction turns each transition into a small dramatic gesture: the slide itself is musical content, not just a way to get somewhere. How fast you slide, how far you slide, and whether the destination note is picked or caught from the slide all become expressive choices.
steps: Play a melodic phrase where at least every other note is connected to the next by a slide — no jumps.
Slide TO the target note, not THROUGH it. Stop cleanly on the destination.
Decide before each slide: is the destination note picked (re-attacked) or unpicked (caught silently from the slide)?
Use the fingertip with constant pressure during the slide — if pressure drops, the slide goes silent.
Experiment with slide speed: a quick slide is a slur, a slow slide is a cry. Both are correct; both are different.
examples: (pick root) → slide up to third → (pick fifth) → slide down to third → (pick root)
(pick fifth) → slow slide up to sixth → (pick seventh) → quick slide to root up an octave
texture:singleLine_noteTransition:slideC.watchOut: Slide connections die when the fretting pressure drops mid-slide. Keep the finger heavy on the string through the entire slide — if you hear a silent zone, you lifted.

texture:singleLine_noteTransition:bendTa.interaction: Single-line with bends to target is the blues-rock voice of Gilmour, B.B. King, Albert Collins — you start on a lower note and bend up INTO the target pitch, so the note is literally born out of expression. The interaction asks for precise pitch control: the bend has to land exactly on the target, no sharp, no flat, or the whole phrase collapses.
steps: Pick the source note one or two frets below your intended target — typically one whole step below.
Bend up to the target pitch using finger, wrist, AND forearm rotation together — do not bend from the finger alone.
Use another fretting-hand finger behind the bending finger for support (middle and ring together, for example).
Hold the bend at pitch for a full beat before releasing or continuing — the destination is the payoff.
Practice bending TO the root or the fifth — those are the most satisfying targets; saving them for bends makes every phrase feel resolved.
examples: (pick second) → bend up a whole step to target the third → hold
(pick fourth) → bend up a whole step to target the fifth → release back to the fourth → pick the third
texture:singleLine_noteTransition:bendTa.watchOut: The failure mode is missing the target pitch — bending too little (sounds sour) or too much (sounds panicked). Before each bend, sing or think the target pitch so your finger has a destination.

texture:doubleStops_noteTransition:clean.interaction: Double stops with clean attack is the Chuck Berry / Keith Richards / classic rock rhythm discipline — every pair of notes individually struck, no ringing between, no legato cheating. The interaction demands that both fingers on both strings release cleanly as the next pair is struck; any lingering creates a blur that kills the rock-rhythm clarity.
steps: Set up a two-note double-stop shape (third + fifth, or root + fourth) and pick both strings simultaneously with a firm downstroke.
Release both fretting-hand fingers together the moment the next pair is struck — synchronized attack and release.
Use palm muting lightly so the strings cut short when you release; this sharpens the clean-attack character.
Keep both fingers perfectly arched so no adjacent string rings sympathetically.
Practice at moderate tempo; clean attack on double stops is harder than single lines because you have doubled the coordination problem.
examples: (third + fifth double stop) → (fourth + sixth double stop) → (fifth + seventh double stop) (ascending clean double stops)
(root + third) → (seventh + second) → (sixth + root) (descending clean sequence)
texture:doubleStops_noteTransition:clean.watchOut: One finger lifting a moment late is the most common flaw — the prior double stop bleeds into the next. Watch the slower finger (usually the pinky) and train both fingers to release at the same instant.

texture:doubleStops_noteTransition:hamme.interaction: Double-stop legato is a specialty — Allman Brothers dual harmonies, Eric Clapton early blues runs, country chicken-pickin' slurs. You pick the double stop once and then hammer or pull to a new double stop without re-striking. The interaction requires that both fingers execute the hammer or pull simultaneously, with equal force, or one note sounds stronger than the other and the harmony collapses.
steps: Pick an opening double stop (e.g., third + fifth), then hammer both fretting fingers forward to the next chord-tone pair (fourth + sixth, say).
The hammering fingers must land at exactly the same moment with the same force — practice slowly to feel the synchronization.
Pull-offs work the same way in reverse: flick both fingers sideways at the same instant.
Keep the picking hand silent during the legato — the sound is entirely fretting-hand generated.
Alternate between hammer-on double stops and pull-off double stops within the same phrase to build both directions.
examples: (picked third + fifth) → hammer → (fourth + sixth) → pull → (third + fifth)
(picked root + third) → hammer → (second + fourth) → hammer → (third + fifth)
texture:doubleStops_noteTransition:hamme.watchOut: Unequal hammer force is the killer — one note sounds clear, the other sounds ghost-soft. If this happens, check which finger is weaker (often the pinky or ring) and practice it with a single-note hammer first before adding the partner.

texture:doubleStops_noteTransition:slide.interaction: Double stops with slide connections is the gliding parallel harmony of country steel guitar, Duane Allman's slide work, and Ry Cooder's desert tones — two notes moving together, linked by a single unified slide. The interaction requires that both fretting fingers slide as a rigid unit, maintaining the interval, without one finger drifting.
steps: Set up a parallel double-stop shape (sixths work beautifully) and plant both fingers.
Slide the whole hand as one unit — both fingers move together, the interval between them stays locked.
Pick the double stop once, then slide to the next position without re-picking.
Keep pressure even on both strings throughout the slide — if you favor one finger, the other goes silent.
Try slides both up and down the neck — each direction has a different feel, and the hand has to know both.
examples: (picked sixth + root) → slide up two frets → (seventh + second) — parallel ascending sixths
(picked third + fifth) → slow slide up a whole step → (fourth + sixth) — unified glide
texture:doubleStops_noteTransition:slide.watchOut: The number-one slide-double-stop failure is one finger lagging, which bends the interval as you slide. Press both strings with the same fingertip pressure and let the whole hand do the moving — treat the shape as a rigid object.

texture:doubleStops_noteTransition:bendT.interaction: Bending into a double-stop target is one of the most expressive moves in rock and country guitar — B.B. King's 'Lucille' moments, the Allman Brothers' 'Statesboro Blues' cries. You bend one string into pitch while the other string stays stationary, and the harmony is born out of the bend itself. The interaction trains controlled finger independence: one finger bends, the other holds.
steps: Set up a double stop where one note is a half or whole step below its target (typically the treble note).
Fret both notes and strike them together — then bend ONLY the target string up to pitch while the other string stays at its original fret.
The non-bending finger needs to stay planted and pressure-locked — any movement and the whole shape drifts.
Hold the bent target in tune for a full beat — the payoff is the sustained harmony.
Practice bending the top note up while the bottom note holds, then swap: bottom bends, top holds.
examples: (start: second + fourth) → bend second up a whole step → (target: third + fourth, sustained harmony)
(start: fourth + sixth) → bend fourth up a whole step → (target: fifth + sixth)
texture:doubleStops_noteTransition:bendT.watchOut: The stationary finger gets pulled by the bending finger unless you actively anchor it. Practice with conscious finger independence — consciously resist the urge of the second finger to follow the bend.

texture:triadPartial_noteTransition:clea.interaction: Triad partials with clean attack is the funk-comping foundation — every three-string voicing struck cleanly with no ringing into the next one. Think Nile Rodgers, Jimmy Nolen — crisp staccato partials that define the groove entirely through rhythm and attack precision. The interaction demands total fretting-hand release discipline: the partial dies the instant you lift, and the next one is a fresh event.
steps: Use a top-three-string triad partial and strike all three strings simultaneously with a sharp downstroke or upstroke.
Release the fretting fingers (not lift entirely, just relax the pressure) between each strike to cut the voicing cold.
Use palm-on-bridge muting lightly so the strings die short when you release the fretting pressure.
Keep the strum path tight — a small wrist flick, not a big arm swing — so each strike is surgical.
Alternate the clean strike with brief silences; the gaps are as important as the hits.
examples: (clean triad partial strike, down) → rest → (same partial, up) → rest — funk comping
(triad partial chord on beat one, clean cut) → (same chord on the 'and of two,' clean cut)
texture:triadPartial_noteTransition:clea.watchOut: Clean-attack triad partials blur when the fretting hand stays pressed while the picking hand strums the next hit — the previous voicing bleeds in. Release the fretting fingers fractionally between every strike.

texture:triadPartial_noteTransition:hamm.interaction: Triad partial legato is a Jerry Garcia, Grant Green, or Derek Trucks move — pick one string of the partial, then hammer or pull to a new partial shape, letting the chord change without re-strumming. The interaction is delicate: not every note of the partial can legato at once, so usually one voice moves via legato while the others stay planted. It is closer to voice-leading than strumming.
steps: Set up a top-three-string triad partial and identify which single note you will move via hammer or pull.
Strum the whole partial, then execute the hammer or pull on just that one string while the other two stay held.
The voice that moves creates an inner-voice melody inside the partial — it is harmonically sophisticated movement.
Let the two stationary notes sustain through the legato — the motion is on only one voice.
Practice moving a different voice each pass: the top, the middle, the bottom. Each creates a different harmonic character.
examples: (strum triad partial: root + third + fifth) → hammer the third up to the fourth → (new partial: root + fourth + fifth)
(strum partial: fifth + root + third) → pull the top third down to the second → (new partial: fifth + root + second)
texture:triadPartial_noteTransition:hamm.watchOut: The stationary voices go silent if the fretting-hand pressure shifts when the moving finger hammers. Keep the other fingers totally planted — only the one doing the legato moves.

texture:triadPartial_noteTransition:slid.interaction: Triad partial slides is the glue of reggae, jazz comping, and psychedelic guitar — Bob Marley sliding partials up and down the neck, Grant Green gliding comp voicings. The interaction is that the partial shape stays rigid and the whole hand slides as a unit, preserving the voicing while translating it to a new fret.
steps: Fret a top-three-string triad partial and plan the destination fret before striking.
Strum the partial once, then slide the whole hand as a locked shape to the target fret.
Do NOT re-strum after the slide — the slide carries the sustain.
Keep even pressure on all three strings through the slide; if any finger lightens, that note goes silent.
Alternate short slides (one or two frets) and long slides (four-plus frets) to feel how distance changes the character.
examples: (strum root-chord partial at fret five) → slide up two frets → (same partial shape as the five-chord voicing at fret seven)
(strum partial at fret seven) → slow slide up to fret ten → (new partial position, no re-strum)
texture:triadPartial_noteTransition:slid.watchOut: The slide fails if the hand releases fret pressure mid-glide — one or more notes go silent. Keep the fingers 'heavy' on the strings through the whole slide like you are dragging a weight.

texture:triadPartial_noteTransition:bend.interaction: Bending into a triad partial is an advanced expressive move — you set up the partial with one note slightly below its target and bend that single note into pitch while the other two voices hold. The interaction creates a shimmering tension-to-resolution effect: you hear the chord form as the bend lands. Think classic Hendrix 'Little Wing' voicings or jazzy soul comping.
steps: Fret a triad partial where one of the three notes is a half or whole step below its target scale tone.
Strike all three strings together — the partial sounds slightly 'wrong' or tense.
Bend the target note up to pitch while the other two notes stay locked and sustained.
Hold the bent partial at its resolved pitch for a full beat.
Release the bend slowly to restore the tense version — the round trip is the full expression.
examples: (triad partial: root + second + fifth, struck) → bend second up to third → (resolved partial: root + third + fifth)
(partial: root + third + fourth) → bend fourth up to fifth → (resolved partial: root + third + fifth)
texture:triadPartial_noteTransition:bend.watchOut: The stationary notes get dragged along by the bending finger unless you actively anchor them. Press the non-bending fingers harder than usual to keep them planted through the bend.

texture:fullChord_noteTransition:cleanAt.interaction: Full chord with clean attack is the sound of rhythm guitar with authority — the Who, Pete Townshend, early Beatles, AC/DC. Every strum is a complete, cleanly cut event; there is no bleed between chords. The interaction demands fretting-hand discipline on a bigger shape: all four-plus fingers have to release together, and the whole voicing has to sound fresh on every strike.
steps: Use a full barre or open-chord voicing and strike all strings with a firm downstroke.
Release fretting pressure briefly between strikes — not lifting, just unclamping — to kill the sustain and create a clean cut.
Use the heel of the picking hand on the bridge as a muting backstop for crisp cutoffs.
Alternate strum directions (down-up-down-up) with equal clarity on each stroke.
Check your fretting-hand thumb — clean attack is easier when the thumb stays relaxed; clench and the chord gets muddy.
examples: (root chord full strum, clean cut) → rest → (root chord, clean) — jagged eighth-note pulse
(four-chord strike, cut) → (root chord strike, cut) — chordal accents with total silence between
texture:fullChord_noteTransition:cleanAt.watchOut: Full-chord clean attack collapses when the fretting hand stays clamped between strikes — the whole voicing rings into the next strum. Learn to release pressure between hits so each strum stands alone.

texture:fullChord_noteTransition:hammerP.interaction: Full-chord hammer-pull is the sound of inner-voice movement within a sustained chord — Hendrix 'Little Wing,' James Taylor fingerpicking, solo acoustic guitar where a single voice moves while the chord underneath stays planted. The interaction is that you cannot hammer the whole chord at once; one finger moves while the rest hold.
steps: Fret a full chord voicing and identify which single fretted note you can hammer or pull without disturbing the rest.
Strum the chord once, then execute the hammer or pull on that one note while every other finger stays planted.
The ringing chord supports the legato motion — the inner voice becomes the melody inside the chord.
Keep the fretting-hand thumb relaxed; tension in the thumb will pull the rest of the chord out of tune.
Practice hammering the top voice (melody), then the middle, then the bottom (bass) — each creates a different character.
examples: (strum root chord) → hammer the top note up from the third to the fourth → (root chord with suspended fourth)
(strum four-chord) → pull the top voice from the second down to the root → (four-chord with root on top)
texture:fullChord_noteTransition:hammerP.watchOut: When you hammer one finger, the others often twitch and the rest of the chord goes silent. Anchor the stationary fingers deliberately — practice without the legato hit first to feel where the anchor tension lives.

texture:fullChord_noteTransition:slideCo.interaction: Sliding full chords is the staple of blues, country, and rock rhythm playing — you fret a barre or movable voicing and slide the whole shape to the next chord without lifting. The interaction is that the fretting hand moves as one rigid unit; lifting breaks the sustain, so the slide itself carries the harmonic motion.
steps: Fret a movable barre-chord voicing — the kind with no open strings — so the shape can travel.
Strum the chord and immediately slide the entire hand to the destination fret without lifting any finger.
Maintain pressure on all strings through the slide; losing pressure kills the chord mid-glide.
Decide whether to re-strum after the slide (a punchier transition) or let the slide carry the sound (a smoother transition).
Try short slides (one fret) and long slides (three-plus frets); long slides are inherently more dramatic.
examples: (strum root-chord barre at fret five) → slide up to fret seven → (five-chord barre, no re-strum)
(strum barre at fret three) → slow slide down to fret one → (new barre voicing of the flat-seventh chord)
texture:fullChord_noteTransition:slideCo.watchOut: The full-chord slide is the fastest way to cramp the fretting hand because you have four-plus fingers pressing during the glide. If you feel tension, reduce your baseline fret pressure — slides work with less grip than you think.

texture:fullChord_noteTransition:bendTar.interaction: Bending into a chord target is rare and striking — Hendrix used it, Stevie Ray Vaughan used it, some country chicken-pickers do it. You fret a full-chord voicing with one note slightly below pitch, strike the chord, and bend that single note into its target to 'complete' the chord. The interaction creates a dramatic resolution effect: the listener hears the chord snap into place.
steps: Fret a chord voicing where ONE of the fretted notes (typically the top voice) is a whole step below its target.
Strum the full chord — it will sound tense because one note is not yet home.
Bend the target string up to pitch while all other fingers hold their chord notes planted.
Hold the resolved chord for a full beat — the bend IS the chord change.
Release slowly to return the chord to its tense state, creating a round-trip expression.
examples: (fret a root chord with the top voice on the second) → bend the second up to the third → (root chord resolved)
(fret a four-chord with the top voice on the fifth below target) → bend up to the sixth → (six-chord added)
texture:fullChord_noteTransition:bendTar.watchOut: The stationary fingers lose their grip when the bending finger pulls them out of position — the whole chord goes dead. Anchor the non-bending fingers harder than usual and keep the thumb relaxed so the bend is isolated.

texture:mixed_noteTransition:cleanAttack.interaction: Mixed-texture clean attack is the Nile Rodgers / James Brown guitar discipline — every chord stab and every single-note fill hits cleanly with zero bleed between events. The interaction is doubly demanding: you have to release cleanly when switching between chord and line, and the single-note fills themselves have to be individually articulated, no legato shortcuts.
steps: Alternate a chord stab and a single-note fill within each bar — chord on beat one, fill in the back half.
Strike the chord with a sharp downstroke, then release fretting pressure completely before the fill starts.
Every note of the fill is individually picked with alternate picking — no hammers, no pull-offs, no slides.
Use palm-on-bridge muting throughout; it helps the clean attack cut sharp.
Practice very slowly first — the chord-to-fill transition is where articulation goes soft.
examples: (clean chord stab on beat one) → (picked fill: fifth → third → root, each note clean) → (clean chord stab on beat one of next bar)
(staccato chord jab) → (picked fill: seventh → root → second, alternate picked)
texture:mixed_noteTransition:cleanAttack.watchOut: The chord-to-single-note transition is where bleeds happen — the chord still rings when you start the fill. Fully unclamp the fretting hand between chord and fill so the texture switch is crisp.

texture:mixed_noteTransition:hammerPull.interaction: Mixed-texture legato is a fluid rhythm-guitar move — chord stabs alternate with legato single-note flourishes that weave in and out of the chord voicings. Think Jerry Garcia rhythm work, or a soul guitarist tagging a chord with a quick hammer-pull flourish. The interaction is that the fretting hand is doing two jobs: holding chords cleanly, then executing legato precisely in the fill.
steps: Stab a full chord, then execute a short legato fill (hammer-on, pull-off, hammer-on) on the top string using chord tones.
The chord stab is picked; the fill begins with ONE pick stroke and uses hammers and pulls for the rest.
Keep the fretting-hand shape flexible — it has to transition from the chord grip to the single-finger legato position quickly.
The legato flourish should feel like a comma at the end of the phrase, not a new statement.
Return to the chord stab to start the next phrase — the contrast between chordal weight and legato lightness is the point.
examples: (chord stab) → (picked fifth, hammer → seventh, pull → fifth, hammer → sixth, tagging the chord)
(chord stab) → (picked third, pull → second, hammer → third, pull → root, flourish before the next stab)
texture:mixed_noteTransition:hammerPull.watchOut: Players often pick the legato notes out of habit. Use strict self-discipline: ONE pick stroke starts the fill, then everything is fretting-hand only. If you pick the hammered note, you are cheating the legato.

texture:mixed_noteTransition:slideConnec.interaction: Mixed-texture slides is the desert-blues, Tinariwen, surf-rock guitar vocabulary — a chord stab followed by a sliding single-note line that glides out of the chord and back in. The interaction is that the slides carry the melodic motion while the chord stabs provide the rhythmic anchor; the whole thing breathes as a gliding phrase punctuated by hits.
steps: Stab a full chord or triad partial on beat one.
Follow with a single-note line where each transition is a slide — no picked re-attacks during the line.
Pick the first note of the fill, then slide to every subsequent note without re-picking.
Keep even fret pressure through all slides — losing pressure silences the line.
Return to a chord stab to close the phrase; the chord is the anchor, the slide is the wind.
examples: (chord stab) → (picked third, slide up → fifth, slide up → sixth, slide up → root up an octave) — sliding fill
(chord stab) → (picked seventh, slide down → fifth, slide up → sixth, slide down → third)
texture:mixed_noteTransition:slideConnec.watchOut: Players usually pick every note of the sliding fill out of muscle memory. Force yourself to pick only the first note and let the slides do the rest — anything else dilutes the character.

texture:mixed_noteTransition:bendTarget.interaction: Mixed-texture bend-target is a rock-soul expressive vocabulary — chord stabs anchor the rhythm, then a single-note fill ends with a bend into a target pitch as the expressive payoff. Think Jimi Hendrix 'Bold as Love,' Stevie Wonder's guitarists, or any soul rhythm player who tags phrases with a bent cry. The interaction is that the bend has to arrive exactly on the beat when the next chord stab would land, so the bend REPLACES the downbeat.
steps: Stab a full chord on beat one.
Pick a short single-note line in the back half of the bar that ends on a bent target pitch.
Time the bend so its peak (the target pitch) lands exactly on beat one of the next bar — the bend is the new downbeat.
Hold the bent target through the start of the next bar, then release slowly as the next chord stab comes.
The contrast between the rhythmic chord and the expressive bend is the entire point; overdo neither.
examples: (chord stab) → (picked fifth → fourth → third) → bend the third up to the fourth (landing on beat one of next bar)
(chord stab) → (picked second → root → seventh) → bend the seventh up to the root (arriving on downbeat)
texture:mixed_noteTransition:bendTarget.watchOut: The bend must hit the target on the beat — not before, not after. Players often bend too slowly and the target arrives late; practice with a metronome so the bend's peak lands precisely on the click.

texture:singleLine_vibrato:straight.interaction: Single-line with straight (no vibrato) is a study in pure pitch — every note sustained with zero movement, which forces you to hear intonation nakedly. The interaction is revealing: without vibrato to hide small imperfections, buzzes, choked notes, and pitch inconsistencies all become audible. It is the ear training you did not know you needed.
steps: Play a slow single-line melody letting each note sustain its full value — no movement from the fretting hand during sustain.
Fret with steady, even pressure — any shift will show up as pitch drift.
Listen for each note's pitch stability; if a note seems to waver, the fretting hand tensed mid-sustain.
Use the picking hand to create the dynamic — soft notes, loud notes — but never use vibrato to 'save' a note.
End phrases on long held straight notes — landing on the root or fifth with zero movement is the discipline.
examples: (long sustained third, no movement) → (long sustained fifth, no movement) → (long sustained root)
(held seventh) → (held root, sustained through two bars, perfectly still)
texture:singleLine_vibrato:straight.watchOut: The reflex to add vibrato on any long note is deep — you will catch yourself doing it unconsciously. Practice with a drone so you can hear any wavering, and train the fretting hand to stay absolutely still during sustain.

texture:singleLine_vibrato:slow.interaction: Single-line with slow vibrato is the voice of B.B. King, David Gilmour, Carlos Santana — wide, deliberate oscillation that turns each sustained note into a vocal cry. The interaction is that slow vibrato is about arm and wrist motion, not finger motion, and its width and speed have to be rhythmically intentional, not just a twitch.
steps: Play a single-note phrase with long sustained notes (two beats or more per note).
On each sustained note, add a slow vibrato at two to three cycles per second — roughly eighth-note pulses at 90 BPM.
The motion comes from the wrist and forearm, not the finger tip — rock the whole hand.
Start the vibrato immediately on the note attack; it should feel like the note was born oscillating.
Make the vibrato wide — up to a quarter step in each direction — so it reads as emotion, not decoration.
examples: (long sustained third with slow wide vibrato from the attack) → (long sustained fifth, same vibrato)
(sustained root up an octave, slow vibrato, wrist-driven) → (same note, same motion, a beat later)
texture:singleLine_vibrato:slow.watchOut: Slow vibrato from the fingertip alone is shallow and nervous — it reads as uncertainty. Use the whole hand-wrist-forearm system so the oscillation has body and weight.

texture:singleLine_vibrato:fast.interaction: Single-line with fast vibrato is the classical and flamenco shimmer — narrow, rapid oscillation at five to seven cycles per second that adds brightness and intensity without changing the note's identity. Think classical nylon-string or Paco de Lucía. The interaction is that fast vibrato is a fingertip motion, not a wrist motion; the finger rolls rapidly on the string.
steps: Play a single-note phrase and on every sustained note add a quick, narrow vibrato.
The motion is in the fingertip only: roll the finger back and forth rapidly without moving the wrist or forearm.
Keep the vibrato narrow — less than a quarter step of pitch motion — so it shimmers rather than wails.
Start the vibrato immediately and keep it steady through the full sustain.
Try it on every note of a simple melody — fast vibrato on every note is a classical / flamenco default.
examples: (held fifth with fast shimmering vibrato) → (held sixth, same shimmer) → (held seventh, same)
(sustained root, fast vibrato from attack) → slide → (sustained third, same fast vibrato)
texture:singleLine_vibrato:fast.watchOut: Fast vibrato from the whole wrist becomes uneven and wobbly — the motion has to be isolated to the fingertip. If the vibrato feels ragged, lock the wrist and let only the finger roll.

texture:singleLine_vibrato:lateEntry.interaction: Single-line with late-entry vibrato is the subtlest and most singerly technique — each note starts pure and straight, and the vibrato blooms in after a beat. This mimics how a great vocalist sustains a note: first the tone, then the emotion. Think Nat King Cole or a lyrical tenor saxophonist. The interaction is pure control: you hold the note still for exactly the right amount of time before letting the vibrato in.
steps: Play a single-note phrase with long sustained notes.
On each note, hold the pitch perfectly straight for the first half of the sustain — zero movement.
At the midpoint of the sustain, begin a slow vibrato that grows in width as the note continues.
The vibrato should start small and bloom gradually — it is a gesture of surrender, not a switch.
End the note at full vibrato width, then release cleanly.
examples: (sustained root: still for one beat, then slow vibrato blooming for the second beat)
(sustained fifth: held straight for the first half, vibrato growing in the second half, released wide)
texture:singleLine_vibrato:lateEntry.watchOut: The hardest part is holding the note perfectly still before the vibrato starts — the hand wants to oscillate immediately. Use the first half-beat as a discipline exercise: zero motion, then bloom.

texture:doubleStops_vibrato:straight.interaction: Double stops with straight (no vibrato) is the Chuck Berry, Rolling Stones rhythm discipline — two notes held perfectly still, and the harmony between them has to speak cleanly without movement to 'decorate' it. The interaction is that straight double stops expose intonation problems: if both notes are not perfectly fretted, the interval wobbles and the harmony sours.
steps: Fret a double stop (third + fifth, or root + fourth) and let it ring without any hand movement.
Pick both strings together and sustain the pair for at least two beats.
Focus on even pressure on both strings — unequal fret pressure pulls one note out of tune.
Listen to the interval carefully; the harmony either rings pure or it fights — there is no middle ground.
Alternate between different double-stop intervals (thirds, sixths, octaves) and feel how each demands its own pressure balance.
examples: (held third + fifth, zero vibrato, sustained two beats)
(held sixth + root, perfectly still, sustained two beats)
texture:doubleStops_vibrato:straight.watchOut: Straight double stops go sour the instant one finger presses harder than the other — the intervals bend unequally. Practice with the fretting hand relaxed so pressure is even across both strings.

texture:doubleStops_vibrato:slow.interaction: Double stops with slow vibrato is the country steel-guitar and classic rock vocabulary — both notes oscillating together in wide, slow motion, creating a harmonized cry. Think Duane Allman slide work or a pedal-steel-style guitar lick. The interaction is that both strings must vibrate IN SYNC; otherwise the harmony wobbles and the intervals go in and out of tune.
steps: Fret a double stop and strike both strings.
Add a slow, wide vibrato using the whole wrist — both strings oscillate together as the hand rocks.
Maintain even pressure on both strings throughout the vibrato so the interval stays locked.
Keep the vibrato rate steady (two to three cycles per second) — uneven vibrato turns the harmony ragged.
End the double stop at the peak of a vibrato cycle, then release cleanly.
examples: (double stop: third + fifth, slow wrist vibrato, two beats)
(double stop: sixth + root, wide slow vibrato, two beats, wrist-driven)
texture:doubleStops_vibrato:slow.watchOut: If the two strings vibrato at different rates, the interval smears — it is the classic failure mode of double-stop vibrato. Treat the fretting hand as a rigid unit and rock the whole system together.

texture:doubleStops_vibrato:fast.interaction: Double stops with fast vibrato is the classical and flamenco technique — narrow, rapid oscillation on both strings simultaneously for a shimmering harmony. Think classical guitar duets, or the ornamented double stops of a Paco de Lucía piece. The interaction is that fast vibrato is fingertip-based, so both fingers have to roll rapidly at the same rate — which is genuinely hard.
steps: Fret a double stop and strike both strings.
Add a narrow, fast vibrato to both strings — fingertip-driven, not wrist-driven.
Both fingers must roll at the same rate; if one is faster, the interval fractures.
Keep the vibrato narrow (less than a quarter step) so it shimmers without wavering the pitch identity.
Practice slowly first — matched fingertip vibrato on two fingers is unusual and requires conscious coordination.
examples: (double stop: fifth + seventh with fast narrow vibrato, shimmer for two beats)
(double stop: third + fifth, fast fingertip vibrato, both fingers matched)
texture:doubleStops_vibrato:fast.watchOut: Usually one finger has more developed vibrato than the other (typically the ring or middle). Train the weaker finger in isolation before trying to match them on a double stop.

texture:doubleStops_vibrato:lateEntry.interaction: Double stops with late-entry vibrato is the most expressive and controlled version of harmonized singing — both notes sustain pure and still, then bloom into vibrato together. This is cinematic guitar, pedal-steel emotion, or the most lyrical classical double stops. The interaction is advanced: you are holding two notes perfectly still, then introducing unison vibrato at a precise moment.
steps: Fret a double stop and strike both strings.
Hold both notes perfectly straight for the first half of the sustain — no hand motion whatsoever.
At the midpoint, begin a slow unison vibrato that grows in width as it develops.
Both fingers must start the vibrato at the same instant and rock at the same rate.
End the double stop at full vibrato and release together cleanly.
examples: (double stop: sixth + root, held straight for one beat, then blooming slow vibrato for the second beat)
(double stop: third + fifth, pure for the first half, vibrato growing wide in the second half)
texture:doubleStops_vibrato:lateEntry.watchOut: One finger starting vibrato a beat before the other ruins the effect — the entrance has to be perfectly simultaneous. Set a mental cue (like a breath) that triggers both fingers at once.

texture:triadPartial_vibrato:straight.interaction: Triad partials with straight (no vibrato) is the crystal-clean funk and reggae comping sound — Nile Rodgers, Jimmy Nolen, early Bob Marley. Three notes held perfectly still, the voicing speaking on its own. The interaction reveals how much clarity a triad partial can produce when nothing moves: the harmonic identity rings pure.
steps: Fret a top-three-string triad partial and strum once.
Let the three notes ring with zero fretting-hand movement — the partial should sound like a bell.
Listen for balance between the three voices; if any voice dominates or fades, adjust pressure on that finger.
Hold the partial for a full bar without any motion.
Alternate between different partial voicings (different inversions), each held straight, to feel how each voicing wants its own balance.
examples: (triad partial: root + third + fifth, held still, sustained four beats)
(partial: third + fifth + root up an octave, sustained perfectly still)
texture:triadPartial_vibrato:straight.watchOut: Straight triad partials expose unequal pressure — one voice always wants to be louder or quieter. Practice with a drone to balance the three voices until they sing as equal partners.

texture:triadPartial_vibrato:slow.interaction: Triad partials with slow vibrato is the soulful jazz-guitar voicing — Grant Green, George Benson, Kenny Burrell sustaining a three-note voicing with wide, deliberate vibrato. The interaction is that all three strings must vibrate together in unison; unlike double stops, now three voices have to move as one.
steps: Fret a top-three-string triad partial and strum once.
Add a slow, wide vibrato using the whole wrist — all three fingers rocking together.
Pressure must be even across all three strings; any imbalance means the partial goes out of tune in vibrato.
Maintain the vibrato rate at two to three cycles per second for four full beats.
Release the partial at the top of a vibrato cycle with all three fingers lifting together.
examples: (triad partial with slow wrist vibrato sustained four beats) — jazz comping style
(partial struck and vibrated immediately from the attack, wide and slow)
texture:triadPartial_vibrato:slow.watchOut: Triad partial vibrato often has one finger lagging — that one note goes sharp while the others stay in tune. Treat the whole fretting hand as a locked unit and let the wrist do the work.

texture:triadPartial_vibrato:fast.interaction: Triad partials with fast vibrato is a specialty classical or Latin technique — shimmering three-note voicings with rapid narrow oscillation. Think of classical trio guitar or ornamented Spanish guitar chords. The interaction is extreme coordination: three fingertips rolling at the same rapid rate is one of the hardest things to do on guitar.
steps: Fret a top-three-string triad partial and strum once.
Add a fast, narrow vibrato using fingertip motion only — the wrist stays locked.
All three fingers must roll at the same rate; any mismatch fractures the harmony.
Keep vibrato narrow (less than a quarter step) so the pitch identity stays pure.
Practice each finger alone first; matched three-finger fast vibrato is the advanced version.
examples: (triad partial with fast narrow fingertip vibrato, shimmer for four beats)
(partial sustained with rapid matched vibrato, all three voices shimmering together)
texture:triadPartial_vibrato:fast.watchOut: Three-finger fast vibrato is extremely rare because it is so hard — most players fake it with wrist vibrato instead. If you can only get two fingers matched, that is still musical; push to three only when those two are rock solid.

texture:triadPartial_vibrato:lateEntry.interaction: Triad partials with late-entry vibrato is the most controlled expressive voicing move — three voices sustaining pure and still, then blooming into unison vibrato. This is advanced jazz-comping vocabulary, or cinematic chord work where patience is the point. The interaction is that all three fingers must enter vibrato at the same instant after a shared moment of stillness.
steps: Fret a triad partial and strum once.
Hold all three voices perfectly straight for the first half of the sustain — total stillness.
At the midpoint, introduce unison vibrato that grows in width.
All three fingers must begin vibrato together — use a mental or breath cue to trigger the entrance.
End the partial at full vibrato width, releasing all three fingers together.
examples: (triad partial held straight for two beats, then all three voices vibrato blooming wide for two beats)
(partial perfectly still for the first half, then all three entering slow vibrato in unison)
texture:triadPartial_vibrato:lateEntry.watchOut: Triad partial late-entry requires holding three notes perfectly still first — which is harder than holding one still. Any finger drift during the still phase ruins the effect. Practice the still phase first and add the vibrato later.

texture:fullChord_vibrato:straight.interaction: Full chords with straight (no vibrato) is the foundation of rhythm guitar accuracy — barre chords or open voicings held completely still, sustained with full ringing clarity. The interaction is brutal: straight full chords expose every imperfect fret, every cramped finger, every unintended string buzz. This is the honest mirror of your chord technique.
steps: Fret a full-chord voicing and strum all six strings (or all intended strings) cleanly.
Let the chord ring its full value without any fretting-hand motion.
Listen to every string — any buzz, any mute, any out-of-tune note reveals a technique gap.
Alternate between chords and let each one ring straight before moving to the next.
End each chord with a clean cut — lift all fingers simultaneously.
examples: (strum root chord full voicing, sustained straight for four beats)
(strum barre four-chord, sustained straight, every string speaking equally)
texture:fullChord_vibrato:straight.watchOut: Straight full chords reveal every weak spot in your chord grip. If a note buzzes, it is not the chord shape's fault — it is your fret pressure or finger arch. Diagnose the weak string and fix the finger angle.

texture:fullChord_vibrato:slow.interaction: Full chords with slow vibrato is the classical guitar and Spanish flamenco rasgueado aesthetic — a full chord sustained with slow unison movement across all strings, creating a singing choir effect. The interaction demands a rigid wrist-driven motion because all four-plus fingers have to oscillate together; any finger moving independently ruins the unison.
steps: Fret a full-chord voicing and strum once.
Add slow vibrato using wrist-and-forearm motion only — the whole hand rocks as a locked unit.
Every fretted finger oscillates at the same rate because they are all rigidly part of the hand shape.
Keep the vibrato slow (two to three cycles per second) and wide for full emotional effect.
Sustain for four beats and release cleanly at a cycle peak.
examples: (root chord with slow wide wrist-driven vibrato, sustained four beats)
(minor four-chord with slow vibrato, cry-like and deliberate)
texture:fullChord_vibrato:slow.watchOut: Finger-level vibrato will never work on a full chord — you have to lock the hand and rock from the wrist. If you feel individual fingers moving, stop and reset the grip.

texture:fullChord_vibrato:fast.interaction: Full chords with fast vibrato is extremely specialized — classical flamenco rasgueado or rapid tremolo chord work where the whole hand shimmers at high speed. The interaction is that fast vibrato on a full chord is physically demanding and rare in most genres, but when executed cleanly it creates an intense shimmering harmonic cloud.
steps: Fret a full-chord voicing and strum once.
Add a rapid vibrato using tight wrist motion — the whole hand oscillates quickly.
Keep the oscillation narrow (less than a quarter step) so the chord identity stays clear.
Sustain for two beats with rapid shimmer and release cleanly.
This technique is tiring — do it in short bursts rather than long sustains.
examples: (full root chord with rapid narrow wrist vibrato, sustained two beats)
(four-chord with fast tight shimmer, two-beat burst)
texture:fullChord_vibrato:fast.watchOut: Fast full-chord vibrato causes wrist fatigue faster than any other vibrato type. Do it briefly — two beats max — and rest the wrist between attempts. Pushing through fatigue injures the wrist.

texture:fullChord_vibrato:lateEntry.interaction: Full chords with late-entry vibrato is the cinematic grand gesture — a full chord sustained perfectly still, then blooming into vibrato after a beat of total stillness. This is the orchestral guitar move, the singer-songwriter signature held chord, the moment of emotional release. The interaction is pure control: hold the chord silent-still, then release into motion at the precise right moment.
steps: Strum a full chord and let it sustain completely still for the first half of its value.
At the midpoint, begin slow unison vibrato from the wrist.
The vibrato should bloom gradually — start narrow and widen into full expression.
End the chord at the peak of a vibrato cycle with all fingers lifting together.
Use a mental breath-cue to trigger the vibrato entrance so it feels inevitable, not arbitrary.
examples: (root chord held straight for two beats, then wrist vibrato blooming wide for the next two beats)
(four-chord still for the first half, vibrato growing for the second half, cinematic)
texture:fullChord_vibrato:lateEntry.watchOut: The still phase is where most players fail — they start vibrato immediately out of habit. Count beats consciously during the still phase and commit to zero motion until the trigger moment.

texture:mixed_vibrato:straight.interaction: Mixed textures with straight vibrato is the Nile Rodgers / Jimmy Nolen funk discipline — chord stabs punched clean, single-note fills sustained pure, no vibrato anywhere. The interaction is that the lack of vibrato becomes the sound: it creates tight, crisp, almost percussive guitar where every note is a pin-sharp event.
steps: Alternate chord stabs and single-note fills within each bar.
Every note — chord and single — is held perfectly still. Zero vibrato, zero wavering.
The chord stab is a short, clean strike; the single-note fill is precisely articulated with no motion on sustains.
Use palm muting on the chord stabs to make them percussive.
Sustain any fill note for its full value with the fretting hand absolutely still.
examples: (staccato chord stab) → (picked fill: fifth → third → root, each note held perfectly still)
(clean chord punch) → (fill: seventh → root → second, each sustained straight)
texture:mixed_vibrato:straight.watchOut: The reflex to add vibrato on the sustained notes of the fill is strong. Resist — the no-vibrato discipline is what makes mixed-texture funk sound tight and alive.

texture:mixed_vibrato:slow.interaction: Mixed textures with slow vibrato is the soul-blues rhythm player's vocabulary — punchy chord stabs followed by sustained fill notes that cry with wide, slow vibrato. Think Curtis Mayfield's lyrical fills, or a Hendrix rhythm phrase where a chord stab is tagged with a bent vibrato'd single note. The interaction is that the chord stabs are tight and rhythmic while the fill notes bloom with expressive vibrato — two opposite characters in one phrase.
steps: Stab a full chord — tight, clean, palm-muted.
Follow with a single-note fill ending on a sustained note with slow wide vibrato.
The chord stab has no vibrato; the fill sustain has full vibrato. Two opposite sonic characters in one phrase.
Use the wrist for the fill's vibrato — wide, deliberate, B.B. King style.
Return to a chord stab for the next phrase, creating a rhythmic-then-expressive alternation.
examples: (chord stab) → (fill: fifth → third → sustained root with slow wide vibrato)
(chord stab) → (fill: seventh → root → sustained third with wrist-driven vibrato)
texture:mixed_vibrato:slow.watchOut: Bringing any vibrato into the chord stab collapses the contrast and the phrase loses its character. Keep the stab ruthlessly still; let the fill have all the vibrato.

texture:mixed_vibrato:fast.interaction: Mixed textures with fast vibrato is a rarer voice — clean chord stabs followed by fills that shimmer with rapid narrow vibrato. Think classical-influenced Latin rock or the ornamented fills of a flamenco-fusion guitarist. The interaction is surprising: fast vibrato is so intense that even a single note of it feels like a climax compared to the rhythmic chord stabs.
steps: Stab a full chord with tight staccato articulation.
Follow with a short single-note fill ending on a sustained note with fast narrow vibrato.
The fast vibrato uses fingertip motion only — the wrist stays locked.
Keep the vibrato narrow (less than a quarter step) so the pitch identity remains clear.
Return to the chord stab for the next phrase — the contrast is the entire point.
examples: (chord stab) → (fill: fifth → sixth → sustained seventh with fast narrow vibrato)
(chord stab) → (fill: third → fourth → sustained fifth with shimmering fingertip vibrato)
texture:mixed_vibrato:fast.watchOut: Fast vibrato is tiring and easily becomes shaky. Keep the fill short so the sustained vibrato note is fresh and clean — long phrases with fast vibrato fall apart quickly.

texture:mixed_vibrato:lateEntry.interaction: Mixed textures with late-entry vibrato is the most cinematic rhythm-guitar move — chord stabs are tight and clean, the single-note fill sustains briefly pure, then vibrato blooms in at the end for the expressive payoff. This is the guitar version of a vocalist delivering a phrase: start pure, end expressive. The interaction requires timing discipline: the vibrato must enter at the precise right moment.
steps: Stab a full chord cleanly — no vibrato, tight articulation.
Follow with a single-note fill ending on a sustained target note.
The sustained target starts perfectly still for the first beat of sustain.
At the midpoint of that note's sustain, bloom slow vibrato from the wrist.
Let the note finish at full vibrato width, then cut to the next chord stab for the next phrase.
examples: (chord stab) → (fill: third → fifth → sustained root: still for one beat, then vibrato blooming for the second beat)
(chord stab) → (fill: seventh → second → sustained third: held straight then vibrato growing at the midpoint)
texture:mixed_vibrato:lateEntry.watchOut: The temptation is to start vibrato immediately when the sustained note begins — which defeats the whole technique. Count beats mentally during the still phase and commit to zero motion until the bloom point.

pitchConstraint:leaps_vibrato:straight.interaction: Leaps want drama and surprise, but stripping vibrato out forces you to land every skip with dead-centered intonation — nowhere to hide behind a shimmer. The combination sounds like early Wes Montgomery octave work or a Bach cello suite on a fretted instrument: every interval reads as architecture, not emotion. Because vibrato usually masks micro-bending on arrivals, removing it turns each leap into an intonation test in disguise.
steps: Set the drone quietly and play the root, the third, and the fifth as pure held tones so your ear locks to center pitch.
Improvise phrases where every move skips at least one scale degree, holding each landing note still for a full beat before the next leap.
Listen for beating against the drone on every arrival — if the note wobbles, you are leaning on unconscious vibrato to cover a missed center.
Finish each phrase on the root or the fifth and let it ring dead-straight for two beats before the next attempt.
Check your fretting-hand thumb: a clamped thumb creates micro-vibrato you cannot hear but the drone reveals.
examples: root → fifth → third → seventh → root up an octave (held dead-straight on every landing)
third → sixth → second → fifth → root (each leap parked for a full beat, zero hand motion)
pitchConstraint:leaps_vibrato:straight.watchOut: The fingers will sneak in a tiny shimmer on arrival notes out of muscle habit, especially after a wide skip where the hand wants to 'settle in.' If you feel your fretting finger rocking at all during sustain, you are cheating — freeze it like you are pressing a photograph.
pitchConstraint:leaps_vibrato:straight.deeperInsight: Removing vibrato from leaps is the fastest diagnostic for whether you actually hear the interval or just feel it — your ear will tell you before your brain does.

pitchConstraint:leaps_vibrato:slow.interaction: Leaps and slow vibrato are the classic blues vocabulary — think B.B. King stabbing up to the minor third and making it weep, or Gilmour walking between chord tones and letting each one breathe. The slow wide wobble gives each landing note its own identity and emotional weight, so the gaps between leaps stop feeling like mechanical jumps and start feeling like statements. The slow cycle also forces you to hold each arrival long enough to breathe.
steps: Pick a slow tempo so every leap gets 2-3 seconds of sustain to apply vibrato.
Play a phrase where every note is at least a skip away from the last, then immediately rock your fretting finger wide and slow — aim for 2-3 oscillations per second.
Make the vibrato start AFTER the note has spoken clearly for about a beat — never instantly, that kills the leap's impact.
Push the peak notes of each leap (especially the third, fifth, and flat-seventh) with the widest motion and let the other landings vibrate more subtly.
Release the vibrato before the next leap so each jump starts from a clean, still pitch.
examples: root → fifth → flat-third → root up an octave (slow wide vibrato on the fifth and the octave root)
second → fifth → seventh → third (vibrato parked on the seventh for a full bar, B.B.-style)
pitchConstraint:leaps_vibrato:slow.watchOut: The wide slow vibrato will drag your pitch flat on the downswing if you only bend UP from the note — make sure the finger passes through dead-center on every cycle, rocking around the pitch, not above it. Also beware of starting the vibrato too early and smearing the leap interval.
pitchConstraint:leaps_vibrato:slow.deeperInsight: In this pairing, the leaps do the intellect and the vibrato does the heart — one supplies the idea, the other supplies the feeling.

pitchConstraint:leaps_vibrato:fast.interaction: Fast vibrato on leaps is flamenco and classical territory — narrow, rapid shimmer that gives every landing note an urgent, vocal intensity. Unlike slow blues vibrato which takes its time, fast vibrato arrives almost immediately and makes the leaps feel like a bel canto singer leaping between registers with operatic fervor. The quick oscillation also hides tiny intonation slips on the arrival notes, so the ear reads the shape of the leap instead of auditing the exact landing pitch.
steps: Warm up the fretting hand with fast narrow shakes on a single fret — 5-7 cycles per second — before adding any leaps.
Play phrases where every interval is a skip, and the moment each note sounds, pulse it with quick narrow vibrato.
Keep the amplitude tight — a quarter tone or less — so the fast rate stays clean instead of blurring into warble.
Favor the sixth, second, and raised-seventh as landing targets; they shimmer most dramatically against a drone.
Every fourth phrase, end on the root and hold it completely straight — silence is the contrast that proves the vibrato was chosen, not habitual.
examples: root → third → sixth → second → fifth (narrow fast shimmer on every landing)
fourth → seventh → third → root up an octave (flamenco-style quick flutter on the octave arrival)
pitchConstraint:leaps_vibrato:fast.watchOut: Fast vibrato tightens the forearm if you drive it from the wrist — use the finger-pad rolling from the big knuckle, not a stiff-wrist shake, or you will cramp out of the round in three minutes. Also watch the amplitude: fast AND wide becomes seasick, not shimmering.
pitchConstraint:leaps_vibrato:fast.deeperInsight: Fast vibrato is less a color than a signal of emotional intensity — use it on peaks, not on every landing, or it stops reading as expression.

pitchConstraint:leaps_vibrato:lateEntry.interaction: Late-entry vibrato on leaps is the most sophisticated vibrato technique in the book — every leap lands pure, hangs in the air dead-straight for a moment, and only THEN blooms into motion. It's the sound of a singer like Ella Fitzgerald phrasing a ballad, or a classical violinist showing off their straight-tone control before letting a note flower. The combination teaches you to trust silence at the front of each sustain and makes every leap feel deliberate and considered.
steps: Play a leap and mentally count 'one-and' before starting any vibrato — the straight portion must be audibly present.
On the 'two' of each sustained note, begin a moderate vibrato and let it build in width as the note decays.
Keep the leaps jagged — every note must be at least a skip from the last, never a neighbor.
Notice the contrast between the pure front and the moving tail of each note; that contrast IS the expression.
End some phrases with a leap whose landing note stays straight with no vibrato at all — that creates the dramatic tension of a promise withheld.
examples: root → fifth → third → seventh (each note straight for a beat, then blooming into slow vibrato on the tail)
sixth → second → fourth → root up an octave (the octave root held pure for two beats before late shimmer)
pitchConstraint:leaps_vibrato:lateEntry.watchOut: The hardest part is not letting the fretting hand twitch into vibrato on impact out of habit — most players add vibrato within 200ms without knowing it. Record yourself and listen for the pure front; if you cannot hear a dead-straight moment on every landing, you are starting too early.
pitchConstraint:leaps_vibrato:lateEntry.deeperInsight: Late-entry vibrato is how you prove the leap's interval mattered musically — the pure moment says 'listen to this pitch,' and the vibrato says 'now feel it.'

pitchConstraint:stepwise_vibrato:straigh.interaction: Stepwise motion with no vibrato is a pure melody exam — you have nowhere to hide your intonation, no shimmer to disguise an out-of-tune neighbor note. It evokes early music, Gregorian chant on a fretted instrument, or the austere melodic lines of Arvo Pärt. Because every move is to an adjacent scale degree, any micro-bending from tension becomes obvious against the drone, and the straightness turns the piece into an intonation laboratory disguised as a pretty melody.
steps: Set a calm tempo and tune every fretting finger to land dead on its fret — no pulling or pushing across the string.
Play a melody that moves only to neighbor notes up or down the scale, one note per beat.
Hold each note for its full value with the fretting finger absolutely still — imagine you are pressing a button, not rocking a cradle.
Every four bars, rest on the root or the third and listen for beating against the drone; silence any wobble before the next phrase.
Try the same melody with your picking hand very close to the bridge, then near the neck — the tonal change is what vibrato usually provides; here it comes from pick placement instead.
examples: root → second → third → fourth → third → second → root (pure scalar, every note still)
fifth → fourth → third → second → root → second → third (descending then resolving, dead straight)
pitchConstraint:stepwise_vibrato:straigh.watchOut: Stepwise lines make the fretting hand relax — which is good — but a relaxed hand often drops into unintentional vibrato on the longer notes. If you cannot keep the fretting finger completely still for 2 full beats, your technique for straight tone is not ready yet. Fix it here before trying leaps.
pitchConstraint:stepwise_vibrato:straigh.deeperInsight: Straight stepwise playing is how classical guitarists test their left-hand hygiene — if a scalar passage cannot be held pure, no expressive ornament will sound clean on top of it.

pitchConstraint:stepwise_vibrato:slow.interaction: Stepwise with slow vibrato is a lullaby, a folk ballad, a Gregorian chant rendered on gut strings. The wide slow wobble turns each adjacent-note move into a tender sigh, and because the melody doesn't have the drama of leaps, the vibrato carries all the emotional weight. This is Willie Nelson phrasing a song, or a flamenco guitarist playing a cante jondo intro — melodic simplicity, expressive warmth.
steps: Play a descending scalar line from the fifth down to the root, one note per slow beat.
On each note, let the fretting finger rock wide and slow — 2-3 cycles over the beat, wide enough to hear a wobble.
Shape the phrase dynamically too: the slow vibrato works best when the volume is also shifting across the phrase.
Release the vibrato just before each move to the next neighbor note so the transition is clean.
Land on the root and let it vibrate slowly to a natural decay, like a singer holding a final syllable.
examples: fifth → fourth → third → second → root (each note rocking wide and slow, singer-songwriter style)
second → third → fourth → fifth → fourth → third (ascending then returning, slow warm vibrato throughout)
pitchConstraint:stepwise_vibrato:slow.watchOut: Slow vibrato on stepwise motion often drifts BELOW the target pitch because the ear stops auditing — you are caught up in the feeling. Every few notes, pause and test one pitch against the drone to verify the center is still right. Also, do not vibrate so wide that your third-to-fourth move becomes ambiguous to the ear.
pitchConstraint:stepwise_vibrato:slow.deeperInsight: A scalar melody with slow vibrato is the entire emotional engine of country and folk — remove either ingredient and you have an exercise, combine them and you have a song.

pitchConstraint:stepwise_vibrato:fast.interaction: Fast vibrato on stepwise motion creates the sound of a Spanish classical guitarist or a Greek bouzouki player — narrow quick shimmer over flowing neighbor-note melodies that feels constantly alive and urgent. Unlike slow vibrato which lets each note breathe, the fast wobble keeps the energy buzzing forward, so the melody feels like a river with ripples on every drop. The combination is harder than it looks because fast vibrato on short notes tends to lose its shape.
steps: Start by holding a single scale note and pulsing it with a narrow rapid shake for 8 beats to warm the hand.
Play a stepwise phrase at a moderate tempo, applying quick narrow vibrato the instant each note sounds.
Keep the amplitude very small — if you can hear the pitch drift more than a quarter tone, slow down or narrow it.
Pay attention to the uphill moves (like root to second to third) versus downhill moves — uphill is harder because the hand's momentum fights the vibrato direction.
Every phrase or two, drop out the vibrato and play a few notes straight so the fast shimmer stays special, not constant.
examples: root → second → third → fourth → fifth (quick narrow shimmer on each ascending note)
seventh → sixth → fifth → fourth (flamenco-style fast shimmer on each descending step)
pitchConstraint:stepwise_vibrato:fast.watchOut: Fast vibrato on short scalar notes often sounds like a nervous twitch rather than music — if the note value is less than two beats, the shimmer might not have enough room to establish. Choose either to slow the tempo or to skip vibrato on the shortest notes. Also watch your thumb: fast vibrato requires a free wrist.
pitchConstraint:stepwise_vibrato:fast.deeperInsight: Fast vibrato is physics-limited — you cannot make a wide fast vibrato without sounding cartoonish, so the amplitude must shrink as the rate climbs.

pitchConstraint:stepwise_vibrato:lateEnt.interaction: Late-entry vibrato on a stepwise melody is a master-class in restraint — each neighbor note arrives pure and only blooms on its longest values. This is how a Segovia recording sounds: the melody walks up and down the scale with classical purity, and vibrato only appears on the emphasized notes, blooming late. The contrast between the still notes and the blooming notes becomes the primary expressive device, more powerful than constant ornamentation.
steps: Identify which notes in your stepwise phrase are long enough to sustain for 2+ beats — only those will get vibrato.
Play the phrase with the short notes held straight and the long notes held straight at first, then blooming into slow vibrato halfway through their duration.
Count mentally: 'one-and-two' pure, 'and-three-and-four' with vibrato on the long notes.
Make sure the straight opening is clearly audible — if it's less than half a beat, the bloom reads as immediate.
End on a long note, let it be straight for a full beat, then let the vibrato blossom through the decay.
examples: root → second → third (held, then blooming) → second → root (held, blooming on release)
fifth → fourth → third (held, blooming) → second → root (long root, late shimmer into decay)
pitchConstraint:stepwise_vibrato:lateEnt.watchOut: The temptation on stepwise lines is to vibrate every note because they all feel equally 'available' — discipline yourself to ONLY vibrate notes that have real duration. A quick passing note with vibrato just sounds like a mistake. Also, beware of starting the vibrato at a predictable count — vary where the bloom arrives to keep it musical.
pitchConstraint:stepwise_vibrato:lateEnt.deeperInsight: Late-entry vibrato on scalar melody is the classical guitarist's answer to silence — the pure front is itself an expressive choice.

pitchConstraint:arch_vibrato:straight.interaction: An arch shape with no vibrato is a pure test of structural thinking — the climb, the peak, the descent must carry the whole phrase with zero shimmer-based expression. It sounds like a Renaissance lute piece or a hymn melody: architectural, patient, intonation-honest. The missing vibrato makes the contour itself the expression — the listener experiences the rise and fall as a physical gesture without any texture layered on top.
steps: Plan the peak before you begin: decide which note in your palette will be the summit (often the fifth, sixth, or root up an octave).
Climb toward the peak with notes of roughly equal duration, each one held dead-straight.
Land on the peak and hold it longer than any other note — 2-4 beats — with absolutely no vibrato. This is the exam.
Descend back toward the root with shorter notes, still straight, as if releasing the tension slowly.
Finish on the root and let it ring pure to full decay before the next arch.
examples: root → third → fifth → sixth → fifth → third → root (classic arch, summit on the sixth held long and straight)
second → fourth → fifth → root up an octave → fifth → third → root (ascending arc, octave peak held pure)
pitchConstraint:arch_vibrato:straight.watchOut: Almost every guitarist will try to add vibrato to the peak note because it FEELS like it needs emotion — don't. The straight peak is the whole point: it sounds like classical music, not blues. If you crack and rock your finger, you've lost the exercise. Also watch for the descent going flat — the relaxing hand wants to let tension out through pitch.
pitchConstraint:arch_vibrato:straight.deeperInsight: The pure-toned summit of an arch is what Renaissance composers did with their crowning notes — no ornament, just the interval speaking for itself, because nothing can improve on a perfect fifth held still.

pitchConstraint:arch_vibrato:slow.interaction: An arch with slow vibrato is the emotional peak of a power ballad — David Gilmour soloing over Comfortably Numb, Jeff Buckley singing a sustained phrase. The arch builds tension toward a summit, and the slow wide vibrato pays it off on the peak note, making that landing feel like a wail that could break hearts. The combination is one of the most universal expressive moves in all of popular music because the arch creates expectation and the vibrato delivers the catharsis.
steps: Climb through 3-4 notes to your peak, keeping them relatively straight so you build expectation.
Arrive at the peak and hold it for at least 2 beats — immediately apply wide slow vibrato, 2-3 cycles per second.
Let the vibrato grow in intensity across the peak: start moderate, then open up by the second half of the sustain.
Descend with less vibrato, fading the intensity as the phrase relaxes — the descent is the release.
End on the root or third with just a whisper of slow vibrato, letting it die naturally.
examples: root → third → fifth → sixth → fifth → third → root (wide slow vibrato parked on the sixth for 2 bars)
second → fourth → fifth → flat-seventh → fifth → fourth → root (bluesy arch, slow vibrato on the flat seven)
pitchConstraint:arch_vibrato:slow.watchOut: The peak note is where most players over-vibrate AMPLITUDE-wise and go sharp — the upswing of the rock is stronger than the downswing for most hands. Rock the finger through center pitch, not up from it. Also, don't start vibrato on the climbing notes or you'll spoil the payoff.
pitchConstraint:arch_vibrato:slow.deeperInsight: The arch-plus-slow-vibrato template is why rock ballad solos feel inevitable — the form itself carries 80 percent of the emotion, and the vibrato just seals the delivery.

pitchConstraint:arch_vibrato:fast.interaction: An arch with fast vibrato evokes flamenco cante, operatic aria, or Yngwie Malmsteen soloing — the melodic shape builds urgency toward a peak, and the narrow fast shimmer on the summit vibrates like a held high note in bel canto. Because fast vibrato is inherently agitated, the arch form channels that agitation into an architectural climax instead of nervous buzzing. The summit note becomes a controlled scream instead of a sob.
steps: Climb toward a high peak — ideally the sixth, seventh, or root up an octave — through mostly straight intermediate notes.
On the peak, engage fast narrow vibrato instantly: 5-7 cycles per second, very tight amplitude.
Hold the peak longer than any other note and let the fast vibrato carry the tension.
Descend quickly with progressively less vibrato, like a singer releasing a held note.
Land on the root dead-straight for contrast — the pure root underneath the recent fast shimmer is the resolution.
examples: root → third → fifth → root up an octave (fast tight shimmer on the octave for 2 beats)
fourth → fifth → sixth → seventh → sixth → fourth (fast vibrato parked on the seventh, classical cadence style)
pitchConstraint:arch_vibrato:fast.watchOut: Fast vibrato on high peak notes causes sympathetic string noise and can sound nervous instead of urgent — keep amplitude narrow and let the sustain carry the intensity rather than the width. Also, do not pre-apply fast vibrato during the climb; reserve it for the peak or it loses its meaning.
pitchConstraint:arch_vibrato:fast.deeperInsight: Fast vibrato belongs to notes at the TOP of a phrase — it's physiologically what happens to the human voice under high pitch strain, so using it anywhere else sounds unnatural.

pitchConstraint:arch_vibrato:lateEntry.interaction: An arch with late-entry vibrato is a Bach aria — the phrase climbs with dead-pure intonation, arrives at the peak as a held straight tone, and only AFTER the note has lived in its own silence does the vibrato bloom in. This combination demands the most discipline of any vibrato type: you must trust the straight front of your peak for at least a beat before adding any movement. It sounds like a singer at the Met, like a cellist in a chamber group, like a master with nothing to prove.
steps: Climb the arch with totally straight notes — every intermediate pitch still as a photograph.
Arrive at the peak and FREEZE: no vibrato at all for at least a full beat.
Only then begin a slow, gentle bloom — starting narrow and widening across the remaining sustain.
Descend the arch gradually relaxing the vibrato back to straight tone.
Land on the root still, let it die straight, and breathe before the next arch.
examples: root → third → fifth → sixth (held pure, then late bloom) → fifth → third → root
fourth → fifth → seventh (straight for 2 beats, then late slow vibrato) → fourth → root
