steps: Start a half-step or whole-step below target on /oh/.
Slide up with the lips rounded the entire way — no vowel opening.
Land cleanly on the target pitch and stabilize.
Hold for the phrase duration.
Experiment with slide length: longer slides for more drama, shorter for subtler entries.
examples: second → third (soul slide into /oh/ third)
fourth → fifth (ballad slide into /oh/ fifth)
vowel:oh_onset:slideIn.watchOut: The dark /oh/ makes slide pitch hard to audit — you may arrive flat without hearing it. Ear-train the landing with a reference pitch before and after each attempt.

vowel:oo_onset:hardAttack.interaction: Hard attack on /oo/ is an unusual combo — the hollow tight-lipped vowel resists the immediate cord closure that hard attack demands. The result is a kind of muted bell sound, immediate but without much edge. This appears in choral music and in certain Peter Gabriel or Kate Bush vocal moments where you need punctuation without brightness.
steps: Round the lips tightly into /oo/ before any sound.
Visualize the pitch target.
Attack at full volume with instant cord closure, even though the vowel wants gentleness.
Hold the tight /oo/ shape for two beats.
Release cleanly.
examples: third (hard /oo/ attack, muted bell tone)
fifth (hard attack on the darker high /oo/)
vowel:oo_onset:hardAttack.watchOut: The tight lip shape makes hard attack feel clamped — if you're bracing with your jaw, back off. The attack should come from the cords, not from squeezing the mouth shut harder.

vowel:oo_onset:soft.interaction: Soft onset on /oo/ is one of the warmest gentlest entries available — pure breath bleeding into a hollow rounded tone. It's the way lullaby singers, Celtic folk vocalists, and ambient artists like Hildegard of Bingen re-enactors start their phrases. Extremely restful and supremely controlled.
steps: Round the lips tightly and exhale softly.
Bring the cords together so /oo/ emerges through breath.
Swell slowly over a full beat — slower than on brighter vowels because /oo/ wants patience.
Hold at moderate volume, never pushing.
Release by diminishing the tone back into breath.
examples: third (soft /oo/ bloom, lullaby-style)
fifth (gentle /oo/ emergence, meditative)
vowel:oo_onset:soft.watchOut: The hollow /oo/ wants to go flat during the swell because it's dark and relaxed. Imagine each note finishing a cent sharp of its true pitch — this compensates for the vowel's natural drift.

vowel:oo_onset:breathy.interaction: Breathy /oo/ is almost an ambient texture rather than a distinct note — it's the sound of wind through a bottle, or a flute-like whisper. This is the onset for dream-pop layering, the vocal texture Cocteau Twins used as an instrument. Practical for recording, rare on stage.
steps: Round the lips and exhale with more air than tone.
Gradually engage the cords so /oo/ emerges through breath.
Keep the whisper quality for the full note duration — no hard closure.
Hold at very low volume.
Release into breath without a hard cutoff.
examples: third (whispered /oo/, ambient texture)
fifth (dream-pop breathy /oo/ high tone)
vowel:oo_onset:breathy.watchOut: Breathy /oo/ uses enormous amounts of air for almost no acoustic payoff. Phrases must be very short — two beats at most — and you need a full inhale between each attempt.

vowel:oo_onset:slideIn.interaction: Slide-in /oo/ is a haunted longing sound — the rounded hollow tone combined with an upward slide produces something that feels like a ghost calling. It's the onset dream-pop and ambient vocalists use for ethereal moments. Very cinematic, almost always slow.
steps: Start a half-step or whole-step below target on /oo/.
Slide up slowly with the lips rounded — slower than on brighter vowels.
Land on target and stabilize; the dark vowel resists bright arrivals.
Hold for the phrase duration.
Release softly.
examples: second → third (slow haunted /oo/ slide)
fourth → fifth (longing /oo/ slide into the fifth)
vowel:oo_onset:slideIn.watchOut: The dark tone makes slide tuning extremely difficult to audit. Always use a reference drone during this drill — the /oo/ slide is the combo most likely to arrive at the wrong pitch without you noticing.

vowel:hum_onset:hardAttack.interaction: Hard attack on a hum is a tight controlled start — lips sealed, instant pitch, full tone through the mask. It's the vocal equivalent of a violin bow attacking a note without any prep. Useful for training clean cord closure without the help of a vowel shape to cushion errors.
steps: Close the lips gently and visualize the target pitch.
Attack at full volume with instant cord closure — the hum should start at full tone.
Feel the buzz on your nose and cheekbones immediately; if the buzz takes time to arrive, the attack was soft.
Hold for two beats with lips closed.
Release cleanly by opening the lips slightly.
examples: third (hard-attack hum, mid-range)
fifth (hard-attack hum, higher position for lift)
vowel:hum_onset:hardAttack.watchOut: Hard attack on a closed mouth is the easiest place to develop a glottal stop — if you hear a click before the tone, back off completely. The cord closure must be decisive but never slam.

vowel:hum_onset:soft.interaction: Soft onset on a hum is the most delicate entry possible — closed mouth, breath first, a tiny whispered sound that barely exists before becoming pitch. This is the practice coaches use to teach feather-light cord engagement, and it's how classical singers warm up before dangerous passages.
steps: Close the lips gently and exhale gently through the nose before any tone.
Bring the cords together to produce the softest possible hum.
Swell slowly over a full beat to moderate volume — the swell should feel almost invisible.
Hold at moderate volume.
Release by slowly reducing pressure, not by cutting off.
examples: third (softest bloom into mid-range hum)
fifth (gentle soft onset into higher hum)
vowel:hum_onset:soft.watchOut: Soft hum onset is one of the only drills where you can safely practice very softly — but don't confuse soft with timid. The pitch must be decisive even if the volume is tiny.

vowel:hum_onset:breathy.interaction: Breathy hum is a bizarre near-silent combo — closed mouth, mostly air with a hint of pitch. It's primarily a cord-flexibility drill because you're forcing the cords to underclose while sealed behind the lips. Not used in performance but extremely useful for training the threshold between breath and tone.
steps: Close the lips and exhale through the nose with deliberate breathiness.
Gradually engage the cords until a whispered hum emerges.
Keep the hum under-closed for the first beat, then allow it to resolve into firmer pitch.
Hold briefly.
Release into pure breath.
examples: third (breathy hum resolving into tone)
fifth (whispered breathy hum at a higher position)
vowel:hum_onset:breathy.watchOut: Breathy hum is deceptively air-hungry. Keep phrases under two beats. And be warned — this drill feels strange in the throat and shouldn't be done more than a few reps at a time.

vowel:hum_onset:slideIn.interaction: Slide-in hum is the most interior expressive entry — closed mouth, pitch sliding up from below to a target buzz location in the face. It's the sound of someone humming a sad melody to themselves in the car. The slide is felt rather than heard because the closed mouth mutes external detail; you're doing the entire entry inside your skull.
steps: Close the lips and start a half-step below the target.
Slide up to the target over a quarter beat, feeling the buzz move higher on your face as pitch rises.
Land cleanly and stabilize the hum.
Hold for two beats.
Release.
examples: second → third (slide hum to the third)
fourth → fifth (slide hum to the fifth)
vowel:hum_onset:slideIn.watchOut: Without external feedback, the slide can drift flat without you realizing. Use a drone pitch playing in the room (or in your ear) to keep the landing honest.

vowel:scatSyllables_onset:hardAttack.interaction: Hard attack on scat syllables is exactly what a trumpet player does — instant pitch, sharp consonant, full volume. The syllables give each attack a different consonant flavor (dop is sharper than bah, bee is brighter than doo), so the onset becomes a study in how consonants shape hard entries. This is bebop vocal rhythm training.
steps: Pick a scat syllable with a sharp consonant (dop, dee, bop).
Visualize target pitch and attack with full volume immediately.
The consonant and the pitch should arrive simultaneously — no consonant before the note.
Hold the syllable's vowel for one or two beats.
Repeat with different consonants on different scale degrees.
examples: third (dop, hard attack)
fifth → root-up-an-octave (dop-DEE, hard attacks in sequence)
vowel:scatSyllables_onset:hardAttack.watchOut: The consonant shouldn't feel like it lands before the pitch — if you hear a 'd-three' instead of a unified 'dee', the attack is lagging the consonant. Sync them tightly.

vowel:scatSyllables_onset:soft.interaction: Soft onset on scat syllables is an unusual combo — scat usually wants punctuation, but soft onset demands gradual emergence. The result is a dreamy floating entry where the syllable appears rather than strikes. Think Chet Baker scatting gently, or a jazz vocalist in a ballad moment. Rare but beautiful.
steps: Pick a softer consonant (doo, bah, woo).
Exhale gently and let the scat syllable emerge through the breath.
The syllable's consonant should be barely audible as the pitch swells in.
Resolve into full tone by the end of the first beat.
Release cleanly.
examples: third (doo, soft emergence)
fifth (woo, gentle bloom)
vowel:scatSyllables_onset:soft.watchOut: Sharp consonants (d, b, p) fight soft onset — if the combo feels wrong, your consonant choice is the problem. Switch to w, y, or h consonants for softer scat.

vowel:scatSyllables_onset:breathy.interaction: Breathy scat is the whispered bebop sound — think a jazz vocalist doing the quietest possible 'doo-bah' into a mic at 2am. The scat syllables give you articulation and the breathiness gives you intimacy. It's the sound of private practice or of a very close-mic'd recording.
steps: Pick a soft scat syllable (doo, woo, bah).
Exhale with more air than tone.
Let the syllable emerge through the breath — the consonant is whispered, the vowel is breathy.
Keep the under-closure for the full duration.
Release into breath.
examples: third (breathy doo, intimate jazz)
fifth (whispered woo, close-mic)
vowel:scatSyllables_onset:breathy.watchOut: Breathy scat burns air very fast because the consonant consumes some of the breath AND the vowel underclosure consumes the rest. Keep phrases very short.

vowel:scatSyllables_onset:slideIn.interaction: Slide-in scat is the bluesy horn-line entry — a scat syllable sliding up from below like a trombone slide. Think Louis Armstrong going 'dah-DAH!' with the first note a slide into the second. The syllables preserve rhythm while the slide adds expression, creating the classic New Orleans swing feel.
steps: Pick a scat syllable with a long vowel (doo, bah, wah).
Start a half-step below the target pitch.
Slide up through the syllable's vowel, landing on target.
Hold the target for one or two beats.
Release on a sharp consonant if you want to bookend the slide.
examples: second → third (doo slide into third)
fourth → fifth (wah slide into fifth)
vowel:scatSyllables_onset:slideIn.watchOut: Sharp consonants don't slide well — d, b, p want to be punched not swooped. Stick to long-vowel syllables (doo, wah, yow) when combining scat with slide-in onset.

vowel:realLyrics_onset:hardAttack.interaction: Hard attack on real lyrics is the sound of rock and pop openings — instant consonant and pitch on the first syllable of a verse or chorus. This is how Whitney Houston started 'I Will Always Love You', how Adele starts most of her songs, how Chris Cornell hit phrase openings. It's demanding because the lyric's consonant and the musical attack must sync perfectly.
steps: Pick a lyric line and identify its first word's opening sound.
Visualize target pitch and imagine the word fully before you sing.
Attack at full volume with instant cord closure on the first syllable.
Carry the line through with whatever melody you use, but the attack is the whole drill.
Rotate across different first-word sounds (hard consonant, soft consonant, pure vowel start) to feel which are easier and which need more preparation.
examples: lyric starting on the third (hard attack on first syllable)
lyric starting on the fifth (hard attack, chorus-opener style)
vowel:realLyrics_onset:hardAttack.watchOut: Lyrics tempt you to 'warm up' into the first note with a bit of breath or slide — that's the exact opposite of hard attack. The first syllable must arrive at full volume, full pitch, full clarity.

vowel:realLyrics_onset:soft.interaction: Soft onset on real lyrics is the intimate ballad opener — breath first, then tone, then the word emerges. This is how Eva Cassidy opens a song, or how Sinatra eases into a quiet standard. The combo works best on lyrics that start with open vowels or soft consonants (w, h, y) because those allow breath to flow.
steps: Pick a lyric line that starts with an open vowel or soft consonant.
Inhale and exhale gently before any tone.
Bring the cords together so the first syllable emerges softly through breath.
Let the tone swell to full volume over the first beat.
Carry the line through with natural phrasing.
examples: lyric starting on the third (soft bloom into the first word)
lyric starting on the fifth (gentle emergence)
vowel:realLyrics_onset:soft.watchOut: Soft onset on a lyric that starts with a hard consonant (k, t, p, b) is impossible without weirdness — the consonant demands precision. Pick your lyric carefully or practice with open-vowel starts.

vowel:realLyrics_onset:breathy.interaction: Breathy lyric onset is the signature sound of intimate indie pop and close-mic vocal takes — Billie Eilish, Bon Iver, Norah Jones all live here. The lyric's first word emerges through air, feeling like someone is whispering the song directly to you. It's a recording-native technique because it doesn't carry well live.
steps: Pick a lyric with a soft first consonant or open vowel.
Exhale with deliberate breathiness and let the first word whisper into tone.
Keep the breathy quality through the first phrase — don't brighten to normal tone until mid-phrase.
Carry the line to a natural breath point.
Release before running out of air.
examples: lyric starting on the third (breathy emergence of first word)
lyric starting on the fifth (whispered first word into firmer tone)
vowel:realLyrics_onset:breathy.watchOut: Breathy lyric onset wastes huge amounts of air; any lyric longer than four words will run you dry. Plan breath points explicitly or shorten the phrase you're practicing.

vowel:realLyrics_onset:slideIn.interaction: Slide-in on real lyrics is the country and blues signature — the first word of the phrase slides up into pitch like Patsy Cline on 'Crazy' or John Mayer on a bluesy line. The lyric's first syllable's vowel must be long enough to hold the slide, so the combo depends on which word you pick.
steps: Choose a lyric whose first word has a long open vowel (ah, oh, ee).
Start a half-step or whole-step below target on that first vowel.
Slide up to the target pitch over a quarter beat.
Land on the target and continue the rest of the line normally.
Practice on different slide distances to match the lyric's emotional weight.
examples: lyric starting on the second sliding to third (country entry)
lyric starting on the fourth sliding to fifth (blues entry)
vowel:realLyrics_onset:slideIn.watchOut: A lyric that begins with a hard consonant makes the slide collapse into an attack — the consonant fixes the start too precisely. Pick lyrics that start with vowels or soft consonants to let the slide live.

pitchConstraint:leaps_onset:hardAttack.interaction: Leaps want drama and hard attacks supply the exclamation point — stack them and every skip arrives already at full volume, so the interval registers as a sudden event rather than an unfolding one. This is the sound of punk horn lines and Pixies hooks, where a major sixth jump lands like a door slamming open and the ear has no ramp to soften the shock.
steps: Set a moderate tempo and visualize two target notes at least a scale-step apart.
Inhale fully, then crack the first note at full volume with zero lead-in — no slur, no fade.
Skip immediately to a note at least a third away and hit that one at the same full volume.
Anchor every third leap back to the root or fifth so the ear has a home to reference.
Reset the breath between phrases so no hard attack runs out of air mid-leap.
examples: root → fifth → second → sixth → root (each note fired full-voice from silence)
third → root up an octave → fourth → seventh → root (every attack a clean strike)
pitchConstraint:leaps_onset:hardAttack.watchOut: Hard attacks paired with leaps tempt the voice to push the interval instead of tune it — pitch goes sharp on the second attack because the body braced. Check that each strike lands dead-center, not just loud.
pitchConstraint:leaps_onset:hardAttack.deeperInsight: Leaps cue the listener to expect a gesture; the hard attack finalizes it into a statement. Pull one away and it becomes either a melody or a shout, not both.

pitchConstraint:leaps_onset:soft.interaction: This is bel canto athleticism — big intervals delivered with a gentle bloom instead of a punch. The soft onset forces you to hear the next leap before you sing it, because you cannot rely on muscular impact to get there. Every skip has to be imagined at pitch first, then coaxed into tone, which is how classical and musical-theater singers keep octave jumps from cracking.
steps: Breathe low and feel the support band engage before any sound.
Mouth the target shape silently, then let air precede pitch so the note blooms into place.
Skip cleanly to the next target, starting that one the same way — air, pitch, full tone.
Rest a full beat between phrases to re-set the breath and re-aim the next leap.
Land a leap on the root or fifth every few phrases so the ear doesn't drift.
examples: root → third → sixth → root up an octave (each bloomed gently into place)
fifth → second → seventh → fourth → root (soft onsets on every skip, no scoops)
pitchConstraint:leaps_onset:soft.watchOut: Soft onsets under a leap constraint tempt the voice into a scoop — a micro-slide that masks imprecise aim. The onset is soft but the target must still be exact; if the pitch moves while the tone blooms, you scooped.
pitchConstraint:leaps_onset:soft.deeperInsight: Soft onsets buy you milliseconds to audiate the interval; classical leap technique is fundamentally the habit of imagining the destination during the onset.

pitchConstraint:leaps_onset:breathy.interaction: Large intervals sung on air — this is the sound of a confession landing in surprising places. The breathy onset erases the muscular brace you would normally use to nail a leap, so you have to trust pitch placement alone. It evokes intimate singers like José González or Sufjan Stevens, where a minor-sixth skip slips out almost by accident.
steps: Exhale a small pre-sigh before the first note to confirm the breath mode.
Start the phrase with more air than tone and let pitch whisper into existence.
Skip to a note a third or more away and repeat the same whisper-into-tone onset.
Keep the volume low and steady so the leap is heard as pitch motion, not as effort.
Land occasional leaps on the root or fifth to ground the wandering.
examples: root → fourth → sixth → second → root (each skip whispered into pitch)
fifth → root up an octave → third → seventh → root (airy onsets, no muscular push)
pitchConstraint:leaps_onset:breathy.watchOut: On larger leaps the voice will sneak in a glottal stop just before the target to secure the pitch — that kills the breathy character. Keep the air flowing continuously across the skip so the onset of the second note is as airy as the first.
pitchConstraint:leaps_onset:breathy.deeperInsight: Breathy onset plus leap is a flexibility diagnostic — if your cords cannot find the higher target through pure breath, they are overbracing your normal singing too.

pitchConstraint:leaps_onset:slideIn.interaction: Every skip arrives with a blues tail attached — you slide up to the new destination from below, which means each leap has a built-in setup gesture. This is where B.B. King guitar phrasing and Aretha vocal runs live: the distance between notes becomes a chance to bend, and the skip itself feels inevitable by the time the slide completes. The two constraints turn every interval into a little story.
steps: Identify two target notes at least a scale-step apart and start below the first one.
Slide up a half-step or more into the first target, then rest briefly to let it settle.
Leap, but approach the new target from a half-step below so the skip ends in a slide.
Keep the slide short and deliberate; a long sigh up negates the leap feeling.
Anchor periodically on the root or fifth by sliding into it from the note just below.
examples: (below)→root → (below)→fifth → (below)→third → (below)→root up an octave (each skip ends in a slide)
(below)→fourth → (below)→seventh → (below)→second → (below)→sixth (bluesy leaps throughout)
pitchConstraint:leaps_onset:slideIn.watchOut: The slide will want to become the whole gesture, turning a leap into a long scoop that obliterates the interval itself. Keep the slide tight — just a half-step of glide — so the ear still hears a skip, not a portamento.
pitchConstraint:leaps_onset:slideIn.deeperInsight: A slide-into-leap works because the brain registers the target pitch the moment the slide completes, not when it started — which is why blues players can skip wide intervals and still feel locked in tune.

pitchConstraint:stepwise_onset:hardAttac.interaction: Stepwise motion is the smoothest pitch strategy, and the hard attack is the most abrupt onset — together they create a line where every connection is easy but every entry is aggressive. Think of a rock vocal chant or a Beatles 'She Loves You' hook: each syllable pounds in at full volume but the pitch only moves one scale-step at a time, which makes the line feel forceful without feeling frantic.
steps: Pick a slow to moderate tempo so each hard attack gets full presence.
Strike the first note cleanly at full volume with no lead-in or slur.
Step to the neighboring scale note and fire it with the same unforgiving attack.
Resist sliding between the neighbor notes — a hard attack demands a clean handoff.
Use a brief gap between phrases so the attacks don't blur into a single forte wash.
examples: root → second → third → fourth → third → second → root (each step struck hard)
fifth → sixth → fifth → fourth → third → fourth → fifth (neighbor motion, full-volume strikes)
pitchConstraint:stepwise_onset:hardAttac.watchOut: Because the pitches are so close, the hard attacks will bleed together and become one continuous shout — you'll lose the articulation that makes each note a fresh event. Listen for a clean re-attack, not a legato line at forte.
pitchConstraint:stepwise_onset:hardAttac.deeperInsight: Stepwise at hard-attack is the machinery of the protest chant: close intervals make the crowd-accessible, hard attacks make the repetition feel urgent.

pitchConstraint:stepwise_onset:soft.interaction: This is the classical art-song default — the line moves by neighbor notes and each one blooms gently into tone. Because both constraints favor smoothness, the real work is making every soft onset distinct enough to be heard as a new note rather than a continuation of the last one. It's Fauré, it's Schubert, it's a lullaby where every syllable matters.
steps: Set a slow pulse and breathe low before starting.
Let air precede pitch on the first note and bloom it into full tone.
Step to the neighbor and restart the onset cleanly — air, pitch, tone — don't slur through.
Keep the volume steady so the onsets are the only articulation cue.
End phrases on the root or third so the line has a tonal home.
examples: third → fourth → third → second → root (each neighbor bloomed gently)
fifth → fourth → third → fourth → fifth → sixth (soft onsets, connected stepwise contour)
pitchConstraint:stepwise_onset:soft.watchOut: A soft onset on stepwise motion will collapse into a legato slur if you aren't strict — the notes stop being articulated events and become one long drone. Re-initiate breath support for each note, even when the pitch only moves a step.
pitchConstraint:stepwise_onset:soft.deeperInsight: The hardest thing in classical art song is distinguishing adjacent soft onsets without breaking the line — the answer is to renew the breath pressure behind each note, not to re-strike it.

pitchConstraint:stepwise_onset:breathy.interaction: The breathiest, most intimate possible gesture: tiny pitch movements delivered on pure air. This is late-night lullaby territory — think Billie Eilish verses or a whispered Sade intro. Because the intervals are small, the breath quality does almost all of the emotional work; you're practically speaking on pitch and letting the neighbor-note motion be the melody.
steps: Start with a quiet exhale and feel the breath mode settle in.
Place the first note with more air than tone and a very small volume.
Step to the next scale note and whisper it into being with the same breath-first approach.
Keep the pitch motion very steady — wide vibrato will break the breathy spell.
Rest between phrases and let silence be part of the texture.
examples: root → second → root → seventh below → root (whispered, each step bloomed on air)
third → fourth → third → second → third (breathy, small-volume, neighbor-note)
pitchConstraint:stepwise_onset:breathy.watchOut: Neighbor motion at a whisper will tempt you to slide microtonally between notes — you'll end up glissandoing rather than stepping. Each note still needs a fresh air-to-pitch onset, even at a whisper.
pitchConstraint:stepwise_onset:breathy.deeperInsight: Intimate vocalists control pitch placement with breath pressure, not volume — this combo is their drill.

pitchConstraint:stepwise_onset:slideIn.interaction: This is the smallest possible slide into the smallest possible step — which is why it sounds so vocal. Every neighbor note gets approached from a half-step below, turning a simple scalar phrase into a line full of gentle cries. Think of a Ray Charles gospel verse or a Willie Nelson scoop where the singer barely moves in pitch but every note seems to ache upward into place.
steps: Set a slow enough tempo that each slide can actually complete before the next note.
Start below the first scale target and slide up a half-step into it.
Step to the neighbor, but approach that neighbor with its own little slide from just below.
Don't let the slides become longer than a half-step — they're ornaments, not the main gesture.
Close phrases by sliding into the root or third for a grounded resolution.
examples: (below)→root → (below)→second → (below)→third → (below)→second → root (each step slid into)
(below)→fifth → (below)→sixth → (below)→fifth → (below)→fourth → (below)→third (scoop-stepped)
pitchConstraint:stepwise_onset:slideIn.watchOut: When both the step and the slide are small, they merge into one big portamento and the stepwise identity vanishes. Each note needs a clearly audible arrival moment — the slide stops, then the step happens.
pitchConstraint:stepwise_onset:slideIn.deeperInsight: Gospel and country vocalists treat every stepwise move as a miniature slide — this combo trains that default to become conscious rather than reflexive.

pitchConstraint:arch_onset:hardAttack.interaction: The arch wants to climb and resolve; the hard attack makes each note along the climb a declaration. Together they create the sound of a fight-anthem or a Springsteen chorus — the phrase rises to a peak at full volume and then punches its way back down. You get no ramp-in relief even on the descent; every note is a hammer strike along a hill-shape.
steps: Plan a peak note in the upper third of your current range.
Fire the first note at full volume and climb stepwise or by skips up to the peak.
Hit the peak with the most confident hard attack of the phrase.
Descend back down with the same no-lead-in attacks, ending near the starting pitch.
Breathe fully between arches so every phrase gets the same commitment.
examples: root → third → fifth → seventh → fifth → third → root (climbs to seventh, hard attacks throughout)
third → fifth → root up an octave → fifth → third → root (arch peak at top octave)
pitchConstraint:arch_onset:hardAttack.watchOut: The descent will tempt you to relax and fade — losing the hard-attack identity in the back half. Keep the intensity constant: the arch is pitch motion, not volume motion.
pitchConstraint:arch_onset:hardAttack.deeperInsight: Protest anthems use rising arches with unyielding volume because it signals commitment — relaxing on the descent would read as backing down.

pitchConstraint:arch_onset:soft.interaction: A classical arch at its purest — Schubert, lieder, Italian aria. The line climbs with each note bloomed gently into place, reaches its peak with the softest, most prepared attack, and descends through the same gentle onsets back to the tonal home. The difficulty is that the peak note, which naturally wants to be struck, must instead be coaxed into pitch from air.
steps: Breathe low and plan the peak note before you start.
Start the first note with a soft air-to-pitch onset, low in the register.
Climb by steps or small skips, re-initiating the soft onset on each new note.
Reach the peak with the most carefully prepared soft onset of the phrase — no grab.
Descend back through the soft onsets to rest on the root or third.
examples: root → third → fifth → sixth → fifth → third → root (each note bloomed, peak at sixth)
second → fourth → sixth → root up an octave → sixth → fourth → second (arch with top-octave peak)
pitchConstraint:arch_onset:soft.watchOut: The peak note will tempt a hard attack even when you're committed to soft — the body braces to secure the high pitch. If the peak suddenly gets louder than the approach, you broke the constraint. Keep breath pressure climbing, not impact.
pitchConstraint:arch_onset:soft.deeperInsight: Classical technique holds that the peak of an arch should be reached 'from within' — meaning the onset quality must not change when the pitch reaches its highest point.

pitchConstraint:arch_onset:breathy.interaction: A breathy arch is barely there — a wisp that rises and falls, more felt than heard. This is the sound of Elizabeth Fraser of the Cocteau Twins, or a Jeff Buckley exhale that shapes a hill out of pure air. The peak of the arch becomes a kind of sigh rather than a statement, and the whole phrase reads as longing without ever raising its voice.
steps: Pick a peak that is challenging but not strenuous, to keep the air mode feasible.
Start quietly with more air than tone, very low in the phrase's range.
Rise stepwise or by small skips, keeping the air-to-tone ratio constant on each onset.
At the peak, resist the instinct to engage full cord closure — let the air stay dominant.
Descend back with the same whispered onsets until the phrase rests on the root.
examples: root → second → third → fourth → third → second → root (whispered arch, peak at fourth)
third → fifth → sixth → seventh → sixth → fifth → third (breathy climb and descent)
pitchConstraint:arch_onset:breathy.watchOut: Breathy tone and rising pitch fight each other — the body wants to engage for the climb, and if it does, the breathiness disappears at exactly the peak. Practice keeping air volume constant while pitch rises.
pitchConstraint:arch_onset:breathy.deeperInsight: Breathy arch is the ultimate test of cord adduction control: ascend a fifth without the cords snapping shut to secure the note.

pitchConstraint:arch_onset:slideIn.interaction: Every note of the arch gets a little blues scoop under it, turning the rising line into a series of sighs climbing a hill. This is the sound of Otis Redding building a chorus or a Stevie Wonder soul verse — you feel the effort of each climb because every note is reached through a small upward slide, and the descending half of the arch gets the same treatment.
steps: Plan the peak and the slide approach pitches for each step.
Slide up a half-step into the first note and let it settle.
Climb stepwise or by small skips, approaching each note with its own slide from below.
At the peak, use a slightly longer slide to mark the summit emotionally.
Descend back through slide-in notes, ending with a final slide into the root.
examples: (below)→root → (below)→third → (below)→fifth → (below)→sixth → (below)→fifth → (below)→third → (below)→root (arch of sighs)
(below)→second → (below)→fourth → (below)→sixth → (below)→root up an octave → (below)→sixth → (below)→fourth → (below)→second
pitchConstraint:arch_onset:slideIn.watchOut: When the line is descending, slides-from-below can start to sound awkward — the gesture fights the contour. Keep the slides very short on the descent so they ornament without contradicting the downward motion.
pitchConstraint:arch_onset:slideIn.deeperInsight: Soul singers often reserve the longest slide-in for the peak of an arch — it makes the summit feel earned and emotional rather than mechanical.

pitchConstraint:seed_onset:hardAttack.interaction: A three-note seed fired with hard attacks is a minimalist rock motif — think Sleater-Kinney riffs or a Pixies vocal stab. Because each iteration only changes one note, the repeated hard attacks drill that tiny difference into the listener's ear. The attack makes the seed feel like a chant and the one-note variation feels like a twitch in the chant's shape.
steps: Compose a three-note seed, writing the scale degrees down.
Strike all three notes at full volume with no lead-in on any of them.
Rest briefly, then repeat the seed, changing exactly one note by one scale degree.
Keep the hard attack identical even on the changed note so the variation registers as pitch, not effort.
Every few iterations return to the original seed as an anchor.
examples: [root → third → fifth] (hit hard) → [root → fourth → fifth] → [root → fourth → sixth] (one change per iteration)
[fifth → third → root] → [fifth → fourth → root] → [sixth → fourth → root] (hard attacks throughout)
pitchConstraint:seed_onset:hardAttack.watchOut: The hard attack will mask pitch inaccuracy on the changed note — you'll hit it confidently but slightly off. Slow down and re-check the variation against the original seed so the one-note change is actually the note you intended.
pitchConstraint:seed_onset:hardAttack.deeperInsight: Pop hooks work on this exact principle — a chanted three-note cell with one note shifted creates a hook that feels familiar and new at once.

pitchConstraint:seed_onset:soft.interaction: The seed becomes a tiny meditation here — three gently bloomed notes, repeated, with one shifting each time. This is the pedagogy behind much Baroque ornamentation: establish a figure, then vary one element at a time to show mastery of variation. The soft onset makes each iteration feel considered rather than mechanical.
steps: Choose a three-note seed in a comfortable part of your range.
Bloom each of the three notes softly into tone, breath before pitch.
Repeat the seed, changing exactly one note; bloom the new note with the same gentle onset.
Keep the timing between notes steady so the variation is heard, not the pacing.
Return to the original seed every few iterations for contrast.
examples: [root → second → third] (bloomed) → [root → second → fourth] → [root → third → fourth] (each onset gentle)
[third → fourth → fifth] → [second → fourth → fifth] → [second → fourth → sixth] (soft onsets on every note)
pitchConstraint:seed_onset:soft.watchOut: Soft onsets across a repeating seed invite legato connections between notes — you'll end up slurring the seed into a single phrase instead of three distinct bloomed events. Re-initiate the onset on each note.
pitchConstraint:seed_onset:soft.deeperInsight: Soft-onset seed work teaches one-element-at-a-time mastery — it's the audiation drill that separates intermediate from advanced singers.

pitchConstraint:seed_onset:breathy.interaction: A whispered three-note motif that shifts slightly each iteration — like a dream where the same sentence keeps repeating but a word changes. The breathy onset reduces muscular intervention, which means your audiation has to work harder to find the right note when you vary. This is ASMR-style intimate music: Phoebe Bridgers, Weyes Blood, small things that change.
steps: Pick a short three-note seed and whisper-sing it first with no rhythm.
Deliver the seed with breathy onsets, keeping air dominant throughout.
Rest briefly, then vary exactly one note and re-sing the seed with the same airy quality.
Control volume tightly — any push ruins the breath mode.
After several variations, return to the original seed to reset your ear.
examples: [third → second → root] (whispered) → [third → second → seventh below] → [fourth → second → seventh below]
[root → third → fifth] (airy) → [root → fourth → fifth] → [root → fourth → sixth]
pitchConstraint:seed_onset:breathy.watchOut: When you change a note, the cords will tighten to secure the new pitch — breaking the breathy onset on exactly the note that is varying. Keep the air-to-tone ratio constant even on the changed note.
pitchConstraint:seed_onset:breathy.deeperInsight: Variation ability at whisper volume is a diagnostic for how well you audiate — if you need the cords to find the note, you don't yet hear it inside.

pitchConstraint:seed_onset:slideIn.interaction: Each note of the seed arrives with a small upward slide, and when a note changes, the slide itself becomes an expressive marker. This is soul-vocal phrasing — Curtis Mayfield shaping a little three-note riff where every note aches into place, and small variations feel like the singer reaching for something new. The blues tail amplifies the emotional weight of each variation.
steps: Compose a three-note seed and identify the scale degrees.
Slide up into each note of the seed, letting the slides be short and deliberate.
Repeat the seed with one note changed; slide into that new note with the same gesture.
Keep the slides to a half-step so the seed's identity stays clear.
Anchor the seed periodically with a slide-in to the root.
examples: [(below)→root → (below)→third → (below)→fifth] → [(below)→root → (below)→fourth → (below)→fifth] (one-note change)
[(below)→third → (below)→fourth → (below)→fifth] → [(below)→third → (below)→fourth → (below)→sixth]
pitchConstraint:seed_onset:slideIn.watchOut: Slide-ins on a repeating seed will start extending into full glissandi — the seed's note count disappears into one long scoop. Keep each slide short enough that three distinct notes remain audible per iteration.
pitchConstraint:seed_onset:slideIn.deeperInsight: Soul singers build entire verses from slide-in seeds with single-note variations — it's how Curtis Mayfield and Al Green construct hooks that feel alive rather than scripted.

pitchConstraint:forbidden_onset:hardAtta.interaction: Take a few notes off the table and then hit every remaining note with a clean, full-volume strike. Forbidding notes forces your ear to work harder, and the hard attack gives you no cover while you do it — you cannot slur into a forbidden note and pretend you meant it. This produces focused, assertive phrasing in the style of rock vocalists who know exactly which notes are in their palette.
steps: Write down the three notes you are forbidding from the current scale.
Plan a short phrase using only the allowed notes and rehearse it silently first.
Strike each allowed note with a full-volume hard attack, no lead-in.
If you accidentally hit a forbidden note, stop and reset rather than push through.
End the phrase on the root or fifth, assuming those aren't among the forbidden ones.
examples: root → fifth → third → fifth → root (forbidding second, fourth, sixth — hard attacks)
third → sixth → root → sixth → third (forbidding second, fourth, seventh — full-volume strikes)
pitchConstraint:forbidden_onset:hardAtta.watchOut: Under the pressure of a hard attack, muscle memory will fire off the forbidden notes the fastest — they're the ones your fingers or voice default to. Slow the tempo until you can plan ahead of each attack.
pitchConstraint:forbidden_onset:hardAtta.deeperInsight: Forbidding notes at hard-attack intensity is the fastest way to expose your default melodic vocabulary — the notes you miss repeatedly are the ones your ear leans on.

pitchConstraint:forbidden_onset:soft.interaction: Remove a few notes from the palette and sing what's left with a prepared, bloomed onset. The forbidden constraint sharpens your note-choice discipline and the soft onset gives you extra milliseconds of audiation, so you can actually hear the next allowed note before you commit. This is an ear-training exercise dressed up as a performance technique.
steps: List the three notes you are avoiding and commit them to memory.
Breathe low and plan the first allowed note before any sound.
Bloom the note softly — air, then pitch, then full tone — and listen to it settle.
Plan the next allowed note during that settle, then bloom it the same way.
End phrases on the root or an allowed anchor note, softly.
examples: root → third → fifth → third → root (forbidding second, fourth, sixth — soft onsets)
third → fifth → seventh → fifth → root (forbidding second, fourth, sixth — each note bloomed)
pitchConstraint:forbidden_onset:soft.watchOut: Soft onsets under a forbidden constraint tempt you to fade onto a forbidden note when you lose track — the gentle bloom hides the violation. Stop the instant you hear yourself approach a forbidden pitch, don't let the bloom complete.
pitchConstraint:forbidden_onset:soft.deeperInsight: Soft onset buys you enough audiation time to catch a forbidden-note mistake before it becomes sound — that's why it's the default pedagogy onset.

pitchConstraint:forbidden_onset:breathy.interaction: Subtract a few notes from your palette and whisper what remains — the combination feels like a secret being told in code. Because the breathy onset already minimizes muscular commitment, you get extra cognitive room to plan the next allowed note. This produces the spacious, intentional vocabulary heard in artists like Billie Eilish or in ambient electronic vocal lines.
steps: Choose three forbidden notes and hold the remaining palette in your head.
Begin with an airy whisper-into-pitch onset on an allowed note.
Glide to the next allowed note with the same breath-dominant quality.
Double-check each new note is not on your forbidden list before committing.
Anchor on the root or an allowed tonal center when the phrase rests.
examples: root → third → fifth → seventh → root (whispered, forbidding second, fourth, sixth)
third → fifth → root up an octave → fifth → third (airy, forbidding second, fourth, seventh)
pitchConstraint:forbidden_onset:breathy.watchOut: Breathy onsets will let forbidden notes slip in almost inaudibly, and you may not even catch the violation because the air-heavy tone masks pitch. Sing out loud periodically to re-check that no forbidden pitches have snuck into your whisper.
pitchConstraint:forbidden_onset:breathy.deeperInsight: At a whisper, you rely almost entirely on audiation to pick allowed notes — which makes this combo a pure test of whether your inner hearing has internalized the forbidden set.

pitchConstraint:forbidden_onset:slideIn.interaction: You've shrunk the available palette, and every allowed note now gets approached from a slide underneath — which means the slide itself has to obey the forbidden rule on the way up. That's the tricky part: if a forbidden note is one below your target, your slide will pass right through it. Think of delta blues vocalists who work with five-note palettes and slide into every target; the missing notes shape the whole aesthetic.
steps: Identify the forbidden notes and visualize the allowed palette as a ladder.
Choose a first target among the allowed notes and slide up from just below it — but never through a forbidden pitch.
Slide into each allowed target, adjusting the slide's starting point if needed to avoid forbidden notes.
Let the slides be short — half-step glides that function as ornaments, not pitch hunts.
Anchor on the root via a slide-in whenever you need to reset.
examples: (below)→root → (below)→third → (below)→fifth → (below)→third → (below)→root (forbidding second, fourth, sixth)
(below)→fifth → (below)→seventh → (below)→root up an octave → (below)→fifth (forbidding second, fourth, sixth)
pitchConstraint:forbidden_onset:slideIn.watchOut: The slide from below will sometimes have to start from a forbidden pitch if that pitch is the nearest neighbor — you'll break the rule during the ornament itself. Plan the slide origin for each target, not just the target.
pitchConstraint:forbidden_onset:slideIn.deeperInsight: Pentatonic blues style is essentially forbidden-notes plus slide-in — the missing second and sixth create the scooping style because the slide has nowhere to stop along the way.

pitchConstraint:targetLanding_onset:hard.interaction: You wander freely through the scale and every phrase ends on the same target note hit at full volume. The hard attack on the landing turns it into a punctuation mark — no matter how the phrase got there, it ends with a definitive strike. This is how jazz vocalists end choruses: a free melodic journey that hammers down on the tonal center or a chord tone.
steps: Pick a target note and commit it to memory.
Begin the phrase from anywhere in the scale with any pitch motion you like.
Navigate toward the target, hearing it in your head as you approach.
Drop hard onto the target at full volume — a clean strike, no lead-in.
Rest briefly, then begin a new phrase with a different melodic route to the same target.
examples: third → fifth → fourth → second → root (target=root, final strike hard)
sixth → fourth → third → fifth → third (target=third, hard attack on landing)
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_onset:hard.watchOut: Under a hard-attack landing, you'll start anticipating the target and shortening the wander — you end phrases too quickly and the 'freedom' disappears. Let the phrase breathe before you drop the hammer on the target.
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_onset:hard.deeperInsight: The hard-attack landing is essentially a cadence gesture — jazz and rock vocalists use it to turn any improvised line into something that feels authored.

pitchConstraint:targetLanding_onset:soft.interaction: Free pitch motion, settled gently onto one target — a chord-tone or the root. Because the landing is a soft onset, you hear the target coming before it sounds, which develops audiation of the tonal center while giving your improvising hand full freedom. This is how classical vocalists shape recitative: wander expressively, then bloom onto the landing.
steps: Choose a target and audiate it loud in your head.
Start the phrase wherever feels natural and let the contour unfold freely.
As you near the end of the phrase, begin hearing the target more clearly internally.
Bloom softly onto the target — air, pitch, tone — so it feels earned, not struck.
Rest, then begin another phrase from a new starting point.
examples: fifth → fourth → sixth → third → root (target=root, soft landing)
second → fourth → third → fifth → third (target=third, bloomed onset on arrival)
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_onset:soft.watchOut: A soft landing invites scooping from below into the target to mask pitch uncertainty. The bloom must be vertical — air-to-pitch-to-tone on the target itself, not a slide from a neighbor.
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_onset:soft.deeperInsight: Soft landing onto a target is how classical singers demonstrate tonal-center mastery without cadencing loudly — the ear knows exactly where you are without you shouting it.

pitchConstraint:targetLanding_onset:brea.interaction: You improvise freely and the phrase resolves onto a target note delivered as a sigh. Because the landing is breathy, the target reads as acceptance rather than declaration — the whole phrase feels like finding something quiet rather than winning something loud. This is Nick Drake's fingerpicking vocal phrasing, or a Bon Iver falsetto release.
steps: Pick a target note in a comfortable register for whisper tone.
Start the phrase anywhere and let pitch motion flow freely through allowed notes.
Lower your volume as you approach the target so the landing fits into a breathy mode.
Whisper-land the target with air dominant, pitch barely settling in.
Hold the breathy target for a beat before starting the next phrase.
examples: fourth → second → third → fifth → root (wander, whisper-land on root)
sixth → fifth → seventh → fourth → third (whispered landing on third)
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_onset:brea.watchOut: A breathy target landing is very easy to miss by a half-step — the breathy mode disguises pitch inaccuracy. Audiate the target loudly just before you whisper it so you know you're on the intended note.
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_onset:brea.deeperInsight: When the target is breathy it reads as surrender; when it's hard it reads as arrival — the same note means opposite things depending on the onset.

pitchConstraint:targetLanding_onset:slid.interaction: You wander freely and the final note of every phrase arrives by a slide from below — the target comes with a built-in cry. This is country and soul vocal cadence: every phrase ends with a scoop-up resolution onto the tonic or a chord tone. The slide makes the landing emotional, and the free pitch motion before it keeps the phrase from feeling formulaic.
steps: Pick a target note and plan the slide approach from a half-step below.
Begin the phrase anywhere and let pitch motion wander freely.
As you approach the end of the phrase, position yourself a half-step below the target.
Slide up into the target and hold it briefly to let the landing register.
Start a new phrase from a different starting pitch after a short rest.
examples: fifth → fourth → sixth → second → (below)→root (wander, slide-in landing)
third → fifth → seventh → fourth → (below)→third (free pitch, slide-up target)
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_onset:slid.watchOut: The slide-in landing will become a crutch — you'll start approaching every note, not just the target, from below. The wandering middle of the phrase should use direct attacks; only the final target gets the slide.
pitchConstraint:targetLanding_onset:slid.deeperInsight: Country vocalists reserve the slide-in exclusively for cadence points — it's what makes a phrase feel like it has resolved rather than just stopped.

rhythmConstraint:river_onset:hardAttack.interaction: Every beat gets one note, and every note gets struck at full volume — the river turns into a march. This is the driving quarter-note pulse of a punk chorus or a Mongolian throat-singing motif: nothing subdivides, nothing fades, every pulse is an even hammer strike. The evenness of the river amplifies the impact of each attack because the listener's body locks into the grid.
steps: Set a moderate tempo with a strong click and feel the quarter pulse in your body.
Strike the first note at full volume on the downbeat with zero lead-in.
Place one note per beat, each fired as a clean hard attack, no slurring between.
Keep volume constant — the river is uniform, and so are the attacks.
Use pitch variation (not dynamics) to keep the line from feeling monotonous.
examples: root → third → fifth → third → root → fourth → fifth → root (quarter pulse, every note hit hard)
fifth → fourth → third → fourth → fifth → sixth → fifth → root (hard attacks, one per beat)
rhythmConstraint:river_onset:hardAttack.watchOut: When every note is a hard attack at quarter pulse, your voice or hand will fatigue and start cheating onsets — the river becomes a legato drone at forte. Take micro-breaths between bars to keep the attacks fresh.
rhythmConstraint:river_onset:hardAttack.deeperInsight: The Ramones built entire songs on this combo — quarter-note root-fifth with hard attacks. The monotony is the statement.

rhythmConstraint:river_onset:soft.interaction: One note per beat, each gently bloomed — this is the sound of Gregorian chant or a slow hymn. The river provides a steady pulse without rhythmic drama, and the soft onset means the character comes entirely from pitch and breath quality. Your job is to keep every bloomed note distinct enough that it's heard as a new event, not as a slur.
steps: Set a slow, meditative tempo and breathe low before starting.
Bloom the first note softly on the downbeat — air, pitch, tone.
Re-initiate the soft onset on each subsequent beat, maintaining the pulse strictly.
Keep volume uniform so onset quality carries the articulation.
Anchor phrases on the root or fifth to give the line a tonal home.
examples: root → second → third → fourth → fifth → fourth → third → root (bloomed quarters)
fifth → sixth → fifth → fourth → third → fourth → fifth → sixth (soft onsets on each beat)
rhythmConstraint:river_onset:soft.watchOut: The steady pulse will tempt you to slur soft onsets into one long legato line — then the articulation vanishes and the river becomes a drone. Make sure each beat gets its own breath-to-pitch moment.
rhythmConstraint:river_onset:soft.deeperInsight: Chant traditions use exactly this combo — quarter-note pulse with soft onsets — because it turns pitch alone into the expressive variable.

rhythmConstraint:river_onset:breathy.interaction: A whispered quarter-note pulse — think lullaby, ambient folk, or the verse of a Beach House song. Each beat gets its own breathy onset, so you're essentially breathing in time with the pulse. The line feels like a gentle heartbeat, and the uniformity of the river makes every subtle variation in breath quality audible.
steps: Set a slow tempo and exhale gently before starting.
Place the first whispered note on the downbeat with more air than tone.
Breathe each subsequent note onto its beat with the same airy onset.
Keep volume very low and uniform — any push breaks the whisper.
Rest between phrases so the breath mode doesn't collapse.
examples: root → second → third → second → root (whispered quarters, evenly paced)
third → fourth → fifth → fourth → third → second → root → second (breathy pulse)
rhythmConstraint:river_onset:breathy.watchOut: Breathy singing at quarter-note pulse will run you out of air fast — the river doesn't pause long enough for a full re-breath. Use small, quick snatch-breaths between beats so you don't collapse mid-phrase.
rhythmConstraint:river_onset:breathy.deeperInsight: Lullabies use this combo because the river's predictability soothes while the breathy tone reads as tenderness — together they produce sleep.

rhythmConstraint:river_onset:slideIn.interaction: Quarter-note pulse with a little scoop under every single note — this is classic country vocal phrasing at quarter time. Every beat brings a new slide-in, and because the river is uniform, the slides start to feel like a rhythmic ornament in themselves. Think of a Patsy Cline ballad or a Hank Williams shuffle where every pulse drips.
steps: Set a moderate country-ballad tempo and feel the quarter grid.
Position yourself a half-step below the first target and slide up into it on beat 1.
On each subsequent beat, slide into the new note from just below.
Keep each slide short so the quarter pulse stays audible.
Anchor the root and fifth with particularly committed slides.
examples: (below)→root → (below)→third → (below)→fifth → (below)→third → (below)→root (scooped quarters)
(below)→fifth → (below)→sixth → (below)→fifth → (below)→fourth → (below)→third → (below)→fourth (country river)
rhythmConstraint:river_onset:slideIn.watchOut: Constant slide-ins at quarter time will start dragging the rhythm — each slide eats time and pushes the next beat late. Keep the slides tiny so the pulse never slips.
rhythmConstraint:river_onset:slideIn.deeperInsight: Country singers use slide-ins at the river tempo to create the illusion of relaxed phrasing while actually staying dead-locked to the pulse — the slide makes 'on time' feel 'behind the beat'.

rhythmConstraint:burst_onset:hardAttack.interaction: Four to six quick notes fired at full volume at the top of each phrase, then silence — this is a vocal or guitar hit-and-run. The burst already creates urgency, and the hard attack gives it teeth. Think of a Stooges vocal stab or a James Brown horn punch: maximum intensity for half a second, then absolute silence while the ear processes what just happened.
steps: Plan a 4-6 note burst using whatever pitch contour the other constraints allow.
Inhale fully, then fire all notes of the burst at full volume with no lead-in.
Stop dead after the last burst note — no fade, no tail.
Rest 2-3 full beats in silence, resisting the urge to fill the gap.
Start the next burst with a different pitch shape so each eruption has identity.
examples: root → fifth → third → seventh → fifth (burst, hard) ... rest ... fourth → sixth → second → fourth (burst)
third → fifth → root up an octave → fifth → third (burst) ... rest ... second → fourth → sixth → second
rhythmConstraint:burst_onset:hardAttack.watchOut: Under speed pressure hard attacks will lose precision — you'll hit the right shape but not the exact pitches. Practice the burst slowly first at hard-attack volume, then speed up.
rhythmConstraint:burst_onset:hardAttack.deeperInsight: Hard-attack bursts are the sonic equivalent of shouting a full sentence in one breath — the silence afterward is when the listener hears what you said.

rhythmConstraint:burst_onset:soft.interaction: A quick cluster of gently bloomed notes, then silence — like a flute trill executed with voice. The soft onset on 4-6 fast notes is a real technical ask because each note has to re-bloom before the next arrives, leaving very little time per onset. This is baroque ornament work translated into vocal or phrasing practice.
steps: Plan a short burst — 4-5 notes is easier than 6 under soft-onset constraint.
Breathe low and audiate the burst shape before sound begins.
Bloom each note softly in rapid succession, re-initiating the onset on each new pitch.
Stop cleanly after the burst and rest in silence.
Restart from a different register so each burst has fresh identity.
examples: root → second → third → second → root (bloomed burst) ... rest ... fifth → fourth → third → fourth → fifth
third → fifth → fourth → third → second (soft burst) ... rest ... sixth → fifth → seventh → fifth → sixth
rhythmConstraint:burst_onset:soft.watchOut: Soft onsets at burst speed will merge into one long legato note — you lose the 4-6 distinct events the burst requires. Practice the burst at half speed first to make each bloom audible, then speed up while preserving the onset quality.
rhythmConstraint:burst_onset:soft.deeperInsight: The upper limit of how fast you can sustain clean soft onsets defines the top speed of classical melismatic singing — this combo drills that ceiling.

rhythmConstraint:burst_onset:breathy.interaction: A whispered flurry — 4-6 quick notes on pure air, then silence. Imagine Elizabeth Fraser or a Japanese koto vocal accent: a quick spray of pitches that feels more like a gesture than a sung phrase. The breathy mode means the burst can't go loud, which forces you to use pitch shape alone to create the sense of urgency.
steps: Plan a quick 4-5 note burst shape.
Exhale gently to set the breathy mode.
Whisper-fire the burst notes in rapid succession, keeping air dominant throughout.
Stop immediately and let the silence hold.
Start the next burst with a different shape and starting register.
examples: root → third → second → fifth → root (whispered burst) ... rest ... fourth → sixth → second → fourth
fifth → sixth → fifth → fourth → third (airy flurry) ... rest ... third → second → fourth → third → root
rhythmConstraint:burst_onset:breathy.watchOut: Breathy bursts will blur into an indistinct sibilant wash — the listener can't tell how many notes were there. Keep each pitch clearly articulated even at whisper volume; the burst must stay countable.
rhythmConstraint:burst_onset:breathy.deeperInsight: A breathy burst is the vocal equivalent of a watercolor brushstroke — one quick motion that must read as intentional shape rather than an accident.

rhythmConstraint:burst_onset:slideIn.interaction: Every note of the rapid burst arrives with a little scoop underneath — and because the burst is fast, those scoops become tiny bends rather than full slides. This is blues-guitar lead territory, or a Jackie Wilson fast melisma: a torrent of notes that all sound like they're being pulled up to their targets just in time. It's virtuosic and vocal no matter what instrument you're on.
steps: Plan a burst of 4-5 notes so the slide-ins have time to register.
On the downbeat, start the burst with a slide up into the first target.
Approach each subsequent note from a half-step below, keeping slides extremely short.
Stop dead after the last note and let the silence hold.
Reset for the next burst with a different starting register.
examples: (below)→root → (below)→third → (below)→fifth → (below)→third → (below)→root (slide-burst) ... rest ... same shape up an octave
(below)→fifth → (below)→sixth → (below)→fifth → (below)→fourth → (below)→third (fast blues-burst)
rhythmConstraint:burst_onset:slideIn.watchOut: Slide-ins on fast notes will blur into a single upward smear — you'll hear a portamento instead of a burst. Make sure each note has a distinct arrival moment before the next slide starts.
rhythmConstraint:burst_onset:slideIn.deeperInsight: Slide-in bursts are the fastest way to reveal how the ear resolves pitch — the brain snaps each slide to its destination as soon as the next note starts.

rhythmConstraint:space_onset:hardAttack.interaction: Half of every bar is rest, and the few notes you place land with full-volume hard attacks — this is sculptural music. Think of Nils Frahm piano stabs or a Talk Talk arrangement where one enormous strike sits inside a vast silence. The hard attack is amplified by the context: because there's so much rest, every struck note feels like a statement carved into the room.
steps: Set a slow tempo and commit to silence for at least half of every bar.
Breathe, then fire a single note or a tight two-note figure at full volume.
Let the rest hold for two or three beats — do not fill the gap.
Place the next attack at a different pitch to avoid monotony.
Make every entry count; there are very few notes so none can be accidental.
examples: root — (rest) — fifth — (rest) — third — (rest) — root (hard strikes, sparse)
third — (rest) — seventh — (rest) — fifth — (rest) — root (punctuated full-volume)
rhythmConstraint:space_onset:hardAttack.watchOut: The silence will pressure you to fill it — one extra hard attack between the planned ones kills the space constraint and makes the line feel restless. Count the rests in real beats, not in impatience.
rhythmConstraint:space_onset:hardAttack.deeperInsight: Space plus hard attack is cinematic music's core drama technique — one loud event in a silent frame creates more intensity than a barrage of notes ever could.

rhythmConstraint:space_onset:soft.interaction: Rests fill half of every bar and the notes you do place arrive as gentle blooms — this is ambient art song, Harold Budd piano territory, Arvo Pärt choral writing. The silence lets you fully audiate each upcoming note, and the soft onset lets it emerge as if from the silence itself rather than interrupting it. It's the most meditative combo on the list.
steps: Set a slow tempo and breathe low.
Bloom the first note into the silence with a soft, air-to-pitch onset.
Let at least a two-beat silence hold before the next note.
Audiate the next pitch during the silence, then bloom it softly into being.
End the phrase on a bloomed root or fifth and let the silence linger.
examples: root — (rest) — third — (rest) — fifth — (rest) — root (bloomed, sparse)
fourth — (rest) — sixth — (rest) — third — (rest) — root (soft onsets in ample space)
rhythmConstraint:space_onset:soft.watchOut: A soft onset in lots of space will drift pitch-wise if you aren't audiating — you'll bloom onto a note close to but not quite the intended pitch. Hear the target clearly before you sound it.
rhythmConstraint:space_onset:soft.deeperInsight: Arvo Pärt's tintinnabuli style is literally this combo — spaced soft onsets — and it's built entirely on audiation ability rather than technique.

rhythmConstraint:space_onset:breathy.interaction: Maximum quiet plus maximum rest — a whispered note emerges out of a long silence and disappears back into it. This is the sound of a closing ECM album or a hospice lullaby: the gesture is almost nothing, but the surrounding silence makes it enormous. The breathy onset means the note barely disturbs the room before the rest returns.
steps: Settle into stillness and set a very slow tempo.
Exhale, then breathe the first note into existence with maximum air.
Let the silence return for 2-3 full beats.
Whisper the next note into the silence at a different pitch.
End the phrase on a breathy anchor (root or fifth) and hold the final silence.
examples: root — (rest) — third — (rest) — fifth — (rest) — third (whispered, lots of space)
second — (rest) — fourth — (rest) — sixth — (rest) — root (breathy, sparse)
rhythmConstraint:space_onset:breathy.watchOut: At whisper volume the note may not be audible at all — you'll mouth it and think you played it. Sing with enough tone that the note lives in the room, even if it's very soft.
rhythmConstraint:space_onset:breathy.deeperInsight: Breathy-space is how memory is scored in film — the whispered note in the long silence triggers recall without demanding attention.

rhythmConstraint:space_onset:slideIn.interaction: Silence for most of the bar, and when a note does arrive, it slides up into place from below — like a ghost approaching and then materializing. This is the sound of a slide guitar phrase in a desert soundtrack, or a Tom Waits vocal ornament: a rare note, scooped into place, surrounded by silence. The contrast between the scoop and the silence creates most of the drama.
steps: Set a slow tempo and commit to sparse placement.
Breathe, then slide up into the first target from a half-step below.
Let the slide complete, then hold the target briefly.
Allow 2-3 beats of silence before the next slide-in.
Anchor periodically by sliding into the root.
examples: (below)→root — (rest) — (below)→fifth — (rest) — (below)→third — (rest) — (below)→root
(below)→third — (rest) — (below)→sixth — (rest) — (below)→fourth — (rest) — (below)→root
rhythmConstraint:space_onset:slideIn.watchOut: In lots of space, the slide will tempt you to make it longer and longer for dramatic effect — at which point it isn't an ornament, it's the whole phrase. Keep each slide under a half-step regardless of how much room you have.
rhythmConstraint:space_onset:slideIn.deeperInsight: Desert-soundtrack guitar style (Calexico, Ennio Morricone) is built on this combo — a sparse slide note contains more longing than any filled phrase could.

rhythmConstraint:offbeat_onset:hardAttac.interaction: Nothing lands on 1 or 3, and every note that does land gets struck at full volume — this is ska-punk vocals or a reggae upstroke turned into a hook. The hard attack makes the offbeat placement feel defiant; the pulse is strong but you refuse to land on it, and the full-volume strikes make sure nobody misses the displacement.
steps: Tap the quarter pulse silently in your foot and vow not to play on those beats.
Attack the 'and' of one at full volume with a clean, no-lead-in strike.
Place every subsequent note on an 'and,' maintaining full volume.
Keep your foot tapping the downbeats so your ear stays aware of where they are.
Rest on an offbeat (not a downbeat) to maintain the constraint through silence.
examples: (and)root → (and)third → (and)fifth → (and)third → (and)root (offbeat attacks, full volume)
(and)fifth → (and)fourth → (and)sixth → (and)fifth → (and)third (hard offbeat strikes)
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_onset:hardAttac.watchOut: Under hard-attack pressure you'll drift toward the downbeat — the voice or hand wants to land on the strong pulse because it feels more powerful. Use a metronome that clicks the downbeats so you can feel the offense of landing on them.
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_onset:hardAttac.deeperInsight: Ska guitar's iconic offbeat upstroke is exactly this combo — hard attack, never on 1 or 3 — and the constraint produces the genre's entire rhythmic identity.

rhythmConstraint:offbeat_onset:soft.interaction: Notes land between the beats and bloom gently into place — this is the rhythmic delicacy of jazz ballad phrasing or a Stan Getz bossa. The soft onset already feels reflective, and pairing it with offbeat placement means every note is both rhythmically and tonally floating. You need a very strong internal pulse to keep the offbeats from collapsing into just 'somewhere around the middle.'
steps: Tap the quarter pulse silently and set a moderate tempo.
Breathe, then bloom the first note on the 'and' of one with a soft onset.
Bloom each subsequent note on an 'and,' keeping the onsets gentle.
Never land on a downbeat, even if the soft-onset timing feels uncertain.
End phrases on an offbeat 'and' so the constraint holds through the release.
examples: (and)root → (and)third → (and)fifth → (and)third → (and)root (bloomed offbeats)
(and)third → (and)second → (and)fourth → (and)third → (and)fifth (soft onsets on every and)
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_onset:soft.watchOut: Soft onsets make offbeat placement imprecise — you'll bloom just before or just after the 'and' and lose the sharpness of the displacement. Tap the pulse so you know exactly where 'between' is.
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_onset:soft.deeperInsight: Jazz ballad phrasing lives in this combo — soft onsets on offbeats — because the gentle entry disguises just how precisely the placement has to be timed.

rhythmConstraint:offbeat_onset:breathy.interaction: Whispered notes placed only between the beats — like a lover's murmur timed to the syncopation of a slow groove. This is the sound of a D'Angelo verse or a Sade bridge: the pulse is rigid but every note arrives on air, off the beat, and the combination feels intimate and groovy at once. The breath mode demands extreme pulse awareness because the voice can't muscle its way back on time.
steps: Tap the quarter pulse silently in your foot.
Exhale gently and whisper the first note on the 'and' of one.
Whisper each subsequent note on an 'and,' keeping air dominant.
Keep the volume very low so the onsets don't collapse into puffy sounds.
Anchor on the root or fifth via a whispered offbeat hit periodically.
examples: (and)root → (and)third → (and)fourth → (and)third → (and)root (whispered offbeats)
(and)second → (and)fourth → (and)sixth → (and)fifth → (and)third (airy, off-grid)
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_onset:breathy.watchOut: Breathy tone plus offbeat placement is the easiest way to sound like you just missed the beat — listeners may hear it as 'late on 1' rather than 'on the and.' Commit to the offbeat with the air so the placement is intentional.
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_onset:breathy.deeperInsight: Neo-soul phrasing is essentially whispered offbeats — the soft air plus the displacement creates the genre's floating, flirtatious quality.

rhythmConstraint:offbeat_onset:slideIn.interaction: Every offbeat note gets approached from below with a slide — and because the notes aren't on the downbeat, the slide has to finish precisely on the 'and.' This is a virtuosic reggae or funk phrasing move: the groove is displaced and decorated, and every entry feels like it had to sneak in just in time. Think of a Toots vocal or a Stevie Wonder clavinet lick.
steps: Tap the quarter pulse silently and commit to offbeat placement.
Start a half-step below the first target and slide up so the slide completes on the 'and' of one.
Continue placing offbeat notes, each one reached by a short slide from below.
Keep each slide brief so the offbeat precision is preserved.
Anchor on the root by sliding into it on an offbeat when resetting the phrase.
examples: (and)(below)→root → (and)(below)→third → (and)(below)→fifth (slide-in offbeats)
(and)(below)→fifth → (and)(below)→fourth → (and)(below)→sixth → (and)(below)→fifth
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_onset:slideIn.watchOut: The slide plus the offbeat adds up to two timing tasks — the slide may finish late and push the note past the 'and,' onto the next downbeat. Start the slide earlier than feels natural so the target lands exactly on the 'and.'
rhythmConstraint:offbeat_onset:slideIn.deeperInsight: Reggae vocalists often slide into offbeat hits specifically because the slide makes the offbeat feel rhythmically delayed — a double syncopation that gives the genre its characteristic lag.

rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_onset:hardAt.interaction: Pick a short rhythmic cell and repeat it, with every note struck at full volume — the rhythm becomes a chant and the pitches shift over it. This is tribal-rock phrasing, a Tool or Mastodon riff, where a pounding rhythmic motif anchors the line and the melody unfolds on top. The hard attack locks the rhythm into the listener's body before the pitches can tell their story.
steps: Choose a short rhythm cell — for example, quarter-eighth-eighth-quarter.
Play the first iteration with all notes hit at full volume and the exact rhythm.
Repeat the cell, but change the pitches while keeping the rhythm identical.
Every few iterations return to the first pitch shape to anchor the ear.
Keep the rhythm cell rigid — if it varies, the seed constraint is broken.
examples: [root → third → fifth → root] in the cell (hard) → [root → fourth → sixth → root] → [fifth → seventh → root up an octave → fifth]
[third → fifth → fourth → third] (hard strikes) → [second → fourth → third → second] → [sixth → root up an octave → seventh → sixth]
rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_onset:hardAt.watchOut: Hard attacks will tempt you to rush the rhythm cell — under intensity the eighth notes compress and the cell loses its identity. Use a metronome so the rhythm stays exact even as the pitches change.
rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_onset:hardAt.deeperInsight: A rhythmic seed with hard attacks is the structural core of most metal and hard-rock riffs — lock the rhythm, vary the pitch, and the brain hears it as a coherent theme.

rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_onset:soft.interaction: A short rhythm cell repeated with gentle onsets and shifting pitches — this is baroque variation form applied to a single rhythm figure. Think of a Bach prelude's pulse-pattern with different pitches each bar, or an Indian raga's rhythmic seed gently unfolding. The soft onset gives you time to think about the pitch change between iterations.
steps: Choose a short rhythm cell and internalize it silently first.
Bloom the first iteration's pitches into the cell with soft onsets.
Repeat the cell, changing the pitches but maintaining exact rhythmic placement.
Bloom each new pitch shape the same way — air, pitch, tone.
Return to the original pitch shape every few iterations as a reference.
examples: [root → third → fifth → third] (bloomed) → [second → fourth → sixth → fourth] → [root → fourth → sixth → fourth]
[fifth → fourth → third → second] (soft onsets) → [fourth → third → second → root] → [sixth → fifth → fourth → third]
rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_onset:soft.watchOut: Soft onsets plus a repeating rhythm will blur into legato — you'll lose the articulation that gives the rhythm cell its identity. Re-initiate breath pressure on each note of the cell, even within a fast pattern.
rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_onset:soft.deeperInsight: Indian classical raga uses this exact structure — a rhythmic cycle with pitches varying on gentle onsets — because it trains both rhythm holding and pitch variation simultaneously.

rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_onset:breath.interaction: Whispered iterations of a rhythm cell with shifting pitches — imagine a bedtime prayer or a hushed children's rhyme. The rhythm gives the line its shape and the breathy onset gives it intimacy. Because the air-heavy mode keeps volume low, the rhythmic pattern has to be airtight or the line sounds random.
steps: Pick a short rhythm cell and tap it silently to internalize it.
Exhale gently and whisper-sing the first pitch iteration, keeping air dominant.
Repeat the rhythm with different pitches, each note still breathy.
Maintain very low, uniform volume throughout all iterations.
Anchor with a final iteration on root-based pitches.
examples: [root → second → third → second] (whispered) → [third → fourth → fifth → fourth] → [root → second → third → second]
[fifth → sixth → fifth → fourth] (airy) → [third → fourth → third → second] → [root → second → root → seventh below]
rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_onset:breath.watchOut: Breathy mode at rhythm-seed speed loses the rhythmic edge — whispered eighth notes can bleed together into one wash. Exaggerate the rhythmic articulation even at whisper level so the cell stays countable.
rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_onset:breath.deeperInsight: A whispered rhythmic seed is the sonic equivalent of a child reciting a rhyme — the rhythm carries the meaning because the whisper alone could not.

rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_onset:slideI.interaction: A short rhythm cell repeated, and every note of every iteration gets a tiny slide underneath. This is the phrasing of a Muscle Shoals soul horn section or a Stax vocal backup — the rhythm is tight but every note is scooped, so the figure feels both locked and loose. The slides turn a mechanical rhythm into something that swings.
steps: Choose a short rhythm cell and internalize it.
Slide up into each note of the first iteration, keeping each slide tight.
Repeat the rhythm with different pitches, sliding into each of them the same way.
Preserve the rhythmic cell exactly — no slide can drag the rhythm.
Anchor a final iteration by sliding into root-based targets.
examples: [(below)→root → (below)→third → (below)→fifth → (below)→third] → [(below)→second → (below)→fourth → (below)→sixth → (below)→fourth]
[(below)→fifth → (below)→fourth → (below)→third → (below)→second] → [(below)→fourth → (below)→third → (below)→second → (below)→root]
rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_onset:slideI.watchOut: Slides on a repeating rhythm will gradually shift each note later as the slides eat rhythmic space — the cell starts dragging. Start slides ahead of the beat so each target arrives exactly on time.
rhythmConstraint:rhythmSeed_onset:slideI.deeperInsight: Soul horn arrangements lock their rhythmic cells with slide-ins specifically to add genre flavor without compromising rhythmic precision — the two constraints cooperate if the slide is disciplined.

rhythmConstraint:triplets_onset:hardAtta.interaction: Three notes per beat, every one hit at full volume — this is a shuffle beat turned aggressive, or a full-throttle blues-rock solo. The triplet feel creates swing but the hard attacks erase any looseness; the line drives forward like a train. Think of a ZZ Top run or a John Bonham tom fill where the triplet pulse is relentless but every note has real weight.
steps: Set a moderate tempo and feel the triplet subdivision before playing.
Place three notes per beat, each struck at full volume with no lead-in.
Keep the accent even across the three subdivisions so the triplet stays intact.
Use pitch motion to add interest since dynamics are fixed.
End phrases on the root or fifth to give the propulsive line a home.
